← side projects
The United States · 1970–2026

Does the country ever feel as good as its data says it is — or does every year feel worse than it was, and every past look better than it felt?

Spoiler: almost never — 44 of 57 years felt worse than the data said ↓ see why

57 years, five scored dimensions, one year at a time · how it was made

2026

The answer ↓

The year in headlines

    Drag the timeline · arrow keys to travel · space to play · colors show the year’s mood
    ⌄

    Mood vs. reality

    The answer to the question at the top: almost never — and the gap between how America is doing and how America feels is widening.

    The gap — what the data said vs. how it felt

    “The data” is the average of each year’s Economy and Markets scores; “the mood” is the Overall Mood score. Crimson shading marks years that felt worse than the data; teal marks the rare opposite.

    The decoupling — every year, feelings vs. facts

    Each dot is a year. On the diagonal, feelings match facts. Below it, the year felt worse than its data. Watch the decades drift right (richer) and down (gloomier).

    The reality check — real survey data, same story

    No model judgments here: the magenta line is the University of Michigan’s Index of Consumer Sentiment (annual average of actual survey responses). The dashed line is what the economy alone — inflation, unemployment, and real GDP-per-capita growth — predicts sentiment should be, from a regression fit on 1970–2025. The shaded gap is pure vibes.

    Five dimensions, fifty-seven years

    Each line is a dimension scored −3 to +3. Hover for any year’s scores; click to travel there.

    The whole span at a glance

    Every score, 1970–2026. Hover a cell for the one-sentence justification; click to travel to that year.

    Decade by decade

    Average score per dimension. Click a decade to travel to its first year.

    The 57-year story — read the full essay ▸

    Told across fifty-seven years, the story of the United States from 1970 to mid-2026 is not a single arc but the slow separation of arcs that Americans had long assumed moved together. In 1970 material prosperity, market performance, the credibility of governing institutions, the vitality of a shared culture, and the national mood were roughly coupled: when the economy faltered, confidence and politics faltered with it, and when the country prospered it tended to feel that it did. Over the following five and a half decades those threads pulled apart. Measured by the five domain scores in this dataset, the economy and equity markets ended the period far stronger and more resilient than they began, while the governance score and the national mood ended weaker — and, in the case of politics, in something close to secular free fall. The America of 2026 is materially richer, technologically transformed, and financially buoyant, yet it trusts its own institutions and feels about its own condition considerably worse than the America of the 1980s and 1990s did.

    The period opens in crisis and disillusion. The 1970s (overall mood -0.50) were the decade of stagflation, Watergate, defeat in Vietnam, and two oil shocks — a sustained assault on the postwar consensus that left every institution diminished. The 1980s (+0.70) and 1990s (+1.00) then delivered the most sustained run of broad-based national confidence in the entire dataset: disinflation, the longest peacetime expansions on record, the peaceful end of the Cold War, and, in the late Clinton years, a rare simultaneous alignment of strong scores across all five domains. The composite peak of 1999 (+12, the highest in the dataset) is the statistical signature of that unipolar moment.

    What follows is a long descent that resists tidy partisan explanation because it spans administrations of both parties. The 2000s (mood -0.60) brought the disputed 2000 election, the dot-com bust, September 11, the Iraq War, and the 2008 financial crisis — the worst decade for national mood since the 1970s. The 2010s were the paradox decade: a strong and steadily recovering economy (average +1.10) and roaring markets (+1.50) coexisting with a collapsing governance score (-1.10) and a mood that never turned convincingly positive (-0.30). The partial 2020s (through mid-2026) carry that paradox to its extreme — markets still elevated (+1.29) atop the lowest politics score of any decade (-1.57) and a mood (-0.71) worse even than the malaise of the 1970s.

    The through-line, then, is divergence: a country that grew steadily wealthier and whose markets grew steadily more powerful while its confidence in self-government eroded and its collective mood soured. Prosperity ceased to purchase either trust or contentment. Understanding 1970–2026 means understanding how a nation could win the arguments of the twentieth century — the Cold War, the inflation of the 1970s, the recessions of 1982 and 2008 — and still arrive at mid-2026 more anxious, more polarized, and less confident in its institutions than it had been at almost any point in the preceding half-century.

    Economic arc

    The economic history of these fifty-seven years is a story of a machine that repeatedly broke, was repaired, and each time rebuilt with a different operating logic. The 1970s were the decade of stagflation, an unprecedented combination of stagnant growth and accelerating prices that the postwar Keynesian toolkit could neither explain nor cure. The decade's economy score averaged a flat 0.0, dragged down by the twin oil shocks and the deep 1973–75 recession — 1974 registered the dataset's worst possible economy reading (-3) alongside 12.3% inflation, a shrinking GDP, and unemployment climbing past 7%. By 1979–80, with inflation near 13% and the prime rate at 21.5%, the old consensus was exhausted.

    The repair came through pain. Paul Volcker's Federal Reserve broke inflation's back by inducing the deepest recession since the 1930s: 1982 posted an economy score of -2 with unemployment at 10.8%, the highest postwar figure, even as CPI collapsed from near 9% to under 4%. The disinflation cleared the ground for what economists would later call the Great Moderation — a quarter-century of milder cycles, anchored expectations, and long expansions. The 1984 boom (economy +3, 7.2% real growth) and the sustained 1990s expansion (decade economy average +1.40, the highest in the dataset, peaking at +3 in 1997–99) represent the payoff, a period when low inflation and steady growth came to seem the natural order rather than a hard-won achievement.

    That confidence proved brittle. The 2000s (economy average -0.10, the only negative decade) bracketed the expansion with two busts: the dot-com unwind and, catastrophically, the 2008–09 financial crisis, which produced back-to-back -3 economy readings and the worst contraction since 1946. The 2010s recovery was real but slow and strange — a decade of near-zero interest rates, quantitative easing, and disinflation so persistent that the Fed worried about too little inflation, not too much. That world ended abruptly with the pandemic: 2020's shutdown (economy -2) triggered the fastest collapse and the largest peacetime fiscal-monetary intervention on record, and its aftermath returned an old enemy, with 2022 inflation hitting 9.1%, the worst since 1981, and the sharpest Fed tightening since Volcker. The 2023–24 soft landing — inflation halved without the widely forecast recession — closed the arc on a note of guarded resilience, though mid-2026 finds growth cooling and inflation still stubbornly above target as tariffs work through supply chains.

    Market arc

    Equity markets over these fifty-seven years tell a more triumphant story than the real economy — and one that increasingly diverged from it. The period opens in the ruins of one of the worst bear markets in American history: the 1973–74 collapse, which handed 1974 the dataset's worst possible markets score (-3) as the Dow bottomed near 577, roughly 45% below its early-1973 peak. The market spent the balance of the 1970s convalescing, with the decade's markets score averaging a modest +0.40 even as inflation quietly ravaged real returns.

    The turn came on August 12, 1982, when the market bottomed and launched what would become one of the great secular bull runs in history. The 1980s (markets average +1.60) delivered blockbuster years — 1985's S&P total return of nearly 32%, the 1989 rally — interrupted but not derailed by the October 1987 crash, whose one-day severity registered barely in the annual score (1987 markets 0) because the year finished roughly flat. The 1990s melt-up was more spectacular still: five consecutive years of 20%-plus returns culminating in the NASDAQ's 85.6% surge in 1999, with 1995, 1997, and 1999 all posting the maximum markets score of +3. The bubble burst in 2000–02, bottoming with 2002's -3, and the 2008 crisis produced another -3.

    The most striking feature of the market arc, however, is how thoroughly it decoupled from the real economy after 2008. The clearest single illustration is 2009: the economy scored -3, its worst possible reading, in the depths of the Great Recession, while the market scored +3 as stocks bottomed in March and staged a historic rally. That divergence became structural. Zero interest rates and quantitative easing powered the 2009–2021 bull market — the 2010s markets average of +1.50 rivals the booming 1980s and 1990s despite a weaker economy and a collapsing governance score — with standout +3 years in 2013, 2017, and 2019. Even the 2020s, the worst decade for both politics and mood, has posted a robust +1.29 markets average, as the 2020 pandemic crash reversed within months and the 2023 AI-driven mega-cap rally erased the 2022 bear market. Markets, in short, spent the back half of this period telling a consistently more optimistic story than either the economy or the country's own sense of itself.

    Political arc

    No dimension in this dataset falls as far or as steadily as the governance score, and its decline is the single most important structural fact of the fifty-seven years. The measure captured here is not partisan advantage but the perceived functionality, legitimacy, and stability of the political system, and by that yardstick the arc runs almost monotonically downhill across the last four decades: 1980s +1.20, 1990s +0.90, 2000s +0.30, 2010s -1.10, 2020s -1.57. Judged by its own standards, American governance was substantially more trusted and more effective in the Reagan–Clinton years than at any point since.

    The decline began from a low base and initially reversed. The politics score bottomed at the dataset's worst possible reading (-3) in 1973, the year Watergate, the Saturday Night Massacre, and the collapse of the imperial presidency detonated together; 1974 (Nixon's resignation, an unelected president and vice president) remained deeply negative. Yet the system visibly repaired itself. The Bicentennial year 1976, the hostage-release and Reagan-recovery year 1981, the landslide-and-summitry years of 1984–85, the Cold War's peaceful end in 1989, and the balanced-budget bipartisanship of 1996 all scored +2, the dataset's high mark for politics. Whatever one thinks of the policies, the machinery of divided-power government in the 1980s and 1990s was widely experienced as working: elections were accepted, major bipartisan legislation passed, and crises were absorbed.

    After roughly 2010 the erosion becomes chronic rather than cyclical, and it spans both parties' periods in power. The 2011 debt-ceiling standoff and credit downgrade, the 2013 shutdown, the bitter 2018 confirmation and investigation battles, and above all the 2020–21 sequence — a contested election followed by the first violent disruption of a peaceful transfer of power in American history on January 6 — mark a system in which the losing side increasingly rejected outcomes and routine governance repeatedly gave way to brinkmanship. The 2020s' record-low average reflects recurring -2 readings across 2020, 2023, 2025, and 2026. The analytically important point, held apart from any partisan reading, is that this is a secular trend, not the property of one leader or one party: the score fell under Democratic and Republican presidents alike, through unified and divided government, which is precisely what makes it look less like ordinary political weather and more like a change in the climate of American self-government.

    Cultural arc

    Culture is the steadiest and most reliably positive dimension in the entire dataset — the one domain whose decade averages never turn negative (1970s +1.40, 1980s +1.70, 1990s +2.00, 2000s +0.70, 2010s +0.80, 2020s +0.43). For most of these fifty-seven years, whatever else was going wrong — recession, scandal, war — Americans went on producing and consuming an extraordinarily vital popular culture, and that vitality served as a kind of national ballast. Even in the shattering years, culture held: 1973 and 1974, catastrophic in every other domain, still scored culture +1.

    The high era ran from the 1970s through the 1990s, the age of the analog mass audience, when a relatively small number of channels, studios, labels, and networks delivered genuinely shared cultural events. This was the world of the M*A*S*H finale and the Thriller phenomenon of 1983, of the blockbuster and the network-television monoculture, when a hit was something the whole country encountered at roughly the same time. That shared culture reached its apex in 1999, the only year in the dataset to earn the maximum culture score of +3 — The Matrix, Fight Club, The Sopranos, a saturated and self-confident popular culture at the exact moment of national prosperity and unipolar power.

    The internet and streaming era then fragmented the monoculture even as it multiplied the raw quantity of content. The 2000s and 2010s culture averages (+0.70 and +0.80) sit well below the 1990s peak — not because Americans made less, but because they increasingly experienced it apart, sorted into algorithmic niches rather than gathered around common events. The one true cultural collapse came in 2020, the only negative culture reading in the dataset (-2), when the pandemic physically shuttered the shared spaces — theaters, concerts, sports, festivals — through which culture is collectively lived, an unprecedented interruption of public life itself. The partial 2020s average of +0.43 marks the lowest cultural vitality of any decade here: a culture still enormously productive, but more fragmented, more contested, and less unifying than at any earlier point in the period.

    National mood arc

    The national mood is where the whole dataset comes to a point, because it is the dimension that most directly answers the question of how it actually felt to be an American in a given year — and its trajectory is a long U that never fully closes. The 1970s were the decade of malaise (-0.50), a word that entered the political vocabulary precisely then: gas lines, double-digit inflation, Watergate, Vietnam's fall, and the Iran hostage crisis produced a pervasive sense that the country's best days might be behind it, captured in 1979's -2 and a Consumer Sentiment Index at a postwar low.

    The recovery of confidence in the 1980s and 1990s is the emotional heart of the period. Mood turned decisively positive (1980s +0.70, 1990s +1.00), and the run of +2 mood years — 1984's Morning in America, the mid-1980s Reagan boom, and the extraordinary late-Clinton stretch of 1996–99, four consecutive +2 readings — represents the most sustained national optimism in the dataset. It was grounded in something real: a won Cold War, a budget in surplus, unemployment at a thirty-year low, and markets compounding at 20% a year. This was the country at its most confident, and 1999 marks the summit before the long descent.

    The twenty-first century has been, in mood terms, a story of shocks that confidence never fully recovered from. The 2000s (-0.60) absorbed the disputed 2000 election, 9/11, and the 2008 crisis; the low point of the entire dataset arrived in 2020, the only -3 mood reading, when pandemic, lockdown, economic freefall, and social rupture converged. What is most telling, though, is the 2010s and 2020s more broadly: even in years of strong growth, record markets, and low unemployment, mood refused to turn convincingly positive (2010s -0.30, 2020s -0.71). This is the defining puzzle of the period's end — a country that is, by most material measures, prosperous and comfortable, yet anxious, polarized, and ill-at-ease, its mood in the partial 2020s scoring worse even than the malaise decade of the 1970s. Prosperity and contentment, once assumed to travel together, had come apart.

    Recurring patterns

    Several patterns recur across the fifty-seven years with enough regularity to look structural rather than incidental. The most consequential is the decoupling of financial markets from the real economy, which grows steadily more pronounced over time. Early in the period the two moved roughly together, but by the twenty-first century they routinely diverged: 2009 is the archetype, with the economy at its -3 floor and the market at +3 in the same twelve months, and the entire post-2008 era generalizes the pattern, as a decade of near-zero rates and quantitative easing produced a 2010s market average (+1.50) worthy of a boom decade atop a merely adequate economy and a broken politics. By the 2020s the market (+1.29) was buoyant while mood (-0.71) and governance (-1.57) hit dataset lows — asset prices telling one story while the country told itself another.

    A second pattern is the remarkable resilience of culture. It is the only dimension whose decade averages never turn negative, and it repeatedly holds up in years that are catastrophic everywhere else — culture scored +1 through the 1973–74 collapse and even +2 amid the 2008 crisis. Popular culture functioned as a kind of national shock absorber, a source of shared vitality that persisted through recession, scandal, and war. The single exception proves the rule: only in 2020, when the pandemic physically closed the theaters, arenas, and festivals through which culture is collectively lived, did the dimension finally go negative, because for the first time the shared spaces themselves were shut.

    Two further patterns concern how crises arrive and how good times end. Oil and inflation shocks recur as the period's most reliable economic triggers — the 1973 embargo, the 1979 spike, the 2008 oil surge, and the 2022 energy-and-inflation shock each detonated or deepened a downturn, and inflation's return in 2022 after four decades of quiescence showed the pattern was dormant, not dead. And repeatedly, each boom sowed the seeds of the next bust: the 1990s melt-up inflated the dot-com bubble that burst in 2000–02; the mid-2000s housing and credit boom produced the 2008 catastrophe; the long cheap-money bull of the 2010s set up the violent 2022 repricing when rates finally normalized. Overarching all of these is the secular decline of the governance score — the one trend that does not oscillate but simply falls, from +1.20 in the 1980s to -1.57 in the 2020s, an erosion of institutional trust that spans both parties and stands as the period's most durable and least reversible development.

    🏅 Best years

    • 1999 — The single best year in the dataset, with a composite +12 and the only maximum culture score (+3): a won Cold War, a budget in surplus, 4% unemployment, a NASDAQ up 85.6%, and a saturated, self-confident popular culture. It is the summit of the American unipolar moment, the high-water mark before the long twenty-first-century descent.
    • 1985 — A composite +11 and the emotional core of the Reagan boom, with the economy in its third year of 4%-plus growth, an S&P total return near 32%, a +2 mood, and the Geneva summit thawing the Cold War. It captures the 1980s at their most confident — hard-won prosperity converted into broad national optimism.
    • 1997 — A composite +11 and arguably the purest year of the 1990s expansion, with 4.5% growth, a 24-year-low unemployment rate, decelerating inflation, a third straight 20%-plus market year, and the bipartisan Balanced Budget Act. Nearly every macro indicator that mattered ran the right way at once.

    🩶 Hardest years

    • 1974 — The worst year in the dataset (composite -9), when the postwar order nearly broke: a president resigned in disgrace, both president and vice president ended the year unelected, inflation hit 12.3%, GDP shrank, and the Dow bottomed roughly 45% below its peak — the only year with both economy and markets at the -3 floor.
    • 2020 — The pandemic year holds the dataset's only -3 mood reading and its only negative culture score (-2), as lockdown, the fastest bear-market collapse in history, Depression-era unemployment, and social rupture converged. It is the deepest single point of the twenty-first-century mood trough.
    • 1973 — The hinge year of the postwar era's unwinding and the sole holder of the -3 politics floor: the OPEC embargo ended cheap energy, Bretton Woods formally died, and Watergate, the Saturday Night Massacre, and John Dean's testimony collapsed the presidency's aura of untouchability all at once.
    • 2008 — The financial-crisis year (composite -5), with economy and markets both at -3 as the housing and credit boom unwound into the deepest panic since 1933 — Lehman's collapse, AIG's nationalization, and the worst contraction since 1946 — the shock from which national confidence never fully recovered.

    The 57-year verdict

    Across fifty-seven years, the American experience from 1970 to mid-2026 is best understood as the story of a country that grew steadily more capable and more prosperous even as it grew steadily less confident in itself and its institutions — a divergence between rising material and market fortunes on one side and declining political trust and national mood on the other. In 1970 those things still moved together, and the country was in visible distress on every front: a stagnating, inflation-ridden economy, a market in the early stages of a brutal bear, a political order about to be convulsed by Watergate, and a mood of resentful, embattled exhaustion as the 1960s finally cracked. By mid-2026 the material picture had been transformed almost beyond recognition — the economy vastly larger and, after the 2023–24 soft landing, more resilient than its 1970s self; the equity market, having survived 1974, 1987, 2000–02, 2008, and 2020, sitting near record highs and compounding wealth on a scale the 1970s could not imagine — and yet the country felt worse about its condition and trusted its government less than it had in that troubled opening year.

    The intervening decades trace the shape of that separation. The recovery of the 1980s and 1990s was genuine and broad: it aligned all five dimensions, producing the sustained confidence that peaked in the late-Clinton years and crested in 1999, the single best year in the dataset and the summit of the American unipolar moment. That alignment was the historical exception, not the norm. The twenty-first century pulled the threads apart. A sequence of shocks — the disputed 2000 election, 9/11, Iraq, the 2008 financial crisis, and the 2020 pandemic — battered the national mood into a trough it never climbed fully out of, while the machinery of self-government entered a decline that, uniquely among the five dimensions, did not oscillate but simply fell: from a +1.20 governance average in the 1980s to -1.57 in the 2020s, across administrations of both parties, through unified and divided control alike. That consistency is what marks it as structural. It is the deepest finding in the dataset, and the one least attributable to any single leader or faction.

    The defining puzzle of the period's end is that prosperity stopped purchasing contentment. The 2010s and 2020s repeatedly delivered strong growth, low unemployment, and soaring markets alongside a mood that would not turn positive and a politics in open dysfunction; by mid-2026 the market stood at +1.29 for the decade while mood sat at -0.71, worse than the malaise of the 1970s, and governance at its lowest reading in the entire record. Americans in 2026 are, by almost every material measure, better off than Americans in 1970 — wealthier, healthier, more connected, living amid an economy and a market that have proven able to absorb blow after blow. But they are also more polarized, more distrustful of their institutions, and more anxious, sorted into fragmented cultural and informational worlds where the shared events that once bound the country — the network broadcast, the common cultural moment, the accepted election result — have thinned out.

    Compared with 1970, then, the country at mid-2026 is richer and more capable but more ill-at-ease; it has won most of the concrete arguments of the last half-century and still feels as though it is losing. The distress of 1970 was acute and, as the 1980s and 1990s proved, curable — the system repaired itself and confidence returned. The unease of 2026 is different in kind: not a sharp crisis against a backdrop of underlying trust, but a chronic erosion of the trust itself, coexisting with material comfort. The overriding lesson of the fifty-seven years is that economic success does not automatically renew a nation's faith in its own capacity to govern itself. The America of mid-2026 has the prosperity the America of 1970 lacked, and lacks the coherence the America of 1970 was then losing. Whether the governance and mood arcs can recover as they did after the 1970s, or whether their long decline is a more permanent settlement, is the question the dataset leaves unresolved — and the one on which the next chapter of the American story will turn.

    The archive: every year in full

    All 57 annual reports — executive summary, scorecard, research domains, timeline, and verdict. Or use the time machine and open a report from there.

    1970
    Econ -1Mark +1Poli -1Cult +1Over -1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    Nineteen-seventy felt, to Americans living through it, like the year the 1960s finally cracked. Cambodia and Kent State exploded the campuses in May; the Hard Hat Riot pitted construction workers against antiwar marchers on Wall Street four days later; a wildcat postal strike put National Guardsmen behind the sorting tables in Manhattan; the Beatles broke up; and Jimi Hendrix and Janis Joplin each died at twenty-seven, weeks apart. Time named 'The Middle Americans' its Man of the Year to capture a mood of resentful, embattled traditionalism, and consumer confidence sank to the worst readings the University of Michigan survey had ever recorded.

    Beneath the noise, the year quietly built the machinery that would run the next fifty years. Nixon and a Democratic Congress produced the densest burst of progressive federal legislation since the Great Society — NEPA taking effect January 1, the Clean Air Act Amendments signed December 31, the EPA opening its doors December 2, OSHA, RICO, the Controlled Substances Act, the Postal Reorganization Act, the Fair Credit Reporting Act, the Bank Secrecy Act, and Amtrak. Milton Friedman published the shareholder-primacy essay that would rewrite American capitalism, Nixon opened a back channel to Beijing through Yahya Khan, Intel prototyped the microprocessor, Bell Labs ported Unix to the PDP-11, and the Boeing 747 entered commercial service. The contemporaries watched Vietnam; the stagehands were changing the world.

    Economically it was the first taste of stagflation — a mild recession with unemployment climbing from 3.5 to 6.1 percent while CPI still ran near 5.6 percent, Arthur Burns replacing William McChesney Martin at the Fed, Penn Central going bankrupt in the largest corporate collapse to that point, a 67-day GM strike, and a bear market that bottomed in May before staging a powerful year-end rally. Cultural life was extraordinarily fertile: Bridge Over Troubled Water, All Things Must Pass, Sweet Baby James, After the Gold Rush, Paranoid, Bitches Brew, Midnight Cowboy taking Best Picture, Love Story dominating the box office, Mary Tyler Moore reshaping the sitcom, Monday Night Football debuting, Willis Reed limping, and Bobby Orr flying. It was, on balance, a year that felt worse than it was and mattered more than anyone at the time realized.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy-1A genuine if shallow recession with unemployment nearly doubling to 6.1 percent while inflation still ran above five percent — the debut of stagflation, mild only compared to the deeper downturns that would follow later in the decade.
    Markets+1Modestly positive full-year returns in the S&P (+4%) and Dow (+5%) mask a -36% peak-to-trough bear market that bottomed in May, but a powerful year-end rally, the successful Fed containment of the Penn Central commercial paper panic, and the launch of NASDAQ made 1970 a better market year than it felt.
    Politics-1Kent State, Jackson State, the Cambodian incursion, a rejected Supreme Court nominee, the largest federal-worker wildcat strike in history, and the Hard Hat Riot outweighed unusual legislative productivity and orderly midterms, leaving the year meaningfully below the era's already-turbulent norm without reaching 1968's or 1973-74's constitutional-crisis level.
    Culture+1Despite Kent State, the Beatles' breakup, and the deaths of Hendrix and Joplin, 1970 produced landmark albums across every emerging genre, birthed modern environmentalism, feminism's mass phase, and gay pride, launched Monday Night Football and The Mary Tyler Moore Show, and delivered unforgettable sports moments — creatively vital against a traumatic backdrop.
    Overall Mood-1Americans felt embattled and exhausted — a country coming apart at the seams under Vietnam, stagflation, and campus violence — but the year was also unusually productive institutionally and creatively, keeping the net mood negative but not deeply so.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Nixon Announces US Troops Have Entered Cambodia in Nationally Televised Address
    2. Four Kent State Students Killed by Ohio National Guard as Campuses Erupt Nationwide
    3. Apollo 13 Crippled in Space; Astronauts Improvise Their Way Home Over Four Tense Days
    4. Postal Workers Walk Off Job in Largest Wildcat Strike Against Federal Government; Nixon Sends Troops to Sort New York's Mail
    5. Penn Central Files Largest Bankruptcy in US History as Commercial Paper Market Panics
    6. Hard Hats Beat Antiwar Marchers on Wall Street Four Days After Kent State
    7. Beatles Officially Break Up as McCartney Releases Solo Album
    8. Willis Reed Limps onto Court as Knicks Win First NBA Title; Bobby Orr Flies for Bruins' Cup
    9. Senate Rejects Carswell for Supreme Court 51-45; Nixon Turns to Blackmun
    10. Palestinian Guerrillas Blow Up Three Jetliners at Dawson's Field as Jordanian Civil War Erupts

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The United States spent all of 1970 in the mild but consequential 'Nixon recession' that the NBER dates from December 1969 through November 1970. Real GDP eked out roughly +0.2 percent for the calendar year with outright contractions in Q1 and Q4, unemployment nearly doubled from 3.5 percent in December 1969 to 6.1 percent in December 1970, and CPI inflation was still running near 5.6 percent — the debut of the 'stagflation' pattern Paul Samuelson would soon name. Arthur Burns took the Federal Reserve chair on January 31, inheriting the tightening from William McChesney Martin and pivoting aggressively toward easing as the year progressed; the fed funds rate fell from a 1969 peak near 9.2 percent to about 5 percent by year-end. The 67-day GM strike that began September 14 cost General Motors more than a billion dollars, idled 400,000 workers across 145 plants, and lopped roughly a full point off Q4 GDP before ending November 11 with a landmark UAW contract that restored an uncapped cost-of-living allowance and created '30-and-out' retirement — economic historians later cite the settlement as a critical propagator of the wage-price spiral. Consumer sentiment sagged to postwar lows, and Michigan's confidence index registered its weakest readings since the modern series began.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth +0.2%. CPI +5.6% year-over-year at December. Unemployment 6.1% (Dec, up from 3.5%). Fed funds rate ~5.0% at year-end (down from 9.2% peak). Long Aaa corporate yields hit ~8.5% in June, an eight-decade high, before rallying. 30-year mortgage rate averaged ~8.5%. Housing starts fell to ~1.43 million, weakest since 1966. Median existing-home price about $23,000-$23,400. Personal saving rate elevated at ~8% of disposable income. Gross federal debt ~$389 billion at calendar year-end (~36% of GDP). WTI crude averaged ~$3.35/barrel. Gold official $35/oz; London PM fix ended near $37.44/oz.

    Major Events

    Arthur Burns replaces William McChesney Martin at the Federal Reserve on January 31. The Penn Central Transportation Company files for bankruptcy on June 21 at $4.6 billion in assets — the largest corporate collapse in US history to that point — triggering a run on the commercial paper market that Burns's Fed contains by suspending Regulation Q ceilings on large CDs and aggressively opening the discount window, an early template for the modern lender-of-last-resort function. Congress charters Freddie Mac through the Emergency Home Finance Act to broaden the mortgage secondary market. The Economic Stabilization Act of 1970 hands Nixon standby authority to impose wage and price controls, which he opposed but would deploy dramatically in August 1971. The 67-day GM strike (September 14-November 11) delivers a landmark UAW victory. A back-office crisis and bear market force Wall Street consolidation — Hayden Stone, Goodbody, and du Pont Glore Forgan collapse or are absorbed — prompting Congress to create the Securities Investor Protection Corporation in December.

    Why this score

    A shallow but genuine recession that saw unemployment nearly double while inflation still ran above five percent — the first appearance of the stagflation pattern that would define the decade — earns a negative mark, though the downturn was mild compared with 1973-75 or 1980-82 that were yet to come.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    Full-year returns were modestly positive — S&P 500 total return of about +4.0 percent, Dow +4.8 percent closing at 838.92 versus 800.36 at end-1969 — but that headline masks brutal volatility. The 1968-1970 bear market bottomed at the May 26 intraday low with the S&P 500 down roughly 36 percent from its November 1968 peak, the deepest drawdown since 1937-42. The average 'computer stock' fell about 80 percent from its 1968 peak, EDS collapsed from $162 to $24, and conglomerate darlings such as Litton and LTV lost eighty percent or more as the 1960s 'go-go' era decisively ended. Long-term Aaa corporate yields peaked near 8.5 percent in June — an eight-decade high — before a powerful bond rally as Burns's Fed pivoted, and the S&P then rose about 20 percent off its May low into year-end. Gold's Bretton Woods peg held at $35/oz, but European central banks were quietly accumulating dollars they could redeem, foreshadowing the August 1971 Nixon shock.

    Character

    Crash-then-recovery

    Major Companies

    The Penn Central Transportation Company filed the largest bankruptcy in US history to that point on June 21 with $4.6 billion in assets, triggering a run on the commercial paper market that Burns's Fed contained by suspending Regulation Q ceilings and opening the discount window. General Motors weathered a 67-day strike (September 14-November 11) that cost it more than $1 billion and produced a landmark UAW contract. IBM shipped the System/370 mainframe in June. Xerox opened PARC in Palo Alto in July, seeding personal computing. Pan Am inaugurated Boeing 747 service on January 22. On Wall Street the 'back-office crisis' combined with the bear market to force the collapse or forced merger of Hayden Stone, Goodbody & Co., and du Pont Glore Forgan, prompting Congress to create the Securities Investor Protection Corporation in December. NASDAQ launched on February 8 as the world's first fully electronic stock market.

    Investment Themes

    The end of the 1960s conglomerate and computer-stock mania was total; investors who had ridden the go-go names lost most of their money and rotated toward what would soon be called the 'Nifty Fifty.' The Penn Central failure permanently changed how corporations financed short-term operations and how the Fed backstops short-term credit. Bond investors caught a monster rally in the second half as the Fed pivoted from tightening to easing. Gold speculation and mounting European redemption pressure telegraphed the coming collapse of Bretton Woods. And the launch of NASDAQ and the creation of SIPC quietly built the plumbing of the modern American equity market.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The year's political spine was drawn on April 30, when Nixon announced on national television that US and South Vietnamese forces had crossed into Cambodia. Within days some 450 to 900 colleges were on strike; on May 4 Ohio National Guardsmen killed four students at Kent State; on May 14-15 police killed two more at Jackson State. On May 8 several hundred New York construction workers attacked antiwar marchers in the Wall Street Hard Hat Riot. In March, more than 210,000 postal employees walked off the job in the largest wildcat strike against the federal government in US history, forcing Nixon to send in the National Guard and setting up the Postal Reorganization Act. The Senate rejected Nixon's second consecutive Supreme Court nominee, G. Harrold Carswell, 51-45 on April 8; Harry Blackmun was confirmed 94-0 on May 12. On June 24 the Senate voted 81-10 to repeal the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, and on June 30 it passed the Cooper-Church Amendment barring ground troops in Cambodia — the first Vietnam-era congressional check on the war power. Interior Secretary Walter Hickel was fired November 25 after his May letter criticizing the administration's tone toward youth was leaked. Nixon closed the year on December 31 signing the Clean Air Act Amendments.

    Domestic Policy

    One of the most legislatively productive years in postwar American history, achieved by a Republican president and a Democratic Congress. Environmental policy was the signature theme: the National Environmental Policy Act took effect January 1, the first Earth Day drew twenty million participants on April 22, the EPA opened December 2 under William Ruckelshaus, and Nixon signed the Clean Air Act Amendments requiring a 90 percent reduction in tailpipe emissions on December 31. Nixon signed OSHA on December 29 creating both OSHA and NIOSH; the Postal Reorganization Act on August 12 replaced the cabinet Post Office Department with the USPS; the Rail Passenger Service Act on October 30 created Amtrak. On crime and drugs he signed the Organized Crime Control Act (October 15, containing RICO), the Comprehensive Drug Abuse Prevention and Control Act (October 27, containing the Controlled Substances Act), and the DC Court Reform and Criminal Procedure Act with its controversial 'no-knock' warrants and preventive detention. The Voting Rights Act Amendments of 1970 extended the Act, banned literacy tests nationally, and lowered the voting age to 18, teed up by Oregon v. Mitchell for the Twenty-sixth Amendment. Two major initiatives failed: the Moynihan-designed Family Assistance Plan passed the House 243-155 in April but died in Senate Finance in November, and a national health insurance push went nowhere.

    Foreign Policy

    Vietnam remained the axis around which everything spun. Nixon's Vietnamization strategy dropped US troop strength from about 475,000 to roughly 335,000, but his April 29 order sending US combat forces into Cambodia's Fishhook and Parrot's Beak regions detonated the home front; ground forces were withdrawn by June 30 under the president's own commitment and the pending Cooper-Church restrictions. My Lai continued to reverberate as the Army charged fourteen officers on March 17 with suppressing information about the massacre, and Lt. William Calley's court-martial opened at Fort Benning in November. Détente took its first concrete steps as SALT I opened in Vienna on April 16, though August U-2 imagery of Soviet construction of a nuclear submarine base at Cienfuegos, Cuba required quiet Kissinger back-channel diplomacy to defuse. The Jordanian Crisis of September — Palestinian Dawson's Field hijackings, King Hussein's crackdown, and Syrian tanks crossing the border — was the year's most acute international emergency; Nixon coordinated with Israel and moved the Sixth Fleet, and the Syrians withdrew. In Latin America, Salvador Allende's September 4 plurality in Chile prompted Nixon's September 15 authorization of a 'Track II' covert operation and his order to CIA Director Helms to 'make the economy scream'; the coup plot led to the October 22 assassination of Chilean Army Chief Rene Schneider, but Allende was confirmed October 24. On October 25 Nixon delivered the after-dinner remark to Pakistani President Yahya Khan that became the operational beginning of the opening to China. Kissinger dominated the Nixon-Rogers foreign policy triangle from the White House basement.

    Important Elections

    The November 3 midterms were pitched by Nixon and Agnew as a referendum on order versus chaos, and the results were mixed. Republicans netted two Senate seats but lost twelve House seats and eleven governorships. In the Senate, Democrats lost incumbents Albert Gore Sr. in Tennessee (to Bill Brock, a portent of Southern realignment) and Joseph Tydings in Maryland, and gave up open seats in Ohio and Connecticut, where Democrat Thomas Dodd's independent bid split the vote. In New York, Conservative Party candidate James L. Buckley won a three-way race with 38.8 percent — the last time a third-party nominee has won a Senate seat. In Texas, Lloyd Bentsen defeated Republican Rep. George H. W. Bush 53-47. In California, John Tunney unseated Republican George Murphy. Virginia's Harry F. Byrd Jr. won as an independent after leaving the Democratic Party. Among governors, Jimmy Carter won Georgia, Reubin Askew won Florida, Dale Bumpers upset Winthrop Rockefeller in Arkansas, John Gilligan won Ohio, Nelson Rockefeller took a fourth term in New York over Arthur Goldberg, and Ronald Reagan was re-elected in California over Jesse Unruh by roughly 500,000 votes, cementing his national conservative standing. Turnout was a modest 44 percent.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    The April 1970 Oscars gave Best Picture to Midnight Cowboy — the only X-rated film ever to win — while the theaters filled with the year's defining releases: Love Story became the top box-office hit at over $106 million on the tagline 'love means never having to say you're sorry'; Robert Altman's M*A*S*H won the Palme d'Or with its antiwar surgical satire; Patton opened with the speech Nixon reportedly watched before ordering the Cambodian incursion; and Five Easy Pieces gave Jack Nicholson the diner scene that entered the vernacular. Airport launched the disaster-film genre, the Woodstock documentary preserved the counterculture's last mass communion, and Little Big Man and Catch-22 continued the New Hollywood assault on the studio system. Attendance kept sliding against television, but a young generation of directors was rewriting what a mainstream American film could be.

    Music

    Simon & Garfunkel's Bridge Over Troubled Water dominated the year, holding the Billboard 200's top spot for ten weeks and later sweeping the Grammys. It was also a year of astonishing genre-defining releases: George Harrison's All Things Must Pass produced the first solo Beatle number one; James Taylor's Sweet Baby James inaugurated the singer-songwriter movement; Neil Young's After the Gold Rush, Joni Mitchell's Ladies of the Canyon, Van Morrison's Moondance, and the Grateful Dead's Workingman's Dead and American Beauty crystallized folk-rock and country-rock; Black Sabbath's debut and Paranoid invented heavy metal; Led Zeppelin III, The Who's Live at Leeds, and Creedence's Cosmo's Factory anchored hard rock; and Miles Davis's Bitches Brew invented jazz fusion. The Jackson 5 stacked four number-one singles, the Carpenters and Diana Ross ruled AM radio, Edwin Starr's 'War' captured the mood, and Harrison's 'My Sweet Lord' closed the year at the top. Then Hendrix (September 18) and Joplin (October 4) died at twenty-seven within weeks of each other, framing the whole year in premature elegy.

    Television

    Nineteen-seventy was the year of the CBS 'rural purge,' with Green Acres, Petticoat Junction, The Beverly Hillbillies, Hee Haw, and Mayberry R.F.D. all shown the door as networks chased younger, urban demographics. Their September replacements — The Mary Tyler Moore Show (with Mary as an unmarried career woman), The Odd Couple, and The Partridge Family — reshaped the sitcom, and All in the Family was already being prepared for its January 1971 debut. Marcus Welby, M.D. topped the 1969-70 season and The Flip Wilson Show topped the 1970-71 season, the first variety hit built around a Black entertainer since Nat King Cole. PBS launched October 5 to knit the public stations together, Sesame Street reshaped children's television in its first full year, and Monday Night Football debuted September 21 on ABC with Howard Cosell, Keith Jackson, and Don Meredith, permanently converting professional football into prime-time spectacle.

    Technology

    Color television finally overtook black-and-white in American households, the pocket calculator arrived (Canon's Pocketronic on the consumer side; the Busicom project that would produce Intel's first microprocessors in laboratories), IBM shipped the System/370 mainframe in June, and DEC's PDP-11 became the decade's workhorse minicomputer. Xerox opened its Palo Alto Research Center in July, seeding what would become personal computing; the eight-inch floppy disk arrived; ARPANET grew to thirteen nodes; and at Bell Labs, Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie were quietly porting Unix to the PDP-11, laying substrate for the internet, mobile phones, and cloud computing decades later. For consumers the year's most visible technological leap was Pan Am's January 22 New York-to-London inaugural of the Boeing 747, the jumbo jet that would democratize international travel.

    Social Trends

    Second-wave feminism went mainstream: Kate Millett's Sexual Politics, Robin Morgan's Sisterhood Is Powerful anthology, and Shulamith Firestone's The Dialectic of Sex all appeared, and on August 26 Betty Friedan's Women's Strike for Equality filled Fifth Avenue on the fiftieth anniversary of the Nineteenth Amendment. The first Pride marches stepped off on June 28 in New York, Los Angeles, and Chicago, marking the Stonewall anniversary and launching gay liberation as a public movement. Environmentalism became a mass identity around the first Earth Day on April 22, which drew roughly twenty million participants. The Jesus movement was cresting in Southern California, the Sun Belt was pulling population from the industrial Northeast and Midwest, the median American age was about twenty-eight, and the baby-boom cohort was hitting college and draft age simultaneously — the demographic engine behind the year's convulsions.

    Crime

    Violent crime was rising sharply and would keep rising through the decade. The Charles Manson trial opened June 15 and ran through the year, fixing an image of the counterculture curdled into horror; the Weather Underground's March 6 townhouse bomb killed three of its own on West 11th Street and drove the group into fugitive terrorism; the August 24 bombing of Sterling Hall at the University of Wisconsin killed researcher Robert Fassnacht; and Jonathan Jackson's August 7 raid on the Marin County courthouse to free the Soledad Brothers left a judge and three others dead, later putting Angela Davis on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list. The National Bomb Data Center recorded roughly three thousand explosive and incendiary attacks in the fifteen months straddling 1970-71 — a wave of political bombings that has since been largely erased from popular memory.

    Sports

    It was one of the great years for iconic moments. On May 8 Willis Reed limped onto Madison Square Garden's floor for Game 7 of the NBA Finals and the Knicks beat the Lakers for their first title, Walt Frazier posting 36 points and 19 assists. Two days later Bobby Orr scored his flying overtime goal to sweep the Bruins to the Stanley Cup, producing the most famous photograph in hockey. Baltimore's Orioles beat Cincinnati in the World Series behind Brooks Robinson's third-base clinic; Kansas City won Super Bowl IV before the AFL-NFL merger took effect that fall, giving America the AFC-NFC alignment and, on September 21, the debut of Monday Night Football. Muhammad Ali returned from three and a half years of exile on October 26 by knocking out Jerry Quarry in Atlanta, setting up the March 1971 'Fight of the Century' with Frazier. Nebraska and Texas shared the college football championship, Pete Maravich went to the Hawks, Curt Flood's antitrust challenge to baseball's reserve clause was underway, and Vince Lombardi died September 3.

    Public Mood

    Embattled and exhausted. Nixon's 'Silent Majority' framing produced a hard-hats-versus-hippies polarization dramatized when construction workers attacked antiwar protesters in the Wall Street Hard Hat Riot on May 8, four days after Kent State. Inflation near 5.7 percent combined with unemployment climbing to 6.1 percent by year-end gave Americans their first taste of stagflation, and the Penn Central bankruptcy in June — the largest corporate collapse in US history to that point — shook faith in the industrial Northeast. Time named 'The Middle Americans' its Man of the Year, articulating a resentful, embattled traditionalism that would fuel Nixon's 1972 landslide. And yet the same year birthed modern environmentalism, launched mass-phase feminism and gay pride, delivered Willis Reed's limp and Bobby Orr's flight, and produced landmark albums in every emerging genre — culturally fertile even as it was politically bleak.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Americans in 1970 believed the year's central drama was the Vietnam War's metastasis into Cambodia, the resulting collapse of the campuses (over 450 colleges struck after Kent State), and the associated question of whether the country was becoming ungovernable. Runners-up in the contemporary imagination were Apollo 13's near-loss and dramatic return, the Beatles' breakup, Nasser's sudden death, the Manson trial, the midterm test of the Silent Majority, and the mounting evidence that the industrial Northeast — symbolized by the Penn Central bankruptcy — was in structural trouble. The dominant emotional register was institutional legitimacy under strain.

    What actually mattered

    In hindsight the year's decisive work was almost the inverse of its headlines. The Vietnam War would end in 1973 for the United States and 1975 in Saigon regardless of what happened in Cambodia; 1970 accelerated but did not determine that outcome. What actually mattered were the quieter developments: the environmental statutory revolution (NEPA taking effect, EPA created, Clean Air Act Amendments signed December 31), OSHA, the Controlled Substances Act creating the architecture of the coming War on Drugs, RICO, Milton Friedman's September 13 essay founding shareholder-primacy capitalism, Nixon's October 25 back-channel message to Beijing through Yahya Khan that opened the door to China, Intel prototyping the microprocessor, Bell Labs porting Unix to the PDP-11, the Boeing 747 democratizing international travel, and the Hard Hat Riot foreshadowing the blue-collar realignment that would remake American politics. Bruce Schulman and Jefferson Cowie's 'long 1970s' scholarship treats 1970 as the pivot on which postwar liberalism turned into something else.

    Most forgotten story

    The Great Postal Strike of March 18-25, when more than 210,000 letter carriers and clerks walked off the job in the largest wildcat strike against the federal government in American history. Nixon federalized New York City's post offices and sent roughly 25,000 troops — including active-duty combat infantry — to sort mail in Manhattan. It was the lead national story for a full week, forced passage of the Postal Reorganization Act that dissolved the cabinet-level Post Office Department and created today's USPS, and permanently altered federal-employee collective bargaining. It has almost entirely fallen out of popular memory, crowded aside by Cambodia and Kent State six weeks later.

    Most underrated story

    Nixon signing NEPA into law on January 1, 1970, followed by the EPA opening its doors on December 2 and the Clean Air Act Amendments signed December 31. Contemporary editorials treated the package as good-government housekeeping riding the wave of Earth Day sentiment. In hindsight it is arguably the single most consequential domestic policy achievement of the Nixon administration and one of the most consequential of the twentieth century — the legal, administrative, and scientific infrastructure that produced measurable declines in lead, particulates, sulfur dioxide, and ambient ozone over the next half-century, added an estimated year or more to average American life expectancy, and became the template regulators from Brussels to Beijing later copied.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    That the same Nixon administration that ordered troops into Cambodia, presided over Kent State, and campaigned on 'law and order' also produced the single densest burst of progressive federal legislation since the Great Society — EPA, Clean Air Act, OSHA, the Fair Credit Reporting Act, the Voting Rights Act extension that lowered the voting age, and even a serious guaranteed-income proposal in the (defeated) Family Assistance Plan. No president since has signed as much market-restraining legislation in a single year. Read in 1970 as tactical triangulation by a besieged conservative, it now looks like the high-water mark of American statist liberalism, achieved under a Republican.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    Apollo 13 near-loss and rescue (April 11-17)The most-watched non-Vietnam news event of the year, but a 'successful failure' with no scientific results and no lasting policy consequences beyond accelerating the wind-down of Apollo; its meaning was emotional rather than structural.
    The Chicago Seven verdict (February 18) and the long Charles Manson trial (opened June 15)Television-and-tabloid obsessions for months; the Chicago Seven convictions were reversed on appeal in 1972 and Manson receded into gothic pop-culture curiosity rather than a turning point in criminal justice.
    The Beatles' breakup (announced April 10)A dominant cultural headline whose impact was almost entirely nostalgic — the band's cultural centrality persisted whether or not they were together, and the solo careers immediately absorbed the same audience.
    The 1970 midterm elections (November 3)Nixon and Agnew campaigned ferociously on the Silent Majority believing they were engineering a realignment; the modest Senate gain and House losses read as a repudiation, and historians now view 1970 as a way-station toward a realignment actually decided in 1972 and 1980.
    Gamal Abdel Nasser's death (September 28)Briefly the biggest international story of the year, but Anwar Sadat quietly reversed Nasserism, expelled Soviet advisers, and made peace with Israel, closing rather than extending the Nasserite era.
    The tactical hunt for COSVN in the Cambodian incursionDaily order-of-battle reporting focused on destroying an enemy headquarters that turned out to be a phantom target; what actually mattered was the political detonation at home and the destabilization of Cambodia.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Milton Friedman's essay 'The Social Responsibility of Business Is to Increase Its Profits' in the New York Times Magazine (September 13)Buried in the Sunday magazine and drawing modest attention at the time, it became the intellectual charter of shareholder-primacy capitalism cited constantly from the 1980s onward as the founding text of a corporate governance revolution that reshaped executive pay, hostile takeovers, and downsizing.
    Nixon's back-channel message to China via Pakistani President Yahya Khan (October 25)A private after-dinner remark — 'It is essential that we open negotiations with China' — relayed weeks later to Zhou Enlai was invisible to contemporaries but was the operational beginning of the opening to China that reshaped the Cold War and the global economy.
    Intel's microprocessor development work on the 4004Covered, if at all, as trade-press curiosity; within a decade the microprocessor rewrote every industry.
    Bell Labs' port of Unix to the PDP-11An internal Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie project that became, via Berkeley Unix and eventually Linux, the substrate of the internet, mobile phones, and cloud computing.
    The Controlled Substances Act (signed October 27)Reported as routine drug-scheduling legislation, it created the statutory architecture — Schedule I, DEA precursor authorities, no-knock warrants — for the fifty-year War on Drugs formally declared by Nixon in 1971.
    The Weather Underground townhouse explosion at 18 West 11th Street (March 6)Covered as a local New York curiosity, it marked the moment the New Left's revolutionary wing collapsed into fugitive terrorism and lost its mass base — the practical end of SDS-descended revolutionary politics as a serious force.

    Representative Headlines

    1. U.S. TROOPS MOVE INTO CAMBODIA; NIXON TAKES TV TO NATION
    2. 4 KENT STATE STUDENTS KILLED BY TROOPS
    3. APOLLO 13 CRIPPLED IN SPACE; 'HOUSTON, WE'VE HAD A PROBLEM' AS ASTRONAUTS RACE HOME IN LUNAR MODULE
    4. POSTAL WORKERS DEFY LAW; NIXON SENDS TROOPS TO SORT NEW YORK'S MAIL
    5. HARD-HATS BEAT ANTI-WAR MARCHERS ON WALL STREET; LINDSAY ASSAILS POLICE INACTION
    6. BOEING'S 747 BEGINS REGULAR SERVICE ON PAN AM'S NEW YORK-LONDON RUN
    7. PENN CENTRAL FILES LARGEST BANKRUPTCY IN U.S. HISTORY
    8. PALESTINIAN GUERRILLAS BLOW UP THREE JETLINERS IN JORDANIAN DESERT AS CIVIL WAR ERUPTS
    9. SENATE REJECTS CARSWELL FOR SUPREME COURT, 51-45
    10. NIXON SIGNS CLEAN AIR ACT; NEW E.P.A. TO ENFORCE TOUGHEST POLLUTION RULES YET

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe National Environmental Policy Act took effect January 1, quietly launching the environmental statutory revolution. Arthur Burns was sworn in as Federal Reserve chairman on January 31, replacing William McChesney Martin and inheriting a recessionary economy with inflation still running near 6 percent. Pan Am's Boeing 747 made its inaugural New York-to-London commercial flight on January 22, dawning the age of mass international travel. Kansas City beat Minnesota in Super Bowl IV on January 11 for the AFL's second straight title before the AFL-NFL merger took effect that fall. Simon & Garfunkel's Bridge Over Troubled Water was released January 26 and would dominate the charts for the year. Southern school districts began desegregating en masse under the Alexander v. Holmes County mandate.
    FebruaryNASDAQ launched on February 8 as the world's first fully electronic stock exchange, operated by the NASD. The Chicago Seven verdict came down February 18, with five defendants convicted of crossing state lines to incite riot at the 1968 Democratic Convention. Nixon nominated Fifth Circuit judge G. Harrold Carswell to the Fortas Supreme Court seat after the Senate had rejected Clement Haynsworth the previous fall. James Taylor's Sweet Baby James was released, launching the singer-songwriter movement.
    MarchThree Weather Underground members died March 6 when a nail bomb they were assembling detonated in a Greenwich Village townhouse, driving the group underground. The Army charged fourteen officers on March 17 with suppressing information about the My Lai massacre. Beginning March 18, over 210,000 postal workers walked off the job in the largest wildcat strike against the federal government in US history; Nixon declared a national emergency and deployed about 25,000 troops, including active-duty infantry, to sort mail in New York City. The strike ended March 25 with concessions that would produce the Postal Reorganization Act.
    AprilThe Senate rejected G. Harrold Carswell for the Supreme Court 51-45 on April 8, prompting Roman Hruska's infamous defense that 'mediocre' people also deserve representation. Paul McCartney announced his departure from the Beatles on April 10, effectively dissolving the band. The Apollo 13 mission ruptured its service module on April 13 and the crew improvised its way home over four tense days. The first Earth Day on April 22 drew roughly twenty million participants across the country. SALT I negotiations opened in Vienna on April 16. On April 30 Nixon announced on national television that US and South Vietnamese forces had entered Cambodia, blindsiding Congress and detonating the campuses.
    MayOn May 4 Ohio National Guardsmen fired into a crowd at Kent State University, killing four students and wounding nine. Within days, 450 to 900 colleges and universities were on strike involving over four million students, the largest student action in US history. On May 8 New York construction workers attacked antiwar demonstrators in the Wall Street Hard Hat Riot. On May 12 the Senate confirmed Harry Blackmun to the Supreme Court 94-0. On May 14-15, police fired into a Jackson State dormitory in Mississippi, killing two students. On May 8 Willis Reed limped onto Madison Square Garden's court and the Knicks beat the Lakers for their first NBA title; on May 10 Bobby Orr scored his flying overtime goal to complete the Bruins' sweep of the Stanley Cup. The S&P 500 bottomed at its bear-market intraday low on May 26, down roughly 36 percent from its 1968 peak.
    JuneThe Charles Manson trial opened June 15. Long Aaa corporate yields hit an eight-decade high of about 8.5 percent as the bond market peaked. On June 21 the Penn Central Transportation Company filed the largest bankruptcy in US history at $4.6 billion in assets, triggering a run on the commercial paper market that Burns's Fed contained by suspending Regulation Q ceilings and aggressively opening the discount window. On June 22 Nixon signed the Voting Rights Act Amendments, extending the Act, banning literacy tests, and lowering the voting age to 18. On June 24 the Senate voted 81-10 to repeal the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution; on June 28 the first Pride marches stepped off in New York, Los Angeles, and Chicago; on June 30 US ground forces withdrew from Cambodia under Nixon's commitment and the pending Cooper-Church Amendment.
    JulyXerox opened its Palo Alto Research Center (PARC) in July, seeding the personal computing revolution. Cesar Chavez's UFW won the grape boycott after five years. Federal environmental reorganization advanced as Reorganization Plan No. 3 moved toward creating the EPA. The Middle East cease-fire negotiations continued under Secretary Rogers's initiative. Wall Street's back-office crisis deepened as several major brokerages moved toward collapse or forced merger.
    AugustThe Sterling Hall bombing at the University of Wisconsin on August 24 killed researcher Robert Fassnacht in a protest against Army Math Research Center work. Angela Davis was placed on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list after guns registered to her were used in the August 7 Marin County courthouse shootout that killed a judge and three others. The 90-day cease-fire in the Suez Canal War of Attrition took effect August 7. Betty Friedan's Women's Strike for Equality filled Fifth Avenue on August 26, the fiftieth anniversary of the Nineteenth Amendment. U-2 imagery revealed Soviet construction of a submarine base at Cienfuegos, Cuba, prompting quiet Kissinger back-channel diplomacy. Nixon signed the Postal Reorganization Act on August 12.
    SeptemberThe 67-day General Motors strike began September 14, ultimately idling 400,000 workers across 145 plants. Milton Friedman's shareholder-primacy essay ran in the New York Times Magazine on September 13. Vince Lombardi died September 3. The Jordanian Crisis exploded as PFLP hijackers blew up three jetliners at Dawson's Field, King Hussein moved against Palestinian factions, and Syrian tanks crossed the border on September 18-19; Nixon and Kissinger coordinated with Israel and repositioned the Sixth Fleet, and the Syrians withdrew. Jimi Hendrix died September 18 at 27. Salvador Allende won a plurality in Chile's September 4 presidential election, and on September 15 Nixon authorized Track II covert action with instructions to CIA Director Helms to 'make the economy scream.' Monday Night Football debuted on ABC on September 21. Nasser died September 28.
    OctoberJanis Joplin died October 4 at 27. PBS launched October 5 to knit the public stations together. On October 7 Nixon proposed a 'five-point peace plan' featuring a cease-fire in place. The Rail Passenger Service Act creating Amtrak was signed October 30. Chilean Army Chief Rene Schneider was assassinated October 22 as part of Track II; Allende was confirmed by the Chilean Congress on October 24. On October 25 Nixon delivered the after-dinner remark to Pakistani President Yahya Khan that opened the back channel to China. Nixon signed the Organized Crime Control Act with RICO on October 15 and the Controlled Substances Act on October 27. Muhammad Ali knocked out Jerry Quarry in Atlanta on October 26, ending his three-and-a-half-year exile from boxing.
    NovemberThe November 3 midterms delivered a mixed verdict: Republicans netted two Senate seats but lost twelve House seats and eleven governorships. James L. Buckley won a New York Senate seat as the Conservative Party nominee; Jimmy Carter won Georgia's governorship; Ronald Reagan was re-elected in California. The GM strike ended November 11 with a landmark UAW contract restoring uncapped COLA and creating '30-and-out' retirement. Lt. William Calley's court-martial for My Lai began at Fort Benning. The Family Assistance Plan died in the Senate Finance Committee. Interior Secretary Walter Hickel was fired November 25 after his May letter criticizing the administration's tone toward youth leaked. Baltimore beat Cincinnati in the World Series behind Brooks Robinson.
    DecemberThe EPA officially opened December 2 under William Ruckelshaus. Congress created the Securities Investor Protection Corporation in December to insure customer accounts against the wave of brokerage failures. In Oregon v. Mitchell (December 21) the Supreme Court upheld the age-18 franchise for federal elections but struck it down for state and local elections, teeing up the Twenty-sixth Amendment. Nixon signed OSHA on December 29 and the Clean Air Act Amendments on December 31. The Fed cut the discount rate to 5.25 percent, capping the year's pivot to easing. George Harrison's All Things Must Pass and 'My Sweet Lord' dominated the year-end charts.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+0.2% (real; roughly flat, with Q1 and Q4 contractions)
    Inflation5.6% (CPI, year ended December 1970)
    Unemployment6.1% (December, seasonally adjusted; up from 3.5% a year earlier)
    S&P 500 return+4.0% total return (price return roughly 0.1%; dividend yield about 3.8%)
    Dow return+4.8% total return (closed at 838.92 vs. 800.36 at end-1969)
    Fed funds rate~4.9-5.0% at year-end (down from ~9.2% peak in Aug 1969); discount rate stepped down to 5.25%
    Oil price~$3.35/barrel (WTI/US posted, average for the year)
    Gold price$35/oz official Bretton Woods; London PM fix ~$37.44/oz at year-end
    Population203.2 million (1970 Census)
    Median home price~$23,000-$23,400 (existing single-family; new-home median ~$23,400)
    National debt~$389 billion gross federal debt at calendar year-end (~36% of GDP); publicly held debt ~$283 billion

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 1970 was to feel the ground shifting under every institution you had been taught to trust. The president who had run on winding down the war widened it into Cambodia on April 30, and four days later Ohio National Guardsmen shot four students dead at Kent State — an image that would move onto the covers of every major magazine and shut down some 450 to 900 college campuses in the largest student strike in American history. Ten days after that, police killed two more students at Jackson State in Mississippi, an atrocity that received a fraction of the coverage and became the year's quiet indictment. Construction workers beat antiwar marchers in downtown Manhattan on May 8, and the Wall Street 'hard hat' became the mascot of the Silent Majority that Nixon and Agnew were courting into a coalition that would, two years later, deliver a landslide.

    It was also the first year Americans lived with what would soon be called stagflation. Unemployment nearly doubled to 6.1 percent while prices kept rising near 5.6 percent, Penn Central went bankrupt in the largest corporate collapse anyone had ever seen, and a 67-day strike at General Motors idled 400,000 workers. Consumer confidence sank to the worst readings the Michigan survey had ever measured. The Beatles broke up in April, Jimi Hendrix died in September and Janis Joplin in October, both at twenty-seven, and a jury in California began hearing the Manson case as if to confirm every parental suspicion about where the 1960s had gone.

    And yet the same country walked out for the first Earth Day, filled Fifth Avenue for the Women's Strike for Equality, held its first Pride marches, watched Willis Reed limp onto the court and Bobby Orr fly through the air, and turned on Monday Night Football and Sesame Street and The Mary Tyler Moore Show for the first time. It signed the Clean Air Act, opened the EPA, created OSHA, launched Amtrak, and — without noticing — laid the intellectual and back-channel foundations of shareholder capitalism, the opening to China, the microprocessor, Unix, and the modern War on Drugs. It was a bruising, exhausted, culturally fertile year in which the visible drama was the Vietnam War's metastasis and the campuses' collapse, but the historically decisive work was being done quietly, in courtrooms and cabinet meetings and Bell Labs cubicles and a Sunday New York Times Magazine essay. Americans in 1970 mostly felt they were watching an empire come apart. In hindsight they were living through the founding year of the world we still inhabit.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    1971
    Econ +1Mark +2Poli +1Cult +2Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    1971 was the year the postwar American order quietly came apart in three places at once. On a Sunday evening in August, from Camp David, Richard Nixon suspended the dollar's convertibility into gold, ended the Bretton Woods system that had governed global finance since 1944, froze all wages and prices for ninety days, and imposed a 10% surcharge on imports. In July he announced from a television studio that Henry Kissinger had secretly flown to Beijing, reversing more than two decades of Sino-American isolation. And in November, in a small trade advertisement in Electronic News, Intel introduced the 4004 microprocessor — a four-bit CPU on a single chip that no mainstream newspaper covered but that would seed the personal-computing, mobile, and networked age.

    Contemporaries did not see it that way. The front pages of 1971 were dominated by Vietnam and its reckonings — the Pentagon Papers, Lieutenant Calley's My Lai conviction, Lam Son 719, Dewey Canyon III, the May Day mass arrests — and by domestic tragedies like the Attica uprising in September, where 43 died when Governor Rockefeller ordered the prison retaken. Ali lost to Frazier at the Garden. Manson was sentenced to death. D.B. Cooper jumped out of a 727 with $200,000 in cash. Nixon declared drug abuse public enemy number one, launching a policy framework that would shape criminal justice for half a century. The 26th Amendment lowered the voting age to 18 in the fastest ratification in constitutional history.

    Amid institutional decay came an astonishing creative peak. Tapestry, What's Going On, Imagine, Blue, Led Zeppelin IV, Who's Next, and Sticky Fingers all arrived in a single year. All in the Family debuted, tearing open what television could depict. The French Connection, Dirty Harry, A Clockwork Orange, and The Last Picture Show anchored the New Hollywood. The women's movement, the environmental movement, and the gay-rights movement all consolidated as durable political forces. The mood was sour, exhausted, and cynical — but the year produced more enduring American culture, and more consequential quiet inventions, than almost any other in the postwar era.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1GDP reaccelerated to ~3.3% and headline inflation moderated, but unemployment rose to 5.9%, wage-price controls concealed rather than cured inflation, and Fed independence was compromised under White House pressure.
    Markets+2S&P 500 total return of +14.3%, Nasdaq's launch year up ~14%, a historic one-day Nixon Shock rally, the landmark Intel IPO amid 391 new issues, plus rising bond and gold prices made 1971 an unusually well-rounded year for financial assets.
    Politics+1Judged against the era, 1971 was comparatively productive and stable — major regulatory expansion, the fastest constitutional amendment in history, orderly Supreme Court transitions, and four historic foreign-policy breakthroughs — offset by Attica, the May Day mass arrests, the moral cost of the Bangladesh tilt, and the seeds of Watergate germinating inside the White House.
    Culture+21971 produced Tapestry, What's Going On, Imagine, Blue, Led Zeppelin IV, Who's Next, Sticky Fingers, The French Connection, A Clockwork Orange, The Last Picture Show, Shaft, All in the Family, and the Ali-Frazier fight — a creative peak matched by few years in postwar American history, even as institutional trust cratered.
    Overall Mood+0The domain scores were solidly positive but the country itself felt exhausted, cynical, and betrayed — Pentagon Papers, Attica, Calley, and Vietnam produced a sour public mood that offset a strong scorecard, yielding a synthesis judgment of roughly neutral.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Supreme Court Sides with Times and Post; Pentagon Papers Publication Cleared
    2. Nixon Ends Dollar-Gold Convertibility, Freezes Wages and Prices, Imposes 10% Import Surcharge
    3. Kissinger's Secret Beijing Trip Revealed; Nixon to Visit China
    4. 43 Dead at Attica as State Troopers Retake Prison on Rockefeller's Order
    5. Lt. Calley Convicted of My Lai Murders; Nixon Orders Him to House Arrest
    6. 26th Amendment Ratified in Record 100 Days; 18-Year-Olds Get the Vote
    7. Joe Frazier Defeats Muhammad Ali in Fight of the Century at Madison Square Garden
    8. Nixon Declares Drug Abuse Public Enemy Number One
    9. UN General Assembly Seats Communist China, Expels Taiwan
    10. Walt Disney World Opens in Orlando; Skyjacker D.B. Cooper Bails Out Over Pacific Northwest with $200,000

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy rebounded from the mild 1969-70 recession with real GDP growth of roughly 3.3-3.4%, but the recovery was defined by policy improvisation rather than natural momentum. President Nixon's August 15 announcement from Camp David — the Nixon Shock — suspended dollar-gold convertibility, imposed a ninety-day wage-price freeze, and slapped a 10% surcharge on dutiable imports, ending the Bretton Woods system in a single televised address. When the freeze expired in November, it was replaced by the Phase II mandatory guideline system administered by the Cost of Living Council. The December Smithsonian Agreement devalued the dollar against gold from $35 to $38 an ounce and revalued major currencies upward. Fed Chair Arthur Burns eased aggressively throughout the year, cutting the discount rate from 5.5% to 4.5%, in what would later be revealed as direct response to White House political pressure. Congress narrowly passed the Lockheed Loan Guarantee, cementing the emerging concept of federal backstop for systemically important firms. Underneath the headline moderation, inflationary pressures were merely suppressed, not resolved.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth ~3.3-3.4%; CPI inflation 4.4% annual average with year-end YoY of ~3.3%; unemployment 5.9% annual average, up a full point from 1970's 4.9%; federal funds rate ranged from ~5.0% to a spring trough near 3.5% to ~4.75% year-end; 10-year Treasury yield fell from ~6.5% to ~5.9%; median new home price ~$25,200; Freddie Mac's 30-year mortgage rate averaged 7.54%; federal deficit ~$23 billion in FY1971; gross federal debt ~$409-425 billion by year-end.

    Major Events

    The Nixon Shock (August 15) — dollar-gold suspension, ninety-day wage-price freeze, 10% import surcharge, and auto excise tax repeal. The Smithsonian Agreement (December 18) devaluing the dollar and revaluing major currencies. Phase II wage-price controls launched November 14. Federal Reserve accommodation under Arthur Burns, with four discount-rate cuts. The Emergency Loan Guarantee Act (August 9) rescuing Lockheed with $250 million after the Rolls-Royce collapse killed the L-1011 engine supply. The Emergency Employment Act (July 12) providing $2.25 billion for public-service jobs. The Tehran and Tripoli OPEC agreements raising posted crude prices.

    Why this score

    Solid recovery from the 1969-70 recession — GDP reaccelerated, headline inflation moderated (albeit artificially via controls), and the decisive if fateful end of Bretton Woods was executed with popular support — but unemployment rose to 5.9%, controls concealed rather than cured inflation, and Fed independence was visibly compromised, yielding a moderately positive rather than strong year.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    1971 was a broad, double-digit total-return year across major U.S. financial assets. The S&P 500 returned +14.3% total return (about +10.8% on price), closing the year near 102.09. The Dow Jones Industrial Average delivered a more modest total return of about +6.1%, closing at 890.20 versus 838.92 at the end of 1970 — still failing to hold decisively above the 1,000 barrier it had first flirted with in 1966. The Nasdaq Composite launched at 100 on February 8 and ended the year around 114, up roughly 14% in its abbreviated inaugural year. Long-term Treasury yields fell from about 6.5% at end-1970 to about 5.9% at year-end, giving bondholders positive total returns. Gold's London close rose from $37.33 in early January to about $43.62 on December 31, a 17% gain that marked the beginning of gold's decade-long bull market. Following Nixon's August 15 announcement, the Dow surged 32.93 points on Monday August 16 — the largest one-day point gain in its history to that date.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies
