Apple released the Mac Plus with one megabyte of RAM and SCSI, while Aldus PageMaker and the Apple LaserWriter together invented desktop publishing. Compact-disc sales nearly tripled to roughly 53 million units as players fell below $200. VCR penetration crossed 40 percent and Blockbuster began aggressive expansion. Nintendo's NES had its first full year and Super Mario Bros. was the defining home title. Cellular phones existed mostly as car-mounted novelties. Almost invisibly, the NSFNET backbone came online at 56 kbps, and Steve Jobs bought Lucasfilm's graphics division for about $5 million and renamed it Pixar.

    Social Trends

    The anti-apartheid divestment movement reached its zenith, culminating in Congress's October 2 override of Reagan's veto of the Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act. The AIDS crisis moved decisively into mainstream consciousness with Surgeon General C. Everett Koop's blunt October report calling for explicit sex education and condom use, breaking the administration's silence at roughly 28,000 cumulative US cases and 16,000 deaths. Nancy Reagan's Just Say No campaign, the June cocaine death of basketball star Len Bias, and the Anti-Drug Abuse Act combined to produce one of the century's great moral panics. The Meese Commission on Pornography released its report in July. Labor was quiet by historical standards, the residual effect of Reagan's 1981 PATCO firings; the Hormel strike ended in defeat. Yuppie iconography, BMWs, brie, personal computers, and aerobics, was in full commercial bloom, and the Immigration Reform and Control Act formalized a new Latino America even as Los Angeles tipped toward a Hispanic plurality.

    Crime

    Crack cocaine drove the year's crime narrative. The June 19 overdose death of Len Bias, two days after the Celtics drafted him, catalyzed a media panic that produced the Anti-Drug Abuse Act and its notorious 100-to-1 crack-to-powder sentencing disparity. On August 20 in Edmond, Oklahoma, postal worker Patrick Sherrill killed fourteen coworkers before shooting himself, giving the language the phrase going postal. Bernhard Goetz, the New York subway vigilante, was tried through 1986 for his 1984 shooting of four Black teenagers. On December 20 in Howard Beach, Queens, a Black man named Michael Griffith was killed by a car while fleeing a white mob, provoking marches led by the Rev. Al Sharpton and becoming a defining civil-rights moment for Ed Koch's New York. Violent crime rates continued their late-decade rise driven substantially by urban crack markets, and on Wall Street the November 14 Ivan Boesky settlement opened the insider-trading dragnet.

    Sports

    It was a banner sports year. Super Bowl XX on January 26 saw the Chicago Bears crush New England 46-10 to cap a 15-1 season, with the Super Bowl Shuffle turning Ditka, McMahon, Payton, and William Perry into pop icons. Larry Bird won his third straight MVP as the Celtics beat Houston in the NBA Finals. The Mets took the World Series in seven from the Red Sox, with Bill Buckner's Game 6 error at Shea Stadium becoming one of the most-replayed moments in American sport. Jack Nicklaus won the Masters at 46, the oldest ever. Roger Clemens went 24-4 with a 2.48 ERA and struck out 20 Mariners on April 29. Michael Jordan dropped 63 on the Celtics in an April playoff loss, prompting Bird's God-disguised-as-Michael-Jordan line. On November 22, Mike Tyson knocked out Trevor Berbick to become, at 20, the youngest heavyweight champion in history. There were no Olympics.

    Public Mood

    The mood seesawed violently between spectacle and disillusion. The July Statue of Liberty centennial staged by David Wolper, with tall ships, fireworks, and Reagan's ceremonial laser, marked the confident patriotic peak of Reaganism, the Morning-in-America afterglow of the 1984 landslide. But within the same twelve months Americans mourned Challenger, absorbed the shock of Chernobyl, thrilled to the near-miss at Reykjavik, and then discovered in November that their government had traded arms with Iran and diverted the proceeds to Nicaragua. The economy felt genuinely good to the median household, disinflation had held, unemployment was falling, and the Dow crossed 1,900 in December. For gay America, AIDS gave a mood of gathering emergency; for urban Black America, crack did the same; for the white middle-class suburb the culture centered, life felt prosperous, mildly anxious, and consumerist. By Christmas, the dominant journalistic frame was Where did Reagan go wrong?

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary Americans, asked in late 1986 what defined the year, would have named the Challenger disaster, Chernobyl, Iran-Contra, the Libya bombing, the People Power revolution in the Philippines, Halley's Comet, Hands Across America, the Statue of Liberty centennial, the Mets-Red Sox World Series, and the Reykjavik summit (reported at the time as a failure). Those were the images that dominated network news, magazine covers, and dinner-table conversation. Halley's Comet in particular consumed months of anticipatory coverage before delivering a visual anticlimax, and the Statue of Liberty weekend was billed as the largest patriotic celebration since the bicentennial.

    What actually mattered

    From 2026, the causal skeleton of the year is a different and only partially overlapping list: Chernobyl (which delegitimized the Soviet state from within), Iran-Contra (which permanently reshaped the presidency's relationship with Congress on covert action), the Tax Reform Act (whose 28 percent top rate and base-broadening logic still frames every tax debate), the Immigration Reform and Control Act (whose amnesty-plus-employer-sanctions architecture is still the baseline), the Anti-Drug Abuse Act (which built the mass-incarceration regime), the Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act (which accelerated the end of apartheid), the Microsoft IPO and the founding of Pixar (the two seed events of the personal-computer and digital-content economies), the CIA's Stinger deployment in Afghanistan (which broke Soviet air superiority), the Saudi-led oil-price collapse (which hollowed out the Soviet budget), the Reykjavik summit (which quietly ended the Cold War arms race), and Koop's blunt AIDS report (which reoriented US public-health messaging on sexuality).

    Most forgotten story

    Hands Across America on May 25, in which roughly 6.5 million Americans paid ten dollars apiece to link hands in a nearly continuous line from Battery Park to the Long Beach pier, backed by a Ken Kragen production, a Bacharach-Bayer Sager theme song, and simultaneous coverage on all three networks. President and Nancy Reagan joined a segment on the White House lawn. It was the peak of 1980s celebrity-charity spectacle after Live Aid and We Are the World, and within a decade it had essentially vanished from public memory, surviving mostly as an ironic reference in It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia. Its erasure is a useful reminder that raw participation and media saturation do not guarantee historical survival.

    Most underrated story

    The 1986 oil-price collapse. When Saudi Arabia abandoned its role as swing producer in late 1985 and cranked output, crude prices fell roughly two-thirds by mid-1986. In the United States the story was framed as a Texas-Oklahoma-Louisiana banking problem and a boon to drivers. In hindsight it is one of the most consequential geopolitical events of the decade: Yegor Gaidar and later economic historians have argued it tipped the Soviet fiscal position from strained to unsustainable and forced Gorbachev's reforms to accelerate beyond his ability to control them. The same crash midwifed the US savings-and-loan crisis via collapsing energy-belt real estate, reshaped Mexico's political economy on the road to NAFTA, and pushed the Gulf states toward the diversification strategies that still define them.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The Reykjavik Summit of October 11-12. Reported at the time as a failure because Reagan and Gorbachev walked out over SDI, it is now understood, from declassified records and the memoirs of George Shultz, Anatoly Chernyaev, and Jack Matlock, as the meeting at which the two leaders agreed in principle on eliminating all ballistic missiles within a decade and seriously discussed abolishing nuclear weapons altogether. Nothing that ambitious has been on the table since. The trust built at Reykjavik made the 1987 INF Treaty, the 1991 START agreement, and the peaceful end of the Cold War achievable. Historians now routinely rank Reykjavik among the most important superpower meetings of the twentieth century; in November 1986 most American newspapers were still calling it a debacle.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    Halley's Comet returns to inner solar system after 76 yearsAnticipated for months on front pages, cable specials, and a Time cover, the actual apparition was a visual anticlimax due to unfavorable Earth-Sun-comet geometry, and it faded from cultural memory almost instantly; the next return in 2061 barely surfaces in public consciousness.
    Hands Across America human chain across the continentDespite roughly 6.5 million paid participants, all-network coverage, and a Bacharach-Bayer Sager theme, the money raised for hunger was modest, the celebrity-charity model soon exhausted itself, and within a decade the event survived mainly as an ironic pop-culture reference.
    Statue of Liberty centennial 'Liberty Weekend'Billed as the biggest patriotic celebration since the bicentennial with tall ships, Elizabeth Taylor, and Reagan's ceremonial laser, it produced great television and no durable civic legacy, vanishing as a cultural touchstone within a decade.
    Mets-Red Sox World Series and the Buckner Game 6 errorOne of the most-watched sports stories of the year and unforgettable within New England and New York, its lasting impact is confined to baseball lore rather than any wider cultural or political consequence.
    Voyager aircraft nonstop unrefueled flight around the worldDick Rutan and Jeana Yeager's nine-day December feat was celebrated as a last-page-of-history aviation record but produced no successor program and no shift in aerospace practice.
    Kurt Waldheim Wehrmacht revelations and US watchlist banDominated European coverage for months and sparked serious diplomatic friction, but faded once Waldheim served a single presidential term and Austria's reckoning with its Nazi past moved on to other symbols.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Microsoft IPO on March 13 at $21 a shareCovered as a routine tech listing on business pages, the offering minted Bill Gates a paper billionaire and would create more new millionaires than any single event in American corporate history, shifting US economic power toward software as an asset class and toward the Pacific Northwest.
    Steve Jobs buys Lucasfilm's graphics division and renames it PixarA February transaction of about $5 million was almost invisible in mainstream press, but it seeded the CGI-animation revolution and Jobs's eventual return to Apple.
    CIA Stinger missiles reach the Afghan mujahideenReported only in fragments, the September 1986 first combat use broke Soviet air superiority in Afghanistan, set Moscow's withdrawal timetable, and armed the network that later became al-Qaeda.
    Saudi-led collapse of oil prices from about $27 to under $10 a barrelFramed at the time as a Texas banking problem and consumer windfall, it hollowed out the Soviet fiscal position and, per Yegor Gaidar and later historians, forced Gorbachev's reforms to accelerate beyond his ability to control them.
    Anti-Drug Abuse Act creates the 100-to-1 crack/powder sentencing disparityRushed through Congress after Len Bias's death and covered as tough-on-drugs legislation, it became the founding statute of modern mass incarceration and drove the tripling of the federal prison population over the next twenty-five years.
    Aum Shinsen no Kai (later Aum Shinrikyo) incorporated in Japan by Shoko AsaharaA July 1986 filing by a former yoga instructor received no US attention but seeded the group that carried out the 1995 Tokyo subway sarin attack, effectively inventing the modern template for a religious group pursuing weapons of mass destruction.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Shuttle Explodes: Seven Dead as Challenger Breaks Apart 73 Seconds Into Flight
    2. Marcos Flees Manila for Hawaii; Aquino Sworn In as People Power Prevails
    3. U.S. Warplanes Strike Tripoli and Benghazi in Retaliation for Berlin Disco Bombing
    4. Soviet Reactor Disaster: Radioactive Cloud Spreads Across Europe After Chernobyl Blast
    5. Millions Link Hands from New York to California in Hands Across America
    6. High Court Upholds Georgia Sodomy Law in Bowers v. Hardwick, 5-4
    7. Reagan Signs Sweeping Tax Overhaul: Top Rate Slashed to 28 Percent
    8. Congress Overrides Reagan Veto, Imposes Sanctions on South Africa
    9. Reykjavik Talks Collapse Over 'Star Wars'; Leaders Leave Empty-Handed
    10. U.S. Sent Arms to Iran; Meese Confirms Profits Diverted to Contras

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe year opened on twin shocks. On January 20 the country observed the first federal Martin Luther King Jr. holiday, and Capital Cities finalized its takeover of ABC. On January 26 the Chicago Bears crushed New England 46-10 in Super Bowl XX to cap a 15-1 season and cement the Super Bowl Shuffle in pop-culture memory. Two days later, on January 28, the Space Shuttle Challenger broke apart 73 seconds after launch, killing all seven aboard including schoolteacher Christa McAuliffe as millions of children watched live; Reagan's Oval Office address that evening about slipping the surly bonds of earth became the emotional keynote of the year.
    FebruaryThe Marcos regime collapsed in the Philippines' People Power revolution on February 22-25, with Corazon Aquino sworn in and Marcos flown to Hawaii after the Reagan administration quietly urged him to leave. Steve Jobs purchased Lucasfilm's graphics division for about $5 million and renamed it Pixar, almost invisibly to the press. The Fed's Reagan appointees openly outvoted Chairman Paul Volcker on a discount-rate cut, an unusual internal defeat Volcker patched over with a coordinated cut alongside the Bundesbank and Bank of Japan. Janet Jackson released Control, and Phil Collins won Album of the Year at the Grammys.
    MarchMicrosoft went public on March 13 at $21 a share, closing its first day at $27.75 and giving the company a $780 million market cap; Oracle listed the day before. The Fed cut the discount rate from 7.5 to 7.0 percent. The Dow broke 1,800 for the first time. Metallica released Master of Puppets. At the Academy Awards on March 24, Out of Africa took Best Picture and The Color Purple was famously shut out on eleven nominations.
    AprilA busy and violent month. WTI crude crashed below $10 a barrel as Saudi Arabia's swing-producer withdrawal took full effect. On April 15 Operation El Dorado Canyon struck Tripoli and Benghazi in retaliation for the La Belle discotheque bombing in West Berlin; one F-111 was lost. On April 26 the Chernobyl reactor exploded in Soviet Ukraine, with the plume detected over Scandinavia days before Moscow admitted the accident. The Fed cut the discount rate to 6.5 percent. KKR completed its $6.2 billion LBO of Beatrice Foods, then the largest ever. Michael Jordan scored 63 on the Celtics in a playoff loss on April 20, and Roger Clemens struck out 20 Mariners on April 29. Batson v. Kentucky barred race-based peremptory jury strikes.
    MayOn May 19 Reagan signed the Firearm Owners Protection Act, rolling back parts of the 1968 Gun Control Act while banning civilian ownership of new machine guns. On May 25, roughly 6.5 million Americans linked hands from Battery Park to the Long Beach pier in Hands Across America, the peak of 1980s celebrity-charity spectacle. Reagan renounced compliance with the unratified SALT II. Robert McFarlane made his secret Tehran mission carrying a chocolate cake and an inscribed Bible in a fruitless attempt at hostage negotiations. Peter Gabriel released So and Run-D.M.C. released Raising Hell. Montreal defeated Calgary 4-1 to win the Stanley Cup.
    JuneThe Supreme Court delivered a heavy docket. Bowers v. Hardwick on June 30 upheld Georgia's sodomy statute 5-4, and Meritor Savings v. Vinson on June 19 unanimously established hostile-workplace sexual harassment as sex discrimination under Title VII. Thornburgh reaffirmed Roe on June 11. GE completed its $6.4 billion acquisition of RCA, gaining NBC. Ferris Bueller's Day Off opened on June 11. On June 19 University of Maryland basketball star Len Bias died of a cocaine overdose two days after being drafted by the Boston Celtics, catalyzing the year's crack panic. The Boston Celtics beat the Rockets 4-2 for the NBA title, and Larry Bird won his third straight MVP. Congress approved $100 million in resumed overt Contra aid. Madonna released True Blue.
    JulyThe Statue of Liberty centennial 'Liberty Weekend,' produced by David Wolper for the July 3-6 rededication, drew Elizabeth II, Mitterrand, and tall ships in New York Harbor for the confident patriotic peak of Reaganism. The Fed cut the discount rate to 6.0 percent. On July 7 the Supreme Court in Bowsher v. Synar struck down Gramm-Rudman's automatic-sequester mechanism. The Dow broke 1,900 for the first time. LTV Corporation, the second-largest US steelmaker, filed the largest industrial bankruptcy in US history on July 17. Judge Harry Claiborne of Nevada was impeached 406-0 by the House on July 22. The Meese Commission on Pornography released its report. Father Lawrence Jenco was released in Lebanon.
    AugustOPEC agreed in early August to restore production quotas, halting the oil price freefall and setting up a partial recovery to the high teens by year end. The Fed cut the discount rate to 5.5 percent, where it would hold. Paul Simon released Graceland. On August 20 in Edmond, Oklahoma, postal worker Patrick Sherrill killed 14 coworkers before shooting himself, giving American English the phrase 'going postal.' Nicholas Daniloff, a US News reporter, was arrested by the KGB in Moscow, opening a diplomatic standoff. Bon Jovi released Slippery When Wet. Spike Lee's She's Gotta Have It opened. Stand By Me arrived in theaters.
    SeptemberBurroughs merged with Sperry to form Unisys in a $4.8 billion deal. Compaq's Deskpro 386 shipped, beating IBM to a 386-based PC. The Fox Broadcasting Company era began later, but on September 8 The Oprah Winfrey Show entered national syndication, quickly displacing Donahue. Blue Velvet opened on September 19. On September 11-12 the Dow fell about 4.6 percent in a two-day sell-off that put program trading and portfolio insurance on the front pages. On September 26 William Rehnquist was confirmed Chief Justice 65-33 and Antonin Scalia was confirmed 98-0 to fill his associate seat. The Daniloff affair was resolved in a de facto swap for accused Soviet spy Gennady Zakharov. The Baker-Miyazawa accord attempted to stabilize the yen. Metallica bassist Cliff Burton was killed when the band's tour bus crashed in Sweden on September 27.
    OctoberLegislation month. On October 1 Reagan signed the Goldwater-Nichols Defense Reorganization Act. On October 2 Congress overrode Reagan's veto to enact the Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act, the first foreign-policy veto override of the twentieth century. On October 9 Fox Broadcasting launched with The Late Show Starring Joan Rivers, and the Senate removed Judge Claiborne. On October 11-12 Reagan and Gorbachev met at Reykjavik, agreeing in principle on ballistic-missile elimination before talks collapsed over SDI. On October 17 Reagan signed the Superfund reauthorization and Title III right-to-know requirements. On October 22 Reagan signed the Tax Reform Act; the same day Surgeon General C. Everett Koop released his blunt AIDS report. On October 27 Reagan signed the Anti-Drug Abuse Act with its 100-to-1 crack/powder disparity. The Mets beat the Red Sox 4-3 in the World Series, with Bill Buckner's Game 6 error on October 25.
    NovemberThe month the Reagan presidency changed. On November 3 the Lebanese magazine Ash-Shiraa revealed the arms-for-hostages deal with Iran. On November 4 midterm voters returned the Senate to Democrats 55-45. On November 6 Reagan signed the Immigration Reform and Control Act. On November 13 Reagan addressed the nation from the Oval Office minimizing the arrangement. On November 14 Ivan Boesky settled with the SEC for $100 million and agreed to cooperate. On November 21-25 Oliver North and Fawn Hall shredded NSC documents. On November 22 Mike Tyson knocked out Trevor Berbick to become, at 20, the youngest heavyweight champion in history. On November 25 Attorney General Meese announced the diversion of Iranian profits to the Contras, Poindexter resigned, and North was fired. Star Trek IV opened November 26. Beastie Boys released Licensed to Ill. Reagan's approval fell from 67 to 46 percent in a single Gallup poll.
    DecemberThe year closed in a mood of gathering unease. Reagan named the Tower Commission to investigate Iran-Contra. Platoon opened December 19, and Little Shop of Horrors the same day. The Dow crossed and closed above 1,900 near year end for the first time. The Voyager aircraft completed its nine-day nonstop unrefueled circumnavigation on December 23. On December 20 in Howard Beach, Queens, a Black man named Michael Griffith was killed by a car while fleeing a white mob, provoking marches led by the Rev. Al Sharpton. CIA Director William Casey suffered a seizure late in the month and would die the following May. Bruce Springsteen's Live/1975-85 box set was moving unprecedented CD numbers, and the country prepared for the Iran-Contra hearings that would define 1987.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+3.5% real (current BEA vintage; contemporary prints closer to 2.8-3.1%)
    InflationCPI-U +1.9% full year; +1.1% December-over-December (lowest since 1961)
    Unemployment6.6% December; peaked near 7.2% mid-summer; averaged around 7.0%
    S&P 500 return+18.7% total return; +14.6% price return (closed 242.17)
    Dow return+22.6% price return (closed 1,895.95)
    Fed funds rateEnded year approximately 6.0-6.5%; discount rate cut four times from 7.5% to 5.5%
    Oil priceWTI ended around $17-18/bbl; began January near $26, crashed below $10 in April
    Gold priceApproximately $390-400/oz year-end (up from about $327 at start)
    PopulationApproximately 240 million
    Median home priceExisting-home median approximately $80,300; new-home median approximately $92,000
    National debtGross federal debt approximately $2.12 trillion at fiscal year-end September 1986; publicly held debt about $1.74 trillion

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 1986 was to live inside a country that could not quite decide whether it was ascending or unraveling, and the honest answer was both at once. For most of the twelve months the surface felt bright: gasoline was suddenly cheap, mortgage rates were falling toward single digits, the Dow crossed 1,900 for the first time, and CPI inflation printed at 1.1 percent in December, the lowest year-end reading since John F. Kennedy was in the White House. Cable had matured, the compact disc had arrived, the Mac Plus sat on a growing minority of desks, and Nintendo's grey box had colonized the family room. In July, Ronald Reagan stood in New York Harbor with the newly refurbished Statue of Liberty and Elizabeth Taylor lit the torch, and it genuinely felt like the country was throwing itself a birthday party it had earned.

    And then the year kept happening. In January the Challenger came apart seventy-three seconds into flight while millions of schoolchildren watched live, and Reagan's Oval Office address about slipping the surly bonds of earth became the emotional low point of the decade to that date. In April Chernobyl reminded everyone that the nuclear age was still one machine failure away from catastrophe. In June a college basketball player named Len Bias died of a cocaine overdose two days after the Celtics drafted him, and the crack panic that followed produced legislation whose sentencing disparities would define federal incarceration for a generation. In October Surgeon General Koop finally said the words the Reagan administration had spent five years refusing to say about AIDS. In November, in the space of three weeks, the country learned that its government had sold missiles to the Ayatollah's Iran in exchange for hostages and then routed the profits to a Nicaraguan insurgency Congress had explicitly forbidden funding, and the president's approval rating fell twenty-one points in a single Gallup poll.

    For the white middle-class suburb the culture centered on, 1986 felt prosperous, mildly anxious, and unmistakably consumerist, with yuppie iconography in full bloom and Tom Cruise flying F-14s on every drive-in screen. For gay America and urban Black America, it felt like a gathering emergency the rest of the country was still learning to see. For Texans and Oklahomans it felt like a private depression. For the historians who would later write it up, it felt like the exact moment the Soviet Union began to fail and Microsoft went public on the same page of the same newspaper, and nobody noticed. It was, in short, a very American year: loud on the surface, quietly consequential underneath, and pointing in more directions than any single citizen could track.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    1987
    Econ +1Mark +0Poli +1Cult +2Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    Nineteen eighty-seven was a year of scandal, spectacle, and quiet structural change — a hinge in the late Cold War that Americans lived as a moral reckoning and now read as the seed year of much of what came next. Ronald Reagan spent the year climbing out of the Iran-Contra revelations that had broken the previous November, absorbing the Tower Commission's report in February, enduring televised joint congressional hearings from May through August, and rebuilding under a new chief of staff, Howard Baker. By December he had recovered enough political capital to host Mikhail Gorbachev at the White House and sign the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty, the first arms-control agreement ever to reduce rather than merely cap nuclear arsenals. In between came the Bork confirmation battle, Gary Hart's ruinous weekend with Donna Rice, the Bakker televangelism scandal, and the 58-hour rescue of Baby Jessica broadcast live on CNN.

    The economy delivered its fifth year of Reagan-era expansion — roughly 3.5% real GDP growth, unemployment down almost a full point to 5.7%, and 3.2 million new payroll jobs — but the character of the year was set by a single afternoon. On October 19, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 508.32 points, or 22.6%, still the largest one-day percentage loss in the index's history, wiping out roughly $500 billion in US equity market capitalization. Alan Greenspan, sworn in as Fed chairman only ten weeks earlier, pledged liquidity, funds rates eased back, and the real economy shrugged: no recession followed for another 33 months. The full-year S&P 500 total return was a modestly positive 5.25%, but the year is remembered for its volatility, its bond-market rout, and its crash.

    Beneath the scandals and the spectacle, the year's most consequential moves were quieter. The FCC abolished the Fairness Doctrine in a 4-0 vote in August, seeding conservative talk radio and, eventually, a polarized broadcast politics. The FDA approved AZT in March, opening the AIDS therapeutic era. The Montreal Protocol on ozone-depleting substances was signed in September and would become the most successful environmental treaty ever negotiated. ACT UP was founded, the AIDS Memorial Quilt was unfurled on the National Mall, hip-hop entered a golden age with Public Enemy, Eric B. & Rakim, and Boogie Down Productions all releasing landmark debuts, U2's 'The Joshua Tree' and Michael Jackson's 'Bad' defined popular music, and the Fox network launched with 'Married... with Children' and 'The Tracey Ullman Show.' The Palestinian Intifada began in December after a Gaza traffic accident. Americans thought the year was about accountability; in hindsight it was mostly about infrastructure.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1A ~3.5% real growth year with unemployment falling nearly a full point to a decade-low 5.7% is squarely above the era's baseline, though reaccelerating inflation, a record trade deficit, and a widening S&L crisis keep it from a stronger mark.
    Markets+0Full-year total returns were modestly positive for large-caps and negative for small-caps, but the year is defined by extreme volatility, the worst single-day crash on record, and a bond-market rout that repriced long rates by more than 300 basis points.
    Politics+1Iran-Contra damaged executive credibility and the Bork battle poisoned judicial confirmations for a generation, yet the government functioned without shutdown or constitutional breach and produced a historic INF Treaty plus a bipartisan post-crash budget summit.
    Culture+2A remarkably rich year for movies, a landmark year for rock, an inflection point for hip-hop, and genuine cultural conversation around Iran-Contra, AIDS activism, televangelism's fall, and the Bloom/Hirsch canon debate — vitality high enough that creative output alone earns the score.
    Overall Mood+0Prosperity and Cold War thaw pulled up; Iran-Contra, Black Monday, AIDS deaths crossing 40,000, and pervasive scandal fatigue pulled down — the year ended less certain than it began, but not broken.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Tower Commission Blames Reagan for Iran-Contra Failures; President Admits Arms-for-Hostages Deal
    2. Bakker Resigns PTL Ministry Amid Sex Scandal; Falwell Takes Over Empire
    3. Gary Hart Withdraws From Presidential Race Over Donna Rice Affair
    4. Iraqi Missiles Kill 37 Aboard U.S. Frigate Stark in Persian Gulf
    5. Reagan at Brandenburg Gate: 'Mr. Gorbachev, Tear Down This Wall!'
    6. Oliver North Defends Contra Diversion in Uniform Before Congress
    7. Senate Rejects Bork for Supreme Court, 58 to 42, in Historic Defeat
    8. Dow Crashes 508 Points, 22.6%, on Black Monday
    9. Baby Jessica Rescued From Texas Well After 58-Hour Ordeal
    10. Reagan and Gorbachev Sign INF Treaty Eliminating Medium-Range Nuclear Missiles

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    A solid mid-cycle year of expansion overshadowed in memory by a single financial event. Real GDP grew roughly 3.5%, the fifth year of the Reagan-era recovery, led by a swing in net exports as the dollar's post-Plaza Accord decline finally produced an industrial rebound, together with strong business fixed investment and inventory rebuilding. Unemployment fell nearly a full point from 6.6% in January to 5.7% in December — one of the decade's largest annual improvements — with nonfarm payrolls adding about 3.2 million jobs and labor-force participation hitting a then-record 65.6%. Inflation, however, reaccelerated meaningfully off 1986's lows, with headline CPI up 4.4% year-over-year, driven by the oil-price recovery and pass-through from a weaker dollar. Housing had a two-track year — coastal metros saw double-digit appreciation while oil-patch cities remained in outright depression, feeding the slow-burning S&L crisis.

    Important Statistics

    GDP growth ~3.5%; CPI +4.4% Dec/Dec (core ~4.2%); unemployment 6.6% to 5.7%; nonfarm payrolls +~3.2 million; labor-force participation ~65.6%; 10-year Treasury from ~7.1% to peak above 10.2%; 30-year mortgage rate from ~9.2% to peak above 11.3%; WTI crude ~$17 at year-end; gold ~$484 (~+24%); trade-weighted dollar down another ~10-15% on the year; merchandise trade deficit at a then-record ~$152 billion; FY1987 federal deficit $149.7 billion (down from $221 billion the year before); personal saving rate ~4%.

    Major Events

    Alan Greenspan's swearing-in as Fed chairman on August 11 ended the Volcker era. The Louvre Accord of February 22 sought to stabilize the dollar after two years of managed decline. The Tax Reform Act of 1986 hit full implementation, reshaping real-estate economics. The FSLIC was declared insolvent, and Congress passed the Competitive Equality Banking Act in August authorizing an inadequate $10.8 billion recapitalization. Texaco filed Chapter 11 on April 12 — at $34.9 billion in assets the largest US corporate bankruptcy on record — driven by a $10.5 billion Pennzoil judgment over Getty Oil. Chrysler acquired American Motors (and Jeep) in March for $1.5 billion. The July merchandise-trade print of $16.5 billion helped trigger the October bond-and-equity break. And the LBO/junk-bond wave under Milken's Drexel Burnham Lambert intensified even as Ivan Boesky's cooperation widened the insider-trading investigation.

    Why this score

    A 3.5% growth year with unemployment falling almost a full point to a decade-low sits squarely above the era's baseline, but reaccelerating inflation, a record trade deficit, a metastasizing S&L crisis, and the confidence hit from October keep it from a higher mark.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    The full-year numbers conceal one of the most volatile years in market history. The S&P 500 delivered a total return of about +5.25% (price return ~+2.0%), the Dow finished at 1938.83 versus 1895.95 at year-end 1986 (+2.3% price), and the NASDAQ Composite fell about 5.3%. But the Dow ran from 1895 at year-start to an all-time high of 2722.42 on August 25 — a 44% gain in eight months — rolled over into a nervous September as bond yields spiked, and then collapsed: down 4.6% on Wednesday October 14, down 4.6% again on Friday October 16, and 22.6% on Black Monday, October 19. The 508.32-point single-session Dow loss remains the largest one-day percentage drop in the index's history, vaporizing roughly $500 billion in US equity market cap in one afternoon and about $1 trillion globally over the surrounding week. Bonds had their own regime shift: the 10-year Treasury yield climbed from ~7.1% in January to above 10.2% in October, one of the sharpest sell-offs of the postwar era. Gold surged ~24% to about $484/oz.

    Character

    Crash-then-recovery

    Major Companies

    IBM remained the largest US company by market cap at the start of the year, with Exxon, GE, AT&T (post-breakup), Ford, GM, and Philip Morris rounding out the giants. Microsoft (IPO March 1986) and Oracle (IPO March 1986) were rapidly rising software names. Texaco filed Chapter 11 on April 12 — at $34.9 billion in assets the largest US corporate bankruptcy on record at the time — forced by a $10.5 billion judgment in Pennzoil's tortious-interference suit over Getty Oil. Chrysler acquired American Motors (and with it Jeep) in March for $1.5 billion, and BP completed its buyout of the remaining minority in Standard Oil of Ohio (Sohio) for about $7.6 billion. The LBO/junk-bond wave under Milken's Drexel Burnham Lambert franchise was intensifying, setting the stage for the RJR Nabisco battle of 1988.

    Investment Themes

    Portfolio insurance and index arbitrage were the innovations that the Brady Commission would ultimately blame for the mechanical breakdown of October 19, when dynamic hedging via S&P futures fed a self-reinforcing sell-off that overwhelmed NYSE specialists and Nasdaq market-makers. The LBO / junk-bond boom under Drexel's Milken franchise dominated corporate deal-making. Ivan Boesky's cooperation widened the insider-trading investigation, foreshadowing Drexel's downfall. Post-crash, the SEC opened the rulemaking that would produce the 1988 'circuit breakers.' A record merchandise trade deficit — the July print of $16.5 billion was widely blamed as a proximate trigger for the October bond spike and equity break — kept the dollar under pressure despite the February Louvre Accord.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Iran-Contra dominated the year. The Tower Commission report of February 26 sharply criticized Reagan for a detached management style and confirmed that NSC staff had run an arms-for-hostages operation with Iran and diverted proceeds to the Contras in apparent violation of the Boland Amendment. Reagan acknowledged the trade in a March 4 televised address; joint congressional hearings ran from May through August, producing Oliver North's star turn in Marine uniform and John Poindexter's assertion that he had authorized the diversion himself without informing the president. The joint committee's November final report concluded that 'the rule of law' had been subordinated to policy goals. On October 23, the Senate rejected Robert Bork's Supreme Court nomination 58-42, the largest defeat margin for a nominee in history; Douglas Ginsburg withdrew after a marijuana revelation, and Reagan then nominated Anthony Kennedy on November 11. On December 8, Reagan and Gorbachev signed the INF Treaty at the White House. Reagan delivered 'Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!' at the Brandenburg Gate on June 12.

    Domestic Policy

    Divided government, with a Democratic Congress led by Speaker Jim Wright and Senate Majority Leader Robert Byrd, forced more consensus-based governance than in Reagan's first term. The Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Reaffirmation Act of 1987 passed in September after Bowsher v. Synar had knocked down Gramm-Rudman's original enforcement mechanism. Following Black Monday, congressional leaders and Treasury Secretary James Baker reached a two-year budget summit deal combining modest tax increases with spending restraint. Alan Greenspan replaced Paul Volcker at the Fed on August 11. The Immigration Reform and Control Act (IRCA) opened its legalization window May 5, ultimately regularizing roughly 1.7 million people. The McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance Act was signed July 22. AZT was approved in March; Reagan gave his first major AIDS speech on May 31, and the Presidential Commission on the HIV Epidemic began work. The FCC repealed the Fairness Doctrine on August 4. The FSLIC was declared insolvent and Congress passed the Competitive Equality Banking Act with an inadequate $10.8 billion recapitalization.

    Foreign Policy

    U.S.-Soviet relations were the defining story. Following near-breakthrough at Reykjavik in 1986, Secretary Shultz's Moscow visits and Gorbachev's ongoing glasnost and perestroika reforms drove negotiations to conclusion; the INF Treaty of December 8 eliminated an entire class of ground-launched ballistic and cruise missiles with intrusive on-site verification. In the Persian Gulf, an Iraqi Exocet strike on the USS Stark killed 37 sailors on May 17; in July the U.S. Navy began Operation Earnest Will, reflagging Kuwaiti tankers and escorting them through the Strait of Hormuz — the largest U.S. naval combat deployment since Vietnam. In Central America, the Esquipulas II peace agreement authored by Costa Rican President Oscar Arias (who won the 1987 Nobel Peace Prize) constrained further Contra aid over Reagan's objections. William Casey died May 6 and was succeeded at CIA by Judge William Webster; Frank Carlucci replaced Caspar Weinberger at Defense on November 23. The First Palestinian Intifada began in Gaza on December 9. Comprehensive Anti-Apartheid Act sanctions continued to shape U.S. policy toward South Africa over the administration's discomfort.

    Important Elections

    1987 was an off-year, dominated by 1988 primary jockeying. On the Democratic side, Gary Hart's May withdrawal after the Miami Herald reported his weekend with Donna Rice reshuffled the field into what the press dubbed 'the Seven Dwarfs': Dukakis, Gephardt, Gore, Jackson, Simon, Babbitt, and briefly Biden, who withdrew on September 23 after plagiarism revelations. On the Republican side, Vice President George H.W. Bush declared October 12 as front-runner, facing Bob Dole, Jack Kemp, Pete du Pont, Alexander Haig, and televangelist Pat Robertson. Gubernatorial races produced Buddy Roemer's insurgent Louisiana victory (Edwin Edwards declining a runoff), Ray Mabus in Mississippi, and Wallace Wilkinson in Kentucky. Kurt Schmoke was elected mayor of Baltimore; Harold Washington was reelected in Chicago in April but died of a heart attack on November 25, with Alderman Eugene Sawyer selected as interim mayor after a contentious late-night Council session.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Nineteen eighty-seven was a bumper year for American cinema. 'Three Men and a Baby' was the top domestic grosser at roughly $168 million, followed by 'Beverly Hills Cop II' and 'Fatal Attraction,' the Adrian Lyne thriller whose 'bunny boiler' Alex Forrest became shorthand for a decade of anxieties about single women, working women, and marital infidelity. Oliver Stone's 'Wall Street' arrived in December carrying Gordon Gekko's 'greed is good' speech, an instant epitaph for the era. Other important releases included Brian De Palma's 'The Untouchables' (with Sean Connery in his Oscar-winning role), Stanley Kubrick's 'Full Metal Jacket,' Barry Levinson's 'Good Morning, Vietnam' (launching Robin Williams as a dramatic star), 'Robocop,' 'Predator,' 'Lethal Weapon,' 'The Princess Bride,' James L. Brooks's 'Broadcast News,' 'Moonstruck,' the Coen brothers' 'Raising Arizona,' Spielberg's 'Empire of the Sun,' and the sleeper hit 'Dirty Dancing.' The March 1987 Oscars, honoring 1986 releases, had given Best Picture to 'Platoon.'

    Music

    The industry's biggest event was Michael Jackson's 'Bad' (August 31), the long-awaited follow-up to 'Thriller,' which produced five Billboard #1 singles. U2's 'The Joshua Tree' (March) was the year's defining critical and commercial statement, landing the band on the cover of Time as 'Rock's Hottest Ticket.' Guns N' Roses released 'Appetite for Destruction' in July, Def Leppard released 'Hysteria,' Whitney Houston's 'Whitney' became the first album by a female artist to debut at #1, and Prince delivered 'Sign o' the Times.' George Michael's 'Faith,' R.E.M.'s commercial breakthrough 'Document,' Bruce Springsteen's 'Tunnel of Love,' Fleetwood Mac's 'Tango in the Night,' and Aerosmith's comeback 'Permanent Vacation' rounded out the year. Los Lobos' cover of 'La Bamba' was the biggest single, and Bon Jovi, Starship, and Whitney Houston dominated pop radio. Hip-hop took a decisive step forward with Public Enemy's 'Yo! Bum Rush the Show,' Eric B. & Rakim's 'Paid in Full,' and LL Cool J's 'Bigger and Deffer' all landing in the same year.

    Television

    Prime time belonged to NBC. 'The Cosby Show' remained #1, followed by 'Family Ties,' 'Cheers,' 'Growing Pains,' 'Night Court,' 'The Golden Girls,' and 'Moonlighting.' Fall 1987 debuts included 'Thirtysomething,' 'A Different World,' 'Full House,' '21 Jump Street,' and, on the brand-new Fox network, 'Married... with Children' and 'The Tracey Ullman Show,' which would soon incubate 'The Simpsons.' David Letterman was in full ascendance in late night. News television was defined by the Iran-Contra hearings, which drew 40-plus million viewers, and by CNN's live coverage of the Baby Jessica rescue, widely credited with cementing cable news as a cultural force. ABC aired the controversial Soviet-occupation miniseries 'Amerika' in February.

    Technology

    It was a transitional year. IBM introduced the PS/2 line in April, bringing the VGA graphics standard and 3.5-inch floppy drives, followed by OS/2 1.0 in December. Microsoft shipped Windows 2.0. Apple's Macintosh II brought color to the Mac, and HyperCard, bundled free, gave millions their first taste of hypermedia. Compact discs overtook vinyl in dollar sales; the CD-ROM standard was formalized. Prodigy, a joint IBM/Sears online service, was in limited launch. Cellular phones were expensive car-mounted novelties. Camcorders were the hot Christmas gift, and Nintendo's NES was becoming ubiquitous with 'The Legend of Zelda' released stateside in August. Two developments barely noticed at the time would prove foundational: the DNS specifications (RFCs 1034 and 1035) were published, and Japanese microbiologist Yoshizumi Ishino described the CRISPR repeat sequence in E. coli.

    Social Trends

    The year had a whiplash quality — Reagan-era self-confidence knocked sideways by scandal, health scares, and a stock crash. Tom Wolfe's 'The Bonfire of the Vanities,' published in October, crystallized New York's mood of racial tension, class arrogance, and reckoning. The yuppie went briefly from aspiration to punchline. AIDS activism moved from grief to organized militancy with the founding of ACT UP in March and its first Wall Street action against AZT pricing; the Second National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights drew half a million on October 11, and the AIDS Memorial Quilt was displayed on the National Mall for the first time. Allan Bloom's 'The Closing of the American Mind' and E.D. Hirsch's 'Cultural Literacy' both became surprise bestsellers, opening the culture-wars debate over the university curriculum. The anti-apartheid divestment movement continued on campuses, and the televangelism collapse gave secular America a satisfying comeuppance narrative against Moral Majority sanctimony.

    Crime

    Bernhard Goetz, the 'subway vigilante,' was acquitted in June of attempted murder for the 1984 shooting of four Black teenagers, convicted only of illegal weapon possession — a verdict that inflamed racial tensions. The Howard Beach case, arising from the December 1986 racial attack in Queens, produced three convictions in December 1987. The Tawana Brawley case erupted in November when the 15-year-old was found in a garbage bag in Wappingers Falls, New York, propelling Reverend Al Sharpton to national prominence (a 1988 grand jury would ultimately find the story a hoax). John Gotti beat federal RICO charges in March. Serial killer Richard Ramirez, the 'Night Stalker,' was on trial in Los Angeles. Cuban detainees seized federal facilities in Oakdale, Louisiana and Atlanta in November, taking hostages before peaceful resolutions. Bess Myerson was indicted in October in a bribery scandal.

    Sports

    The New York Giants beat the Denver Broncos 39-20 in Super Bowl XXI on January 25 with Phil Simms as MVP. The 1987 NFL season was disrupted by a 24-day players' strike that produced three weeks of embarrassing replacement-player games. The Minnesota Twins beat the St. Louis Cardinals in seven games in a World Series that was the first decided entirely by home-team wins and the first played partly indoors, at the Metrodome. Mark McGwire hit 49 home runs as an Oakland rookie, and Andre Dawson won MVP on a last-place Cubs team. The Los Angeles Lakers beat the Boston Celtics in six for the NBA title in Magic-Bird's climactic meeting, with Magic Johnson winning both regular-season and Finals MVP. The Edmonton Oilers won Wayne Gretzky's fourth Stanley Cup. Sugar Ray Leonard beat Marvin Hagler in the 'Super Fight' by controversial split decision on April 6, and Mike Tyson unified the heavyweight titles at age 21 by beating Tony Tucker on August 1. Larry Mize chipped in to steal the Masters from Greg Norman, Ivan Lendl and Steffi Graf dominated tennis, Alysheba won the Kentucky Derby and Preakness, and Indiana won the NCAA basketball title on Keith Smart's baseline jumper against Syracuse.

    Public Mood

    Chastened, gossipy, still prosperous, and increasingly aware that the Reagan era had a shelf life. Contemporary journalism captured a president stumbling from Iran-Contra revelations through summer hearings, coupled with prostate surgery in January and Nancy Reagan's mastectomy in October; Time and Newsweek repeatedly asked whether Reagan was still in command. Black Monday briefly punctured the decade's most self-confident narrative — greed, deregulation, and yuppie ascendancy. AIDS was moving from 'gay plague' framing toward broader public alarm, aided by Randy Shilts's 'And the Band Played On.' The televangelism scandals delivered comeuppance to Moral Majority sanctimony. Underneath the anxiety, however, the Cold War was clearly winding down: Gorbachev was on magazine covers, the INF Treaty was signed in Washington in December, and the possibility of a genuinely different world felt real for the first time in a generation. The year ended feeling less certain than it began.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Americans in 1987 believed the year was fundamentally about accountability — whether Reagan would survive Iran-Contra, whether the Senate would tolerate a Bork-style originalist, whether televangelism could be trusted, whether a womanizing frontrunner could be president, whether the markets could sustain their euphoria. The televised congressional hearings on Iran-Contra were the summer's obsession; the Bakker sex-and-hush-money implosion was the spring's; the Gary Hart / Donna Rice affair felt like an epochal moment for political journalism; Baby Jessica's rescue felt like a national ritual; Black Monday felt like the end of a decade of self-confidence. Those were the stories that dominated magazine covers, evening news broadcasts, and morning conversation.

    What actually mattered

    What actually mattered was quieter and more structural. The INF Treaty and the Berlin Wall speech helped normalize the endgame of the Cold War two years before anyone realized it was ending. The FCC's 4-0 vote in August to abolish the Fairness Doctrine remade broadcast politics and set the technical conditions for conservative talk radio and cable-opinion television. Alan Greenspan's swearing-in as Fed chairman on August 11 defined a generation of monetary policy. The FDA's approval of AZT in March opened the AIDS therapeutic era. The Montreal Protocol became the most successful environmental treaty in history. The Palestinian Intifada that began in December reset Middle East politics. And a wave of cultural and technological debuts — the Fox network, 'The Tracey Ullman Show' (soon to birth 'The Simpsons'), the golden age of hip-hop, HyperCard, the CRISPR sequence in a Japanese microbiology journal — composed the texture of the coming decades.

    Most forgotten story

    The sinking of the MV Dona Paz in the Philippines on December 20, 1987 — a ferry-tanker collision that killed roughly 4,000 people, making it the deadliest peacetime maritime disaster in history. It ran briefly on international wires and vanished from Western consciousness, a striking illustration of coverage geography in the pre-internet era.

    Most underrated story

    The FCC's abolition of the Fairness Doctrine on August 4, a 4-0 vote by an obscure agency treated as a regulatory story in a busy news year. Within 18 months it had enabled the nationally syndicated Rush Limbaugh Show; within a decade it underwrote the entire conservative talk-radio ecosystem; within two decades it was routinely invoked in explanations of American political polarization and the rise of Fox News-style opinion broadcasting. No comparable 1987 decision so thoroughly rewrote the informational environment of American democracy.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    That 'Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!' — now one of the most quoted lines in postwar American oratory — received relatively little coverage from the media when Reagan delivered it at the Brandenburg Gate on June 12. Time and again observers noted the speech was not treated as a signature address until after the wall actually fell in November 1989. The trajectory of that single sentence, from largely-ignored applause line to canonized Cold War quotation, captures how much the meaning of 1987 was retro-assigned by later events.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The televised Iran-Contra hearings and Oliver North's testimonyIn the moment it felt like a Watergate sequel that could end Reagan's presidency, but the political damage was contained: North's convictions were vacated in 1990, no top official went to prison, and the affair produced neither structural reform of covert action nor a lasting realignment.
    The Jim Bakker / PTL televangelism scandalThe most-covered soft-news story of the spring, but the televangelism industry consolidated and continued; culturally it now reads as a period piece rather than a turning point.
    Gary Hart and Donna Rice on the Monkey BusinessThe 'new rules' theory that Hart forever changed how the press covered candidates' private lives has not aged well; subsequent scandals showed the norms were far messier and more selective than the 1987 narrative implied.
    The Baby Jessica McClure well rescueThe archetypal 'cable-news-before-cable-news' event drew Reagan calling every American her godparent, but its cultural residue is thin — remembered fondly, not consequentially.
    The Baby M surrogacy custody caseFelt like a watershed on reproductive technology, but the 1988 New Jersey Supreme Court reversal blunted its precedential weight and surrogacy law developed state-by-state along paths only loosely connected to Whitehead's fight.
    The USS Stark attack and the Tanker WarThe May 17 Iraqi Exocet strike killed 37 U.S. sailors and drove Operation Earnest Will, but the episode fit awkwardly into the tidy story that emerged four years later when Iraq became the enemy in a different Gulf War.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The FCC's repeal of the Fairness Doctrine on August 4A 4-0 vote by an obscure agency, buried in inside-the-Beltway coverage, that within 18 months had enabled the nationally syndicated Rush Limbaugh Show and within two decades was routinely invoked as a load-bearing cause of American political polarization and the rise of Fox News-style opinion broadcasting.
    Alan Greenspan replaces Paul Volcker at the Federal Reserve on August 11Treated as an orderly succession, Greenspan would run the Fed for 18-and-a-half years, shaping the Great Moderation, the dot-com bubble, the 2000s housing bubble, and — arguably — the intellectual conditions for the 2008 crisis.
    The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer, signed September 16Given respectful but modest coverage as a technical treaty, it is now regularly cited as the most successful environmental treaty ever negotiated, credited with preventing hundreds of millions of skin cancers and achieving universal ratification.
    FDA approval of AZT on March 20The first proof that HIV was pharmacologically tractable, opening the drug-development path that produced protease inhibitors and eventually turned HIV from a near-certain death sentence into a manageable chronic condition.
    The First Palestinian Intifada begins December 9A traffic collision in Jabalia refugee camp triggered the uprising that would run until 1993, birth Hamas as a distinct organization, force the PLO to formally recognize Israel, and set the terms of the Oslo process — and eventually its collapse.
    Yoshizumi Ishino's CRISPR repeat sequence paper, published in DecemberAn obscure Japanese microbiology paper on E. coli, unnoticed by news media, that two-and-a-half decades later became the seed of CRISPR-Cas9 and the defining biotechnology of the 21st century.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Tower Commission Faults Reagan on Iran-Contra; President Says He Was Not Fully Informed
    2. Bakker Resigns PTL Ministry Amid Sex, Hush-Money Allegations
    3. Gary Hart Quits Presidential Race After Miami Herald Report on Donna Rice
    4. 37 U.S. Sailors Killed as Iraqi Missiles Strike Frigate Stark in Persian Gulf
    5. Reagan at Brandenburg Gate: 'Mr. Gorbachev, Tear Down This Wall!'
    6. Oliver North Tells Congress He Believed Superiors Approved Diversion of Iran Funds to Contras
    7. Senate Rejects Bork for Supreme Court, 58-42, in Rebuke to Reagan
    8. Dow Plunges 508 Points in Record Rout; Wall Street's Worst Day Since 1929
    9. Baby Jessica Pulled Alive From Texas Well After 58-Hour Ordeal
    10. Reagan and Gorbachev Sign Treaty Banning Medium-Range Nuclear Missiles

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryRonald Reagan underwent prostate surgery early in the month as the 100th Congress convened with Democrats consolidating control (Jim Wright as Speaker, Robert Byrd as Senate Majority Leader). On January 17, an anti-intimidation march led by Coretta Scott King and Hosea Williams into Forsyth County, Georgia — a county that had expelled its Black residents in 1912 — drew a counter-mob and thousands of federal marshals and National Guard troops. The New York Giants won Super Bowl XXI 39-20 over the Denver Broncos on January 25 with Phil Simms as MVP. Oral Roberts made his notorious 'God will call me home' fundraising appeal, demanding $8 million by March.
    FebruaryReagan replaced Chief of Staff Donald Regan with former Senate Majority Leader Howard Baker after Nancy Reagan's behind-the-scenes intervention. The Tower Commission released its report on February 26, sharply criticizing Reagan's detached management style and confirming the arms-for-hostages diversion. On February 22 the G-6 signed the Louvre Accord attempting to stabilize the dollar. ABC broadcast the controversial Soviet-occupation miniseries 'Amerika.' Public Enemy released their debut album 'Yo! Bum Rush the Show.'
    MarchReagan delivered a nationally televised Iran-Contra address on March 4, acknowledging that 'what began as a strategic opening to Iran deteriorated, in its implementation, into trading arms for hostages.' The FDA approved AZT on March 20, the first antiretroviral drug for HIV/AIDS. Jim Bakker resigned from the PTL ministry on March 19 amid Jessica Hahn revelations. Larry Kramer founded ACT UP in New York. U2 released 'The Joshua Tree' on March 9, and Prince released 'Sign o' the Times.' Chrysler completed its $1.5 billion acquisition of American Motors. IBM introduced the PS/2 line on April 2 (announced late March).
    AprilTexaco filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on April 12 — at $34.9 billion in assets the largest U.S. corporate bankruptcy on record, driven by a $10.5 billion Pennzoil judgment. The Supreme Court decided McCleskey v. Kemp 5-4 on April 22, rejecting an equal-protection challenge to Georgia's death penalty despite statistical evidence of racial disparities. Sugar Ray Leonard beat Marvin Hagler in the 'Super Fight' on April 6. Fox launched 'Married... with Children' and 'The Tracey Ullman Show' as the network's opening salvo. Harold Washington was reelected mayor of Chicago.
    MayGary Hart withdrew from the 1988 presidential race on May 8 after the Miami Herald reported his weekend with Donna Rice. IRCA's amnesty legalization window opened May 5. An Iraqi F-1 Mirage struck the USS Stark with two Exocet missiles on May 17, killing 37 sailors. CIA Director William Casey died on May 6 following a brain-tumor operation. Reagan gave his first major public speech on AIDS on May 31. Joint televised congressional hearings on Iran-Contra began. Mike Tyson beat Pinklon Thomas to continue unifying the heavyweight titles.
    JuneReagan delivered 'Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!' at the Brandenburg Gate on June 12. Justice Lewis Powell announced his retirement on June 26. Bernhard Goetz was acquitted June 16 of attempted murder for the 1984 subway shooting. The Los Angeles Lakers beat the Boston Celtics in six games for the NBA title, with Magic Johnson as MVP. The Supreme Court struck down Louisiana's creation-science law 7-2 in Edwards v. Aguillard on June 19. Whitney Houston released 'Whitney,' the first album by a female artist to debut at #1. Brian De Palma's 'The Untouchables' opened.
    JulyReagan nominated Robert Bork on July 1; Senator Edward Kennedy responded within hours with the 'Robert Bork's America' floor speech. Oliver North testified in his Marine uniform to the joint congressional Iran-Contra committee from July 7-14. The U.S. Navy began Operation Earnest Will, reflagging and escorting Kuwaiti tankers through the Strait of Hormuz. The McKinney-Vento Homeless Assistance Act was signed July 22. Guns N' Roses released 'Appetite for Destruction' on July 21. Eric B. & Rakim released 'Paid in Full.'
    AugustThe FCC voted 4-0 on August 4 to abolish the Fairness Doctrine. On August 7, five Central American presidents signed the Esquipulas II peace agreement authored by Costa Rican President Oscar Arias. Alan Greenspan was sworn in as Federal Reserve Chairman on August 11, succeeding Paul Volcker. The Dow reached an all-time intraday high of 2722.42 on August 25. Congress passed the Competitive Equality Banking Act authorizing $10.8 billion FSLIC recapitalization. Michael Jackson released 'Bad' on August 31. Mike Tyson beat Tony Tucker on August 1 to become undisputed heavyweight champion at 21. The Pan American Games opened in Indianapolis.
    SeptemberGreenspan raised the discount rate from 5.5% to 6.0% on September 4 — his first policy action and the first discount-rate hike in more than three years. The Constitution's Bicentennial was celebrated in Philadelphia and nationwide on September 17. On the same day 24 nations signed the Montreal Protocol on ozone-depleting substances. Joe Biden withdrew from the presidential race September 23 after plagiarism revelations. Randy Shilts's 'And the Band Played On' was published. Pope John Paul II conducted a nine-city U.S. tour, including a meeting with AIDS patients in San Francisco. 'Fatal Attraction' opened.
    OctoberBaby Jessica McClure was rescued from a Texas well in a 58-hour ordeal broadcast live on CNN (October 14-16). The Dow fell 4.6% on October 14, 4.6% again on October 16, and 22.6% (508.32 points) on Black Monday, October 19 — the largest single-day percentage loss in the index's history. The Second National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights drew roughly 500,000 on October 11, with the AIDS Memorial Quilt on the National Mall for the first time. The Senate rejected Bork 58-42 on October 23. The Minnesota Twins won the World Series over St. Louis in seven games. George H.W. Bush declared his presidential candidacy October 12. Tom Wolfe's 'The Bonfire of the Vanities' was published. Bruce Springsteen released 'Tunnel of Love.'
    NovemberDouglas Ginsburg withdrew his Supreme Court nomination on November 7 after admitting past marijuana use; Reagan nominated Anthony Kennedy on November 11. Chicago Mayor Harold Washington died of a heart attack on November 25; Alderman Eugene Sawyer was selected interim mayor after a contentious Council session. Cuban detainees at Oakdale, Louisiana (November 21) and Atlanta (November 23) staged coordinated uprisings over a repatriation agreement. Caspar Weinberger resigned as Secretary of Defense on November 23, succeeded by Frank Carlucci. The joint congressional Iran-Contra report was released. Baker-led budget summit negotiations produced a two-year deficit-reduction framework. The Tawana Brawley case emerged in Wappingers Falls, New York.
    DecemberReagan and Gorbachev signed the INF Treaty at the White House on December 8, eliminating an entire class of nuclear missiles. The First Palestinian Intifada began December 9 following a fatal traffic collision in Jabalia refugee camp. The MV Dona Paz ferry sank in the Philippines on December 20, killing roughly 4,000 people — the deadliest peacetime maritime disaster in history. Yoshizumi Ishino published his description of the CRISPR repeat sequence in E. coli. Microsoft shipped Windows 2.0 on December 9; IBM shipped OS/2 1.0. Oliver Stone's 'Wall Street' opened, giving the culture Gordon Gekko's 'greed is good' speech. Larry Flynt v. Falwell was argued at the Supreme Court.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~3.5% real (later BEA vintages; contemporary print was closer to 2.9% before revision)
    InflationCPI +4.4% Dec/Dec; annual average ~3.65%; core CPI ~4.2%
    Unemployment5.7% in December (down from 6.6% in January); ~6.2% annual average
    S&P 500 return+5.25% total return (~+2.0% price)
    Dow return+2.3% price (close 1938.83 vs 1895.95); intraday high 2722.42 on August 25; -22.6% on October 19
    Fed funds rate~6.0% at start of year, ~7.3% in early October, ~6.75% at year-end; discount rate raised from 5.5% to 6.0% on September 4
    Oil priceWTI ~$17/barrel at year-end (recovered from 1986 lows near $10; intra-year peak above $22)
    Gold price~$484/oz at year-end (vs ~$391 at end-1986; ~+24%)
    Population~242 million
    Median home priceExisting-home median ~$85,600; new-home median ~$104,500-$111,000
    National debt~$2.43 trillion at calendar year-end (FY1987 ending September 30: ~$2.35 trillion); FY1987 deficit $149.7 billion

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 1987 was to live inside a whiplash — a year that began with a wounded president, a diminished Reagan whose Iran-Contra weekend TV briefings looked more haggard than reassuring, and ended with that same president standing in the East Room shaking Mikhail Gorbachev's hand as they signed away an entire class of nuclear weapons. Between those bookends the country cycled through a summer of televised congressional hearings starring Oliver North in his Marine uniform, a spring rush of Bakker jokes, a May morning when Gary Hart's presidential campaign died in a Miami Herald stakeout, a June ruling that gave Bernhard Goetz a walk on the shooting charges, a July stretch when the Dow blasted past 2700 and yuppies felt untouchable, and an October Monday when 22.6% of the stock market's value vanished in a single afternoon and, three days later, Jessica McClure was pulled alive from a Texas well while three networks broke into primetime and Reagan called every American her godparent. The mood was gossipy, chastened, still prosperous, and increasingly aware that the Reagan era had a shelf life.

    Yet ordinary life had a specific texture. You paid a 9%-and-rising mortgage rate if you were buying a house, watched compact discs finally overtake vinyl at the record store, saw a camcorder near the top of your family's Christmas list, and rented a movie on VHS to play through a set-top box. You watched 'The Cosby Show' with 25 million other households on Thursday nights, or the new 'Married... with Children' on the strange fourth network your cable box had just started to carry. If you were young and cared about music, U2's 'The Joshua Tree' and Guns N' Roses' 'Appetite for Destruction' fought for your stereo with Michael Jackson's 'Bad' and Whitney Houston's 'Whitney,' and — if you were paying close attention — Public Enemy's first record hinted at what was coming. If you were gay in New York or San Francisco or Los Angeles, 1987 was the year AIDS killed more of your friends than the year before, the year AZT was finally approved, and the year ACT UP told you that grief could be organized into action.

    Under the noise, the country was rewiring itself. An FCC vote no one paid attention to in August was quietly authorizing Rush Limbaugh. Alan Greenspan was quietly beginning what would become an eighteen-and-a-half-year Fed tenure. Gorbachev was quietly ending the Cold War. The First Intifada was quietly starting in Gaza. It was a year that felt like scandal and near-catastrophe; it turned out to be a year of foundations.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    1988
    Econ +2Mark +2Poli +1Cult +1Over +1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    1988 was the coronation year of the Reagan era that history now reads as a hinge. George H.W. Bush's 40-state, 426-electoral-vote landslide over Michael Dukakis capped a third consecutive Republican presidential win and appeared to confirm a durable conservative lock on the White House; it would prove to be the last true landslide of the modern era. The Cold War visibly began to end with the INF Treaty entering force on June 1, Reagan's celebrated Moscow Summit, Soviet troops beginning their withdrawal from Afghanistan on May 15, and Gorbachev's December UN speech pledging a unilateral 500,000-troop cut. At home, the economy hummed at 4.2 percent real growth with unemployment at a 14-year low, but the Greenspan Fed spent the year fighting overheating that would ultimately produce the 1990-91 recession.

    Beneath the noise, 1988 quietly seeded the next four decades. James Hansen's June 23 Senate testimony brought climate change into mainstream American politics. Osama bin Laden and Ayman al-Zawahiri founded al-Qaeda in a Peshawar guesthouse. Robert Morris's self-replicating worm crippled a large slice of the still-tiny internet and gave birth to cybersecurity as an institutional discipline. Anthony Kennedy joined the Supreme Court 97-0 and would define American law on gay rights, campaign finance, and abortion for thirty years. Prozac reached pharmacy shelves and began the SSRI era. RJR Nabisco's $25 billion KKR buyout crowned the LBO wave that had already peaked, and Drexel Burnham Lambert agreed in December to plead guilty and pay $650 million in fines.

    Culturally the year was rich but morally weary. Public Enemy, N.W.A., and Tracy Chapman remade popular music; Rain Man, Die Hard, and Roseanne remade Hollywood and prime-time television; Kirk Gibson's limping walk-off home run, Doug Williams's Super Bowl, Flo-Jo's still-standing world records, Ben Johnson's disqualification, and Wayne Gretzky's trade to Los Angeles supplied one of the richest sports years in modern memory. Yet the Willie Horton ad, the Jimmy Swaggart confession, the AIDS crisis, the S&L slow-motion collapse, the Pan Am 103 bombing over Lockerbie, and Time's naming Endangered Earth as Person of the Year all pointed to a country that was quietly ready, without quite knowing it, for a change.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+2A late-cycle year of 4%+ real growth with unemployment at a 14-year low and clear overheating pressures managed by a decisive but not yet punitive Fed tightening cycle.
    Markets+2Solid double-digit total returns across all major indices with subsided post-crash volatility and 36% earnings growth, tempered by thin retail volume and a still-scarred investor psyche.
    Politics+1A clean transition and productive lame-duck legislation with the INF Treaty ratified, offset by Iran-Contra prosecutions, Meese's forced resignation, the Vincennes tragedy, and an ugly campaign.
    Culture+1A creatively strong year for music, film, and television tempered by televangelist scandals, the Willie Horton ad, Pan Am 103, and a diffuse late-Reagan-era exhaustion.
    Overall Mood+1An anxious-but-comfortable Morning-in-America afterglow of prosperity, peace-through-strength Cold War thaw, and Reagan's 63% exit approval, shadowed by AIDS, crack, homelessness, and a joyless campaign.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Bush Elected 41st President in 40-State Landslide Over Dukakis
    2. INF Treaty Enters Force; Reagan and Gorbachev Toast New Era at Moscow Summit
    3. Pan Am 747 Destroyed by Terror Bomb Over Lockerbie; 270 Dead
    4. USS Vincennes Downs Iran Air 655 in Persian Gulf; 290 Civilians Killed
    5. KKR Wins RJR Nabisco in Record $25 Billion Buyout
    6. Yellowstone Ablaze: 793,000 Acres Burn as 9,000 Firefighters Battle Fires
    7. Soviet Troops Begin Long March Home From Afghanistan Under Geneva Accords
    8. Ben Johnson Stripped of Olympic Gold After Positive Steroid Test
    9. Kirk Gibson's Walk-Off Home Run Powers Dodgers Past A's in Game 1
    10. Iran-Contra Grand Jury Indicts North, Poindexter, Secord, and Hakim

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The US economy in 1988 delivered one of the strongest full years of the Reagan expansion, with real GDP growing roughly 4.2 percent, payrolls adding more than 3 million jobs, and unemployment falling from 5.7 percent in January to 5.3 percent in December, the lowest jobless rate since May 1974. Growth was broadly based, driven by business fixed investment, a weaker dollar boosting exports as a delayed payoff from the 1985 Plaza Accord, and still-firm consumer spending. But the economy was visibly running hot: capacity utilization pushed above 84 percent, wage growth accelerated, and headline CPI drifted from roughly 4.1 percent on average to about 4.4 percent December-over-December, with core CPI near 4.7 percent. Alan Greenspan's Fed, in his first full year as Chair, pivoted decisively to tightening, lifting the federal funds rate from roughly 6.5 percent early in the year to about 8.75 percent by year-end and raising the discount rate for the first time since 1984. Housing cooled, with 30-year mortgages averaging 10.3 percent and starts sliding to roughly 1.49 million units. The federal deficit narrowed to about $155 billion, the trade deficit began meaningfully narrowing, and the national debt closed FY1988 near $2.60 trillion.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth ~4.2%; CPI ~4.1% annual/4.4% Dec-Dec; core CPI ~4.7%; unemployment 5.3% December; fed funds rate ~8.75% year-end; 10-year Treasury ~9.1% year-end; 30-year mortgage ~10.3% average; median existing home price ~$89,300; housing starts ~1.49 million; personal saving rate ~4.3%; federal deficit ~$155 billion (FY1988); national debt ~$2.60 trillion (FY-end).

    Major Events

    The Greenspan Fed's aggressive tightening cycle established the new Chair's inflation-fighting credibility while planting seeds of the 1990-91 recession. The historic Midwest drought, the worst since the Dust Bowl, cut the corn crop by roughly 30 percent and spiked grain prices mid-year. The Basel I Accord was signed in July, establishing the first internationally coordinated 8 percent bank capital standard. The S&L crisis deepened materially: the FSLIC was itself insolvent, more than 200 thrifts failed or were resolved, and the Southwest Plan packaged and sold dozens of insolvent Texas S&Ls with tax subsidies, foreshadowing FIRREA and the RTC of 1989. Bush's election, delivered on the read my lips no new taxes pledge, framed early Bush-era fiscal politics. And the trade deficit began a meaningful narrowing as post-Plaza dollar depreciation worked through.

    Why this score

    Score +2: A late-cycle year with 4%+ growth, 5.3% unemployment, and clear overheating was excellent by the era's baseline despite the coming inflation cost, and among the best years of the Reagan expansion in level terms.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    Financial markets in 1988 were dominated by recovery from Black Monday and the LBO boom. The S&P 500 delivered a total return of roughly 16.6 percent (about 12.4 percent price plus dividends), the Dow returned about 16 percent total (11.85 percent price), and the NASDAQ Composite returned about 15.4 percent. But breadth and volume told a more cautious story: NYSE volume fell sharply from 1987's frenzied levels, retail participation collapsed after the crash, and stocks spent much of the year simply reclaiming pre-crash levels (the Dow did not decisively surpass its August 1987 high until August 1989). Volatility fell dramatically from extraordinary 1987 readings. Ten-year Treasuries ended the year near 9.1 percent as the yield curve flattened sharply on Fed tightening. WTI crude closed near $17 with OPEC discipline breaking down; gold drifted from $485 in January to roughly $410 by year-end as real rates rose. The character of the year was a subdued, institutionally led melt-up in a market still psychologically scarred, with S&P 500 operating earnings up roughly 36 percent more than absorbing multiple compression.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    The RJR Nabisco leveraged buyout was the defining deal: KKR's winning $25 billion bid in late November was the largest LBO in history and remains the era's emblem, immortalized in Barbarians at the Gate. Philip Morris's $13.1 billion acquisition of Kraft in October was the largest non-oil M&A deal on record. Grand Metropolitan's hostile $5.7 billion takeover of Pillsbury and Campeau Corporation's disastrous $6.6 billion acquisition of Federated Department Stores rounded out the takeover mania. Junk-bond issuance from Drexel Burnham Lambert peaked even as the SEC and US Attorney Rudy Giuliani closed in; Drexel agreed in December to plead guilty to six felony counts and pay $650 million, effectively beginning its terminal decline. Michael Milken would be indicted in March 1989. Texaco emerged from Chapter 11 in April after settling with Pennzoil. In technology, Steve Jobs unveiled NeXT and Apple's Macintosh II and SE/30 anchored the creative-class desktop.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant investment themes were leveraged-buyout finance, junk bonds, food-and-tobacco consolidation, hostile M&A, and Japanese asset accumulation as the Nikkei accelerated toward its December 1989 peak. Institutional investors dominated flows as retail participation collapsed after Black Monday, and portfolio insurance strategies fell out of favor. Fed tightening drove flight into short-duration assets, and the flattening yield curve pushed sophisticated investors toward event-driven and merger-arb strategies where earnings and deal spreads offered clearer edge than duration risk.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The dominant political event was Vice President George H.W. Bush's November 8 defeat of Massachusetts Governor Michael Dukakis, 426-111 in the Electoral College with 53.4 percent of the popular vote, making him the first sitting VP elected president since Van Buren in 1836. Second was ratification and entry into force of the INF Treaty on June 1, the first Cold War arms accord to eliminate an entire class of nuclear weapons, capped by Reagan's fourth summit with Gorbachev in Moscow May 29 to June 2. Iran-Contra indictments came down against Oliver North, John Poindexter, Richard Secord, and Albert Hakim on March 16. Operation Praying Mantis on April 18 was the largest US surface engagement since World War II, followed by the July 3 USS Vincennes shootdown of Iran Air 655 (290 civilians killed). Attorney General Meese resigned in July amid the Wedtech Independent Counsel report; Dick Thornburgh was unanimously confirmed as successor. Anthony Kennedy joined the Supreme Court 97-0. Reagan signed the Civil Liberties Act on August 10 granting reparations to interned Japanese Americans. Federal grand juries indicted Panama's Manuel Noriega in February. Pan Am Flight 103 was destroyed over Lockerbie on December 21.

    Domestic Policy

    Reagan's final year pivoted from Iran-Contra wounds toward legacy-building, and the lame-duck partnership with the Democratic 100th Congress produced a substantial legislative haul. The Family Support Act of October 13, driven by Senator Moynihan, restructured AFDC to require work, education, or training via the new JOBS program and strengthened child-support enforcement. The Fair Housing Amendments Act of September 13 added disability and familial status to federal fair-housing protections. The Civil Rights Restoration Act, enacted in March over Reagan's veto, reversed Grove City College and restored broad institution-wide anti-discrimination coverage. The Anti-Drug Abuse Act of November 18 created the drug czar, restored the federal death penalty for drug-kingpin murders, and imposed the 100-to-1 crack-versus-powder sentencing disparity. The Omnibus Trade and Competitiveness Act of August 23 included the Super 301 provision. The Medicare Catastrophic Coverage Act of July 1 was later repealed after senior backlash.
