Amazon.com went public on May 15 at $18 a share, raising about $54 million and valuing the company near $438 million. Osama bin Laden gave his first Western television interview to CNN's Peter Arnett and Peter Bergen in an Afghan mountain camp, declaring war on the United States. On May 27, the Supreme Court in Clinton v. Jones unanimously ruled a sitting president had no immunity from civil suits over pre-office conduct, and Clinton signed the NATO-Russia Founding Act in Paris. The Lost World: Jurassic Park opened.
    JuneRadiohead released OK Computer, quickly recognized as the decade's most important rock album. Timothy McVeigh was convicted June 2 and sentenced to death June 13 for the Oklahoma City bombing. On June 11 Michael Jordan's Chicago Bulls won their fifth NBA title with the flu game, and later closed out the Utah Jazz 4-2. State attorneys general and tobacco companies announced a proposed $368.5 billion global settlement on June 20. Clinton launched the President's Initiative on Race on June 14 at UC San Diego. The Supreme Court decided Reno v. ACLU, Printz v. United States, City of Boerne v. Flores, and the assisted-suicide cases. On June 28, Mike Tyson was disqualified in Las Vegas for biting a piece of Evander Holyfield's ear.
    JulyHong Kong reverted to Chinese sovereignty on July 1, ending 156 years of British rule; Albright led the U.S. delegation. On July 2 Thailand devalued the baht, launching the Asian financial crisis. Mars Pathfinder landed on July 4, and the Sojourner rover beamed back iconic images. The NATO Madrid Summit on July 8-9 invited Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic. Gianni Versace was murdered on the steps of his Miami Beach mansion on July 15 by Andrew Cunanan, who killed himself eight days later. GOP leaders' coup attempt against Gingrich collapsed; Bill Paxon resigned July 17. The Dow crossed 8,000 on July 16. The Senate passed Byrd-Hagel 95-0 on July 25 against any Kyoto treaty exempting developing countries. Lilith Fair launched.
    AugustThe UPS Teamsters walked off the job for fifteen days in a strike widely read as a labor-side win. On August 1, Boeing closed its $13 billion acquisition of McDonnell Douglas. Microsoft on August 6 invested $150 million in a near-bankrupt Apple and announced Office for Mac. On August 5, Clinton signed the Balanced Budget Act and the Taxpayer Relief Act. South Park premiered on Comedy Central on August 13. Princess Diana was killed in a Paris car crash on August 31, producing an unprecedented American media response. Consumer confidence hit 134.5, the highest since 1969. Netflix was founded on August 29 in Scotts Valley, California.
    SeptemberMother Teresa died in Calcutta on September 5, five days after Diana, compounding a week of global mourning; Diana's funeral drew an estimated 2.5 billion global viewers on September 6 and Elton John's Candle in the Wind 1997 became the best-selling single in U.S. history to that point. Steve Jobs was named interim CEO of Apple on September 16. Seinfeld's ninth and final season began on NBC. Sinn Fein was admitted to Northern Ireland peace talks in Stormont. Jiang Zemin's preparations for his October state visit dominated diplomatic pages.
    OctoberPromise Keepers' Stand in the Gap rally drew an estimated 600,000 to 800,000 Christian men to the National Mall on October 4, one of the largest gatherings in D.C. history. Boogie Nights opened. On October 20-23, Hong Kong's stock market crashed on Asian-crisis contagion. On October 25 the Million Woman March filled Philadelphia. Jiang Zemin began the first Chinese state visit to Washington since Tiananmen on October 26. On Monday October 27 the Dow fell 554.26 points, a 7.18 percent single-day loss and then the largest in history, triggering NYSE trading curbs and an early close; the next day it rebounded 337 points on record billion-share volume. Saddam Hussein began expelling American UNSCOM inspectors. Dean Smith retired from UNC as men's Division I basketball's winningest coach.
    NovemberCongress declined to renew Clinton's fast-track trade authority, a defeat delivered by his own party. WorldCom launched a hostile $37 billion bid for MCI on November 10, then the largest takeover in history. Shania Twain released Come On Over. The Florida Marlins beat the Cleveland Indians in seven games to win the World Series in their fifth season of existence, the fastest expansion team ever to do so. Rudy Giuliani won re-election as New York City mayor over Ruth Messinger by about 58 to 41 percent; Christine Todd Whitman narrowly won re-election in New Jersey; Jim Gilmore's No Car Tax pledge carried Virginia. The Louise Woodward nanny trial verdict dominated cable.
    DecemberSouth Korea required a then-record $57 billion IMF-led package to contain its financial crisis. On December 11 Vice President Gore signed the Kyoto Protocol in Japan, which Clinton never submitted for ratification. The WTO Financial Services Agreement opened global banking, insurance, and securities markets. Titanic opened on December 19, launching what would become the highest-grossing film of all time. Good Will Hunting, As Good as It Gets, and Jackie Brown opened alongside it. Terry Nichols was convicted December 23 of conspiracy and manslaughter (not murder) in the Oklahoma City bombing. Microsoft acquired Hotmail. Intel's Andy Grove was named TIME Person of the Year. The S&P 500 closed at 970.43, up 33.4 percent on total return for the year.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+4.5% real (BEA, calendar year 1997)
    InflationCPI-U +1.7% Dec/Dec; +2.3% annual average; core CPI +2.2% Dec/Dec
    Unemployment4.7% December; 4.9% annual average (down from 5.3% January)
    S&P 500 return+33.4% total return; +31.0% price (close 970.43)
    Dow return+22.6% price; ~+24.9% total return (close 7,908.25)
    Fed funds rate5.50% target at year-end (one 25 bp hike, March 25)
    Oil price~$17.60/bbl WTI year-end (down from ~$25 at start of year)
    Gold price~$290/oz year-end (down ~21% from ~$369)
    Population~267 million (Census, growing ~1% annually)
    Median home priceN/A
    National debt~$5.41 trillion gross federal debt end-FY1997; ~$5.5 trillion at calendar year-end; FY1997 deficit only $21.9 billion (0.3% of GDP)

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 1997 was to feel, most of the time, that the country had already won the argument. The Cold War was six years gone, the deficit was disappearing on its own, the Dow kept crossing new round numbers, and unemployment kept sliding to levels most working adults had never seen. The Clinton second term opened in January with an inaugural address about the bridge to the twenty-first century and a Democrat cabinet stacked with new faces, Madeleine Albright, William Cohen, Andrew Cuomo, Bill Richardson, Alexis Herman. By August a Democratic president and a Republican Congress that had spent 1995 and 1996 tearing each other apart had signed a bipartisan budget deal delivering a capital-gains cut, a child credit, Roth IRAs, education credits, and children's health insurance. Consumer confidence was the highest since Nixon's first term. In most kitchens and most 401(k) statements, life was quietly, undeniably better than it had been five years earlier.

    The texture of the year, though, was as much tabloid as macro. Americans spent much of it staring at spectacles that had nothing to do with GDP: Tiger Woods walking to a twelve-shot Masters win at twenty-one, Michael Jordan's flu game, thirty-nine bodies in Rancho Santa Fe under purple shrouds, a bank-robbery firefight in North Hollywood, Mike Tyson spitting out a piece of Evander Holyfield's ear, Andrew Cunanan stalking Gianni Versace to the steps of his Miami Beach mansion, Ellen DeGeneres saying yep, I'm gay on Oprah, the JonBenet Ramsey tabloid noise, and above all the last week of August, when the wreckage of a Mercedes in a Paris tunnel and the death of Mother Teresa five days later produced a jag of televised mourning that felt like it lasted a month. The country was prosperous, distracted, and increasingly comfortable watching its own life on cable.

    Underneath, quietly, the future was arriving. Amazon priced its IPO in May at $18. Reed Hastings incorporated Netflix in August. Steve Jobs came back to Apple in September and Michael Dell said shut it down. Deep Blue beat Kasparov. The Supreme Court said a sitting president could be sued and, separately, that the internet was fully protected speech. In an Afghan cave Osama bin Laden told Peter Arnett of CNN that Americans were now legitimate targets. In Kyoto the Senate killed U.S. climate policy on a preemptive 95-0 vote. On July 1, Hong Kong went home to China. If you were paying close attention, you could feel the ground shifting; almost no one was paying that kind of attention. For most Americans, 1997 was the year the country felt richer than it ever had, the news was mostly about famous people dying or misbehaving, and the biggest change in daily life was that everyone suddenly had an email address.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    1998
    Econ +3Mark +2Poli -1Cult +2Over +2
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    Nineteen ninety-eight was a hinge year masquerading as a scandal year. On the surface, it was the finest year of the Clinton boom: 4.5 percent real GDP growth, 1.6 percent inflation, unemployment at 4.4 percent, the first federal budget surplus since 1969, and equities near all-time highs, capped by a NASDAQ that returned nearly 40 percent and an S&P 500 that returned 28.6 percent on total return. Yet the story the country actually watched was a presidential sex-and-perjury drama that began with a Drudge Report scoop in January, ripened into the September release of the Starr Report on the young World Wide Web, and culminated on December 19 in only the second presidential impeachment in American history.

    Beneath the noise, the year was quietly detonating three futures. The August 7 al-Qaeda bombings of the US embassies in Kenya and Tanzania, followed by Osama bin Laden's placement on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list and the Operation Infinite Reach cruise-missile strikes on Sudan and Afghanistan, marked the opening battle of what would become the global war on terror. On September 4, Larry Page and Sergey Brin incorporated Google in a Menlo Park garage, an event that attracted no press coverage. And the near-collapse of Long-Term Capital Management, coordinated-rescued by the New York Fed on September 23, previewed the systemic-risk dynamics of 2008. India and Pakistan tested nuclear weapons within weeks of each other in May, and Congress passed the Digital Millennium Copyright Act whose Section 512 safe harbor would become the legal scaffolding of the entire user-generated-content economy.

    The public mood, according to every contemporaneous poll, was extraordinary. Consumer confidence hit a then-record 138 in June, Americans told pollsters they felt better about the country than at almost any point in decades, and Clinton's approval ratings paradoxically rose during his impeachment because voters disliked his behavior but hated the pursuit more. Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa spent the summer chasing Roger Maris, Michael Jordan hit his Last Shot in June, the 114-win Yankees swept October, and the Titanic soundtrack sold like nothing else. It was a prosperous, distracted, and slightly embarrassed year, the last full year of the end-of-history mood before the actual twenty-first century arrived on the scene.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+3A top-decile year even by 1990s standards, combining 4.5% growth, 1.6% inflation, 4.4% unemployment, and the first federal surplus in a generation, with no domestic recession despite the worst emerging-market crisis since the early 1980s.
    Markets+2Outstanding headline returns (S&P +28.6%, NASDAQ +39.6%, third straight 20%-plus year) and a historic Fed-rescue rebound, tempered by the brutal August-October drawdown, a negative Russell 2000, and famously narrow breadth.
    Politics-1The second presidential impeachment in US history, plus a Speaker's resignation and his successor's resignation, produced real institutional stress, but a booming economy, orderly midterm, bipartisan legislative output (TEA-21, DMCA, NATO enlargement), and the Good Friday Agreement kept it well short of true crisis territory.
    Culture+2A vibrant year with landmark works from The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill and Saving Private Ryan to Sex and the City and The Big Lebowski, the founding of Google and launch of the iMac, and a historic home-run chase, offset modestly by the tabloid-impeachment atmosphere and a wave of hate crimes and school shootings.
    Overall Mood+2Americans by mid-year told pollsters they felt better about the country than at almost any point in decades, and Clinton's approval rose during impeachment; the mood was prosperous, distracted, and slightly embarrassed but on net unusually good.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Clinton Denies Affair With White House Intern; Starr Investigation Widens
    2. House Impeaches President Clinton on Perjury, Obstruction of Justice
    3. Terror Bombs Destroy US Embassies in Kenya and Tanzania; 224 Dead
    4. US Launches Cruise Missile Strikes on Sudan and Afghanistan
    5. Mark McGwire Passes Roger Maris With 62nd Home Run; Finishes With 70
    6. India and Pakistan Conduct Rival Nuclear Tests; World Reacts
    7. Democrats Defy History With House Gains; Gingrich Resigns as Speaker
    8. Fed Orchestrates $3.6 Billion Wall Street Rescue of Long-Term Capital
    9. Good Friday Peace Accord Reached in Northern Ireland
    10. Federal Budget Posts First Surplus Since 1969

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    Nineteen ninety-eight was, on the surface, the finest year of the Clinton boom: 4.5 percent real GDP growth, 1.6 percent year-end CPI inflation, a 4.4 percent December unemployment rate, and a roughly $69 billion federal surplus that was the first since 1969. Beneath the placid headline, the year was punctuated by the most dangerous financial episode of the decade, as Russia's August 17 default and the September near-collapse of Long-Term Capital Management briefly threatened to freeze global credit and forced the Federal Reserve into three back-to-back rate cuts. The year ended with equities near all-time highs, oil near $11 a barrel, mortgage rates below 7 percent, and the American consumer utterly ignoring both the emerging-market crisis and the impeachment of a sitting president. Housing had its best year of the decade with existing-home sales above 4.9 million units and a record 66.3 percent homeownership rate, and payrolls added roughly 3 million jobs.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth 4.5%; CPI 1.6% year-end; unemployment 4.4% in December (4.3% low in April); nonfarm payrolls +3 million; federal fiscal 1998 surplus roughly $69 billion; federal funds rate cut from 5.50% to 4.75%; 30-year fixed mortgage averaging 6.94% (Freddie Mac); NAR median existing-home price roughly $128,400; WTI crude ending near $11-12 after averaging $20 a year earlier; current-account deficit blowing out toward $220 billion; Conference Board consumer confidence hitting a then-record 138 in June; personal saving rate falling toward zero.

    Major Events

    The federal budget swung into surplus for the first time in a generation on capital-gains-fed receipts and the 1997 Balanced Budget Act. The Asian financial crisis that began in 1997 continued to gut US exports to East Asia and collapse commodity prices, keeping inflation at its lowest year-end reading since 1986. On August 17 Russia defaulted on ruble-denominated debt and devalued, triggering a global flight to quality that detonated LTCM, whose $125 billion of levered positions were wound down September 23 by a New York Fed-convened rescue of $3.625 billion from fourteen creditor banks. Alan Greenspan's FOMC cut rates 25 basis points three times (September 29, October 15 inter-meeting, and November 17), moving the target from 5.50 percent to 4.75 percent. Oil collapsed as OPEC discipline broke and Asian demand vanished, with WTI bottoming near $10.72 in mid-December. Housing set records for existing-home sales and starts, and the American consumer ignored the entire scare.

    Why this score

    Even judged against the standards of the roaring 1990s, 1998 was a top-decile year with elite growth, tame inflation, near-full employment, the first federal surplus in a generation, and no domestic recession despite the worst emerging-market crisis since the early 1980s, so it earns the maximum score.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    Headline returns were outstanding but the tape was violent. The S&P 500 rallied roughly 22 percent from January through an intraday high near 1,190 on July 17, then fell about 19 percent by an October 8 low near 959 on Russian default and LTCM fears; from that low through year-end the index rose more than 28 percent on the strength of three Fed rate cuts, closing 1998 at 1,229.23 for a 26.7 percent price return and 28.6 percent total return. The Dow finished at 9,181.43 for a 16.1 percent price return, and the NASDAQ Composite closed at 2,192.69 for a 39.6 percent gain, its third consecutive year of 20-percent-plus returns and the year in which 'dot-com' moved from jargon to household word. On August 31 the Dow dropped 512.61 points (6.4 percent) to 7,539, the second-largest point drop in Dow history at the time. Treasuries rallied hard with the 10-year yield falling from about 5.75 percent to roughly 4.65 percent, and credit spreads gapped wide in the fall and only partially recovered.

    Character

    Crash-then-recovery

    Major Companies

    The M&A wave crested with the announcement in April of the $70-plus billion Citicorp-Travelers combination to create Citigroup (which would force repeal of Glass-Steagall the following year), the NationsBank-BankAmerica merger to form the new Bank of America, BP-Amoco in August, the Daimler-Benz and Chrysler close in November, Deutsche Bank agreeing to buy Bankers Trust in late November, and on December 1 the largest merger ever at the time, Exxon's roughly $80 billion acquisition of Mobil, a direct response to the oil price collapse. Market leadership was famously narrow, carried by roughly fifty mega-cap growth and technology names including Microsoft, Cisco, Intel, GE, Wal-Mart, Lucent, AOL, and Dell, while the Russell 2000 finished the year down about 3.4 percent. IPO mania set records: eBay priced on September 24 at $18 and closed the day at $53.50, Broadcast.com tripled on day one in July, and on November 13 theglobe.com posted a 606 percent first-day gain, the largest in history to that point. Amazon rose roughly nine-fold on the year and AOL rose about six-fold and joined the S&P 500 in December.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant themes were narrow-breadth mega-cap growth leadership, the acceleration of the dot-com bubble in internet stocks, a global flight to quality that crushed emerging-market and high-yield credit while sending Treasury yields to generational lows, and the Fed's rediscovered willingness to backstop private counterparties in a crisis, a doctrine that would shape the next two decades. Under the surface, more than half the stocks in the S&P 500 were below their 200-day moving average even as the cap-weighted index made new highs, and the LTCM rescue previewed the too-big-to-fail dynamic that would define the 2008 response.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The Clinton-Lewinsky scandal consumed the year from the January 17 breaking of the story through Clinton's January 26 televised denial ('I did not have sexual relations with that woman'), his August 17 grand jury testimony and same-night admission of an 'inappropriate' relationship, the September 11 web release of the Starr Report, and culminating in the December 19 House vote to approve two articles of impeachment, making Clinton only the second president in American history to be impeached. Days after the November 3 midterms, in which Democrats gained five House seats (the first sixth-year-of-a-presidency gain since 1934), Speaker Newt Gingrich resigned and his designated successor Bob Livingston resigned on the same day as the impeachment vote over his own affairs, elevating Dennis Hastert. The August 7 al-Qaeda embassy bombings in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam killed 224 including 12 Americans and prompted the August 20 Operation Infinite Reach cruise-missile strikes on Sudan and Afghanistan. Operation Desert Fox from December 16 to 19 bombed Iraq for obstructing UN weapons inspectors. The Good Friday Agreement of April 10, negotiated with former Senator George Mitchell as chair and Clinton's personal diplomacy, largely ended the Troubles in Northern Ireland. The November 23 Tobacco Master Settlement Agreement, at $206 billion, was the largest civil litigation settlement in US history.

    Domestic Policy

    Clinton's January State of the Union, delivered a week into the Lewinsky story, was a political triumph organized around 'Save Social Security First' and produced an approval spike into the high 60s. The year's biggest bipartisan legislative accomplishment was the Transportation Equity Act for the 21st Century (TEA-21), a six-year $218 billion surface transportation reauthorization signed June 9 and the largest public-works bill in US history to that date. Congress also passed the Workforce Investment Act, the Internet Tax Freedom Act imposing a three-year moratorium on new state and local internet-access taxes, the Digital Millennium Copyright Act signed October 28 whose Section 512 safe harbor would become the legal scaffolding for the entire user-generated-content economy, the Higher Education Amendments, and the IRS Restructuring and Reform Act. A late push for tobacco regulation via the McCain bill collapsed in the Senate in June under a $40 million industry ad campaign. The Supreme Court in Clinton v. City of New York struck down the Line Item Veto Act, in Faragher and Burlington Industries significantly clarified employer liability for workplace sexual harassment, and in Oncale unanimously held that same-sex sexual harassment is actionable under Title VII. The Court's nine-member composition remained unchanged, its longest span without personnel change in over a century.

    Foreign Policy

    Foreign policy was dominated by three theaters and one bright spot. In Iraq, Saddam Hussein's escalating obstruction of UNSCOM inspections produced a rolling crisis, prompting the October 31 Iraq Liberation Act making regime change formal US policy and, after chief inspector Richard Butler reported continued non-cooperation in December, the four-day Operation Desert Fox in which some 415 cruise missiles and 600 bomber sorties struck suspected WMD sites; UNSCOM did not return for four years. Al-Qaeda emerged in February with Osama bin Laden's World Islamic Front fatwa calling for attacks on Americans, then executed the August 7 embassy bombings, drawing the August 20 Operation Infinite Reach strikes and a November SDNY grand jury indictment. In the Balkans, Serbian forces under Milošević launched a brutal Kosovo crackdown that Richard Holbrooke temporarily contained in October before the Račak massacre reignited the crisis in January 1999. Treasury Secretary Rubin, Deputy Larry Summers, and Fed Chair Greenspan (the 'Committee to Save the World') managed Asian and Russian financial contagion. India and Pakistan conducted competing nuclear tests in May, triggering Glenn Amendment sanctions. Clinton made a nine-day state visit to China in June-July, the first since Tiananmen. The Wye River Memorandum was signed October 23 by Netanyahu and Arafat. The Senate ratified NATO enlargement to Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic on April 30 by 80-19. The signature diplomatic triumph was the April 10 Good Friday Agreement.

    Important Elections

    The November 3 midterms produced results extraordinary by any historical measure: Democrats picked up a net five House seats (the first sixth-year gain for a president's party since 1822 and the first midterm gain of any kind since 1934), leaving the chamber roughly 223 Republicans to 211 Democrats and one Independent (Bernie Sanders), while the Senate ended 55-45 Republican with no net change. Charles Schumer ousted Al D'Amato in New York, John Edwards defeated Lauch Faircloth in North Carolina, and Peter Fitzgerald ousted Carol Moseley Braun in Illinois. In gubernatorial races, Jeb Bush was elected governor of Florida and his brother George W. Bush was re-elected in Texas by a 68-31 landslide that immediately propelled him to front-runner status for the 2000 Republican nomination; Democrat Gray Davis won California; and Jesse Ventura, the Reform Party candidate and former professional wrestler, stunned the political world by winning the Minnesota governorship with 37 percent in a three-way race. The consensus interpretation was unambiguous: voters had punished the Republican impeachment drive, rewarded the booming economy, and signaled disgust with the expanding Starr investigation. Gingrich resigned within 72 hours.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Nineteen ninety-eight was a strong year at the box office, split neatly between adult prestige and populist spectacle. Steven Spielberg's Saving Private Ryan, released July 24, reset the standard for war-movie realism with its Omaha Beach opening and earned $216 million domestically to lead the year; it would win Spielberg his second Best Director Oscar but famously lose Best Picture to Shakespeare in Love. Armageddon and There's Something About Mary rounded out the top three, with the Farrelly brothers' gross-out sensibility becoming a signature of the year. Other major hits included A Bug's Life (Pixar), The Waterboy, Doctor Dolittle, Rush Hour (the Jackie Chan and Chris Tucker breakout), Deep Impact, and the notorious Godzilla flop. Culturally significant releases piled up: The Big Lebowski went from modest performer to instant cult status, Wes Anderson broke through with Rushmore, Jim Carrey took his first serious turn in The Truman Show (a strikingly prescient meditation on media saturation), and Pleasantville, American History X, Happiness, Elizabeth, Life Is Beautiful, and Steven Soderbergh's comeback Out of Sight all made marks. James Cameron's Titanic, released the prior December, tied Ben-Hur's record with 11 Oscars at the March 23 ceremony and Cameron's 'I'm the king of the world!' became a lasting cultural moment as the film crossed $600 million domestically by midyear.

    Music

    Total album sales were near their all-time peak, and the year's number one seller was the Titanic soundtrack driven by Celine Dion's inescapable 'My Heart Will Go On.' Teen pop broke wide open with multi-platinum debuts from the Backstreet Boys and 'N Sync, setting up the Britney Spears and Christina Aguilera wave that would crest in 1999. The year's critical apex was Lauryn Hill's The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill, released August 25, which debuted at number one and won five Grammys including Album of the Year, the first hip-hop record ever to do so. Madonna's Ray of Light was her electronica-influenced reinvention with William Orbit, and other massive albums included Shania Twain's still-selling Come On Over, Garth Brooks' Double Live, Alanis Morissette's Supposed Former Infatuation Junkie, DMX's back-to-back number one debuts, Jay-Z's Vol. 2 Hard Knock Life, and Beastie Boys' Hello Nasty. Dominant singles included Brandy and Monica's 'The Boy Is Mine' (13 weeks at number one), Next's 'Too Close,' Aerosmith's 'I Don't Want to Miss a Thing,' Usher's 'Nice and Slow,' and Will Smith's 'Gettin' Jiggy Wit It.' Hip-hop and R&B dominated the charts, grunge had fully receded, and nu-metal was ascendant on the strength of Korn's Follow the Leader.

    Television

    Seinfeld's May 14 finale drew roughly 76 million viewers and defined the year in television, closing one of the emblematic sitcoms of the decade. NBC's Thursday 'Must See TV' bloc of Friends, ER, Frasier, and Just Shoot Me remained a cultural anchor, but two new arrivals hinted at what came next: HBO's Sex and the City premiered June 6 and effectively kicked open the door for prestige cable, and NBC's Will and Grace debuted September 21 as a mainstream sitcom with a gay lead. Felicity, That '70s Show, and Charmed launched on the WB and Fox, Buffy the Vampire Slayer hit its stride in season two, The Simpsons was still in its long peak, and South Park had exploded from a 1997 novelty into a full-blown phenomenon. Ally McBeal became the culture-war lightning rod of the year, earning a Time magazine 'Is Feminism Dead?' cover. Cable news was transformed by the Lewinsky saga: the still-young MSNBC and Fox News leaned into wall-to-wall scandal coverage, and the 24-hour panel show format, running Day X chyrons, and perpetual 'special report' bugs were essentially invented on the story.

    Technology

    The internet moved from novelty to necessity in 1998. AOL surpassed 14 million subscribers and served as the on-ramp for most American households, its 'You've got mail' greeting so iconic that Nora Ephron built a top-grossing December rom-com around it. eBay went public on September 24 with a huge first-day pop, Amazon expanded from books into music and video, and on September 4 Larry Page and Sergey Brin quietly incorporated Google Inc. in a Menlo Park garage on the strength of a $100,000 check from Andy Bechtolsheim, an event that received essentially no press coverage but would prove the most economically consequential of the year. Consumer hardware belonged to the Apple iMac G3, released August 15 in Bondi blue, the translucent all-in-one from Jony Ive that saved Apple and validated Steve Jobs's second act. Windows 98 shipped June 25 as the last consumer Windows built on DOS, its integration of Internet Explorer providing fuel for the landmark US v. Microsoft antitrust case filed May 18. DVD players dropped under $500 during the holidays, digital cameras remained expensive and low-resolution, and the PlayStation dominated gaming with Metal Gear Solid and Resident Evil 2 while the Nintendo 64 delivered The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time. Cell phones spread rapidly in the Nokia 5110 and 6110 era, and the FDA's March 27 approval of Viagra became both a genuine pharmaceutical blockbuster and a punchline on every late-night monologue.

    Social Trends

    The mood was materially comfortable, politically distracted, and slightly embarrassed. Americans told pollsters they felt better about the country than at almost any point in decades, but the year's dominant story was a presidential sex scandal dissected hour by hour on newly muscular cable news and the still-young web. LGBTQ rights was the most active social front: Matthew Shepard's murder in October galvanized a coalition around hate-crime legislation, while the April 30 cancellation of Ellen DeGeneres's Ellen a year after her 'Yep, I'm Gay' Time cover was widely framed as backlash. The Southern Baptist Convention drew national controversy in June with an amendment declaring that a wife should 'submit herself graciously' to her husband, while interest in yoga, Eastern religion, and 'spiritual but not religious' self-identification continued to grow. Charter schools were expanding rapidly, the standards-and-testing movement that would culminate in No Child Left Behind was gathering steam, and internet use in classrooms was rising fast under E-Rate funding. Late-'90s cultural criticism captured a widespread sense that peace and prosperity had left the country weightless, a mood The Truman Show made unusually explicit by asking whether reality itself had become entertainment.

    Crime

    Violent crime continued its sustained decline from the early-1990s peak, one of the era's most under-noticed good-news stories, but the year still produced shocking headlines. On June 7 James Byrd Jr., a Black man in Jasper, Texas, was chained to a pickup truck and dragged to his death by three white supremacists, one of the most horrific hate crimes of the decade. Anti-abortion violence spiked: the January 29 Birmingham, Alabama abortion clinic bombing killed an off-duty officer and was ultimately attributed to Eric Rudolph, and Dr. Barnett Slepian was assassinated by sniper in his Amherst, New York home on October 23. School violence entered the national conversation ahead of Columbine with the March 24 Jonesboro, Arkansas shooting (two students aged 11 and 13 killed four classmates and a teacher) and the May 21 Springfield, Oregon shooting by Kip Kinkel. Texas executed Karla Faye Tucker on February 3, the first woman put to death in the state since the Civil War, reopening the national capital punishment debate. Unabomber Ted Kaczynski pleaded guilty January 22 and drew life without parole in May, and the FBI added Osama bin Laden to its Ten Most Wanted list following the August embassy bombings.

    Sports

    The summer belonged to Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa, whose home run chase shattered Roger Maris's 37-year-old record of 61: McGwire hit number 62 off the Cubs' Steve Trachsel on September 8 and finished with 70, while Sosa finished with 66. It was contemporaneously credited with saving baseball after the 1994 strike, a framing later contaminated by the steroids revelations. The New York Yankees went 114-48 and swept the San Diego Padres in October to cap what is widely cited as one of the greatest teams in baseball history. Michael Jordan hit the 'Last Shot' over Bryon Russell in Game 6 on June 14 to clinch the Bulls' sixth title in eight years over Utah, and John Elway finally won his first ring as the Denver Broncos beat Green Bay 31-24 in Super Bowl XXXII. The Detroit Red Wings swept Washington for a second straight Stanley Cup dedicated to Vladimir Konstantinov. At the Nagano Winter Olympics, NHL players participated for the first time, women's hockey made its Olympic debut with the US winning gold over Canada, 15-year-old Tara Lipinski took figure skating gold over Michelle Kwan, and Picabo Street won the super-G. Kentucky won the men's NCAA basketball title over Utah, Tennessee's Lady Vols went 39-0 for a third straight title, Mark O'Meara took both the Masters and the Open Championship, and Randy Moss caught 17 touchdowns as a rookie on a 15-1 Vikings team.

    Public Mood

    Prosperous, distracted, and slightly embarrassed. Unemployment fell to 4.4 percent by year-end, inflation was minimal, the federal budget produced its first surplus since 1969, and Americans by mid-year told pollsters they felt better about the country than at almost any point in decades, yet the year's dominant story was a tawdry sex-and-perjury spectacle that made the country feel small. Clinton's approval rose during impeachment; the public disliked his behavior but hated the pursuit more. Beneath the noise there was a widespread sense that peace and prosperity had left the country weightless, that the actual 21st century was assembling itself off-camera, and that the country was living through the last full year of a distinctly end-of-history mood.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Americans in 1998 thought the year's central drama was whether a sitting president would be removed from office over an affair with a White House intern, a question that felt existential in November and dwindled to a partisan curiosity by the following spring. They thought Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa were healing baseball, that John Glenn's return to space signaled a revived NASA, that El Niño was a portent, that the budget surplus meant fiscal politics had been solved, and that the Asian financial crisis was a foreign problem now safely contained by the Committee to Save the World. The Starr Report, released in September on the new medium of the World Wide Web, was one of the most-downloaded government documents in history, and every ancillary character from Linda Tripp to Vernon Jordan to Betty Currie became a household name.

    What actually mattered

    In retrospect the substantive stakes of impeachment look small and its cultural stakes very large: it was the moment cable news, opposition research, independent counsels, and internet-era rumor blended into a permanent political mode. What actually mattered in 1998 was that al-Qaeda declared and executed a war on the United States and the government failed to metabolize the threat; that the technical and legal foundations of the modern internet economy were poured with Google, the iMac, the DMCA safe harbors, and the AOL-Netscape deal; that a hedge fund blowup revealed the fragility of the deregulated financial system a decade before 2008; that India and Pakistan became nuclear powers within weeks of each other in May; and that the Tobacco Master Settlement Agreement created the template for later mass-tort settlements from opioids to Volkswagen.

    Most forgotten story

    The Tobacco Master Settlement Agreement of November 23, 1998, in which 46 state attorneys general and the four largest US tobacco companies settled for $206 billion over 25 years. It was the largest civil litigation settlement in US history at the time, the template for later mass-tort settlements, and the instrument that killed the Marlboro Man and Joe Camel, restructured tobacco advertising, funded state health programs, and effectively cartelized the industry by protecting the majors from state suits. It should be a defining regulatory event of the late twentieth century; almost no one under forty has heard of it.

    Most underrated story

    The August 7 East Africa embassy bombings and the US response. Treated at the time as a shocking but distant terrorist attack, and the retaliatory cruise-missile strikes second-guessed largely on domestic-political 'wag the dog' grounds, they are now understood as the moment a specific enemy declared war, executed a mass-casualty attack on legally US soil, and was formally identified as a top-tier national security priority. Every serious retrospective, from the 9/11 Commission Report to Steve Coll's Ghost Wars to Lawrence Wright's The Looming Tower to Richard Clarke's Against All Enemies, locates the critical inflection point in 1998, not 2001.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The sheer invisibility of Google's founding. On September 4, 1998, in a year of aggressive media attention to technology stories from Microsoft antitrust to AOL-Netscape to the iMac to the beginnings of the dot-com bubble, the incorporation of what would become the defining company of the twenty-first-century information economy attracted essentially no coverage. Most Americans learned Google existed only at its 2004 IPO. That a company of that scale could form invisibly in the middle of the most tech-saturated news cycle in American history is the year's cleanest illustration of how badly contemporary attention tracks long-run importance.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The granular day-by-day details of the Lewinsky scandal (blue dress, cigar, semantics of 'sexual relations,' 'it depends on what the meaning of is is')Consumed roughly 1,400 network newscast minutes and hardened partisan combat, but the specifics have almost entirely faded from cultural memory; Clinton finished his term with high approval and the daily grand-jury minutiae are now the province of period-piece television.
    Operation Desert Fox and the Iraq inspection crisis of DecemberTreated at the time as a possible prelude to a wider war with a live 'wag the dog' sub-controversy, in hindsight it is a footnote sandwiched between the 1991 Gulf War and the 2003 invasion.
    The McGwire-Sosa home run chaseFramed at the time as a cleansing moment that saved baseball after the 1994 strike, but retrospectively contaminated by the steroid revelations of the early 2000s; the numbers remain in the record book but are rarely celebrated.
    The 1997-98 El NiñoDominated weather coverage for the first half of the year and was a Saturday Night Live punchline, but faded into a routine meteorological reference rather than the once-in-a-generation event it was billed as.
    John Glenn's return to space aboard Discovery in October at age 77A feel-good national event framed by NASA as gerontological research, but STS-95 produced no major science, had no successors, and its long-term significance is essentially zero.
    The deaths of Sonny Bono in January and Frank Sinatra in MaySinatra was covered as the closing of an American mid-century and received saturation obituary treatment; culturally significant at the time, historically minor.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Osama bin Laden's February 23 World Islamic Front fatwa declaring the duty of every Muslim to kill AmericansReceived essentially zero mainstream US press attention but is now treated as the founding document of al-Qaeda's American war and the intellectual predicate for the 9/11 attacks.
    The August 7 East Africa embassy bombings and Operation Infinite ReachCovered largely through the lens of whether Clinton was 'wagging the dog' but in hindsight the opening battle of the global war on terror, with every serious retrospective locating the critical intelligence-failure inflection in 1998.
    The incorporation of Google on September 4 in a Menlo Park garageAttracted no press coverage but arguably the most economically consequential single event of the year, founding the defining company of the twenty-first-century information economy.
    The near-collapse and Fed-orchestrated rescue of Long-Term Capital ManagementCovered mostly as financial-page news, only after 2008 was it understood as the dress rehearsal that exposed leveraged-derivative systemic risk, too-big-to-fail dynamics, and the Fed's willingness to backstop private counterparties.
    The Digital Millennium Copyright Act signed October 28Its Section 512 safe harbor for online platforms went almost uncovered outside trade press but became the legal scaffolding on which YouTube, Facebook, and essentially every user-generated-content platform of the following twenty years was built.
    The launch of the Apple iMac G3 in August and Steve Jobs's return to profitabilityCovered as a translucent design curiosity, not as the beginning of the most valuable company in the world's return from near-bankruptcy and the model for design-driven consumer technology.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Clinton Denies Sexual Relationship With Former White House Intern
    2. Starr Report Alleges Perjury and Obstruction; House Weighs Impeachment
    3. Bombs at Two US Embassies in Africa Kill Hundreds; Toll Rising
    4. US Missiles Strike Sudan and Afghanistan in Response to Terror Attacks
    5. India Detonates Nuclear Devices; Pakistan Follows Weeks Later
    6. McGwire Passes Maris, Sosa Close Behind in Historic Home Run Race
    7. 'It Depends on What the Meaning of Is Is': Clinton Testifies to Grand Jury
    8. Fed Orchestrates Wall Street Rescue of Giant Hedge Fund LTCM
    9. House Votes to Impeach Clinton on Perjury, Obstruction Charges
    10. Northern Ireland Peace Deal Reached on Good Friday After Decades of Conflict

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe year opened with three simultaneous shocks. On January 17 Clinton gave a sworn deposition in the Paula Jones civil suit denying a sexual relationship with Monica Lewinsky, and on January 21 the Washington Post broke news of the Starr investigation into perjury and obstruction; on January 26 Clinton delivered his televised 'I did not have sexual relations with that woman' denial and Hillary Clinton spoke of a 'vast right-wing conspiracy' the next morning. Sonny Bono died in a skiing accident on January 5, Pope John Paul II made a historic visit to Cuba, Ted Kaczynski pleaded guilty as the Unabomber on January 22, and on January 25 Denver beat Green Bay 31-24 in Super Bowl XXXII for John Elway's first ring. Underneath, the Asian financial crisis continued to gut US exports and depress commodity prices, and Clinton's January 27 State of the Union unveiled 'Save Social Security First' and sent his approval soaring.
    FebruaryTexas executed Karla Faye Tucker on February 3, the first woman put to death in the state since the Civil War and a moment that reopened the national capital punishment debate over the objections of Pope John Paul II and Pat Robertson. On February 7-22 the Nagano Winter Olympics featured NHL players for the first time, women's hockey making its Olympic debut with the US winning gold over Canada, and 15-year-old Tara Lipinski taking figure skating gold over Michelle Kwan. UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan brokered a temporary reprieve in the Iraq inspection crisis. And on February 23, largely unnoticed by mainstream US press, Osama bin Laden issued his World Islamic Front fatwa declaring it the duty of every Muslim to kill Americans.
    MarchOn March 24 two boys aged 11 and 13 killed four students and a teacher at Westside Middle School in Jonesboro, Arkansas, an early harbinger of the school-shooting era. Madonna released Ray of Light on March 3. Titanic swept the March 23 Academy Awards, tying Ben-Hur's 11 Oscars including Best Picture. On March 27 the FDA approved Viagra, which became an overnight cultural phenomenon and pharmaceutical blockbuster. In the Supreme Court, Oncale v. Sundowner Offshore Services unanimously held that same-sex sexual harassment is actionable under Title VII.
    AprilThe Good Friday Agreement was signed in Belfast on April 10, largely ending the Troubles in Northern Ireland and standing as the diplomatic triumph of the year and of Clinton's presidency. On April 6 Citicorp and Travelers announced their $70-plus billion combination to create Citigroup, forcing the eventual repeal of Glass-Steagall the following year; NationsBank and BankAmerica also announced their merger. Unemployment hit a low of 4.3 percent. Ellen DeGeneres's Ellen was canceled by ABC on April 30 roughly a year after her 'Yep, I'm Gay' Time cover, framed at the time as backlash. On April 30 the Senate also ratified NATO enlargement to Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic by 80-19.
    MayOn May 11 and 13 India conducted its Pokhran-II nuclear tests, and on May 28 and 30 Pakistan responded with its own tests, triggering Glenn Amendment sanctions and creating two new declared nuclear powers within weeks. On May 14 the Seinfeld finale drew roughly 76 million viewers. On May 17 David Wells threw a perfect game for the Yankees. On May 18 the Justice Department and 20 states filed the landmark US v. Microsoft antitrust suit. On May 21 Kip Kinkel shot four dead at Thurston High School in Springfield, Oregon. Frank Sinatra died on May 14.
    JuneOn June 7 James Byrd Jr. was chained to a pickup truck and dragged to death by three white supremacists in Jasper, Texas, one of the most horrific hate crimes of the decade. On June 9 Clinton signed TEA-21, the $218 billion transportation reauthorization and largest public-works bill in US history to date. On June 14 Michael Jordan hit the Last Shot over Bryon Russell to clinch the Bulls' sixth NBA title. On June 25 Windows 98 shipped, the last consumer Windows built on DOS. On June 6 HBO's Sex and the City premiered. The Supreme Court decided Faragher and Burlington Industries clarifying sexual harassment liability, and struck down the Line Item Veto Act. The McCain tobacco bill collapsed in the Senate under a $40 million industry ad campaign. Consumer confidence hit a then-record 138. Clinton departed on his nine-day state visit to China, the first US presidential visit since Tiananmen.
    JulyThe S&P 500 hit an intraday high near 1,190 on July 17, capping a 22 percent rally from January and marking the top before the fall crisis. Saving Private Ryan opened on July 24 to instant acclaim, resetting the standard for war-film realism. Broadcast.com priced its IPO and tripled on day one. There's Something About Mary opened and became a summer blockbuster. Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa continued their inexorable pursuit of Roger Maris.
    AugustOn August 7 near-simultaneous al-Qaeda truck bombings destroyed the US embassies in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam, killing 224 including 12 Americans and injuring more than 4,500 in the opening battle of what would become the global war on terror. On August 15 Apple released the Bondi blue iMac G3, saving the company. On August 17 Clinton testified before Starr's grand jury and admitted an 'inappropriate' relationship with Lewinsky in a nationally televised address that evening; hours later Russia defaulted on ruble-denominated debt and devalued, triggering a global flight to quality. On August 20 Operation Infinite Reach launched cruise missiles at al-Qaeda camps in Afghanistan and the Al-Shifa plant in Sudan. On August 25 Lauryn Hill released The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill. On August 31 the Dow fell 512 points, the second-largest point drop in its history to that time.
    SeptemberOn September 4 Larry Page and Sergey Brin quietly incorporated Google Inc. in a Menlo Park garage, an event that attracted no press coverage. On September 8 Mark McGwire hit home run number 62 off the Cubs' Steve Trachsel to break Roger Maris's 37-year-old record. On September 9 Starr delivered his report to Congress, which posted it to the internet on September 11. On September 23 the New York Fed convened fourteen creditor banks to inject $3.625 billion into Long-Term Capital Management, whose $125 billion of levered positions had unraveled after the Russia default; Alan Greenspan's FOMC cut rates 25 basis points on September 29. On September 24 eBay went public at $18 and closed at $53.50.
    OctoberOn October 8 the S&P 500 hit its low near 959, capping a 19 percent fall from July, but the same day the House voted to authorize a formal impeachment inquiry. The Fed cut rates in an inter-meeting move on October 15. On October 6-7 University of Wyoming student Matthew Shepard was beaten and tied to a fence outside Laramie in an anti-gay hate crime; he died October 12. On October 23 Israeli PM Netanyahu and PLO Chair Arafat signed the Wye River Memorandum at the White House after intense Clinton-brokered talks. On October 28 Clinton signed the Digital Millennium Copyright Act. On October 29 John Glenn returned to space aboard Discovery at age 77. On October 31 Clinton signed the Iraq Liberation Act making regime change formal US policy.
    NovemberOn November 3 Democrats gained five House seats in the midterms, the first sixth-year gain for a president's party since 1822, and Jesse Ventura won the Minnesota governorship as a Reform Party candidate while Jeb Bush won Florida and George W. Bush was re-elected in Texas by a landslide; on November 6 Newt Gingrich announced his resignation as Speaker. The Fed cut rates a third time on November 17. On November 13 theglobe.com posted a 606 percent first-day IPO gain, the largest in history to that point. Daimler-Benz and Chrysler closed their merger. On November 23 the Tobacco Master Settlement Agreement was signed for $206 billion over 25 years, the largest civil litigation settlement in US history. On November 24 America Online announced its $4.2 billion acquisition of Netscape.
    DecemberOn December 1 Exxon announced its roughly $80 billion acquisition of Mobil, the largest merger ever at the time and a direct response to the oil price collapse, with WTI crude bottoming near $10.72 mid-month. From December 16 to 19 US and British forces launched Operation Desert Fox, a four-day cruise-missile campaign against Iraq for obstructing UN weapons inspectors. On December 19 the House approved two articles of impeachment against Clinton for perjury and obstruction of justice, making him only the second president in US history to be impeached; on the same day Speaker-elect Bob Livingston stunned the House by disclosing his own affairs and resigning, elevating Dennis Hastert. The S&P 500 closed the year at 1,229.23 for a 28.6 percent total return, the NASDAQ Composite at 2,192.69 for a 39.6 percent gain, and consumers largely ignored the entire spectacle.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth4.5% real (modern BEA vintage; contemporary Q4-over-Q4 near 4.8%)
    Inflation1.6% CPI-U December-over-December (core CPI ~2.4%)
    Unemployment4.4% (December); 4.5% annual average
    S&P 500 return+28.6% total return (+26.7% price to 1,229.23)
    Dow return+16.1% price return to 9,181.43 (NASDAQ Composite +39.6% to 2,192.69; Russell 2000 -3.4%)
    Fed funds rate4.75% year-end target (down from 5.50% after three 25bp cuts)
    Oil price~$11-12/bbl WTI year-end (intraday low near $10.72 in December)
    Gold price~$287-290/oz London PM fix year-end
    Population~270 million
    Median home priceN/A
    National debt~$5.53 trillion end-FY1998; ~$5.61 trillion calendar year-end

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 1998 was to feel, most days, that things were going pretty well. Your job was probably secure, your 401(k) statement was thrilling, and your neighborhood was safer than at any point since the Kennedy administration. Gas was under a dollar in many parts of the country, your home was appreciating, and mortgage rates were the friendliest in a generation. If you were logging on for the first time, a beige box in a spare bedroom greeted you with a modem screech and AOL's cheerful 'You've got mail.' If you had money in the market, you watched the Dow cross 9,000 in April, panic through the summer as Russia defaulted and a hedge fund named LTCM briefly threatened to break the financial system, and then rocket back to end the year up sharply, with a NASDAQ that put on nearly forty percent behind names like Cisco, Microsoft, Intel, AOL, and a still-scrappy Amazon.

    You also felt embarrassed a lot. The president of the United States admitted on national television in August that he had lied about an affair with a White House intern, and by December the House had impeached him. The Speaker of the House resigned. His designated successor resigned on the same day as the impeachment vote, over his own affairs. Cable news, still a young medium, spent the year inventing the modern permanent-scandal format around the story: the Day 200 chyron, the panel of shouting lawyers, the perpetual 'special report' bug. You could feel, as Peggy Noonan and Maureen Dowd both wrote at the time, that the country had become a little tawdry and small.

    And so you sought better company elsewhere. You watched McGwire and Sosa chase Roger Maris through the summer in what felt like a cleansing baseball miracle. You watched Michael Jordan hit the Last Shot in June, John Glenn return to space in October at age 77, John Elway finally win his ring in January, and the Yankees win 114 regular-season games. You cried at Saving Private Ryan and quoted The Big Lebowski and bought The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill and, if you were teenaged, argued about whether the Backstreet Boys or 'N Sync was better. Two shocking hate crimes, the dragging murder of James Byrd Jr. in Texas and the beating of Matthew Shepard in Wyoming, punched through the good times and hinted at darker currents. Twin cruise-missile strikes on Sudan and Afghanistan in August felt, at the time, like a distraction from Lewinsky rather than the opening of a war. It would take another three years to understand which was which. Nineteen ninety-eight, in the end, was the last year the United States could still pretend history had ended.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    1999
    Econ +3Mark +3Poli +1Cult +3Over +2
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    In 1999 the United States lived through what history now calls the last full year of the American unipolar moment: a Cold War won, a budget in surplus, unemployment at a thirty-year low of 4.0 percent, real GDP growing 4.7 percent, and equity markets closing an unprecedented fifth consecutive year of twenty-percent-plus returns, with the NASDAQ up 85.6 percent to close above 4,000. The federal government ran a $124 billion surplus, the second in a row, and Washington debated what to do with revenue rather than how to cut it. Alan Greenspan's Fed nudged rates from 4.75 to 5.50 percent to lean against exuberance, but core inflation held under two percent, productivity kept accelerating, and the "new economy" thesis reigned unchallenged.

    Yet beneath the numbers, the year was defined by two competing moods: giddy pre-millennial prosperity and an almost apocalyptic undercurrent. Two students murdered twelve classmates and a teacher at Columbine High School on April 20, permanently reshaping the politics of guns, schools, and youth culture. The Senate acquitted Bill Clinton on February 12 after only the second presidential impeachment trial in American history. NATO fought an eleven-week air war over Kosovo without a single U.S. combat death. John F. Kennedy Jr. died in a plane crash off Martha's Vineyard on July 16. The Battle of Seattle detonated the anti-globalization movement in late November. Y2K stockpiling and countdown-clock celebration coexisted through December, and the calendar turned uneventfully into 2000.

    Read from a quarter-century away, 1999 looks like the year the American story quietly changed subjects while the country was looking the other way. The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act repealed Glass-Steagall and helped assemble the architecture of the 2008 crisis. Google took its first institutional funding, Salesforce and Alibaba were founded, Napster launched, The Sopranos premiered, and Vermont's Supreme Court in Baker v. State began the legal chain that would end at Obergefell. Ahmed Ressam was arrested at the Canadian border with LAX-bound explosives, and on New Year's Eve Boris Yeltsin resigned and elevated Vladimir Putin. Americans thought they were living in the last chapter of the twentieth century; they were actually living in the prologue to the twenty-first.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+3Full-year growth near 4.7 percent, unemployment at a 30-year low of 4.0 percent, core inflation under 2 percent, a widening federal surplus, and accelerating productivity together made 1999 about as good as a modern American macro year gets.
    Markets+3Equity returns were extraordinary across every major index (S&P +21%, Dow +27%, NASDAQ +85.6%), IPO and M&A activity set records, and realized volatility stayed moderate, with narrow breadth and stretched tech multiples belonging to the 2000-02 postmortem rather than 1999's own tape.
    Politics+1Despite an impeachment trial and a rejected arms-control treaty, 1999 was a functional year: no shutdown, a record surplus, a competently executed Kosovo campaign, an orderly acquittal under constitutional procedure, and passage of significant bipartisan legislation.
    Culture+3A landmark year for American culture, delivering one of the greatest years in cinema history, the launch of prestige TV via The Sopranos, boundary-shattering hip-hop and pop debuts, a technological inflection via Napster and mainstream internet, and iconic sports moments, all inside a broadly cohesive pre-9/11 public life.
    Overall Mood+2Anxious prosperity: full employment, roaring markets, and a surplus produced genuine national good feeling, but Columbine, impeachment fatigue, Y2K dread, and the psychic hollowness captured by Fight Club and American Beauty kept the mood a notch below euphoric.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Senate Acquits Clinton on Impeachment; Both Articles Fail as 10 Republicans Cross Over
    2. 12 Classmates and a Teacher Killed at Columbine High School Before Gunmen Turn Weapons on Themselves
    3. NATO Launches Air War Over Kosovo; Milosevic Capitulates After 78-Day Campaign
    4. Dow Tops 10,000 for First Time in March, 11,000 in May; NASDAQ Soars 85 Percent to Close Year Above 4,000
    5. JFK Jr., Wife and Sister-in-Law Killed as Small Plane Crashes Off Martha's Vineyard
    6. U.S. Women Beat China in Shootout to Win World Cup Before 90,000 at Rose Bowl
    7. Clinton Signs Financial Services Modernization Act, Ending Depression-Era Ban on Bank Mergers
    8. Anti-Globalization Protests Shut Down WTO Ministerial in 'Battle of Seattle'
    9. Star Wars: Episode I Opens to Record Crowds and Divided Fan Reaction
    10. Yeltsin Resigns; Prime Minister Putin Becomes Acting President as Panama Canal Transfers to Panama

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The United States economy in 1999 delivered its sixth consecutive year of extraordinary expansion and, by nearly every conventional metric, one of the strongest years of the post-war era. Real GDP grew roughly 4.7 percent, payrolls added about three million jobs, and the civilian unemployment rate fell from 4.3 percent in January to 4.0 percent in December — the lowest year-end print since January 1970. Headline CPI accelerated to 2.7 percent on rebounding energy prices, but core inflation stayed tame at 1.9 percent, allowing Alan Greenspan's Fed to sustain its 'new economy' productivity thesis while raising the fed funds target from 4.75 to 5.50 percent through three quarter-point hikes. The federal government ran a $124 billion unified budget surplus, the second consecutive surplus after decades of deficits, and Washington began an unfamiliar debate about what to do with excess revenue.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP +4.7% Y/Y; unemployment 4.0% in December (30-year low); headline CPI +2.7%, core CPI +1.9%; productivity +2.9%; fed funds 4.75% to 5.50%; ten-year Treasury 4.65% to 6.44%; thirty-year fixed mortgage averaging 7.44% (ending near 8.06%); median existing-home price ~$137,600 (up ~6%); homeownership rate 67.0% (record); WTI crude from roughly $12 in February to $25.60 at year-end; personal saving rate down to ~2.4%; Consumer Confidence Index near 141; FY1999 unified surplus ~$124 billion; total federal debt ~$5.77 trillion.

    Major Events

    The euro launched January 1 as an accounting currency across eleven EU states. Brazil floated the real in mid-January in the last aftershock of the 1997-98 emerging-market crisis. The Fed pivoted to tightening with three quarter-point hikes on June 30, August 24, and November 16, reversing the previous fall's LTCM-era emergency cuts. OPEC's March production cut led by Saudi Arabia, Venezuela, and Mexico roughly doubled crude prices over the year. Clinton signed the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Financial Services Modernization Act on November 12, repealing Glass-Steagall and formally blessing the Citicorp-Travelers structure. Y2K remediation drove an unprecedented multi-hundred-billion-dollar corporate IT capex pull-forward, and mass protests disrupted the WTO ministerial in Seattle in late November, an early warning that the free-trade consensus was fraying.

    Why this score

    Growth near 4.7 percent, unemployment at a thirty-year low of 4.0 percent, core inflation under two percent, a widening federal surplus, and rising productivity together made 1999 about as good as a modern American macro year gets.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    Financial markets delivered a spectacular, if increasingly narrow and speculative, year. The S&P 500 returned +21.04 percent on a total-return basis, its fifth consecutive year of twenty-percent-plus total returns, an unprecedented streak. The Dow Jones Industrial Average returned +27.21 percent, closing above 11,000 for the first time in May and finishing the year at 11,497. The NASDAQ Composite gained roughly 85.6 percent, its best annual performance ever before or since, closing at 4,069 on December 31 after crossing 4,000 for the first time in the final weeks. Realized volatility was moderate through most of the year despite the parabolic tech tape, and market breadth was famously poor: outside technology, telecom, and biotech, most sectors were flat to down, and the equal-weighted S&P badly trailed the cap-weighted index. Ten-year Treasury yields backed up from about 4.65 percent to roughly 6.44 percent as the Fed tightened and inflation expectations firmed, making 1999 one of the worst years for long Treasuries in a generation.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    The dot-com IPO frenzy set records, with roughly 486 IPOs pricing during the year, about 248 of them internet-related, raising some $69 billion; the average first-day pop for internet IPOs exceeded 65 percent. VA Linux Systems went public December 9 and closed up 697.5 percent on its first day, the largest one-day IPO pop in U.S. history. Priceline, Red Hat, TheStreet.com, Ariba, Akamai, F5 Networks, and Kana became overnight sensations, and UPS raised roughly $5.47 billion on November 10 in the largest IPO to that point. M&A activity smashed records at roughly $3.4 trillion globally, led by Vodafone AirTouch's hostile bid for Mannesmann announced November 14 at nearly $180 billion; Exxon-Mobil closed November 30 in an $81 billion Standard Oil reunion; Viacom and CBS announced a $37 billion combination in September; and MCI WorldCom announced a $115 billion agreement to buy Sprint in October. Iridium filed one of the largest tech-related bankruptcies to that date in August, an early crack in the telecom-boom thesis.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant themes were the dot-com and internet trade, the productivity-driven 'new economy' story that justified extreme tech multiples, and a broad rotation out of anything that did not look like technology, telecom, or biotech. Y2K remediation supercharged corporate IT spending. The Washington Agreement on Gold signed by fifteen European central banks on September 26 capped official gold sales and lending, spiking gold from roughly $255 to $325 within days before settling near $290 at year-end and ending three years of decline. OPEC's March production cut more than doubled crude prices, quietly reintroducing energy inflation. Rising Treasury yields and a tightening Fed made duration a losing bet, and narrow breadth left value and small-cap investors badly behind the headline indices.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The year opened with only the second presidential impeachment trial in American history; the Senate acquitted Bill Clinton on February 12, with the perjury count failing 45-55 and obstruction splitting 50-50. On March 24, NATO launched Operation Allied Force against Yugoslavia in its first major combat operation, an eleven-week air campaign that ended June 10 with Milosevic's withdrawal from Kosovo and no U.S. combat deaths; a May 7 accidental strike on the Chinese embassy in Belgrade ruptured relations with Beijing. The Columbine massacre on April 20 reshaped domestic politics of guns and schools. The Senate rejected the Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty on October 13 by 48-51, the first major arms-control treaty rejected in eighty years. Clinton signed Gramm-Leach-Bliley on November 12, repealing Glass-Steagall. The Panama Canal was handed over to Panama on December 31, and hours later Boris Yeltsin resigned and made Vladimir Putin acting president of Russia.

    Domestic Policy

    Governance was defined by the paradox of intense partisan trench warfare atop an unusually placid economy. The federal government ran a $124 billion surplus, the largest in nominal terms to that point, and the CBO projected trillions more, opening an unfamiliar debate over what to do with excess revenue. Clinton pushed a 'Save Social Security First' framework while Republicans passed a roughly $792 billion, ten-year tax cut that Clinton vetoed in September. Year-end appropriations passed without a shutdown, a notable improvement over the mid-1990s. Signature legislation included Gramm-Leach-Bliley, the Ticket to Work and Work Incentives Improvement Act, the Foster Care Independence Act, and the Y2K Act. Stalled priorities included McCain-Feingold campaign finance reform, a patients' bill of rights, gun-show background checks, tobacco regulation, and Medicare prescription drug coverage. The Rehnquist Court continued its federalism revolution in Alden v. Maine and Florida Prepaid, decided landmark ADA cases in Olmstead and the Sutton trilogy, and revived the right to travel in Saenz v. Roe. Attorney General Janet Reno appointed former Senator John Danforth as special counsel after August revelations that FBI agents had used pyrotechnic devices at Waco in 1993.

    Foreign Policy

    Kosovo dominated the first half of the year and produced an operationally remarkable outcome with three difficult legacies: strained relations with China after the Belgrade embassy strike, strained relations with Russia after its dash to Pristina airport, and unresolved questions about presidential war powers. NATO admitted Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic on March 12 in its first post-Cold War expansion. Middle East peace efforts revived after Ehud Barak's May election in Israel but produced no final agreement. William Perry's October North Korea policy review recommended phased engagement, and Pyongyang suspended long-range missile testing in September. The United States backed the Australian-led INTERFET intervention in East Timor and laid the groundwork for Plan Colombia. Relations with China deteriorated over Belgrade, the Cox Report on nuclear espionage, and a rejected WTO deal in April; a revised WTO accession framework was negotiated in Beijing in November. The Seattle WTO ministerial collapsed from November 30 to December 3 amid the Battle of Seattle. On December 14, Ahmed Ressam was arrested at the Canadian border with explosives intended for LAX, giving Americans their first significant taste of the al-Qaeda threat.

    Important Elections

    Nineteen ninety-nine was an off-year with limited but consequential contests. In Louisiana, Republican Mike Foster won reelection outright in October; in Kentucky, Democrat Paul Patton was reelected in the state's first consecutive-term win; in Mississippi, Democrat Ronnie Musgrove edged Republican Mike Parker in a race so close it was decided by the legislature in January 2000. In Virginia, Republicans took full control of the General Assembly for the first time since Reconstruction, and Democrat John Street narrowly won the Philadelphia mayoralty. The most consequential 1999 election activity was invisible on ballots: George W. Bush's fundraising juggernaut cleared much of the Republican establishment field by year's end, leaving John McCain, Steve Forbes, and Alan Keyes as his main rivals; Bill Bradley emerged as a serious threat to Al Gore on the Democratic side; Pat Buchanan bolted for the Reform Party in October; and Hillary Clinton formed an exploratory committee for the New York Senate seat being vacated by Daniel Patrick Moynihan.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Nineteen ninety-nine is widely regarded as one of the greatest single years in American cinema, a canonical inflection point that produced The Matrix, Fight Club, Being John Malkovich, Magnolia, Election, Three Kings, The Insider, Eyes Wide Shut (Kubrick's final film, released after his March death), Toy Story 2, The Iron Giant, The Sixth Sense, and eventual Best Picture winner American Beauty. Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace was the year's biggest box-office event at roughly $431 million domestic, drawing lines around blocks for weeks but delivering the first modern case of a scorched-earth fan backlash. The Sixth Sense followed at about $293 million, and The Blair Witch Project turned a shoestring budget into $140 million through nascent viral internet marketing. The Best Picture Oscar handed out in March for 1998 films went controversially to Shakespeare in Love over Saving Private Ryan.

    Music

    The year was defined by the crest of the teen-pop wave and the ascendance of rap-rock and nu-metal. Britney Spears's ...Baby One More Time (January) sold more than ten million copies domestically, the Backstreet Boys' Millennium opened at 1.1 million copies its first week, and Christina Aguilera, Ricky Martin, and 98 Degrees rounded out the sound of top-40 radio. On the rock side, Woodstock '99 collapsed into fires, riots, and sexual assaults in Rome, New York, becoming a symbol of the male aggression that ran through Limp Bizkit, Korn, and a chart-topping Rage Against the Machine. Hip-hop had a landmark year: Dr. Dre's 2001 reintroduced him after seven years and launched Eminem, whose Slim Shady LP made him the genre's most controversial figure; Jay-Z, TLC's FanMail, and Santana's Grammy-sweeping Supernatural (with Rob Thomas on 'Smooth') dominated crossover airplay. Napster launched in June and began a slow-motion apocalypse for an industry that ironically posted its peak year for CD revenue.

    Television

    HBO's The Sopranos premiered January 10 and is broadly credited as the ignition of the prestige-TV era; Sex and the City deepened its cultural grip in its second season, and South Park was in its explosive early run, releasing its Bigger, Longer and Uncut film in June. Broadcast still dominated total viewership behind ER, Friends, Frasier, Everybody Loves Raymond, and The Practice, while ABC's Who Wants to Be a Millionaire debuted in August and became a monster hit large enough to reorganize primetime around it. The West Wing premiered September 22, Family Guy launched after Super Bowl XXXIII in January, and SpongeBob SquarePants arrived on Nickelodeon in May. Cable news was rising but not yet dominant, and WWF's Monday Night Raw was in the ratings-record heart of its Attitude Era.

    Technology

    1999 was the year the consumer internet became genuinely mainstream, with roughly half of American households owning a computer and about forty percent online, most of them still on dial-up. AOL peaked in cultural power with more than twenty million subscribers and its ubiquitous 'You've Got Mail' chime. Napster launched in June; the BlackBerry 850 pager arrived in January; Wi-Fi (802.11b) was ratified in September; TiVo and ReplayTV shipped the first DVRs; PayPal's precursor Confinity launched; MSN Messenger joined AIM and ICQ; the Sega Dreamcast launched on 9/9/99; and Apple's iBook became the first laptop with built-in Wi-Fi. Off the front pages, Google took its first institutional funding of $25 million from Sequoia and Kleiner Perkins, Salesforce was founded in March, and Alibaba was founded in Hangzhou in April, quietly seeding the platform economy of the next quarter century.

    Social Trends

    The dominant social trend was anxious prosperity: full employment, a federal surplus, and roaring markets coexisted with millennial dread, Y2K stockpiling, and a moral panic over teen violence in the wake of Columbine. Films like Fight Club, The Matrix, American Beauty, and Office Space channeled a widespread sense that middle-class success was psychically hollow. Anti-globalization organizing burst into visibility with the Battle of Seattle in late November, foreshadowing a decade of trade politics. On LGBTQ rights, Vermont's Supreme Court ruled in Baker v. State on December 20 that same-sex couples deserved equal benefits, effectively starting the legal chain that would end at Obergefell. Sunbelt migration, SUV sales, and McMansion construction crested, and the Hispanic population moved toward surpassing African Americans as the country's largest minority group.

    Crime

    The Columbine High School massacre on April 20, in which Eric Harris and Dylan Klebold killed twelve classmates and a teacher before killing themselves, was the year's defining trauma and reshaped conversations about gun control, media violence, and school safety for a generation. On February 4, four NYPD officers fired forty-one shots at Amadou Diallo, an unarmed West African immigrant, in his Bronx doorway, igniting sustained protests over racial profiling. Mark Barton killed nine people at two Atlanta brokerages on July 29 before taking his own life, intensifying debate over day-trading and workplace violence; Buford Furrow attacked a Jewish community center in Granada Hills on August 10; and a gunman killed seven at Wedgwood Baptist Church in Fort Worth on September 15. Aaron McKinney was convicted in November for the murder of Matthew Shepard, and John William King was sentenced to death in Texas for the dragging murder of James Byrd Jr. Overall crime rates continued their steep 1990s decline even as high-profile mass violence produced a rolling sense of dread.

    Sports

    Sports in 1999 were dense with era-defining moments. Denver won Super Bowl XXXIII 34-19 over Atlanta in John Elway's final game; the Yankees swept the Braves in the World Series for their twenty-fifth title; the San Antonio Spurs beat the Knicks 4-1 in the lockout-shortened NBA Finals for Tim Duncan and David Robinson's first championship; and the Dallas Stars edged the Buffalo Sabres in six on Brett Hull's controversial 'skate in the crease' triple-overtime goal. The U.S. Women's National Team won the FIFA Women's World Cup on July 10 in front of 90,000 at the Rose Bowl, with Brandi Chastain's sports-bra shootout celebration becoming one of the decade's iconic images. Wayne Gretzky retired April 18; seventeen-year-old Serena Williams won her first Grand Slam at the U.S. Open; Andre Agassi completed a career Grand Slam; Lance Armstrong won his first Tour de France; Payne Stewart won the U.S. Open in June and died in an eerie Learjet decompression accident on October 25; and Tiger Woods won eight PGA events including the PGA Championship as he built toward his historic 2000 season.

    Public Mood

    The prevailing public mood was anxious prosperity. Clinton's job approval hovered near sixty percent even as the Lewinsky hangover left a bipartisan sourness about political culture, and headlines oscillated between millennial celebration and hoarding-supplies survivalism. In hindsight commentators have repeatedly identified 1999 as the last 'normal' year, the final unguarded moment before 9/11 reset American consciousness.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Americans in 1999 organized their attention around three big themes: the sordid final act of the Clinton scandals culminating in the February Senate acquittal; a millennial cusp charged with both apocalyptic anxiety (Y2K, Columbine, doomsday religious currents) and celebration (peace, prosperity, dot-com euphoria, end-of-history mood); and a foreign policy of muscular liberal interventionism epitomized by the Kosovo air war. Wall-to-wall coverage was lavished on the JFK Jr. plane crash, the Elian Gonzalez custody saga, and the countdown to midnight on December 31.

    What actually mattered

    In hindsight the durable stories were structural and mostly off the front page: financial deregulation via Gramm-Leach-Bliley that primed the 2008 crisis; the coming-of-age of the platform economy through the founding, funding, and first product launches of Google, Salesforce, Alibaba, PayPal, and BlackBerry; Napster's demonstration that the internet could dismantle a legacy media business model overnight; the premiere of The Sopranos and the birth of prestige TV; Baker v. State in Vermont starting the legal march to nationwide marriage equality; the ascent of Vladimir Putin on New Year's Eve; the maturation of an al-Qaeda threat that was in memos but not on front pages; and the Columbine template that would recur indefinitely.

    Most forgotten story

    The Panama Canal handover on December 31, 1999.
