The United States surrendered sovereignty over one of the twentieth century's flagship American infrastructure projects, ending a colonial-era arrangement that had produced riots and hostage crises within living memory. The ceremony was low-key, President Clinton did not attend, and coverage was crowded out by Y2K vigils. A quarter century later almost no American remembers the date.

    Most underrated story

    The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Financial Services Modernization Act, signed November 12. It repealed the Depression-era Glass-Steagall separation of commercial banking, investment banking, and insurance, and permitted the financial-supermarket holding-company model that produced Citigroup as it existed heading into 2008. Business-page coverage at the time was dry; nearly every serious history of the 2008 crisis treats it as a load-bearing precondition.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The scale of what was being built in silence in 1999 by people almost no one had heard of. In a single twelve-month span, Google took its first institutional funding, Salesforce was founded, Alibaba was founded in Hangzhou, PayPal's precursor Confinity launched, BlackBerry shipped, broadband crossed key thresholds, and Napster demonstrated the vulnerability of every media incumbent. The magazine covers were about Alan Greenspan, Bill Gates, and Jeff Bezos; the actual next-generation platform builders were beneath notice.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Clinton impeachment trial and February 12 acquittalThe Senate acquitted on both articles, Clinton's approval never dipped below the mid-60s, and the episode's main long-term legacy — normalizing impeachment as a partisan tool — was not recognized until decades later.
    The death of John F. Kennedy Jr. in a July 16 plane crash off Martha's VineyardIt produced a week of wall-to-wall coverage rivaling Princess Diana's death but had no political or historical consequence beyond the emotional moment.
    The Y2K bug and its associated survivalist and stockpiling coverageWhen the calendar turned uneventfully, coverage collapsed overnight and the question of whether the fix worked or the problem was overhyped has never really been resolved in popular memory.
    The Elian Gonzalez custody saga that erupted in late NovemberIts only durable legacy was its plausible effect on Cuban-American turnout in Florida in November 2000; the underlying Cuba policy questions went nowhere.
    The Kosovo air war (March 24 to June 10)It ended with Serbian withdrawal and briefly seemed to define a new humanitarian-intervention doctrine, but after 9/11 both the doctrine and the war faded from American memory almost entirely.
    Woodstock '99 and its riots, fires, and sexual assaultsA major end-of-summer story that has been largely forgotten outside recent nostalgia documentaries.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Financial Services Modernization Act signed November 12It repealed the core of Glass-Steagall and enabled the financial-supermarket holding-company model at the center of the 2008 crisis architecture.
    The founding, funding, and first product launches of the 21st-century tech oligopoly (Google's first $25M institutional round, Salesforce, Alibaba, Confinity/PayPal, BlackBerry)None was front-page news in 1999, but within a decade these companies had replaced most of the twentieth-century incumbents that dominated the era's magazine covers.
    Vladimir Putin's elevation to Russian prime minister in August and acting president on New Year's EveCovered mostly as a Russian domestic story at the time, the sequence reshaped the strategic landscape for the next quarter century of geopolitics.
    Ahmed Ressam's December 14 arrest at Port Angeles with LAX-bound explosivesReceived modest coverage folded into Y2K anxiety, but the al-Qaeda logistical network it exposed was the same one that struck on 9/11.
    Napster's June launch and the January 10 premiere of HBO's The SopranosNapster became the standard case study for internet disruption of legacy media, and The Sopranos launched the prestige-TV era that reorganized American entertainment.
    Baker v. State from the Vermont Supreme Court on December 20The ruling that same-sex couples were entitled to equal benefits was a regional story then and is now recognized as the effective starting point of the legal march to Obergefell.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Senate Acquits Clinton on Both Articles; President Vows to Complete Work of Nation
    2. NATO Warplanes Strike Yugoslavia as Alliance Opens Air Campaign Over Kosovo
    3. Dow Closes Above 10,000 for First Time as Bull Market Roars On
    4. Two Students Kill 12 Classmates and Teacher at Columbine High Before Turning Guns on Themselves
    5. JFK Jr., Wife and Sister-in-Law Feared Dead in Plane Crash Off Martha's Vineyard
    6. U.S. Women Win World Cup as Chastain's Shootout Kick Sends Rose Bowl Into Frenzy
    7. Clinton Signs Sweeping Overhaul of Depression-Era Banking Laws
    8. Tear Gas and Broken Windows in Seattle as WTO Talks Collapse Amid Mass Protests
    9. Cuban Boy Rescued Off Florida Becomes Political Flashpoint in Miami Custody Fight
    10. Yeltsin Resigns; Little-Known Prime Minister Putin Becomes Acting President as Russia Enters New Century

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe euro launched on January 1 as an accounting currency across eleven EU states, reshaping global FX markets. The Senate impeachment trial of Bill Clinton opened January 7 with Chief Justice William Rehnquist presiding, and J. Dennis Hastert was sworn in as Speaker of the House. HBO's The Sopranos premiered January 10, and Britney Spears's ...Baby One More Time was released January 12. Denver won Super Bowl XXXIII 34-19 on January 31 in John Elway's final game, and Family Guy debuted after the broadcast. Brazil abandoned its currency peg mid-month, and Pope John Paul II visited St. Louis.
    FebruaryOn February 4, four NYPD officers fired forty-one shots at Amadou Diallo, an unarmed West African immigrant, in the Bronx doorway of his apartment building. Eminem's Slim Shady LP was released and became hip-hop's most controversial debut in years. On February 12, the Senate acquitted President Clinton on both impeachment articles — perjury 45-55, obstruction 50-50 — falling far short of the 67 votes needed to convict. John William King was convicted in Texas of the 1998 dragging murder of James Byrd Jr. Oil prices bottomed near $12 per barrel before beginning their long climb.
    MarchThe Dow closed above 10,000 for the first time on March 29 as the bull market roared on. Filmmaker Stanley Kubrick died on March 7. On March 12, NATO admitted Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic in its first post-Cold War expansion. On March 24, NATO launched Operation Allied Force against Yugoslavia, opening an eleven-week air campaign over Kosovo without UN Security Council authorization. The Wachowskis' The Matrix opened March 31 and reset the vocabulary of action cinema. Salesforce was founded, and the OPEC production-cut agreement began pushing crude prices sharply higher.
    AprilOn April 20, two students killed twelve classmates and a teacher at Columbine High School in Littleton, Colorado, before killing themselves — the year's defining trauma and a template for how mass shootings would be covered for decades. Wayne Gretzky played his final NHL game on April 18. Alibaba was founded in Hangzhou. Allegations broke that Chinese agents had stolen U.S. nuclear-weapons secrets from Los Alamos, and scientist Wen Ho Lee was fired as the story built through the spring.
    MayThe Dow topped 11,000 for the first time on May 3. On May 7, U.S. bombers accidentally struck the Chinese embassy in Belgrade, killing three Chinese journalists and rupturing relations with Beijing. Star Wars: Episode I - The Phantom Menace opened May 19 to record ticket demand and a startling wave of fan backlash. The Cox Committee report on Chinese espionage was released, inflaming relations further. The Backstreet Boys' Millennium album opened at a record 1.1 million copies first-week, and SpongeBob SquarePants premiered on Nickelodeon.
    JuneOn June 10, Yugoslavia withdrew from Kosovo and Operation Allied Force ended, with KFOR peacekeepers deploying. Napster was released to the public by 18-year-old Shawn Fanning, quietly detonating the music industry's business model. The Supreme Court decided Alden v. Maine, Florida Prepaid, Olmstead v. L.C., and the ADA Sutton trilogy on June 23. On June 30, the Federal Reserve raised the fed funds target 25 basis points to 5.00 percent in the first of three tightening moves. The Dallas Stars beat the Buffalo Sabres for the Stanley Cup on Brett Hull's controversial triple-overtime goal on June 19, and Payne Stewart won the U.S. Open. Google received its first institutional funding of $25 million from Sequoia and Kleiner Perkins.
    JulyTreasury Secretary Robert Rubin resigned July 2 and was succeeded by Lawrence Summers. On July 10, the U.S. Women's National Team won the FIFA Women's World Cup at the Rose Bowl on Brandi Chastain's shootout penalty against China, drawing 90,000 fans and a huge television audience. On July 16, John F. Kennedy Jr., his wife Carolyn Bessette, and her sister Lauren died when his small plane crashed off Martha's Vineyard. Lance Armstrong won his first Tour de France. Apple released the iBook, the first laptop with built-in Wi-Fi. Woodstock '99 collapsed into fires and assaults July 22-25 in Rome, New York. On July 29, day trader Mark Barton killed nine people at two Atlanta brokerages before taking his own life.
    AugustOn August 10, Buford Furrow attacked a Jewish community center in Granada Hills, California, wounding five children and later killing a Filipino-American postal worker. The Federal Reserve raised the fed funds target another 25 basis points to 5.25 percent on August 24. Iridium, the Motorola-backed satellite-phone venture, filed one of the largest tech-related bankruptcies to date, an early crack in the telecom-boom thesis. Vladimir Putin was appointed prime minister of Russia by Boris Yeltsin. Who Wants to Be a Millionaire debuted on ABC and quickly became a monster prime-time hit. FBI revelations that agents had used pyrotechnic devices at Waco in 1993 prompted Attorney General Janet Reno to appoint former Senator John Danforth as special counsel.
    SeptemberThe Washington Agreement on Gold was signed September 26 by fifteen European central banks, spiking gold from $255 to $325 per ounce within days. Sega launched the Dreamcast in the U.S. on September 9. The West Wing premiered on NBC on September 22. Wi-Fi (802.11b) was ratified. Viacom and CBS announced a $37 billion combination. A gunman killed seven at Wedgwood Baptist Church in Fort Worth on September 15. North Korea suspended long-range missile testing after quiet diplomacy tied to William Perry's policy review, and Barak-Arafat talks resumed at Sharm el-Sheikh.
    OctoberOn October 13, the Senate rejected the Comprehensive Nuclear Test Ban Treaty 48-51, the first major arms-control treaty rejected in eighty years and the sharpest foreign-policy defeat of Clinton's second term. MCI WorldCom announced a $115 billion agreement to buy Sprint. Pat Buchanan bolted the GOP for the Reform Party. Payne Stewart died October 25 in an eerie Learjet decompression accident. The New York Yankees swept the Atlanta Braves in the World Series for their twenty-fifth title. Senator John Chafee of Rhode Island died October 24 and was succeeded by his son Lincoln by appointment. William Perry's phased-engagement North Korea report was released.
    NovemberOn November 2, Democrat Paul Patton won reelection in Kentucky and Ronnie Musgrove edged Mike Parker in Mississippi in a race so close the legislature would decide it. UPS priced the largest IPO in U.S. history to that point on November 10, raising $5.47 billion. On November 12, President Clinton signed the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, repealing the core of Glass-Steagall. The Federal Reserve raised the fed funds target to 5.50 percent on November 16. Vodafone AirTouch launched a hostile $180 billion bid for Mannesmann on November 14, the largest deal ever attempted. Exxon-Mobil closed its $81 billion merger November 30. The Battle of Seattle erupted November 29 as anti-globalization protesters shut down the WTO ministerial. Aaron McKinney was convicted of murdering Matthew Shepard. Elian Gonzalez was rescued off Florida in late November.
    DecemberVA Linux Systems went public December 9 and closed up 697.5 percent on its first day, the largest one-day IPO pop in U.S. history. On December 14, Ahmed Ressam was arrested at Port Angeles, Washington, entering the U.S. with explosives intended for LAX. The Vermont Supreme Court decided Baker v. State on December 20, requiring equal benefits for same-sex couples. The NASDAQ crossed 4,000 for the first time and closed the year at 4,069, up 85.6 percent. The Panama Canal was handed over to Panama at noon on December 31, ending nearly a century of U.S. administration. Hours later, Boris Yeltsin resigned and elevated Vladimir Putin to acting president. The calendar turned to 2000 with none of the Y2K catastrophes materializing.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+4.7% (real, Y/Y; contemporaneous BEA vintage 4.1-4.2%, later revised up)
    Inflation+2.7% headline CPI (Dec/Dec); core CPI +1.9%
    Unemployment4.0% December (annual average ~4.2%); lowest year-end since January 1970
    S&P 500 return+21.04% total return (+19.5% price)
    Dow return+27.21% total return (+25.2% price); closed at 11,497
    Fed funds rate4.75% at start; 5.50% at year-end (three 25 bp hikes on 6/30, 8/24, 11/16)
    Oil priceWTI ~$25.60/bbl at year-end, up from ~$12/bbl in mid-February
    Gold price~$290/oz at year-end (spiked from ~$255 after Sept. 26 Washington Agreement)
    Population~279 million (mid-1999)
    Median home price~$137,600 (existing homes, NAR, year-end); homeownership rate 67.0% (record)
    National debt~$5.77 trillion total federal public debt at Dec. 31; FY1999 unified budget surplus ~$124 billion

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 1999 was to live inside a paradox that most Americans, at the time, barely registered as one. The country was, by nearly every conventional measure, at the top of its game. Jobs were everywhere and unemployment sat at a thirty-year low; the federal government ran the second surplus in decades and projected trillions more to come; the Dow tore through 10,000 in March and 11,000 in May; the NASDAQ nearly doubled; twenty-six-year-old CEOs graced newsweekly covers; median household income hit a record; welfare rolls fell; crime kept dropping. AOL chimed "You've Got Mail" into more than twenty million homes, and a Californian named Shawn Fanning released a program called Napster that would blow the music industry apart within a year without anyone quite realizing it.

    And yet, underneath the prosperity, the year hummed with dread. Columbine on April 20 detonated a national reckoning with guns, teenagers, and school safety that never really ended. Two students, twelve classmates and a teacher dead, and a template established for every mass shooting that would follow. Y2K anxiety filled bookstores with survivalist manuals and basements with bottled water. The Clinton impeachment trial that dominated January and February left a bipartisan sourness about political culture that outlasted the president's acquittal. Films like The Matrix, Fight Club, American Beauty, and Office Space channeled a widespread suspicion that middle-class success was psychically hollow. Woodstock '99 burned in Rome, New York. JFK Jr. went down off Martha's Vineyard in July. A payday-trader murdered nine in Atlanta.

    Foreign policy felt, briefly, like a triumph. NATO won its Kosovo air war without a single American combat death, and the Panama Canal was handed over on New Year's Eve as if to signal that the imperial century was safely behind us. China, Russia, and a Saudi millionaire named bin Laden were problems on the margins of the front page. In Seattle in late November, tens of thousands of protesters made the case that the globalization consensus was already cracking, but few in power heard them.

    The overwhelming feeling was of an era ending well. It was possible to believe, and many Americans did, that the great arguments of the twentieth century were mostly settled and that the next century would be a matter of extending the winning template. Historians and essayists have since converged on the same phrase for that feeling: the last normal year. In 1999 Americans thought they were writing the final chapter of one story. They were, in fact, writing the prologue to the next.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2000
    Econ +1Mark -2Poli +0Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The United States in 2000 lived through what looked at the time like the placid, self-satisfied denouement of a decade of peace and prosperity, and what history now reads as the pivot point at which almost every crisis of the twenty-first century was quietly loaded and cocked. The economy still ran hot in the tenth year of the longest expansion in postwar history, unemployment averaged 4.0% and touched a 30-year low, and the federal government closed its books with a record $236 billion surplus. But the Nasdaq peaked at 5,048 on March 10 and lost 39% for the year, oil spiked to nearly $37 a barrel, the Fed's tightening cycle inverted the yield curve, and by December the manufacturing sector was already in recession.

    Politics were consumed first by a lame-duck Clinton presidency chasing Middle East peace and North Korean missile deals that never closed, and then by a presidential election that ended with a 537-vote margin in Florida, a 36-day recount saga, and a 5-4 Supreme Court decision effectively awarding the presidency to George W. Bush over Al Gore. The transfer of power was orderly; the manner of resolution left durable partisan wounds. Congress granted China Permanent Normal Trade Relations, passing what would become the most consequential economic policy decision of the decade. The USS Cole was bombed in Aden harbor on October 12, killing seventeen sailors in a barely-noticed dress rehearsal for September 11.

    Culturally, the country was noisily prosperous and mildly distracted. Survivor reinvented television, NSYNC moved 2.4 million albums in a week, Tiger Woods delivered the greatest single season in golf history, Napster taught a generation to think of music as free, and the AOL-Time Warner merger was hailed as the defining deal of the internet age. The dominant mood in end-of-year retrospectives was anxious prosperity — a country that had run out of enemies, argued about hanging chads, and watched Richard Hatch win a million dollars naked on an island, unable to see the shape of what was coming next.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Full-year growth near 4%, unemployment at a 30-year low, a record $236B fiscal surplus, and disinflation intact through core CPI made 2000 a strong headline year above the 2000s baseline, tempered by visible H2 deceleration, curve inversion, an energy shock, and the incipient tech capex bust.
    Markets-2The Nasdaq's -39% is among the worst calendar-year prints in US equity history and the S&P's -9% ended a string of five double-digit-return years, with only strong fixed-income and value/small-cap offsets keeping it above -3.
    Politics+0A prosperous, stable Clinton lame-duck period combined with a genuinely unprecedented post-election constitutional crisis resolved 5-4 by the Supreme Court left 'stability' and 'crisis' roughly canceling each other for the era.
    Culture+1Commercially enormous and creatively significant — peak record-industry sales, the first great season of prestige TV, Tiger's transcendent summer, Gladiator and Crouching Tiger at the multiplex, Radiohead reinventing itself — but pulled toward the middle by teen-pop homogeneity and an ugly, divisive election.
    Overall Mood+0Anxious prosperity: real peace and record surpluses buoyed the public, but a market crash, an oil spike, a 36-day election crisis, and a diffuse sense of institutional strain kept the national mood roughly neutral for a year that felt boring at the time and momentous in hindsight.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Supreme Court Halts Florida Recount 5-4; Bush Wins Presidency After 36-Day Standoff
    2. AOL Announces $165 Billion Merger With Time Warner, Largest Deal in History
    3. Nasdaq Plunges Into Bear Market After March Peak; Dot-Coms Crash
    4. Federal Agents Seize Elian Gonzalez in Pre-Dawn Miami Raid
    5. Judge Orders Breakup of Microsoft in Landmark Antitrust Ruling
    6. USS Cole Struck by Suicide Bombers in Yemen; 17 US Sailors Killed
    7. Camp David Summit Collapses; Second Intifada Erupts in Israel and Palestinian Territories
    8. Congress Grants China Permanent Normal Trade Relations, Paving WTO Accession
    9. Vermont Becomes First State to Legalize Civil Unions for Same-Sex Couples
    10. Firestone Recalls 6.5 Million Tires as Ford Explorer Rollover Deaths Mount

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The United States entered 2000 in the tenth year of the longest business-cycle expansion in postwar history, and the calendar year became a paradox: the real economy still ran hot at the top of the cycle while equity markets — especially technology — collapsed from generational-bubble valuations. Real GDP grew about 4.1% for the full year on the current BEA vintage (contemporary reports were near 5%), unemployment averaged 4.0% and touched a 30-year low of 3.8% in April, and the federal government posted a record $236 billion unified surplus. But growth was front-loaded, and Q4 slowed to roughly 1% as manufacturing turned down (ISM slipped below 50 in autumn), business tech capex rolled over, and the Conference Board's Leading Economic Index went negative. Headline CPI ran at 3.4% by year-end, driven almost entirely by energy: WTI crude spiked to nearly $37/bbl in September before easing, natural-gas prices roughly tripled into year-end, and the California electricity crisis began. The Fed under Alan Greenspan finished a 175-bp tightening cycle with a 50-bp hike to 6.50% on May 16 and held there for the rest of the year, even as the Treasury curve inverted (10-year yields fell from 6.7% to 5.1%) — a classic recession signal that the NBER would eventually date to a March 2001 peak. Housing was a source of steady strength rather than the bubble to come, with median existing-home prices at about $139,000 and homeownership at a then-record 67.4%.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth ~4.1% (Q4 decelerating to ~1%). Unemployment: 4.0% average, 3.8% low. Headline CPI: 3.4% Y/Y (Dec); core CPI: ~2.6%. Fed funds: 6.50% year-end. WTI oil: peak ~$37, close ~$26-27. Gold: ~$273/oz. 30-year mortgage: ~8.05% average. Median existing-home price: ~$139,000. Homeownership: 67.4%. FY2000 federal surplus: ~$236 billion. Gross federal debt: ~$5.67 trillion. Personal saving rate: ~2% (postwar low at the time). Wage growth: ~4% Y/Y. Consumer Confidence Index: all-time high of 144.7 in May, easing to 128 by December.

    Major Events

    The Y2K non-event on January 1 quietly ended a multi-year corporate IT-spending surge; the Fed's May 16 50-bp hike to 6.50% capped the tightening cycle; unemployment bottomed at 3.8% in April; OPEC-driven oil spikes to ~$37 prompted a coordinated 30-million-barrel Strategic Petroleum Reserve release in September; the California electricity crisis emerged in summer and fall with rolling blackouts and utility insolvency risk; manufacturing slipped into contraction in H2 as business investment rolled over; and the 36-day Florida recount, resolved by Bush v. Gore on December 12, weighed on business sentiment. Treasury's controversial 30-year buyback program, taken to reduce debt during the surplus era, contributed to a curve inversion that prefigured recession.

    Why this score

    Full-year growth near 4%, unemployment at a 30-year low, a record fiscal surplus, and disinflation intact through core CPI made 2000 a strong headline year clearly above the 2000s baseline — but visible H2 deceleration, curve inversion, an energy shock, and the incipient tech capex bust argue against a higher score.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    The S&P 500 delivered about -9.1% total return (price return roughly -10.1%), ending the year near 1,320 after peaking at 1,527 on March 24 — its first negative calendar year since 1990 and the end of a five-year streak of double-digit returns. The Dow Jones Industrial Average returned roughly -4.9% on a total-return basis, aided by lower tech weighting. The Nasdaq Composite lost 39.3% — its worst calendar year ever — closing near 2,470, down more than 50% from its March 10 intraday peak of 5,132. Breadth was better than headlines suggested outside tech and telecom: value beat growth by roughly the widest margin in decades (Russell 1000 Value outperforming Russell 1000 Growth by about 30 percentage points), small caps held up (Russell 2000 roughly -3%), and utilities, staples, healthcare, and financials all posted positive total returns. Investment-grade bonds had a strong year as long yields fell — the Lehman US Aggregate returned about +11.6% and the 10-year Treasury yield declined from 6.7% to 5.1% — while high yield was crushed by telecom credit blowups, returning roughly -5%.

    Character

    Bear

    Major Companies

    AOL announced its $165 billion acquisition of Time Warner on January 10 — then the largest merger in history and, in retrospect, a bell-ringing top for internet-era valuations. Cisco Systems briefly became the world's most valuable company at roughly $550 billion in late March before losing three-quarters of its value by the end of 2001. Microsoft was ruled an illegal monopolist by Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson on April 3, with a June 7 breakup order that triggered a Microsoft-led tech selloff (later overturned on appeal). Intel warned on Q3 revenue on September 21 and lost 22% in a single day; Apple guided down on September 28 and fell about 52% in one day. Enron closed the year near $83, valued around $70 billion and named America's Most Innovative Company by Fortune for the sixth straight year — accounting-fraud revelations still a year away. A cascade of dot-com failures — boo.com, Pets.com (liquidated November 9, nine months after its IPO), Living.com, Toysmart, drkoop.com, APBnews, and dozens more — became the emblem of the bust; the IPO window essentially slammed shut after Q1. Big non-tech M&A continued: JP Morgan agreed to be acquired by Chase Manhattan in December (~$36 billion), Pfizer completed its hostile purchase of Warner-Lambert (~$90 billion) in June, and Glaxo Wellcome merged with SmithKline Beecham to form GlaxoSmithKline.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was the deflation of the dot-com bubble and the return of valuation discipline. Value crushed growth by a historic margin, defensive sectors (utilities, staples, healthcare, financials) massively outperformed tech and telecom, small caps held up better than large caps, and long-duration Treasuries rallied hard as the Fed's tightening cycle ended and recession signals accumulated. Telecom credit blew up (Lucent, Nortel, the CLECs), foreshadowing broader corporate credit stress in 2001-2002. The Y2K IT-spending hangover reinforced the tech capex bust. Beneath the surface, the era's deregulatory consensus continued to advance, with the Commodity Futures Modernization Act (passed in December's omnibus) exempting most over-the-counter derivatives — including credit-default swaps — from CFTC oversight, an act later blamed for enabling the 2008 crisis.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The defining event was the contested Bush-Gore presidential election: a 537-vote margin in Florida, five weeks of hanging chads and butterfly ballots, a Florida Supreme Court order for a statewide manual recount on December 8, a US Supreme Court stay on December 9, and a 5-4 ruling on December 12 (with 7-2 on the underlying equal-protection concern) that ended the recount and effectively awarded the presidency to George W. Bush. Gore conceded on December 13. On October 12, al-Qaeda suicide bombers struck the USS Cole in Aden harbor, killing 17 US sailors; the Clinton administration deferred retaliation. The Camp David II summit in July between Barak and Arafat collapsed, and the Second Intifada erupted on September 28 after Ariel Sharon's visit to the Temple Mount. Federal agents raided the home of Elian Gonzalez's Miami relatives on April 22 and returned the boy to Cuba, producing the year's iconic Alan Diaz photograph and probably costing Gore Florida. Congress granted China Permanent Normal Trade Relations in October (PL 106-286), paving the way for WTO accession. In March, John McCain's insurgent primary campaign collapsed in South Carolina after his New Hampshire landslide, handing Bush the Republican nomination. Vermont enacted the nation's first civil-union law in April. The November 7 elections produced a 50-50 Senate — the first evenly divided Senate since 1881.

    Domestic Policy

    Domestic governance unfolded under a lame-duck Clinton and a Republican Congress, so legislative output was modest and largely bipartisan. Beyond PNTR with China and the AGOA/Caribbean Basin Trade Partnership package signed May 22, Clinton signed the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act, the Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act (creating T- and U-visas), the Electronic Signatures Act (E-SIGN) giving digital signatures legal parity, the Iran Nonproliferation Act, and the LIFE Act on immigration adjustment. The Commodity Futures Modernization Act, buried in December's omnibus, exempted most over-the-counter derivatives — including credit-default swaps — from CFTC regulation, reflecting the era's bipartisan deregulatory consensus. The FY2000 unified budget surplus was ~$236 billion, the largest in nominal terms to that point, fueled by dot-com capital-gains revenue; debates raged over the disposition of projected trillion-dollar surpluses, with Bush proposing tax cuts and Gore a Social Security lockbox, but no major legislation passed. Clinton exercised power through regulation, finalizing the Roadless Rule protecting 58.5 million acres of national forest, expansive Antiquities Act designations, and long-pending OSHA ergonomics rules (later nullified in 2001). The Supreme Court issued landmark rulings including Stenberg v. Carhart (striking Nebraska's partial-birth abortion ban), Boy Scouts v. Dale (upholding exclusion of gay scoutmasters), Dickerson v. United States (affirming Miranda 7-2), and United States v. Morrison (striking VAWA's civil-remedy provision).

    Foreign Policy

    Clinton's final year was consumed by unfinished business. In the Middle East, the July Camp David II summit foundered over Jerusalem, refugees, and territory; the December 23 Clinton Parameters outlined the most detailed US framework for a two-state solution to that point but the outbreak of the Second Intifada made agreement impossible. In November Clinton became the first US president to visit Vietnam since the war. In March he visited India and Pakistan to stabilize South Asia after the 1998 nuclear tests and 1999 Kargil war. He made an August trip to Nigeria and Tanzania in support of AGOA. Secretary of State Madeleine Albright visited Pyongyang in October — the highest-level US visit ever — and met Kim Jong Il on limits to the North's missile program; Clinton considered but declined a presidential trip, and the deal died with the administration. In the Balkans, Slobodan Milosevic was defeated in September Yugoslav elections and forced from power in the October 5 Bulldozer Revolution; roughly 5,000 US troops remained in Kosovo and Bosnia. Counterterrorism was the neglected crisis: the millennium plot (Ahmed Ressam's December 1999 border arrest) dominated early-2000 policy, and the response to the Cole was hesitant — the CIA and FBI could not definitively attribute the attack before Clinton left office. The US maintained no-fly zones over Iraq under Operations Northern and Southern Watch, with routine bombing of air defenses throughout the year.

    Important Elections

    In the presidential race, Bush and running mate Dick Cheney (former Defense Secretary and Halliburton CEO, who had led Bush's VP search and selected himself) defeated Gore and running mate Senator Joe Lieberman of Connecticut — the first Jewish major-party nominee for national office. Ralph Nader took 2.74% on the Green Party line; his 97,488 votes in Florida vastly exceeded the Bush margin, making his campaign among the most consequential third-party performances in modern American history. Pat Buchanan took about 0.4% on the Reform Party ticket. The final popular vote was Gore 50,999,897 (48.4%) to Bush 50,456,002 (47.9%) — the first popular-vote-losing electoral winner since 1888. Electoral vote: Bush 271, Gore 266 (one DC elector abstained in protest of DC's lack of representation). The House remained narrowly Republican at 221-212, with Speaker Dennis Hastert continuing. The Senate went to 50-50: Democrats picked up seats in Delaware (Carper), Florida (Nelson), Michigan (Stabenow), Minnesota (Dayton), Missouri (the posthumously-elected Mel Carnahan, whose widow Jean was appointed to the seat, defeating incumbent John Ashcroft), and Washington (Cantwell). Hillary Rodham Clinton became the first sitting First Lady elected to public office, beating Rick Lazio 55-43 in New York. Cheney's tie-breaking vote would give Republicans nominal Senate control starting January 20, 2001 — until Jim Jeffords flipped to independent in May 2001 and handed Democrats the majority.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Domestic box office was led by How the Grinch Stole Christmas ($260M with Jim Carrey), Cast Away ($233M, Zemeckis and Hanks with Wilson the volleyball), Mission: Impossible 2 ($215M), Gladiator ($188M — Ridley Scott's May release with Russell Crowe), What Women Want ($183M), The Perfect Storm ($182M), Meet the Parents ($166M), X-Men ($157M — the film that legitimized the modern superhero franchise), Scary Movie ($157M), and What Lies Beneath ($155M). Critical highlights included Traffic and Erin Brockovich (both Soderbergh, making him the first director in 60 years to earn dual Best Director nominations), Almost Famous (Cameron Crowe's love letter to '70s rock journalism), Requiem for a Dream (Aronofsky), Wonder Boys, You Can Count on Me, O Brother, Where Art Thou? (the Coens, whose bluegrass soundtrack became a phenomenon), Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (Ang Lee's foreign-language crossover, ultimately over $128M domestic), Memento (Nolan's breakthrough), American Psycho, and High Fidelity. The Best Picture Oscar awarded in March 2000 went to American Beauty for the 1999 film year; Gladiator would take the prize a year later. Tom Hanks lost 55 pounds for Cast Away, and X-Men's success cleared the runway for Spider-Man and a decade later the MCU.

    Music

    Teen pop and rap-rock defined mainstream radio, with NSYNC's No Strings Attached the year's biggest album by a wide margin — 2.4 million copies in its first week (a record that stood 15 years) and roughly 9.9 million US units for the year — followed by Eminem's The Marshall Mathers LP (May 23, 1.76 million first-week, eventually diamond) and Britney Spears's Oops!... I Did It Again (May 16, 1.3 million first-week). Massive sellers also included Creed, 3 Doors Down, matchbox twenty, Faith Hill's Breathe, Destiny's Child, Nelly's Country Grammar, and Linkin Park's Hybrid Theory (October). Dominant singles included Faith Hill's Breathe, Destiny's Child's Say My Name and Independent Women Pt. I, Sisqo's Thong Song, Vertical Horizon's Everything You Want, Aaliyah's Try Again, Madonna's Music, and Eminem's The Real Slim Shady. Nu-metal and rap-rock peaked (Limp Bizkit's Chocolate Starfish and the Hot Dog Flavored Water dropped in October with over a million first-week), teen pop reached its bubble top, hip-hop moved to the center of pop's gravity, country was quietly enormous, Moby's Play reached suburbia through ad licensing, and Radiohead's Kid A (Oct 2) shocked the industry by debuting at #1 without a conventional single. Industry album sales hit near-record highs — the last hurrah before Napster and iTunes rewrote the business. The Album of the Year Grammy awarded in February 2001 went to Steely Dan's Two Against Nature over Eminem, a snub widely criticized at the time.

    Television

    Reality TV as we know it began on May 31 when Survivor: Borneo premiered on CBS; its August 23 finale drew 51.7 million viewers, and CBS immediately booked Survivor: The Australian Outback for the post-Super Bowl slot. Who Wants to Be a Millionaire had America asking Regis Philbin's Is that your final answer? three nights a week on ABC, and Big Brother also debuted (July, CBS). The economics of reality programming became undeniable. Scripted television was in a golden run: The Sopranos aired its second season on HBO from January to April, The West Wing (in its second season by fall) swept five Emmys in September including Outstanding Drama, ER remained NBC's ratings juggernaut, and Friends (season 7) held the Nielsen top five. Malcolm in the Middle debuted in January to huge buzz, and CSI: Crime Scene Investigation launched October 6 to instant franchise status; Gilmore Girls and Curb Your Enthusiasm both bowed in October. Cable news was ascendant — Fox News was still a distant third but already reshaping political coverage during the recount — and MTV's TRL with Carson Daly remained the pop-culture town square for teens.

    Technology

    Consumer technology sat in a strange in-between. Household broadband was still a minority (roughly 4-6% of homes) and dial-up dominated; AOL claimed over 26 million subscribers and the modem screech was still the sound of getting online. Napster peaked near 80 million registered users before litigation began strangling it. The Sony PlayStation 2 launched in the US on October 26 to enormous demand and shortages, doubling as many families' first DVD player and accelerating DVD's takeover of VHS; the Sega Dreamcast was fading, and Nintendo GameCube and Microsoft Xbox loomed for 2001. Cellphones were everywhere but dumb: the Nokia 3310 launched in Q3 and became one of the best-selling phones ever, text messaging was still a novelty, Palm Pilots stood in for smartphones, and BlackBerry's 957 gained traction with executives for wireless email. The iPod would not arrive until October 2001; MP3 players in 2000 meant clunky Rio, Nomad, and Archos units with 32-64MB of storage. Google was two years old and gaining share against AltaVista, Yahoo, and Ask Jeeves; Amazon was still primarily a bookstore; eBay was booming; TiVo (launched 1999) was becoming a verb but had only a few hundred thousand subscribers. USB was displacing serial ports and CD burners were becoming standard on new PCs, powering the ripping-and-burning economy. Windows Me shipped in September to poor reviews; Windows 2000 (February) was better received in the enterprise.

    Social Trends

    Anti-globalization energy from Seattle 1999 rolled forward: the April 16-17 IMF/World Bank protests in Washington (A16) drew tens of thousands. The Million Mom March on May 14 brought several hundred thousand to the National Mall demanding gun control after Columbine and continuing mass shootings. Vermont enacted the nation's first civil-union law in April, effective July 1 — the first legal recognition of same-sex partnerships in America — and the Take Back Vermont backlash cost several state legislators their seats in November. The Boy Scouts of America v. Dale decision on June 28 upheld the BSA's right to exclude gay scoutmasters and triggered sustained protest and corporate sponsorship withdrawals. The Confederate flag came down from atop the South Carolina statehouse on July 1 after NAACP-led boycotts. The 2000 Census counted 281.4 million Americans (up 13.2% from 1990, the largest decennial numerical increase to that point), let respondents check more than one race for the first time (6.8 million did), recorded Hispanic Americans at 35.3 million and roughly equal in number to African Americans, and confirmed that non-Hispanic whites were now a minority in California. Sun Belt suburbs — Phoenix, Las Vegas, Atlanta, Charlotte, Dallas, Houston — grew fastest; the Rust Belt continued its slow decline. The Southern Baptist Convention revised its Baptist Faith and Message in June to bar women pastors and add wifely submission language, prompting Jimmy Carter to leave the SBC in October. Pope John Paul II declared a Jubilee Year and issued the March 12 Day of Pardon apologizing for church members' historic sins.

    Crime

    The Wendy's massacre in Flushing, Queens on May 24 killed five employees, and the Wichita Massacre in December shocked Kansas. The still-unsolved JonBenet Ramsey case returned to headlines with grand jury developments. Illinois Governor George Ryan declared a moratorium on executions in January after 13 death-row exonerations — an inflection in the national death-penalty debate. The Rampart LAPD corruption scandal continued to unfold, ultimately leading to over 100 overturned convictions. In February, a New York jury acquitted the four NYPD officers who had shot Amadou Diallo in 1999, triggering protests. In Mount Morris Township, Michigan, a first-grade boy shot and killed classmate Kayla Rolland on February 29, the youngest shooter in a US school killing. The federal Microsoft antitrust case dominated white-collar law, with Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson's April 3 monopoly finding and June 7 breakup order. Napster and RIAA litigation created a new kind of everyday piracy criminality that turned millions of college students into technical lawbreakers.

    Sports

    Kurt Warner's St. Louis Rams beat the Tennessee Titans 23-16 in Super Bowl XXXIV (January 30 in Atlanta) on The Tackle, Mike Jones stopping Kevin Dyson one yard short as time expired. Shaq and Kobe's Lakers took the NBA Finals 4-2 over Indiana in June, with Shaq claiming regular-season MVP, Finals MVP, and All-Star MVP. The New Jersey Devils beat the Dallas Stars 4-2 for the Stanley Cup on Jason Arnott's double-overtime winner in Game 6. In October the Yankees beat the Mets 4-1 in the first Subway Series since 1956, Derek Jeter took MVP, and the Roger Clemens-Mike Piazza broken-bat incident in Game 2 defined the edge. The Sydney Olympics (Sept 15 - Oct 1) saw the US lead the medal table with 93 medals (37 gold); Marion Jones won five (later stripped for doping), the US women's soccer team took silver, Rulon Gardner shocked Aleksandr Karelin for Greco-Roman gold, and Michael Johnson took the 400m. Oklahoma claimed the BCS championship over Florida State. And Tiger Woods delivered arguably the greatest single season in golf history, winning the US Open by a still-record 15 strokes at Pebble Beach in June, the Open at St Andrews, and the PGA Championship, setting the stage for the Tiger Slam completion at the 2001 Masters.

    Public Mood

    Journalism kept reaching for a word for the vibe and settling on anxious prosperity. The economy was still strong on paper — 4% unemployment, GDP growth above 4%, a record federal surplus — but dot-com layoffs were front-page news by fall, gasoline was pinching household budgets, and the market had begun its long slide from the March peak. The election itself produced a peculiar national mood, half-farce and half-constitutional crisis, that Time, Newsweek, and the New York Times covered with a mix of exhaustion and civic dread. Frank Rich and Maureen Dowd wrote about a country bored with peace and prosperity; the words malaise and distracted appear repeatedly in end-of-year retrospectives. Late-night monologues about hanging chads, Regis catchphrases, and Britney's snake at the VMAs were the shared texts. There was no premonition — even from the USS Cole — that the following September would upend the decade. If the '90s ended with Y2K passing quietly, 2000 was the year the country coasted a little too confidently on its own boom, sold each other pet-food-by-mail stocks, argued about butterfly ballots, and watched Richard Hatch win a million dollars naked on an island.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Americans in December of 2000 believed the year's central drama was the 36-day Florida recount and the question of which centrist would inherit a peaceful, prosperous country. They believed the important economic story was whether Alan Greenspan could engineer a soft landing from a booming market. They believed the important cultural stories were reality TV, Napster, and the browser wars. They believed the important foreign stories were the Middle East peace process and the humanitarian-intervention debate left over from Kosovo. The Elian Gonzalez custody drama, the Firestone/Ford Explorer tire recall, the AOL-Time Warner merger, and the Wen Ho Lee espionage case all received far more sustained coverage than the stories that would actually define the next twenty years.

    What actually mattered

    What actually mattered was the collapse of a stock-market bubble that would define the decade's investment culture, the entry of China into the global trading system on terms that would reshape American manufacturing and eventually American politics, the maturation of an al-Qaeda network in Afghanistan that would strike New York and Washington eleven months later, the constitutional precedent of a Supreme Court resolving a presidential election on partisan lines, and the political ascent of Vladimir Putin in Moscow. Vermont's civil-union law, covered as a niche state story, was the hinge on which the same-sex marriage movement would pivot. Almost none of these were headline stories in December of 2000.

    Most forgotten story

    The Wen Ho Lee case. For much of 1999 and the first half of 2000 it was treated as one of the gravest espionage scandals since the Rosenbergs, with front-page New York Times coverage the paper later formally apologized for. When the case collapsed in September and a federal judge apologized to Lee from the bench for the government's conduct, the story evaporated. It is almost never referenced today, yet it was a foundational episode in the modern debates over racial profiling of Chinese-American scientists, Department of Justice overreach, and press credulity toward national-security leaks — debates that resurfaced repeatedly around the China Initiative of the late 2010s.

    Most underrated story

    The USS Cole bombing on October 12. Seventeen dead sailors on a Navy destroyer, an attack claimed by an organization the intelligence community had been tracking for two years, and essentially no American military response — a fact Osama bin Laden himself later cited as confirmation that the United States was a paper tiger. Retrospective histories, from the 9/11 Commission Report to Steve Coll's Ghost Wars to Lawrence Wright's The Looming Tower, treat the Cole as the decisive missed opportunity of the pre-9/11 period. At the time, buried under Bush-Gore coverage, it received a fraction of the attention devoted to Elian Gonzalez.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    How little any of the political conversation in 2000 was about foreign policy or terrorism. In the three presidential debates terrorism was barely mentioned; the closest either candidate came was a brief exchange about rogue states. Bush campaigned on a humble foreign policy and against nation-building; Gore emphasized fiscal discipline and Medicare. Less than a year later the entire framework of American public life would be reorganized around a threat that neither candidate — and neither party's platform — treated as a top-tier concern. From today's vantage the near-total absence of national-security debate in a campaign held in the same year as the Cole bombing is genuinely startling.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Elian Gonzalez custody drama and the April 22 pre-dawn INS raidConsumed cable news for six months and produced an iconic photograph, but at the national level changed almost nothing except arguably hardening Cuban-American opposition to Gore in Florida; evaporated the moment the plane took Elian back to Havana.
    Y2K bug remediation and midnight anxietyThe federal government spent an estimated $100 billion in remediation; when nothing happened at midnight, coverage vanished within days, leaving a cautionary tale about threat inflation and some quietly modernized legacy code.
    The Firestone/Ford Explorer tire recallThe summer's runaway story with congressional hearings and hundreds of deaths produced the TREAD Act, then largely disappeared from public memory, subsumed by later product-liability sagas.
    The AOL-Time Warner merger as the definitive statement of internet-era dominanceHailed on front pages as the deal that proved old media had been swallowed by new media, then collapsed within eighteen months into what is now taught in business schools as the worst merger in corporate history.
    Reality-television mania around Survivor and Big BrotherSerious sociological analysis in 2000 framed reality TV as the end of scripted television and a coarsening of public life; it turned out to be a durable programming genre but a minor cultural marker rather than the civilizational shift columnists described.
    The Wen Ho Lee espionage caseFront-page fixation for months on the Los Alamos scientist accused of passing nuclear secrets to China; when the case collapsed in September and a federal judge apologized from the bench, the story faded from view.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The October 12 bombing of the USS Cole in Aden harborSame organization and operational template as September 11, and the muted response is now treated by the 9/11 Commission and subsequent historians as one of the most consequential missed warnings in modern American history.
    The March 10 Nasdaq peak at 5,048Reported at the time as a milestone rather than a top; the subsequent 78% decline over two and a half years wiped out roughly $5 trillion in market value and ended the technology exuberance that had defined the late 1990s.
    Congressional approval of Permanent Normal Trade Relations with China in OctoberReported as a technical trade matter with modest opposition from unions; now widely regarded as the single most consequential economic policy decision of the decade, driving the China Shock that hollowed out Midwestern manufacturing and plausibly reshaped American politics.
    Vladimir Putin's May 7 inauguration as President of RussiaNoted respectfully as the orderly succession from Boris Yeltsin, with very few American commentators identifying him as a KGB revanchist; two decades of Russian foreign policy from Georgia in 2008 to Ukraine in 2022 trace directly to that quiet handoff.
    Vermont's civil-union law signed by Governor Howard Dean on April 26Covered as a niche state story and a political liability for Dean; in fact the legal and rhetorical hinge on which the entire same-sex marriage movement pivoted toward Massachusetts in 2003 and Obergefell in 2015.
    The outbreak of the Second Intifada in late SeptemberFramed as another chapter in a familiar regional conflict; in fact ended the Oslo peace process, hardened the politics that shaped the post-9/11 US posture toward the Middle East, and reset the assumptions of a generation of American foreign-policy makers.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Supreme Court Halts Florida Recount 5-4; Gore Concedes as Bush Claims Presidency
    2. AOL to Buy Time Warner in $165 Billion Deal, Largest Merger in History
    3. Federal Agents Seize Elian Gonzalez in Pre-Dawn Miami Raid
    4. NASDAQ Crosses 5,000 for First Time as 'New Economy' Vindicated
    5. Federal Judge Orders Breakup of Microsoft in Landmark Antitrust Ruling
    6. Vermont Becomes First State to Legalize Civil Unions for Same-Sex Couples
    7. Camp David Summit Collapses; Clinton Blames Arafat as Peace Push Stalls
    8. Firestone Recalls 6.5 Million Tires as Deaths on Ford Explorers Mount
    9. Suicide Bombers Strike USS Cole in Yemen; 17 US Sailors Dead
    10. Scientists Unveil Working Draft of Human Genome; Clinton Hails 'Language of God'

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe Y2K bug proved a dud in the first minutes of the year, quietly ending a multi-year IT-spending surge. On January 10, AOL announced its $165 billion acquisition of Time Warner — then the largest merger in history and, in retrospect, a bell-ringing top for internet-era valuations. Illinois Governor George Ryan declared a moratorium on executions after 13 death-row exonerations, a national inflection in the death-penalty debate. Malcolm in the Middle debuted on Fox to major buzz, and The Sopranos began its second season on HBO. Super Bowl XXXIV ended on January 30 with Kurt Warner's Rams beating the Titans 23-16 on Mike Jones's tackle a yard short of the goal line.
    FebruaryThe FOMC raised the federal funds target 25 bps to 5.75% on February 2, continuing the tightening cycle. Windows 2000 shipped and was well received in the enterprise. A New York jury acquitted the four NYPD officers who had shot Amadou Diallo in 1999, triggering protests. Unemployment fell toward its 30-year low. On February 29, a first-grade boy shot and killed classmate Kayla Rolland in Mount Morris Township, Michigan — the youngest shooter in a US school killing.
    MarchThe Nasdaq Composite peaked at an intraday high near 5,132 and a closing high of 5,048.62 on March 10, having doubled in a year — a level not seen again for fifteen years. On March 21 the Fed raised the funds rate 25 bps to 6.00%. John McCain's insurgent primary campaign collapsed in South Carolina, handing Bush the Republican nomination; Bill Bradley conceded to Gore after Super Tuesday. Clinton visited India and Pakistan to stabilize South Asia. Pope John Paul II issued the Day of Pardon apologizing for church members' historical sins. NSYNC's No Strings Attached moved 2.4 million copies in its first week, a record that would stand for 15 years.
    AprilOn April 3, Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson ruled Microsoft an illegal monopolist. On April 22, federal agents executed a pre-dawn armed raid on the Miami home of Elian Gonzalez's relatives, producing Alan Diaz's Pulitzer-winning photograph. On April 26, Governor Howard Dean signed Vermont's civil-union law, the first legal recognition of same-sex partnerships in the country. Metallica sued Napster after finding a demo of I Disappear leaked online, opening the file-sharing wars. Tens of thousands of anti-globalization protesters converged on Washington for A16 IMF/World Bank actions. Unemployment touched a 30-year low of 3.8%.
    MayThe FOMC hiked the funds rate a full 50 bps to 6.50% on May 16 — the largest single move of the cycle and the final hike for the year. The Million Mom March brought several hundred thousand demonstrators to the National Mall demanding gun control. NSYNC's dominance continued while Britney Spears's Oops!... I Did It Again dropped on May 16 (1.3 million first-week) and Eminem's The Marshall Mathers LP dropped on May 23 (1.76 million first-week). Vladimir Putin was inaugurated as President of Russia on May 7, the orderly succession from Boris Yeltsin. Consumer confidence hit an all-time high of 144.7. Survivor: Borneo premiered on CBS on May 31.
    JuneThe Supreme Court closed its term with a burst of major decisions: Stenberg v. Carhart striking Nebraska's partial-birth abortion ban 5-4, Boy Scouts v. Dale upholding the BSA's exclusion of gay scoutmasters 5-4, Dickerson v. United States affirming Miranda 7-2, and Morrison striking the civil-remedy provision of VAWA. On June 7 Judge Jackson ordered the breakup of Microsoft. Tiger Woods won the US Open at Pebble Beach by a still-record 15 strokes. The Human Genome Project's working draft was announced with Clinton at the White House hailing it as the language of God. The Shaq-and-Kobe Lakers took the NBA Finals over Indiana. Pfizer completed its ~$90 billion hostile purchase of Warner-Lambert. Elian Gonzalez returned to Cuba. The Southern Baptist Convention revised its Baptist Faith and Message to bar women pastors and add wifely submission language.
    JulyCamp David II opened on July 11, with Clinton personally shuttling between Barak and Arafat; the summit collapsed by July 25 over Jerusalem, refugees, and territory. The Confederate flag came down from the South Carolina statehouse dome on July 1. Vermont's civil-union law took effect the same day. A Ninth Circuit stay temporarily halted a district-court injunction against Napster. The Concorde crashed outside Paris on July 25, killing 113. Tiger Woods won the Open Championship at St Andrews to complete the career Grand Slam. Bush announced Dick Cheney as his running mate; the Republican convention opened at month's end in Philadelphia. Big Brother debuted on CBS.
    AugustTiger Woods won the PGA Championship, his third major of the year. The Democratic convention nominated Al Gore and Senator Joe Lieberman of Connecticut, the first Jewish major-party nominee for national office. Survivor: Borneo's finale drew 51.7 million viewers as Richard Hatch won the million. Clinton traveled to Nigeria and Tanzania supporting AGOA. Firestone's massive tire recall on Ford Explorers accelerated, with congressional hearings looming and hundreds of deaths under investigation. Ford and Firestone traded blame publicly.
    SeptemberOPEC discipline and low inventories pushed WTI crude to nearly $37 a barrel — a level unseen since the 1990 Gulf War; the Clinton administration announced a coordinated 30-million-barrel Strategic Petroleum Reserve release. On September 21, Intel warned on Q3 revenue and lost 22% in a day; on September 28, Apple guided down and fell about 52% — tech bellwethers were breaking. The Sydney Summer Olympics opened on September 15, with the US eventually leading the medal table (Marion Jones's five medals, Rulon Gardner's shock of Karelin, Michael Johnson's 400m gold). The Second Intifada erupted on September 28 after Ariel Sharon's Temple Mount visit. Slobodan Milosevic lost the Yugoslav election, opening the way for his October ouster. The Wen Ho Lee case collapsed with a plea to a single count and a federal judge's apology from the bench.
    OctoberOn October 12, al-Qaeda suicide bombers struck the USS Cole in Aden harbor, killing 17 US sailors — barely noticed in an election-consumed news cycle. Congress passed Permanent Normal Trade Relations for China, paving the way for WTO accession. Slobodan Milosevic was forced from power in the October 5 Bulldozer Revolution. Secretary Albright visited Pyongyang, meeting Kim Jong Il on missile limits. The Yankees beat the Mets 4-1 in the first Subway Series since 1956. The Sony PlayStation 2 launched in the US on October 26 to enormous demand and shortages, doubling as many families' first DVD player. CSI: Crime Scene Investigation premiered, and Radiohead's Kid A shocked the industry by debuting at #1.
    NovemberElection night on November 7 produced pandemonium: networks called Florida for Gore, retracted, called it for Bush, retracted again. The 537-vote margin in Florida triggered a 36-day recount saga defined by hanging chads, butterfly ballots, and Secretary of State Katherine Harris. Democrats gained Senate seats to produce a 50-50 tie, and Hillary Clinton became the first sitting First Lady elected to public office. Pets.com liquidated on November 9, nine months after its IPO — the emblem of the dot-com bust. Clinton became the first US president to visit Vietnam since the war. Manufacturing decelerated further, with ISM slipping into contraction.
    DecemberThe Florida Supreme Court ordered a statewide manual recount on December 8; the US Supreme Court stayed the recount on December 9 and, in a 5-4 ruling on December 12 (with 7-2 on the underlying equal-protection concern), effectively awarded the presidency to George W. Bush. Gore conceded on December 13. Congress passed the Commodity Futures Modernization Act as part of a $450 billion omnibus signed December 21, exempting most over-the-counter derivatives from CFTC regulation. Clinton issued the December 23 Clinton Parameters for Israeli-Palestinian peace. JP Morgan agreed to be acquired by Chase Manhattan for roughly $36 billion. The Wichita Massacre shocked Kansas. WTI crude ended the year near $26-27; the 10-year Treasury yield closed near 5.1%, well below the 6.50% fed funds rate — a fully inverted curve heading into 2001.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~4.1% real (current BEA vintage; reported near 5% at the time)
    Inflation3.4% CPI-U year-over-year (December); core CPI ~2.6%
    Unemployment4.0% annual average; 3.8% low in April (30-year low); 3.9% in December
    S&P 500 return-9.1% total return (price return ~-10.1%)
    Dow return-4.9% total return (price return ~-6.2%)
    Fed funds rate6.50% year-end (raised from 5.50% via three hikes in Feb, Mar, and May)
    Oil priceWTI ~$26-27/bbl at year-end; peaked near $37/bbl in September
    Gold price~$273/oz year-end (near 20-year low)
    Population281,421,906 (2000 Census; +13.2% from 1990)
    Median home priceN/A
    National debt~$5.67 trillion gross federal debt at year-end; debt held by the public ~$3.4 trillion and declining amid a ~$236 billion unified surplus

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2000 was to live inside a paradox that only became visible in the rearview mirror. The country was, by almost any measurable standard, at the top of the postwar mountain. Unemployment was at a thirty-year low, the federal budget was in surplus for the third straight year, inflation was tame outside the gas pump, and the Cold War had been over long enough that the very idea of a peer adversary felt like a period piece. Newsweek and Time and the network anchors reached repeatedly for the word malaise to describe the public mood, not because anything was going wrong but because the American middle class had run out of large, obvious things to worry about and was left contemplating hanging chads, celebrity divorces, and the surreal spectacle of Richard Hatch outmaneuvering a Rhode Island truck driver on a beach in Borneo.

    At the same time, the year had a persistent low-grade static of unease. Gasoline was pinching household budgets in a way it had not for a decade. Dot-com companies that had been valued in billions in March were laying off staff by November. The Nasdaq had lost 39% for the year. The AOL-Time Warner merger, announced in January as the definitive statement that new media had swallowed old, was already looking suspect. The presidential election, widely predicted to be boring, produced a 36-day constitutional crisis, a 537-vote margin in Florida, and a 5-4 Supreme Court decision that half the country never accepted. The Confederate flag came down in South Carolina, Vermont recognized civil unions, the Southern Baptists forbade women pastors, and Jimmy Carter walked out of his church. The Second Intifada began, the Elian Gonzalez raid produced the year's iconic photograph, and seventeen sailors died on the USS Cole in an attack that most Americans could not have located on a map two weeks later.

    What made 2000 distinctive was the way genuine peace and genuine prosperity coexisted with a diffuse sense that the machinery underneath was making strange noises. Americans debated whether the Fed could engineer a soft landing, whether Napster was theft, and whether reality TV was the end of civilization. They almost never debated terrorism, Chinese manufacturing, or the risk that a Supreme Court might one day pick a president. It felt like an ordinary year, maybe a slightly boring one. It was, in fact, the last one of its kind — the closing bar of the long 1990s, played in a room where nobody yet knew the music was about to stop.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2001
    Econ -1Mark -2Poli +1Cult -1Over -2
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The United States entered 2001 exhausted from the closest, most contested presidential election in modern history and still working off the hangover of the dot-com bubble. George W. Bush took office on January 20 having lost the popular vote to Al Gore, installed by a 5-4 Supreme Court decision that a substantial share of the country regarded as illegitimate. A hung 50-50 Senate, a stalling economy sliding toward its first recession in a decade, and a slow-news summer dominated by the Chandra Levy affair, the "Summer of the Shark," and a fractious debate over embryonic stem-cell research gave the first eight months of the year a strangely small, hangover-ish character — Time and Newsweek ran cover stories about missing interns and beach maulings while Alan Greenspan quietly cut interest rates again and again to arrest a mild but real downturn.

    Then, on the morning of September 11, nineteen al-Qaeda hijackers destroyed the World Trade Center, struck the Pentagon, and crashed a fourth airliner into a Pennsylvania field, killing nearly 3,000 people in the deadliest attack on American soil in the nation's history. The country that existed on September 12 bore only partial resemblance to the one that had existed the day before. Financial markets closed for the longest stretch since the Great Depression; Congress passed the Authorization for Use of Military Force and, within weeks, the USA PATRIOT Act by near-unanimous margins; the United States invaded Afghanistan on October 7 and had toppled the Taliban by mid-November; and a still-unsolved anthrax mail campaign killed five more Americans and closed Senate office buildings for months, layering a second, quieter terror onto the first. Bush's approval rating hit 90 percent, the highest ever recorded, as a fractured country rallied with a speed and unity that surprised nearly everyone.

    By year's end the emergency had begun to acquire the outlines of routine: Hamid Karzai had been installed in Kabul, TSA had federalized airport security, and Americans were adjusting to a newly militarized, security-conscious normal even as Enron's spectacular bankruptcy — the largest in U.S. history to that point — served notice that the corporate excesses of the boom years had not fully been reckoned with. Beneath the year's dominant story, a set of quieter developments — China's accession to the WTO, Apple's unveiling of the iPod, the launch of a free online encyclopedia called Wikipedia, Bush's tax cut and the Fed's aggressive easing cycle, and the withdrawal from the ABM Treaty — passed with comparatively little notice but would prove, in the judgment of later historians, at least as consequential to the shape of the following two decades as the attacks themselves.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy-1A mild, NBER-dated eight-month recession (March-November) produced only about 1% full-year GDP growth and a modest 0.3% peak-to-trough output decline, but job losses of 1.7 million, rising unemployment, and collapsing consumer confidence made the downturn feel considerably worse than the output numbers alone suggested.
    Markets-2A second consecutive losing year for the S&P 500 and a further 21% NASDAQ decline (roughly 60% below its 2000 peak), the longest NYSE closure since the Great Depression, moribund IPO issuance, and the largest corporate bankruptcy in U.S. history to date made 2001 a genuinely severe year for equities, cushioned only by a sharp fourth-quarter rally.
    Politics+1Despite a disputed election, a Senate that flipped control mid-year, and the deadliest attack on American soil in history, the federal government responded with rapid bipartisan cohesion, peaceful transfers of power at every level, and functioning institutions — a creditable performance overshadowed by durable civil-liberties costs that would only become fully apparent later.
    Culture-1Real creative vitality in music, television, and film, and an epochal (if unrecognized) product launch in the iPod, were overshadowed and reoriented by mass trauma, a climate of fear from the anthrax letters, and the abrupt foreclosure of pre-9/11 irony and escapism.
    Overall Mood-2A once-in-a-generation act of mass violence against civilians on home soil, compounded by a bioterror scare that lingered for months, left the national psyche shaken and newly fearful even as the economy avoided true catastrophe and the political system briefly rallied around a shared sense of purpose.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. George W. Bush is inaugurated as the 43rd president after the Supreme Court's Bush v. Gore decision settles the disputed Florida recount
    2. The Chandra Levy disappearance and Congressman Gary Condit's affair dominate a slow, scandal-hungry pre-9/11 summer
    3. NASCAR legend Dale Earnhardt is killed in a last-lap crash at the Daytona 500
    4. Timothy McVeigh is executed for the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing, the first federal execution in 38 years
    5. Senator Jim Jeffords leaves the Republican Party, flipping control of the 50-50 Senate to the Democrats
    6. Bush signs a $1.35 trillion tax cut, and rebate checks of $300-$600 arrive in mailboxes that summer
    7. Nineteen al-Qaeda hijackers attack the World Trade Center and the Pentagon on September 11, killing nearly 3,000
    8. Anthrax-laced letters mailed to media outlets and senators kill five people and shut down Senate office buildings
    9. The United States invades Afghanistan, toppling the Taliban and taking Kabul by mid-November
    10. The USA PATRIOT Act becomes law and Enron collapses into the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history to that point

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy in 2001 absorbed three overlapping shocks: the ongoing deflation of the dot-com bubble that had peaked in March 2000, the September 11 attacks that struck the heart of the world's largest financial center, and the beginning of a corporate-accounting scandal wave that culminated in Enron's collapse. The National Bureau of Economic Research eventually dated a recession from March to November 2001, ending the record 120-month expansion that began in 1991, but the downturn was shallow in output terms even as it was harsh in the labor market. The housing sector, buoyed by plunging mortgage rates, acted as the economy's shock absorber throughout the year, while consumer confidence collapsed from euphoric January levels to a nine-year low by November.

    Important Statistics

    Full-year real GDP growth came in near +1.0%, the weakest since 1991, with peak-to-trough output down only about 0.3%. A net 1.735 million jobs were shed, and unemployment rose from 4.2% in February to 5.8% by December. Headline CPI decelerated sharply, from roughly 3.4% year-over-year in early 2001 to about 1.6% by December, with full-year average inflation near 2.8%. The 30-year mortgage rate averaged about 6.97%, the lowest annual average in a generation, and the median existing-home price rose to roughly $147,800, up about 6% year over year.

    Major Events

    Alan Greenspan's Fed cut the federal funds rate eleven times over the year, from 6.50% to 1.75%, including a surprise 50-basis-point inter-meeting cut on January 3 and further cuts immediately after the September 11 attacks. Bush signed the $1.35 trillion Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act on June 7, mailing rebate checks to households that summer; fiscal 2001 nonetheless closed with a $128 billion surplus, the last the federal government would record. The California electricity crisis produced rolling blackouts and PG&E's April Chapter 11 filing, and Enron's December 2 bankruptcy, the largest in U.S. history to that point, capped the year. Congress passed a $15 billion airline bailout within days of September 11 as the attacks devastated the aviation industry.

    Why this score

    Economy score: -1. A relatively mild recession with GDP still growing about 1% for the year, but real distress in labor markets, business investment, and consumer confidence, compounded by the singular shock of 9/11 and the onset of a corporate-scandal wave that undermined institutional trust — worse than a neutral year, but far short of the severe contractions of other eras.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities closed a second consecutive down year, an outcome not seen since 1973-74. The S&P 500 fell 13.0% in price terms (closing at 1,148.08), the Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 7.1% (closing at 10,021.50), and the tech-heavy NASDAQ Composite fell another 21.05% to 1,950.40, roughly 61% below its March 2000 peak. Markets closed after the September 11 attacks and did not reopen until September 17 — the longest NYSE closure since the Great Depression — and the Dow fell 7.13% on reopening day before an extraordinary fourth-quarter rally lifted the S&P back above its pre-attack level and the Dow back above 10,000 by year-end.

    Character

    Bear

    Major Companies

    Enron Corp. collapsed from $83 a share in January to under $1 by late November before filing for what was then the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history on December 2. The AOL Time Warner merger closed January 11 at a nominal $183 billion, the largest merger ever at the time and destined to become a byword for dot-com-era hubris, while Hewlett-Packard announced its $25 billion acquisition of Compaq in September. Bankruptcies also swept PG&E, Bethlehem Steel, Polaroid, and Excite@Home, and tech and telecom bellwethers Cisco, Lucent, Nortel, and Sun Microsystems suffered severe breadth deterioration.

    Investment Themes

    Flight to quality and fixed income dominated as the 10-year Treasury yield fell to near 5.05% and the Fed's easing cycle drove a refinancing boom; IPO issuance was essentially dormant for the year. Commodities told a disinflationary story, with WTI crude falling about 26% to finish near $19-20 a barrel and gold closing essentially flat near $278/oz at the tail end of its long secular bottom. Product launches that would define the coming decade — Apple's Mac OS X and first iPod, Microsoft's Windows XP and Xbox — passed largely unnoticed by markets fixated on the dot-com bust and the September attacks.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    George W. Bush was inaugurated January 20 following the Supreme Court's Bush v. Gore ruling, having lost the popular vote but prevailed 271-266 in the Electoral College. On May 24, Vermont Senator Jim Jeffords left the Republican Party, flipping control of the 50-50 Senate to the Democrats under Tom Daschle effective June 6. The Hainan Island EP-3 incident in April produced an 11-day standoff with China. Then, on September 11, nineteen al-Qaeda hijackers destroyed the World Trade Center, struck the Pentagon, and crashed United Flight 93 in Pennsylvania, killing nearly 3,000 people. Congress passed the Authorization for Use of Military Force on September 18 and the USA PATRIOT Act on October 26, both by overwhelming bipartisan margins, while a still-unsolved anthrax letter campaign killed five people and shuttered Senate office buildings for months.

    Domestic Policy

    Before September 11, Bush's domestic agenda centered on the June 7 tax cut, faith-based social service initiatives, education reform, and energy policy, including Vice President Cheney's controversial closed-door National Energy Policy task force. Bush's August 9 prime-time address announced a compromise allowing federal funding only for research on existing embryonic stem-cell lines. After the attacks, domestic policy collapsed into homeland security and economic stabilization: a $15 billion airline bailout, the Aviation and Transportation Security Act creating the Transportation Security Administration, and a new Office of Homeland Security under Tom Ridge. The bipartisan No Child Left Behind Act cleared Congress in December. Muslim, Arab, and South Asian Americans faced a sharp spike in hate crimes and federal scrutiny, including the secretive detention of more than 1,200 non-citizens.

    Foreign Policy

    Bush's foreign policy team began the year in a posture of skeptical unilateralism — rejecting the Kyoto Protocol and the International Criminal Court, distancing from Clinton-era North Korea engagement, and pursuing missile defense over Russian objections. September 11 transformed the picture entirely: NATO invoked Article 5 for the first time in its history, and the U.S. assembled a rapid international coalition, securing Pakistani cooperation and launching the invasion of Afghanistan on October 7. Kabul fell to the Northern Alliance on November 13, and the Bonn Agreement of December 5 installed Hamid Karzai as interim leader, though Osama bin Laden escaped U.S. forces at Tora Bora in December. Bush announced withdrawal from the 1972 ABM Treaty on December 13, and by year's end Rumsfeld and Wolfowitz were already directing planners toward Iraq.

    Important Elections

    In off-year elections on November 6, Democrats picked up both open governorships at stake: Mark Warner won in Virginia and Jim McGreevey won in New Jersey, an early signal that state-level politics had returned to pre-9/11 competitiveness. In New York City, Republican Michael Bloomberg — a political newcomer who spent some $74 million of his own money and was boosted by Rudy Giuliani's endorsement — narrowly defeated Democrat Mark Green to become mayor. Democrat James Hahn defeated Antonio Villaraigosa in a runoff for Los Angeles mayor. Giuliani himself, leaving office at year's end, was named Time's Person of the Year for his post-attack leadership.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Box office was dominated late in the year by twin fantasy blockbusters, Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone (November 16) and The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring (December 19), which reshaped Hollywood for the coming decade. Shrek became a $267 million hit and later won the first Best Animated Feature Oscar, while Pixar's Monsters, Inc. extended its studio's winning streak. Michael Bay's Pearl Harbor, released that May, took on a disturbing new resonance in retrospect, and studios scrambled to scrub Twin Towers imagery from posters for Spider-Man and other upcoming releases. Critically significant releases included Mulholland Drive, The Royal Tenenbaums, Donnie Darko (briefly pulled from some theaters for its plane-crash imagery), Memento, and Training Day, while A Beautiful Mind, released in late December, would go on to win Best Picture the following March.

    Music

    Linkin Park's Hybrid Theory was the year's top-selling album amid a nu-metal wave, while teen pop remained commercially dominant with releases from 'NSYNC, Britney Spears, and the Backstreet Boys. Hip-hop had a landmark year: Jay-Z's The Blueprint, released on September 11 itself, introduced producers Kanye West and Just Blaze, and Alicia Keys' debut Songs in A Minor launched a new R&B star. A garage-rock revival broke through with The Strokes' Is This It and The White Stripes' White Blood Cells.
