In the aftermath of the attacks, Alan Jackson's "Where Were You (When the World Stopped Turning)" and the Concert for New York City became touchstones of a new, explicitly patriotic musical mode, and Enya's "Only Time" became an unlikely hit after being played over news footage.

    Television

    Reality television's Survivor franchise remained dominant, while HBO cemented its prestige-drama identity with The Sopranos' third season and the June debut of Six Feet Under. Fox's 24, premiering November 6 with a real-time terrorism premise, landed with startling and unplanned timeliness, and The West Wing aired a hastily written post-9/11 special episode that was widely watched and debated. Cable news reached new cultural prominence as Fox News overtook CNN in prime-time ratings during the post-attack news cycle, a lasting shift in the media landscape.

    Technology

    Consumer technology sat at a transitional hinge: dial-up AOL remained the modal home connection even as broadband gained ground, and peer-to-peer file sharing exploded after a court shut down Napster in July. Apple unveiled the first iPod on October 23 to a muted reception, while Microsoft released Windows XP and launched the Xbox that November alongside Nintendo's GameCube. Wikipedia, a free open-edit encyclopedia founded on January 15, drew almost no attention at the time but would prove one of the era's most enduring creations.

    Social Trends

    Before September, the year's social energy centered on anti-globalization protests continuing from Seattle and a fierce debate over embryonic stem-cell research. After the attacks, a surge of patriotic display swept the country alongside a sharp spike in hate crimes against Muslim, Sikh, and Arab Americans, including the murder of Sikh gas-station owner Balbir Singh Sodhi in Arizona. Church attendance spiked briefly before receding to prior levels within weeks, and the bounds of acceptable public speech narrowed noticeably, as seen in the controversy over Bill Maher's comments on Politically Incorrect.

    Crime

    Timothy McVeigh was executed on June 11 for the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing, the first federal execution in 38 years. FBI counterintelligence agent Robert Hanssen was arrested in February for spying for Russia over 22 years, one of the worst intelligence breaches in U.S. history. Andrea Yates's drowning of her five children in Houston prompted a national conversation about postpartum psychosis, and the anthrax letters launched the FBI's largest-ever investigation. Overall violent crime continued its long decline from the 1990s.

    Sports

    Barry Bonds hit 73 home runs, shattering the single-season record at the height of the steroid era, and Tiger Woods completed the "Tiger Slam" by winning the Masters while holding all four major titles simultaneously. Dale Earnhardt was killed in a last-lap crash at the Daytona 500 in February, a national trauma for NASCAR near its cultural peak. The Arizona Diamondbacks beat the New York Yankees in a dramatic seven-game World Series that doubled as a post-9/11 civic ritual in New York, and an unheralded backup named Tom Brady led the New England Patriots into the following February's Super Bowl.

    Public Mood

    The year had two distinct moods. Through August, the national conversation felt small and hangover-ish, preoccupied with a stagnating economy, the Chandra Levy affair, and the contested legitimacy of the new administration. After September 11, the country moved through shock and grief, a burst of civic unity that drove Bush's approval to a record 90 percent, an anthrax-driven paranoid interlude that fall, and by December the beginnings of a wartime normalcy paired with a fierce, largely uninterrogated patriotism.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Before September, Americans believed the important stories of the year were whether the dot-com correction would be a soft landing, whether Congressman Gary Condit had been involved in Chandra Levy's disappearance, whether stem-cell research was morally permissible, and whether sharks were newly aggressive off American beaches. After September, the public conversation narrowed almost entirely to al-Qaeda, anthrax, the war in Afghanistan, and the fear of a second attack.

    What actually mattered

    Historians now emphasize a set of structural developments that ran largely beneath the headlines: China's accession to the WTO on December 11, which would reshape global manufacturing and American industrial employment for a generation; Apple's quiet unveiling of the iPod; the Bush tax cuts, whose ten-year sunset structured a decade of subsequent fiscal fights; Greenspan's aggressive rate-cutting cycle, later linked to the seeds of the mid-2000s housing bubble; and the internal Bush-administration deliberations that were already turning toward Iraq by year's end.

    Most forgotten story

    The Chandra Levy disappearance and the Gary Condit affair, which dominated wall-to-wall cable coverage for most of the summer and vanished from the national conversation almost overnight after September 11 — now remembered mainly as the emblem of a media culture with nothing bigger to cover.

    Most underrated story

    China's formal accession to the World Trade Organization on December 11, 2001, treated at the time as a back-page trade story overshadowed by the fall of Kandahar the same week, but now regarded by economic historians as one of the most consequential single events of the year for its role in reshaping global manufacturing, U.S. industrial employment, and the strategic rivalry that would define the following two decades.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    How much of 2001's lasting impact came not from the September 11 shock itself but from the deliberate policy choices made in its wake and alongside it — the AUMF, the PATRIOT Act, the tax cuts, the ABM withdrawal, and the WTO accession — an unusually large cluster of load-bearing decisions made in a single calendar year, most of them under-debated at the time.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Chandra Levy disappearance and the Gary Condit affairA lurid summer mystery inflated to national-crisis scale by cable news with nothing else to cover; it was instantly displaced by September 11 and today survives mainly as a case study in pre-9/11 tabloid news culture.
    Time magazine's "Summer of the Shark" panicA statistically unremarkable run of shark attacks was amplified into a national media obsession that vanished completely once September 11 gave the news cycle a genuine crisis to cover.
    The Hainan Island EP-3 incident with ChinaTreated at the time as a potentially era-defining confrontation with a rising China, it was resolved diplomatically within eleven days and quickly overshadowed by the far more consequential (and quieter) story of China's WTO accession that December.
    The Timothy McVeigh executionCovered as symbolic closure on 1990s domestic terrorism, that framing dissolved within three months as "terrorism" acquired an entirely new meaning after September 11.
    Dale Earnhardt's death at the Daytona 500A defining trauma for NASCAR's enormous fanbase that drove real safety reforms inside the sport, but had little effect on national life outside it.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    China's accession to the World Trade Organization (December 11)Now regarded by economic historians as the single most consequential economic event of the year, restructuring global manufacturing, accelerating the hollowing-out of American industrial employment, and setting the table for every subsequent U.S.-China dispute.
    Apple's unveiling of the iPod (October 23)Received tepidly at the time, it is now seen as the pivot on which Apple's transformation into a consumer-electronics giant turned, launching the personal-media revolution that led directly to the iPhone.
    Wikipedia's quiet launch (January 15)A side project that drew almost no press attention has since become the largest reference work in human history.
    The Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act (June 2001)Its ten-year sunset provisions became the fiscal architecture the United States lived inside for decades, contributing heavily to the shift from federal surplus to structural deficit.
    The withdrawal from the ABM Treaty (December 13)Buried under war coverage, it ended the cornerstone of the strategic nuclear order in place since 1972 and is now cited as an inflection point in the collapse of Cold War arms-control architecture.
    The August 6 Presidential Daily Brief titled "Bin Laden Determined to Strike in US"Unknown to the public in 2001, it became, after its 2004 declassification, perhaps the most-cited intelligence document in American history.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Bush Sworn In as 43rd President, Pledges to Restore Civility After Bitter Recount
    2. Missing Intern Chandra Levy and Rep. Gary Condit Dominate Summer News Cycle
    3. U.S. Spy Plane Down in China; 24 Crew Members Held on Hainan Island
    4. McVeigh Executed for Oklahoma City Bombing, First Federal Execution Since 1963
    5. U.S. ATTACKED
    6. America Strikes Back: U.S. and British Forces Bomb Taliban, al-Qaeda Targets in Afghanistan
    7. Anthrax Letter Closes Senate Offices as Bioterror Fears Grip the Nation
    8. Bush Signs Sweeping Anti-Terror Law, Granting Broad New Surveillance Powers
    9. Northern Alliance Enters Kabul as Taliban Flee South
    10. Enron Files Largest Bankruptcy in U.S. History

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryGeorge W. Bush was inaugurated as the 43rd president on January 20 following the Supreme Court's Bush v. Gore ruling, with large protests marking the day. The AOL Time Warner merger closed January 11 at a nominal $183 billion, the largest in history at the time, and the Fed made a surprise inter-meeting 50-basis-point rate cut on January 3 to counter the slowing economy. Wikipedia launched quietly on January 15, and iTunes debuted on January 9, both destined for outsized significance.
    FebruaryThe Senate narrowly confirmed John Ashcroft as Attorney General 42-58, reflecting sharp partisan polarization. FBI counterintelligence agent Robert Hanssen was arrested on February 18 for spying for Russia over 22 years, one of the worst intelligence breaches in U.S. history. That same day, NASCAR legend Dale Earnhardt was killed in a last-lap crash at the Daytona 500, a national trauma for stock-car racing's huge fanbase.
    MarchThe economy, unbeknownst to contemporaries, entered what the NBER would later date as the start of a recession, as the dot-com bust deepened and the NASDAQ continued its slide. Bush announced the U.S. would not implement the Kyoto Protocol on climate change, signaling an early unilateralist turn in foreign policy. Apple released Mac OS X on March 24, a foundational step in the company's eventual turnaround.
    AprilA U.S. Navy EP-3 surveillance plane collided with a Chinese fighter jet on April 1, forcing an emergency landing on Hainan Island and an 11-day standoff before the crew's release — the first foreign-policy crisis of the Bush presidency. California's electricity crisis worsened as PG&E filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on April 6 amid rolling blackouts. Tiger Woods completed the "Tiger Slam" by winning the Masters, and large anti-globalization protests greeted the Summit of the Americas in Quebec City.
    MayVermont Senator Jim Jeffords announced on May 24 that he would leave the Republican Party to become an independent caucusing with Democrats, setting up a mid-year flip of Senate control. Chandra Levy, a 24-year-old Washington intern, disappeared on May 1, beginning a saga that would dominate the summer's news cycle. Cheney's National Energy Policy task force produced its report amid a brewing transparency controversy, and Shrek opened to blockbuster business.
    JuneDemocrats formally took control of the Senate on June 6 as Tom Daschle replaced Trent Lott as Majority Leader, dramatically reshaping the legislative landscape. Bush signed the $1.35 trillion Economic Growth and Tax Relief Reconciliation Act on June 7, the largest tax cut since Reagan. Timothy McVeigh was executed on June 11 for the Oklahoma City bombing, the first federal execution in 38 years, while the Los Angeles Lakers swept to the NBA title and the Colorado Avalanche won the Stanley Cup.
    JulyThe Chandra Levy story, and Congressman Gary Condit's entanglement in it, reached a media saturation point that would later be seen as the high-water mark of pre-9/11 tabloid news culture. Time magazine's July 30 "Summer of the Shark" cover captured another slow-news-season media panic. A federal court effectively shut down Napster, accelerating a shift to newer peer-to-peer file-sharing services, and rebate checks from the June tax cut began arriving in mailboxes.
    AugustBush gave his first prime-time address on August 9, announcing a compromise policy permitting federal funding only for research on existing embryonic stem-cell lines. An August 6 Presidential Daily Brief titled "Bin Laden Determined to Strike in US" drew no public attention at the time but would later become one of the most scrutinized documents in American history. Consumer confidence continued sliding as the recession deepened, and the Danny Almonte Little League age-fraud scandal broke late in the month.
    SeptemberOn September 11, nineteen al-Qaeda hijackers destroyed the World Trade Center, struck the Pentagon, and crashed United Flight 93 in Pennsylvania, killing nearly 3,000 people in the deadliest attack on American soil in history; financial markets closed for a week, the longest NYSE shutdown since the Great Depression. Congress passed the Authorization for Use of Military Force on September 18, and Bush addressed a joint session of Congress on September 20 to overwhelming public support. Anthrax-laced letters, mailed beginning around September 18, would soon reach media outlets and senators, and Jay-Z released The Blueprint on the day of the attacks itself.
    OctoberThe United States and Britain began bombing Taliban and al-Qaeda targets in Afghanistan on October 7, opening Operation Enduring Freedom. The anthrax letter attacks intensified through the month, killing several victims and shutting down the Hart Senate Office Building, while Congress passed the USA PATRIOT Act on October 26 by overwhelming bipartisan margins. Apple unveiled the first iPod on October 23 to a muted reception, and Microsoft released Windows XP two days later; the Concert for New York City on October 20 became a major post-9/11 cultural event.
    NovemberKabul fell to the Northern Alliance on November 13 as the Taliban government collapsed under the weight of U.S. airstrikes and coalition ground operations. Off-year elections on November 6 saw Democrats win open governorships in Virginia and New Jersey and Republican newcomer Michael Bloomberg narrowly elected mayor of New York City. The Arizona Diamondbacks defeated the New York Yankees in a dramatic seven-game World Series that doubled as a civic ritual for a grieving New York, and Microsoft's Xbox and Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone both launched that month.
    DecemberEnron filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on December 2, the largest corporate bankruptcy in U.S. history to that point, following the collapse of a proposed rescue merger with Dynegy. China formally joined the World Trade Organization on December 11 after fifteen years of negotiation, an event that drew little attention amid war coverage but would prove enormously consequential. Bush announced U.S. withdrawal from the ABM Treaty on December 13, the Bonn Agreement installed Hamid Karzai as Afghanistan's interim leader, and The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring opened in theaters as the year closed on a note of uneasy, watchful recovery.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+1.0% real GDP for the year (weakest since 1991); NBER dated a mild recession from March to November 2001
    Inflation~2.8% average annual CPI, decelerating from ~3.4% YoY in early 2001 to ~1.6% YoY by December
    UnemploymentRose from 4.2% in February to 5.8% in December
    S&P 500 return-13.0% price return (-11.9% total return); closed at 1,148.08
    Dow return-7.1%; closed at 10,021.50
    Fed funds rateCut eleven times over the year, from 6.50% to 1.75% (-475 bp)
    Oil priceWTI crude fell about 26% to finish near $19-20 per barrel
    Gold price~$278/oz, essentially flat on the year
    Population281.4 million (2000 Census, released through 2001)
    Median home price~$147,800 median existing-home price (NAR), up about 6% year over year
    National debt~$5.77 trillion gross federal debt (~31.5% of GDP); FY2001 closed with a $128 billion surplus, the last U.S. budget surplus recorded to date

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2001 was to live through two entirely different years stitched together at the seam of a single Tuesday morning. For the first eight and a half months, the country felt tired and small. The bitter, unresolved argument over who had actually won the presidency lingered into the new administration; the stock market kept grinding lower as the dot-com fantasy died in slow motion; and the national conversation, starved of any larger drama, fixed obsessively on a missing congressional intern, a spate of shark attacks, and the ethics of stem-cell research. It was, as more than one writer later observed, a holiday from history — a country coasting on the fumes of 1990s prosperity and Clinton-era scandal fatigue, mildly anxious about a softening economy but fundamentally unserious about the world beyond its borders.

    Then came September 11, and the texture of daily life changed in ways that no single word quite captures. There was the raw, communal grief of the days immediately after — flags everywhere, strangers embracing, a president's approval rating reaching heights never before recorded. There was the genuine and remarkable spectacle of a bitterly divided Washington, fresh off a recount crisis and a Senate flip, passing sweeping legislation with near-unanimous votes and sending soldiers to Afghanistan within a month. And there was something darker underneath the unity: the anthrax letters that killed five people and closed the Senate's office buildings, the surveillance and detention powers granted almost without debate, the hate crimes against Muslim and Sikh Americans, and a pervasive, low-grade dread that another attack was always just days away. Baseball's improbable, cathartic World Series in Yankee Stadium and the crowds who gathered to sing at the Concert for New York City were not distractions from the fear so much as evidence of how urgently people needed something to put it aside.

    By December, normalcy of a strange new kind had begun to set in — a normalcy that included a federalized airport-security bureaucracy, a war already underway on the other side of the world, and constant, low-level vigilance that would have seemed paranoid a year earlier. Economically, most Americans were spared the worst: the recession was mild, mortgage rates were the lowest in a generation, and rebate checks softened the blow of a bad year. But the psychological ledger told a different story. The country had discovered, in the span of a single morning, that its enormous distance from the world's violence was not guaranteed, and that discovery reshaped everything from air travel to foreign policy to the boundaries of acceptable dissent. Few Americans in December 2001 could have named China's WTO accession or Apple's new iPod as the developments future historians would call pivotal — what they knew, viscerally, was that the year had cracked history in two, and that the country on the far side of that crack was warier, more frightened, and more united than the one that had entered it.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2002
    Econ +0Mark -3Poli +1Cult -1Over -1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2002 was the year of the counterpunch that curdled into overreach. Twelve months after 9/11, the United States was still inside the emotional field of the attacks: flags on porches, near-90 percent approval ratings for President Bush, an Afghanistan war that seemed to be going well, and a governing coalition confident enough to reorganize the federal executive around a new Department of Homeland Security and to authorize preemptive war against Iraq. In November, Republicans defied a century of midterm history and gained seats in both chambers of Congress, a feat that felt at the time like the electorate's endorsement of a national-security presidency and that hardened the political incentives to invade Iraq in March 2003.

    The economy told a very different story. The recession had technically ended in November 2001, but 2002 was a jobless recovery: real GDP grew under 2 percent, unemployment drifted from 5.7 to 6.0 percent, and nonfarm payrolls fell another 500,000. The Fed held the funds rate at 1.75 percent for eleven months and then cut to 1.25 percent in November as Ben Bernanke gave his famous deflation speech. Equity markets suffered their third straight down year, the worst of the three, with the S&P 500 down 22 percent in total return and the Nasdaq down another 32 percent, capitulating to their bear-market lows on October 9. The corporate-scandal parade (Enron's aftermath, WorldCom's $11 billion fraud, Adelphia, Tyco, Andersen's conviction) drove Sarbanes-Oxley and made "perp walk" a household phrase. Housing, propelled by 6 percent mortgages, was the singular bright spot, though nobody yet called it a bubble.

    Culturally the year felt watchful and grim. The Boston Globe Spotlight team broke the Catholic Church abuse story on January 6, detonating a decades-long institutional reckoning. The D.C. Beltway sniper paralyzed the capital for three weeks in October. Bruce Springsteen released The Rising, Spider-Man broke box-office records, American Idol premiered, and The Wire premiered on HBO. Time named the Whistleblowers Persons of the Year, an emblem for a country simultaneously flag-waving on the surface and losing faith in its institutions underneath. The stories that dominated the front pages have faded; the stories that ran below the fold (Friendster, China's first WTO year, North Korea's uranium admission, the ABM Treaty withdrawal, and Bush's West Point doctrine) are the ones historians now write books about.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+0A technically expanding but subpar jobless recovery with rising unemployment, historically low inflation, an easy Fed, and a housing bright spot, on balance neutral for the era.
    Markets-3The worst of three consecutive down years, with the S&P 500 off 22 percent, the Nasdaq off 32 percent, an October capitulation low, and the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history.
    Politics+1Unusually productive governance (Homeland Security, Sarbanes-Oxley, NCLB, McCain-Feingold, HAVA), a rare presidential-party midterm gain, and a stable Court, offset by early civil-liberties erosion and the seeds of the Iraq policy failure.
    Culture-1A watchful, transitional year of institutional scandal, sniper-and-abduction anxiety, and pre-war dread, only partly redeemed by strong output in film, music, and the birth of American Idol and prestige cable.
    Overall Mood-1A country that was flag-waving on the surface but visibly losing faith in its institutions underneath, weighed down by a punishing market, corporate disgrace, the Catholic Church revelations, and mounting anxiety about a war not yet begun.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Bush Declares Iran, Iraq and North Korea an 'Axis of Evil' in State of the Union
    2. Boston Globe Spotlight Team Exposes Decades of Priest Abuse Cover-Up in Boston Archdiocese
    3. Arthur Andersen Convicted of Obstruction of Justice, Ending 89-Year-Old Accounting Giant
    4. WorldCom Files $107 Billion Chapter 11, the Largest Bankruptcy in U.S. History
    5. Bush Signs Sarbanes-Oxley Act, Sweeping Corporate Accountability Reform
    6. Beltway Sniper Terrorizes Washington-Baltimore Region for Three Weeks Before Arrests
    7. Congress Authorizes Use of Force Against Iraq in Bipartisan 77-23 Senate Vote
    8. Republicans Defy Midterm Curse, Reclaim Senate and Expand House Majority
    9. Bush Signs Homeland Security Act Creating Largest Federal Department Since 1947
    10. North Korea Admits Secret Uranium Program, Ending 1994 Agreed Framework

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The United States economy in 2002 was a landscape of grinding, ambiguous recovery layered over spectacular financial disgrace. The NBER-dated recession had officially ended in November 2001, and by the numbers 2002 was an expansion year, yet almost nothing in the daily experience of investors, workers, or CEOs felt like one. Real GDP grew roughly 1.7 to 1.8 percent, well below the postwar trend and squarely in jobless-recovery territory. Growth was front-loaded on a first-quarter inventory rebuild and then decelerated as business capex stayed on strike and the dot-com hangover kept weighing on tech. Consumer spending, buttressed by cheap mortgages and cash-out refinancing, plus red-hot residential construction, did most of the work of keeping the expansion alive. Housing was the singular bright spot and, in retrospect, the seedbed of the bubble: 30-year mortgage rates finished near 6.0 percent, existing home sales set a record above 5.5 million units, and the Case-Shiller 20-city index rose roughly 10 percent for the year. Corporate governance disgrace, not aggregate demand, was the year's dominant business narrative.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth roughly 1.7-1.8 percent. Headline CPI up about 2.4 percent December over December (annual average closer to 1.6 percent); core CPI decelerated to roughly 1.9 percent, the lowest in about four decades. Unemployment rose from 5.7 percent in January to 6.0 percent in December, with nonfarm payrolls declining another roughly 500,000 on net. Federal funds rate held at 1.75 percent for eleven months before a surprise 50 basis point cut to 1.25 percent on November 6. Ten-year Treasury yields fell from about 5.1 percent to 3.8 percent. Personal savings rate hovered in the 3-4 percent range while personal bankruptcy filings set a then-record above 1.5 million. Gross federal debt closed near $6.4 trillion as the fiscal 2002 budget swung to a $158 billion deficit from a $128 billion surplus the year before.

    Major Events

    The year's corporate calendar was dominated by scandal. Kmart filed the largest retail bankruptcy in U.S. history on January 22 and Global Crossing followed on January 28. Arthur Andersen was convicted of obstruction of justice on June 15, effectively killing the 89-year-old firm and ending the Big Five era. Adelphia filed on June 25 amid the Rigas family self-dealing revelations, and on the same day WorldCom disclosed $3.8 billion (ultimately roughly $11 billion) in accounting fraud, filing for Chapter 11 on July 21 with $107 billion in assets, the largest U.S. bankruptcy on record. Tyco's Kozlowski and Swartz were indicted in September and ImClone's Sam Waksal pleaded guilty in October, entangling Martha Stewart. Congress responded with Sarbanes-Oxley, signed July 30. Airlines cracked under 9/11-era demand shock: US Airways filed August 11 and United Airlines on December 9. The Fed pivoted intellectually on November 21 when Governor Ben Bernanke delivered his deflation speech. The Venezuelan general strike beginning December 2 knocked out roughly 2 million barrels a day and pushed oil from around $20 to over $30.

    Why this score

    A technically expanding but subpar, jobless recovery with historically low inflation and easy Fed policy: mediocre by the era's baseline, saved by housing and consumption, offset by corporate scandal and rising unemployment, netting to a neutral 0.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    The equity market suffered its third consecutive down year, the first such streak since 1939-1941, and 2002 was the worst of the three. Full-year total returns were roughly -22.1 percent for the S&P 500, -16.8 percent for the Dow, and -31.5 percent for the Nasdaq Composite. The bear market bottomed on October 9 with the S&P at 776.76, the Nasdaq at 1,114.11, and the Dow at 7,286, declines from the 2000 peaks of roughly 49, 78, and 38 percent respectively. Volatility was elevated, with the VIX repeatedly above 40 and briefly touching the mid-40s in July and October. Ten-year Treasuries returned roughly +15 percent as yields fell from 5.1 to 3.8 percent on a flight to quality and deflation scare, and gold returned about +25 percent to $347, its best year since 1979 and the start of a secular bull that would run into 2011. WTI crude nearly doubled from around $20 to $31-32 on the Venezuelan strike and the Iraq drumbeat, and the CRB commodity index rose about 23 percent. It was overwhelmingly a risk-off tape.

    Character

    Bear

    Major Companies

    The dominant firms of the moment were GE, Microsoft, ExxonMobil, Wal-Mart, Citigroup, and Pfizer. Apple was a small computer company that had just released the flat-panel iMac G4 and its first Windows-compatible iPod; Google was still private; Amazon was recovering from the crash. Corporate news was dominated by scandal and bankruptcy: Kmart (January 22), Global Crossing (January 28), Adelphia (June 25), WorldCom (July 21, at $107 billion the largest U.S. bankruptcy on record), US Airways (August 11), and United Airlines (December 9). Arthur Andersen was convicted on June 15 and effectively ceased to exist. Notable M&A included HP's controversial closing of the Compaq merger in May and Pfizer's July agreement to acquire Pharmacia. IPO issuance was moribund, with fewer than 100 deals for the year, one of the weakest calendars since the mid-1970s; a notable exception was Netflix, which went public in May at $15 per share.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant themes were flight to quality, the deflation trade, and the birth of the new commodity supercycle. Long-duration Treasuries and gold were the winners; junk-bond spreads blew out to roughly 1,000 basis points over Treasuries mid-year before tightening late. Within equities, defensive staples, homebuilders, and gold miners were the notable positives while tech, telecom, and financials led the losses. Housing had begun its ascent as mortgage rates hit multi-decade lows, cash-out refinancing became a major channel of consumer spending, and the Case-Shiller 20-city index posted a double-digit annual gain, though few contemporary commentators labeled it a bubble. The corporate-governance crisis put a permanent premium on audit quality, board independence, and accrual reversal analysis. And the Fed's November pivot toward deflation-fighting rhetoric locked in the low-rate environment that would help inflate the mid-decade credit cycle.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The year's political calendar was densely legislative and heavily foreign-policy weighted. Bush's January 29 State of the Union framed Iraq, Iran, and North Korea as an Axis of Evil. Guantanamo Bay's Camp X-Ray opened on January 11. Sarbanes-Oxley, the most sweeping securities and accounting overhaul since the New Deal, was signed July 30. McCain-Feingold banning soft money was signed March 27. The Iraq AUMF passed on October 16 (77-23 Senate, 296-133 House) with yes votes from Clinton, Kerry, Edwards, Biden, and Daschle. The Homeland Security Act was signed November 25, folding 22 agencies and 180,000 employees into a new cabinet department. The National Security Strategy of September formally enshrined the Bush Doctrine of preemption. The Republican midterm sweep on November 5 gained seats in both chambers, an achievement matched in modern history only by FDR in 1934. Senator Paul Wellstone died in a plane crash on October 25; Trent Lott fell from Senate leadership in December after praising Strom Thurmond's 1948 segregationist candidacy, and was replaced by Bill Frist. The D.C. Beltway sniper and the Bali bombing (October 12, 202 dead including 7 Americans) bookended the fall.

    Domestic Policy

    Domestic policy was shaped by two overriding pressures: the post-9/11 security architecture and a rolling wave of corporate scandals. The signature institutional legacy was the Department of Homeland Security. Bush initially opposed a cabinet department but pivoted in June and then used the bill to force through civil-service and union carve-outs that Democrats objected to, an issue Republicans successfully weaponized against Georgia Senator Max Cleland. No Child Left Behind, signed January 8, launched the era of federally mandated standardized testing and adequate-yearly-progress targets. The corporate governance crisis produced Sarbanes-Oxley by a nearly unanimous vote. Bush signed a $190 billion, ten-year farm bill in May and obtained fast-track Trade Promotion Authority in August. The Help America Vote Act of October funded new voting machines and mandated provisional ballots in the wake of the Florida recount. Civil-liberties tensions were pronounced: NSEERS registration of Muslim-majority-country nationals began in September, the enemy-combatant designations of Yaser Hamdi and Jose Padilla began working through the courts, and Guantanamo drew international condemnation. The Supreme Court delivered Zelman v. Simmons-Harris upholding school vouchers, Atkins v. Virginia barring the execution of the intellectually disabled, and Ring v. Arizona requiring juries to find death-eligibility factors.

    Foreign Policy

    Foreign policy was foundationally reorganized around the Bush Doctrine, articulated at the January SOTU, the June 1 West Point commencement, and the September National Security Strategy. In Afghanistan, Operation Anaconda in the Shah-i-Kot valley in early March was the largest U.S. ground engagement of the war to that point; Hamid Karzai was chosen as transitional head of state in June. The Iraq buildup was the year's dominant thread: Cheney's August 26 VFW speech, Bush's September 12 UN address, the October 16 AUMF, and the unanimous November 8 passage of UN Security Council Resolution 1441 that returned inspectors under Hans Blix. The U.S. withdrew from the 1972 ABM Treaty on June 13, the first exit from a major arms-control agreement in the nuclear era, and Bush and Putin signed the SORT/Moscow Treaty on May 24 capping deployed strategic warheads. NATO invited seven new members at the Prague summit in November, the largest single expansion in alliance history. In October, U.S. envoy James Kelly confronted North Korea over a covert uranium-enrichment program; Pyongyang's acknowledgment collapsed the 1994 Agreed Framework, and by year-end IAEA inspectors had been expelled. Israel launched Operation Defensive Shield after suicide bombings, and Bush's June 24 Rose Garden speech called for a Palestinian state and, implicitly, for Arafat's replacement. The U.S. unsigned the Rome Statute in May; the ICC formally came into force July 1.

    Important Elections

    The 2002 midterm was a striking Republican success. Since the Civil War, only FDR in 1934 had picked up seats in both chambers in his first midterm; Bush's 60-plus percent approval and Karl Rove's aggressive political operation, with the president appearing at 108 events in the final weeks, produced comparable results. Republicans reclaimed the Senate 51-48-1 on pickups by Jim Talent in Missouri, Saxby Chambliss over triple-amputee Vietnam veteran Max Cleland in Georgia, Norm Coleman over Walter Mondale in Minnesota after Wellstone's death, John Sununu in New Hampshire, and Elizabeth Dole for Jesse Helms's open seat in North Carolina, offset partially by Frank Lautenberg holding Robert Torricelli's New Jersey seat after Torricelli withdrew amid ethics problems. The House Republican majority expanded to roughly 229-205, aided by post-2000-census redistricting. Democrats netted governorships even as they lost Congress, picking up Michigan (Granholm), Illinois (Blagojevich), Pennsylvania (Rendell), Kansas (Sebelius), Arizona (Napolitano), and New Mexico (Richardson), while Republicans took Maryland, Georgia (the first Republican governor there since Reconstruction), and Hawaii, and Jeb Bush was re-elected in Florida. Dick Gephardt stepped down as House Democratic leader and was replaced by Nancy Pelosi, the first woman to lead a party in either chamber of Congress.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    The year belonged to blockbusters and their business logic. Spider-Man opened on May 3 with the first $100 million opening weekend in history and finished at $407 million domestic, effectively inaugurating the modern superhero franchise era; Attack of the Clones and The Two Towers, whose Helm's Deep sequence redefined digital spectacle, filled out the summer and winter tentpoles. My Big Fat Greek Wedding became the sleeper of the decade, grossing more than $240 million on a shoestring budget. The critical slate was unusually deep: Chicago (which would win Best Picture at the March 2003 Oscars, the first musical since Oliver!), The Pianist, The Hours, Adaptation, Gangs of New York, Catch Me If You Can, Road to Perdition, Far from Heaven, Punch-Drunk Love, About Schmidt, and Spike Lee's 25th Hour, the year's rawest engagement with post-9/11 New York. Michael Moore's Bowling for Columbine won the documentary Oscar and helped make theatrical documentary commercially viable. At the March 2002 ceremony honoring 2001 films, Halle Berry and Denzel Washington became the first Black winners of both lead acting Oscars in the same year.

    Music

    Eminem's The Eminem Show was the year's best-selling album at roughly 7.6 million copies, and his semi-autobiographical film 8 Mile produced Lose Yourself, which would win the Oscar for Best Original Song, a first for hip-hop. Nelly's Nellyville with Hot in Herre and Dilemma was inescapable, and Avril Lavigne's Let Go turned a 17-year-old Canadian into a global phenomenon and repositioned teen pop toward pop-punk. Norah Jones's Come Away with Me quietly launched a neo-jazz revival that would sweep the 2003 Grammys with eight wins including Album of the Year, while Ashanti rode the Murder Inc. R&B wave. The garage rock revival crested with The White Stripes, The Vines, The Hives, and Interpol's landmark Turn on the Bright Lights, while Wilco's Yankee Hotel Foxtrot, famously rejected by Reprise and released on Nonesuch, became the critical album of the year. Bruce Springsteen reunited the E Street Band for The Rising, the era's defining 9/11 record, and Coldplay's A Rush of Blood to the Head made them global stars. Ringtones became a real revenue line, Kazaa and LimeWire filled the Napster vacuum, and CD sales began their long uninterrupted decline.

    Television

    American Idol debuted on Fox on June 11 and by Kelly Clarkson's coronation on September 4 was drawing more than 22 million viewers, remaking the network landscape overnight. The Bachelor and MTV's The Osbournes (March 5) inaugurated the celebrity-family and dating-competition templates that would define the decade's reality boom. HBO cemented its prestige reputation: The Sopranos was mid-run, Six Feet Under was in its second season, Curb Your Enthusiasm was finding its voice, and most importantly The Wire premiered on June 2. FX's The Shield launched basic-cable prestige drama, and Firefly aired and was canceled. Broadcast anchors Friends, CSI, ER, Everybody Loves Raymond, Law & Order, and Survivor still ruled the ratings, but the erosion was visible. Cable news, remade by 9/11, saw Fox News pass CNN in ratings and never look back; Jon Stewart's Daily Show was becoming a cultural force; TiVo was reshaping how power users watched.

    Technology

    The dot-com hangover was still in the air but consumer technology was quietly powering through. Broadband adoption crossed a key inflection as cable and DSL took a large minority share of home internet from dial-up, and Wi-Fi went from novelty to standard in laptops with Starbucks rolling out T-Mobile hotspots. BlackBerry became ubiquitous on Wall Street and in Washington, and the first widely marketed U.S. camera phone (the Sanyo SCP-5300 for Sprint) launched in November. Apple released the flat-panel iMac G4 in January and its first Windows-compatible iPod later in the year, though iTunes was still Mac-only and the Music Store was a year away. Xbox and Halo were reshaping console gaming against the PS2, and Grand Theft Auto: Vice City (October 27) was the game of the year and a cultural event. DVD overtook VHS in household penetration and rentals; Netflix, still a DVD-by-mail service, went public in May at $15 per share. Friendster launched in the spring and LinkedIn was founded in December, opening the social-networking era, and Google was becoming the default search engine as AdWords matured.

    Social Trends

    The dominant social axis was the security-versus-liberty debate that had defined life since 9/11. The Department of Homeland Security was signed into law in November as the largest federal reorganization since 1947, the Patriot Act's first full year saw growing ACLU pushback, and the NSEERS special-registration program targeting men from majority-Muslim countries began in September. Anti-war organizing exploded in the fall: ANSWER and the newly formed United for Peace and Justice mobilized mass marches, and the October 26 Washington demonstration drew an estimated 100,000, one of the largest since Vietnam. Fear became a consumer product with duct tape, plastic sheeting, and the color-coded threat system introduced in March. The religious right was politically ascendant even as Americans identifying as no religion quietly rose in the surveys, and Hispanic Americans had recently overtaken African Americans as the country's largest minority, per Census 2000 tabulations. Same-sex marriage was not legal anywhere in the country, and the cultural politics around it were still muted.

    Crime

    Beyond the D.C. Beltway sniper attacks of October, which killed ten people and paralyzed schools and gas stations across the Washington-Baltimore corridor for three weeks, 2002 was defined by a wave of high-profile abduction cases: Danielle van Dam in San Diego in the spring, Elizabeth Smart taken from her Salt Lake City bedroom on June 5, and Samantha Runnion abducted and killed in Orange County in July. The pattern drove the launch of the national AMBER Alert system that would be formalized federally the following year. Andrea Yates was convicted in March of drowning her five children, a verdict later overturned. Chandra Levy's remains were found in Rock Creek Park in May, ending Congressman Gary Condit's political career; Winona Ryder was convicted of shoplifting at Saks in November. Overall the U.S. crime rate continued its long decline from its early-1990s peak, but the tabloid crime cycle made the year feel far more menacing than the statistics warranted.

    Sports

    Super Bowl XXXVI on February 3 saw the New England Patriots beat the St. Louis Rams 20-17 on Adam Vinatieri's field goal, announcing Tom Brady and pairing the moment with U2's halftime tribute to 9/11 victims. The Anaheim Angels beat the San Francisco Giants in a seven-game World Series after coming back from a 5-0 deficit in Game 6, with Barry Bonds turning in a supernatural regular season (.370/.582/.799, walked a record 198 times) that stands as the peak of the steroid era. The Lakers won their third straight NBA title over the Nets, and the Detroit Red Wings took the Stanley Cup over Carolina. The Salt Lake City Winter Olympics gave the U.S. its best Winter Games ever to that point, with 34 medals and 10 gold, and produced Apolo Anton Ohno's breakout and Sarah Hughes's upset gold in figure skating. Tiger Woods won the Masters and U.S. Open, Serena Williams completed the Serena Slam at the U.S. Open in September, Lance Armstrong won his fourth Tour de France, and the U.S. men's soccer team reached the quarterfinals of the Korea/Japan World Cup, their best modern finish.

    Public Mood

    The year felt watchful, patriotic, and grim. The first anniversary of 9/11 was the emotional pivot: a nationally televised commemoration at Ground Zero, church bells at 8:46 a.m., the Tribute in Light installed above Manhattan. New York was still visibly rebuilding, anthrax paranoia had faded but not disappeared, and cable news had discovered permanent-crisis programming. Frank Rich, Maureen Dowd, and George Packer tracked a country that was flag-lapel-pin patriotic on the surface but increasingly divided beneath it, on Iraq, on the Catholic Church scandal, on Enron-era distrust of every institution that had commanded reflexive respect a decade earlier. Time named the Whistleblowers (WorldCom's Cynthia Cooper, Enron's Sherron Watkins, and the FBI's Coleen Rowley) Persons of the Year, a choice that captured the atmosphere of institutional failure and reluctant courage. And yet the malls were full, Spider-Man was breaking records, and American Idol was uniting families in front of the television, a country simultaneously anxious and normal.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporaries thought 2002 was principally about the ongoing War on Terror as a continuation of 9/11: the Afghanistan campaign, the hunt for Osama bin Laden (still at large), the Axis of Evil, the color-coded threat system, the anthrax mystery. They thought Enron and WorldCom were the defining business stories, that the Beltway sniper and the Elizabeth Smart abduction were the defining domestic stories, and that Iraq was one of several diplomatic problems the administration was managing. The Republican midterm sweep was read as a mandate for the president's national-security agenda. Every institution felt like it was being tested against a post-9/11 standard, and the year's overwhelming self-understanding was that America was fighting back after the largest attack on its soil in living memory.

    What actually mattered

    In hindsight, two decisions did more to shape the next twenty years than everything else combined: the choice to go to war in Iraq, formalized politically with the October AUMF, and the choice to build a permanent domestic security state through the Homeland Security Act of November. Alongside them, two quieter stories did comparable civilizational work, the Boston Globe's Catholic Church revelations and the quiet birth of consumer social networking (Friendster in the spring, LinkedIn in December, the maturation of Google). Two more, barely on the front page, created the great-power problems that now define American strategy: North Korea's admission of a covert uranium program in October, which reignited the Korean nuclear crisis, and China's first full year in the WTO, whose China shock to U.S. manufacturing would run through the 2016 election.

    Most forgotten story

    The Salt Lake City Olympic figure-skating scandal. For a genuinely astonishing stretch in February 2002 the Sale/Pelletier versus Berezhnaya/Sikharulidze pairs controversy was the most-covered event in America, a French judge, Russian mafia allegations, a duplicated gold medal, and an emergency overhaul of Olympic judging. It felt at the time like a scandal of civilizational importance. Two decades later almost no one under 40 could name a participant. It is the purest example of a story that felt enormous and left almost no cultural residue.

    Most underrated story

    The Boston Globe Spotlight investigation of the Archdiocese of Boston, opening January 6, 2002. It ran on inside pages of most national papers at first and was widely treated as a regional Catholic story. In hindsight it detonated the global clerical-abuse reckoning that consumed the Church for two decades, forced Cardinal Bernard Law's resignation, produced multi-billion-dollar settlement flows, drove numerous dioceses into bankruptcy, rewrote the norms of accountability journalism, and became the template for later reckonings from Penn State to gymnastics to Hollywood in 2017. Its impact on the moral standing of a two-thousand-year-old institution is almost impossible to overstate.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    How thoroughly the Iraq War decision was already made by the autumn of 2002. Contemporaries treated the October congressional authorization and the November UN resolution as steps in a diplomatic process that inspections might still divert. Documents released since (memos to Rumsfeld from the summer, the Downing Street memo describing intelligence being fixed around the policy, and Cheney's August VFW speech preempting the UN track) make clear the decision was effectively locked in months earlier. The public deliberation that filled newspapers for six months was, in retrospect, largely theater. That is the biggest gap between the year as it felt and the year as it actually was.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Salt Lake City Olympic figure-skating judging scandalProduced a duplicate gold medal and rewrote Olympic judging, but is now essentially forgotten outside skating circles; a piece of Olympics folklore rather than a hinge event.
    The D.C. Beltway sniper attacksSaturated cable news for three weeks and produced Chief Charles Moose as a household name, but delivered no lasting policy shift after the proposed federal ballistic-fingerprinting database was killed; remembered mainly as a lurid true-crime episode.
    The resolution of the Chandra Levy caseLevy's remains were found in May and ended Congressman Gary Condit's career, but the story left no political residue and is remembered mainly as a canonical missing-white-woman cable-news episode.
    The Winona Ryder shoplifting trialConsumed tabloid and entertainment coverage in November but vanished from cultural memory within a few years; now cited only as an artifact of early-2000s celebrity-trial saturation.
    The Martha Stewart/ImClone/Sam Waksal insider-trading storyFramed a whole greedy-CEO narrative alongside Enron and WorldCom, but Stewart's brand survived her eventual conviction and the case is now a footnote to the corporate-scandal era, with Sarbanes-Oxley being the actual policy legacy.
    The Elizabeth Smart abductionOne of the most-covered domestic stories of the year and a catalyst for the AMBER Alert brand and the 2003 PROTECT Act, but the case itself has settled into true-crime memory rather than a pivot in national life.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The Boston Globe Spotlight investigation of the Boston ArchdioceseFirst treated as a regional Catholic story, it detonated a global clerical-abuse reckoning that produced billions in settlements, drove multiple dioceses into bankruptcy, and rewrote the norms of institutional accountability journalism through Penn State, gymnastics, and 2017.
    Bush's June 1 West Point commencement speechBarely covered on cable at the time, it laid out what became the Bush Doctrine of preventive war and unilateral action, now treated as the intellectual charter for the Iraq invasion and a decade of Middle East policy.
    U.S. withdrawal from the ABM Treaty, effective June 13Modestly covered at the time, it opened the collapse of the Cold War arms-control architecture and has been cited by Russian officials as a founding grievance in the deteriorating U.S.-Russia relationship.
    North Korea's admission of a covert uranium-enrichment program in OctoberConsumed by the Iraq news cycle, it collapsed the Agreed Framework, led to the December expulsion of IAEA inspectors, and is now understood as the origin point of the modern North Korean nuclear crisis and its 2006 test.
    The launch of Friendster and founding of LinkedInBarely a story at the time, these launches marked the birth window of consumer social networking, the platform category that would reshape media, politics, and daily life within a decade.
    China's first full year in the WTOTrade coverage was thin, but the year opened the China shock to U.S. manufacturing employment later quantified by David Autor and colleagues, with political consequences that ran directly through the 2016 election.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Bush Names Iran, Iraq and North Korea 'Axis of Evil' in State of the Union
    2. Church Kept Abusive Priest at Work: Boston Globe Spotlight Series Opens
    3. WorldCom Files Largest Bankruptcy in U.S. History at $107 Billion
    4. President Signs Sweeping Corporate-Fraud Bill Sarbanes-Oxley
    5. Congress Authorizes Use of Force Against Iraq in Bipartisan Vote
    6. Sniper Suspects Arrested at Maryland Rest Stop After Three-Week Terror
    7. Bush Signs Homeland Security Act, Largest Federal Reorganization in 50 Years
    8. U.N. Security Council Unanimously Backs Tough Iraq Inspection Regime Under Resolution 1441
    9. North Korea Admits Secret Nuclear Program; Agreed Framework in Ruins
    10. Republicans Defy History, Gain Seats in Both Chambers at Midterms

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe year opened at high political and financial velocity. On January 6 the Boston Globe's Spotlight team published its first story alleging systematic clerical-abuse cover-up in the Archdiocese of Boston, opening what would become the year's most consequential institutional reckoning. On January 8 Bush signed No Child Left Behind. On January 11 the first 20 detainees arrived at Camp X-Ray at Guantanamo. Kmart filed the largest retail bankruptcy in U.S. history on January 22 and Global Crossing followed on January 28. Bush closed the month on January 29 with a State of the Union framing Iraq, Iran, and North Korea as an Axis of Evil, the phrase that would define the year's foreign policy.
    FebruaryThe Salt Lake City Winter Olympics opened on February 8 under unprecedented post-9/11 security, with Bush carrying a tattered flag from Ground Zero into the stadium. The Games were dominated by the pairs figure-skating judging scandal that ended in a duplicate gold medal for Sale and Pelletier alongside the Russian pair, by Apolo Anton Ohno's short-track breakout, and by Sarah Hughes's upset gold. On February 3 the New England Patriots beat the St. Louis Rams 20-17 in Super Bowl XXXVI on Adam Vinatieri's field goal, announcing Tom Brady. Wall Street Journal reporter Daniel Pearl was confirmed murdered in Pakistan in mid-February.
    MarchThe largest U.S. ground engagement of the Afghan war to that point, Operation Anaconda in the Shah-i-Kot valley, unfolded in early March and revealed the durability of al-Qaeda and Taliban remnants. Andrea Yates was convicted on March 12 of drowning her five children. FX launched The Shield the same night, opening the era of basic-cable prestige drama. The color-coded Homeland Security threat system arrived. Bush signed McCain-Feingold banning soft money on March 27, and Israel launched Operation Defensive Shield after the Passover suicide bombing in Netanya. Halle Berry and Denzel Washington became the first Black winners of both lead acting Oscars in the same year.
    AprilThe Venezuelan coup attempt against Hugo Chavez on April 11-13 was briefly welcomed by the State Department, embarrassing the administration when Chavez was restored to power within 48 hours. Congressman James Traficant of Ohio was convicted on federal corruption charges. Wilco released Yankee Hotel Foxtrot after being dropped by Reprise, and the album became the critical release of the year. WorldCom quietly began unraveling inside its accounting department as internal auditor Cynthia Cooper started pulling threads that would detonate in June.
    MayChandra Levy's remains were found in Rock Creek Park on May 22, effectively ending Congressman Gary Condit's political career. Bush and Putin signed the SORT/Moscow Treaty on May 24 capping deployed strategic warheads. The U.S. formally unsigned the Rome Statute of the ICC. Netflix went public on May 22 at $15 per share. HP closed its controversial Compaq merger. Spider-Man opened on May 3 with the first $100 million opening weekend in history, inaugurating the modern superhero franchise era. Bush signed the $190 billion Farm Security and Rural Investment Act.
    JuneBush delivered his West Point commencement address on June 1 laying out the doctrine of preventive war that would later be understood as the intellectual charter for the Iraq invasion. American Idol premiered on Fox on June 11. On June 13 the U.S. withdrawal from the 1972 ABM Treaty took effect. Arthur Andersen was convicted of obstruction of justice on June 15, effectively ending the 89-year-old firm and the Big Five era. Sam Waksal of ImClone entangled Martha Stewart. Adelphia filed for bankruptcy and WorldCom disclosed $3.8 billion of accounting fraud on June 25. Elizabeth Smart was abducted from her Salt Lake City bedroom on June 5. The Supreme Court decided Zelman v. Simmons-Harris upholding school vouchers and Atkins v. Virginia barring the execution of the intellectually disabled. The Wire premiered on HBO on June 2.
    JulyThe International Criminal Court came into force on July 1. Nine trapped Pennsylvania miners were pulled out alive from Quecreek after 77 hours underground on July 28, a rare unambiguously uplifting national story. Rep. James Traficant was expelled from the House 420-1 in only the fifth expulsion in House history. WorldCom filed for Chapter 11 on July 21 with $107 billion in assets, the largest U.S. bankruptcy on record. Bush signed Sarbanes-Oxley on July 30, the most sweeping accounting overhaul since the New Deal. Bruce Springsteen released The Rising, the era's defining 9/11 record. Samantha Runnion was abducted and killed in Orange County.
    AugustVice President Cheney's speech to the VFW on August 26 declared Saddam Hussein was reconstituting nuclear weapons, effectively preempting the UN track. US Airways filed Chapter 11 on August 11. Bush obtained Trade Promotion Authority. The garage-rock revival crested and Kelly Clarkson began pulling away from the American Idol field. Colombia's Alvaro Uribe took office with strong U.S. backing under Plan Colombia. Antiwar organizing began accelerating in advance of an autumn expected to be dominated by an Iraq congressional debate.
    SeptemberThe National Security Strategy released in September formally enshrined the Bush Doctrine of preemption. Bush addressed the UN General Assembly on September 12 demanding Iraqi compliance. The first anniversary of 9/11 the day before was marked by a nationally televised commemoration at Ground Zero, church bells at 8:46 a.m., and the installation of the Tribute in Light. Tyco's Dennis Kozlowski and Mark Swartz were indicted. NSEERS special registration of men from mostly Muslim-majority countries began. Serena Williams completed the Serena Slam at the U.S. Open. Robert Torricelli withdrew from his New Jersey Senate race amid ethics problems and was replaced on the ballot by Frank Lautenberg.
    OctoberThe month was extraordinary in every direction. The Beltway sniper began killing on October 2 and paralyzed the Washington-Baltimore corridor until the arrest of John Allen Muhammad and Lee Boyd Malvo at a Maryland rest stop on October 24. The Bali nightclub bombing on October 12 killed 202. Bush signed the Iraq AUMF on October 16 after a 77-23 Senate and 296-133 House passage. In mid-October James Kelly confronted North Korea over its covert uranium-enrichment program; Pyongyang's acknowledgment ended the Agreed Framework. Senator Paul Wellstone died in a plane crash on October 25. The equity bear market bottomed on October 9 with the S&P at 776.76 and the Nasdaq at 1,114.11. Bush signed the Help America Vote Act. Grand Theft Auto: Vice City was released on October 27 and became the game of the year.
    NovemberRepublicans defied a century of midterm history on November 5, gaining seats in both chambers and reclaiming the Senate 51-48-1, a result matched in modern presidential history only by FDR in 1934. On November 6 the Fed surprised markets with a 50 basis-point cut to 1.25 percent. On November 8 the UN Security Council unanimously passed Resolution 1441 giving Iraq a final opportunity to disarm, and Hans Blix's inspectors returned later that month. NATO invited seven new members at the Prague summit. Bush signed the Homeland Security Act on November 25, folding 22 agencies and 180,000 employees into a new cabinet department. On November 21 Ben Bernanke delivered his deflation speech at the National Economists Club. Nancy Pelosi was elected House Democratic Leader, the first woman to lead a party in either chamber.
    DecemberCardinal Bernard Law resigned as Archbishop of Boston on December 13 under the weight of the Spotlight revelations. Trent Lott resigned as Senate Republican Leader on December 20 after praising Strom Thurmond's 1948 segregationist candidacy, and Bill Frist replaced him. United Airlines filed Chapter 11 on December 9. Treasury Secretary Paul O'Neill was pushed out in early December along with NEC director Larry Lindsey, with John Snow nominated to replace O'Neill. The Venezuelan general strike beginning December 2 knocked out roughly 2 million barrels a day of production and pushed oil above $30. North Korea expelled IAEA inspectors and reopened Yongbyon, formally beginning the second nuclear crisis. The Two Towers opened in theaters on December 18.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~1.7-1.8% real GDP growth
    Inflation~2.4% headline CPI Dec/Dec; ~1.6% annual average; core CPI ~1.9% (lowest in roughly 40 years)
    Unemployment5.7% in January, rising to 6.0% in December (cyclical peak neighborhood)
    S&P 500 return-22.1% total return (third consecutive down year)
    Dow return-16.8% total return
    Fed funds rate1.75% most of the year; cut 50 bps to 1.25% on November 6
    Oil priceWTI opened near $20; closed near $31-32 per barrel
    Gold price~$347/oz year-end (up ~25%, best year since 1979)
    Population~288 million (mid-year)
    Median home priceN/A
    National debt~$6.4 trillion gross federal debt at year-end (~57-58% of GDP); FY2002 unified deficit ~$158 billion after a $128 billion surplus in FY2001

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2002 was to live inside two moods at once. On the surface it was a patriotic year, tightly focused, unusually productive in Washington, and preoccupied with a single question: what should the country do with the moral authority and political capital that 9/11 had conferred on it? Twelve months earlier the World Trade Center had fallen; the country had responded with a broadly successful Afghanistan campaign and a stunning display of national solidarity. In 2002 the emotional aftermath had not dissipated. Flags still hung from suburban porches. The Salt Lake City Olympics opened with a tattered flag from Ground Zero. The first anniversary was a nationally televised ceremony with bells at 8:46 a.m. and names read aloud until sundown. The president had a 60-plus percent approval rating, Congress was passing enormous bipartisan bills at a rate not seen in a generation (No Child Left Behind, McCain-Feingold, Sarbanes-Oxley, Help America Vote, the Homeland Security Act, the Iraq authorization), and in November his party did something no first-term presidency had done since 1934, gaining seats in both chambers at the midterm.

    Underneath, the year felt anxious and drained. The economy was in the jobless-recovery doldrums, with unemployment drifting up to 6 percent, the Nasdaq bottoming near a quarter of its 2000 peak, and the stock market grinding through its third consecutive down year. Every month brought a new corporate scandal, a new perp walk, and a new indictment: Enron's aftermath, Arthur Andersen's conviction, WorldCom's $11 billion accounting fraud and record bankruptcy, Adelphia, Tyco, Kozlowski's shower curtain, Martha Stewart. The Catholic Church, revealed by the Boston Globe's Spotlight team to have systematically shielded predator priests, cratered as a source of moral authority. In October a sniper paralyzed the Washington-Baltimore corridor for three weeks, hanging tarps around gas pumps and canceling recess. Elizabeth Smart was taken from her bedroom. Fear itself became a consumer product, sold in duct tape and plastic sheeting.

    And running underneath both moods was Iraq, moving with the inevitability of a river current: the January axis-of-evil speech, the June West Point address, the September UN speech, the October authorization vote, the November resolution, the return of inspectors, the mounting troop deployments. Contemporaries understood it as a diplomatic process that could still be diverted. Historians would understand it as a decision already made. It was possible in 2002 to feel, on any given morning, that America was resolute, competent, and coming back, and equally possible to feel that its institutions were rotting and that a catastrophic mistake was about to be committed in the country's name. Time's Persons of the Year, the three women whistleblowers, captured the paradox exactly: institutional failure and reluctant individual courage, in a country that was flag-lapel-pin patriotic on the surface and had begun to lose faith in almost everything underneath.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2003
    Econ +1Mark +3Poli +1Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2003 was defined by war and, quietly beneath the war, by an inflection. President George W. Bush launched Operation Iraqi Freedom on March 19-20; Baghdad fell within three weeks, Saddam Hussein's statue came down in Firdos Square on April 9, and Bush declared major combat over from the deck of the USS Abraham Lincoln beneath a 'Mission Accomplished' banner on May 1. An insurgency began almost immediately, the stated weapons-of-mass-destruction rationale failed to materialize, and by year's end more Americans had died in the occupation than in the invasion itself. The December 13 capture of Saddam in a spider hole near Tikrit briefly restored optimism, but the character of the war had already changed.

    Beneath the headlines, the country's balance sheet turned. Real GDP grew at a 6.9% annualized pace in the third quarter, the strongest single quarter in nineteen years; unemployment fell from a June peak of 6.3% to 5.7% by December; the Fed cut rates to a 45-year low of 1.00% to insure against deflation; and financial markets staged one of the best years of the decade, with the S&P 500 returning 28.7% and the NASDAQ up 50%. The Bush administration signed two of its signature laws, JGTRRA in May and Medicare Part D in December, at a combined generational fiscal cost that pushed the FY2003 deficit to $377.6 billion.

    Culturally, the country was pulling apart. The February 15 global anti-war protests were the largest coordinated demonstration in history; the Dixie Chicks were burned off country radio for a single anti-Bush remark; the Supreme Court's Lawrence v. Texas ruling in June and the Massachusetts Goodridge ruling in November opened the marriage-equality argument; the California recall installed Arnold Schwarzenegger; MySpace, iTunes, LinkedIn, Skype, and WordPress all launched; SARS, the Northeast blackout, and the Columbia disaster punctuated a year that felt like a permanent orange alert. Time's Person of the Year was 'The American Soldier.'

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Real growth accelerated sharply in the second half, unemployment fell from 6.3% in June to 5.7% by December, and deflation risk faded, though a widening deficit and elevated annual jobless rate temper the score.
    Markets+3A 28.7% S&P total return, a 50% NASDAQ gain, powerful small-cap breadth, tightening credit spreads, and the clean end of a three-year bear market make 2003 one of the strongest single years for U.S. financial assets in the decade.
    Politics+1For 2000s norms, stable unified government produced two major bipartisan laws (Medicare Part D, PEPFAR) and orderly state elections including the extraordinary but constitutional California recall, even as the WMD rationale collapsed and the occupation quagmire planted the seeds of the credibility problems that would dominate the second term.
    Culture+1Genuine creative peaks in hip-hop, prestige television, and independent film, plus foundational tech launches (iTunes, MySpace, LinkedIn, Skype, WordPress, Safari), outweigh a year defined by war anxiety, a sharpening culture-war split, and jobless-recovery unease.
    Overall Mood+0The mood was genuinely divided: markets and creative culture were on the upswing and Saddam's capture ended the year on a high, but the WMD failure, the emerging insurgency, jobless-recovery anxiety, and the sense of living under permanent orange alert kept the country's felt experience closer to neutral than the objective indicators suggested.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. U.S. and Coalition Forces Launch War on Iraq with 'Shock and Awe' Bombing of Baghdad
    2. Space Shuttle Columbia Breaks Up on Reentry Over Texas, All Seven Astronauts Killed
    3. Bush Declares 'Mission Accomplished' Aboard USS Abraham Lincoln
    4. U.S. Forces Capture Saddam Hussein in Spider Hole Near Tikrit
    5. Massive Blackout Knocks Out Power to 50 Million Across Northeast and Midwest
    6. Arnold Schwarzenegger Elected Governor as California Recalls Gray Davis
    7. Supreme Court Strikes Down State Sodomy Laws in Lawrence v. Texas
    8. SARS Outbreak Prompts Global Travel Alerts and Toronto Quarantine
    9. Massachusetts High Court Rules State Must Allow Same-Sex Marriage
    10. Bush Signs Medicare Prescription Drug Benefit into Law

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    2003 was a recovery year whose durability was widely doubted at the time but is legible today as the pivot from the post-dot-com malaise into a five-year expansion. A hesitant, jobless first half gave way to a spectacular Q3 annualized 6.9% GDP print, full-year real growth of roughly 2.8%, and payroll gains that finally returned in the fourth quarter as unemployment fell from a June peak of 6.3% to 5.7% by December. Inflation stayed benign at around 2.3% headline CPI and below 1.5% core, which itself became the story: the Fed cut its target rate to a 45-year low of 1.00% in June explicitly to insure against Japan-style deflation. The Bush administration's second major tax cut (JGTRRA, signed May 28), Iraq war spending, and slower revenues combined to push the FY2003 deficit to $377.6 billion, more than double the prior year, while housing boomed, mortgage debt grew about 14%, and the dollar fell roughly 15%.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth ~2.8% (Q3 annualized 6.9%, the strongest single quarter since 1984); CPI-U ~2.3% annual average, ~1.9% December year-over-year; U-3 unemployment averaged 6.0%, peaked 6.3% in June, ended 5.7%; fed funds cut once (June 25) to 1.00%; 30-year mortgage rate hit a record-low 5.21% in June, full-year average 5.83%; existing single-family median home price ~$170,000 (+7%); housing starts ~1.85 million (highest since the late 1970s); federal deficit $377.6 billion for FY2003; total public debt ~$6.98 trillion.

    Major Events

    The March 20 invasion of Iraq resolved the war overhang that had suppressed sentiment but opened an open-ended fiscal commitment. The Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act, signed May 28, accelerated income-tax cuts, dropped the top rate on qualified dividends and long-term capital gains to 15%, and sent child-tax-credit checks in July at a ten-year cost of roughly $350 billion. The Fed's June 25 cut to 1.00% was explicitly framed as deflation insurance. The August 14-16 Northeast blackout cut power to 55 million people across eight states and Ontario, briefly disrupting refining and manufacturing. Hurricane Isabel hit North Carolina in mid-September at a ~$5.5 billion cost. The Medicare Modernization Act signed December 8 created Part D at a headline $400 billion, later restated at $534 billion. The federal deficit tripled year-over-year, and the DXY fell roughly 15%.

    Why this score

    Real growth accelerated sharply through the second half, unemployment turned decisively lower after mid-year, deflation risk faded, and the recovery moved from jobless to self-sustaining, though a widening deficit and still-elevated annual jobless rate keep the score modestly positive rather than strong.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    One of the strongest years for U.S. financial assets in the entire decade. The S&P 500 bottomed intraday near 789 on March 11-12 on the eve of the invasion, ending the three-year post-dot-com bear market, and rallied roughly 39% from that low to close 1,111.92 for a +28.7% total return (+26.4% price). The Dow gained 25.3% to close 10,453.92, the NASDAQ Composite gained a stunning 50.0% to close 2,003.37, and the Russell 2000 rose about 47% signaling powerful small-cap breadth. Credit spreads tightened all year. Treasury yields whipsawed as the 10-year fell from about 4.0% in January to 3.11% in mid-June before backing up to about 4.25% by year-end, one of the sharpest mid-cycle bond selloffs on record. Gold broke above $400 for the first time since 1996 to close ~$416, WTI ended near $32.50, and the dollar index fell roughly 15%, its worst year since the mid-1980s.

    Character

    Crash-then-recovery

    Major Companies

    Microsoft paid its first-ever dividend in early 2003 and doubled it in the fall, a hinge moment for tech capital return policy driven directly by the JGTRRA dividend tax cut. Bank of America agreed October 27 to acquire FleetBoston Financial for roughly $47 billion, the year's largest U.S. deal; Manulife bought John Hancock for about $11 billion; and Oracle launched what would become a two-year hostile pursuit of PeopleSoft in June. Freddie Mac restated earnings in June by nearly $5 billion, forcing out its CEO, COO, and CFO and drawing a $125 million fine, previewing the GSE governance problems to come. Mirant filed the year's largest U.S. bankruptcy in July at about $19 billion, a post-Enron energy-merchant flameout. HealthSouth CEO Richard Scrushy was charged in November with a $2.7 billion fraud; Boeing CEO Phil Condit resigned December 1 amid the Air Force tanker scandal; and China Life Insurance's November debut was the largest IPO of the year globally.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was reflation: risk assets gapped higher once war uncertainty resolved, small caps and technology led, and the falling dollar plus rising gold and oil marked the beginning of the mid-2000s commodity supercycle. The Fed's June cut to 1.00% and its 'considerable period' language anchored a steep yield curve that fueled cash-out refinancing waves, a housing boom with starts at their highest since the late 1970s, and rapid mortgage-debt growth (~14% year-over-year). Eliot Spitzer's September 3 charges against Canary Capital cracked open the mutual-fund market-timing and late-trading scandal that would ensnare Janus, Bank One, Strong Capital, and Putnam. Major M&A returned after a two-year drought, the IPO window began to thaw, and issuers filed their first full-year Sarbanes-Oxley Section 404 attestations.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The single defining event was the March 19-20 invasion of Iraq, followed by the April 9 fall of Baghdad, Bush's May 1 'Mission Accomplished' speech, the summer emergence of the insurgency (including the August 19 UN headquarters bombing that killed envoy Sergio Vieira de Mello and the October 27 Red Cross bombing), and the December 13 capture of Saddam Hussein near Tikrit. The Plame affair unfolded from Joseph Wilson's July 6 New York Times op-ed through Robert Novak's July 14 column outing Valerie Plame to Patrick Fitzgerald's December appointment as Special Counsel. The Medicare Prescription Drug, Improvement, and Modernization Act was signed December 8 after a three-hour middle-of-the-night House roll call. Landmark Supreme Court rulings included Lawrence v. Texas, Grutter and Gratz v. Bollinger on affirmative action, and McConnell v. FEC upholding McCain-Feingold. The California recall on October 7 removed Governor Gray Davis and installed Arnold Schwarzenegger. The Department of Homeland Security began operations January 24 under Secretary Tom Ridge, absorbing 22 agencies and 180,000 employees.

    Domestic Policy

    Bush governed as a domestic-policy activist even as war dominated headlines. Two signature laws passed: JGTRRA in May accelerated 2001 tax cuts, dropped the top rate on dividends and long-term capital gains to 15%, expanded the child tax credit, and cost roughly $350 billion over ten years; Medicare Part D in December created the largest expansion of Medicare since 1965 at a headline $400 billion (later $534 billion), passed 220-215 in a House vote held open for three hours under Tom DeLay's pressure. The federal deficit ballooned to roughly $377 billion for FY2003. Congress remained under unified Republican control, with Bill Frist as Senate Majority Leader after Trent Lott's Strom Thurmond remarks forced his December 2002 resignation from that post, Dennis Hastert as Speaker, and Nancy Pelosi taking over as House Minority Leader, the first woman to lead a party in either chamber. Lawrence v. Texas was a watershed for gay rights; the Massachusetts Goodridge ruling prompted Bush to signal support for a Federal Marriage Amendment. The Do Not Call Registry launched in the summer and enrolled tens of millions within weeks. The Partial-Birth Abortion Ban Act was signed November 5. A proposed 'PATRIOT Act II' leaked and was quietly shelved after bipartisan pushback.

    Foreign Policy

    Foreign policy in 2003 was Iraq, and Iraq was the Bush Doctrine's first full test. Secretary Powell's February 5 UN Security Council presentation alleging mobile weapons labs and reconstituted nuclear programs, a presentation he would later regret, failed to win a second resolution as France, Germany, and Russia refused to authorize force, splitting NATO and prompting Donald Rumsfeld's 'Old Europe' framing. Post-invasion, Jay Garner's short-lived ORHA gave way to L. Paul Bremer's Coalition Provisional Authority; Bremer's May de-Baathification and disbanding-the-army orders are now widely regarded as strategic errors that fueled the insurgency. In November, Bremer announced a June 2004 sovereignty handover. Afghanistan continued at lower intensity; NATO took command of ISAF in August, its first mission outside Europe. The six-party talks on North Korea began in Beijing in August after Pyongyang's January withdrawal from the NPT. Libya agreed December 19 to abandon its WMD programs. Iran acknowledged undeclared nuclear activities and agreed to suspend enrichment. Bush's July Africa tour launched PEPFAR, the $15 billion HIV/AIDS initiative Congress had authorized in May. The Roadmap for Peace was published in April but made little headway.

    Important Elections

    2003 was an off-year; federal elections were limited to specials.
