The 2004 Democratic presidential field took shape all year, with former Vermont Governor Howard Dean's internet-fueled, anti-war insurgency dominating the second half and widely seen as the frontrunner by December; John Kerry, John Edwards, Joe Lieberman, Bob Graham, Dick Gephardt, Dennis Kucinich, Carol Moseley Braun, Al Sharpton, and Wesley Clark (in September) filled out the field. Bush faced no serious Republican primary challenge. In gubernatorial races, the California recall on October 7 removed Gray Davis and installed Arnold Schwarzenegger with 48.6% against 135 candidates, giving Republicans control of the largest state. In regular November elections, Republican Haley Barbour defeated Democratic incumbent Ronnie Musgrove in Mississippi, Democrat Kathleen Blanco defeated Republican Bobby Jindal in Louisiana to become the state's first female governor, and Republican Ernie Fletcher became Kentucky's first GOP governor in 32 years, foreshadowing the 2004 Southern realignment. Philadelphia Mayor John Street won reelection after the discovery of an FBI listening device in his office.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Peter Jackson's The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King closed the trilogy in December and swept 11 Oscars, tying Ben-Hur and Titanic; Finding Nemo became Pixar's biggest hit yet, and Pirates of the Caribbean resurrected Johnny Depp and Disney's live-action ambitions. The Matrix Reloaded and Revolutions disappointed, while X2 and Bruce Almighty rounded out the tentpoles. Sofia Coppola's Lost in Translation, Clint Eastwood's Mystic River (Sean Penn and Tim Robbins both won Oscars), Kill Bill Vol. 1, Elf, School of Rock, Master and Commander, 21 Grams, Big Fish, and Monster (Best Actress for Charlize Theron) defined the year's prestige and cult film culture. Documentaries Capturing the Friedmans and Errol Morris's The Fog of War were benchmarks. At the March Oscars, held four days after the invasion began, Chicago won Best Picture and Michael Moore delivered his 'shame on you, Mr. Bush' anti-war speech.

    Music

    Hip-hop and R&B owned 2003 as thoroughly as any genre owned any year that decade. 50 Cent's Get Rich or Die Tryin' moved 872,000 copies its first week and made 'In Da Club' the year's inescapable single, while OutKast's Speakerboxxx/The Love Below produced 'Hey Ya!' and would win Album of the Year. Beyonce's solo debut Dangerously in Love (with 'Crazy in Love'), Jay-Z's ostensibly farewell Black Album, The White Stripes' Elephant ('Seven Nation Army'), Evanescence's Fallen, and Justin Timberlake's Justified defined the year's soundtrack. The Dixie Chicks controversy, ignited by Natalie Maines's March London remark that the band was 'ashamed' Bush was from Texas, previewed the red/blue cultural sorting to come. Structurally the industry was in crisis: the RIAA began suing individual file-sharers in September, and album sales continued a multi-year slide even as iTunes offered a path forward.

    Television

    Reality television reached full saturation. American Idol's second season, culminating in Ruben Studdard's narrow May win over Clay Aiken, became the most-watched entertainment show in America; Joe Millionaire's February finale drew roughly 40 million viewers, The Bachelor kept growing, Queer Eye for the Straight Guy became a Bravo phenomenon, The Simple Life launched Paris Hilton and Nicole Richie, and MTV's Newlyweds invented the modern celebrity-couple format. Scripted TV was entering what critics would call the second Golden Age: HBO's imperial run continued with The Sopranos, Six Feet Under, Sex and the City, and Curb Your Enthusiasm; The Wire aired its docks season; FX debuted Nip/Tuck; and Fox premiered Arrested Development in November. CSI, Friends in its penultimate season, ER, Everybody Loves Raymond, Law & Order, and The West Wing anchored broadcast. The Daily Show with Jon Stewart became the outlet where younger viewers processed the war, and Fox News definitively passed CNN in prime time during the invasion.

    Technology

    2003 was the year the modern consumer-internet era took visible shape. Apple's iTunes Music Store launched April 28 at 99 cents a song, sold a million tracks in its first week, and expanded to Windows in October; the third-generation iPod dominated the MP3 market. MySpace, Skype, LinkedIn, WordPress, Second Life, and Safari all launched between January and August, quietly assembling the Web 2.0 stack. Broadband crossed the one-third-of-home-users threshold, Intel's Centrino platform put Wi-Fi in mainstream laptops, camera phones became default, text messaging finally took off across U.S. carriers, and BlackBerry rose in white-collar offices. In gaming, Call of Duty launched in October and Xbox Live expanded. The SoBig and Blaster worms in August caused billions in damages and sparked a national cybersecurity conversation. And the Do Not Call Registry, launched June 27, enrolled tens of millions of numbers within weeks.

    Social Trends

    The anti-war movement was the year's largest social movement by orders of magnitude, powered by the February 15 global demonstrations and the emergence of MoveOn.org as a template for internet-first organizing. Same-sex marriage became a mainstream national argument for the first time: Lawrence v. Texas struck down state sodomy laws in June, the Episcopal Church consecrated openly gay Bishop Gene Robinson in November, and Massachusetts's Goodridge ruling in mid-November set the stage for legal marriages in May 2004. Rick Warren's The Purpose Driven Life was the bestselling nonfiction book of the year through evangelical distribution networks. The Census Bureau announced in June that Hispanics had passed African Americans as the largest U.S. minority. In education, No Child Left Behind entered its first full implementation year, and the Supreme Court's Grutter and Gratz rulings preserved but constrained affirmative action.

    Crime

    Cable news was in a wall-to-wall true-crime cycle. Laci Peterson's Christmas Eve 2002 disappearance and Scott Peterson's April arrest anchored the year; the D.C. sniper trials of John Allen Muhammad (convicted November, sentenced to death) and Lee Boyd Malvo (life) dominated the fall; Kobe Bryant was charged in Colorado in July; Michael Jackson was arrested in November on child molestation charges; the BTK Killer resurfaced with communications after decades of silence; and Elizabeth Smart was recovered in March after nine months in captivity. On January 11, Illinois Governor George Ryan commuted 167 death sentences in one of the most significant single acts in the modern death-penalty debate. HealthSouth CEO Richard Scrushy was charged in November with a $2.7 billion fraud, Martha Stewart was indicted June 4, and Congressman Bill Janklow was convicted of manslaughter in December.

    Sports

    Tampa Bay routed Oakland 48-21 in Super Bowl XXXVII, Jon Gruden coaching against the team that traded him. The San Antonio Spurs took the NBA Finals 4-2 over New Jersey with Tim Duncan as MVP, and Michael Jordan retired for the third and final time; the June draft delivered LeBron James (#1), Carmelo Anthony, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh in arguably the most consequential class in league history. The Florida Marlins beat the Yankees 4-2 in the World Series after the Steve Bartman NLCS foul ball and Aaron Boone's ALCS Game 7 walk-off against Boston. New Jersey won the Stanley Cup, Syracuse and Carmelo Anthony won the men's NCAA title, LSU and USC split the college football championship, Mike Weir became the first Canadian and first left-hander to win the Masters, Lance Armstrong took his fifth straight Tour de France, Serena Williams completed her Serena Slam, and Annika Sorenstam became the first woman since 1945 to play a PGA Tour event.

    Public Mood

    The year felt anxious, angry, and unresolved. The post-9/11 patriotic consensus had frayed into the country's first real culture-war argument since the Clinton impeachment; cable news was omnipresent, the color-coded threat advisory became both a running joke and a real source of dread, and SARS, mad cow disease, and the Blaster worm compounded a sense that low-probability catastrophes were suddenly everywhere. Commentators wrote of a country living in a 'permanent state of orange.' The jobless recovery kept unemployment above 6% for most of the year and the phrase itself entered common use. Iraq's early triumphalism curdled into unease by summer as WMDs failed to materialize and the insurgency began; Saddam's December capture briefly restored optimism but by year's end Frank Rich and Maureen Dowd were already writing about a country pulling apart. Time's Person of the Year was 'The American Soldier.'

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary Americans believed the year's stakes were about winning a war, restoring pre-9/11 security, and whether Howard Dean's internet-fueled insurgent campaign could unseat Bush in 2004. The prestige press treated the Iraq invasion, the Columbia disaster, the SARS outbreak, the capture of Saddam Hussein, and the California recall as the year's civilizational events. Cable news obsessed over Laci Peterson, Kobe Bryant, and Michael Jackson. The Dow's return above 10,000 and the end of the three-year bear market registered as ordinary business news rather than a generational pivot.

    What actually mattered

    The durable pivots were quieter. Lawrence v. Texas and Goodridge rewired American family law and became the doctrinal spine of Obergefell twelve years later. MySpace, iTunes, LinkedIn, WordPress, Skype, and Safari assembled the consumer Web 2.0 stack. The Human Genome Project's completion underwrote two decades of genomics, personalized medicine, and mRNA vaccine platforms. The Bush tax cuts became the single largest structural driver of federal deficits in the following two decades. And Iraq, correctly identified as huge, was misjudged in character: treated as a discrete war rather than the opening act of a regional decomposition that would produce ISIS, the Syrian catastrophe, and a lasting deformation of American institutional trust.

    Most forgotten story

    The 2003 SARS outbreak. Between February and July, SARS-CoV-1 spread to 29 countries, infected roughly 8,100 people, killed 774, cratered Asian air travel, and put Toronto under a WHO travel advisory. It was, at the time, the most serious respiratory-disease scare in a generation, and public-health officials treated it as a fire drill for something worse. Within a decade most Americans had forgotten it entirely, until COVID-19 in 2020 made the 2003 dry run painfully retrospective; scholars now regard SARS as the missed warning that should have prompted the surge capacity, surveillance infrastructure, and mRNA-vaccine investment still absent seventeen years later. A close runner-up is the August 14 Northeast blackout, which left 55 million people in eight U.S. states and Ontario without power, generated a bipartisan reliability agenda that produced the mandatory NERC standards in the 2005 Energy Policy Act, and today is remembered by almost no one under thirty.

    Most underrated story

    Lawrence v. Texas. In one 6-3 decision the Supreme Court struck down every remaining state sodomy law, overruled Bowers v. Hardwick, and recognized a constitutional liberty interest in intimate personal relationships not confined to heterosexual couples. Justice Kennedy's opinion is the doctrinal spine of United States v. Windsor (2013) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015); every subsequent LGBTQ+ rights victory traces to it. Justice Scalia's dissent warned it would compel same-sex marriage, and he was right within five months when Massachusetts's Goodridge ruling arrived. When Justice Thomas's Dobbs concurrence in 2022 named Lawrence as a target for reconsideration, it confirmed retrospectively how load-bearing the 2003 ruling had become. Historians of American law now regard it as the single most consequential civil-rights ruling of the 2000s.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    How completely the WMD failure was absorbed without political accountability. The invasion's public justification rested on Iraqi weapons of mass destruction and alleged al-Qaeda ties; both claims collapsed under evidence through 2003 and 2004, culminating in David Kay's Iraq Survey Group report in October and the Duelfer Report the following year. Yet President Bush was re-elected in 2004, Secretary Powell remained in office through January 2005, and no senior official was disciplined. From 2026 this is the year's most surprising feature: the machinery of accountability, from press to Congress to electorate, largely absorbed a catastrophic intelligence failure as ordinary politics, a turning point now widely cited in the decline of American institutional trust that culminates in the populist politics of the following two decades. A close second is the near-total absence of climate framing in a year when the European heat wave killed an estimated 70,000 people in August, reported as a weather story rather than a warning.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Laci Peterson case and Scott Peterson's April arrestWall-to-wall cable coverage validated the missing-woman true-crime formula that would define the rest of the decade, but the case itself changed no law and shaped no institution beyond television programming.
    The Kobe Bryant sexual assault case (charges filed July 2003)The case collapsed in 2004 and Bryant returned to superstardom; coverage focused on celebrity rather than the substantive issues that later #MeToo conversations would surface, so its cultural half-life at the time was short.
    The Bennifer phenomenon and the release of GigliIt was pure tabloid oxygen. Two decades later it registers as a footnote, briefly revived only by the couple's 2021 reunion.
    The Michael Jackson child-molestation arrest (November 2003)Coverage was framed as an era-defining event; the 2005 acquittal, Jackson's 2009 death, and the later Leaving Neverland revelations mean the 2003 arrest now reads as one chapter in a longer, uglier saga.
    The retirement of the Concorde (October 2003)Networks ran elegiac segments about the end of supersonic passenger travel; in retrospect it was a minor aviation event, and nothing much depended on the fact that it never returned.
    The Elizabeth Smart rescue (March 2003)A genuinely joyful and enormous story at the moment, but its lasting influence was mainly on Amber Alert protocols and cable's true-crime template rather than any broader historical arc.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    MySpace launched (August 2003)Barely covered in mainstream press, it became the first mass-market social network and within three years was the most-visited site in the U.S., laying the template Facebook would inherit.
    The iTunes Music Store opened (April 28, 2003)Covered as a tech-industry story, it broke the album as the unit of music consumption, legitimized paid digital downloads while Napster's ashes were still warm, and set the economic terms of the streaming era.
    Lawrence v. Texas (June 26, 2003)Read as a discrete gay-rights victory at the time; in hindsight it is the doctrinal spine of Windsor and Obergefell, and every subsequent LGBTQ+ rights victory traces to it.
    The Human Genome Project's completion (April 2003)Announced on the 50th anniversary of the double helix and crowded out by Iraq coverage, it underwrote two decades of genomics, personalized medicine, mRNA vaccine platforms, and CRISPR-era biology.
    The Bush tax cuts (JGTRRA, signed May 2003)Covered as ordinary partisan legislation, together with the 2001 cuts they became the single largest structural driver of federal deficits in the following two decades and shaped fiscal politics through 2010, 2012, and 2017.
    North Korea withdrew from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (January 10, 2003)Buried under Iraq coverage, it set the stage for every subsequent DPRK nuclear crisis and the six weapons tests through 2017.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Shock and Awe: U.S. and Coalition Forces Launch War on Iraq
    2. Columbia Lost: Seven Astronauts Killed on Reentry Over Texas
    3. Mission Accomplished: Bush Declares Major Combat Over in Iraq
    4. Saddam Captured in a Spider Hole Near Tikrit
    5. Blackout Cripples Northeast: 50 Million Without Power from Detroit to New York
    6. Schwarzenegger Wins California in Historic Recall
    7. Supreme Court Strikes Down Sodomy Laws in Sweeping Ruling
    8. Massachusetts High Court Clears Way for Gay Marriage
    9. SARS Spreads: WHO Issues Rare Global Travel Advisory as Toronto, Hong Kong Reel
    10. Medicare Overhaul Signed: Prescription Drug Benefit Added

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe Department of Homeland Security began operations on January 24 under Secretary Tom Ridge, absorbing 22 agencies and 180,000 employees in the largest federal reorganization since the 1947 creation of the Defense Department. North Korea formally withdrew from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty on January 10. Illinois Governor George Ryan commuted 167 death sentences on January 11 in one of the most consequential single acts in the modern death-penalty debate. Bush's State of the Union address on January 28 included the 'sixteen words' claim that Iraq had sought uranium in Africa, and Tampa Bay routed Oakland 48-21 in Super Bowl XXXVII on January 26.
    FebruarySpace Shuttle Columbia disintegrated on reentry over Texas on February 1, killing all seven astronauts including Israeli Ilan Ramon and delivering a national trauma that echoed Challenger. Secretary of State Colin Powell delivered his UN Security Council presentation on February 5 alleging mobile Iraqi weapons labs and reconstituted nuclear programs, a case he would later regret. On February 15, coordinated anti-war protests in hundreds of cities produced what would be called the largest anti-war demonstration in history, with several hundred thousand marching in New York and San Francisco. Treasury Secretary John Snow was sworn in. 50 Cent's Get Rich or Die Tryin' debuted at #1 and moved 872,000 copies in its first week.
    MarchOperation Iraqi Freedom began with the shock-and-awe air campaign on March 19-20 and a ground invasion led by U.S. and UK forces. Elizabeth Smart was recovered on March 12 after nine months in captivity. Natalie Maines of the Dixie Chicks told a London audience on March 10 that the band was 'ashamed' Bush was from Texas, triggering country-radio boycotts and CD burnings. The SARS outbreak reached the U.S. in March; the WHO issued its first travel advisory. At the March 23 Oscars, held four days after the invasion began, Chicago won Best Picture and Michael Moore delivered his 'shame on you, Mr. Bush' acceptance speech. The S&P 500 bottomed intraday near 789 on March 11-12, ending the three-year post-dot-com bear market. Consumer confidence sank to about 61 amid war fears.
    AprilBaghdad fell on April 9 and the toppling of Saddam Hussein's statue in Firdos Square became the iconic image of the invasion's initial phase. Scott Peterson was arrested on April 18 in the death of his wife Laci, launching the year's most-covered non-Iraq media story. The Human Genome Project was declared complete on April 14, timed to the 50th anniversary of Watson and Crick's double helix. Apple launched the iTunes Music Store on April 28 at 99 cents a song, selling a million tracks in its first week. Mike Weir won the Masters as the first Canadian and first left-hander to do so. The Iraqi Governing Council was still to be formed as Jay Garner's ORHA took nominal charge.
    MayPresident Bush declared 'major combat operations have ended' from the deck of the USS Abraham Lincoln beneath a 'Mission Accomplished' banner on May 1, an image that would haunt his administration. L. Paul Bremer took over the Coalition Provisional Authority and issued Order 1 (de-Baathification) and Order 2 (disbanding the Iraqi Army), decisions now widely regarded as strategic errors that fueled the insurgency. Congress passed and Bush signed the Jobs and Growth Tax Relief Reconciliation Act on May 28, cutting the top rate on qualified dividends and long-term capital gains to 15%, with Vice President Cheney breaking a 51-50 Senate tie. LinkedIn and WordPress both launched. EPA Administrator Christine Todd Whitman resigned. Congress authorized PEPFAR. Annika Sorenstam played the Colonial, the first woman on the PGA Tour since 1945.
    JuneThe Federal Reserve cut its target rate 25 basis points on June 25 to a 45-year low of 1.00%, explicitly framed as insurance against Japan-style deflation. The Supreme Court decided Lawrence v. Texas on June 26, striking down state sodomy laws in a decision that would become the doctrinal spine of marriage equality, and issued Grutter and Gratz on June 23 preserving affirmative action in higher education. Freddie Mac disclosed a nearly $5 billion earnings restatement that forced out its top three executives. Martha Stewart was indicted on June 4. The San Antonio Spurs took the NBA Finals 4-2 over New Jersey, and the NBA draft delivered LeBron James, Carmelo Anthony, Dwyane Wade, and Chris Bosh in arguably the most consequential class in league history. The Do Not Call Registry opened for enrollment on June 27. Unemployment peaked at 6.3%. The 30-year mortgage rate hit a record-low 5.21%.
    JulyJoseph Wilson published 'What I Didn't Find in Africa' in the New York Times on July 6, and Robert Novak outed Valerie Plame in his July 14 column, opening what would become the Plame affair and the Fitzgerald investigation. Kobe Bryant was charged with sexual assault in Eagle, Colorado. Bush signed the JGTRRA-authorized child-tax-credit checks that went out in July. Bush toured five African countries. Nip/Tuck premiered on FX. Uday and Qusay Hussein were killed by U.S. forces in Mosul on July 22. Mirant filed the year's largest U.S. bankruptcy at about $19 billion. The Iraqi Governing Council was appointed by the CPA.
    AugustThe Northeast blackout on August 14 cut power to roughly 55 million people across eight U.S. states and Ontario in the largest blackout in North American history to that point. The UN headquarters in Baghdad was bombed on August 19, killing envoy Sergio Vieira de Mello and driving the UN largely out of Iraq. NATO took command of the International Security Assistance Force in Kabul, its first mission outside Europe. The six-party talks on North Korea began in Beijing. The SoBig and Blaster worms caused billions in damages. Skype and MySpace both launched. The Ten Commandments monument was removed from the Alabama Judicial Building. The European heat wave killed an estimated 70,000 people, largely reported as a weather story rather than a climate story.
    SeptemberNew York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer announced charges on September 3 against Canary Capital, cracking open the mutual-fund market-timing and late-trading scandal that would ensnare Janus, Bank One, Strong Capital, and Putnam. The RIAA filed its first 261 lawsuits against individual file-sharers on September 8. Hurricane Isabel made landfall in North Carolina on September 18, causing about $5.5 billion in damage. Retired General Wesley Clark entered the Democratic presidential primary. Cancun WTO talks collapsed. Johnny Cash died on September 12.
    OctoberVoters in California recalled Governor Gray Davis on October 7 and elected Arnold Schwarzenegger with 48.6% against 135 candidates. The Steve Bartman NLCS foul ball on October 14 and Aaron Boone's ALCS Game 7 walk-off against Boston on October 16 produced two of the most memorable postseason moments of the decade. Bank of America agreed on October 27 to acquire FleetBoston Financial for roughly $47 billion, the year's largest U.S. deal. The Red Cross headquarters in Baghdad was bombed on October 27. The Concorde made its final flight on October 24. iTunes launched for Windows. The Florida Marlins beat the Yankees 4-2 in the World Series. David Kay's Iraq Survey Group delivered an interim report finding no WMD stockpiles.
    NovemberThe Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court ruled in Goodridge v. Department of Public Health on November 18 that the state constitution required equal marriage rights, setting up the country's first legal same-sex marriages the following May. Bush signed the Partial-Birth Abortion Ban Act on November 5, the first federal restriction on an abortion procedure since Roe. The Episcopal Church consecrated Gene Robinson as its first openly gay bishop. Michael Jackson was arrested on child molestation charges on November 20. Arrested Development premiered on Fox on November 2. HealthSouth CEO Richard Scrushy was charged with a $2.7 billion fraud. Freddie Mac was fined $125 million. D.C. sniper John Allen Muhammad was convicted and sentenced to death; Malvo got life. The Iraqi Governing Council and CPA announced a June 2004 sovereignty handover.
    DecemberU.S. forces of the 4th Infantry Division captured Saddam Hussein in a spider hole near Tikrit on December 13, prompting L. Paul Bremer's 'Ladies and gentlemen, we got him' announcement and a brief boost in war support. Bush signed the Medicare Prescription Drug, Improvement, and Modernization Act on December 8, creating Part D at a headline $400 billion cost, after a House vote held open for three hours by Tom DeLay. Libya announced on December 19 it would abandon its WMD programs. Peter Jackson's The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King opened December 17 en route to 11 Oscars. Boeing CEO Phil Condit resigned December 1 amid the Air Force tanker scandal. Patrick Fitzgerald was appointed Special Counsel in the Plame investigation. Congressman Bill Janklow was convicted of manslaughter. The S&P 500 closed 1,111.92 (+28.7% total return), the NASDAQ 2,003.37 (+50%), and unemployment fell to 5.7%. Time named 'The American Soldier' Person of the Year.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~2.8% full-year real GDP (Q4/Q4 ~4.4%; Q3 annualized 6.9%)
    InflationCPI-U ~2.3% annual average; ~1.9% December year-over-year; core CPI below 1.5%
    Unemployment6.0% annual average; peaked 6.3% in June; ended December at 5.7%
    S&P 500 return+28.7% total return (+26.4% price); closed 1,111.92
    Dow return+25.3% (price); closed 10,453.92
    Fed funds rate1.00% at year-end (cut once, 25 bp on June 25, from 1.25%)
    Oil priceWTI ~$32.50/barrel year-end (up ~4% on the year; briefly above $37 pre-war)
    Gold price~$416/oz year-end (up ~19-20% from ~$342)
    Population~290 million (Hispanics surpassed African Americans as largest minority)
    Median home price~$170,000 median existing single-family (+~7%); ~$195,000 new-home; 30-year mortgage bottomed at 5.21% in June
    National debt~$6.98 trillion total public debt (Dec 31); ~59-60% of GDP; FY2003 deficit $377.6 billion

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2003 was to live inside a televised war that felt, by turns, righteous and shameful; to spend an anxious year watching cable news color-coded threat levels and to laugh, uneasily, at the joke of it; to see the country's cultural coalition of the post-9/11 months fracture along a red/blue seam that would define the next twenty years. The war opened in March with a shock-and-awe air campaign that looked, on the split screen, like a video game, and then modulated into something stranger: a rapid conventional victory declared complete in May beneath a banner reading Mission Accomplished, followed almost immediately by an occupation nobody had a plan for. Weapons of mass destruction were not found, the summer bombings of the UN and Red Cross headquarters in Baghdad announced the insurgency, and by December, when Paul Bremer told the world 'we got him,' the mood had already curdled from triumph into unease.

    Yet 2003 also felt, for many Americans, like a return to normal life. The economy, after two flat years, roared back in the third quarter. The stock market rallied nearly 30%. Home prices climbed, mortgage rates hit a record low, and cash-out refinancings poured money into consumer pockets. Fifty Cent's Get Rich or Die Tryin' and OutKast's Speakerboxxx/The Love Below dominated the summer; The Return of the King and Finding Nemo dominated the theaters; The Sopranos, The Wire, and the second Golden Age of television flowered on HBO and FX; American Idol became the country's shared campfire; and the iPod, iTunes, and the first stirrings of MySpace and LinkedIn hinted at a coming rewiring of daily life. The California recall put a bodybuilder-turned-movie-star in the governor's mansion of the largest state, in a campaign that looked like a joke and prefigured a political era.

    It was, above all, an ambiguous year. The Supreme Court struck down sodomy laws in Lawrence v. Texas and the Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court cleared the way for gay marriage in Goodridge, decisions that in hindsight rewired American family law; the Human Genome Project was declared complete; the SARS outbreak came and went with a warning that would not be heeded until 2020. Americans in 2003 felt they were living through the war, and they were. But they were also, without quite noticing, living through the foundations of the next twenty years being poured all around them.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2004
    Econ +2Mark +2Poli +1Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2004 was the year the post-9/11 decade's two great arguments -- the Iraq War and the identity of George W. Bush's America -- were put to a direct national vote, and the vote changed almost nothing about the trajectory of either. Bush defeated Senator John Kerry 286-251 in the Electoral College, a bitter, high-turnout contest decided by roughly 118,000 votes in Ohio, becoming the first president since his father to win an outright popular-vote majority. The result settled the immediate political question but not the underlying one: an insurgency in Iraq that had metastasized since the previous year's "Mission Accomplished" optimism, a public record already showing that the invasion's core rationale -- Iraqi weapons of mass destruction -- did not exist, and a country whose citizens, per exit polls, described themselves as motivated as much by "moral values" as by war or economy.

    Underneath the political theater, the economy quietly had its best year of the decade. Real GDP grew about 3.8%, nonfarm payrolls finally broke the "jobless recovery" narrative by adding roughly two million jobs, and unemployment fell from 5.7% to 5.4%. The Federal Reserve ended its emergency 1% rate era with five predictable quarter-point hikes, equities delivered a strong across-the-board year led by a record-breaking small-cap rally and Google's culture-defining IPO, and the housing market roared toward its eventual bubble peak. Inflation ticked up on an oil shock that pushed crude to a then-shocking intraday high above $55 a barrel, and the federal deficit hit a record $412.7 billion, a warning sign largely ignored amid the good headline numbers.

    Culturally and institutionally, 2004 was a hinge year disguised as a headline year. Abu Ghraib, the Reagan funeral, the Swift Boat ads, Rathergate, and Janet Jackson's halftime-show malfunction consumed the year's attention and, with the partial exception of Abu Ghraib, faded quickly from historical consequence. Meanwhile, a Harvard sophomore's dorm-room website, an invite-only email product assumed to be an April Fool's joke, a Massachusetts court ruling on marriage, and a little-known Illinois state senator's convention speech went almost unremarked and went on to reorder American life far more durably than anything that led the evening news. It was a year that felt, in the moment, like a referendum on a war and a president; in hindsight, it looks like the year the infrastructure of the next twenty years was quietly assembled underneath the argument.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+2Real GDP grew about 3.8%, the jobless recovery finally broke with roughly two million jobs added, and unemployment fell to 5.4%, though a record $412.7 billion deficit, an emergent housing bubble, and a sharp oil shock kept it short of exceptional.
    Markets+2The S&P 500 returned 10.9%, small-caps surged nearly 18%, volatility hit multi-decade lows, and Google's IPO re-opened tech capital markets, though a nearly flat Dow and rising rates capped the upside.
    Politics+1A bitterly contested but peacefully accepted presidential election with record turnout and a landmark bipartisan intelligence-reform law reflect a functioning constitutional system, but Abu Ghraib, the collapse of the WMD rationale, and deepening partisan polarization drag the score down from a higher mark.
    Culture+1A war-shadowed, culturally polarized year nonetheless produced foundational launches (Facebook, Gmail, Firefox), landmark music and film (Kanye West, Arcade Fire, Sideways, Eternal Sunshine), and a unifying Red Sox championship that broke an 86-year curse.
    Overall Mood+0Objective indicators -- a strong economy, a peacefully resolved election, real creative vitality -- were undercut by the felt experience of a grinding insurgency, Abu Ghraib, and a country that exit polls and pundits alike described as culturally and politically split down the middle.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. George W. Bush defeats John Kerry, wins second term as president
    2. Abu Ghraib prisoner-abuse photographs shock the nation and the world
    3. 9/11 Commission releases its final report on the September 11 attacks
    4. Ronald Reagan dies at 93; nation observes six days of mourning
    5. Massachusetts becomes the first state to legalize same-sex marriage
    6. Boston Red Sox break the 86-year "Curse of the Bambino," win the World Series
    7. Janet Jackson's Super Bowl halftime "wardrobe malfunction" ignites decency furor
    8. Google goes public in a landmark, closely watched IPO
    9. US Marines fight the largest urban battle since Vietnam to retake Fallujah
    10. Indian Ocean tsunami kills over 225,000, dominates year-end news

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The US economy delivered its strongest, most broadly healthy year of the 2001-2007 expansion, with real GDP growth of roughly 3.8% finally translating into robust hiring after three years of a "jobless recovery." The Federal Reserve, under Alan Greenspan (reappointed to an unprecedented fifth term by Bush in May), ended its emergency 1.00% federal funds rate on June 30 and delivered four more identical quarter-point hikes through the year, closing 2004 at 2.25% in a deliberately telegraphed "measured" tightening cycle. Long-term Treasury yields barely moved despite the tightening -- the puzzle Greenspan would later call a "conundrum" -- which kept mortgage rates low and fed a booming housing market. Consumer spending held up, corporate profits recovered strongly, and the manufacturing job losses that had defined the recovery's early years finally began to slow, though wage growth stayed tepid and became a central theme of Kerry's economic critique of outsourcing.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew about 3.8% (Q4/Q4); headline CPI rose 3.3% year-over-year while core CPI ran a cooler 2.2%; unemployment fell from 5.7% in January to 5.4% in December; nonfarm payrolls added roughly 2.0-2.2 million jobs; the federal funds rate rose from 1.00% to 2.25% across five hikes; the FY2004 federal budget deficit hit a then-record $412.7 billion, about 3.5% of GDP; existing-home sales and new-home sales both set records, and median existing-home prices rose about 9% to roughly $195,200.

    Major Events

    The Fed's five-step tightening cycle (June 30-December 14) ended the emergency-rate era; Greenspan's reappointment to a fifth term signaled policy continuity; an extraordinary Atlantic hurricane season (Charley, Frances, Ivan, and Jeanne, all within six weeks) inflicted tens of billions in insured losses and briefly disrupted Gulf energy output; WTI crude spiked from about $32 to an intraday record above $55 in October on Chinese demand, hurricane damage, and Middle East risk before settling near $43; the dollar slid to a record low near $1.35 against the euro; and the Indian Ocean tsunami on December 26 killed more than 225,000 people and prompted a US aid commitment that grew from an initial $35 million to $350 million.

    Why this score

    By the standards of a decade bookended by the dot-com bust and the 2008 financial crisis, 2004 combined strong growth, decisive job creation, and manageable inflation into the healthiest year of the expansion, but a record nominal deficit, a visibly inflating housing bubble, and a sharp oil shock prevented an even higher mark.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    US equities had a solidly above-average year across the board. The S&P 500 returned 10.88% (closing at 1,211.92), the NASDAQ Composite returned 8.59% (closing at 2,175.44), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average lagged with a 3.15% return (closing at 10,783.01). Breadth was a defining feature of the year: small-caps dramatically outperformed large-caps, with the Russell 2000 up roughly 18%, while realized volatility fell to multi-decade lows, with the VIX averaging in the mid-teens and closing near 13.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    Google's August 19 IPO -- priced at $85 via a novel Dutch auction, raising $1.67 billion at a market cap north of $23 billion and finishing the year near $200 -- was the year's defining corporate market event, symbolically re-legitimizing tech IPOs after the dot-com collapse. JPMorgan Chase closed its roughly $58 billion stock acquisition of Bank One on July 1, bringing Jamie Dimon into the firm as president and setting up his path to CEO. Cingular Wireless agreed to buy AT&T Wireless for about $41 billion, creating the largest US mobile carrier; Sanofi's roughly $64 billion acquisition of Aventis reshaped European pharma; and IBM sold its PC business to China's Lenovo for about $1.75 billion in December, a landmark signal of PC manufacturing's shift eastward. AIG and Marsh & McLennan came under Eliot Spitzer's scrutiny over bid-rigging allegations in October, battering AIG's stock and setting up Hank Greenberg's 2005 departure.

    Investment Themes

    Investors rotated into small-caps and cyclicals as the jobless recovery finally broke, while Sarbanes-Oxley Section 404 internal-controls attestation went live for large filers, raising compliance costs and driving a wave of restatements. Spitzer's mutual-fund late-trading and market-timing investigations continued producing settlements throughout the year, and the CBOE's March 2004 launch of VIX futures planted the seed of the modern volatility-trading complex. Record-low realized volatility, a resurgent tech IPO market, and a booming housing sector -- fueled by mortgage rates that stayed low even as the Fed tightened -- were the year's dominant investment narratives.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Bush's re-election over Kerry (November 2) headlined a year that also saw the release of the unanimous 9/11 Commission report (July 22), the Abu Ghraib prisoner-abuse scandal (broke April 28), the June 28 transfer of sovereignty to Iraq's interim government under Ayad Allawi, and two major battles for Fallujah -- the halted April assault following the killing of four Blackwater contractors, and the massive November retaking of the city, the largest US urban battle since Hue in 1968. Ronald Reagan's death on June 5 and six-day state funeral produced a rare moment of bipartisan reflection mid-campaign, while Yasser Arafat's death on November 11 reshaped the Israeli-Palestinian landscape heading into Bush's second term.

    Domestic Policy

    The 108th Congress produced a substantial election-year legislative record: the Working Families Tax Relief Act (October 4) extended middle-class provisions of the 2001-2003 tax cuts, the American Jobs Creation Act (October 22) repealed the WTO-illegal FSC/ETI export subsidy, and the Intelligence Reform and Terrorism Prevention Act (signed December 17) implemented the 9/11 Commission's central recommendation by creating the Director of National Intelligence and the National Counterterrorism Center after a near-collapse over House objections. Bush's push to partially privatize Social Security, a constitutional same-sex-marriage ban (defeated in the Senate 48-50), and comprehensive immigration reform all stalled, and the federal Assault Weapons Ban expired on September 13 without renewal. The Supreme Court's unchanged nine-justice bench issued major national-security rulings on June 28 -- Hamdi v. Rumsfeld and Rasul v. Bush checked executive detention power, while Rumsfeld v. Padilla was dismissed on jurisdictional grounds -- alongside Blakely v. Washington, which upended sentencing-guidelines doctrine. House Majority Leader Tom DeLay was admonished three times by the House Ethics Committee in early October, and the federal deficit reached a then-record $412.7 billion.

    Foreign Policy

    Iraq dominated the year: American troop strength held around 130,000-150,000, US deaths in Iraq rose to roughly 849 from 486 in 2003, and the September 30 Duelfer Report definitively concluded Iraq had possessed no WMD stockpiles at the time of invasion, gutting the war's original rationale. Afghanistan held its first direct presidential election on October 9, won by Hamid Karzai. NATO underwent its largest-ever expansion on March 29, admitting seven new members, while the EU expanded to 25 members on May 1. The March 11 Madrid train bombings killed 191 and triggered the electoral defeat of Spain's pro-US government and its withdrawal from the Iraq coalition -- the first major crack in the alliance. The US continued Six-Party Talks with North Korea, operationalized Libya's WMD dismantlement, and closed the year responding to the December 26 Indian Ocean tsunami with a mission that grew to $350 million in aid plus significant military relief.

    Important Elections

    Bush defeated Kerry 286-251 in the Electoral College (about 50.7% to 48.3% of the popular vote), with Ohio's 20 electoral votes decisive by roughly 118,000 votes and turnout surging to about 122 million, the highest raw total in American history to that point. Kerry secured the Democratic nomination after Howard Dean's Iowa collapse and chose John Edwards as his running mate; Barack Obama's July 27 keynote at the Boston convention made him a national figure ahead of his landslide Illinois Senate win. Congressional Republicans expanded their majorities, with the Senate moving from 51-48-1 to 55-44-1 as John Thune ousted Democratic leader Tom Daschle in South Dakota, and the House Republican majority growing to 232-202-1 aided by a mid-decade Texas redistricting. Eleven states passed constitutional amendments banning same-sex marriage on November 2, and California voters approved Proposition 71's $3 billion stem-cell research bond.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Domestic box office was led by Shrek 2 (~$441M), Spider-Man 2 (~$373M), and Mel Gibson's intensely divisive The Passion of the Christ (~$370M), alongside The Incredibles, Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban, and The Bourne Supremacy. Critically, 2004 was an unusually strong year for adult drama in multiplexes: Alexander Payne's Sideways, Michel Gondry and Charlie Kaufman's Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, Clint Eastwood's Million Dollar Baby (which would sweep the following March's Oscars), and Martin Scorsese's The Aviator all landed with mainstream audiences. Michael Moore's Fahrenheit 9/11 won the Palme d'Or and became the highest-grossing documentary in US history at roughly $119 million, a genuine cultural weapon in the pre-election summer, while The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King swept the February Oscars 11-for-11.

    Music

    Usher's Confessions was the runaway album of the year, selling over 8 million copies and spinning off the year-end #1 single "Yeah!" along with "Burn" and "My Boo"; OutKast's Speakerboxxx/The Love Below won the Grammy for Album of the Year. Kanye West's debut The College Dropout, Green Day's American Idiot, and Arcade Fire's Funeral (championed by a landmark Pitchfork review) reshaped hip-hop, punk, and indie rock respectively, while The Killers' Hot Fuss, Modest Mouse's Good News for People Who Love Bad News, and U2's How to Dismantle an Atomic Bomb rounded out a strong rock year. Crunk and Southern hip-hop (Lil Jon), pop-R&B (Usher, Alicia Keys), and the post-punk revival (Killers, Franz Ferdinand, Interpol) all crested simultaneously, and country remained commercially enormous with Gretchen Wilson's "Redneck Woman."

    Television

    ABC's fall launches of Lost and Desperate Housewives revived a fourth-place network and defined the year's broadcast conversation, while Friends ended its run on May 6 to 52.5 million viewers and Sex and the City closed out in February. HBO's The Sopranos aired a truncated fifth season and Deadwood debuted, sustaining prestige cable's rise, while reality television (The Apprentice, American Idol, The Bachelor) remained dominant and Donald Trump's NBC debut made "You're fired" a national catchphrase. Jon Stewart's Daily Show entered its full political-satire prime, with his October 15 Crossfire appearance becoming a defining media-criticism moment.

    Technology

    The iPod-and-iTunes flywheel defined consumer tech: the iPod Mini launched in early 2004, iTunes crossed 100 million downloads by July and 200 million by December, and the Motorola RAZR debuted in November. Facebook (February 4), Gmail (April 1), Firefox 1.0 (November 9), and World of Warcraft (November 23) all launched within the year, quietly laying the groundwork for the next two decades of digital life even though almost none of them registered as major news stories at the time. The political blogosphere came of age during the campaign, most visibly in September's "Rathergate," when bloggers exposed forged documents underlying a CBS story on Bush's National Guard service, precipitating Dan Rather's eventual departure.

    Social Trends

    Massachusetts's May 17 legalization of same-sex marriage, following the Goodridge ruling, was the year's sharpest civil-rights fault line, prompting eleven state constitutional bans that all passed on November 2. Evangelical Christian political and cultural influence appeared to be at its 2000s peak, visible in The Passion of the Christ's evangelical mobilization and the marriage-amendment turnout strategy, even as the Catholic Church continued absorbing the John Jay Report's devastating accounting of clergy sexual abuse. The housing bubble was in full inflation and homeownership approached an all-time peak near 69%, while Hispanic Americans, having passed African Americans as the largest minority group, continued driving Sun Belt suburban and exurban growth.

    Crime

    The Scott Peterson trial for the murder of his pregnant wife Laci dominated cable news, ending in a November 12 conviction and December 13 death sentence, while the Kobe Bryant sexual assault case was dismissed September 1 after the accuser declined to testify. Martha Stewart was convicted March 5 in the ImClone insider-trading matter and reported to federal prison in October, and the BTK Killer resumed taunting Wichita police in March after 25 years of silence. Abu Ghraib functioned as the year's defining criminal-justice story in a global sense, though only low-ranking soldiers faced prosecution.

    Sports

    The Boston Red Sox's comeback from a 3-0 ALCS deficit against the Yankees -- the only such comeback in major-league history -- followed by a sweep of the Cardinals to break the 86-year "Curse of the Bambino," was the sports story of the decade. Michael Phelps announced himself at the Athens Olympics with six golds and two bronzes as the US topped the medal table, the Detroit Pistons upset the star-laden Lakers in the NBA Finals, and the New England Patriots won a second Super Bowl behind Adam Vinatieri's last-second field goal. The BALCO steroid scandal shadowed track and baseball all year, the NHL's entire 2004-05 season was wiped out by a lockout beginning in September, and the Pistons-Pacers "Malice at the Palace" brawl in November became the most infamous incident in NBA history.

    Public Mood

    2004 felt anxious, polarized, and combative: the Iraq war's shift from 2003's shock-and-awe optimism to grinding insurgency, Abu Ghraib, and a viciously fought campaign (Swift Boat ads against Fahrenheit 9/11, "flip-flopper" against "Mission Accomplished") reinforced a sense of a country split into mutually incomprehensible red and blue nations. Election-night talk of "moral values" voters, however contested by later analysts, crystallized that feeling even as a genuine surge of creative vitality -- The Sopranos, Lost, Kanye West, Arcade Fire, the Red Sox's curse-breaking title -- ran underneath the surface tension, producing a year that felt heavy in the moment but aged into one people remember fondly.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporaneously, Americans believed 2004 was fundamentally about the Iraq War and whether to reelect George W. Bush, with everything else legible through that frame. Abu Ghraib, the two Battles of Fallujah, the collapse of the WMD rationale, the Swift Boat ads, Rathergate, the Reagan funeral, and the hurricanes hitting Florida all dominated coverage volume because they fit that master narrative of a nation deciding whether to ratify or repudiate a war and a presidency.

    What actually mattered

    In retrospect, the year's actual hinge points were mostly quiet ones that occurred below the fold: three product launches -- Facebook, Gmail, and Firefox 1.0 -- that reorganized the information economy; a Massachusetts court ruling that began the fastest civil-rights attitudinal shift in the modern polling era; a convention keynote by an unknown Illinois state senator that produced the next Democratic president; and a fraudulent Ukrainian election that set Vladimir Putin's Russia on a path running through Georgia in 2008, Crimea in 2014, and the full invasion of 2022. The Iraq War and the election mattered, but chiefly as the last narrow, comprehensible national contest before platform-driven politics arrived.

    Most forgotten story

    The collapse of Howard Dean's presidential campaign after his Iowa "scream" is now remembered, if at all, as a punchline, but Dean's actual innovation -- small-dollar internet fundraising, netroots organizing, and a fifty-state strategy he later brought to the DNC chairmanship -- became the template for every insurgent presidential campaign that followed, from Obama in 2008 to Sanders and Trump.

    Most underrated story

    Mark Zuckerberg's launch of "TheFacebook" from a Harvard dorm on February 4 received no coverage outside the Harvard Crimson that year, yet no other artifact of 2004 has done more to reorganize political polarization, local news economics, the attention economy, and election dynamics in the two decades since.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The biggest hindsight surprise is how systematically wrong the era's sober expert consensus was about which 2004 stories would matter most: the professional ranked list of Iraq, the election, Reagan, the Swift Boats, the hurricanes, and the Red Sox inverts almost cleanly against a twenty-year retrospective ranking, in which social platforms, mobile computing, and a Ukrainian color revolution turn out to have been the era's true pivots.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    Janet Jackson's Super Bowl halftime "wardrobe malfunction"It produced a record FCC fine and a national decency panic, but the broadcast-censorship regime it spawned was quickly outflanked by cable and streaming, leaving it a curio of the last mass-broadcast moment.
    "Rathergate" and the forged Killian documentsDan Rather's resignation felt like proof that bloggers had toppled the mainstream press, but network news's deeper collapse came from platform economics rather than any single font-verification scandal.
    The Swift Boat Veterans for Truth ad campaignIt helped sink Kerry's candidacy and coined a verb, but its real legacy -- paid smear campaigns dressed as veterans' testimony -- simply dissolved into the ambient dark money of every subsequent election.
    Ronald Reagan's six-day state funeralIt froze the news cycle as the largest civic ritual since JFK's funeral, but Reagan's ideological project had already been fully absorbed into both parties, so it reshaped nothing going forward.
    Michael Moore's Fahrenheit 9/11It became the highest-grossing documentary in US history and the summer's political event, yet failed at its stated goal when Bush won reelection four months later, leaving it an artifact of the pre-YouTube media moment.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Mark Zuckerberg launches "TheFacebook" from a Harvard dorm room (February 4)Coverage was confined to the Harvard Crimson, yet Facebook/Meta's algorithms would go on to shape elections, mental health, and the information ecosystem for the next two decades.
    Google's invite-only Gmail launches with 1GB of storage (April 1)Widely assumed to be an April Fool's joke, it conditioned users to trust a search company with personal correspondence and helped put Google on the path from search engine to internet operating layer.
    Massachusetts legalizes same-sex marriage under Goodridge v. Department of Public Health (May 17)The eleven state bans that November were read as the movement's ceiling, but Goodridge instead set the direct legal line to nationwide marriage equality in Obergefell v. Hodges eleven years later.
    Barack Obama's Democratic National Convention keynote (July 27)Contemporary observers assumed it might yield a Senate career at best; instead it launched a 43-year-old state senator to the presidency within four years.
    Ukraine's Orange Revolution begins after a fraudulent presidential runoff (late November)US papers covered it as a feel-good post-Soviet democracy story, but Putin read it as a Western-engineered threat and reoriented Russian strategy toward preventing further "color revolutions," a line running to Georgia in 2008, Crimea in 2014, and the 2022 invasion.
    The federal Assault Weapons Ban expires without renewal (September 13)It passed almost unnoticed in a news week dominated by campaign coverage, but its lapse reshaped the US firearms market and the political dynamics of every mass shooting debate that followed.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Photos From Abu Ghraib Show U.S. Soldiers Abusing Iraqi Prisoners
    2. Massachusetts Becomes First State to Legalize Gay Marriage
    3. Reagan, 40th President, Dead at 93; Nation Mourns
    4. 9/11 Commission Faults Both Administrations, Calls Attacks Preventable
    5. Obama Electrifies Convention: 'There Is Not a Liberal America and a Conservative America'
    6. Charley, Frances, Ivan, Jeanne: Four Hurricanes Batter Florida in Six Weeks
    7. Bush Wins Second Term; Ohio Decides It
    8. Marines Retake Fallujah in Bloodiest Urban Battle Since Vietnam
    9. Red Sox End 86-Year Curse, Sweep Cardinals for World Series Title
    10. Tsunami Devastates South Asia; Death Toll Rises Past 100,000

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryJohn Kerry wins the Iowa caucuses (January 19) as Howard Dean's campaign implodes following his exuberant concession-speech "scream," then wins New Hampshire (January 27) to become the clear Democratic frontrunner. JPMorgan Chase announces its roughly $58 billion acquisition of Bank One, NBC's The Apprentice debuts and makes "You're fired" a catchphrase, and Apple's iPod Mini goes on sale. Unemployment stands at 5.7% as the economy continues climbing out of the jobless recovery, and Bush proposes a temporary guest-worker immigration program that will ultimately go nowhere in Congress.
    FebruarySuper Bowl XXXVIII sees the Patriots edge the Panthers on an Adam Vinatieri field goal, but the halftime show's Janet Jackson "wardrobe malfunction" triggers a national decency panic and record FCC fine. Mark Zuckerberg quietly launches "TheFacebook" from a Harvard dorm room (February 4), San Francisco Mayor Gavin Newsom directs the city to issue same-sex marriage licenses (later voided), and Mel Gibson's The Passion of the Christ opens to blockbuster evangelical turnout. The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King sweeps the Oscars 11-for-11, and Jeffrey Skilling is indicted in the Enron case.
    MarchKerry effectively clinches the Democratic nomination on Super Tuesday (March 2). The Madrid train bombings (March 11) kill 191 and lead to the electoral defeat of Spain's pro-US government and its withdrawal from Iraq, the first crack in the coalition. NATO completes its largest-ever expansion, admitting seven new members (March 29), Martha Stewart is convicted of obstruction in the ImClone case (March 5), the CBOE launches VIX futures, and Kanye West releases his debut album The College Dropout.
    AprilCBS's 60 Minutes II and The New Yorker's Seymour Hersh reveal the Abu Ghraib prisoner-abuse photographs (April 28), igniting an international scandal that will dominate the rest of the year. Google's Gmail launches as an invite-only service offering an unprecedented 1GB of storage (April 1). Four Blackwater contractors are killed and mutilated in Fallujah (March 31), triggering the first Battle of Fallujah, while Muqtada al-Sadr's Mahdi Army rises against US forces in Najaf; Casey Sheehan, whose mother Cindy will become a prominent anti-war figure in 2005, is killed in Sadr City on April 4.
    MayMassachusetts becomes the first US state to legalize same-sex marriage (May 17) following the Goodridge ruling, setting up a backlash that will produce eleven state constitutional bans in November. Friends airs its series finale to 52.5 million viewers (May 6), the European Union expands to 25 members (May 1), and the US signs both the US-Australia Free Trade Agreement (May 18) and CAFTA (May 28). Kerry's eventual running-mate selection of John Edwards is still weeks away, but speculation intensifies through the month.
    JuneRonald Reagan dies at 93 (June 5), and the nation observes six days of mourning culminating in a National Cathedral service and burial at his Simi Valley library (June 11). The Federal Reserve ends its emergency 1% rate era with a quarter-point hike to 1.25% (June 30), and the Coalition Provisional Authority transfers sovereignty to Iraq's interim government under Ayad Allawi two days ahead of schedule (June 28). On the same day, the Supreme Court decides Hamdi v. Rumsfeld and Rasul v. Bush, checking executive detention power, dismisses Rumsfeld v. Padilla on jurisdictional grounds, and hands down Blakely v. Washington, upending sentencing-guidelines doctrine.
    JulyKerry selects Senator John Edwards as his running mate (July 6). The 9/11 Commission releases its unanimous final report to bestseller sales (July 22), and the Democratic National Convention in Boston (July 26-29) is capped by a keynote from an little-known Illinois state senate candidate named Barack Obama (July 27) that makes him a national figure overnight. Congress passes the Project BioShield Act (July 21), authorizing $5.6 billion for bioterrorism countermeasures, and iTunes crosses 100 million downloads.
    AugustThe Federal Reserve raises rates again to 1.50% (August 10). Michael Phelps announces himself at the Athens Summer Olympics (August 13-29) with six golds and two bronzes as the US tops the medal table. The Republican National Convention in New York (August 30-September 2) leans heavily on 9/11 imagery amid roughly 500,000 protesters and about 1,800 arrests, while the Swift Boat Veterans for Truth ad campaign against Kerry's war record intensifies, and a second Najaf uprising by Sadr's Mahdi Army is resolved through a truce brokered by Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani.
    SeptemberThe Kobe Bryant sexual-assault case is dismissed (September 1) after the accuser declines to testify. The federal Assault Weapons Ban expires without renewal (September 13), and the NHL lockout begins, eventually wiping out the entire 2004-05 season (September 16). "Rathergate" erupts when CBS airs a National Guard story on Bush built around documents bloggers quickly expose as forged, beginning Dan Rather's downfall. Secretary of State Colin Powell becomes the first sitting US official to call the Darfur conflict a genocide (September 9), the Fed hikes rates again to 1.75% (September 21), and Green Day's American Idiot and Arcade Fire's Funeral are both released.
    OctoberThe presidential and vice-presidential debates (September 30, October 5, October 8, October 13) produce no game-changing moment, though Kerry is judged the first debate's winner. Afghanistan holds its first direct presidential election, won by Hamid Karzai (October 9). Oil prices hit an intraday record above $55 a barrel. The Boston Red Sox complete a historic comeback from a 3-0 ALCS deficit against the Yankees and sweep the Cardinals to win the World Series, ending the 86-year "Curse of the Bambino" (October 27). Ashlee Simpson's lip-sync collapse on Saturday Night Live (October 23) becomes an early viral moment, Osama bin Laden releases a pre-election videotape (October 29), the House Ethics Committee admonishes Majority Leader Tom DeLay three times, and Chief Justice William Rehnquist is diagnosed with thyroid cancer.
    NovemberGeorge W. Bush defeats John Kerry, winning re-election 286-251 in the Electoral College with Ohio the decisive state (November 2); Republicans expand their Senate and House majorities, with John Thune ousting Democratic leader Tom Daschle in South Dakota. US Marines and Iraqi forces launch the massive Second Battle of Fallujah, the largest urban combat operation since Hue in 1968. Yasser Arafat dies (November 11), the Federal Reserve raises rates again to 2.00% (November 10), Firefox 1.0, Halo 2, the Nintendo DS, and World of Warcraft all launch, the Pistons-Pacers "Malice at the Palace" brawl erupts (November 19), and a fraudulent Ukrainian presidential runoff (November 21) ignites the Orange Revolution.
    DecemberBush signs the Intelligence Reform and Terrorism Prevention Act (December 17), creating the Director of National Intelligence and the National Counterterrorism Center after implementing the 9/11 Commission's central recommendation. The Federal Reserve's fifth and final hike of the year brings rates to 2.25% (December 14), Scott Peterson is sentenced to death (December 13), and IBM sells its PC business to China's Lenovo for about $1.75 billion. The Justice Department quietly repudiates the 2002 Bybee "torture memo" late in the month. The year closes with the Indian Ocean tsunami (December 26), which kills more than 225,000 people across South and Southeast Asia and dominates the final week of American news.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~3.8% real GDP growth (Q4/Q4); ~4.2% annual average
    Inflation3.3% CPI (December year-over-year); core CPI 2.2%
    Unemployment5.4% (December), down from 5.7% in January
    S&P 500 return+10.88% total return (closed at 1,211.92)
    Dow return+3.15% (closed at 10,783.01)
    Fed funds rateRaised from 1.00% to 2.25% across five 25bp hikes (June-December)
    Oil priceWTI closed the year near $43/barrel, up from ~$32 at end of 2003, after an intraday record above $55 in October
    Gold price~$438/oz year-end, up about 5% from ~$417
    Population~293 million
    Median home price~$195,200 (NAR median existing-home price, up about 9%)
    National debt~$7.35 trillion gross federal debt at FY2004 close (September); ~$7.6 trillion by calendar year-end

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2004 was to live inside an argument that felt like it would settle everything and, in the end, settled almost nothing. The presidential race between Bush and Kerry absorbed an extraordinary share of the country's emotional energy -- Swift Boat ads and Fahrenheit 9/11, red America and blue America, "flip-flopper" and "Mission Accomplished" -- and the record 122 million who turned out to vote did so believing the outcome would determine whether the Iraq War, and the presidency that launched it, would be ratified or repudiated. Abu Ghraib's photographs, released in the spring, had already curdled whatever moral clarity the invasion once claimed, and the autumn's Duelfer Report formally confirmed that the war's founding premise -- Iraqi weapons of mass destruction -- had never existed. Watching Marines retake Fallujah street by street in November, in the largest urban battle since Vietnam, was to feel the war's costs becoming permanent even as its justification kept receding.

    And yet for most Americans, most of the year did not feel like a war. It felt like a normal, or even good, year to have a job and a mortgage: unemployment fell for the twelfth straight month, the stock market climbed steadily with historically low volatility, mortgage rates stayed remarkably cheap even as the Fed tightened, and home values kept rising in a boom that felt, at the time, like simple broad-based prosperity rather than the credit bubble it would later be understood as. Gas prices climbing toward $2 a gallon and a genuinely competitive Google IPO were closer to most people's daily experience of "the economy" than the federal deficit figures economists fretted over.

    Emotionally, the year ran on two tracks that rarely intersected. One track was heavy: Reagan's funeral, a genuine and bipartisan moment of collective grief, sat awkwardly beside the ugliest presidential campaign in a generation; the Iraq war's mounting toll and Abu Ghraib produced a national mood the culture itself described as exhausted and embattled. The other track was vital and even joyful: the Red Sox's impossible comeback and curse-breaking championship provided the closest thing to unifying civic euphoria the country had that year, prestige television and adult drama in film thrived, and college kids were quietly building things -- a dorm-room website, a beta email service -- that no news organization thought worth covering. Few Americans in December 2004 could have told you that a Harvard sophomore's side project mattered more than the election they had just fought over, or that a fraudulent runoff in Kyiv would matter more than Fallujah. That gap between what felt urgent and what was actually consequential is the defining texture of what it was like to live through 2004: a year of loud, exhausting, genuinely important argument, conducted mostly about the wrong questions, while the future assembled itself in the margins.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2005
    Econ +1Mark +0Poli -1Cult +1Over -1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2005 was the year the promise of George W. Bush's re-election curdled. Bush opened his second term with an inaugural address devoted almost entirely to "ending tyranny in our world" and a domestic agenda built around partially privatizing Social Security, and he spoke of having earned "political capital" he intended to spend. By December the Social Security push was dead, his approval rating had fallen from the low 50s into the high 30s, his House Majority Leader and his Vice President's chief of staff had been indicted, his nominee for the Supreme Court had been withdrawn under fire from his own party's base, and a secret National Security Agency surveillance program had just been exposed on the front page of the New York Times. No single event did this by itself; instead, a cascade of institutional failures and self-inflicted wounds—capped by Hurricane Katrina's devastation of New Orleans and the almost surreally slow, disorganized federal response—converted a president who had just won re-election by three million votes into one visibly losing control of the narrative of his own presidency.

    Beneath the political turbulence, the underlying economy performed solidly by the standards of its era. Real GDP grew roughly 3.5%, unemployment fell to 4.9% by December, its lowest level since before 9/11, and inflation, though pushed higher by a historic run-up in oil and gasoline prices, remained broadly contained in its core measure. This was also the year the decade's defining imbalance—the housing bubble—reached its practical peak, with existing-home sales, new-home sales, and home-equity extraction all setting records even as Alan Greenspan, in his final year as Federal Reserve chairman, raised interest rates at every one of the Fed's eight meetings and conceded only that some local markets showed "froth." Equity markets, by contrast, had their quietest year of the 2003–2007 bull run: the S&P 500 eked out a mid-single-digit total return and the Dow actually finished lower, even as energy stocks, commodities, and a wave of leveraged buyouts and megadeals (Procter & Gamble–Gillette, SBC–AT&T, Bank of America–MBNA) signaled that risk appetite remained very much alive.

    2005 also quietly hosted the two forces that would define the following fifteen years far more than anything on the evening news that year: the birth of the modern internet platform, with YouTube founded in February and uploading its first video in April, Reddit launching in June, and MySpace becoming the country's dominant social network before being bought by News Corp for $580 million; and the first clear glimpse of the surveillance state and the coming financial crisis, both revealed in the same final weeks of the year almost without anyone noticing their scale. Culturally, Americans spent 2005 absorbed by a parade of stories that felt enormous at the time and evaporated almost as fast—the Terri Schiavo right-to-die fight, the Michael Jackson trial, the disappearance of Natalee Holloway, Cindy Sheehan's roadside vigil outside Crawford—while the death of Pope John Paul II, the retirement of Sandra Day O'Connor and death of William Rehnquist reshaping the Supreme Court, and the slow contamination of Iraq's new democracy by sectarian politics did the quieter, more durable work of shaping the next two decades.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Growth held near trend at 3.5%, unemployment fell below 5% for the first time since 2001, and core inflation stayed tame, though the expansion was increasingly propped up by an unsustainable housing bubble and negative household savings.
    Markets+0The S&P 500 managed only a mid-single-digit total return and the Dow finished slightly negative in the quietest, narrowest-breadth year of the 2003-2007 bull market, rescued from a lower score by strong energy, commodity, small-cap, and international performance.
    Politics-1Government kept functioning and completed a Supreme Court transition and several major statutes, but a catastrophic FEMA failure during Katrina, the collapse of Bush's signature domestic initiative, the indictment of the House Majority Leader and the vice president's chief of staff, and a revealed warrantless-surveillance program add up to a year of real institutional dysfunction even by the polarized standards of the 2000s.
    Culture+1Genuine creative highs in hip-hop, prestige cable, and independent film and platform-defining tech launches (YouTube, iPod Video, Xbox 360) outweighed a steady diet of tabloid saturation and a gathering national mood of exhaustion and diminished trust.
    Overall Mood-1Solid macroeconomic data and vibrant consumer culture were increasingly overshadowed by the sense—crystallized by Katrina, the Iraq war's drift, and a run of scandals—that American institutions and leadership were failing to perform, leaving the year's felt experience more sour than its economic indicators alone would suggest.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Hurricane Katrina floods New Orleans and overwhelms the federal emergency response
    2. Bush is sworn in for a second term, pledging to advance freedom and end tyranny abroad
    3. Bush's push to partially privatize Social Security collapses without a floor vote
    4. Sandra Day O'Connor retires, William Rehnquist dies, and John Roberts is confirmed Chief Justice after Harriet Miers's nomination is withdrawn
    5. The Terri Schiavo right-to-die case triggers extraordinary weekend legislation from Congress
    6. Tom DeLay is indicted and the Jack Abramoff lobbying scandal spreads through Washington
    7. Vice-presidential aide Scooter Libby is indicted in the CIA leak/Valerie Plame affair
    8. Gasoline tops $3 a gallon and U.S. combat deaths in Iraq pass 2,000 as the war grinds on
    9. The New York Times reveals a secret NSA program of warrantless surveillance on Americans
    10. The Chicago White Sox sweep the World Series, their first title in 88 years, as Michael Jackson is acquitted and the housing boom hits record highs

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy delivered its fourth straight year of solid post-recession expansion, growing real GDP by roughly 3.5% even as it absorbed a historic hurricane season, a spike in oil prices, and the beginning of the end of the housing boom. The labor market kept healing, with the unemployment rate falling from 5.3% in January to 4.9% in December, the first sub-5% reading since August 2001, while headline inflation ran hot on energy costs but core inflation stayed contained near 2.2%. The defining monetary story of the year was Alan Greenspan's final full year running the Federal Reserve: the FOMC raised the federal funds rate by a quarter point at all eight of its scheduled meetings, taking it from 2.25% to 4.25%, even as long-term Treasury yields barely moved, a divergence Greenspan himself called a "conundrum" and which, in hindsight, was an early warning sign of a late-cycle economy.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth came in near 3.5% for the year (Q4/Q4 about 3.1%); December CPI ran 3.4% year-over-year with core CPI near 2.2%; unemployment fell to 4.9% by December with roughly 2 million nonfarm payroll jobs added; the federal funds rate rose from 2.25% to 4.25% across eight hikes; the FY2005 federal deficit narrowed to $318 billion (about 2.5% of GDP) on strong revenue growth, even as gross federal debt rose to roughly $8.17 trillion by year-end; existing-home sales hit an all-time record of about 7.08 million units and the median existing-home price rose 12-13% to roughly $219,000; and the personal saving rate briefly turned negative for the first time since the Great Depression.

    Major Events

    Hurricane Katrina made landfall on August 29 and became the costliest U.S. natural disaster to that point, causing an estimated $125-150 billion in damage, killing more than 1,800 people, and knocking out roughly a quarter of Gulf oil production; Hurricanes Rita and Wilma followed within two months in the most active Atlantic hurricane season on record, and Congress ultimately appropriated more than $100 billion in relief and reconstruction funding. The housing bubble reached what proved to be its peak, with record home sales, record price appreciation, and an estimated $750 billion in mortgage equity extracted by homeowners even as Greenspan conceded "froth" in some markets. WTI crude spiked to a nominal record above $70 a barrel after Katrina and closed the year near $61, up about 40%, pushing retail gasoline briefly above $3 a gallon. The Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act took effect October 17 and triggered a rush of more than 2 million personal bankruptcy filings beforehand, while CAFTA-DR extended NAFTA-style trade terms to Central America and the Dominican Republic. Bush's push to add personal investment accounts to Social Security, the year's most heavily promoted domestic economic initiative, was effectively dead by summer in the face of unified Democratic opposition and public skepticism.

    Why this score

    A genuinely solid year of trend-level growth, falling unemployment, and manageable core inflation earns a positive score, but the expansion's increasing dependence on an unsustainable housing bubble, record-low household savings, and the shock absorbers of Katrina and $70 oil keep it from scoring higher.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    By the standards of the mid-2000s bull market, 2005 was sedate and range-bound. The S&P 500 closed the year at 1,248.29, up just 3.0% on price (about 4.9% including dividends) from the 2004 close, the weakest showing of the 2003-2007 recovery; the Dow Jones Industrial Average actually finished down 0.6% on price at 10,717.50, dragged lower by General Motors, which lost roughly half its market value amid mounting losses; and the NASDAQ Composite rose a modest 1.4% to 2,205.32. Realized volatility was unusually low all year, with the VIX averaging in the low teens, while small-caps (Russell 2000 +4.6%) and international equities (MSCI EAFE roughly +13% in dollar terms) both meaningfully outperformed the large-cap U.S. benchmarks, an early signal of the mid-decade global commodity supercycle. Sector leadership belonged decisively to energy, up more than 30%, and utilities, while technology and consumer discretionary names lagged.

    Character

    Sideways

    Major Companies

    The year's M&A wave revived the leveraged-buyout era: Procter & Gamble's roughly $57 billion acquisition of Gillette, SBC Communications' purchase of AT&T Corp. (with SBC renaming itself AT&T Inc.), Bank of America's announced $35 billion purchase of MBNA, and the roughly $11 billion take-private of SunGard, the largest LBO since RJR Nabisco. Corporate distress clustered in the second half: Delta and Northwest Airlines both filed for Chapter 11 on the same day in September, GM parts spinoff Delphi filed the largest auto-supplier bankruptcy in U.S. history in October, and futures broker Refco collapsed within weeks of its August IPO after its CEO was arrested for concealing roughly $430 million in bad debts. General Motors and Ford were both downgraded to junk by S&P in May, a landmark "fallen angel" moment for corporate credit. Google, which had gone public in 2004, roughly doubled during the year on the strength of continued growth, and Apple's stock more than doubled on the runaway success of the iPod, making both companies the emblematic growth stories of the year alongside Baidu's tripling on its August NYSE debut.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant themes were energy and commodities (oil, gold, and copper all rallying on tightening global supply and demand), the deepening housing and mortgage-credit boom that record nonprime and private-label mortgage issuance kept inflating even as rates rose only modestly, and a private-equity and LBO resurgence funded by cheap credit. The flattening Treasury yield curve—the Greenspan "conundrum" of steadily rising short rates alongside stubbornly anchored long rates—was the year's most closely watched market signal and, in hindsight, an early warning of the credit cycle turning. International and small-cap equities' outperformance of U.S. large-caps also pointed to a broadening global growth and weak-dollar trade that would run for several more years.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Bush was inaugurated for a second term on January 20 with an address centered on ending tyranny abroad.
