Hurricane Katrina's landfall on August 29 exposed a catastrophic breakdown at FEMA under director Michael Brown, whose slow, uncomprehending response became a defining national embarrassment and drove Bush's approval rating below 40%. The Supreme Court was remade within months: Sandra Day O'Connor announced her retirement in July, Chief Justice William Rehnquist died in September, John Roberts was confirmed as the youngest Chief Justice since John Marshall, and Bush's nomination of White House Counsel Harriet Miers for O'Connor's seat collapsed under conservative criticism before he named Samuel Alito. In March, Congress took the extraordinary step of passing weekend legislation to move the Terri Schiavo right-to-die case to federal court, a move federal courts ultimately declined to act on and that polling showed the public broadly opposed. Bush's push to add personal accounts to Social Security, his stated top domestic priority, died quietly by late spring. In the fall, House Majority Leader Tom DeLay was indicted in Texas and stepped down from leadership, the Jack Abramoff lobbying scandal metastasized across both parties, and Vice President Cheney's chief of staff Scooter Libby was indicted in the Valerie Plame/CIA leak affair. The year closed with the New York Times' December 16 revelation that Bush had secretly authorized warrantless NSA surveillance of international communications involving Americans, opening a multiyear fight over executive power.

    Domestic Policy

    The 109th Congress, under unified Republican control, passed a substantial business- and right-leaning agenda: the Class Action Fairness Act moved most large class actions to federal court, the Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act made consumer bankruptcy sharply harder to obtain, the REAL ID Act set federal standards for driver's licenses and tightened asylum rules over unusual bipartisan objection, and the Energy Policy Act and the earmark-laden SAFETEA-LU highway bill (including the notorious "Bridge to Nowhere") both became law. Senate Republicans, led by Bill Frist, and Democrats struck the "Gang of 14" deal in May to avert the "nuclear option" showdown over judicial filibusters, clearing several circuit-court nominees while preserving the filibuster for "extraordinary circumstances." Cabinet turnover installed Condoleezza Rice at State, Alberto Gonzales at Justice, and Michael Chertoff at Homeland Security. Katrina reopened a national conversation about race and poverty, amplified by Kanye West's on-air declaration that "George Bush doesn't care about black people," while the death of Rosa Parks in October brought her to lie in honor in the Capitol Rotunda. Immigration reheated as an issue with the civilian Minuteman Project patrolling the Arizona border and the House's December passage of the hardline Sensenbrenner bill, which would trigger mass immigrant-rights marches the following spring.

    Foreign Policy

    Iraq dominated foreign policy: roughly 150,000 U.S. troops remained deployed through a year of transitional elections in January, a constitutional referendum in October, and parliamentary elections in December, even as insurgent violence rose sharply, U.S. combat deaths passed 2,000 in October, and Cindy Sheehan's August vigil outside Bush's Crawford ranch helped revive domestic anti-war sentiment that culminated in a large Washington march in September. Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice pursued a "Freedom Agenda" and "transformational diplomacy," backing Lebanon's Cedar Revolution and the Syrian troop withdrawal it produced, Ukraine's and Georgia's post-revolutionary governments, and Israel's unilateral disengagement from Gaza. The Six-Party Talks produced a September joint statement on North Korean denuclearization that quickly frayed over financial sanctions, Iran's nuclear ambitions escalated with Mahmoud Ahmadinejad's election and the resumption of uranium conversion, and Bush and Indian Prime Minister Manmohan Singh announced a landmark civil nuclear cooperation framework in July. Deputy Secretary of State Robert Zoellick's call for China to become a "responsible stakeholder" set the tone of U.S.-China rhetoric for years. Late in the year, the Washington Post's revelation of secret CIA "black site" prisons and John McCain's Detainee Treatment Act banning cruel treatment of detainees, alongside the NSA warrantless-surveillance disclosure, opened a sustained national argument over the legal limits of the war on terror.

    Important Elections

    2005 was an off-year with no federal races, but state contests were widely read as an early referendum on Bush's second term. Democrat Tim Kaine won the Virginia governorship and Jon Corzine won New Jersey's, both notable wins in swing or Bush-won territory; Republican Michael Bloomberg was easily re-elected mayor of New York City. Governor Arnold Schwarzenegger's slate of four ballot initiatives in a November 8 California special election was rejected decisively by voters, pushing him toward the political center ahead of his 2006 re-election. The Kansas Board of Education's vote to allow "intelligent design" critiques of evolution in science standards became a marker of the culture wars. Collectively, the results pointed toward the Democratic wave that would sweep the 2006 midterms.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    2005 paired a transitional box-office year—theatrical attendance fell for a third straight year—with an unusually strong crop of adult drama that would go on to dominate the following spring's awards season. Star Wars: Episode III - Revenge of the Sith closed George Lucas's prequel trilogy as the year's top domestic earner, alongside The Chronicles of Narnia, Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire, War of the Worlds, and King Kong, while Wedding Crashers revived the R-rated studio comedy as the summer's sleeper hit. The year's own films proved culturally weightier than its box office suggested: Ang Lee's Brokeback Mountain, Paul Haggis's Crash, George Clooney's Good Night, and Good Luck and Syriana, Steven Spielberg's Munich, and Bennett Miller's Capote together defined a Bush-era cinema preoccupied with politics, prejudice, and moral compromise. March of the Penguins became a surprise documentary blockbuster and Werner Herzog's Grizzly Man drew wide critical acclaim.

    Music

    The iPod-and-iTunes economy continued reshaping how Americans bought music, reviving the single via 99-cent downloads even as the album still mattered commercially. Mariah Carey's comeback album The Emancipation of Mimi was the year's top seller and "We Belong Together" its dominant single, while Kanye West's Late Registration was the year's critical hip-hop event, released just three days before his unscripted Katrina telethon remark. 50 Cent's The Massacre was a commercial juggernaut, country crossover peaked with Carrie Underwood's American Idol win and the continued dominance of Rascal Flatts, Kenny Chesney, and Toby Keith, and rock and pop touchstones included Green Day's American Idiot, Gwen Stefani's Love. Angel. Music. Baby., Coldplay's X&Y, Kelly Clarkson's Breakaway, Fall Out Boy's From Under the Cork Tree, and Sufjan Stevens's Illinois. Daddy Yankee's "Gasolina" brought reggaeton into the mainstream, and the July 2 Live 8 concerts reprised Live Aid on a global stage.

    Television

    This was mid-boom for television's "second golden age": HBO's Deadwood, Six Feet Under's celebrated final season, Rome, Curb Your Enthusiasm, and Entourage anchored prestige cable while FX ran The Shield and Rescue Me. On broadcast, Grey's Anatomy emerged as a Sunday-night phenomenon, Lost remained the year's buzziest drama, Desperate Housewives the ratings giant, and House hit its stride, while American Idol stayed the country's single most-watched program and Dancing with the Stars premiered to instant success. The Office premiered in March to weak ratings but growing cultural traction, It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia launched on FX, and The Colbert Report spun off from The Daily Show in October, its very first broadcast coining "truthiness," later named Word of the Year. The old broadcast-news era visibly closed: Peter Jennings died in August, Dan Rather stepped down from the CBS anchor chair in the aftermath of the 2004 Bush National Guard memo controversy, and Ted Koppel signed off Nightline in November.

    Technology

    2005 was a genuine platform-shifting year for consumer technology. Apple replaced the iPod Mini with the iPod Nano in September and introduced the iPod Video in October, while the Motorola RAZR became the year's aspirational handset on its way to over 130 million units sold, and Microsoft's Xbox 360 launched in November to open the seventh console generation. On the internet, YouTube was founded in February and uploaded its first video in April, Reddit launched in June, MySpace was the country's dominant social network before being bought by News Corp for $580 million, and "TheFacebook" expanded beyond colleges to high schools while remaining mostly closed to the public. Apple added podcast support to iTunes, broadband finally overtook dial-up in U.S. households, and the term "Web 2.0" crystallized at an October industry conference even as almost no one yet grasped how completely these small, barely noticed launches would remake media within a decade.

    Social Trends

    Immigration built toward a national flashpoint as the civilian Minuteman Project patrolled the Arizona border and the House passed the hardline Sensenbrenner bill in December, setting up the massive immigrant-rights marches of 2006. Evangelical political power reached a high-water mark through the Schiavo case and the Justice Sunday rallies, only to suffer a serious defeat when the Kitzmiller v. Dover ruling in December barred intelligent design from public-school science classes. Gay rights advanced unevenly, with Connecticut becoming the first state to pass civil unions through its legislature rather than the courts even as other states enacted constitutional marriage bans. Anti-war sentiment, dormant for years, revived sharply after Cindy Sheehan's Crawford vigil and a Washington march that drew well over 100,000 people in September, and the Nation of Islam's Millions More Movement marked the tenth anniversary of the Million Man March in October.

    Crime

    BTK killer Dennis Rader was arrested in February and pleaded guilty in June; Michael Jackson was acquitted on all counts in his child-molestation trial in June; Scott Peterson was sentenced to death in March for the murder of his wife Laci; and Robert Blake was acquitted of murder charges the same month. The disappearance of Natalee Holloway in Aruba in May, alongside the earlier "Runaway Bride" episode, became fixtures of what critics that year themselves began calling "missing white woman syndrome" cable coverage. The Supreme Court's Roper v. Simmons decision in March abolished the juvenile death penalty, and Army reservist Lynndie England was convicted in the Abu Ghraib prisoner-abuse fallout in September.

    Sports

    The New England Patriots won their third Super Bowl in four years over the Philadelphia Eagles in February, cementing the Belichick-Brady dynasty, while the San Antonio Spurs beat the Detroit Pistons for the NBA title in June. The entire 2004-05 NHL season was lost to a lockout, the first full cancellation by a major North American pro league, before hockey returned in the fall under a new salary cap. The Chicago White Sox swept the Houston Astros in October to win their first World Series since 1917, and North Carolina beat Illinois for the men's NCAA basketball title. Tiger Woods reclaimed dominance with wins at the Masters and the British Open, Roger Federer won Wimbledon and the U.S. Open, and Lance Armstrong won a seventh consecutive Tour de France and retired as a national hero, a legacy that would later collapse under doping revelations. Congressional steroid hearings in March, featuring Mark McGwire's evasive testimony and Rafael Palmeiro's later-refuted denial before he failed a drug test that August, became the year's defining sports scandal, even as rookie Danica Patrick's strong showing at the Indianapolis 500 made her a genuine crossover star.

    Public Mood

    2005 felt like the year the American self-image cracked. Bush had been re-elected in 2004 amid talk of a durable Republican majority and political capital to spend; by year's end his approval had fallen into the high 30s, Social Security privatization had died, Iraq showed no sign of stabilizing, and Katrina had exposed a government unable to protect its own citizens, a moment amplified by Kanye West's blunt on-air rebuke of the president. Joan Didion's The Year of Magical Thinking became the year's defining literary event, capturing a national mood of ambient grief, while Time magazine's choice of Bono and Bill and Melinda Gates as Persons of the Year read as an implicit rebuke of political leadership. Consumer culture kept humming—the housing boom, the iPod, and a peaking celebrity-tabloid industry epitomized by TMZ's November launch and the Brangelina and Tomkat sagas—even as gas prices above $3 a gallon and The Colbert Report's coinage of "truthiness" captured a country losing confidence that official narratives matched reality.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary Americans believed 2005 was about the war (the Iraqi elections, the insurgency, Cindy Sheehan's vigil), the storm (Katrina and the failed federal response), the culture wars (Schiavo, intelligent design, same-sex marriage battles), and an unusually rich celebrity-crime docket (Michael Jackson, BTK, the Runaway Bride, Natalee Holloway).

    What actually mattered

    The quieter developments with the longest reach were John Roberts's confirmation as Chief Justice, which set up two decades of a conservative Supreme Court; the housing market topping out while regulators and the incoming Fed chairman played down the risk; the birth of the modern internet platform in YouTube, Reddit, and the coining of "Web 2.0"; the first serious U.S.-China strategic-economic collision over the blocked CNOOC-Unocal deal; and the entrenchment of sectarian, Sunni-excluding politics in Iraq's new constitution and elections, which would help produce the 2006-07 civil war and the rise of AQI and eventually ISIS.

    Most forgotten story

    The Kashmir earthquake of October 8, 2005 killed roughly 87,000 people and left over three million homeless in Pakistani-administered Kashmir, one of the deadliest natural disasters of the decade worldwide, yet it registered barely at all in American media, sandwiched between Katrina fatigue, the Harriet Miers withdrawal, and the Plame investigation.

    Most underrated story

    The New York Times' December 16 revelation by James Risen and Eric Lichtblau that the NSA was conducting warrantless surveillance of Americans was arguably the most consequential act of journalism of the decade, establishing years before Snowden that the executive branch had unilaterally reinterpreted FISA to permit mass domestic surveillance, yet it was pushed off the front page within days by holiday news.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    That YouTube—in 2005 three founders, a rented server, and a 19-second video of an elephant—would within a decade surpass any American broadcast network as a media platform, reshape political radicalization, and create "creator" as a career, a trajectory almost no one in the mainstream press saw coming.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Terri Schiavo right-to-die case and Congress's emergency weekend legislationFederal courts ultimately deferred to Florida law, the autopsy confirmed irreversible brain damage, and polling showed the public broadly opposed the congressional intervention, which in hindsight look more like cable-news-driven congressional theater than a lasting turning point.
    The Michael Jackson child-molestation trial and acquittalJackson lived only four more years, the 2019 documentary Leaving Neverland reframed the entire story, and none of the legal or cultural questions raised at trial stayed settled.
    The "Runaway Bride" episode and the disappearance of Natalee Holloway in ArubaBoth became case studies in the newsroom priorities critics were already calling "missing white woman syndrome" in 2005 itself, rather than events with any lasting downstream consequence.
    Cindy Sheehan's Camp Casey vigil outside Bush's Crawford ranchIt generated enormous coverage and was framed as an antiwar inflection point, but produced no policy shift, and the antiwar movement it seemed to catalyze never coalesced into a lasting political force.
    Congressional steroid hearings featuring Mark McGwire and Rafael PalmeiroThe hearings felt like a watershed for American sport, but PED enforcement remained selectively applied afterward and the testimony mostly served as political theater rather than a real turning point.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    YouTube's founding and first video uploadAlmost entirely ignored by the mainstream press, it was bought by Google a year later for $1.65 billion and went on to restructure television, music, politics, children's media, and celebrity itself.
    Reddit's launch as a small Y Combinator projectBarely noticed outside tech blogs, it became the substrate for GameStop-era retail investing, major political subcultures, and a large share of the modern web's discourse.
    The New York Times' NSA warrantless-surveillance revelationPublished just before Christmas and quickly overtaken by holiday news, it is the direct ancestor of the Snowden revelations, FISA reform fights, and the entire modern surveillance debate.
    CNOOC's blocked bid for UnocalCovered as an ordinary business story, it is now seen as the opening skirmish of U.S.-China economic decoupling and the first serious American political recognition of Chinese state capital as a strategic rival.
    Robert Shiller's renewed housing-bubble warnings as the Fed and incoming chairman Ben Bernanke downplayed the riskAlmost no front page treated the housing market's peak as the defining story it now clearly was, even as the imbalances built that year led directly to the 2008 financial crisis.
    The Sunni boycott and sectarian entrenchment in Iraq's constitutional referendum and electionsCoverage focused on the purple-finger democratic milestone, but the exclusion of Sunni politics from the new order set the stage for the 2006-07 civil war and the rise of AQI and, later, ISIS.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Bush Sworn In for Second Term, Vows to End Tyranny in the World
    2. Iraqis Vote in First Free Election in Half a Century Despite Insurgent Threats
    3. Pope John Paul II Dies at 84; Cardinal Ratzinger Elected Benedict XVI
    4. Congress Passes Emergency Weekend Bill in Terri Schiavo Right-to-Die Case
    5. Fed Raises Rates for Eighth Time This Year; Bernanke Tapped to Succeed Greenspan
    6. New Orleans Under Water: Levees Fail, Thousands Stranded at the Superdome
    7. Roberts Confirmed as Chief Justice; Miers Nomination Withdrawn, Alito Named
    8. House Majority Leader Tom DeLay Indicted; Abramoff Lobbying Scandal Widens
    9. Grand Jury Indicts Libby in CIA Leak Case as U.S. Iraq Deaths Pass 2,000
    10. Bush Confirms Secret NSA Program to Monitor Calls Without Warrants

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryGeorge W. Bush was inaugurated for a second term on January 20 with an address centered on ending tyranny abroad, and immediately launched a nationwide campaign for personal Social Security investment accounts. Iraq held its first post-invasion national elections on January 30, producing the iconic image of purple-inked fingers even as insurgent violence continued. Procter & Gamble announced its roughly $57 billion acquisition of Gillette, and Margaret Spellings was sworn in as Education Secretary.
    FebruaryThe Federal Reserve raised the federal funds rate for the first of eight times that year. BTK serial killer Dennis Rader was arrested in Wichita on February 25, closing a case that had haunted the city for decades. New England won Super Bowl XXXIX over Philadelphia, and, largely unnoticed, three former PayPal employees founded a video-sharing startup called YouTube.
    MarchCongress convened an extraordinary Palm Sunday weekend session to pass legislation moving the Terri Schiavo right-to-die case to federal court, a move federal judges ultimately declined to act on; Schiavo died on March 31. The Supreme Court's Roper v. Simmons decision abolished the juvenile death penalty, Scott Peterson was sentenced to death for his wife's murder, Robert Blake was acquitted of murder charges, congressional hearings put baseball's steroid era on public display, and Dan Rather stepped down as CBS anchor.
    AprilPope John Paul II died on April 2, drawing four million pilgrims to Rome for his funeral, and Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger was elected Benedict XVI on April 19. The Bankruptcy Abuse Prevention and Consumer Protection Act was signed into law, triggering a rush of filings ahead of its October effective date, and YouTube's first video, "Me at the zoo," was uploaded on April 23.
    MayThe REAL ID Act became law, General Motors and Ford were downgraded to junk credit ratings, and the Senate's bipartisan "Gang of 14" struck a deal averting a showdown over judicial filibusters. Vanity Fair revealed former FBI official Mark Felt as Deep Throat, closing a 33-year Watergate mystery, and Carrie Underwood won American Idol.
    JuneMichael Jackson was acquitted on all counts in his child-molestation trial. Reddit launched as a small startup, Apple added podcast support to iTunes, Mahmoud Ahmadinejad was elected president of Iran, and the Fed raised rates again on June 30, its sixth hike of the year.
    JulySandra Day O'Connor announced her retirement from the Supreme Court on July 1, and Bush nominated D.C. Circuit judge John Roberts to succeed her on July 19. Coordinated bombings struck London's transit system on July 7, resonating deeply in post-9/11 America. Bush and Indian Prime Minister Manmohan Singh announced a landmark civil nuclear cooperation framework, and Lance Armstrong won his seventh consecutive Tour de France and retired.
    AugustHurricane Katrina made landfall on August 29, flooding New Orleans and killing more than 1,800 people in the costliest U.S. natural disaster to that point; FEMA's slow, disorganized response became a defining political scandal. Chief Justice William Rehnquist's health was failing as Bush installed John Bolton as U.N. Ambassador via recess appointment, signed CAFTA-DR and the Energy Policy Act into law, and Congress passed the earmark-heavy SAFETEA-LU highway bill. Kanye West's Late Registration was released just three days before his live-television rebuke of Bush's Katrina response, and Israel began its unilateral disengagement from Gaza.
    SeptemberChief Justice Rehnquist died on September 3, prompting Bush to withdraw Roberts's associate-justice nomination and renominate him as Chief Justice; the Senate confirmed him 78-22 on September 29. Delta and Northwest Airlines both filed for bankruptcy on September 14, Hurricane Rita struck the Gulf Coast on September 24 as an anti-war march drew well over 100,000 to Washington the same day, and Tom DeLay was indicted on September 28. The Six-Party Talks produced a joint statement on North Korean denuclearization, and Army reservist Lynndie England was convicted in the Abu Ghraib prisoner-abuse case.
    OctoberHarriet Miers's Supreme Court nomination collapsed under conservative criticism and was withdrawn on October 27, and Bush nominated Samuel Alito on October 31. Scooter Libby was indicted in the CIA leak/Plame affair on October 28, the same week U.S. combat deaths in Iraq passed 2,000. Auto-parts giant Delphi filed the largest U.S. auto-supplier bankruptcy in history, futures broker Refco collapsed weeks after its IPO, civil-rights icon Rosa Parks died and lay in honor in the Capitol Rotunda, and a devastating earthquake in Kashmir killed roughly 87,000 people with almost no American media attention.
    NovemberMicrosoft launched the Xbox 360 on November 22, opening the seventh console generation. Democrats Tim Kaine and Jon Corzine won the Virginia and New Jersey governorships, Michael Bloomberg was re-elected mayor of New York City, and Governor Schwarzenegger's slate of four California ballot initiatives was decisively rejected in a November 8 special election. The Washington Post revealed secret CIA "black site" prisons, and Rep. John Murtha's call for a phased Iraq withdrawal marked a hinge point in elite opinion on the war.
    DecemberIraq held full parliamentary elections on December 15, and the House passed the hardline Sensenbrenner immigration bill on December 16, setting up 2006's mass immigrant-rights marches. That same week, the New York Times revealed that Bush had secretly authorized warrantless NSA surveillance of Americans' international communications. The Kitzmiller v. Dover ruling barred intelligent design from public-school science classes on December 20, John McCain's Detainee Treatment Act was signed December 30, and the Fed's eighth and final rate hike of the year on December 13 took the federal funds rate to 4.25% as Alan Greenspan's tenure neared its end.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~3.5% real GDP growth for the year (Q4/Q4 ~3.1%)
    InflationCPI-U (Dec. YoY) 3.4%; core CPI (ex food & energy) ~2.2%
    Unemployment4.9% in December 2005, down from 5.4% in December 2004
    S&P 500 return+3.0% price / ~+4.9% total return (close 1,248.29)
    Dow return-0.6% price / ~+1.7% total return (close 10,717.50)
    Fed funds rate2.25% (January) to 4.25% (December), eight 25bp hikes
    Oil priceWTI ~$61/bbl year-end (up ~40%; intraday peak above $70 post-Katrina)
    Gold price~$513/oz year-end, a 24-year high
    Population~296.5-297 million
    Median home price~$219,000 median existing-home price (NAR), up ~12-13% YoY
    National debt~$7.93 trillion at FY2005 close; ~$8.17 trillion at calendar year-end

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2005 was to feel a widening gap between the economy in the data and the country in the mirror. The numbers said things were fine: unemployment under 5% for the first time in four years, home values still climbing, gas prices painful but affordable, a fourth consecutive year of solid growth. Yet the year's dominant memory is of a government that seemed to be coming apart at the seams in slow motion on live television. Katrina was the hinge. For four days, Americans watched a major U.S. city flood, watched their fellow citizens - overwhelmingly poor and Black - stranded on rooftops and packed into a fetid Superdome, and watched federal, state, and local officials point fingers at one another while people died waiting for water and buses that did not come. It did not matter that the storm itself was a freak of nature; what stuck was the sense that the world's most powerful government could not perform the most basic function of protecting its own citizens.

    That feeling bled into everything else. Bush had opened the year still riding the momentum of a decisive re-election, promising to spend his "political capital" on Social Security reform and a freedom agenda abroad. By December, the Social Security plan was dead, the Iraqi elections that were supposed to vindicate the war coexisted with a rising insurgency and 2,000 dead American soldiers, his handpicked Supreme Court nominee had been humiliated into withdrawal by his own party's base, his House Majority Leader and his vice president's top aide were both under criminal indictment, and a secret surveillance program had just been exposed in the country's paper of record. An ordinary American reading the newspaper in December could reasonably wonder whether the government functioned at all, even though, underneath, the machinery of state - courts, elections, statutes - had in fact kept grinding forward without any true constitutional rupture.

    Off the front page, life had a different texture entirely: an iPod in every pocket, a new video site called YouTube nobody had heard of yet, a housing market that made everyone feel richer on paper, a World Series drought ending in Chicago, prestige cable television at a genuine creative peak, and an endless carousel of tabloid obsessions - Michael Jackson, Natalee Holloway, the runaway bride - that filled hours of cable news with sound and fury signifying very little. Joan Didion's meditation on grief and the country's embrace of Bono and the Gateses as its moral exemplars both captured something true: a nation more comfortable mourning and admiring from a distance than trusting its own leaders. The lasting sensation of 2005, looking back, is of an America that still had money in its pocket and a phone in its hand but had quietly stopped believing that the people running the country knew what they were doing - a mood the year itself named, fittingly, "truthiness."

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2006
    Econ +1Mark +2Poli -1Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    In 2006 the United States economy delivered what would later be recognized as the final untroubled year of the 2000s expansion. Growth decelerated from the torrid pace of 2004-2005 to a still-respectable 2.8 percent, unemployment fell to a five-year low of 4.4 percent, corporate profits set records, and the stock market posted its best year since 2003, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average closing above its January 2000 peak for the first time. Ben Bernanke replaced Alan Greenspan at the Federal Reserve in February and steered the funds rate to a cycle peak of 5.25 percent by June, then held steady as inflation cooled from a midyear spike. Beneath this placid surface, however, the decade-long housing boom quietly inflected: home prices, sales, and construction all rolled over between spring and midyear, and by December the first subprime mortgage lenders had begun to fail, tremors that few contemporaries connected to anything systemic.

    Politically, 2006 was the year the Bush presidency's second term visibly broke. Iraq descended into open sectarian civil war after the February bombing of the Golden Mosque in Samarra, American casualties mounted, and public patience collapsed; North Korea detonated its first nuclear device in October; and a cascade of scandals, including Jack Abramoff's lobbying corruption, Mark Foley's page messages, and the Dubai Ports World furor, compounded a sense of Republican misgovernance following Hurricane Katrina. On November 7, voters delivered the most decisive midterm rebuke since 1994, handing Democrats control of both the House and Senate and positioning Nancy Pelosi to become the first female Speaker of the House. Rumsfeld resigned the next day, and the bipartisan Iraq Study Group's December report calling the war "grave and deteriorating" set the stage for a fundamental strategic reassessment.

    Culturally, 2006 felt like a weary, cynical year presided over by an unpopular president and an unresolved war, yet it was quietly one of the most consequential years in the emergence of the platform economy. Twitter launched in March, Facebook opened beyond college campuses in September, Amazon's cloud-computing services quietly went live with almost no press notice, and Google's October purchase of YouTube for $1.65 billion signaled the arrival of user-generated video as a mass medium, developments Time magazine gestured toward by naming "You" its Person of the Year. Read from decades later, 2006 is less the year of the Democratic wave and Iraq's collapse, real as those were, and more the hinge year in which the financial crisis and the social-media age were both quietly seeded while the nation's attention was fixed elsewhere.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Growth remained solid and unemployment hit a cycle low even as record corporate profits masked a housing-market rollover and a negative national savings rate that presaged the crisis to come.
    Markets+2Every major U.S. equity index posted double-digit gains and the Dow set fresh all-time highs amid record M&A activity and low volatility, though NASDAQ's lag and the market's untroubled digestion of Amaranth's collapse hinted at underlying complacency.
    Politics-1A deteriorating war, a wave of corruption and sex scandals, and a stinging midterm rebuke of the governing party marked deep dysfunction, though the constitutional system, from the courts checking executive power to a clean transfer of congressional control, continued to function.
    Culture+1A politically exhausted, war-weary public mood was offset by an unusually fertile year for digital innovation (YouTube, Facebook, Twitter, the Wii) and strong prestige film and television.
    Overall Mood+0Prosperity and technological excitement coexisted with war fatigue, scandal exhaustion, and a housing market quietly turning down, leaving Americans with a genuinely mixed sense of where the country stood.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Democrats swept to control of both the House and Senate in the November midterms, the party's biggest congressional gain since 1994.
    2. Donald Rumsfeld resigned as Secretary of Defense the day after the election, replaced by Robert Gates.
    3. Iraq slid into open sectarian civil war after the bombing of the Golden Mosque in Samarra in February.
    4. North Korea conducted its first nuclear weapons test in October.
    5. Saddam Hussein was executed by hanging on December 30 after conviction by an Iraqi tribunal.
    6. Ben Bernanke succeeded Alan Greenspan as Federal Reserve Chairman in February, inheriting an economy at a cyclical peak.
    7. The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at a record high in October, its first new peak since January 2000.
    8. The decade-long U.S. housing boom peaked and began to cool, with home prices, sales, and construction all turning down.
    9. Google acquired the video-sharing site YouTube for $1.65 billion, cementing the rise of user-generated online video.
    10. Massive nationwide immigration marches, including a 'Day Without Immigrants' boycott on May 1, accompanied the collapse of comprehensive immigration reform in Congress.

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The economy delivered late-cycle prosperity that masked an inflection point. Growth ran hot in the first quarter before decelerating sharply as residential investment turned negative, while a tightening labor market pushed unemployment to its lowest level in five years and corporate profits reached record highs. Inflation spiked at midyear on record oil prices before cooling as energy costs retreated, giving the Federal Reserve room to pause its two-year tightening campaign after a final hike in June. The housing market, the central actor of the year, rolled over decisively: sales, starts, and eventually national home prices all turned down after a decade of uninterrupted appreciation, and the first cracks in subprime lending appeared by year's end even as most economists treated them as contained.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth for the year came in at roughly 2.8 percent, with an extraordinary 5.4 percent annualized first quarter giving way to near-stagnant middle quarters as housing subtracted from growth. Unemployment fell from 4.7 percent in January to 4.4 percent in December as nonfarm payrolls added about 2.1 million jobs. Headline CPI inflation peaked near 4.3 percent year-over-year in June before ending the year at 2.5 percent, while core CPI actually rose to 2.6 percent, the highest since 2001. The Federal Reserve raised the funds rate to 5.25 percent by June 29 and held there through year-end. Existing home sales fell about 8 percent and new home sales about 18 percent, while the median existing-home price of roughly $221,900 was essentially flat for the first time in a decade. The federal budget deficit narrowed to $248 billion, or 1.8 percent of GDP, even as the personal saving rate turned negative for the first full year since the Great Depression.

    Major Events

    Ben Bernanke was sworn in as Federal Reserve Chairman on February 1, succeeding Alan Greenspan after eighteen and a half years, and continued the tightening cycle to its 5.25 percent peak before pausing. Ford Motor Company posted a $12.7 billion annual loss, the largest in its history, and mortgaged nearly all its assets in November to finance Alan Mulally's turnaround, while General Motors lost roughly $2 billion; ExxonMobil, by contrast, booked $39.5 billion in profit, the largest ever reported by a U.S. corporation. A record global M&A wave worth roughly $3.9 trillion included AT&T's $67 billion purchase of BellSouth, Bank of America's acquisition of MBNA, and the buildup to Blackstone's record $39 billion buyout of Equity Office Properties. Subprime lenders Ownit Mortgage Solutions and Mortgage Lenders Network collapsed in December as early defaults surged on 2006-vintage loans, foreshadowing the following year's crisis. Jeffrey Skilling and Kenneth Lay were convicted in the Enron fraud case in May; Lay died before sentencing in July.

    Why this score

    A cyclical-low unemployment rate, record corporate profits, and a contained year-end inflation reading justify a modestly positive score, but the decisive rollover of the housing market, the first subprime lender failures, and a personal saving rate that turned negative for the first time since the 1930s prevent a stronger rating.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities enjoyed their best year since 2003. The Dow Jones Industrial Average returned about 19.0 percent including dividends and set its first new all-time closing high since January 2000 on October 3, finishing the year at 12,463. The S&P 500 returned 15.8 percent to close at 1,418, and the NASDAQ Composite returned 10.4 percent to close at 2,415, still roughly half its dot-com-era peak. Small-cap stocks and international developed markets outperformed U.S. large caps, with the MSCI EAFE index returning roughly 26 percent in dollar terms. The year's only significant setback was a sharp correction from early May to mid-June, in which the S&P 500 fell about 7.7 percent on inflation fears and emerging-market weakness, before the market resumed an almost uninterrupted rally into year-end. Volatility was exceptionally low, with the VIX averaging under 13 for the year.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    ExxonMobil reported $39.5 billion in annual profit, the largest ever posted by a U.S. corporation to that point, while the five major Wall Street investment banks, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Merrill Lynch, Lehman Brothers, and Bear Stearns, recorded record earnings and bonuses. Google's $1.65 billion stock acquisition of YouTube in October became the year's most symbolically important deal, alongside Disney's $7.4 billion purchase of Pixar and AT&T's $67 billion acquisition of BellSouth. MasterCard's May IPO was among the largest and best-performing new issues of the decade, roughly doubling within months. On the losing side, Ford Motor Company posted a record $12.7 billion annual loss and had to mortgage nearly all its assets to fund its restructuring, while General Motors also posted a substantial loss, underscoring the deepening crisis in Detroit even as financial and technology companies thrived.

    Investment Themes

    Private equity and leveraged buyouts dominated the year, with global M&A hitting a record $3.9 trillion, including the Freescale Semiconductor, HCA, and Kinder Morgan buyouts and the announcement of the record-setting $39 billion Equity Office Properties deal. Gold surged 23 percent to its best year since 1979 as a hedge against inflation and dollar weakness, while copper and industrial metals boomed on global demand before pulling back. Credit spreads compressed to cycle tights and complacency about risk was widespread, epitomized by the market's largely untroubled digestion of hedge fund Amaranth Advisors' $6.6 billion collapse in September, the largest hedge fund failure since Long-Term Capital Management, which was read at the time as evidence the financial system had matured rather than as a warning sign.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Democrats swept both chambers of Congress in the November 7 midterms, gaining 31 House seats and six Senate seats to end twelve years of Republican control, positioning Nancy Pelosi to become the first female Speaker of the House. Rumsfeld resigned as Defense Secretary the following day and was succeeded by Robert Gates. The Supreme Court's Hamdan v. Rumsfeld ruling in June struck down the administration's military commissions as unlawful, prompting Congress to pass the Military Commissions Act in October. A cascade of scandals, including Jack Abramoff's guilty plea in January and the wider lobbying-corruption probe, the September revelation of Mark Foley's explicit messages to congressional pages, and the February-March Dubai Ports World controversy, compounded public disillusionment with Republican governance. The bipartisan Iraq Study Group released its report in December, describing the war as "grave and deteriorating" and recommending a fundamental strategic shift.

    Domestic Policy

    The Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit took effect January 1, the largest expansion of Medicare since its creation, with a rocky rollout that nonetheless exceeded enrollment projections by year's end. Congress reauthorized the USA PATRIOT Act in March, passed the Pension Protection Act in August (the biggest pension-law overhaul since 1974), reauthorized the Voting Rights Act for 25 years in July, and passed the Secure Fence Act in October authorizing 700 miles of border fencing after comprehensive immigration reform collapsed in the House despite millions marching in the spring. President Bush issued the first veto of his presidency in July, rejecting a bill to expand federally funded stem cell research. Treasury Secretary John Snow was replaced by Henry Paulson and White House Chief of Staff Andrew Card by Josh Bolten as part of a broader second-term staff shake-up, while state ballot measures banning same-sex marriage passed in seven of eight states and Michigan voted to ban affirmative action.

    Foreign Policy

    Iraq dominated foreign policy: the February bombing of the Golden Mosque in Samarra triggered open sectarian civil war, American troop deaths reached 823 for the year, and Nouri al-Maliki became prime minister in May, even as a U.S. airstrike killed al-Qaeda in Iraq leader Abu Musab al-Zarqawi in June and Saddam Hussein was executed in December. The month-long 2006 Lebanon War between Israel and Hezbollah tested American diplomacy over the summer, while Iran's resumed uranium enrichment drew a UN Security Council referral and the first sanctions resolution in December. North Korea test-fired a long-range missile in July and conducted its first nuclear test in October, prompting UN sanctions and a resumption of the Six-Party Talks. In Afghanistan, NATO assumed lead security responsibility in the south in July as the Taliban insurgency visibly reintensified, and the administration acknowledged the CIA's secret detention program for the first time in September.

    Important Elections

    The November 7 midterms were the year's defining political event: Democrats gained 31 House seats to take control 233-202 and netted exactly the six Senate seats needed for a 51-49 majority, with Jim Webb's narrow Virginia upset of George Allen and Jon Tester's win in Montana providing the decisive margins. Harry Reid became Senate Majority Leader and Nancy Pelosi the first woman elected Speaker of the House. Democrats also gained six governorships, including Deval Patrick's historic election as Massachusetts's first Black governor and Eliot Spitzer's landslide win in New York, while Arnold Schwarzenegger's reelection in California was a rare Republican bright spot. The election was widely understood as a referendum on the Iraq War, the Bush administration's competence, and a Republican culture of corruption embodied by Abramoff, Foley, Bob Ney, and Randy Cunningham.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Man's Chest was the year's dominant blockbuster, crossing $1 billion worldwide faster than any film before it, alongside hits like Cars, Casino Royale (Daniel Craig's acclaimed Bond debut), Superman Returns, and The Da Vinci Code. Prestige cinema flourished with Martin Scorsese's The Departed, Clint Eastwood's paired Iwo Jima films Flags of Our Fathers and Letters from Iwo Jima, Alejandro Gonzalez Inarritu's Babel, Guillermo del Toro's Pan's Labyrinth, and Alfonso Cuaron's Children of Men. Sacha Baron Cohen's Borat became a genuine comedic phenomenon, Al Gore's An Inconvenient Truth pushed climate change into mainstream conversation en route to a Best Documentary Oscar, and United 93 and World Trade Center offered the first major dramatizations of the September 11 attacks.

    Music

    The Disney Channel's High School Musical soundtrack was the year's best-selling album, epitomizing a maturing digital-pop landscape alongside Justin Timberlake's FutureSex/LoveSounds, Beyonce's B'Day, and Gnarls Barkley's Grammy-winning "Crazy." Bob Dylan's Modern Times debuted at number one to critical acclaim, and Taylor Swift released her self-titled debut album in October, a modest success at the time that loomed far larger in hindsight. Daniel Powter's "Bad Day" was Billboard's top song of the year, and the industry's structural shift continued as digital downloads eclipsed CD singles amid an ongoing decline in album sales.

    Television

    American Idol remained the most-watched program on television, with Taylor Hicks crowned the season-five winner, while the fall brought debuts that would define the next decade of television: NBC's Heroes and 30 Rock, and Showtime's Dexter. Established dramas Grey's Anatomy, Lost, 24 (at its Emmy-winning creative peak), and Battlestar Galactica thrived alongside the final season of The Sopranos, and Stephen Colbert's April 29 White House Correspondents' Dinner speech became an early viral sensation that signaled late-night comedy's growing role as trusted news for younger viewers. Cable's prestige-drama ascendance over broadcast, rising DVR adoption, and the emergence of online video all pointed toward a coming transformation of the medium.

    Technology

    2006 was the last full year before the smartphone era: BlackBerrys ruled the professional class while consumers carried flip phones like the Motorola RAZR, with Apple's iPhone still a year from announcement. Twitter launched in March, Facebook opened beyond college campuses to the general public in September, and Google's October acquisition of YouTube for $1.65 billion legitimized user-generated video as a mass medium, developments that led Time magazine to name "You" its Person of the Year. The Nintendo Wii and PlayStation 3 launched in November alongside the year-old Xbox 360, ushering in a new console generation, while Amazon's largely unnoticed launch of its S3 and EC2 cloud-computing services quietly laid the infrastructure for the next two decades of internet business.

    Social Trends

    The Iraq War's collapse into civil war and Hurricane Katrina's still-visible aftermath drove a pervasive national fatigue, while millions of immigrants and supporters marched nationwide that spring, including a "Day Without Immigrants" boycott on May 1, mobilizing Latino communities in unprecedented numbers after comprehensive reform legislation collapsed in Congress. Ted Haggard's resignation as president of the National Association of Evangelicals following a sex-and-drugs scandal embarrassed the religious right, and the U.S. population crossed 300 million on October 17, a milestone widely marked in the press. Same-sex marriage bans passed in seven of eight states voting on the question in November, while Al Gore's An Inconvenient Truth helped move climate change into the cultural mainstream.

    Crime

    The Duke lacrosse rape case, later shown to be a wrongful accusation, dominated cable news and cultural commentary as a proxy fight over race, class, and campus culture. The West Virginia Sago Mine disaster killed twelve miners in January, and the October shooting of five Amish schoolgirls in Nickel Mines, Pennsylvania, became a national moment of grief distinguished by the community's public forgiveness. Polygamist sect leader Warren Jeffs was captured in August after months on the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list, and the Barry Bonds steroid scandal escalated with the publication of Game of Shadows in March.

    Sports

    The Pittsburgh Steelers defeated the Seattle Seahawks in a poorly officiated Super Bowl XL, and the Miami Heat won the NBA title behind a dominant Dwyane Wade, while the St. Louis Cardinals beat the Detroit Tigers in the World Series. Italy won the FIFA World Cup final over France in a match remembered for Zinedine Zidane's headbutt on Marco Materazzi, and the University of Texas's Rose Bowl win over USC in January, powered by Vince Young, was hailed as one of the greatest college football games ever played. Tiger Woods won two major championships, Floyd Landis's Tour de France victory was clouded almost immediately by a doping positive, and Kentucky Derby winner Barbaro's catastrophic breakdown at the Preakness became a national story of public mourning for the horse.

    Public Mood

    The prevailing mood was weary and cynical: an unpopular, increasingly bloody war, a still-visible Katrina recovery, gas prices above $3 a gallon, and a president whose approval ratings sank into the mid-30s combined to sour the national temper. Yet beneath that surface, a genuine excitement about emerging digital culture, YouTube, Facebook, Twitter, the Wii, and affordable high-definition television, gave many Americans, especially younger ones, a sense that ordinary people rather than institutions were about to remake culture, a sentiment Time magazine captured in naming "You" its Person of the Year.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporaries believed 2006 would be remembered above all as the year the Iraq War was lost politically at home: the Samarra bombing and ensuing civil war, the NSA warrantless-surveillance controversy, Samuel Alito's Supreme Court confirmation, the Enron convictions, the immigration megamarches, the Israel-Hezbollah war, the Foley and Abramoff scandals, and the culminating Democratic sweep in November all felt like the defining story of a repudiated administration and a nation reassessing the war on terror.

    What actually mattered

    In retrospect, the year's most load-bearing developments were structural rather than electoral: Ben Bernanke's takeover of the Federal Reserve positioned the man who would manage the 2008 crisis response, the housing market's peak and the first subprime failures quietly began the countdown to that crisis, and the almost simultaneous emergence of Twitter, Facebook's public launch, Amazon's cloud infrastructure, and Google's purchase of YouTube built the technological substrate of the next two decades, while Iraq's civil war discredited the neoconservative foreign-policy synthesis for a generation and North Korea's nuclear test permanently altered nonproliferation politics.

    Most forgotten story

    The Dubai Ports World controversy, in which a bipartisan revolt forced a UAE state-owned company to abandon its acquisition of terminal operations at six American ports, was arguably the biggest domestic political story of early 2006 and a rare moment of Bush losing control of his own party, yet it has almost entirely vanished from popular memory of the decade, crowded out by Iraq, Katrina, and the financial crisis, its legacy surviving mainly in the expanded foreign-investment review process it inspired.

    Most underrated story

    Amazon's launch of its S3 and EC2 cloud-computing services in 2006 drew almost no mainstream coverage at the time but permanently changed the economics of starting a technology company, providing the infrastructure underlying nearly every consequential digital business of the following two decades, from the app economy to streaming to the generative-AI boom.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The single most striking hindsight surprise is that the peak of the U.S. housing bubble and the founding of the modern social-media era happened within the same twelve months, with almost no contemporary commentator connecting the two; front pages that summer were dominated by Iraq, Cheney's hunting accident, and the JonBenet Ramsey case rather than the quiet convergence of a coming financial collapse and a new information architecture.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    Vice President Dick Cheney's accidental shooting of a hunting companion in Texas in FebruaryIt generated a week of cable-news fixation over White House secrecy but produced no policy consequences and never intersected with anything structural about the administration's power.
    The Dubai Ports World controversy over a UAE company's proposed takeover of terminal operations at six U.S. portsOnce DP World divested, port security never became the chronic threat contemporaries feared, and the episode was overshadowed by the larger, more clearly told stories of Katrina and Iraq.
    The Duke lacrosse rape caseThough a genuine miscarriage of justice, it seeded no durable policy reforms and now survives mainly as a journalism-ethics case study rather than a turning point.
    John Mark Karr's false confession to the JonBenet Ramsey killingDNA cleared him within weeks, leaving behind only a case study in cable news's appetite for cold-case theater and confession bias.
    The Mark Foley congressional page scandalIt was quickly absorbed into the larger midterm narrative, since the decisive driver of the Republican defeat was Iraq rather than any single scandal.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Amazon's launch of S3 and EC2 cloud-computing servicesIt made on-demand computing infrastructure a commodity, underwriting virtually every major internet business and platform built over the following two decades.
    Twitter's launch in MarchIt passed almost unnoticed but would go on to reshape journalism, political communication, and protest movements from Tahrir Square to the American presidency itself.
    Facebook's opening to the general public in SeptemberWithin a decade it became the world's dominant news-distribution platform and the central case study in debates over disinformation and platform accountability.
    The founding of WikiLeaks in OctoberIt registered as a curiosity at the time but later delivered the Manning cables and the 2016 DNC email releases, becoming a hinge point in American political history.
    Early subprime mortgage warning signs, including the failure of Ownit Mortgage Solutions in DecemberMainstream coverage was minimal, but the resetting adjustable-rate mortgages and early defaults flagged that autumn were the opening chapter of the 2008 financial crisis.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Bombing of Golden Mosque in Samarra Ignites Sunni-Shia Civil War in Iraq
    2. Democrats Sweep Both Houses of Congress in Midterm Wave
    3. Rumsfeld Resigns as Defense Secretary Day After Election Rout
    4. North Korea Conducts First Nuclear Weapons Test
    5. Saddam Hussein Executed by Hanging in Baghdad
    6. Dow Jones Closes Above 12,000, Erasing Six-Year-Old Record High
    7. Google Buys YouTube for $1.65 Billion
    8. Bernanke Succeeds Greenspan as Fed Chairman After 18 Years
    9. Foley Resigns Amid Page Scandal as GOP Corruption Cases Mount
    10. Millions March in 'Day Without Immigrants' Protests Nationwide

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryAlan Greenspan chaired his final Federal Reserve meeting on January 31, delivering a quarter-point rate hike before Ben Bernanke was sworn in as his successor the next day. The Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit took effect January 1 in the largest expansion of Medicare since its creation, while the Sago Mine disaster in West Virginia killed twelve miners on January 2. Samuel Alito was confirmed to the Supreme Court on January 31 after a failed filibuster attempt, and lobbyist Jack Abramoff pleaded guilty to fraud and conspiracy, opening a corruption investigation that would ensnare more than twenty officials.
    FebruaryBernanke's first month as Fed Chairman coincided with a bipartisan uproar over Dubai Ports World's proposed acquisition of terminal operations at six American ports, a controversy that would force the company to divest within weeks. Vice President Cheney accidentally shot a hunting companion on a Texas quail hunt on February 11, drawing a week of intense media scrutiny. Most consequentially, the February 22 bombing of the Golden Mosque in Samarra ignited sectarian violence that pushed Iraq into open civil war for the remainder of the year.
    MarchTwitter quietly launched on March 21 with Jack Dorsey's first post, unnoticed outside tech circles. Congress reauthorized the USA PATRIOT Act on March 9, and the Duke lacrosse rape allegation surfaced, quickly becoming a national media obsession. Nationwide immigration marches began building momentum, with an estimated 500,000 demonstrators in Los Angeles on March 25 protesting the House's enforcement-only immigration bill.
    AprilJosh Bolten replaced Andrew Card as White House Chief of Staff in a broader second-term staff shake-up. Stephen Colbert's roast of President Bush at the White House Correspondents' Dinner on April 29 became an early viral sensation. Immigration reform negotiations continued in Congress even as the House and Senate remained far apart on any compromise.
    MayMillions participated in the 'Day Without Immigrants' boycott and marches on May 1, the culmination of the spring's immigration protests, even as comprehensive reform stalled in Congress. USA Today revealed the NSA's bulk collection of Americans' phone records, extending the prior year's warrantless-wiretapping revelations, while former Enron executives Jeffrey Skilling and Kenneth Lay were convicted of fraud and conspiracy on May 25. Nouri al-Maliki became Iraq's prime minister, and a sharp market correction began amid inflation fears and emerging-market weakness.
    JuneA U.S. airstrike killed al-Qaeda in Iraq leader Abu Musab al-Zarqawi on June 7, a rare tactical success amid worsening sectarian violence. The Supreme Court ruled in Hamdan v. Rumsfeld on June 29 that the administration's Guantanamo military commissions were unlawful, the same day the Federal Reserve delivered its final rate hike of the cycle, bringing the funds rate to 5.25 percent.
    JulyPresident Bush issued the first veto of his presidency, rejecting a bill to expand federally funded stem cell research. War broke out between Israel and Hezbollah on July 12, drawing sharp criticism of the administration's slow diplomatic response, while oil prices hit a then-record intraday high near $78 a barrel on July 14 amid the fighting. Henry Paulson was confirmed as Treasury Secretary, Kenneth Lay died before sentencing on July 5, and national home prices, as measured by the Case-Shiller index, reached their all-time peak before beginning a historic decline.
    AugustThe month-long Lebanon war ended with a UN-brokered ceasefire on August 14. The International Astronomical Union voted to demote Pluto from full planetary status on August 24, a rare lighthearted science story amid a grim year. Polygamist sect leader Warren Jeffs was captured after months as a fugitive, and Amazon quietly launched its EC2 cloud-computing service, an event almost entirely unnoticed by the mainstream press.
    SeptemberFacebook opened registration to the general public on September 26, following a backlash earlier in the month over its new News Feed feature. Republican Congressman Mark Foley resigned after revelations of sexually explicit messages to teenage congressional pages, a scandal that would damage Republicans in the coming election. The Bush administration publicly acknowledged the CIA's secret detention program for the first time, and hedge fund Amaranth Advisors collapsed, losing $6.6 billion in a week on natural gas trades.
    OctoberNorth Korea conducted its first nuclear weapons test on October 9, the same day Google announced its $1.65 billion acquisition of YouTube. The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at a record high on October 3 for the first time since January 2000, and the U.S. population officially crossed 300 million on October 17. An Amish schoolhouse shooting in Nickel Mines, Pennsylvania, killed five girls on October 2, and Congress passed the Military Commissions Act and the Secure Fence Act authorizing border fencing.
    NovemberDemocrats swept to control of both the House and Senate in the November 7 midterm elections, the party's largest gain since 1994, positioning Nancy Pelosi to become the first female Speaker of the House. Donald Rumsfeld resigned as Defense Secretary the following day, to be replaced by Robert Gates. Televangelist Ted Haggard resigned amid a sex scandal, and the Nintendo Wii and PlayStation 3 both launched in North America, ushering in a new console generation.
    DecemberThe bipartisan Iraq Study Group released its report on December 6, describing the war as 'grave and deteriorating' and recommending a fundamental change in strategy. Former President Gerald Ford died on December 26 at age 93, prompting a week of national commemoration. Saddam Hussein was executed by hanging on December 30 after his conviction for the 1982 Dujail massacre, closing out a year that had begun with Iraq's descent into civil war.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~2.8% real GDP growth for the year (initial BEA estimate ~3.3-3.4%, later revised down)
    InflationHeadline CPI averaged ~3.2% for the year; December YoY 2.5% headline, 2.6% core (peak ~4.3% YoY in June)
    Unemployment4.7% in January falling to 4.4% by December (cycle low)
    S&P 500 return+15.8% total return (+13.6% price); closed at 1,418.30
    Dow return+19.0% total return (+16.3% price); closed at 12,463.15, first all-time closing high since January 2000
    Fed funds rateRaised in 25bp steps (March, May, June) to 5.25% by June 29, then held at 5.25% through year-end
    Oil priceWTI opened ~$61/bbl, peaked at an intraday all-time high near $78.40 on July 14, closed the year at ~$61.05/bbl
    Gold priceRose from ~$517/oz to close near $636/oz, a ~23% gain and its best year since 1979
    Population~299 million, crossing 300 million on October 17, 2006
    Median home price~$221,900 median existing-home price for the year, essentially flat versus 2005
    National debt~$8.68 trillion gross national debt at calendar year-end (~64% of GDP); FY2006 federal deficit $248 billion (1.8% of GDP)

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    For most Americans, 2006 felt like a year of exhaustion rather than crisis. The Iraq War, which had seemed merely difficult in 2004 and 2005, curdled into what looked from the living room television like an outright civil war after the Samarra mosque bombing in February, and the casualty reports, sectarian massacres, and increasingly desperate administration rhetoric wore down a public that had once rallied behind the war on terror. Gas prices flirting with $3 a gallon, a president whose approval ratings sank into the mid-30s, and a seemingly endless parade of scandals, from Abramoff to Foley to Cunningham to DeLay, gave the year a distinctly soured, cynical flavor that culminated in the decisive Democratic sweep of November and the resignation of Donald Rumsfeld.

    And yet, for anyone whose life was organized around a paycheck, a mortgage, or a retirement account rather than cable news, 2006 could feel like one of the better years of the decade. Unemployment was at a five-year low, the stock market notched its best year since 2003 and finally reclaimed its dot-com-era high, gasoline prices eased back from their summer spike, and household net worth reached a record high. For a homeowner who had ridden the boom, the first signs that the market was turning, slowing sales, flattening prices, registered as barely a hiccup rather than the opening act of a catastrophe; only the small community of subprime borrowers facing resetting adjustable-rate mortgages, and the analysts quietly flagging trouble at lenders like Ownit, could sense what was coming.

    Meanwhile, daily life was being reorganized in ways that felt exciting rather than ominous. A twenty-something in 2006 was as likely to be watching YouTube clips, setting up a Facebook profile once it opened beyond college campuses, or experimenting with a new service called Twitter as fretting over Baghdad, and Time magazine's choice of "You" as Person of the Year captured a genuine, if inchoate, sense that ordinary people were about to remake media and culture on their own terms. The Wii and PlayStation 3 arrived at Thanksgiving to lines around the block, American Idol pulled thirty million viewers a week, and Borat somehow became a shared cultural reference point.

    What almost no one felt in 2006, not the pundits obsessing over Cheney's hunting accident, not the traders shrugging off Amaranth's implosion, not the homeowners watching their equity plateau, was that the year's quietest developments, a cloud-computing service, a microblogging site, a mortgage lender's bankruptcy filing, would matter more to the next twenty years than the headlines that consumed it. To live through 2006 was to feel a nation simultaneously tired of a war it could not win, cautiously confident about its economic prospects, and unknowingly present at the creation of the technological and financial forces that would define the following decade.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2007
    Econ +0Mark +1Poli +0Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2007 was the hinge year of the American twenty-first century, though almost no one experienced it that way at the time. On the surface it looked like a continuation of the mid-2000s: the economy grew, the Dow and S&P 500 set fresh all-time highs in October, unemployment averaged under five percent for most of the year, and consumers kept spending. Politically, Washington settled into the friction of divided government after Democrats retook both chambers of Congress in the 2006 midterms and Nancy Pelosi became the first woman Speaker of the House. President Bush, deeply unpopular over Iraq, gambled on a troop "surge" under General David Petraeus that, combined with the grassroots Sunni revolt against Al-Qaeda known as the Anbar Awakening, brought violence down sharply by year's end even as it produced the war's bloodiest spring and summer for American forces.

    Beneath that placid surface, the architecture of the next decade was being poured. Apple's iPhone, released June 29, would restructure how Americans communicated, shopped, dated, and organized politically. Google answered in November with the Android-based Open Handset Alliance; Amazon shipped the first Kindle the same month; Netflix quietly began streaming video in January; Facebook opened its developer platform in May and Twitter broke through at South by Southwest in March. None of this registered as the year's biggest story, because the year's biggest story was unfolding, just as quietly, in the bond and mortgage markets. New Century Financial's April bankruptcy, the June-July collapse of two Bear Stearns hedge funds, and above all BNP Paribas's August 9 freeze of three funds it could no longer price marked the opening of what the National Bureau of Economic Research later dated as the start of the Great Recession in December 2007 — a crisis that Ben Bernanke and Henry Paulson spent most of the year publicly insisting was "contained."

    Culturally, 2007 was a year of real creative richness — Mad Men and the Sopranos finale on television, No Country for Old Men and There Will Be Blood in theaters, Rihanna's "Umbrella" inescapable on the radio — shadowed by anxiety: the Virginia Tech massacre in April was the deadliest mass shooting in American history to that point, the November Writers Guild strike hollowed out scripted television, and a nervy, tabloid-saturated news cycle (Anna Nicole Smith, Britney Spears, Michael Vick, Barry Bonds's tainted home-run record) coexisted with the first stirrings of the Obama phenomenon. Judged against its own era's expectations, 2007 was a year of solid, unspectacular economic and political performance riding on top of a foundation that was already cracking, producing a country that felt simultaneously prosperous and uneasy — correctly, as it turned out, on both counts.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+0Full-year GDP growth stayed positive near 2% and unemployment averaged under 5%, but residential investment collapsed, headline inflation hit a 4.1% December peak, and the year ended with the NBER-dated onset of the Great Recession, so a middling headline conceals a decisively negative trajectory.
    Markets+1The S&P 500, Dow, and NASDAQ all posted positive full-year total returns and reached fresh all-time closing highs on October 9, but a 21% drop in financials, collapsing breadth, and a slide into a nascent bear market by December keep the year from scoring higher.
    Politics+0Divided government produced real legislative output (an energy law, a minimum-wage increase, ethics reform) and the Iraq surge began working by year's end, but a cabinet-level scandal cost the Attorney General his job, comprehensive immigration reform died in the Senate, and the war remained deeply unpopular, adding up to a middling year of governance by friction rather than crisis or triumph.
    Culture+1A genuinely landmark creative year — Mad Men, No Country for Old Men, There Will Be Blood, Juno, the iPhone — was pulled downward by the Virginia Tech massacre, the writers' strike, and a fraying, tabloid-heavy public mood, netting out to modestly positive.
    Overall Mood+0Record stock highs, a landmark phone launch, and an electrifying primary season sat uneasily against a deeply unpopular war, a collapsing housing market, and the first quiet tremors of a financial panic, leaving the country's felt experience closer to anxious neutrality than confident forward motion.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. President Bush announces a troop "surge" of over 20,000 additional soldiers to Iraq under General David Petraeus
    2. Democrats take control of Congress; Nancy Pelosi becomes the first woman Speaker of the House
    3. A gunman kills 32 people at Virginia Tech in the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history to that point
    4. Apple releases the iPhone, redefining the mobile phone and consumer technology
    5. The subprime mortgage crisis erupts as New Century Financial collapses, Bear Stearns hedge funds fail, and BNP Paribas freezes redemptions
    6. The Federal Reserve cuts interest rates three times as credit markets seize up, while the Dow closes above 14,000 for the first time
    7. Barack Obama emerges as Hillary Clinton's chief rival for the 2008 Democratic presidential nomination
    8. Barry Bonds breaks Hank Aaron's career home run record amid a lingering steroid scandal
    9. Generals Petraeus and Crocker testify to Congress that the surge is reducing violence, amid the "General Betray Us" controversy
    10. Attorney General Alberto Gonzales resigns amid the U.S. Attorneys firing scandal

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy in 2007 was the pivot between the long post-2001 expansion and the deepest downturn since the Great Depression. Real GDP still grew at roughly 2%, carried by consumer spending and exports, but residential fixed investment collapsed at close to an 18% annual pace and subtracted nearly a full point from growth. Payroll growth decelerated steadily through the year and the unemployment rate, after touching a cycle low of 4.4% in March, drifted up to 5.0% by December, the first sustained rise of the expansion. Chairman Bernanke spent the first half of the year publicly describing subprime troubles as "likely to be contained," then reversed course after the August funding freeze, cutting the discount rate and beginning a sequence of federal funds rate cuts that took the target from 5.25% to 4.25% by year-end and introducing new liquidity tools such as the Term Auction Facility.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth approximately +2.0% for the year (vintages range 1.8%-2.2%); headline CPI +4.1% year-over-year in December (core 2.4%); unemployment rose from 4.6% in January to 5.0% in December; federal funds rate cut from 5.25% to 4.25% across three moves (September, October, December); 30-year mortgage rate averaged roughly 6.34% for the year; median existing-home price fell to about $217,900, its first year-over-year decline since the Great Depression, with the Case-Shiller 20-city index down about 9% and existing-home sales down roughly 13%; WTI crude rose about 57% to close near $96/barrel; gross federal debt reached about $9.2 trillion by December 31.

    Major Events

    New Century Financial, the second-largest subprime lender, filed for Chapter 11 on April 2, part of a wave of more than two dozen subprime-lender failures culminating in American Home Mortgage's August 6 bankruptcy. In June, Bear Stearns pledged up to $3.2 billion to bail out two of its structured-credit hedge funds, which were declared worthless by July, the first clear signal that AAA-rated mortgage securities were mispriced. On August 9, BNP Paribas froze three funds citing a total evaporation of liquidity, an event now widely marked as the start of the global credit crunch, prompting emergency injections from the ECB and the Fed. Northern Rock suffered the first British bank run in roughly 150 years on September 14, and Countrywide Financial, the largest U.S. mortgage originator, drew down its entire credit line and took an emergency investment from Bank of America in August to avoid collapse. The Bush administration launched the HOPE NOW alliance in the fall to freeze teaser rates on some subprime loans, while oil surged from about $50 to nearly $100 a barrel over the course of the year.

    Why this score

    A score of 0 reflects a year that was still positive on paper — growth stayed above 1.5% and unemployment averaged under 5% — but whose second half saw residential investment in freefall, credit markets seizing up, and the onset (later NBER-dated to December 2007) of the Great Recession, so the year's deteriorating trajectory offsets its still-respectable headline numbers.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities delivered the extraordinary combination of positive full-year returns and a slide into a nascent bear market by December. The S&P 500 gained roughly 3.5% in price (about 5.5% total return) to close at 1,468.36, the Dow rose about 6.4% (roughly 8.9% total return) to 13,264.82, and the NASDAQ Composite gained nearly 10% to 2,652.28. All three indices set closing all-time or multi-year highs on October 9 — the S&P at 1,565.15, the Dow at 14,164.53 — levels the S&P would not reclaim until 2013. Sector breadth was unusually narrow: energy (+34%) and materials (+22%) led while financials fell about 21% amid mounting subprime write-downs, and technology's "quadruple play" of Apple, Google, RIM, and Amazon carried much of the index's gains.

    Character

    Transitional

    Major Companies

    Blackstone Group's June 22 IPO and Fortress Investment Group's earlier February listing marked the top of the private-equity boom; VMware's August IPO, up 76% on debut, was the largest tech offering since Google. On the losing side, Merrill Lynch took roughly $8 billion in third-quarter write-downs and forced out CEO Stan O'Neal in October, while Citigroup's mounting CDO exposure cost Chuck Prince his job in November, three months after his "as long as the music is playing, you've got to get up and dance" remark. Countrywide Financial nearly failed and took an emergency Bank of America investment. Sovereign wealth funds stepped in to recapitalize wounded banks late in the year, with Abu Dhabi taking a $7.5 billion stake in Citigroup and China Investment Corporation putting $5 billion into Morgan Stanley. The largest completed deals were the roughly $99 billion RBS-led breakup of ABN AMRO, the largest bank takeover in history and in hindsight a disastrous overreach that helped sink RBS, and the $45 billion KKR/TPG/Goldman leveraged buyout of TXU, the largest LBO ever completed.

    Investment Themes

    The year's dominant theme was the collision between a still-raging private-equity and leveraged-buyout boom in the first half and a full-blown credit crunch in the second, as structured mortgage products revealed themselves to be badly mispriced. Sovereign wealth funds emerged as a new class of marginal buyer, recapitalizing Wall Street's wounded banks. Energy and materials stocks benefited from crude oil's surge toward $100 a barrel, while financials were pummeled and consumer discretionary names suffered as housing-linked spending slowed. Gold rose roughly 31% to about $833 an ounce as a hedge against both inflation and financial-system stress.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    President Bush announced a troop surge for Iraq on January 10, deploying General David Petraeus to implement a new counterinsurgency strategy that, paired with the Anbar Awakening of Sunni tribes against Al-Qaeda, cut violence sharply by year's end after the war's deadliest months for American troops that spring and summer. The 110th Congress convened January 4 under Democratic control, with Nancy Pelosi as the first female Speaker of the House and Harry Reid as Senate Majority Leader. Attorney General Alberto Gonzales resigned August 27 after months of scandal over the politicized firing of U.S. Attorneys. A grand bipartisan immigration-reform compromise negotiated by Senators Kennedy and Kyl collapsed on a failed June 28 cloture vote. Bush vetoed the SCHIP children's health insurance expansion twice in the fall. On April 16, a gunman killed 32 people at Virginia Tech, then the deadliest mass shooting in U.S. history. Bush commuted Scooter Libby's prison sentence in the CIA-leak case on July 2, and Petraeus and Ambassador Ryan Crocker delivered high-profile Iraq testimony to Congress in September, briefly overshadowed by the MoveOn.org "General Betray Us" advertisement.

    Domestic Policy

    Divided government defined the year, with Democrats controlling both chambers and Bush wielding his veto repeatedly, especially over Iraq war-funding timelines. Congress nonetheless passed the Energy Independence and Security Act (raising fuel-economy standards for the first time since 1975), the first minimum-wage increase since 1997 (phasing the federal floor from $5.15 to $7.25), the Honest Leadership and Open Government Act tightening lobbying rules, the Implementing Recommendations of the 9/11 Commission Act, and the America COMPETES Act on science funding.
