Obama announced an Afghanistan troop surge of 30,000 on December 1, and the Fort Hood shooting on November 5, which killed 13 people, became the year's deadliest single act of violence on US soil. Obama's October 9 Nobel Peace Prize, awarded less than nine months into his term, was met with widespread surprise.

    Domestic Policy

    Health-care reform consumed the domestic agenda from summer onward, moving through House passage in November and a party-line Senate cloture vote on Christmas Eve after Majority Leader Harry Reid secured conservative Democrats' votes through concessions such as the Cornhusker Kickback for Nebraska's Ben Nelson. The administration paired the Recovery Act with continued TARP-funded interventions, including the Chrysler and General Motors bankruptcies and the Cash for Clunkers program, while the March revelation that AIG had paid $165 million in retention bonuses after receiving $170 billion in federal aid produced bipartisan fury. Other significant legislation included the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act, Obama's first bill, signed January 29, CHIP reauthorization, the Family Smoking Prevention and Tobacco Control Act, the Credit CARD Act, and the Matthew Shepard and James Byrd Jr. Hate Crimes Prevention Act, while the American Clean Energy and Security Act, the Waxman-Markey cap-and-trade bill, passed the House in June but died in the Senate. Obama also reversed Bush-era restrictions on embryonic stem-cell research and the Mexico City abortion-funding policy, and the EPA's December greenhouse-gas endangerment finding laid the groundwork for future climate regulation.

    Foreign Policy

    Obama pursued a broad diplomatic reset, announcing in February that combat operations in Iraq would end by August 2010, ordering an initial Afghanistan troop increase in March followed by a much larger 30,000-troop surge announced in December, and delivering outreach speeches in Cairo, Ankara, and Prague. Guantanamo's closure was ordered on Obama's second day in office but stalled after Congress blocked funding to relocate detainees, while the administration's release of Bush-era torture memos and its decision to try Khalid Sheikh Mohammed in federal court proved contentious. Relations with Russia warmed through the reset and New START negotiations even as the administration's September cancellation of Bush-era missile-defense plans in Poland and the Czech Republic angered Eastern European allies. Iran's disputed June election set off the Green Movement protests, which the administration was criticized for answering cautiously, while North Korea's second nuclear test in May, the April rescue of Captain Richard Phillips from Somali pirates, and the disappointing December Copenhagen climate conference rounded out a year in which the G-20 formally supplanted the G-8 as the leading forum for global economic coordination.

    Important Elections

    The 2009 off-year elections were widely read as early referenda on the new administration: Republican Bob McDonnell won Virginia's governorship by 17 points and Republican Chris Christie unseated incumbent Jon Corzine in New Jersey, both seen as warning signs for Democrats heading into 2010. In New York's 23rd congressional district, a Tea Party-backed third-party challenge split Republicans and allowed Democrat Bill Owens to win a seat the party had not held since the nineteenth century, previewing the insurgent primary battles of 2010. Al Franken was finally seated as Minnesota's senator on July 7 after an eight-month recount fight decided by 312 votes, and Michael Bloomberg narrowly won a third term as New York City mayor after the city council controversially lifted term limits.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Domestic box office hit a record roughly $10.6 billion despite the recession, driven partly by higher-priced 3D tickets. James Cameron's Avatar opened December 18 and began its run toward becoming the highest-grossing film ever made, while Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen, Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince, The Twilight Saga: New Moon, Pixar's Up, and the raucous comedy The Hangover topped the box office. Prestige releases included Kathryn Bigelow's The Hurt Locker, Tarantino's Inglourious Basterds, Precious, the Coen brothers' A Serious Man, and Jason Reitman's recession-attuned Up in the Air, while the February ceremony honoring 2008 films crowned Slumdog Millionaire and the Academy announced an expansion to ten Best Picture nominees.

    Music

    Pop charts turned toward a female-driven dance-pop revival led by Lady Gaga, whose Poker Face closed the year atop the Hot 100 and whose album The Fame Monster reshaped pop aesthetics. The Black Eyed Peas held the Hot 100's top spot for a combined 26 weeks, Beyonce's Single Ladies became the year's most-imitated cultural artifact, and Taylor Swift's Fearless was the year's best-selling album. Hip-hop had a strong year with Drake's breakthrough So Far Gone mixtape and Jay-Z's The Blueprint 3, indie acts like Animal Collective, Grizzly Bear, and Phoenix drew critical acclaim, and Susan Boyle's viral April television audition drove her debut album to the year's best US sales.

    Television

    Cable's prestige wave crested with Mad Men's second consecutive Emmy win and Breaking Bad's acclaimed second season, while broadcast television found new life in Glee and the family-sitcom revival Modern Family, both premiering to strong reception. American Idol remained the top-rated unscripted show despite a controversial finale, Jersey Shore's December premiere on MTV became an instant cultural lightning rod, and NBC's disastrous decision to move Jay Leno to 10 p.m. five nights a week, installing Conan O'Brien at the Tonight Show in his place, set up the late-night implosion that would follow in early 2010.

    Technology

    2009 was the tipping point for the modern smartphone era: Apple released the iPhone 3GS, Verizon's Motorola Droid gave Android its breakout moment against AT&T's iPhone exclusivity, and BlackBerry and Palm began visibly losing ground. Twitter became the year's breakout web service, amplifying everything from the Kanye West-Taylor Swift VMA moment to Iran's Green Movement protests, while Facebook introduced the Like button and surpassed MySpace in US traffic on its way to 350 million users worldwide. Windows 7 arrived in October to relief after Vista, Amazon's Kindle 2 pushed e-books toward the mainstream, and, almost entirely unnoticed at the time, the Bitcoin network's genesis block was mined on January 3.

    Social Trends

    The Tea Party emerged as the year's dominant grassroots movement, growing from Rick Santelli's February rant into Tax Day protests, a September march on Washington, and increasingly hostile August town halls over health-care reform. LGBT rights advanced unevenly: Iowa and Vermont legalized same-sex marriage in April and the National Equality March drew roughly 200,000 people to Washington in October, but Maine voters overturned their state's law in a November ballot measure. Religious disaffiliation continued to climb, with the religiously unaffiliated approaching a sixth of the population and Christian identification falling to 76 percent of adults, while the recession drove a sharp, historically unusual drop in net migration from Mexico and a decline in the national birth rate.

    Crime

    Bernie Madoff was sentenced June 29 to 150 years for the largest Ponzi scheme in history, a roughly $65 billion fraud that became a symbolic outlet for financial-crisis anger. The November 5 Fort Hood shooting, in which Army psychiatrist Nidal Hasan killed 13 people, was the year's deadliest mass killing on US soil, while the April 3 Binghamton immigration-center shooting, the May 31 murder of late-term abortion provider George Tiller, and the August rediscovery of kidnapping victim Jaycee Dugard after 18 years in captivity also shocked the country. Despite recession-era fears of a crime wave, the national crime rate continued its long decline through the year.

    Sports

    The Pittsburgh Steelers beat the Arizona Cardinals in a classic Super Bowl XLIII, the Los Angeles Lakers won the NBA title over Orlando for Kobe Bryant's first ring without Shaquille O'Neal, and the New York Yankees beat the Philadelphia Phillies for their 27th World Series title in the new Yankee Stadium. Usain Bolt set 100- and 200-meter world records at the Berlin World Championships that would stand for years, while Tiger Woods capped a strong on-course season with a Thanksgiving-weekend car crash that ignited the scandal that upended his career and dominated year-end sports coverage.

    Public Mood

    The public mood was a paradox: culturally vibrant and creatively rich even as the country absorbed real economic pain, with the euphoria of Obama's inauguration curdling within months into recession anxiety, Tea Party anger, and a bitter health-care fight, so that by December the hope of January felt like a distant memory.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    At the time, Americans believed the year was about rescue, of the banks, of General Motors and Chrysler, of the housing market, and of national morale through a historic inauguration, with cable news simultaneously fixated on a parallel celebrity news cycle involving Michael Jackson, Sully Sullenberger, Sarah Palin, Tiger Woods, and Balloon Boy that offered a form of escape from that economic anxiety.

    What actually mattered

    The more consequential developments were largely invisible at the time: the Federal Reserve's commitment to near-zero rates and large-scale asset purchases reshaped monetary policy for over a decade, the Bitcoin network was born on a cryptography mailing list, Rick Santelli's rant grew into the Tea Party movement that would remake the Republican Party, Nidal Hasan's contacts with Anwar al-Awlaki previewed the internet-radicalized lone-wolf terrorism model of the 2010s, and the failed Copenhagen climate summit pushed serious international climate action out by years.

    Most forgotten story

    South Carolina governor Mark Sanford's six-day disappearance in June 2009, later revealed as a secret trip to Argentina to see his mistress and euphemized at the time as hiking the Appalachian Trail, was treated as a defining political scandal and effectively ended his presidential ambitions, yet has since faded almost entirely from public memory.

    Most underrated story

    The Bitcoin genesis block, mined by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto on January 3, 2009, received no mainstream press coverage at all, since the network launched with essentially two participants, yet it seeded an entire asset class, a global debate over the nature of money, and a decade of financial regulation.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    Obama's October 9 Nobel Peace Prize, awarded less than nine months into his presidency and greeted with bewilderment by a majority of Americans, aged with striking irony once its recipient went on to escalate the Afghanistan war, expand drone strikes dramatically, and order the killing of an American citizen without trial.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    Captain Chesley Sullenberger's January 15 water landing of US Airways Flight 1549 on the Hudson RiverIt supplied a badly needed moment of national relief and led every newscast for a week, but it changed no policy or history, and its cultural afterlife owes mostly to the later Tom Hanks film.
    The wall-to-wall coverage of Michael Jackson's death on June 25It was arguably the most saturated celebrity-death coverage of the internet era, crashing Wikipedia and flooding Twitter, but beyond a posthumous sales bump and a subsequent manslaughter trial it left little lasting historical mark.
    The October 15 Balloon Boy hoaxCable news chased an empty homemade balloon across Colorado for hours before it was exposed as a reality-TV stunt within a day, and it is now remembered only as a punchline about the era's news judgment.
    The April 8-12 hijacking of the Maersk Alabama and the rescue of Captain Richard Phillips from Somali piratesIt felt like an early defining foreign-policy test for the new administration, but piracy off the Horn of Africa peaked and then collapsed within a couple of years as shipping convoys and armed guards became routine.
    Governor Mark Sanford's disappearance and confessed affair, alongside the Kanye West-Taylor Swift VMA incident and Tiger Woods's Thanksgiving-weekend scandalEach generated saturation tabloid coverage but none produced any durable shift in politics or culture beyond individual reputations.
    The July arrest of Harvard scholar Henry Louis Gates at his own home and the subsequent White House Beer SummitIt was treated as a national inflection point on race and policing, but in hindsight it is a footnote, since the real inflection points on that subject came later, with Trayvon Martin in 2012 and Ferguson in 2014.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The mining of the Bitcoin genesis block by Satoshi Nakamoto on January 3It received zero press coverage at the time but became the seed of an entirely new asset class, a geopolitical debate over the nature of money, and a decade of financial regulation.
    Rick Santelli's February 19 CNBC rant against mortgage relief from the floor of the Chicago Mercantile ExchangeDismissed at the time as a five-minute cable outburst, it is now recognized as the founding moment of the Tea Party movement, which delivered the 2010 Republican House wave and provided the populist scaffolding Donald Trump would later climb onto.
    Nidal Hasan's correspondence with Anwar al-Awlaki, the November 5 Fort Hood shooting, and the December 25 attempted bombing of a Detroit-bound flightTogether they marked the emergence of Al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula and the internet-propagated lone-wolf radicalization model that would dominate Western counterterrorism for the next decade, though Fort Hood was covered largely as workplace violence at the time.
    The Iranian Green Movement protests that erupted after the disputed June 12 electionIts use of cell-phone video and Twitter organizing was a direct rehearsal for the Arab Spring eighteen months later, though it was not treated as world-historical by the American press at the time.
    The Federal Reserve's decision to hold rates near zero and normalize large-scale asset purchases, alongside the government's decision not to pursue criminal prosecutions of executives responsible for mortgage-crisis fraudBoth decisions were barely front-page news in 2009, but the first reshaped asset prices and monetary politics for over a decade, while the second fed a distrust of institutions that would fuel Occupy Wall Street and populist movements on both the left and right.
    The November 6 launch of the Motorola Droid on VerizonOvershadowed by iPhone headlines at the time, it marked Android's transition from experiment to credible rival and the start of the mobile duopoly that would be worth trillions of dollars.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Obama Takes Oath as 44th President, Vows a New Era of Responsibility
    2. Airliner Ditches in Hudson River; All 155 Aboard Saved by Pilot's Calm
    3. President Signs $787 Billion Stimulus Bill; Only Three Republicans Join
    4. S&P 500 Bottoms Below 700, Its Lowest Close in More Than a Decade
    5. General Motors Files for Bankruptcy Protection; U.S. to Take 60% Stake
    6. Michael Jackson, King of Pop, Dead at 50; Internet Traffic Buckles
    7. Sotomayor Confirmed as First Hispanic Justice, 68-31
    8. Unemployment Hits 10%, Highest Rate Since 1983
    9. Obama Awarded Nobel Peace Prize; White House Calls Itself Humbled and Surprised
    10. Army Psychiatrist Kills 13 at Fort Hood in Deadliest Attack on a US Military Base

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryBarack Obama was sworn in as the 44th president and first African American to hold the office on January 20 before nearly two million people on the National Mall, a jolt of hope arriving just as Circuit City liquidated and the economy contracted at its steepest pace of the recession. Days earlier, on January 15, Captain Chesley Sullenberger safely ditched US Airways Flight 1549 in the Hudson River, and on January 3 the Bitcoin network's genesis block was mined in near-total obscurity; Obama signed the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act on January 29, his first bill as president, the same week Illinois's state senate removed Governor Rod Blagojevich from office.
    FebruaryObama signed the $787 billion American Recovery and Reinvestment Act on February 17 with support from only three Senate Republicans, and two days later CNBC's Rick Santelli delivered the on-air rant against mortgage relief that would give rise to the Tea Party movement. Treasury unveiled its Financial Stability Plan and stress-test framework for the banking system as stocks continued sliding toward their eventual March low.
    MarchThe S&P 500 closed at a generational low of 676.53 on March 9, down 57 percent from its 2007 peak, before Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke's March 18 announcement of large-scale Treasury and mortgage-bond purchases ignited a powerful market reversal. The AIG bonus scandal erupted after revelations the insurer had paid $165 million in retention bonuses despite $170 billion in federal aid, Bernie Madoff pleaded guilty to running history's largest Ponzi scheme, and Obama lifted Bush-era restrictions on federal funding of embryonic stem-cell research.
    AprilChrysler entered a government-supervised bankruptcy on April 30, days after Pennsylvania senator Arlen Specter switched parties to become a Democrat. Navy SEAL snipers rescued Captain Richard Phillips from Somali pirates who had hijacked the Maersk Alabama, Iowa's supreme court and the Vermont legislature legalized same-sex marriage, and Obama delivered a Prague speech calling for a world without nuclear weapons.
    MayThe May 7 bank stress-test results, requiring ten of nineteen major banks to raise roughly $75 billion in new capital, became the psychological turning point for confidence in the financial system. Obama nominated Sonia Sotomayor to the Supreme Court on May 26, North Korea conducted its second nuclear test, and Kansas abortion provider George Tiller was murdered at his church on May 31.
    JuneGeneral Motors filed for bankruptcy on June 1 and emerged five weeks later with the US Treasury holding 60 percent of its equity, the same period the World Health Organization declared H1N1 influenza a global pandemic. Michael Jackson died suddenly on June 25 alongside Farrah Fawcett, triggering a saturation news event; Bernie Madoff was sentenced to 150 years in prison on June 29, and the House narrowly passed the Waxman-Markey cap-and-trade bill, which would later die in the Senate.
    JulyAl Franken was finally seated as Minnesota's senator on July 7 after an eight-month recount battle, giving Democrats a fleeting 60-vote Senate supermajority. The Cash for Clunkers auto-incentive program launched and quickly exhausted its funding, and the July 16 arrest of Harvard scholar Henry Louis Gates at his own home prompted Obama's acted stupidly remark and the subsequent White House Beer Summit.
    AugustSonia Sotomayor was confirmed to the Supreme Court on August 6 as the first Latina justice, and August recess town halls on health-care reform turned openly hostile, fueled by talk of death panels and rising Tea Party energy. Ben Bernanke was reappointed Fed chairman and Senator Ted Kennedy died of brain cancer on August 25, ending the window for a Senate supermajority.
    SeptemberA September 12 Taxpayer March on Washington drew a large Tea Party crowd, and Congressman Joe Wilson shouted You lie! at Obama during a September 9 address to Congress on health care. The Kanye West-Taylor Swift VMA incident on September 13 became one of the internet's first viral celebrity moments, and Obama disclosed Iran's secret Fordow enrichment site at the G-20 summit in Pittsburgh.
    OctoberUnemployment breached 10 percent in October for the first time since the early 1980s, and Obama's October 9 Nobel Peace Prize was greeted with widespread public skepticism even from supporters. The Balloon Boy hoax briefly transfixed cable news on October 15, and the Matthew Shepard and James Byrd Jr. Hate Crimes Prevention Act was signed into law on October 28.
    NovemberThe House passed its health-care reform bill by a narrow 220-215 vote on November 7, and Republicans won governorships in Virginia and New Jersey on November 3 in results widely read as a warning to Democrats. Army psychiatrist Nidal Hasan killed 13 people at Fort Hood on November 5, the Motorola Droid launched on Verizon giving Android its breakout moment, and Tiger Woods's Thanksgiving-weekend car crash set off the scandal that would consume his career.
    DecemberObama announced a 30,000-troop surge in Afghanistan from West Point on December 1, then traveled to Oslo to accept his Nobel Peace Prize on December 10 by defending the justness of that same war. The Copenhagen climate summit ended in disappointment on December 18, the same day Avatar opened in theaters, the Senate passed its health-care bill in a rare Christmas Eve cloture vote, and a failed Christmas Day bombing attempt aboard a Detroit-bound flight closed out the year on an ominous note.

    Median home priceMedian existing-home sale price of approximately $173,000, down from about $198,000 in 2008
    National debtGross federal debt of approximately $12.3 trillion at FY2009 end (September 30); FY2009 deficit of $1.41 trillion, about 9.8% of GDP

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2009 was to live inside a strange, disorienting split-screen. On one channel ran the raw economic pain of the worst recession since the Great Depression: friends and neighbors losing jobs at a rate of hundreds of thousands a month, unemployment climbing past 10 percent by October, houses underwater or in foreclosure, and a stock market that had cut nearly 60 percent of its value at the March low. On the other channel ran genuine hope: a young, historic new president sworn in before an ocean of people on the National Mall, promising a new era of responsibility, and a Congress and White House moving with real urgency, a $787 billion stimulus within a month of inauguration, bank rescues, and an audacious push for near-universal health coverage that would occupy the entire second half of the year.

    For most people, the recession was the year's lived reality. Layoffs, pay freezes, and a sense of economic vertigo touched nearly every household, even as economists debated whether the trough had technically passed by summer. The relentless drumbeat of bad unemployment numbers coexisted uneasily with a roaring stock-market recovery that felt, to most ordinary Americans without large equity holdings, like a rescue for Wall Street that had not yet reached Main Street. Anger at that disconnect, at AIG's bonuses, at bailed-out banks repaying government loans within months while foreclosures kept rising, curdled by summer into the Tea Party's town-hall confrontations, a genuinely new and confrontational note in American political life.

    Yet 2009 was not purely grim. It was, almost paradoxically, one of the richer years in recent popular culture: Lady Gaga's arrival, a genuine golden age of prestige television running alongside broadcast comfort food, and Avatar's December opening delivering the kind of communal big-screen event Hollywood had been chasing for years. Twitter and smartphones were quietly rewiring how news and gossip moved, giving events from Susan Boyle's audition to the Iranian protests a virality that had not existed a year earlier.

    By December, the emotional arc had bent downward from January's euphoria. Unemployment was still near 10 percent, health-care reform was mired in Senate procedure, Afghanistan was escalating rather than winding down, and the year's grief, from Michael Jackson to Fort Hood to the flu pandemic, had taken its toll. Most Americans could sense, without yet having the vocabulary for it, that they were living through a hinge moment: the last full year of the pre-smartphone, pre-social-media decade, and the first full year of an economic and political order that would take most of the following decade to fully reveal itself.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2010
    Econ +1Mark +2Poli -1Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The United States in 2010 was a nation still climbing out of the wreckage of the Great Recession, and it climbed with visible effort. The economy grew again, roughly 2.5 percent for the year, but unemployment stayed above nine percent from January through December, and nearly half of the jobless had been out of work for more than six months. Washington produced two of the most consequential pieces of legislation in a generation, the Affordable Care Act in March and the Dodd-Frank financial reform act in July, but did so amid ferocious partisan combat, and voters answered in November with the largest midterm swing since 1948, handing House Republicans 63 net seats and ending the Democratic trifecta that had governed since 2009.

    Wall Street told a different story from Main Street. The S&P 500 returned better than 15 percent, gold and small-cap stocks soared, and Apple's iPad, launched in April, minted a new consumer-technology category even as it helped push Apple past Microsoft to become the world's most valuable technology company. Yet housing kept falling, foreclosures hit a record, and the autumn foreclosure-paperwork scandal exposed how unresolved the mortgage crisis truly remained; a one-day market plunge in May, in which the Dow fell and then clawed back nearly 1,000 points within minutes, offered a visceral reminder of how fragile market plumbing had become. The BP Deepwater Horizon blowout, meanwhile, fouled the Gulf of Mexico for eighty-seven days and became the year's dominant televised anxiety, even as its long-run consequences would prove far smaller than contemporaries feared.

    Beneath the headline fights, 2010 quietly planted almost every seed of the decade to come. Instagram launched in October as an obscure photo app; Citizens United rewired campaign finance in January; the Tea Party's midterm triumph began the Republican Party's populist turn; the Stuxnet computer worm was discovered, inaugurating an era of state-on-state cyberwarfare; and on December 17, a Tunisian fruit vendor named Mohamed Bouazizi set himself on fire, an act almost unnoticed in the United States that would ignite the Arab Spring within weeks. None of this was legible at the time. To live through 2010 as an American was to feel exhausted by gridlock, anxious about jobs and oil-soaked pelicans, and cautiously hopeful about a slow-motion recovery, not to sense that the year was quietly rewriting the twenty-first century.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Growth resumed and was reasonably broad-based, but unemployment above nine percent all year, a still-falling housing market, and a record wave of foreclosures kept the recovery well short of what the depth of the 2008-09 collapse should have produced.
    Markets+2Double-digit total returns across the S&P 500, Dow, and Nasdaq, sharp small-cap and commodity gains, and an increasingly reflationary Fed made 2010 a strongly positive year for investors despite the May flash crash and a summer eurozone-driven swoon.
    Politics-1Landmark legislation including the Affordable Care Act, Dodd-Frank, New START, and the repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell coexisted with record polarization, a rebuked Supreme Court ruling on campaign finance, and a historic midterm wave that ended unified Democratic government.
    Culture+1A genuinely strong creative year across film, television, and music was offset by a grinding jobless recovery, the Gulf oil spill, and bitter fights over immigration and religion that kept public life tense.
    Overall Mood+0Investors, legislators, and culture-makers all had genuinely good years, but the typical American experienced 2010 through stagnant employment, a still-collapsing housing market, and exhausting political rancor, netting out close to neutral.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. President Obama signs the Affordable Care Act into law after a yearlong legislative battle (March 23).
    2. The BP Deepwater Horizon rig explodes, unleashing the largest oil spill in U.S. history in the Gulf of Mexico (April 20-July 15).
    3. Congress passes the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (July 21).
    4. Republicans gain 63 House seats in a historic midterm wave, the largest swing since 1948 (November 2).
    5. The Supreme Court's Citizens United v. FEC ruling strikes down limits on corporate political spending (January 21).
    6. Apple unveils and ships the iPad, creating the modern tablet computing category (January-April).
    7. Congress repeals Don't Ask, Don't Tell, ending the ban on openly gay military service (December 22).
    8. The Senate ratifies the New START arms-control treaty with Russia (December 22).
    9. WikiLeaks releases hundreds of thousands of classified documents, including the Cablegate diplomatic cache (July-November).
    10. A 7.0-magnitude earthquake devastates Haiti, killing well over 100,000 people and triggering a massive American relief effort (January 12).

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy in 2010 was in the early, uneven stage of recovery from the Great Recession. Real GDP grew roughly 2.5 percent for the year, powered early on by inventory restocking and the tail of the 2009 stimulus, before decelerating sharply at midyear amid fears of a double dip fed by the European sovereign debt crisis, then reaccelerating in the fourth quarter after the Federal Reserve's second round of quantitative easing and a December deal extending the Bush-era tax cuts. Inflation was unusually subdued, with core CPI falling to its lowest reading since the 1960s, while unemployment remained the year's central wound, opening January at 9.8 percent and closing December still above 9 percent, with long-term joblessness at historic highs.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP rose approximately 2.5 percent for the year, later revised toward 2.7 percent; unemployment ran from 9.8 percent in January to roughly 9.3-9.4 percent in December, averaging near 9.6 percent; headline CPI inflation ended the year around 1.5 percent, with core inflation near 0.8 percent, the lowest since the 1960s; the federal deficit reached approximately $1.29 trillion, about 8.7 percent of GDP, pushing gross federal debt to roughly $14.0 trillion by year-end; existing home sales totaled about 4.91 million units, the weakest since 1997, while median existing-home prices held near $172,900; and a record 2.9 million properties received foreclosure filings even as 30-year mortgage rates fell to generational lows, averaging near 4.69 percent for the year.

    Major Events

    The year's defining economic events were legislative and institutional as much as cyclical: the signing of the Affordable Care Act in March, the most sweeping healthcare overhaul since Medicare; passage of the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform Act in July, which created the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Volcker Rule; the eruption of the European sovereign debt crisis in spring, with bailouts for Greece in May and Ireland in November; the Federal Reserve's launch of a second quantitative-easing program in November, committing $600 billion to Treasury purchases to fight deflation risk; the Deepwater Horizon blowout in the Gulf of Mexico in April, which killed eleven workers and triggered a deepwater drilling moratorium; the autumn foreclosure-paperwork scandal, which froze foreclosure processing at major banks and prompted a fifty-state investigation; the expiration of the federal homebuyer tax credit at the end of April, which pulled housing demand forward and produced a weak summer for sales; and the December compromise extending the Bush-era tax cuts for two years alongside a payroll tax cut and extended unemployment benefits.

    Why this score

    A score of +1 reflects genuine but incomplete healing: growth resumed and was reasonably broad-based, corporate profits recovered strongly, and financial markets thrived, but persistent unemployment above 9 percent, sub-1-percent core inflation, a still-falling housing market, and a fresh foreclosure-documentation scandal meant the recovery remained well short of what the depth of the 2008-09 collapse should have produced.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities delivered strong double-digit gains despite a turbulent middle third of the year: the S&P 500 returned about 15.1 percent including dividends, closing at 1,257.64; the Dow Jones Industrial Average returned about 14.1 percent, closing at 11,577.51; and the NASDAQ Composite returned about 17.8 percent, closing at 2,652.87, led by Apple, Amazon, Netflix, and Priceline. The path was V-shaped: markets rose in the first quarter, fell roughly 16 percent peak-to-trough into early July amid the European debt crisis and double-dip fears, and then rallied powerfully from September through year-end once the Fed's new bond-buying program was telegraphed and delivered. Small-caps outperformed sharply, with the Russell 2000 returning about 26.9 percent, and volatility remained elevated for a positive year, with the VIX spiking above 45 intraday during May's flash crash.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    Apple was the year's defining company, crossing $300 billion in market capitalization and passing Microsoft to become the world's most valuable technology company on the strength of the iPad and iPhone 4. General Motors returned to public markets in November in what was then the largest IPO in U.S. history, validating the 2009 TARP-era auto rescue, while Tesla's June IPO marked the first American automaker listing since Ford in 1956. Financial giants including JPMorgan Chase, Goldman Sachs, which paid a $550 million SEC settlement over its Abacus CDO, Bank of America, and Citigroup remained profitable but under heavy regulatory and legal pressure, and the Treasury profitably exited its Citigroup stake in December. Notable deals included Intel's acquisition of McAfee and HP's bidding-war purchase of 3Par, while Groupon turned down a reported $6 billion Google buyout offer and both Blockbuster and MGM filed for bankruptcy.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant investment theme was reflation: anticipation and then delivery of the Fed's second quantitative-easing program drove a broad risk-asset rally from late summer onward, compressing real interest rates and lifting stocks, gold, and industrial commodities together. Gold closed the year up about 29.5 percent to fresh nominal highs on real-rate compression, dollar anxiety, and central-bank buying, while oil rose about 15 percent and silver nearly doubled. Small-cap and technology stocks led the broader market, and the emergence of the iPad-driven mobile computing category, alongside high-profile IPOs like GM and Tesla, made growth and recovery narratives the year's most rewarded investment themes.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The year's central political events were the passage of the Affordable Care Act in March after Democrats used budget reconciliation to work around the loss of their Senate supermajority; passage of the Dodd-Frank financial reform act in July; the Citizens United v. FEC ruling in January, which struck down limits on corporate political spending and drew a rare presidential rebuke during the State of the Union address; the November midterm elections, in which Republicans gained 63 House seats, the largest swing since 1948; the repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell and ratification of the New START arms treaty with Russia, both accomplished in the December lame-duck session; and the WikiLeaks releases of the Afghan and Iraq war logs and the Cablegate diplomatic cables, which embarrassed American diplomacy and led to the arrest of Army Private Bradley Manning.

    Domestic Policy

    Obama began 2010 with a Democratic trifecta but lost his 60-seat Senate supermajority when Republican Scott Brown won the Massachusetts special election in January, forcing Democrats to pass health reform's final piece through reconciliation. The resulting Affordable Care Act created an individual mandate, insurance exchanges, a Medicaid expansion, a ban on pre-existing-condition exclusions, and a provision letting young adults stay on parental insurance until age 26. Dodd-Frank followed in July, creating the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Volcker Rule after intense industry lobbying. Sustained unemployment near 9.6 percent and the looming expiration of the Bush tax cuts drove a bitter year-end negotiation, resolved by a lame-duck deal extending all tax rates for two years in exchange for extended unemployment insurance and a payroll tax cut, a compromise that infuriated many congressional liberals. Other significant actions included the Fair Sentencing Act narrowing the crack-powder cocaine sentencing disparity, the Food Safety Modernization Act, and Elena Kagan's confirmation to the Supreme Court following Justice Stevens's retirement; the DREAM Act, by contrast, passed the House but failed in the Senate, and Arizona's SB 1070 immigration law drew a federal lawsuit and partial injunction.

    Foreign Policy

    Afghanistan absorbed the bulk of foreign-policy attention as the 30,000-troop surge Obama ordered in December 2009 reached full deployment, pushing U.S. troop levels toward 100,000 and producing the war's deadliest year for American forces; General David Petraeus took command in July after General Stanley McChrystal was fired over disparaging remarks published in Rolling Stone. In Iraq, combat operations formally ended August 31 under the renamed Operation New Dawn as troop levels fell below 50,000. The administration's signature diplomatic achievement was the New START treaty with Russia, signed in April and ratified by the Senate in December, capping deployed strategic warheads and capping off a broader reset with Moscow that also yielded Russian cooperation on Iran sanctions. Tensions rose with China over currency policy, Google's withdrawal from the mainland, and the South China Sea, while North Korea's sinking of the Cheonan and shelling of Yeonpyeong Island produced a serious peninsula crisis. The United States led a major relief effort after the January Haiti earthquake, and a September relaunch of Israeli-Palestinian talks collapsed within weeks over the settlement-moratorium dispute.

    Important Elections

    Scott Brown's stunning January victory in the Massachusetts Senate special election, filling the seat held by Edward Kennedy for nearly half a century, cost Democrats their filibuster-proof Senate majority and reshaped the health-reform endgame. Tea Party insurgents upended a string of Republican Senate primaries, with Rand Paul in Kentucky, Marco Rubio in Florida, Christine O'Donnell in Delaware, Sharron Angle in Nevada, Mike Lee in Utah, and Joe Miller's primary defeat of incumbent Lisa Murkowski in Alaska, who then won on a historic write-in campaign. The November 2 general election delivered Republicans a net 63 House seats, the largest swing since 1948, and six Senate seats without flipping the chamber; Republicans also netted six governorships, including Wisconsin's Scott Walker, Ohio's John Kasich, and New Mexico's Susana Martinez, the nation's first Latina governor, while Democrats held California, New York, and Hawaii. Coming in a decennial redistricting year, the Republican wave at the state legislative level would shape congressional maps for the following decade.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Kathryn Bigelow's The Hurt Locker won Best Picture at the March Oscars, making Bigelow the first woman to win Best Director, but the year's commercial and cultural axis ran through Toy Story 3, the first animated film to gross over a billion dollars worldwide, and Christopher Nolan's Inception, an original blockbuster that dominated conversation about dream logic and ambiguous endings. David Fincher's The Social Network offered a defining, Aaron Sorkin-scripted portrait of the Zuckerberg era, while Darren Aronofsky's Black Swan, the Coen brothers' True Grit, David O. Russell's The Fighter, and Debra Granik's Winter's Bone, which introduced Jennifer Lawrence, rounded out an unusually strong year for adult-oriented mainstream cinema, even as post-converted 3D releases like Clash of the Titans drew backlash.

    Music

    Kesha's TiK ToK topped Billboard's year-end Hot 100 in a year crowded with pop and dance-pop hits, including Train's Hey, Soul Sister, Katy Perry's California Gurls and Firework, Eminem and Rihanna's Love the Way You Lie, and Cee Lo Green's Forget You. On albums, Kanye West's My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy, released in November, was widely hailed as the year's, and eventually the decade's, creative high-water mark, while Eminem's Recovery was the year's best-selling album worldwide, Taylor Swift's Speak Now moved a million copies in its first week, and Arcade Fire's The Suburbs won an upset Grammy for Album of the Year. Electronic dance music began crossing decisively into the pop mainstream through David Guetta and Deadmau5, and country crossover surged behind Lady Antebellum's Need You Now.

    Television

    Cable prestige television reached an inflection point, with AMC's Mad Men and Breaking Bad both building critical acclaim, AMC's Halloween debut of The Walking Dead proving cable could deliver blockbuster genre television, and HBO launching Boardwalk Empire with a Scorsese-directed pilot. Broadcast television still commanded the largest audiences, led by American Idol's ninth season, Simon Cowell's last, and NBC's Sunday Night Football, while Modern Family won its first Best Comedy Emmy and Glee remained at its cultural peak. Late-night television saw resolution of its 2009-10 succession fight as Conan O'Brien departed The Tonight Show in January and relaunched on TBS in November, with Jay Leno returning to NBC's 11:35 slot in March.

    Technology

    Apple's iPad, unveiled in January and shipped in April, created the modern tablet category and sold roughly 15 million units by year-end, while the iPhone 4's June launch brought a Retina display and FaceTime alongside a reception controversy nicknamed Antennagate. Instagram launched quietly in October, Facebook crossed 500 million users in July, making Mark Zuckerberg Time's Person of the Year, and Android overtook the iPhone in U.S. smartphone activations by year-end behind devices like the Samsung Galaxy S. Microsoft's Kinect sensor for Xbox 360 became the fastest-selling consumer electronics device on record, Verizon launched commercial 4G LTE service in December, and Groupon, Foursquare, and the newly launched Pinterest defined the year's startup zeitgeist.

    Social Trends

    The Tea Party movement, born in 2009 reaction to TARP and the stimulus, consolidated into a durable electoral force that reshaped the Republican Party through 2010's primaries and midterms. Arizona's SB 1070 immigration enforcement law ignited national protests and boycotts, the Park51 community center controversy near Ground Zero and Pastor Terry Jones's threatened Quran burning made religious tolerance a summer-long flashpoint, and Pew's continuing documentation of the rise of religiously unaffiliated Americans pointed to a longer-run sociological shift. A wave of publicized LGBT teen suicides, including Rutgers student Tyler Clementi, spurred the viral It Gets Better Project and built momentum toward the December repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell.

    Crime

    Faisal Shahzad's failed Times Square car bombing on May 1 briefly raised fears of a successful domestic terror attack, while the arrest of ten Russian sleeper agents in June, including Anna Chapman, resolved through a Cold War-style prisoner swap, became a strange, almost nostalgic espionage story. Amy Bishop's February shooting of three colleagues at a University of Alabama Huntsville faculty meeting shocked academia, and the fall's foreclosure-paperwork scandal, while a civil and regulatory matter rather than a criminal one, exposed systemic fraud in mortgage-servicing documentation across the industry.

    Sports

    The New Orleans Saints' Super Bowl XLIV win over the Colts in February provided a cathartic post-Katrina moment for the city, while the San Francisco Giants won their first World Series since moving from New York and the Chicago Blackhawks ended a 49-year Stanley Cup drought. The Lakers beat the Celtics for Kobe Bryant's fifth championship, the Vancouver Winter Olympics saw the U.S. lead the medal count while Canada won gold on Sidney Crosby's overtime goal against Team USA in hockey, and Landon Donovan's stoppage-time World Cup goal against Algeria became a landmark moment for American soccer. LeBron James's televised Decision to leave Cleveland for Miami reshaped NBA free agency and turned him into a villain for much of the sports-watching public.

    Public Mood

    The prevailing public mood was anxious, exhausted, and polarized: unemployment near 9.6 percent all year, an unrelenting housing crisis, and the summer-long oil spill made ordinary life feel governmentally overwhelmed, and commentators wrote openly of an age of anger as Glenn Beck's August Restoring Honor rally and Jon Stewart and Stephen Colbert's answering Rally to Restore Sanity dramatized a country arguing with itself in real time on cable news and the still-new medium of Twitter. Yet the same year produced an unusually strong run of pop-cultural achievement, and the It Gets Better movement and the December repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell allowed 2010 to close on a genuine note of social progress.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary Americans believed 2010 was defined by the Gulf oil spill, the healthcare fight and its electoral backlash, a fragile jobless recovery, and the Tea Party as an anti-Obama protest movement. Most assumed the iPad might well flop, dismissed WikiLeaks as a fringe curiosity, saw Instagram as one photo app among dozens, read Citizens United as a technical First Amendment ruling, and took the Arab world's autocracies for stable fixtures of the map.

    What actually mattered

    What actually mattered ran nearly opposite to contemporary perception: the Affordable Care Act became the durable center of American health policy despite feeling politically catastrophic at the time; the Tea Party proved to be the leading edge of a full Republican realignment rather than an ephemeral protest; Citizens United rewired campaign finance for a generation; the iPad established mobile computing as a mainstream paradigm; Instagram and Facebook's climb past 500 million users marked the true beginning of algorithmic social media's grip on culture and politics; the discovery of Stuxnet inaugurated an era of state-on-state cyberwarfare; and Mohamed Bouazizi's death in Tunisia in December set the entire Arab world on a course toward revolution and civil war.

    Most forgotten story

    The rescue of the 33 Chilean miners in October, watched live by an estimated one billion people over 22 hours as each man was hauled to the surface in the Fenix capsule, was one of the most-watched media events of the year and yet left no policy legacy, no geopolitical consequence, and almost no trace in serious retrospectives of the decade, pure spectacle without downstream effect.

    Most underrated story

    Mohamed Bouazizi's self-immolation in Sidi Bouzid, Tunisia, on December 17 received almost no American front-page coverage, yet within weeks it ignited the Tunisian revolution and the broader Arab Spring, a cascade that would topple governments across the Middle East, spark the Syrian civil war, redraw European migration politics, and give rise to and eventually destroy ISIS, arguably the single most consequential event of 2010, and the one Americans were least aware of as it happened.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The most striking surprise in hindsight is how completely the BP oil spill has faded from political and cultural memory. In the summer of 2010, serious commentators compared Deepwater Horizon to Chernobyl and predicted it would define the Obama presidency and permanently transform the Gulf economy; instead, the environmental recovery outpaced expectations, the drilling moratorium proved short-lived, BP returned to profitability, and the episode now occupies a surprisingly small place in the story of the decade.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The BP Deepwater Horizon oil spillThe environmental recovery outpaced dire predictions, the deepwater drilling moratorium was short-lived, BP returned to profitability, and the episode plays a surprisingly minor role in the decade's political narrative despite being the most-covered domestic story of the year.
    The rescue of the 33 Chilean minersA genuine billion-viewer global media event, it produced no policy change and no lasting consequence, and now survives mainly as a feel-good footnote and a Hollywood film that flopped at the box office.
    The Park51 community center controversy near Ground ZeroThe project was eventually built as a much smaller condominium development, and the controversy is remembered mainly as an early symptom of the Islamophobic politics of the following decade rather than as a consequential event in its own right.
    The Icelandic volcano Eyjafjallajokull's eruptionIt grounded European aviation for six days and prompted predictions of a rethinking of air-travel vulnerability, but within months it was forgotten outside Iceland with no lasting policy effect.
    Toyota's sudden unintended acceleration recallSubsequent NHTSA and NASA investigations largely exonerated Toyota's electronics, traced most incidents to floor mats and driver error, and the company's market share and reputation fully recovered.
    General Stanley McChrystal's firing over the Rolling Stone profileIt felt like a genuine civil-military crisis in June, but Petraeus's seamless transition and the unchanged Afghanistan strategy reduced the episode to a footnote about journalistic access.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Mohamed Bouazizi's self-immolation in Tunisia on December 17It ignited the Tunisian revolution within weeks and the broader Arab Spring, toppling regimes across the Middle East and reshaping American foreign policy for more than a decade.
    The launch of Instagram on October 6A minor tech-blog story at the time, it was acquired by Facebook within 18 months and became a central engine of influencer culture, algorithmic feeds, and youth social-media life for the following decade.
    The discovery of the Stuxnet computer wormInitially covered as a technical curiosity, it was later revealed as a U.S.-Israeli cyberweapon that physically destroyed Iranian centrifuges, marking the birth of state-on-state cyberwarfare.
    The first commercial Bitcoin transaction, later known as Bitcoin Pizza Day, on May 22Laszlo Hanyecz's purchase of two pizzas for 10,000 bitcoin received zero mainstream coverage but marked cryptocurrency's transition from cypherpunk experiment to a multi-trillion-dollar asset class.
    The foreclosure robo-signing scandalCovered mainly as a business-page story about processing irregularities, it exposed how unresolved the mortgage crisis remained and fed directly into the following year's Occupy Wall Street movement and the broader delegitimization of major banks.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Obama Signs Landmark Health Care Bill Into Law
    2. Gulf Oil Disaster: BP Struggles for Months to Cap Ruptured Well
    3. Tea Party Wave Sweeps Republicans Back Into House Majority
    4. Supreme Court Clears Way for Unlimited Corporate Campaign Spending
    5. Apple's iPad Debuts to Long Lines and Skeptical Reviews
    6. Dow Plunges Nearly 1,000 Points in Minutes, Then Mostly Recovers
    7. Fed Launches $600 Billion Bond-Buying Program to Fight Stagnation
    8. All 33 Chilean Miners Reach the Surface After 69 Days Underground
    9. WikiLeaks' Cablegate Dump Embarrasses U.S. Diplomacy Worldwide
    10. Congress Ends Don't Ask, Don't Tell in Lame-Duck Session

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryA 7.0-magnitude earthquake devastated Port-au-Prince, Haiti, on January 12, killing well over 100,000 people and prompting a massive American relief effort under former presidents Bush and Clinton; Republican Scott Brown's stunning January 19 upset in the Massachusetts Senate special election cost Democrats their filibuster-proof Senate majority, and two days later the Supreme Court's 5-4 Citizens United v. FEC ruling struck down limits on corporate political spending. Steve Jobs unveiled the iPad on January 27, and Conan O'Brien's contentious departure from The Tonight Show concluded the same week.
    FebruaryThe Toyota sudden unintended acceleration recall dominated business news as CEO Akio Toyoda testified before Congress on February 24, even as investigators would later trace most incidents to floor mats and driver error. The New Orleans Saints won Super Bowl XLIV over Indianapolis on February 7 in a cathartic post-Katrina moment, the Vancouver Winter Olympics opened on February 12, and a University of Alabama Huntsville professor, Amy Bishop, shot and killed three colleagues at a faculty meeting.
    MarchPresident Obama signed the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act on March 23, followed a week later by a reconciliation package of fixes, completing the most sweeping health-care overhaul since Medicare after a bruising yearlong legislative fight. Kathryn Bigelow's The Hurt Locker won Best Picture at the March 7 Oscars, making her the first woman to win Best Director, while North Korea's sinking of the South Korean warship Cheonan on March 26 set off a serious crisis on the Korean peninsula.
    AprilThe BP Deepwater Horizon rig exploded on April 20, killing eleven workers and beginning an 87-day oil spill that would dominate the summer's news cycle; Apple's iPad shipped on April 3 to long lines and eventual runaway sales. Justice John Paul Stevens announced his Supreme Court retirement, President Obama signed the New START arms treaty with Russia in Prague on April 8, the Nuclear Security Summit convened 47 world leaders in Washington, and Arizona Governor Jan Brewer signed the controversial SB 1070 immigration law on April 23 as the Icelandic volcano Eyjafjallajokull grounded European air travel for days.
    MayFaisal Shahzad's failed Times Square car bombing on May 1 briefly raised fears of a successful domestic terror attack, and Elena Kagan was nominated to replace the retiring Justice Stevens. Wall Street was jolted on May 6 by a flash crash in which the Dow plunged nearly 1,000 points in minutes before recovering most of the loss the same session, while BP's repeated failed attempts to cap its ruptured well kept the Gulf spill as the summer's central anxiety.
    JuneGeneral Stanley McChrystal was fired after a Rolling Stone profile revealed disparaging comments by him and his staff, clearing the way for General David Petraeus to take Afghanistan command in July; ten Russian sleeper agents, including Anna Chapman, were arrested and later swapped in a Cold War-style prisoner exchange. Apple's iPhone 4 launched June 24 to strong sales but also antenna-reception complaints, Tesla went public on June 29 in the first American automaker IPO since 1956, and antivirus researchers quietly discovered the Stuxnet worm, later revealed as a state-sponsored cyberweapon against Iran's nuclear program.
    JulyCongress passed the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act, which President Obama signed on July 21, creating the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and the Volcker Rule in the most sweeping financial-regulation overhaul since the New Deal. BP finally capped its ruptured well on July 15 after 87 days of spillage, Goldman Sachs paid a $550 million SEC settlement over its Abacus CDO, and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton's Hanoi speech asserting a U.S. interest in the South China Sea signaled the coming pivot to Asia.
    AugustU.S. combat operations in Iraq formally ended August 31 as Operation Iraqi Freedom was renamed Operation New Dawn and troop levels fell below 50,000; the Senate confirmed Elena Kagan to the Supreme Court, 63-37, giving the Court three female justices for the first time. The Park51 controversy near Ground Zero and Pastor Terry Jones's threatened Quran burning consumed national attention, Glenn Beck's Restoring Honor rally drew large crowds to the Lincoln Memorial on August 28, and Fed Chairman Ben Bernanke's Jackson Hole speech telegraphed a second round of quantitative easing.
    SeptemberAMC premiered Boardwalk Empire as the fall television season showcased a maturing prestige-TV landscape, while Rutgers student Tyler Clementi's suicide after an invasion of his privacy helped spur the viral It Gets Better Project addressing LGBT teen bullying. Tea Party insurgent Christine O'Donnell upset establishment favorite Mike Castle in Delaware's Senate primary, Congress passed the Small Business Jobs Act, and the housing market's summer swoon deepened as the effects of the expired homebuyer tax credit fully registered in sales data.
    OctoberInstagram launched quietly on October 6 as a niche photo-sharing app, while an estimated one billion people worldwide watched live as all 33 trapped Chilean miners were rescued from the San Jose mine on October 12-13. AMC's The Walking Dead premiered on Halloween to strong ratings, Jon Stewart and Stephen Colbert's Rally to Restore Sanity and/or Fear drew roughly 215,000 people to the National Mall on October 30, and the foreclosure robo-signing scandal broke, prompting major loan servicers to freeze foreclosure processing.
    NovemberRepublicans gained a net 63 House seats in the November 2 midterm elections, the largest partisan swing since 1948, while Democrats retained a narrower Senate majority; Republicans also netted six governorships nationwide. General Motors returned to public markets on November 17 in the largest IPO in U.S. history, the Federal Reserve launched its $600 billion bond-buying program on November 3, North Korea shelled Yeonpyeong Island, and WikiLeaks began releasing its Cablegate trove of diplomatic cables on November 28.
    DecemberIn the lame-duck session, Congress repealed Don't Ask, Don't Tell and the Senate ratified the New START treaty, both on December 22, while a broader tax deal extended the Bush-era tax cuts for two years alongside extended unemployment benefits and a payroll tax cut. Kanye West released My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy to widespread acclaim, the Census Bureau reported a U.S. population of 308,745,538, and on December 17, largely unnoticed by American media, a Tunisian fruit vendor named Mohamed Bouazizi set himself on fire, setting in motion the Arab Spring.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~2.5% real GDP growth for 2010 (later BEA revisions ~2.7%)
    InflationCPI ~1.5% headline / ~0.8% core at year-end (y/y), the lowest core reading since the 1960s
    Unemployment9.8% in January falling to ~9.3-9.4% in December; averaged roughly 9.6% for the year
    S&P 500 return+15.1% total return (price +12.8%); closed at 1,257.64 vs. 1,115.10 at end-2009
    Dow return+14.1% total return (price +11.0%); closed at 11,577.51
    Fed funds rate0-0.25% target range, unchanged all year
    Oil priceWTI crude closed near $91.38/barrel, up roughly 15% for the year
    Gold priceGold closed near $1,421/oz, up roughly 29.5% for the year to fresh nominal highs
    Population308,745,538 (2010 Census, April 1 reference date), up 9.7% from 2000
    Median home price~$172,900 median existing single-family home price (NAR, 2010 calendar-year average)
    National debt~$14.0 trillion gross federal debt at year-end (deficit ~$1.29 trillion, ~8.7% of GDP)

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2010 was to feel the strange dissonance of a recovery that showed up in the statistics before it showed up in daily life. The stock market, gold, and corporate profits all had genuinely good years; the iPad arrived to long lines and would soon be in a fifth of American households; and yet more than nine in every hundred workers who wanted a job could not find one for the entire year, and nearly half of them had been searching for over six months. That gap between an economy healing on paper and a labor market that felt frozen defined the texture of the year for most households, a persistent, low-grade anxiety rather than the acute panic of 2008-09, but anxiety all the same, compounded by a housing market still falling and a foreclosure crisis that, if anything, was getting worse.

    Politically, it was a year of exhausting, high-stakes combat that ended in decisive rebuke. The passage of the Affordable Care Act in March felt to its champions like the fulfillment of a decades-long liberal ambition and to its opponents like government overreach on an unprecedented scale; either way, it consumed the nation's attention for months. The summer brought the sight of oil gushing uncapped into the Gulf of Mexico for eighty-seven days, a slow-motion disaster broadcast on a permanent live feed that made the federal government look overmatched by a corporation's mechanical failure. By November, voters had had enough: the 63-seat House swing was the clearest verdict of the year, a repudiation delivered with old-fashioned electoral force even as the country avoided anything like a constitutional crisis.

    And yet, running underneath all of this argument, Americans were quietly building the world of the following decade without quite realizing it. They downloaded a new photo app called Instagram, argued about the ethics of a Supreme Court campaign-finance ruling most understood only vaguely, watched a Tea Party movement they assumed was a temporary tantrum, and paid no attention whatsoever to a Tunisian street vendor's death in mid-December. None of that felt like history in the making; it felt like noise beneath the real story of jobs, oil, and health care.

    What united the contradictions of 2010 was a certain resilience of ordinary life. People still went to the movies for Inception and Toy Story 3, still argued about LeBron James's decision to leave Cleveland for Miami, still watched Breaking Bad build toward greatness on basic cable, and by December could watch Congress end Don't Ask, Don't Tell as proof that progress, however grinding, was still possible. It was not a triumphant year, nor a catastrophic one; it was a tired, contentious, quietly consequential year, one that felt smaller than it actually was.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2011
    Econ -1Mark +0Poli -2Cult +1Over -1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2011 opened with cautious optimism that the U.S. economy was finally shaking off the 2008-09 financial crisis, and closed instead as a year Americans remembered mainly for anxiety, catharsis, and dysfunction in roughly equal measure. On May 2, Navy SEALs killed Osama bin Laden in Abbottabad, Pakistan, producing the single most unifying moment of the post-9/11 decade and closing, at least symbolically, the chapter that began on September 11, 2001. But the bin Laden high proved to be the year's emotional peak rather than its trend line: a summer-long standoff between a newly Republican House and the Obama White House over the federal debt ceiling ended in a Budget Control Act that satisfied no one, and two days later Standard & Poor's stripped the United States of its AAA credit rating for the first time in history, a symbolic wound that shook markets and consumer confidence alike.

    Abroad, the Arab Spring swept from Tunisia through Egypt, Libya, Yemen, and into Syria, toppling Hosni Mubarak and Muammar Gaddafi and inaugurating a Syrian uprising that, largely unnoticed at the time, would become the era's defining humanitarian catastrophe. At home, the year alternated between real tragedy - the Tucson shooting of Rep. Gabrielle Giffords in January, the Joplin, Missouri tornado and an unprecedented tornado season in the spring, a plague of billion-dollar weather disasters - and genuine cultural catharsis, from the royal wedding to Adele's 21 to the debut of Game of Thrones. Occupy Wall Street erupted in September and, even after being cleared from Zuccotti Park in November, permanently inserted the language of 'the 1 percent' into American political discourse. Divided government in Washington produced near-shutdowns, a failed deficit 'Super Committee,' and a general sense, voiced across the political spectrum, that the country's governing institutions were no longer equal to its problems.

    Read now, 2011 looks less like the year of Arab liberation and celebrity meltdowns it appeared to be at the time and more like the year in which the decade's real architecture was quietly assembled: the Syrian civil war's opening act, the normalization of drone killing of American citizens via Anwar al-Awlaki, Vladimir Putin's announced return to the Russian presidency, the debut of Snapchat and Bitcoin's first parity with the dollar, and IBM Watson's Jeopardy! victory as an early public glimpse of the coming machine-learning era. Americans living through 2011 experienced it as an exhausting, cathartic, unsettled year bracketed by a death (bin Laden's) and an ending (the Iraq War) - not yet aware that its truest legacies lay in stories still unfolding at the margins of the front page.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy-1Real GDP grew just 1.6% and unemployment finished the year at a still-elevated 8.5% with real household incomes falling, a subdued year that avoided a double-dip but fell well short of a genuine recovery.
    Markets+0The S&P 500 closed effectively flat for the year (+2.1% total return) after a violent summer swoon of nearly 17%, making 2011 a neutral year on returns but a punishing one on realized volatility.
    Politics-2A first-ever U.S. credit downgrade, a manufactured debt-ceiling crisis, and a failed deficit Super Committee marked one of the most dysfunctional years of divided government in modern memory, offset only partly by genuine foreign-policy successes.
    Culture+1A creatively rich year - Adele's 21, Game of Thrones, Bridesmaids, an all-time World Series game, and Occupy's new moral vocabulary - outperformed the surrounding gloom without reaching landmark-decade status.
    Overall Mood-1Bin Laden's death and moments of unity were real, but debt-ceiling brinkmanship, the credit downgrade, natural disasters, and stubborn unemployment left Americans in a genuinely sour, anxious mood for most of the year.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Osama bin Laden killed by U.S. Navy SEALs in Abbottabad, Pakistan
    2. The Arab Spring topples Mubarak in Egypt and Gaddafi in Libya
    3. Debt-ceiling crisis ends in Budget Control Act; S&P strips U.S. of its AAA credit rating
    4. Tohoku earthquake and tsunami devastate Japan and trigger the Fukushima nuclear crisis
    5. Occupy Wall Street spreads from Zuccotti Park to cities nationwide
    6. Rep. Gabrielle Giffords shot at a Tucson constituent event; six killed
    7. Last U.S. troops leave Iraq, ending nearly nine years of war
    8. Apple co-founder Steve Jobs dies at 56
    9. Joplin, Missouri tornado kills 158 in the nation's deadliest twister in decades
    10. Penn State-Jerry Sandusky scandal ends Joe Paterno's coaching career

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy in 2011 delivered the weakest full year of the post-2008 recovery, growing just 1.6% on a Q4/Q4 basis after economists had entered the year expecting growth above 3%. A near-stall in the first half - first-quarter growth was revised down to barely positive - gave way to a stronger fourth quarter that reassured markets a double-dip recession had been avoided, but the underlying story was one of a fragile expansion buffeted by shocks from abroad, from Japan's earthquake to Europe's sovereign debt crisis. Unemployment fell from 9.4% to 8.5% over the year, a genuine but incomplete improvement clouded by a falling labor-force participation rate that showed much of the decline reflected discouraged workers leaving the labor force rather than robust hiring. Inflation, driven by gasoline and food, spiked toward 4% by September before easing, while housing remained deeply distressed, with prices still falling and roughly a fifth of mortgaged homes underwater.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew 1.6% for the year; unemployment fell from 9.4% to 8.5%, though U-6 underemployment stayed near 15% and labor-force participation slipped to 64.0%. Headline CPI peaked at 3.9% in September and closed the year at 3.0%, with core inflation at 2.2%. Nonfarm payrolls added roughly 2.1 million jobs. The federal deficit ran about $1.30 trillion (8.4% of GDP), and gross federal debt reached roughly $15.2 trillion, essentially 100% of GDP. Real median household income fell to about $50,100, the lowest in real terms since the mid-1990s, and the poverty rate rose to 15.0%, a 17-year high. Existing-home prices fell further to a median around $166,100, and the 30-year mortgage rate averaged a record-low 4.45% for the year.

    Major Events

    The year's defining economic event was the Budget Control Act of August 2, which raised the debt ceiling after a summer-long standoff but only by creating a 'Super Committee' charged with finding further savings - a committee that failed in November, arming the sequestration mechanism that would later bite in 2013. Two days after the deal, Standard & Poor's downgraded U.S. sovereign debt from AAA to AA+ for the first time in history, citing political dysfunction rather than any near-term default risk; markets convulsed even as, paradoxically, Treasury yields fell as investors fled a worsening European crisis. The Federal Reserve, holding rates near zero for a third straight year, completed its second round of quantitative easing in June and then took unprecedented additional steps in the fall - explicit calendar-based forward guidance to mid-2013 and Operation Twist - to support a faltering recovery. Abroad, Japan's March 11 earthquake and tsunami disrupted global supply chains and dented U.S. manufacturing, the Libyan war removed substantial oil supply from the market, and Europe's sovereign debt crisis intensified with bailouts, bond-market panics, and forced changes of government in Italy and Greece.

    Why this score

    A score of -1 reflects a year that avoided outright recession but fell well short of a genuine recovery: growth below trend, unemployment still above 8%, falling real household income, a first-ever national credit downgrade, and consumer confidence briefly touching recession-era lows collectively made 2011 a below-average year even by the subdued standards of the early-2010s recovery.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities finished 2011 nearly exactly where they started - the S&P 500 closed at 1,257.60, essentially flat on price from its 2010 close - masking one of the most volatile years in market history. The index fell nearly 17% peak-to-trough between late July and early October during the debt-ceiling and downgrade panic, with six separate trading days in August posting swings greater than 4%, a frequency unmatched outside 1929, 1987, and 2008, before recovering into year-end. The Dow was the only major index to gain on a price basis (+5.5%, +8.4% total return), while the Nasdaq and small-cap Russell 2000 both finished lower. Breadth was weak, with more than half of S&P constituents down on the year, financials cratering roughly 17% amid a rout in Bank of America and Citigroup, and defensive sectors like utilities and consumer staples leading. Long-dated Treasuries and gold were the year's standout asset classes, with the 10-year yield collapsing from 3.30% to 1.88% and gold notching an eleventh straight annual gain after touching a record above $1,900.

    Character

    Crash-then-recovery

    Major Companies

    Apple surpassed Exxon Mobil as the world's most valuable public company during the summer, even as its co-founder Steve Jobs, having resigned the CEO role in August, died on October 5. The IPO calendar reopened for consumer internet companies, with LinkedIn's May debut, Pandora in June, Groupon in November, and Zynga in December. Bank of America nearly cratered before Warren Buffett's $5 billion preferred-stock investment in August; Hewlett-Packard ousted CEO Leo Apotheker after a disastrous tenure and installed Meg Whitman; Netflix badly miscalculated with a price hike and the aborted 'Qwikster' DVD spinoff, losing roughly 800,000 subscribers and 75% of its stock value. Major bankruptcies included Borders, Solyndra, and MF Global, whose October 31 collapse under Jon Corzine exposed roughly $1.6 billion in missing customer funds. Big deals included Google's $12.5 billion purchase of Motorola Mobility and Microsoft's then-record $8.5 billion acquisition of Skype, while AT&T's proposed $39 billion takeover of T-Mobile USA collapsed in December under antitrust pressure.

    Investment Themes

    Flight to safety dominated: investors piled into long-dated Treasuries and gold even as U.S. sovereign debt was downgraded, a paradox explained mainly by Europe's worsening sovereign crisis making American debt the least-bad option available. Defensive, dividend-paying sectors outperformed cyclicals and financials, which were hammered by European exposure fears and prolonged near-zero rates. The reopening of the technology and social-media IPO window (LinkedIn, Pandora, Groupon, Zynga) previewed the coming decade's platform-company boom, even as commodity volatility - gold's parabolic run and drawdown, silver's spike toward $50 and subsequent collapse, cotton's spike to record highs - reflected a market whipsawed by macro shocks rather than fundamentals.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    2011 was dominated by the killing of Osama bin Laden on May 2, which briefly lifted President Obama's approval and reset the national conversation around counterterrorism, and by a summer-long debt-ceiling standoff between the new Republican House and the White House that produced the Budget Control Act on August 2 and, two days later, the first-ever S&P downgrade of U.S. sovereign debt. The Tucson shooting of Rep. Gabrielle Giffords in January became a searing early test of political civility, while the NATO-backed Libyan intervention culminated in Muammar Gaddafi's killing in October without Congress ever resolving the constitutional question of whether the campaign required War Powers authorization. December brought the formal end of the Iraq War as the last American troops withdrew. Domestically, Wisconsin's Act 10 fight and the September launch of Occupy Wall Street framed the year's populist energy from opposite ends of the spectrum, while the September 20 repeal of Don't Ask, Don't Tell stood as the year's most significant civil-rights milestone. Late in the year, the Solyndra bankruptcy and the Fast and Furious gunwalking scandal became the leading Republican critiques of the administration, even as a crowded Republican presidential primary field jockeyed for position behind frontrunner Mitt Romney.

    Domestic Policy

    Divided government defined the 112th Congress: House Republicans, empowered by roughly 87 Tea-Party-aligned freshmen under Speaker John Boehner, treated compromise on spending as near-apostasy, while Senate Democrats under Harry Reid held a narrow majority. A near-shutdown was averted by minutes in April, and the summer's 'Gang of Six' and Boehner-Obama 'grand bargain' talks over roughly $4 trillion in deficit reduction collapsed in late July, reportedly over new revenue, leaving the Budget Control Act and its automatic sequestration trigger as the fallback. The bipartisan Super Committee it created missed its November deadline entirely, guaranteeing future across-the-board cuts. Congress did manage genuine bipartisan achievements, including the Leahy-Smith America Invents Act's overhaul of patent law, a four-year PATRIOT Act extension, and long-stalled free-trade agreements with South Korea, Colombia, and Panama, while Obama's $447 billion American Jobs Act was largely blocked. State capitals saw unusual ferment: Wisconsin's public-sector union fight triggered a summer of recall elections, Ohio voters repealed Governor Kasich's similar law by referendum in November, and Alabama and Georgia enacted the era's harshest state immigration crackdowns. The Supreme Court, meanwhile, protected Westboro Baptist Church picketing, upheld arbitration class-action waivers, decertified the Wal-Mart v. Dukes class action, and struck down California's ban on selling violent video games to minors, while granting certiorari on the Affordable Care Act challenge that would dominate its following term.

    Foreign Policy

    Foreign policy was extraordinarily active even as domestic policy stalled. The Arab Spring forced improvised U.S. responses across the region: Washington eventually called for Mubarak's and then Assad's departure, backed a NATO air campaign that helped topple Gaddafi without deploying U.S. ground troops or seeking War Powers authorization, and quietly muted criticism of Bahrain's Saudi-backed crackdown. Beyond bin Laden, counterterrorism policy took a legally fraught turn with the September 30 drone killing of American citizen Anwar al-Awlaki in Yemen, establishing a precedent for extrajudicial targeting of U.S. nationals that would be debated for years. The Iraq War ended in December after Status of Forces negotiations collapsed over troop immunity, while Obama announced a parallel drawdown timeline for the Afghan surge; an August helicopter shoot-down in Wardak province killed 30 Americans, the war's deadliest single incident, and a November strike that killed 24 Pakistani soldiers at Salala triggered a months-long closure of NATO supply lines. The New START arms treaty entered into force in February, Obama unveiled a 'pivot to Asia' in a November address to the Australian Parliament, and Kim Jong-il's death in December installed his untested son Kim Jong-un in North Korea. Leon Panetta replaced Robert Gates at the Pentagon and David Petraeus moved from Afghanistan command to head the CIA.

    Important Elections

    With no federal elections in 2011, the year's most consequential votes came at the state level: Kentucky, Louisiana, and Mississippi all re-elected or elected Republican or incumbent governors, while Mississippi voters rejected a 'fetal personhood' ballot initiative even in a deeply conservative state. Ohio's November referendum repealing Governor Kasich's anti-union SB5 by a 61-39 margin was arguably the year's single most consequential vote, a major setback for the Republican wave's public-sector agenda, while Wisconsin's summer recall elections trimmed but did not flip the state senate and set up Governor Walker's own 2012 recall.
