Arizona recalled state Senate President Russell Pearce, architect of SB 1070, in the state's first successful legislative recall. The Republican presidential primary effectively launched with the August Ames Straw Poll, a chaotic sequence of frontrunners - Bachmann, Perry, Cain, and Gingrich each taking a turn - and Mitt Romney's steady, well-funded persistence positioning him for the January 2012 Iowa caucuses.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows - Part 2 closed out the decade-defining franchise as the year's top domestic grosser, while Bridesmaids became the summer's sleeper hit and reframed studio comedy around female ensembles, and The Help was a late-summer breakout. Critical favorites included The Tree of Life, Drive, Moneyball, Hugo, Melancholia, Take Shelter, A Separation, and Midnight in Paris, while the silent pastiche The Artist, opening stateside in late November, was already positioned as an Oscar favorite. The February ceremony honoring 2010 films had crowned The King's Speech.

    Music

    Adele's 21, released in February, was the year's defining album and one of the best-selling of the decade, propelled by 'Rolling in the Deep' and 'Someone Like You.' Jay-Z and Kanye West's Watch the Throne, Beyonce's 4, Drake's Take Care, and Lady Gaga's Born This Way were major releases, while Bon Iver, Fleet Foxes, PJ Harvey, and Lana Del Rey's viral 'Video Games' signaled indie and internet-native aesthetics gaining mainstream traction. LMFAO's 'Party Rock Anthem' and Foster the People's 'Pumped Up Kicks' ruled radio, EDM broke through via Skrillex, and Amy Winehouse's death on July 23 was a widely felt cultural loss.

    Television

    Broadcast staples like NCIS, American Idol, and Dancing with the Stars still drew huge audiences, and Charlie Sheen's public meltdown and firing from Two and a Half Men was itself a defining early-year media event. But cultural energy was visibly migrating to cable: HBO's Game of Thrones premiered in April, Showtime's Homeland and FX's American Horror Story debuted in the fall, Breaking Bad delivered a scorching fourth season, and Parks and Recreation and Louie became critical darlings, even as reality staples like Jersey Shore and the Real Housewives franchises marked the genre's saturation. Netflix's botched 'Qwikster' announcement embarrassed the company even as it quietly commissioned House of Cards, planting the flag for original streaming programming.

    Technology

    The iPhone 4S launched in October with Siri, introducing consumer voice assistants to the mainstream a day before Steve Jobs's death, while Instagram crossed 10 million users and Snapchat launched in September. Twitter became the year's political and cultural nervous system through the Arab Spring and Occupy, even as Facebook crossed roughly 800 million users and Google+ debuted to little lasting effect. Tablets went mainstream with the iPad 2 and Amazon's $199 Kindle Fire, while Minecraft exited beta and Skyrim and Modern Warfare 3 defined the gaming year.

    Social Trends

    Occupy Wall Street's 'we are the 99 percent' framing and Wisconsin's Act 10 union fight defined a year of populist mobilization from left and right alike. Don't Ask, Don't Tell's repeal and New York's legalization of same-sex marriage marked unmistakable, historic momentum toward LGBT equality, while Slutwalks spreading from Toronto previewed the mid-decade feminist wave. Pew's continued tracking of the 'rise of the nones' and Rob Bell's controversial book Love Wins captured a slow-motion shift in American religious life, and student debt crossing $1 trillion for the first time became a rallying point for economic-inequality politics.

    Crime

    The summer's dominant story was the Casey Anthony trial, whose July 5 acquittal produced genuine national fury and cemented HLN's Nancy Grace-fueled true-crime coverage as a cable-news format. The Penn State-Jerry Sandusky scandal, breaking in November, ended Joe Paterno's career and shocked college sports to its core, while Georgia's execution of Troy Davis despite international protest, and Rep. Anthony Weiner's resignation after a sexting scandal, both became emblematic controversies of the year.

    Sports

    The Green Bay Packers won Super Bowl XLV in February, the Dallas Mavericks upset LeBron James's Miami Heat for the NBA title in June, and the St. Louis Cardinals won one of the greatest World Series ever played in October, capped by David Freese's Game 6 walk-off. Novak Djokovic put together one of the great seasons in tennis history, winning three of four majors, while the Penn State scandal and an NBA lockout that delayed the following season both cast long shadows over the sports world.

    Public Mood

    Contemporary accounts describe a year of exhausted, anxious weariness punctuated by real catharsis - Gallup's economic confidence index cratered to crisis-era lows during the August debt-ceiling fight even as the bin Laden raid, the royal wedding, and an unforgettable World Series gave the year emotional texture beyond its grim economics. Time named 'The Protester' its Person of the Year, capturing a global mood of dissent that Americans recognized in Occupy and the Arab Spring alike.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    At the time, Americans believed 2011 was about the death of bin Laden, the promise of the Arab Spring, the debt-ceiling fight and credit downgrade, the rise of Occupy Wall Street, and the death of Steve Jobs - the punctuation marks on an era of post-9/11 anxiety and financial-crisis recovery.

    What actually mattered

    Historians now foreground different threads: the opening of the Syrian civil war in Deraa, the normalization of drone-based extrajudicial killing via the al-Awlaki strike, Vladimir Putin's announced return to the Russian presidency, the Iraq withdrawal that created the vacuum ISIS would fill by 2014, the founding of Snapchat and Bitcoin's first sustained dollar parity, the S&P downgrade as the birth of permanent debt-ceiling brinksmanship, and IBM Watson's Jeopardy! win as an early public glimpse of the machine-learning era.

    Most forgotten story

    The Casey Anthony trial and acquittal, arguably the most-covered story of the summer, left almost no lasting trace - no reform legislation, no jurisprudential shift - surviving mainly as a case study in cable news's tabloidization of legal coverage.

    Most underrated story

    The March 2011 crackdown on protesters in Deraa, Syria, framed at the time as one node of the broader Arab Spring, in fact set in motion the civil war, refugee crisis, and rise of ISIS that would reshape European and Middle Eastern politics for the following decade.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    That the Arab Spring, confidently compared at the time to the democratic wave of 1989, produced almost no consolidated democracies - instead yielding state collapse in Libya, a genocidal civil war in Syria, a military restoration in Egypt, and a durable regional counter-revolution - stands as one of the more dramatic misreadings in recent journalism.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Casey Anthony murder trialIts gavel-to-gavel cable coverage produced enormous but purely emotional engagement; it left no legislative or jurisprudential legacy and is remembered mainly as a tabloid-news case study.
    Charlie Sheen's 'tiger blood' meltdownA viral celebrity implosion that dominated entertainment coverage for weeks, it faded into a footnote in the early history of internet-fueled celebrity breakdowns.
    The royal wedding of William and KateWatched by tens of millions of Americans, it produced a single unifying news cycle with essentially no downstream political or cultural consequence.
    The Solyndra bankruptcy and the Fast and Furious gunwalking scandalBoth consumed congressional hearings and cable-news coverage as emblems of Obama-era cronyism, but neither produced lasting policy change and both were swamped by later, larger controversies.
    Rebecca Black's viral song 'Friday'Treated by earnest cultural critics as proof that YouTube was corroding music, it is remembered today only as a nostalgia meme.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The Assad regime's crackdown on protesters in DeraaIt was the opening act of a civil war that would kill more than half a million people, displace 13 million, catalyze Europe's 2015 refugee crisis, and incubate ISIS.
    The drone killing of Anwar al-AwlakiA single day of debate obscured what became the defining precedent for extrajudicial targeted killing of U.S. citizens by their own government.
    Vladimir Putin's announcement that he would return to the presidencyTriggering Moscow's largest protests since 1993, it marked the pivot toward the revanchist, repressive Putin who would annex Crimea in 2014 and invade Ukraine in 2022.
    The founding of Snapchat and Bitcoin's first parity with the dollarBoth were dismissed as fringe curiosities outside the niche tech press yet proved foundational to the social-media and cryptocurrency economies of the following decade.
    IBM Watson's Jeopardy! victory over Ken Jennings and Brad RutterCovered as a novelty game-show stunt, it was the first mass-public demonstration that machine learning was crossing into commercial-grade capability, a signal on the same timeline as the eventual arrival of large language models.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Bin Laden Killed in U.S. Raid on Pakistani Compound
    2. Mubarak Steps Down After 18 Days of Protest in Tahrir Square
    3. Massive Quake and Tsunami Devastate Japan; Fukushima Reactors in Crisis
    4. Debt-Ceiling Deal Averts Default as S&P Strips U.S. of Its AAA Rating
    5. Gaddafi Killed as Libyan Rebels Overrun Sirte
    6. Steve Jobs, Apple Visionary, Dies at 56
    7. Occupy Wall Street Protests Spread to Cities Nationwide
    8. Jury Acquits Casey Anthony in Death of Daughter Caylee
    9. Last U.S. Troops Leave Iraq as Nearly Nine-Year War Ends
    10. Congresswoman Giffords Shot at Tucson Constituent Event; Six Killed

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe 112th Congress convened on January 3 with John Boehner as Speaker of a new Republican House majority. Five days later, gunman Jared Loughner opened fire at a constituent event in Tucson, Arizona, killing six people and gravely wounding Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, sparking a national debate over political rhetoric. Tunisia's president Zine El Abidine Ben Ali fled the country on January 14 after weeks of protest, opening what would become the Arab Spring. William Daley replaced Rahm Emanuel as White House Chief of Staff, and Rep. Chris Lee resigned after a Craigslist photo scandal.
    FebruaryTens of thousands of protesters occupied the Wisconsin state capitol in Madison against Gov. Scott Walker's Act 10 collective-bargaining bill, and 14 Democratic state senators fled to Illinois to deny a quorum. Egypt's Hosni Mubarak stepped down February 11 after 18 days of protest in Tahrir Square. The New START arms-control treaty entered into force, the Justice Department announced it would no longer defend the Defense of Marriage Act, and IBM's Watson defeated Ken Jennings and Brad Rutter on Jeopardy!. Adele's 21 was released February 22, and Charlie Sheen's public meltdown and firing from Two and a Half Men became a defining early-year media spectacle.
    MarchA magnitude-9.0 earthquake and tsunami struck northeast Japan on March 11, killing more than 15,000 people and crippling the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant, an event that rattled global supply chains and energy policy for years. The UN Security Council authorized a no-fly zone over Libya, and the U.S. joined a NATO-led air campaign against Muammar Gaddafi's forces. In Syria, government forces began a violent crackdown on protesters in the southern city of Deraa, an event that drew little attention at the time but opened the country's civil war. The Nintendo 3DS launched, and Terry Jones's Quran burning in Florida triggered deadly riots in Afghanistan.
    AprilA government shutdown was averted by a last-minute continuing resolution just before midnight on April 8. A catastrophic Super Outbreak of tornadoes tore across Alabama, Mississippi, Tennessee, and Georgia from April 25 to 28, killing more than 320 people. HBO's Game of Thrones premiered April 17, and the royal wedding of Prince William and Catherine Middleton on April 29 drew an estimated 22.8 million American television viewers. The Supreme Court upheld arbitration class-action waivers in AT&T Mobility v. Concepcion, and Connecticut won the NCAA men's basketball title.
    MayNavy SEAL Team Six killed Osama bin Laden in a raid on his compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan, on May 2, prompting spontaneous celebrations outside the White House and at Ground Zero. LinkedIn's IPO on May 18 reopened the social-media stock market. Preacher Harold Camping's predicted May 21 doomsday failed to arrive. The EF-5 Joplin, Missouri tornado killed 158 people on May 22, the deadliest single U.S. tornado in decades. Dominique Strauss-Kahn was arrested in New York on sexual-assault charges, and Lady Gaga's Born This Way was released.
    JuneNew York became the largest state to legalize same-sex marriage by legislative vote on June 24. Robert Gates retired as Secretary of Defense, succeeded by Leon Panetta, and Obama announced that the 33,000-troop Afghanistan surge would fully withdraw by summer 2012. The Supreme Court decertified the massive employment class action in Wal-Mart Stores v. Dukes and struck down California's ban on selling violent video games to minors in Brown v. Entertainment Merchants Association. The Dallas Mavericks defeated the Miami Heat for the NBA championship, and QE2 concluded as scheduled.
    JulyA jury in Orlando acquitted Casey Anthony on July 5 of murdering her daughter Caylee, provoking national outrage and saturation cable coverage. Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows - Part 2 closed out the decade-long film franchise atop the box office. Boehner-Obama 'grand bargain' negotiations over roughly $4 trillion in deficit reduction collapsed in the final week of the month amid escalating debt-ceiling brinkmanship. Singer Amy Winehouse died on July 23, the Atlanta Public Schools testing-cheating scandal was exposed, and the FAA partially shut down amid a funding lapse.
    AugustCongress passed the Budget Control Act on August 2, raising the debt ceiling hours before a projected default. Two days later, Standard & Poor's stripped the United States of its AAA credit rating for the first time in history, and markets convulsed, with the Dow falling 634 points the following Monday. A helicopter shoot-down in Wardak province, Afghanistan, killed 30 Americans, including 22 Navy SEALs, the war's deadliest single incident. Gabrielle Giffords made a dramatic return to the House floor to vote for the debt deal, Wisconsin's recall elections narrowed but did not flip the state senate, Warren Buffett injected $5 billion into Bank of America, and Rick Perry entered the presidential race and briefly led the field.
    SeptemberOccupy Wall Street began in Zuccotti Park on September 17 and spread to dozens of cities within weeks under the banner 'We are the 99 percent.' 'Don't Ask, Don't Tell' was formally repealed on September 20, and Georgia executed Troy Davis on September 21 despite international protest over doubts about his guilt. The Federal Reserve launched Operation Twist, gold hit a record above $1,900 an ounce, Vladimir Putin announced he would return to the Russian presidency, and a U.S. drone strike killed American-born cleric Anwar al-Awlaki in Yemen on September 30. Netflix's botched 'Qwikster' spinoff announcement infuriated subscribers, and Snapchat quietly launched.
    OctoberThe iPhone 4S launched October 4 with Siri, and Apple co-founder Steve Jobs died the next day, October 5, prompting an outpouring of public mourning. The St. Louis Cardinals won an instant-classic World Series over the Texas Rangers, capped by David Freese's Game 6 walk-off home run. MF Global collapsed into bankruptcy on October 31 amid roughly $1.6 billion in missing customer funds. Muammar Gaddafi was captured and killed in Sirte, Libya, on October 20, and Herman Cain's '9-9-9' tax plan propelled him to the top of Republican primary polls.
    NovemberThe Penn State-Jerry Sandusky child sexual-abuse scandal erupted on November 5, ending Joe Paterno's decades-long coaching career days later. The congressional deficit 'Super Committee' missed its November 23 deadline and failed, arming future automatic sequestration. New York police cleared the Occupy Wall Street encampment from Zuccotti Park on November 15. Ohio voters repealed Gov. Kasich's anti-union SB5 by referendum, and Arizona recalled state Senate President Russell Pearce, author of SB 1070. Amazon's Kindle Fire tablet launched, and Herman Cain's presidential campaign was engulfed by sexual-harassment allegations.
    DecemberNorth Korean leader Kim Jong-il died on December 17 and was succeeded by his son, Kim Jong-un. The last U.S. troops crossed out of Iraq on December 18, formally ending nearly nine years of war. Herman Cain withdrew from the presidential race amid scandal as Newt Gingrich rose in the polls. AT&T's proposed $39 billion takeover of T-Mobile USA collapsed under antitrust pressure, Congress resolved a year-end standoff over the payroll-tax cut with a two-month extension, and the European Central Bank's massive three-year LTRO liquidity injection eased the eurozone crisis heading into 2012.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+1.6% real GDP growth (Q4/Q4), the weakest full year of the post-2008 recovery to date
    InflationCPI +3.0% year-over-year in December (peaked at 3.9% in September); core CPI +2.2%
    Unemployment8.5% in December, down from 9.4% a year earlier (peaked near 9.2% in June)
    S&P 500 return+2.1% total return (price essentially flat at 1,257.60, -0.003%)
    Dow return+8.4% total return (+5.5% price)
    Fed funds rate0-0.25% throughout the year, unchanged; Fed launched Operation Twist and 'mid-2013' forward guidance
    Oil priceWTI crude closed near $98.83/barrel (from $91.38 at year-start); Brent finished near $107
    Gold priceClosed near $1,565/oz, up roughly 10% on the year after an intraday record above $1,921 in September
    Population308.7 million per 2010 Census results released during 2011, up 9.7% from 2000
    Median home priceRoughly $166,100 median existing-home price (NAR), down from about $172,000 in 2010
    National debtGross federal debt ~$15.2 trillion (~100% of GDP); publicly held debt ~$10.5 trillion; FY2011 deficit ~$1.30 trillion (8.4% of GDP)

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2011 was to live inside a year that never quite settled on a mood. It began with the shock of the Tucson shooting and the slow-burning spectacle of Wisconsin's capitol occupation, then pivoted, within months, to genuine catharsis: the May night when word spread that Osama bin Laden was dead, and strangers gathered spontaneously outside the White House and at Ground Zero to sing the national anthem and chant 'USA.' For one evening, at least, the fractious country felt briefly whole again, a feeling that had grown rare since 2008.

    That unity did not last the summer. By August, Americans were watching their elected government negotiate the debt ceiling like a hostage situation, and then watching Standard & Poor's strip the United States of a credit rating it had held for as long as the rating existed. It was a symbolic wound more than a substantive one - Treasuries actually rallied - but it landed as a verdict on Washington's competence, and consumer confidence cratered to levels not seen since the worst of the financial crisis. Unemployment stayed stubbornly close to 9 percent for most of the year, real incomes fell, and the housing market that had wrecked the economy in 2008 still had not found a bottom. For millions of Americans, the recovery that economists kept promising simply never arrived in their own paychecks or their own home equity.

    And yet 2011 was not simply grim. It was the year Adele's voice was inescapable, when Game of Thrones and a spectacular World Series Game 6 gave people something to argue about besides the deficit, when the phone in your pocket started talking back to you, and when a scrappy encampment in a Manhattan park put a new vocabulary - 'the 1 percent,' 'we are the 99 percent' - into everyday conversation. Occupy Wall Street did not win any legislative victory, but it captured something real: a widening sense that the financial system had been rescued while ordinary households had been left to fend for themselves. Same-sex couples in New York could marry by year's end, and gay and lesbian troops could serve openly for the first time, both genuine and lasting changes.

    Overseas, the year felt like an ending and a beginning simultaneously: the Iraq War closed in December after nearly nine years, even as Egypt, Libya, and especially Syria opened questions about the Arab Spring's promise that would not be answered for a decade. Few Americans watching the fall of dictators in the spring imagined that Syria's uprising, covered as one more regional story, would eventually reshape European and Middle Eastern politics for a generation.

    By New Year's Eve, the abiding feeling was exhaustion tempered by resilience - a country that had survived a credit downgrade, a devastating tornado season, and a summer of manufactured crisis, and that still found room for wonder, outrage, and hope in roughly equal measure.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2012
    Econ +1Mark +2Poli +1Cult +1Over +1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The United States in 2012 completed a fourth year of recovery from the Great Recession that was real but still fragile. Real GDP grew a little over 2 percent, unemployment fell from 8.3 percent in January to 7.8 percent in December, the housing market decisively bottomed and turned upward, and the Federal Reserve, still led by Ben Bernanke, pushed policy into genuinely new territory: an open-ended, labor-market-linked bond-buying program in September (QE3) followed in December by the first explicit numerical forward guidance in the Fed's history, the so-called Evans Rule. Financial markets had a strong year almost in spite of the headlines, with the S&P 500 returning roughly 16 percent on a total-return basis, propelled by Mario Draghi's July vow to do "whatever it takes" to save the euro and by the Fed's own aggressive easing, even as Facebook's botched Nasdaq debut, JPMorgan's "London Whale" losses, and the Libor-rigging scandal reminded investors that Wall Street's post-crisis culture remained unreformed.

    Politically, the year turned on a single decisive event: President Obama's re-election over Mitt Romney by 332 electoral votes to 206, making him the first Democrat since Franklin Roosevelt to win a popular-vote majority twice. The Supreme Court's surprise 5-4 ruling in June upholding the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate, authored by Chief Justice Roberts, secured Obama's signature first-term achievement just months before the election. Yet divided government produced one of the least productive Congresses in modern history, and the year closed with Washington sliding toward the self-imposed "fiscal cliff," resolved only in the final hours before New Year's Day 2013. Obama's own personal endorsement of same-sex marriage in May, the DACA program shielding young undocumented immigrants announced in June, and the killing of Ambassador Christopher Stevens and three other Americans in Benghazi in September all became durable touchstones of the term, for very different reasons.

    Culturally, 2012 held both genuine highs and a gathering darkness. The London Olympics delivered Michael Phelps's record-setting farewell and Gabby Douglas's historic all-around gold; "Gangnam Style" became the first video to reach a billion YouTube views; Instagram, acquired by Facebook for a then-mocked billion dollars, was quietly becoming the future of visual culture; and Frank Ocean's Channel Orange and Kendrick Lamar's good kid, m.A.A.d city announced a new era in popular music. But the year's final months delivered the Aurora theater massacre in July and, most searingly, the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting in December, twenty first-graders and six educators murdered in Newtown, Connecticut, an atrocity that reopened the gun debate and then, contrary to nearly every expectation at the time, changed no federal law at all. Viewed from a decade or more later, 2012 looks like a hinge year whose real significance, the birth of the permanent-QE era, Xi Jinping's ascension in China, Trayvon Martin's killing as the incubator of a new civil-rights movement, and Instagram's quiet rise, was almost invisible beneath the noise of the presidential campaign that consumed the nation's attention all year.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Growth held near 2 percent, unemployment fell meaningfully below 8 percent for the first time since early 2009, and housing decisively turned upward, a clearly above-neutral year even with still-elevated joblessness and fiscal-cliff drag looming.
    Markets+2Double-digit total returns across the S&P 500, Dow, and Nasdaq, low realized volatility, and a decisive euro-crisis rally combined with aggressive Fed easing to make 2012 a strongly positive year for risk assets despite the Facebook IPO fiasco and London Whale losses.
    Politics+1A legitimate and decisive presidential election, a Supreme Court ruling accepted across the spectrum, and no shutdown or debt-ceiling breach were offset by a historically unproductive Congress, chronic fiscal-cliff drift, and the searing Benghazi and Fast and Furious controversies.
    Culture+1A creatively strong year in music, film, television, and the Olympics was counterbalanced by the Aurora and Sandy Hook mass shootings and the darker undertones of an increasingly screen-mediated, polarized public sphere.
    Overall Mood+1A genuinely healing economy, a resolved and non-catastrophic election, and real cultural highs left most Americans cautiously optimistic through the fall, but Sandy Hook's horror in the final weeks of December cast a long shadow that tempered the year's overall verdict.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Barack Obama wins re-election over Mitt Romney, 332-206 in the Electoral College, becoming the first Democrat since FDR to win the popular vote twice (November 6).
    2. The Supreme Court upholds the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate in NFIB v. Sebelius, with Chief Justice Roberts casting the deciding vote (June 28).
    3. A gunman kills 20 first-graders and 6 educators at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Connecticut (December 14).
    4. Militants attack the U.S. diplomatic compound in Benghazi, Libya, killing Ambassador J. Christopher Stevens and three other Americans (September 11).
    5. Superstorm Sandy devastates the New Jersey shore and floods lower Manhattan, killing roughly 150 Americans days before the election (October 22-30).
    6. A gunman kills 12 and wounds 70 at a midnight Batman screening in Aurora, Colorado (July 20).
    7. The Federal Reserve launches QE3, an open-ended $40 billion-a-month bond-buying program tied to the labor market (September 13).
    8. Facebook's highly anticipated Nasdaq IPO stumbles out of the gate, and the stock loses half its value within months (May 18).
    9. Michael Phelps retires as the most decorated Olympian in history and Gabby Douglas becomes the first Black woman to win Olympic gymnastics all-around gold at the London Games (July 27-August 12).
    10. Colorado and Washington voters legalize recreational marijuana, the first U.S. states to do so, while Maine, Maryland, and Washington approve same-sex marriage at the ballot box (November 6).

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy turned in a solid if unspectacular fourth year of post-recession expansion, with real GDP growing roughly 2.2-2.3 percent for the year even as growth decelerated markedly through the fourth quarter to an anemic 0.1 percent annualized pace amid slower inventory build and defense-spending pullbacks tied to the looming fiscal cliff. Inflation was mild and receding, with headline CPI up 1.7 percent year-over-year in December versus 3.0 percent a year earlier, driven mainly by softening energy prices even as shelter costs began to firm. Unemployment, still the defining wound of the post-crisis era, improved meaningfully from 8.3 percent in January to 7.8 percent in December, the first sustained sub-8-percent readings since early 2009, though labor-force participation kept falling and long-term joblessness remained historically elevated. The housing market's turn was the year's most consequential structural development: the Case-Shiller 20-City index bottomed early in the year and rose 6-7 percent through December, median existing-home prices climbed roughly 11 percent, housing starts jumped about 28 percent to their highest level since 2008, and 30-year mortgage rates hit an all-time low of 3.31 percent in mid-November.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew approximately 2.2-2.3 percent for the year; unemployment fell from 8.3 percent in January to 7.8 percent in December (later benchmarked to 7.9 percent); headline CPI inflation ended the year at 1.7 percent year-over-year with core CPI at 1.9 percent; gross federal debt crossed $16 trillion in early September and closed the year near $16.4 trillion, roughly 100-103 percent of GDP; the fiscal-year 2012 deficit was approximately $1.09 trillion, the fourth straight year above $1 trillion; median existing-home prices rose about 11 percent to roughly $180,000; housing starts rose approximately 28 percent; and the University of Michigan consumer sentiment index climbed to a five-year high of 82.7 in November before dipping on fiscal-cliff anxiety.

    Major Events

    The year's defining economic events were mostly monetary and fiscal. February's extension of the payroll-tax holiday and emergency unemployment benefits averted a mid-year fiscal drag. The Federal Reserve dominated policy: in June it extended "Operation Twist"; on September 13 it launched QE3, an open-ended $40 billion-a-month agency-MBS purchase program tied to labor-market outcomes rather than a calendar date, a genuine regime shift; and on December 12 it expanded the program to $85 billion a month while introducing the "Evans Rule," the Fed's first explicit numerical forward guidance, committing to near-zero rates until unemployment fell to 6.5 percent or projected inflation rose above 2.5 percent. Hurricane Sandy struck the Northeast October 22-30, causing roughly $65-70 billion in damage and forcing the first two-day weather closure of the NYSE since 1888. The "fiscal cliff," the simultaneous expiration of the Bush tax cuts and payroll-tax holiday alongside automatic sequester cuts, dominated fourth-quarter policy discussion and was resolved only by the American Taxpayer Relief Act signed January 2, 2013. Corporate distress marked the year too: Eastman Kodak filed for Chapter 11 in January and Hostess Brands liquidated in November.

    Why this score

    A score of +1 reflects genuine, broadening healing: growth held near 2 percent, unemployment fell meaningfully below 8 percent for the first time since early 2009, inflation stayed tame, and housing decisively turned, but the recovery remained short of exceptional given still-elevated joblessness, falling labor-force participation, and a fiscal cliff that threatened to undo the year's gains just as it ended.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities delivered strong double-digit gains on a path that ran from a nervous first-quarter surge through a mid-year swoon on Europe to a powerful late-summer rally on central-bank action and a choppy fourth quarter. The S&P 500 finished at 1,426.19, up 13.4 percent on price and about 16.0 percent on total return; the Dow closed at 13,104.14, up 7.3 percent on price and roughly 10.2 percent total return; and the Nasdaq Composite finished at 3,019.51, up 15.9 percent. Realized volatility was moderate, with the VIX ending the year near 18 and the index posting no new all-time high, remaining below its October 2007 peak. On February 21 the Dow closed above 13,000 for the first time since May 2008, and Draghi's July 26 "whatever it takes" speech ended the acute phase of the euro crisis and unleashed a global risk-on rally that carried through the Fed's QE3 announcement in September.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    Facebook's May 18 IPO priced at $38 a share for a $104 billion valuation, the largest tech IPO ever at the time, but a Nasdaq trading glitch, over-allocation, and weakening fundamentals sent the stock below $20 by early September, making it the year's poster child for retail disillusionment with tech IPOs. Apple briefly became the most valuable public company in history, crossing $650 billion in September and peaking near $702 a share, before falling roughly 25 percent into year-end. JPMorgan's "London Whale" losses at its Chief Investment Office grew from an initially disclosed $2 billion in May to roughly $6.2 billion, reviving too-big-to-fail concerns, while on June 21 Moody's downgraded 15 large global banks including Bank of America, Citigroup, Goldman Sachs, and JPMorgan Chase. The Libor manipulation scandal broke into full view with Barclays' $453 million settlement on June 27, implicating major U.S. banks. Knight Capital's runaway trading algorithm generated $440 million in losses in 45 minutes on August 1, effectively destroying the firm. Major M&A included Glencore's roughly $30 billion agreed acquisition of Xstrata and SoftBank's $20 billion deal for control of Sprint, while Kodak filed for bankruptcy in January and Hostess Brands liquidated in November.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was a central-bank-driven risk rally: Draghi's euro-crisis backstop and the Fed's QE3 and Evans Rule together compressed volatility and lifted risk assets broadly even amid a decelerating real economy. Ten-year Treasury yields ended the year around 1.76 percent in a roughly 1.40-2.40 percent range, with the fed funds target pinned at 0-0.25 percent throughout. WTI crude closed at $91.82, down from $98.83 at end-2011 after ranging from the mid-$70s to over $109, while Brent averaged roughly $112, a record annual average driven partly by Iran sanctions. Gold ended the year near $1,675 an ounce, up roughly 7 percent for a twelfth consecutive annual gain, though momentum was clearly fading, and the dollar index ended little changed near 79.8.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Barack Obama defeated Republican challenger Mitt Romney on November 6, winning 332 electoral votes to 206 and roughly 51.1 percent of the popular vote, becoming the first Democrat since Franklin Roosevelt to win over 50 percent of the popular vote twice. On June 28 the Supreme Court, in a 5-4 ruling authored by Chief Justice Roberts, upheld the ACA's individual mandate as a valid exercise of the taxing power while striking down the mandatory Medicaid expansion as unconstitutionally coercive on the states. On September 11, coordinated attacks on the U.S. diplomatic compound and CIA annex in Benghazi, Libya killed Ambassador J. Christopher Stevens, Sean Smith, and CIA contractors Tyrone Woods and Glen Doherty, the first killing of a sitting U.S. ambassador since 1979, and became a persistent controversy over the administration's initial public account. On December 14, a gunman killed 20 first-graders and 6 educators at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Connecticut, reopening the national gun-control debate. All year Washington moved toward the January 2013 "fiscal cliff" of expiring tax cuts and automatic sequester cuts, resolved only by the American Taxpayer Relief Act signed just after midnight on January 1, 2013. On June 28 the House also voted to hold Attorney General Eric Holder in criminal contempt of Congress, the first sitting cabinet officer ever so cited, over the botched "Fast and Furious" investigation, and on November 9 CIA Director David Petraeus resigned after an affair with his biographer was exposed by an FBI cyberharassment investigation.

    Domestic Policy

    Divided government defined the year: Democrats held the White House and a 53-47 Senate under Harry Reid, while Republicans held the House 242-193 under Speaker John Boehner, and the 112th Congress would end as one of the least productive on record, with most consequential domestic action coming through executive orders, agency rulemaking, and the courts rather than statutes. The signal legislative achievement was the February reauthorization of the payroll-tax cut and unemployment benefits; Obama also signed the JOBS Act in April, easing securities rules and legalizing equity crowdfunding, MAP-21's two-year surface-transportation reauthorization in July, and the STOCK Act in April, banning congressional insider trading. The Violence Against Women Act reauthorization died in the House over LGBT and immigrant-victim protections, and the DREAM Act remained blocked, prompting Obama's June 15 announcement of Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA), granting renewable deportation relief to eligible undocumented immigrants brought to the U.S. as children. Regulatory action dominated elsewhere: HHS's ACA contraception-coverage mandate ignited a fight with Catholic bishops that produced a compromise "accommodation," and the EPA finalized the first federal mercury limits on power plants. On civil rights, Obama's May 9 endorsement of same-sex marriage, the November ballot wins in Maine, Maryland, and Washington, and Colorado and Washington's landmark legalization of recreational marijuana marked the year's most durable social shifts, while Trayvon Martin's February 26 shooting in Sanford, Florida and George Zimmerman's delayed arrest under Florida's Stand Your Ground law sparked nationwide "hoodie march" protests that foreshadowed the Black Lives Matter era. The Supreme Court's Arizona v. United States ruling in June struck down three of four provisions of Arizona's SB 1070. Scandals beyond Fast and Furious and Petraeus included a lavish GSA conference in Las Vegas and a Secret Service prostitution scandal in Cartagena; the IRS's targeting of conservative nonprofits' tax-exempt applications was already underway but would not surface publicly until 2013.

    Foreign Policy

    Obama's foreign policy centered on winding down the wars of the previous decade, pivoting to Asia, and a light-footprint approach to counterterrorism. In Afghanistan, the 2009 surge forces completed their drawdown in September, and Obama signed the U.S.-Afghanistan Enduring Strategic Partnership Agreement in Kabul on May 2; NATO's Chicago summit set a timeline for full security transition by end-2014, even as record "green-on-blue" insider attacks strained coalition trust. Iran policy tightened dramatically as the EU joined a full oil embargo and Iran was cut off from SWIFT messaging in March, collapsing the rial, while Obama and Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu sparred publicly over a military "red line," a dispute crystallized by Netanyahu's September UN cartoon-bomb speech. The Arab Spring's aftermath dominated the year: Syria's civil war intensified, with Obama declaring chemical-weapons use a "red line" in August; Muslim Brotherhood candidate Mohamed Morsi won Egypt's presidency in June; and the post-Qaddafi transition in Libya unraveled, with Benghazi its most searing symbol. Defense Secretary Leon Panetta formalized the "pivot to Asia" in June, announcing 60 percent of U.S. naval assets would be based in the Pacific by 2020, and Obama made a landmark November visit to Burma/Myanmar, the first by a sitting U.S. president. Russia policy soured as Vladimir Putin returned to the Kremlin in May amid mass protests, ending the "reset," even as Congress passed the Magnitsky Act in December while simultaneously granting Russia permanent normal trade relations. Chinese dissident Chen Guangcheng's dramatic escape to the U.S. embassy in Beijing in April briefly strained U.S.-China relations, and Xi Jinping's ascension as General Secretary of the Communist Party at the November 18th Party Congress was covered as a routine handoff at the time.

    Important Elections

    The presidential race saw Romney emerge from a protracted Republican primary against Rick Santorum, Newt Gingrich, and Ron Paul to clinch the nomination by late April and select Congressman Paul Ryan as his running mate in August. Obama's decisive win over Romney, sealed by strong debate-and-storm dynamics including Hurricane Sandy's late-October landfall, produced a coalition of minorities, young voters, and single women that came to be called the "Obama coalition." Senate elections gave Democrats a net gain of two seats, expanding their caucus to 55-45, including Elizabeth Warren's defeat of Scott Brown in Massachusetts, Tammy Baldwin becoming the first openly gay U.S. senator in Wisconsin, and Claire McCaskill's win over Todd Akin in Missouri after his "legitimate rape" remark effectively ended his campaign. House elections left Republicans with a reduced but intact majority of roughly 234-201, even as Democrats won more votes nationwide, a fact largely attributed to post-2010 redistricting. In governors' races, Republicans netted one seat to end at 30-19-1, and Wisconsin Governor Scott Walker survived a June recall election against Tom Barrett. On ballot measures, Colorado and Washington legalized recreational marijuana, Maine, Maryland, and Washington approved same-sex marriage while Minnesota rejected a constitutional ban, and California voters approved Governor Jerry Brown's Proposition 30 tax increase.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Domestic box office rebounded to $10.8 billion. Marvel's The Avengers grossed $623 million domestic and $1.52 billion worldwide, consolidating the shared-universe blockbuster model that would define Hollywood for a decade, while The Dark Knight Rises closed Christopher Nolan's Batman trilogy at $448 million domestic and The Hunger Games minted Jennifer Lawrence as a star. Skyfall revived Bond at $304 million domestic. Prestige standouts included Steven Spielberg's Lincoln, Kathryn Bigelow's Zero Dark Thirty, which ignited a torture-debate firestorm, Quentin Tarantino's Django Unchained, Ben Affleck's Argo, Ang Lee's Life of Pi, David O. Russell's Silver Linings Playbook, Wes Anderson's Moonrise Kingdom, and Paul Thomas Anderson's The Master.

    Music

    The year's defining singles were Carly Rae Jepsen's "Call Me Maybe," Gotye's "Somebody That I Used to Know" featuring Kimbra, fun.'s "We Are Young" and "Some Nights," Taylor Swift's "We Are Never Ever Getting Back Together," and Macklemore & Ryan Lewis's "Thrift Shop." Adele's 21 remained the year's top-selling album, joined by Taylor Swift's Red and Mumford & Sons' Babel. Frank Ocean's Channel Orange, released days after Ocean's Tumblr post about a first love with a man, was the critical album of the year, and Kendrick Lamar's good kid, m.A.A.d city announced a generational talent. EDM crossed fully into the mainstream through Skrillex, Avicii, and Calvin Harris, and PSY's "Gangnam Style," uploaded in July, became the first video to reach a billion YouTube views in December, inaugurating the K-pop crossover. Whitney Houston's death on February 11 cast a pall over Grammy weekend.

    Television

    This was a peak-TV inflection year. Netflix greenlit House of Cards, betting strategically on original streaming programming, while AMC's Breaking Bad aired the first half of its final season and HBO's Girls premiered in April, defining a strain of millennial cultural discourse. Homeland won the Emmy for Outstanding Drama and Modern Family repeated as Outstanding Comedy, while Louie, Mad Men, and Downton Abbey drove critical attention. Duck Dynasty became a cable ratings juggernaut and Honey Boo Boo debuted on TLC, and second-screen, live-tweeted viewing became normalized around NBC's Sunday Night Football, the most-watched program in America.

    Technology

    The iPhone 5 arrived September 21 with a 4-inch screen and LTE, briefly making Apple the most valuable public company ever, though Apple Maps became a first-order fiasco that drew a rare public apology from Tim Cook. Facebook filed to go public in February and IPO'd on May 18 at $38, a botched Nasdaq debut whose stock collapse defined tech's summer, while Facebook's acquisition of Instagram for $1 billion, then a thirteen-employee photo app, closed in September to widespread mockery. Samsung's Galaxy S III cemented itself as the credible iPhone rival, Google's Nexus 7 proved a good $199 Android tablet was possible, and Microsoft's October launch of Windows 8 and Surface was widely panned. Twitter reached roughly 200 million active users and became inescapable in politics and news, Pinterest was the year's breakout growth story, and Google unveiled Google Glass at I/O in June.

    Social Trends

    The "99 percent vs. 1 percent" frame from the faded Occupy Wall Street movement continued to shape political language. Trayvon Martin's killing and the ensuing Million Hoodie marches defined the spring, while the August Chick-fil-A controversy over Dan Cathy's anti-same-sex-marriage comments produced dueling "appreciation day" and "kiss-in" protests. The January 18 SOPA/PIPA internet blackout, with Wikipedia, Google, and Reddit going dark, was arguably the first mass internet-user protest to kill federal legislation. Pew's October "Nones on the Rise" report documented that religiously unaffiliated Americans had climbed to roughly 20 percent of adults, and the Census Bureau's finding that non-Hispanic white births had fallen below 50 percent of all U.S. births for the first time drove much of the year's political commentary.

    Crime

    Beyond Aurora, Sandy Hook, and Trayvon Martin, the Jerry Sandusky trial and June 22 conviction of the former Penn State assistant coach on 45 counts of child sexual abuse led to Joe Paterno's statue removal and unprecedented NCAA sanctions after the Freeh Report. The Sikh temple shooting in Oak Creek, Wisconsin killed six on August 5, and Kansas City Chiefs linebacker Jovan Belcher killed his girlfriend and himself on December 1, prompting Bob Costas's on-air gun-control commentary. Colorado and Washington's November 6 legalization of recreational marijuana marked a landmark moment in the unwinding of the drug war.

    Sports

    The New York Giants beat the New England Patriots 21-17 in Super Bowl XLVI. LeBron James won his first NBA title, MVP, and Finals MVP as the Miami Heat beat Oklahoma City 4-1, while the San Francisco Giants swept the Detroit Tigers in the World Series and the Los Angeles Kings won the Stanley Cup just before the 2012-13 lockout. At the London Olympics, Michael Phelps retired as the most decorated Olympian in history, Gabby Douglas became the first Black woman to win the individual all-around gymnastics gold, and Missy Franklin broke out with four golds. Lance Armstrong was stripped of his seven Tour de France titles by USADA in October, the year's biggest anti-doping story, and a replacement-referee "Fail Mary" call in Seattle on September 24 ended the NFL's officiating lockout.

    Public Mood

    The year felt like a hinge: a genuine but uneven recovery, a psychically dominant six-month election that produced triumphalism on the left and existential crisis on the right, and a smartphone-and-social-media stack that finished eating everyday life through Instagram, a public Facebook, and a dominant Twitter. Then Sandy Hook broke everything in the year's final month; the sense of a country unable to protect its own children after yet another mass shooting produced a grief-and-fury tone that carried unresolved into 2013.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    The 2012 news calendar treated the year as fundamentally about the presidential election, secondarily about recovery from the financial crisis, and only intermittently about culture, technology, and foreign affairs; the prestige press's own "top stories" lists ran election, Sandy Hook, Sandy, Aurora, Benghazi, the fiscal cliff, the London Olympics, the ACA ruling, and the Mars Curiosity landing.

    What actually mattered

    What actually mattered was structural: the completion of the central-bank pivot toward permanent balance-sheet policy through Draghi's "whatever it takes" speech and the Fed's QE3; the entrenchment of a new generation of leaders in Xi Jinping, Kim Jong-un, and the restored Putin; the migration of American social and cultural life onto Instagram, YouTube, and Twitter; and the beginning of a national reckoning on race and policing, seeded by Trayvon Martin, that would use those same platforms as its nervous system for the following decade.

    Most forgotten story

    The Chen Guangcheng embassy standoff in late April and early May 2012, in which the blind Chinese civil-rights lawyer escaped house arrest and took refuge in the U.S. embassy in Beijing during Secretary Clinton's visit, was on every front page for a week as a potential rupture in U.S.-China relations, yet is barely remembered today, eclipsed by the far larger deterioration in that relationship along other axes in the years since.

    Most underrated story

    Xi Jinping's elevation to General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party at the 18th Party Congress in November was covered as a routine ten-year handoff, with most American analysts expecting a cautious, collectively-constrained reformer; instead it proved to be the single most consequential leadership change of the decade, setting in motion the militarization of the South China Sea, the crushing of Hong Kong's autonomy, the Xinjiang security state, and the reframing of great-power competition as the organizing principle of American foreign policy.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The biggest hindsight surprise is that Sandy Hook changed nothing legislatively. In the immediate aftermath, virtually every commentator, including many gun-rights supporters, assumed some meaningful federal gun legislation would follow; instead the Manchin-Toomey background-check bill failed in the Senate in 2013 despite roughly ninety percent public support, establishing a template of legislative inaction after mass shootings that would repeat for a decade.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The blow-by-blow of the Obama-Romney campaign ("binders full of women," the Etch-a-Sketch metaphor, "you didn't build that," Big Bird, and the three debates)Obama's second term became a story of executive action, ACA implementation, and confrontation with a Tea Party House rather than of anything specifically litigated in the campaign, and Romney himself receded from Republican politics almost immediately.
    The fiscal-cliff panic of November and DecemberFront pages ran countdown clocks warning of a sudden austerity shock, but the eventual American Taxpayer Relief Act simply made most Bush tax cuts permanent and postponed the sequester by two months, after which the deficit debate went dormant for a decade.
    The "Kony 2012" viral videoWatched over 100 million times in six days, it briefly convinced many college students that the Lord's Resistance Army was the pressing moral issue of the moment, but filmmaker Jason Russell's public breakdown weeks later and Kony's continued freedom left it a cautionary tale about virality without follow-through.
    The Chick-fil-A "appreciation day," the Susan G. Komen/Planned Parenthood grant flap, the Sandra Fluke/Rush Limbaugh contraception fight, and Todd Akin's "legitimate rape" commentTreated as culture-defining and briefly election-moving, none of them altered the underlying policy trajectory on gay marriage, reproductive health, or abortion politics.
    David Petraeus's resignation as CIA Director over an extramarital affairIt ended a prominent career but did not change the agency, the war, or the political landscape, and is now mostly cited as a pre-Snowden footnote about how easily digital exhaust can expose officials.
    The NFL replacement-referee "Fail Mary" call and the Mayan-calendar "end of the world" jokeBoth held the top of the news cycle for days, but the former was resolved by the league's swift return of its regular officials and the latter, obviously, by the world not ending.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Mario Draghi's July 26 "whatever it takes" speech in LondonCovered in the U.S. business press as a technical central-bank story, it is now regarded as one of the most consequential monetary events of the century, ending the acute eurozone crisis and providing the template for a decade of central-bank crisis interventions.
    The Federal Reserve's September 13 announcement of QE3Treated as a specialist story, it quietly institutionalized the market "put" on asset prices that would define the 2010s bull market and, later, the 2020-21 pandemic-era interventions.
    Facebook's April acquisition of Instagram for $1 billionWidely mocked as overpayment for a thirteen-employee photo app with no revenue, it became within a decade the anchor of Meta's advertising business and the platform that redefined youth culture and the visual language of the smartphone era.
    The Trayvon Martin shooting and George Zimmerman's delayed arrestFramed at the time mainly as a Florida gun-law and Stand Your Ground controversy, it became the incubator of Black Lives Matter after Zimmerman's 2013 acquittal, and the pattern of viral video, protest, and racial reckoning it established defined the following decade.
    Xi Jinping's ascension as General Secretary at the 18th Party CongressCovered as a routine handoff, it produced the single most consequential leadership change in the world during the 2010s, reshaping U.S.-China relations for the following decade and beyond.
    Obama's DACA memorandum and his ABC News endorsement of same-sex marriageBoth received far less coverage than their long-run weight warranted, reshaping the immigration debate for a decade and marking the moment elite Democratic opinion consolidated permanently on marriage equality three years before Obergefell.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Obama Wins Second Term, Defeats Romney 332-206 in Electoral College
    2. Roberts Casts Deciding Vote as Supreme Court Upholds Obama's Health Law
    3. "Whatever It Takes": Draghi Vows to Save the Euro
    4. Fed Launches Open-Ended Bond Buying in QE3
    5. Twenty Children Killed in Newtown School Massacre
    6. Superstorm Sandy Batters Northeast Days Before Election
    7. Ambassador Stevens Killed in Attack on U.S. Consulate in Benghazi
    8. Facebook Stumbles Out of the Gate in $104 Billion Nasdaq Debut
    9. Twelve Dead as Gunman Opens Fire at Aurora, Colorado, Movie Theater
    10. Phelps Retires as Most Decorated Olympian Ever at London Games

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryEastman Kodak filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on January 19, a symbolic end to the film-photography era, while the Republican primary opened with Rick Santorum's razor-thin (later recount-confirmed) Iowa win, Mitt Romney's New Hampshire victory, and Newt Gingrich's fiery South Carolina upset. On January 18, Wikipedia, Google, and Reddit joined a mass online blackout against the SOPA and PIPA anti-piracy bills, an unprecedented internet protest that helped kill both in Congress within days.
    FebruaryWhitney Houston died on February 11, casting a pall over Grammy weekend, and the New York Giants beat the New England Patriots 21-17 in Super Bowl XLVI. Congress reauthorized the payroll-tax cut and unemployment benefits on February 22, unemployment stood at 8.3 percent, the Dow closed above 13,000 for the first time since 2008 on February 21, and on February 26 seventeen-year-old Trayvon Martin was shot and killed in Sanford, Florida, by neighborhood-watch volunteer George Zimmerman, whose delayed arrest would ignite nationwide protest that spring.
    MarchThe Kony 2012 video, released March 5 by Invisible Children, was viewed over 100 million times within a week before collapsing into controversy after its co-director's public breakdown. President Obama remarked on March 23 that if he had a son, "he'd look like Trayvon," and days later, at the Seoul Nuclear Security Summit on March 26, an open microphone caught him telling outgoing Russian President Medvedev he would have "more flexibility" on missile defense after the election, a line Romney would use against him for months. Attorney General Holder publicly defended the legal basis for drone strikes against U.S. citizens abroad in a March speech at Northwestern.
    AprilCongress passed the JOBS Act, signed April 5, easing securities rules and legalizing equity crowdfunding, and the STOCK Act banning congressional insider trading was signed April 4. Chinese dissident Chen Guangcheng made a dramatic escape to the U.S. embassy in Beijing, briefly straining relations during Secretary Clinton's visit, and Facebook agreed to acquire Instagram for $1 billion, a deal widely mocked at the time. Romney effectively clinched the Republican nomination by late April as Santorum suspended his campaign.
    MayObama became the first sitting president to endorse same-sex marriage in a May 9 ABC News interview, days after Vice President Biden's own remarks accelerated the shift. Obama signed the U.S.-Afghanistan Enduring Strategic Partnership Agreement in Kabul on May 2, and Facebook's long-awaited IPO on May 18 priced at $38 a share for a $104 billion valuation, only to break issue within days amid a Nasdaq trading glitch. JPMorgan disclosed roughly $2 billion in "London Whale" trading losses at its Chief Investment Office, a figure that would balloon in subsequent months, and Vladimir Putin was inaugurated to a new term as Russian president amid mass protests.
    JuneThe Supreme Court upheld the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate 5-4 in NFIB v. Sebelius on June 28, with Chief Justice Roberts casting the deciding vote, while striking down the law's mandatory Medicaid expansion; the same day, the House voted to hold Attorney General Holder in criminal contempt over the Fast and Furious investigation. Obama announced the DACA program on June 15, shielding young undocumented immigrants from deportation. The Supreme Court's Arizona v. United States ruling on June 25 struck down most of Arizona's SB 1070 immigration law. Moody's downgraded 15 major global banks on June 21, Barclays settled Libor-rigging charges for $453 million on June 27, and Defense Secretary Panetta announced the formal "pivot to Asia" naval rebalancing.
    JulyA gunman killed 12 and wounded 70 at a midnight screening of The Dark Knight Rises in Aurora, Colorado, on July 20. Mario Draghi's July 26 "whatever it takes" speech in London ended the acute phase of the eurozone crisis and ignited a global risk-on rally. Congress passed the MAP-21 transportation reauthorization, signed July 6, Frank Ocean released Channel Orange on July 10 days after a candid Tumblr post, PSY uploaded "Gangnam Style" to YouTube on July 15, and the London Olympics opened July 27.
    AugustThe London Olympics ran through August 12, with Michael Phelps retiring as the most decorated Olympian in history and Gabby Douglas becoming the first Black woman to win the individual all-around gymnastics gold. Knight Capital's runaway trading algorithm generated $440 million in losses in 45 minutes on August 1, effectively destroying the firm. Romney selected Congressman Paul Ryan as his running mate on August 11 ahead of the Tampa convention, Todd Akin's "legitimate rape" remark effectively ended his Missouri Senate campaign, a gunman killed six at a Sikh temple in Oak Creek, Wisconsin on August 5, and the Chick-fil-A "appreciation day" controversy over the company's stance on same-sex marriage dominated the culture pages.
    SeptemberMilitants attacked the U.S. diplomatic compound in Benghazi, Libya on September 11, killing Ambassador J. Christopher Stevens and three other Americans, the same day protesters breached the U.S. embassy in Cairo. The Federal Reserve launched QE3, an open-ended $40 billion-a-month bond-buying program, on September 13. The Chicago Teachers Union struck for the first time in 25 years from September 10-18, Apple's iPhone 5 launched September 21 alongside the widely panned Apple Maps, Apple's stock peaked near $702 making it briefly the most valuable public company in history, and the NFL's replacement-referee "Fail Mary" call in Seattle on September 24 ended the league's officiating lockout.
    OctoberThe first presidential debate in Denver on October 3 was widely judged a clear win for Romney and tightened the race, before the second debate at Hofstra and the third, foreign-policy-focused debate in Boca Raton restored Obama's footing. Malala Yousafzai was shot by the Pakistani Taliban on October 9, and Lance Armstrong was stripped of his seven Tour de France titles by USADA that same month. Superstorm Sandy made landfall in the Northeast October 22-30, devastating the Jersey Shore, flooding lower Manhattan and the subway system, and producing the widely noted image of Governor Chris Christie touring damage alongside President Obama. Kendrick Lamar's good kid, m.A.A.d city and Taylor Swift's Red were both released October 22.
    NovemberBarack Obama defeated Mitt Romney on November 6, winning 332 electoral votes to 206 and roughly 51.1 percent of the popular vote; Democrats gained two Senate seats including Elizabeth Warren's win in Massachusetts and Tammy Baldwin's historic win in Wisconsin. The same day, Colorado and Washington voters legalized recreational marijuana and Maine, Maryland, and Washington approved same-sex marriage at the ballot box. CIA Director David Petraeus resigned November 9 after an exposed affair, Xi Jinping was named General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party at the 18th Party Congress, Obama made a landmark visit to Burma/Myanmar, Hostess Brands announced liquidation, and Apple's iPad mini launched November 2.
    DecemberA gunman killed 20 first-graders and 6 educators at Sandy Hook Elementary School in Newtown, Connecticut on December 14, reopening the national gun-control debate and prompting Vice President Biden's task force. The Federal Reserve expanded QE3 to $85 billion a month on December 12 and introduced the numerical "Evans Rule" forward guidance, and "Gangnam Style" became the first YouTube video to reach one billion views on December 21, the same day the Mayan-calendar "apocalypse" failed to occur. Congress passed the Magnitsky Act, and fiscal-cliff negotiations between the White House and congressional Republicans ran to the final hours, resolved only by the American Taxpayer Relief Act signed just after midnight on January 1, 2013.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth~2.2-2.3% real GDP growth (annual average); Q4/Q4 growth closer to 2.0%, with an anemic 0.1% annualized Q4 print
    InflationHeadline CPI +1.7% year-over-year in December 2012 (down from 3.0% in December 2011); core CPI +1.9%
    Unemployment8.3% in January falling to 7.8% in December (initial print; later benchmarked to 7.9%), first sustained sub-8% readings since early 2009
    S&P 500 return+13.4% price return (~16.0% total return); closed at 1,426.19
    Dow return+7.3% price return (~10.2% total return); closed at 13,104.14
    Fed funds rate0-0.25% target range all year; Fed introduced the numerical "Evans Rule" forward guidance in December, tying rates to a 6.5% unemployment threshold
    Oil priceWTI crude closed at $91.82/barrel (down from $98.83 at end-2011); Brent averaged a record ~$112
    Gold priceGold closed near $1,675/oz, up roughly 7% for a twelfth consecutive annual gain
    Population~314 million (Census Bureau estimate)
    Median home priceMedian existing-home price rose ~11% to roughly $180,000 as the Case-Shiller 20-City index bottomed and rose 6-7% through December
    National debtGross federal debt crossed $16 trillion in early September, ending near $16.4 trillion (~100-103% of GDP); FY2012 deficit ~$1.09 trillion, the fourth consecutive year above $1 trillion

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2012 was to live inside an election that seemed to swallow the entire calendar and then, in its final month, to be reminded with brutal force of how much lay outside politics altogether. For most of the year the dominant emotional current was cautious, hard-won relief: the unemployment rate that had haunted the country since 2009 finally dropped below 8 percent, the housing market that had defined the crisis finally turned upward, mortgage rates fell to levels no one alive had ever seen, and the stock market climbed steadily enough that even people who did not follow it closely sensed that the worst of the crisis was, at last, behind them. That relief coexisted with real exhaustion. The Republican primary's parade of debates, the ceaseless attack ads in swing states, the "47 percent" video, the three presidential debates dissected minute by minute on cable, all of it produced a sense of a country talking mostly to itself, at enormous volume, about a contest whose outcome, once decided on November 6, resolved with unusual speed and grace given how bitter the campaign had been.

    Culturally, 2012 had a lightness that would look, from later years, almost innocent: Gangnam Style as a genuinely delightful global joke, the Avengers filling multiplexes with an optimism about American pop culture's continuing dominance, Michael Phelps and Gabby Douglas giving the country two weeks of unambiguous pride at the London Olympics, and a new photo app called Instagram quietly becoming part of daily life for millions of people who had no idea Facebook had just bought it for a sum that felt absurd. For many Americans, especially younger ones, the year also carried a current of hope on social issues that had felt immovable a decade earlier: a sitting president endorsing gay marriage, three states approving it at the ballot box for the first time, and two states voting to legalize marijuana, developments that read as generational, not partisan, in a way that few political stories of the year did.

    And then December arrived, and Sandy Hook made all of it feel small. The murder of twenty children in their first-grade classroom, following on the heels of the Aurora theater shooting five months earlier, produced a kind of grief that briefly united a divided country in shock, and a widespread, almost universal assumption that this, finally, was the atrocity that would force Congress to act. That assumption would prove wrong within months, but at the close of 2012 itself, Americans did not yet know that; they knew only that a year which had begun with cautious optimism about jobs and housing, moved through a bruising but resolvable election, and delivered genuine cultural joys along the way, was ending with the country's oldest unresolved argument, over guns and children and what a society owes its most vulnerable members, reopened in the rawest possible way. It was a year, in the end, of two truths sitting uneasily together: that things were, by the numbers, getting better, and that no number could explain what had happened in Newtown.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2013
    Econ +1Mark +3Poli -2Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The American economy in 2013 turned in a performance that read far better in hindsight than it felt in real time. Real GDP grew a modest 1.8 percent on a Q4/Q4 basis, held back by fiscal drag from the expiration of the payroll-tax holiday, the March 1 sequester's roughly $85 billion in automatic spending cuts, and a sixteen-day government shutdown in October. Beneath that sluggish headline, however, private-sector momentum was clearly firming: housing starts and home prices posted their strongest gains since the mid-2000s, auto sales hit their best pace since 2007, and unemployment fell a full point, from 8.0 to 6.7 percent. Financial markets, for their part, had one of the best years of the postwar era — the S&P 500 broke its 2007 record in March and finished with a total return near 32 percent, even as the bond market suffered its worst year since 1994 and gold crashed 28 percent, all set against Ben Bernanke's clumsy telegraphing of the eventual end of quantitative easing, which triggered the summer's "taper tantrum" before the Fed unexpectedly held off tapering until December.

    Politically, 2013 was a year of dysfunction and revelation in roughly equal measure. Obama's second inauguration, with its unusually progressive invocation of Seneca Falls, Selma, and Stonewall, gave way almost immediately to a Republican House that would produce the least productive Congress in modern memory: Manchin-Toomey's post-Newtown background-check compromise died in the Senate in April, comprehensive immigration reform passed the Senate only to die in the House, and the year's fiscal fights culminated in a sixteen-day shutdown and near debt-ceiling default in October. A spring "scandal season" of IRS targeting of conservative groups and secret DOJ seizures of reporters' phone records gave way in June to Edward Snowden's revelations of NSA mass surveillance, a story with consequences the country is still absorbing. The Supreme Court struck down the Defense of Marriage Act and gutted the Voting Rights Act's preclearance formula in the same week, the Boston Marathon bombing brought terrorism back to American soil, and the acquittal of George Zimmerman in July gave rise, almost unnoticed at the time, to a Facebook hashtag called Black Lives Matter. Abroad, Obama's retreat from a threatened Syria strike into a Russian-brokered chemical-weapons deal was read as a blow to American credibility, even as quiet diplomacy with Iran produced a genuine breakthrough in November.

    Culturally, the year had unusual vitality even amid the political gloom: Breaking Bad's finale and Netflix's first original series reset the terms of prestige television, Beyoncé's surprise self-titled album rewrote release strategy, and Pope Francis's election felt like an authentic changing of the guard. Underneath the headlines that dominated 2013 — the shutdown, the bombing, the papal transition, a viral summer of twerking and royal babies — ran a set of quieter developments that would prove far more consequential: Xi Jinping's consolidation of power in China, Bitcoin's first real bull run, a Facebook post that became a movement, and an NSA contractor's leaked documents that would reshape the relationship between American technology companies and their own government for a generation. Judged by the standards of its own moment, 2013 felt like gridlock, exhaustion, and a slow, uneven recovery; judged by what it set in motion, it stands as one of the decade's genuine hinge years.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Growth was subpar and inflation uncomfortably low, but private-sector momentum accelerated through the year despite real fiscal drag, and the labor market, housing, and household balance sheets all improved materially.
    Markets+3A roughly 32 percent S&P 500 total return with low realized volatility, a decisive breakout above 2007 highs, and broad participation across sectors made 2013 one of the strongest equity years of the postwar era.
    Politics-2A sixteen-day shutdown, debt-ceiling brinkmanship, the least productive Congress in modern memory, a botched health-care rollout, and a cascade of surveillance and scandal revelations drove congressional approval to single digits and eroded trust in federal institutions.
    Culture+1Landmark television and music, marriage equality's tipping point, and the birth of Black Lives Matter gave the year real creative and social vitality, offset by the Boston bombing, Newtown's political failure, and surveillance-driven erosion of trust.
    Overall Mood+0A booming stock market and a healing, if unglamorous, economy were counterbalanced by historic legislative dysfunction, a domestic terror attack, and the unsettling scope of the Snowden revelations, leaving a genuinely mixed and somewhat exhausted national mood.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Boston Marathon bombing kills three and wounds hundreds; Watertown manhunt locks down the city
    2. Edward Snowden reveals sweeping NSA surveillance programs and flees to Russia
    3. Supreme Court strikes down the Defense of Marriage Act and guts the Voting Rights Act in the same week
    4. George Zimmerman acquitted in the killing of Trayvon Martin; Black Lives Matter is born in the aftermath
    5. Sixteen-day federal government shutdown over the Affordable Care Act brings the country to the brink of default
    6. HealthCare.gov's launch collapses, badly damaging the rollout of Obama's signature health law
    7. Pope Benedict XVI resigns, first pontiff to do so in nearly six centuries; Francis elected in his place
    8. Senate rejects the Manchin-Toomey background-check bill months after the Newtown massacre
    9. Obama pulls back from striking Syria after chemical attacks; Russia brokers removal of Assad's weapons stockpile
    10. Detroit files the largest municipal bankruptcy in United States history

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The American economy in 2013 turned in a workmanlike performance that felt better in hindsight than it did in real time. Real GDP grew roughly 1.8 percent for the full year on a Q4/Q4 basis (about 2.2 percent on an annual-average basis after later BEA revisions), a deceleration from 2012 widely blamed on fiscal drag: the January expiration of the payroll-tax holiday effectively raised payroll taxes on most workers, the March 1 sequester imposed roughly $85 billion of automatic federal spending cuts, and the sixteen-day government shutdown in October shaved an estimated 0.2 to 0.4 percentage points off fourth-quarter growth. Underlying private-sector momentum was nonetheless clearly firming over the course of the year: housing starts rose to about 925,000 units, auto sales climbed back above 15.5 million for their best pace since 2007, and by year-end forecasters were talking about genuine "escape velocity" heading into 2014.

    Important Statistics

    Full-year real GDP growth came in at approximately 1.8 percent Q4/Q4 (about 2.2 percent annual-average per later revisions). Headline CPI rose about 1.5 percent year-over-year in December 2013 (core CPI about 1.7 percent), well below the Federal Reserve's 2 percent target and a source of ongoing disinflation concern. The unemployment rate fell from 8.0 percent in January to 6.7 percent in December, though the labor-force participation rate also dropped, from about 63.6 to 62.8 percent, meaning much of the improvement reflected workers leaving the labor force rather than finding jobs; the economy added roughly 2.3 million payroll jobs over the year. The federal funds target remained at 0 to 0.25 percent all year, while the 10-year Treasury yield rose from about 1.76 percent in January to roughly 3.03 percent by year-end. The 30-year fixed mortgage rate climbed from about 3.35 to 4.48 percent; the S&P/Case-Shiller 20-city home-price index rose about 13.4 percent year-over-year, the strongest annual gain since 2005, and the median existing-home price rose about 11 percent to roughly $197,000. WTI crude ended the year near $98.42 a barrel and Brent near $110; gold closed near $1,205 an ounce, down about 28 percent, and silver near $19.50, down about 36 percent. Total federal debt crossed roughly $17.35 trillion by year-end, up from about $16.4 trillion, while the FY2013 deficit fell to about $680 billion (4.1 percent of GDP), the first sub-trillion-dollar deficit since 2008.

    Major Events

    The year opened with the January 1 American Taxpayer Relief Act, which resolved the "fiscal cliff" by making most Bush-era tax rates permanent while letting the payroll-tax holiday lapse and raising the top marginal rate to 39.6 percent. The March 1 sequestration cuts took effect after Congress failed to reach a broader deal, producing furloughs across federal agencies and a visible drag on defense spending. Ben Bernanke's May 22 congressional testimony and June 19 FOMC press conference, in which he signaled the Fed could begin "tapering" its $85-billion-a-month asset purchases, triggered the "taper tantrum," sending the 10-year Treasury yield from about 1.66 percent in early May to just over 3 percent by early September; on September 18 the FOMC surprised markets by declining to taper after all. The October 1–17 government shutdown coincided with brinkmanship over the debt ceiling that was resolved only hours before Treasury's projected default date. On October 9 Obama nominated Janet Yellen to succeed Bernanke, and on December 18 the FOMC, in Bernanke's last meeting as chair, finally announced the taper, trimming purchases to $75 billion a month. The October 1 launch of the HealthCare.gov federal insurance exchange went disastrously wrong, a failure whose effects were more political than macroeconomic. The July 18 Detroit municipal bankruptcy, the largest in U.S. history at roughly $18–$20 billion, rattled the municipal-bond market and coincided with a sharp second-half slide in Puerto Rico's bonds amid deepening fiscal concerns there.

    Why this score

    A subpar full-year growth rate and uncomfortably low inflation kept the recovery from feeling complete, but private-sector momentum clearly accelerated over the course of the year despite meaningful fiscal drag, and the labor market, housing, and household balance sheets all improved materially, justifying a modestly positive score.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    Equities began a historic run in 2013: the S&P 500 broke its 2007 nominal record on March 28 and never looked back, finishing the year with a total return of about 32.4 percent (roughly 29.6 percent price-only), its best calendar year since 1997. The Dow Jones Industrial Average returned roughly 29.7 percent on a total-return basis and the NASDAQ Composite gained roughly 38 percent, with volatility unusually subdued (the VIX averaged in the low teens). The bond market, by contrast, suffered its worst year since 1994, with the Barclays U.S. Aggregate down about 2 percent as the taper-tantrum rate surge crushed duration, and emerging markets — the so-called "Fragile Five" of Brazil, India, Indonesia, South Africa, and Turkey — saw sharp currency and equity sell-offs over the summer. Gold suffered its worst year in more than three decades, falling roughly 28 percent to close near $1,205 an ounce after an April 12–15 flash crash that was one of the largest two-day drops on record.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    The IPO market roared back, headlined by Twitter's November 7 debut (priced at $26, closing the year near $63), alongside high-profile deals from Hilton Worldwide, Zoetis (Pfizer's animal-health spin-off), and Coty. Major M&A included Berkshire Hathaway and 3G Capital's $28 billion take-private of H.J.
