Heinz, Michael Dell and Silver Lake's roughly $25 billion take-private of Dell, and Verizon's $130 billion buyout of Vodafone's 45 percent stake in Verizon Wireless. Bitcoin had its breakout year, rising from about $13 in January to a November peak above $1,100 before crashing on the December closure of BTC China withdrawals and ending the year near $750. Detroit's July 18 Chapter 9 filing, the largest municipal bankruptcy in U.S. history, was the year's marquee corporate-style failure in an otherwise recovering environment.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was a rotation out of bonds and gold and into equities as growth expectations firmed and the Fed's eventual exit from quantitative easing came into view. The taper tantrum showed how sensitive global capital flows had become to the smallest signals from the Federal Reserve, hammering emerging-market currencies and bonds over the summer even as U.S. equities kept climbing. Municipal credit came under scrutiny after Detroit's bankruptcy and Puerto Rico's second-half bond slide, while the IPO market's revival, capped by Twitter's debut, and Bitcoin's emergence as a genuine, if disreputable, asset class both signaled a market newly willing to price speculative growth stories again.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Barack Obama was sworn in for his second term on January 20 (public ceremony January 21), delivering an unusually progressive inaugural address that invoked Seneca Falls, Selma, and Stonewall, even as a near-total turnover of his national-security and economic team got underway: John Kerry at State, Chuck Hagel at Defense after becoming the first Cabinet nominee ever filibustered, Jack Lew at Treasury, and Susan Rice as National Security Advisor after her U.N. nomination for State was torpedoed by Benghazi criticism. The March 1 sequester's $85 billion in automatic cuts became the fiscal backdrop for the rest of the year. On April 15, the Boston Marathon bombing killed three and wounded roughly 264, triggering an unprecedented shelter-in-place order during the manhunt for the Tsarnaev brothers. In early May, an inspector general's report revealed IRS targeting of conservative groups' tax-exempt applications, and the Justice Department was found to have secretly seized AP reporters' phone records and labeled a Fox News reporter a possible co-conspirator in a leak case. Beginning June 5, Edward Snowden's leaks to The Guardian and The Washington Post revealed the NSA's bulk telephony metadata collection and the PRISM program, triggering a global diplomatic firestorm. In late June the Supreme Court decided three era-defining cases in one week: Shelby County v. Holder struck down the Voting Rights Act's preclearance coverage formula, United States v. Windsor struck down DOMA's Section 3, and Hollingsworth v. Perry effectively restored same-sex marriage in California. On July 13, George Zimmerman was acquitted in the killing of Trayvon Martin, and in the days that followed Alicia Garza, Patrisse Cullors, and Opal Tometi coined the phrase and hashtag Black Lives Matter. Syrian chemical-weapons attacks in Ghouta on August 21 led Obama to seek, and effectively abandon, congressional authorization for strikes, resolved instead through a Russian-brokered deal to remove Assad's declared stockpile. A sixteen-day government shutdown from October 1 to 17, driven by House Republican demands to defund the Affordable Care Act, coincided with the disastrous October 1 launch of HealthCare.gov and brought the country to the edge of a debt-ceiling default.

    Domestic Policy

    Obama's second-term domestic agenda ran into a wall of Republican House opposition almost immediately. The post-Newtown gun-control push reached its Senate vote on April 17, when the bipartisan Manchin-Toomey background-check compromise failed 54–46, six votes short of cloture, a defeat Obama called "a pretty shameful day for Washington." Comprehensive immigration reform fared briefly better: the "Gang of Eight" Senate bill combining a path to citizenship with dramatic border-enforcement increases passed 68–32 on June 27, but Speaker Boehner refused to bring it to the House floor and it died there. Legislation that did pass included the Violence Against Women Reauthorization Act, extending protections to LGBT victims, Native Americans, and immigrants, and the December 26 Bipartisan Budget Act (Ryan-Murray), which set two years of spending levels and partially rolled back sequestration — the first true budget agreement in years. The 113th Congress otherwise became one of the least productive in modern history, enacting only 72 laws in 2013. The most consequential procedural change came on November 21, when Majority Leader Harry Reid deployed the "nuclear option," eliminating the 60-vote filibuster threshold for executive-branch and non-Supreme-Court judicial nominations, a change that would accelerate confirmations and set a precedent Republicans would extend to Supreme Court nominees in 2017.

    Foreign Policy

    The overarching second-term theme was rebalancing away from the wars of the 2000s, but the year turned out to be dominated by unwanted crises. The Syrian civil war produced the Ghouta chemical attacks and the awkward pivot from threatened strikes to a Russia-brokered chemical-weapons agreement that removed roughly 1,300 tons of declared agents but left Assad in power and the U.S. with diminished leverage, while ISIS was quietly consolidating in eastern Syria and western Iraq. The year's signature diplomatic achievement was the Iran interim nuclear deal, the Joint Plan of Action signed in Geneva on November 24 by the P5+1 and Iran after the June election of reformer Hassan Rouhani, freezing most enrichment above 5 percent and providing limited sanctions relief in exchange for enhanced inspections. Obama and Xi Jinping held a shirtsleeves summit at Sunnylands, California, in June, but the Snowden revelations complicated relations with Beijing, Moscow (whose asylum grant to Snowden led Obama to cancel a September summit with Putin), and European allies after reports the NSA had tapped Angela Merkel's cellphone. Egypt's July 3 military coup against Mohamed Morsi, China's November declaration of an Air Defense Identification Zone over the East China Sea, and continued but reduced-pace drone strikes in Pakistan and Yemen rounded out a turbulent year abroad.

    Important Elections

    2013 was an off-year with no federal general elections, but several contests carried significant signal. In Virginia, Democrat Terry McAuliffe defeated Republican Ken Cuccinelli 47.8 to 45.2 percent, the first time in 40 years Virginia elected a governor of the sitting president's party. In New Jersey, Republican Chris Christie won reelection in a landslide, 60 to 38 percent, briefly making him the presumed 2016 GOP frontrunner. In New York City, Public Advocate Bill de Blasio crushed Republican Joe Lhota 73 to 24 percent, ending 20 years of Republican and independent mayors. Ed Markey won the Massachusetts Senate special election to fill John Kerry's seat, and Newark Mayor Cory Booker won the New Jersey Senate special election to fill the seat of the late Frank Lautenberg. The collective read was that shutdown backlash was real and helped Democrats where it mattered, while Christie's landslide showed a plausible, if short-lived, GOP path through center-right pragmatism.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    The Best Picture Oscar for 2012 films went to Argo at the February 24 ceremony. For films released in 2013, the dominant conversation was Steve McQueen's 12 Years a Slave, starring Chiwetel Ejiofor and breakout Lupita Nyong'o, which would win Best Picture the following year; Alfonso Cuarón's Gravity, with Sandra Bullock, was the year's technical achievement and grossed over $700 million globally. David O. Russell's American Hustle, Martin Scorsese's The Wolf of Wall Street, and Spike Jonze's Her, a remarkably prescient story of a man falling in love with an AI, rounded out the prestige conversation. Box office was led by Disney's Frozen, Iron Man 3, The Hunger Games: Catching Fire, Despicable Me 2, and Man of Steel, while Ryan Coogler's Fruitvale Station, released weeks after Zimmerman's acquittal, hit with unusual force, and Matthew McConaughey and Jared Leto advanced the "McConaissance" in Dallas Buyers Club.

    Music

    The two dominant albums were Daft Punk's Random Access Memories, whose lead single "Get Lucky" became the summer's ubiquitous song, and Beyoncé's self-titled BEYONCÉ, a surprise, no-promotion visual album drop in December that rewrote industry release strategy. Kanye West's Yeezus was his most abrasive and divisive statement, while Vampire Weekend, Arcade Fire, Drake, and Lorde, whose "Royals" topped the Hot 100 at age 16, all released landmark work. The Billboard year-end No. 1 was Macklemore and Ryan Lewis's "Thrift Shop," while "Blurred Lines" by Robin Thicke, Pharrell, and T.I. was the summer's inescapable and later legally contentious smash. Streaming, led by Spotify, was becoming ubiquitous in the U.S.

    Television

    Breaking Bad's final eight episodes captured the culture like no drama since The Sopranos, its September 29 finale a genuine national event. Netflix launched its original-programming era with House of Cards in February, the first release-all-at-once binge-watching template, and Orange Is the New Black in July. Game of Thrones aired its "Red Wedding" in June, Scandal made Kerry Washington a live-tweet phenomenon, and Duck Dynasty peaked at nearly 12 million viewers before a December controversy over Phil Robertson's remarks in GQ. Jimmy Fallon's move to The Tonight Show was announced in April, and 30 Rock ended its run in January.

    Technology

    The iPhone 5s launched September 20 with Touch ID and a 64-bit chip, alongside iOS 7's radical flat-design overhaul. The PlayStation 4 and Xbox One both launched in November, opening the eighth console generation. Google Glass entered its Explorer beta, sparking a "Glasshole" backlash, while Snapchat rejected a reported $3 billion Facebook acquisition offer in November and Twitter's IPO priced at $26 on November 7. Bitcoin broke $1,000 for the first time in November before crashing, and the Tesla Model S was named Motor Trend's Car of the Year. The Snowden revelations pushed encryption from a niche technical concern into everyday consumer conversation.

    Social Trends

    Beyond the founding of Black Lives Matter and marriage-equality momentum, the "Fight for $15" fast-food-worker strikes escalated across cities beginning that summer, and the Moral Mondays protests in North Carolina opposed the state's rightward legislative turn. Immigration reform's Senate passage and House death defined the year's policy frustration, while Wendy Davis's pink-sneaker filibuster against Texas abortion restrictions went viral in June. Same-sex marriage was legal in roughly 17 states plus D.C. by year-end, and religious "nones" continued to rise toward roughly a fifth of American adults.

    Crime

    The Cleveland kidnappings ended May 6 when Amanda Berry escaped Ariel Castro's home after a decade of captivity; Castro hanged himself in prison that September. James "Whitey" Bulger was convicted in Boston in August, and the June arrest of NFL player Aaron Hernandez for the murder of Odin Lloyd shocked professional football. The September 16 Washington Navy Yard shooting killed 12, and the November 1 LAX shooting killed a TSA officer, the agency's first line-of-duty death.

    Sports

    The Baltimore Ravens beat the San Francisco 49ers 34–31 in Super Bowl XLVII, a game remembered for a 34-minute Superdome blackout. The Miami Heat beat the San Antonio Spurs in seven games in the NBA Finals, with Ray Allen's Game 6 corner three becoming one of the most famous shots in NBA history. The Chicago Blackhawks won the Stanley Cup, and the Boston Red Sox won the World Series in six games over the St. Louis Cardinals, a bearded, cathartic championship for a city still healing from the marathon bombing. Auburn's "Kick Six" against Alabama on November 30 was the play of the college football season, Alex Rodriguez was suspended 211 games in the Biogenesis PED scandal, and Jason Collins became the first active male athlete in a major U.S. pro sport to come out as gay.

    Public Mood

    The year felt suspended between the recovery's grinding slowness and the accelerating strangeness of the digital present. Unemployment fell from 8.0 to 6.7 percent, but wage growth stayed flat and a "recovery for the 1%" narrative hardened. The government shutdown and HealthCare.gov debacle produced peak disillusionment with Washington competence, and Obama's approval fell into the low 40s by year's end. Yet culturally the year felt generative: prestige TV at its apex, Beyoncé rewriting release strategy, marriage equality suddenly inevitable, and a new pope who felt genuinely new, even as Snowden's revelations forced a reckoning with surveillance and executive power that several year-end essays described as a "loss of innocence about the internet."

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    At the time, the American conversation was organized around the second-term Obama agenda: would ACA implementation survive, would immigration reform pass the House, would Manchin-Toomey break the post-Newtown gun-control logjam, and would Syria intervention happen. Foreign policy was framed through drones, "leading from behind," and the Iran opening; tech coverage fixated on Google Glass, 3-D printing, and whether Twitter's IPO would live up to Facebook's.

    What actually mattered

    What actually mattered was mostly happening off-camera: the platform economy locking in, with Uber's August Series C valuing it at $3.5 billion and Ethereum being drafted; a new protest infrastructure being born on Facebook and Twitter; China entering a fundamentally new strategic posture under Xi Jinping; Russia beginning its return as a Middle East power through the Syria chemical-weapons deal; and the question of whether liberal-democratic states could sustain both mass digital surveillance and public trust, an answer that would go on to shape everything from Brexit to January 6.

    Most forgotten story

    The Chelyabinsk meteor of February 15, 2013, a twenty-meter asteroid that exploded over Russia with about thirty times the energy of Hiroshima and injured roughly 1,500 people, mostly from shattered glass, was captured on hundreds of dashcams and briefly seemed poised to reset public thinking about planetary defense. It quietly did reshape the field, informing the U.N.'s International Asteroid Warning Network and NASA's later DART mission, but has otherwise vanished almost completely from public memory.

    Most underrated story

    The founding of the Black Lives Matter hashtag in the week after the Zimmerman verdict registered barely at all in mainstream press at the time, yet no 2013 event aged into more importance: it seeded the frame through which the country would process police killings for the next decade and provided the template for the hashtag-organized protest that would culminate in the largest demonstrations in American history in 2020.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The biggest hindsight surprise is how much of the surveillance architecture Snowden exposed in June 2013 not only survived but grew: the USA FREEDOM Act of 2015 trimmed one program at the edges, but commercial data brokers, ad-tech telemetry, and law-enforcement geofence warrants filled the gap several times over. The Snowden moment was treated at the time as a hinge; it turned out, in practice, to be closer to a speed bump.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The sixteen-day October government shutdown and debt-ceiling standoffIt did not repeal the Affordable Care Act, its stated purpose, and did not trigger default; it blends into a longer sequence of Boehner-era budget confrontations and its main lasting effect, reputational damage to House Republicans, faded within a year.
    The George Zimmerman trial as a courtroom dramaThe trial itself matters less in hindsight than what it catalyzed off-camera; Zimmerman receded into a series of tabloid arrests and "stand your ground" did not undergo the sweeping legal reform that seemed imminent that summer.
    The Boston Marathon bombing as a national-security turning pointA genuine tragedy that killed three and wounded hundreds, but as a policy or geopolitical hinge it produced no significant legislation, no war, and no doctrine shift, reading now as the last major post-9/11-style homeland-security spectacle rather than the start of a new era.
    The papal transition from Benedict XVI to FrancisHistorically notable, but the "Francis effect" pundits predicted, a mass Catholic revival and doctrinal liberalization, largely did not materialize, and Mass attendance continued to decline in the West.
    The year's viral obsessions, including the royal baby, Miley Cyrus's VMA performance, the Harlem Shake, and Manti Te'o's fake girlfriendThese were 2013's defining viral moments but are now footnotes, useful chiefly as a snapshot of the moment social media began setting the news agenda.
    The March Cyprus bank bail-inTreated at the time as a possible detonator of a renewed Eurozone crisis and a precedent for confiscating depositor funds, it turned out to be a contained event and the last acute chapter of the euro crisis rather than the first chapter of a new one.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Alicia Garza's Facebook post and the founding of #BlackLivesMatterIt registered barely at all in mainstream press in 2013 but became the organizing frame for Ferguson, Baltimore, and eventually the largest protest movement in U.S. history in 2020.
    Xi Jinping's consolidation of power and the launch of the "Chinese Dream" and Belt and Road conceptsWestern coverage treated him as a conventional technocratic successor to Hu Jintao, but almost every subsequent U.S.-China rupture, from the trade war to tech decoupling, traces to decisions set in motion this year.
    The long-tail consequences of Snowden's NSA disclosuresBeyond the immediate privacy debate, they triggered the encryption wars that pushed end-to-end encryption into consumer messaging, shattered U.S. moral authority on internet governance, and inaugurated an era in which a single leaker could reshape geopolitics.
    The Rana Plaza garment-factory collapse in BangladeshCovered at the time as a distant industrial accident that killed 1,134 workers, it became the reference point that reshaped global apparel supply chains and mainstreamed ESG-style supplier auditing across retail.
    Bitcoin's first true bull run, from about $13 in January to above $1,100 in NovemberMainstream press treated it as a novelty or a bubble, but it was the year cryptocurrency graduated into a recognized asset class, with the entire subsequent crypto and DeFi arc, including Ethereum's white paper, starting here.
    Snapchat's rejection of Facebook's roughly $3 billion acquisition offerReported as a punchline about arrogant founders, it became the moment the ephemeral-video and Stories format escaped Facebook's grasp, setting up the short-video pivot that would eventually culminate in TikTok's U.S. dominance.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Boston Marathon Bombing Kills Three, Wounds Hundreds; Manhunt Locks Down City for Days
    2. Pope Benedict XVI to Resign, First Pontiff to Step Down in Nearly Six Centuries; Cardinals Elect Francis of Buenos Aires
    3. N.S.A. Collecting Phone Records of Millions of Americans Daily — Contractor Edward Snowden Identifies Himself as Source
    4. Zimmerman Acquitted in Trayvon Martin Killing; Protests Erupt in Cities Across the Country
    5. Supreme Court Strikes Down Defense of Marriage Act, Guts Voting Rights Act Preclearance in Same Week
    6. Detroit Files for Bankruptcy, Largest U.S. City Ever to Do So
    7. Government Shuts Down as House Republicans Demand Health Law Delay; Default Feared as Debt Ceiling Nears
    8. HealthCare.gov Launch Falters; White House Concedes Site "Not Working" for Most Users
    9. Obama Pulls Back From Syria Strike After British Vote, Cuts Deal With Russia to Remove Assad's Chemical Weapons
    10. Nelson Mandela, Anti-Apartheid Icon and First Black President of South Africa, Dies at 95

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryObama was privately sworn in for his second term on January 20 (public ceremony January 21), delivering an unusually progressive inaugural address invoking Seneca Falls, Selma, and Stonewall. The American Taxpayer Relief Act, signed January 2, resolved the "fiscal cliff" by making most Bush-era tax rates permanent while letting the payroll-tax holiday expire. Alabama beat Notre Dame 42–14 in the BCS Championship on January 7, Vine launched January 24, and 30 Rock ended its run.
    FebruaryJohn Kerry was sworn in as Secretary of State February 1, while Chuck Hagel endured a bruising, historic filibuster before his eventual confirmation at Defense. Pope Benedict XVI stunned the world by announcing his resignation on February 11, the first papal abdication in nearly six hundred years. A twenty-meter meteor exploded over Chelyabinsk, Russia, on February 15, injuring roughly 1,500 people. The Baltimore Ravens beat the San Francisco 49ers 34–31 in a Super Bowl XLVII remembered for a 34-minute Superdome blackout, and Netflix launched House of Cards, its first major original series.
    MarchThe March 1 sequester imposed roughly $85 billion in automatic federal spending cuts after Congress failed to reach a broader deal, triggering furloughs across federal agencies. Jorge Mario Bergoglio was elected Pope Francis on March 13, becoming the first Latin American and first Jesuit pontiff. Hugo Chávez died on March 5, bringing Nicolás Maduro to power in Venezuela, and Xi Jinping formally assumed China's presidency. A Cyprus bank bail-in briefly threatened to reignite the Eurozone crisis, and the S&P 500 broke its 2007 nominal record on March 28. The Violence Against Women Reauthorization Act was signed March 7.
    AprilOn April 15, brothers Tamerlan and Dzhokhar Tsarnaev detonated two bombs near the Boston Marathon finish line, killing three and wounding roughly 264; an unprecedented shelter-in-place order locked down metropolitan Boston during the manhunt that followed. Two days later, the Senate rejected the Manchin-Toomey background-check compromise 54–46, a bitter defeat for the post-Newtown gun-control push. The April 24 collapse of the Rana Plaza garment factory in Bangladesh killed 1,134 workers, and Jason Collins became the first active male athlete in a major U.S. pro sport to publicly come out as gay.
    MayAn inspector general's report revealed the IRS had used keyword screens to flag conservative groups' tax-exempt applications for extra scrutiny, and the Justice Department was found to have secretly seized AP reporters' phone records as part of a leak investigation. Amanda Berry escaped Ariel Castro's Cleveland home on May 6 after a decade of captivity. Ben Bernanke's May 22 congressional testimony first raised the prospect of tapering the Fed's bond purchases, unsettling markets, and Yahoo announced its $1.1 billion acquisition of Tumblr.
    JuneBeginning June 5, Edward Snowden's leaks to The Guardian and The Washington Post revealed the NSA's bulk telephony metadata collection and the PRISM program; he identified himself as the source on June 9 before fleeing to Moscow. Bernanke's June 19 FOMC press conference deepened the "taper tantrum," sending Treasury yields sharply higher. In one landmark week, the Supreme Court struck down the Defense of Marriage Act in United States v. Windsor, gutted the Voting Rights Act's preclearance formula in Shelby County v. Holder, and effectively restored same-sex marriage in California in Hollingsworth v. Perry. The Senate passed the bipartisan "Gang of Eight" immigration bill 68–32 on June 27, and the Miami Heat beat the San Antonio Spurs in seven games for the NBA title, capped by Ray Allen's iconic Game 6 three-pointer.
    JulyGeorge Zimmerman was acquitted on July 13 in the killing of Trayvon Martin, and in the days that followed Alicia Garza, Patrisse Cullors, and Opal Tometi coined the phrase and hashtag Black Lives Matter. Detroit filed for Chapter 9 bankruptcy on July 18, the largest municipal bankruptcy in U.S. history. Egypt's military ousted President Mohamed Morsi in a coup on July 3, and Chelsea (then Bradley) Manning was convicted on July 30 in the WikiLeaks case.
    AugustMiley Cyrus's twerking performance with Robin Thicke at the MTV Video Music Awards on August 25 became the summer's defining viral moment. The 50th anniversary of the March on Washington was marked August 24, and James "Whitey" Bulger was convicted in Boston. Sarin gas attacks in Ghouta, Syria, killed over 1,000 civilians on August 21, crossing Obama's declared "red line" and prompting him to seek, on August 31, congressional authorization for strikes that would ultimately not occur. Alex Rodriguez was suspended 211 games in the Biogenesis doping scandal.
    SeptemberAs a congressional vote on Syria strikes headed toward defeat, Secretary of State Kerry's offhand remark about Assad surrendering chemical weapons became the basis for a Russian-brokered framework announced September 14. The Washington Navy Yard shooting killed 12 on September 16, and the FOMC surprised markets on September 18 by declining to taper its bond purchases after all. Ted Cruz's 21-hour Senate floor stand against the Affordable Care Act in late September set the stage for the shutdown to come, while Apple launched the iPhone 5s and iOS 7's flat-design overhaul, and Breaking Bad's finale aired September 29 to a national audience.
    OctoberThe federal government shut down for sixteen days beginning October 1, driven by House Republican demands to defund the Affordable Care Act, the same day HealthCare.gov launched and immediately crashed. The standoff, entangled with a looming debt-ceiling deadline, was resolved only hours before Treasury's projected default date on October 17. Obama nominated Janet Yellen to succeed Bernanke as Fed chair on October 9, Cory Booker won the New Jersey Senate special election, and the Boston Red Sox won the World Series in six games over the St. Louis Cardinals, a cathartic championship for a city still healing from the marathon bombing.
    NovemberIn off-year elections on November 5, Terry McAuliffe won the Virginia governorship, Chris Christie was reelected in New Jersey by a landslide, and Bill de Blasio was elected mayor of New York City. Twitter's IPO priced at $26 on November 7, the same day Obama apologized for the collapse of his "if you like your plan, you can keep it" promise. Majority Leader Harry Reid triggered the Senate's "nuclear option" on November 21, eliminating the filibuster for most nominations, and the Iran interim nuclear deal was signed in Geneva on November 24. China declared an Air Defense Identification Zone over the East China Sea on November 23, and the PlayStation 4 and Xbox One both launched, opening the eighth console generation.
    DecemberNelson Mandela died on December 5 at age 95, prompting a global outpouring of mourning and a memorial service attended by dozens of world leaders. The FOMC's December 18 meeting, Bernanke's last as chair, finally announced the taper of bond purchases. Congress passed the Ryan-Murray Bipartisan Budget Act, signed into law December 26, the first genuine two-party budget agreement in years. Beyoncé released her surprise, no-promotion self-titled visual album on December 13, and the year closed with the S&P 500 near record highs and unemployment down to 6.7 percent.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growthApproximately +1.8% (Q4/Q4); about +2.2% on an annual-average basis after later BEA revisions
    InflationHeadline CPI approximately +1.5% YoY in December 2013 (core CPI about +1.7%)
    UnemploymentFell from 8.0% in January to 6.7% in December; roughly 2.3 million payroll jobs added
    S&P 500 returnApproximately +32.4% total return (about +29.6% price only), best calendar year since 1997
    Dow returnApproximately +29.7% total return (about +26.5% price only)
    Fed funds rateHeld at 0-0.25% all year; first taper (to $75 billion/month in asset purchases) announced at the December 18 FOMC meeting
    Oil priceWTI ended the year near $98.42/barrel; Brent ended near $110/barrel
    Gold priceEnded the year near $1,205/oz, down about 28% for the year
    PopulationApproximately 316 million
    Median home priceNAR median existing-home price approximately $197,000 (+11% YoY); Case-Shiller 20-city index +13.4% YoY
    National debtGross federal debt approximately $17.35 trillion by year-end, up from about $16.4 trillion; FY2013 deficit about $680 billion (4.1% of GDP)

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2013 was to live in a country that felt, on the surface, exhausted by its own government and quietly unnerved by what it was learning about its own surveillance state, even as the underlying economy and stock market were staging one of the better years in a generation. The dominant emotional register of the year was gridlock: a Congress that would go on to pass only 72 laws, a sixteen-day shutdown that furloughed 800,000 workers over a health-care law that then failed spectacularly on its own website, and a president whose approval rating drifted into the low 40s by December. Layered on top of that was a genuine crisis of trust in institutions, as the spring's IRS and AP scandals gave way in June to Edward Snowden's revelations that the NSA was collecting the phone records of ordinary Americans by the hundreds of millions, a story with resonance across the entire political spectrum that made both Obama-voting liberals and libertarian-leaning conservatives uneasy about the reach of the post-9/11 security state.

    And yet, for anyone paying attention to their bank statement or their 401(k), 2013 also felt like the year the recovery finally became real. Home prices were rising at their fastest pace since before the crash, the stock market notched its best year since 1997, and the unemployment rate fell a full point over twelve months. Culturally, the year had an unusual charge to it: Breaking Bad ended its run as a genuine national event, Netflix's House of Cards and Orange Is the New Black suggested television was being reinvented in real time, Beyoncé dropped an album with no warning and changed how the music industry thought about release dates, and the Supreme Court's rulings on DOMA made marriage equality feel, for the first time, inevitable rather than aspirational. A new pope who spoke differently than his predecessors, a royal baby, a viral dance move, and a bombing at the Boston Marathon that united a wounded city all competed for the same shrinking span of American attention.

    What almost no one fully grasped at the time was how many of the year's quietest stories would prove to be its most important. A Facebook post written in the hours after George Zimmerman's acquittal would, within a decade, become the organizing language for the largest protest movement in American history. A Chinese leader largely dismissed as a cautious technocrat was already setting in motion the strategic rivalry that would define the following ten years, and a digital currency trading for pocket change in January was worth over a thousand dollars by November. The American of 2013, watching Ted Cruz talk for twenty-one hours and refreshing a broken HealthCare.gov page, could be forgiven for feeling that Washington had simply stopped working. What that same American could not have known was that the year's real business was being conducted almost entirely somewhere else.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2014
    Econ +2Mark +2Poli -1Cult +0Over -1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2014 was, by the numbers, the strongest year of the entire post-Great-Recession expansion, even though it did not always feel that way while it was happening. A brutal "polar vortex" winter dragged first-quarter GDP into a rare contraction, but growth then roared back to 4.6 percent in the second quarter and 5.0 percent in the third, the labor market added roughly three million jobs (the best calendar year since 1999), and unemployment fell from 6.7 percent to 5.6 percent. Janet Yellen was sworn in as the first woman to chair the Federal Reserve, which wound down its bond-buying program and ended QE3 in October even as it kept short-term rates at zero. The year's biggest economic surprise arrived in its second half, when a US shale boom and OPEC's decision to defend market share rather than cut output sent oil prices tumbling by nearly half, delivering American consumers a de facto tax cut even as it battered energy producers and, eventually, the Russian economy. Wall Street logged a sixth consecutive up year, punctuated by Alibaba's record-setting IPO and the busiest M&A calendar since 2007.

    Politically and geopolitically, however, 2014 unraveled whatever calm the Obama administration had hoped to find in its second term. Russia annexed Crimea and fomented a war in eastern Ukraine that culminated in the shooting-down of Malaysia Airlines Flight 17; a small but ruthless force calling itself the Islamic State swept through Iraq, captured Mosul, declared a caliphate, and beheaded American hostages on camera, drawing the United States back into a war it had just left; and an Ebola epidemic in West Africa produced a brief but intense domestic panic after cases reached Dallas. In November, Republicans rode a wave election to full control of Congress, their largest House majority since 1928 and their largest Senate gain since 1980, a repudiation that left Obama's approval mired in the low 40s. In the campaign's aftermath, Obama governed increasingly by executive action, shielding millions of undocumented parents from deportation and, most strikingly, announcing on December 17 the restoration of diplomatic relations with Cuba after fifty-three years of estrangement.

    At home, the killing of Michael Brown in Ferguson and the chokehold death of Eric Garner in New York, both followed by grand juries declining to indict the officers involved, ignited protests across the country and gave rise to Black Lives Matter as a mass movement, while the viral, life-affirming Ice Bucket Challenge and the darker, misogynistic harassment campaign known as Gamergate revealed the internet's competing capacities for good and for cruelty. A genuinely rich year in film, television, and music unfolded against this backdrop of fraying institutional trust. In hindsight, 2014 looks like the pivot year of the entire decade: the year great-power territorial war returned to Europe, transnational jihadism reasserted itself, an online reactionary politics found its proving ground, and a long era of quiet post-Cold-War assumptions began visibly to end -- even though, at the time, it mostly felt like a middling year of exotic bad news layered atop a finally-recovering economy.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+2Growth accelerated to the strongest full-year pace of the recovery, three million jobs were added and unemployment fell from 6.7% to 5.6%, the deficit shrank to its smallest share of GDP since 2007, and collapsing gasoline prices amounted to a real windfall for consumers, even as wage growth and labor-force participation stayed stubbornly weak.
    Markets+2A sixth consecutive up year for the S&P 500 with double-digit total returns, a record-breaking Alibaba IPO, and the busiest M&A calendar since 2007 were tempered by narrowing small-cap breadth, an October Treasury flash crash, and a brutal fourth-quarter rout in energy and high-yield credit once oil collapsed.
    Politics-1Divided government produced a handful of narrow bipartisan wins, but the year was defined by executive-legislative gridlock, a wave election handing Republicans full control of Congress, an escalation to unilateral executive action on immigration, and institutional strains from the CIA torture-report fight to Ferguson, though it never approached the acute dysfunction of 2013's shutdown.
    Culture+0A creatively vital year in film, television, and music and genuine civil-rights progress in marriage equality's rapid state-by-state expansion were counterbalanced by Ferguson, Eric Garner's death, ISIS beheadings, and the emergence of Gamergate, leaving vitality and fracture in roughly equal measure.
    Overall Mood-1Real gains in jobs, growth, and gas prices were overshadowed in the lived national mood by a cascade of unnerving shocks -- Ferguson, ISIS beheadings, an Ebola scare, a vanished airliner, and a repudiating midterm election -- that left Americans more anxious and less trusting of institutions than the economic data alone would suggest.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Republicans capture the Senate and expand their House majority in a midterm wave election
    2. The Islamic State seizes Mosul, declares a caliphate, and beheads American hostages on camera
    3. Russia annexes Crimea and fuels a war in eastern Ukraine; Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 is shot down
    4. Michael Brown's killing sparks unrest in Ferguson, and Eric Garner's death fuels a national reckoning over policing
    5. Ebola reaches the United States, sickening health workers in Dallas and triggering a national scare
    6. The Ice Bucket Challenge becomes a global viral sensation, raising over $100 million for ALS research
    7. Obama and Raul Castro announce the restoration of US-Cuba diplomatic relations after 53 years
    8. Oil prices collapse by nearly half amid a US shale boom and OPEC's refusal to cut production
    9. Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 vanishes with 239 aboard, launching a monthslong, unresolved search
    10. The Senate releases its report on CIA torture as North Korea-linked hackers breach Sony Pictures

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The United States entered 2014 in the sixth year of the post-Great Recession expansion, with the Federal Reserve still holding short-term rates at zero and running the third round of quantitative easing. The year began stumbling: an unusually brutal "polar vortex" winter helped drag first-quarter real GDP into an annualized contraction of roughly one to two percent, reviving talk of "secular stagnation." Growth then roared back to 4.6 percent in the second quarter and 5.0 percent in the third, and by year's end 2014 stood as the strongest full-year growth performance of the entire recovery to that point. Janet Yellen was sworn in as the first woman to chair the Federal Reserve on February 3, and the FOMC wound down QE3 in $10 billion increments before formally ending net asset purchases on October 29. The labor market's improvement was the year's clearest good-news story: employers added roughly three million jobs, the best calendar year since 1999, and the unemployment rate fell from 6.7 percent in January to 5.6 percent in December, even as labor-force participation kept sliding and wage growth stayed anemic near 2 percent. The dominant surprise of the second half was a collapse in oil prices, as a surging US shale industry and OPEC's decision to defend market share rather than cut output sent crude tumbling from roughly $107 to about $53 a barrel, functioning as a windfall tax cut for consumers even as it hammered energy-producing regions and high-yield credit markets.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew 2.4 percent for the full year (Q1 roughly -0.9%, Q2 +4.6%, Q3 +5.0%), the strongest annual reading of the post-crisis recovery. Headline CPI inflation cooled to just 0.8 percent year-over-year by December as gasoline prices crashed, with core CPI at 1.6 percent. The unemployment rate fell 110 basis points over the year to 5.6 percent, and U-6 underemployment fell to 11.2 percent, even as labor-force participation slipped toward 62.7 percent. The federal funds rate stayed at 0-0.25 percent all year while the Fed's balance sheet finished near $4.5 trillion; the 10-year Treasury yield, which most forecasters expected to rise, instead fell from 3.03 percent to 2.17 percent. The federal budget deficit narrowed to about $483 billion (2.8 percent of GDP), its smallest since 2007, even as gross federal debt crossed roughly $18.15 trillion. Freddie Mac's average 30-year mortgage rate slid from about 4.5 percent to 3.87 percent over the year; the median existing-home price rose about 5.7 percent to roughly $208,900, a marked cooling from 2013's double-digit appreciation. Consumer confidence (Conference Board) climbed from 77.5 to 92.6, a seven-year high, and household net worth topped $82 trillion.

    Major Events

    Beyond the growth rebound and the oil collapse, Detroit exited the largest municipal bankruptcy in US history in December, and the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate and insurance exchanges took full effect on January 1, with roughly 8 million people enrolled in exchange plans by the March 31 deadline. GM's ignition-switch recall, covering some 30 million vehicles worldwide, produced a Justice Department investigation and a $35 million NHTSA fine and became one of the year's largest corporate-governance scandals. Treasury issued anti-inversion guidance on September 22 to slow a wave of corporate tax-inversion deals, including the Burger King-Tim Hortons merger and AbbVie's aborted pursuit of Shire. The Russian ruble lost roughly half its value against the dollar in the year's final weeks as oil cratered, forcing Russia's central bank to hike rates to 17 percent on December 16 -- an early sign that 2014's oil shock carried geopolitical as well as economic consequences.

    Why this score

    A score of +2 is warranted because growth accelerated to the best full-year pace of the recovery, three million jobs were added, unemployment fell by more than a full percentage point, the deficit shrank to its smallest share of GDP since before the crisis, and cheap gasoline amounted to a real windfall for households -- genuine, broad-based strength that fell short of the very top of the scale only because wage growth stayed weak, labor-force participation kept eroding, and the year opened with an outright GDP contraction.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    US equities notched a sixth consecutive up year, with the S&P 500 closing at 2,058.90 (total return +13.7%, price +11.4%) and setting 53 record closing highs; the Dow Jones Industrial Average returned +10.0% to close at 17,823.07, briefly crossing 18,000 in December, and the Nasdaq Composite returned +14.8% to close at 4,736.05. Breadth deteriorated sharply beneath the surface, however: the small-cap Russell 2000 managed only a +4.9% total return, and the fourth quarter brought real stress -- an October 15 Treasury "flash crash" in which the 10-year yield plunged 33 basis points intraday before recovering, a VIX spike to about 26 in mid-October, and a brutal selloff in energy equities and high-yield energy credit once oil began its collapse. Gold ended the year essentially flat near $1,184/oz after 2013's rout, the dollar index surged nearly 13% for its best year in a decade, and the 10-year Treasury yield fell from 3.03% to 2.17% even as most forecasters had expected rates to rise.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    Alibaba's September 19 NYSE listing raised roughly $25 billion, the largest IPO in world history to that point, and lifted Yahoo's shares on the strength of its large stake. Facebook's $19 billion acquisition of WhatsApp, announced in February, set the tone for a blockbuster M&A year that also produced AT&T's $48.5 billion purchase of DirecTV, Comcast's ultimately abandoned $45 billion bid for Time Warner Cable, Actavis' roughly $66 billion deal for Allergan, Medtronic's $43 billion acquisition of Covidien, Halliburton's $34.6 billion bid for Baker Hughes, and Kinder Morgan's roughly $70 billion consolidation of its master limited partnerships -- total global announced M&A topped $3.5 trillion, the busiest year since 2007. GT Advanced Technologies, Apple's sapphire-glass supplier, went bankrupt in October, and RadioShack's terminal decline became a fixture of retail-distress coverage heading into its early-2015 bankruptcy filing.

    Investment Themes

    The year's dominant investment narrative flipped midstream: for the first three quarters the story was a steady, low-volatility bull market riding record buybacks, cheap financing, and an unprecedented M&A and IPO calendar; in the fourth quarter it became a story about the oil shock, as US shale supply and OPEC's decision to defend market share rather than cut production sent crude down nearly 50% from its June peak, hammering energy equities, high-yield energy bonds, and oil-exporting currencies, most dramatically the Russian ruble. Long-duration Treasuries rallied hard as yields fell against consensus expectations, rewarding investors positioned for disinflation over reflation, while a stronger dollar and the Fed's ongoing QE taper set up the following year's more turbulent debate over when interest-rate "liftoff" would finally arrive.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The year's defining political event was the November 4 midterm election, in which Republicans gained nine Senate seats -- their largest gain since 1980 -- to retake the chamber, and netted thirteen House seats for their largest majority since 1928, on turnout that was the lowest for a federal election since 1942. The result followed a year dominated by crises abroad and scandal at home: a spring VA wait-time scandal, in which the Phoenix VA medical center was found to have concealed months-long delays for veterans seeking care, forced Secretary Eric Shinseki's resignation and a bipartisan Choice Act reform; the Islamic State's June capture of Mosul and declaration of a caliphate prompted US airstrikes in Iraq from August and Syria from September, after the videotaped beheadings of American journalists James Foley and Steven Sotloff shocked the public; Russia's annexation of Crimea in March and its stoking of war in the Donbas brought Western sanctions and, after the July shoot-down of Malaysia Airlines Flight 17, a decisive hardening of the transatlantic response. Domestically, the August 9 police killing of Michael Brown in Ferguson and the July 17 chokehold death of Eric Garner in Staten Island, followed by grand juries' decisions in November and December not to indict the officers involved, triggered nationwide protests and gave rise to Black Lives Matter as a mass movement. A surge of more than 68,000 unaccompanied Central American minors crossing the southwest border prompted Obama, after the midterms, to announce sweeping executive action shielding millions of undocumented parents from deportation, drawing an immediate multistate lawsuit. Elsewhere, the May 31 prisoner exchange of Sgt. Bowe Bergdahl for five Guantanamo detainees proved politically contentious, the December 9 release of the Senate's CIA torture report reopened the post-9/11 interrogation debate, the Lois Lerner email controversy kept the IRS targeting investigation alive, and the year closed with Obama's December 17 announcement, alongside Raul Castro, of restored US-Cuba diplomatic relations after more than five decades of estrangement.

    Domestic Policy

    The year opened with the Affordable Care Act's first full year of implementation: after 2013's disastrous HealthCare.gov launch was repaired, roughly 8 million people enrolled in exchange plans by the March 31 deadline and Medicaid expansion, adopted by 27 states plus DC, covered millions more, though litigation over federal-exchange subsidies (Halbig v. Burwell, King v. Burwell) reached the Supreme Court, which granted certiorari in November. Kathleen Sebelius resigned as HHS Secretary in April, replaced by Sylvia Mathews Burwell. Legislative output was thin but included the VA Choice Act, the 2014 Farm Bill, the bipartisan Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act, and a $1.1 trillion "cromnibus" spending bill in December that funded most of the government through September 2015 while funding DHS only through February to enable a fight over Obama's immigration action. Major immigration reform died in the House after Speaker Boehner declined to bring the Senate's 2013 "Gang of Eight" bill to a vote, prompting Obama's unilateral November 20 deferred-action expansion (DAPA), which twenty-six states led by Texas immediately sued to block. The Supreme Court's term delivered several landmark rulings: McCutcheon v. FEC struck down aggregate campaign-contribution limits; Burwell v. Hobby Lobby allowed closely-held corporations to claim religious exemptions from the ACA's contraceptive mandate; Riley v. California required warrants for cell-phone searches; NLRB v. Noel Canning narrowed the recess-appointment power; and in October the Court declined to hear appeals on same-sex marriage bans, effectively extending marriage equality to additional states. Executive activism grew as gridlock deepened: Obama proposed the EPA's Clean Power Plan, raised federal contractors' minimum wage by executive order, and pursued the Cuba opening, while House Republicans, led by new Majority Leader Kevin McCarthy after Eric Cantor's shocking June primary defeat, voted to sue the administration over ACA implementation.

    Foreign Policy

    Three crises opened almost simultaneously and defined the administration's foreign policy: Russia's seizure of Crimea and the war it stoked in the Donbas, the Islamic State's blitz across Iraq and Syria, and an Ebola epidemic in West Africa. Russia's annexation, following the February ouster of Ukrainian President Viktor Yanukovych, brought successive rounds of US and EU sanctions, hardened decisively after the July 17 downing of MH17, and prompted NATO's September Wales Summit to approve a Readiness Action Plan for Eastern Europe. Against ISIS, a coalition eventually including over 60 nations conducted airstrikes in Iraq from August 8 and in Syria from September 22, while the administration insisted there would be "no boots on the ground" even as US troop numbers in Iraq climbed toward 3,000 by year's end; the Kurdish town of Kobani became a symbolic siege that US airpower and Kurdish forces held. Afghanistan's combat mission, Operation Enduring Freedom, formally ended December 28, replaced by the smaller Resolute Support mission, after Ashraf Ghani's disputed election was resolved through a US-brokered power-sharing deal with Abdullah Abdullah. Iran nuclear talks under the 2013 Joint Plan of Action missed deadlines in July and November and were extended into 2015. Secretary of State Kerry's Israeli-Palestinian talks collapsed in April, and a 50-day war in Gaza (Operation Protective Edge) killed roughly 2,200 Palestinians and 73 Israelis before an August 26 ceasefire. The West African Ebola epidemic, which killed roughly 11,000 people, prompted Obama's September 16 deployment of up to 3,000 US military personnel; the death of Thomas Eric Duncan in Dallas and the infection of two nurses who treated him triggered a domestic panic and quarantine disputes. In Asia, Obama and Xi Jinping announced a bilateral US-China climate accord in November, laying groundwork for the following year's Paris agreement, while in December North Korea-linked hackers breached Sony Pictures over "The Interview," prompting Obama to vow a "proportional response."

    Important Elections

    Republicans gained nine Senate seats (ten counting a December Louisiana runoff), moving from 45 to 54 seats and flipping the chamber for the first time since 2006, with pickups including Colorado, North Carolina, Arkansas, Iowa (Joni Ernst, the first woman elected to federal office from that state), Montana, West Virginia (the first Republican senator there since 1959), South Dakota, Alaska, and Louisiana. In the House, Republicans netted thirteen seats for a 247-188 majority, their largest since Herbert Hoover, a result driven partly by Eric Cantor's stunning June primary loss to David Brat, the first sitting House Majority Leader ever defeated in a primary. Of thirty-six governorships on the ballot, Republicans gained a net two, including upset wins in Maryland (Larry Hogan) and Massachusetts (Charlie Baker) and Bruce Rauner's win in Illinois, while Democrats picked up Pennsylvania and Alaska. Minimum-wage increases passed in four Republican-leaning states, and recreational marijuana was legalized in Oregon, Alaska, and DC. The result was widely read as a repudiation of Obama's second-term standing, with his approval hovering around 42-44 percent; it empowered incoming Majority Leader Mitch McConnell, ratified divided government for Obama's final two years, and set up a far more favorable 2016 Senate map for Democrats even as it positioned rising Republican figures -- Ernst, Cotton, Sasse, Gardner -- for the future.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Domestic box office totaled $10.36 billion, actually down from 2013 as attendance hit a two-decade low, even though the year produced a strong slate of both blockbusters and acclaimed original work. Guardians of the Galaxy proved Marvel's biggest creative risk could still be a commercial triumph, while Captain America: The Winter Soldier, The LEGO Movie, X-Men: Days of Future Past, and Transformers: Age of Extinction anchored the franchise calendar; The Hunger Games: Mockingjay - Part 1 and the Clint Eastwood-directed American Sniper (which qualified in limited December release before its wide 2015 rollout) led year-end grosses. Culturally significant releases included Richard Linklater's twelve-years-in-the-making Boyhood, Wes Anderson's The Grand Budapest Hotel, Christopher Nolan's Interstellar, Damien Chazelle's Whiplash, David Fincher's Gone Girl, Ava DuVernay's Selma, and Alejandro Gonzalez Inarritu's Birdman. The March 2014 Academy Awards gave Best Picture to 12 Years a Slave, the first such honor for a Black-directed film, while Selma's mere two nominations the following awards season presaged the #OscarsSoWhite reckoning; Ellen DeGeneres's Oscar-night selfie became, briefly, the most-retweeted post in Twitter's history.

    Music

    Streaming crossed a threshold as on-demand streams grew 54 percent year-over-year while album sales fell to their lowest level since SoundScan tracking began in 1991; Taylor Swift pulled her catalog from Spotify in November, framing streaming as a devaluation of music, a debate that defined the year's discourse. Her album 1989 sold 1.287 million copies in its first week, the only album to go platinum in 2014, powered by "Shake It Off" and the chart-topping "Blank Space." Pharrell Williams's "Happy" was Billboard's top song of the year, and Frozen's soundtrack, with the inescapable "Let It Go," was the year's second-best-selling album. Sam Smith broke through with "In the Lonely Hour," Beyonce's surprise self-titled album continued to dominate conversation, and Ed Sheeran's "x" produced the hit "Thinking Out Loud." At the January Grammys, Daft Punk's "Random Access Memories" won Album of the Year, while Macklemore & Ryan Lewis's Best Rap Album win over Kendrick Lamar's "good kid, m.A.A.d city" drew widespread criticism.

    Television

    "Peak TV" accelerated as streaming originals became genuine prestige players: Netflix's House of Cards and Orange Is the New Black drew Emmy nominations, and Amazon's Transparent won Golden Globes, the first major streaming series to do so. Broadcast and cable hits included The Big Bang Theory, The Walking Dead's blockbuster season-five premiere, Game of Thrones' fourth season, the McConaughey-and-Harrelson event series True Detective, FX's Fargo, and the first half of Mad Men's final season; the Emmys crowned Breaking Bad Outstanding Drama in its final-season sweep. Late night was transformed as Jimmy Fallon took over The Tonight Show in February and Stephen Colbert ended The Colbert Report in December to prepare for Late Show. Bill Cosby's career collapsed after comedian Hannibal Buress's October stand-up bit revived long-dormant sexual-assault allegations, and dozens of accusers came forward by year's end.

    Technology

    Apple's iPhone 6 and 6 Plus, launched September 19 with larger screens answering Samsung's Galaxy Note formula, drove the company's biggest launch to date, while Apple Watch was announced (for 2015 launch) and Apple Pay debuted in October as the first credible mobile-payments push; the "Bendgate" controversy over flexing iPhone 6 Plus units was the year's tech-schadenfreude moment. Facebook bought WhatsApp for $19 billion in February and Oculus VR for $2 billion in March, both bets whose scale was widely underread at the time. Amazon quietly launched the Echo in November, introducing Alexa and the smart-speaker category, even as its Fire Phone flopped and forced a $170 million write-down. Uber raised funding at valuations reaching $40 billion by December, Airbnb reached $10 billion, and the "unicorn" era of private tech valuations took hold. Net neutrality became a mainstream policy fight after John Oliver's viral segment drove four million comments to the FCC and Obama endorsed Title II reclassification in November, while the Heartbleed OpenSSL bug, the iCloud celebrity photo hack ("The Fappening"), and massive breaches at Home Depot and JPMorgan Chase made 2014 a landmark year for cybersecurity anxiety.

    Social Trends

    Beyond Ferguson and Garner, the Fight for $15 fast-food worker movement staged strikes in 150 cities, the September 21 People's Climate March drew roughly 400,000 to New York, the largest climate demonstration in history to that point, and marriage equality's cascade -- from 17 states at the start of the year to 35 plus DC by year's end -- felt like inevitable moral progress even as it set up 2015's Obergefell decision. Campus anti-sexual-assault activism accelerated behind Emma Sulkowicz's "Carry That Weight" mattress protest and the White House's "It's On Us" campaign, though Rolling Stone's later-retracted University of Virginia rape story became a cautionary tale for journalism. Pew Research documented an accelerating "rise of the nones," with religiously unaffiliated Americans reaching roughly 23 percent of adults. Gamergate, beginning in August, targeted women in game development and criticism with coordinated harassment and became an early organizing template for what would later be called the online alt-right. US population reached approximately 318.9 million, with growth slowing to historic lows and non-Hispanic whites falling to about 62 percent of the population.

    Crime

    The killings of Michael Brown in Ferguson and Eric Garner in Staten Island, both followed by non-indictments, dominated the year's criminal-justice conversation and were compounded by the November 22 police killing of 12-year-old Tamir Rice in Cleveland. Elliot Rodger's May 23 Isla Vista massacre killed six and spawned the #YesAllWomen hashtag alongside early recognition of "incel" ideology, and the April 2 Fort Hood shooting killed three plus the gunman. On December 20, NYPD officers Rafael Ramos and Wenjian Liu were assassinated in Brooklyn by a gunman who framed the killings as retaliation for Garner and Brown, sharply escalating tensions between police unions and Mayor Bill de Blasio. The Bill Cosby allegations, revived by a viral comedy bit, reopened debate over statutes of limitations and institutional complicity in covering up sexual assault by powerful men.

    Sports

    Super Bowl XLVIII was the most lopsided in recent memory as the Seattle Seahawks routed the Denver Broncos 43-8. The San Antonio Spurs beat the Miami Heat 4-1 in the NBA Finals, ending the Big Three era, and LeBron James announced his return to Cleveland in a July Sports Illustrated essay; the Donald Sterling recording scandal forced the Clippers owner's lifetime ban and a $2 billion sale to Steve Ballmer. The San Francisco Giants beat the Kansas City Royals in a seven-game World Series defined by Madison Bumgarner's legendary relief pitching, and the Los Angeles Kings won the Stanley Cup. The Sochi Winter Olympics and the US men's soccer team's Round of 16 run at the Brazil World Cup, anchored by Tim Howard's record-setting 16 saves against Belgium, both became genuine national moments, while Derek Jeter's farewell season and Ray Rice's and Adrian Peterson's off-field scandals dominated football and baseball headlines alike.

    Public Mood

    2014 felt like a year of shattered illusions after a period of guarded optimism: the recovery was real, with unemployment falling and gas prices collapsing under $2.50 by year's end, but the mood was anxious rather than triumphant, and Obama's approval sagged into the low 40s ahead of a midterm "shellacking." The dominant feeling was of a frayed social fabric, with Ferguson, Gamergate, campus assault, ISIS beheadings, and celebrity misconduct all filtered through an accelerating social-media outrage cycle that made 2014 the year social media stopped feeling like the future and started feeling like a problem. Yet countercurrents ran alongside the anxiety: marriage equality's momentum felt like durable moral progress, the Ice Bucket Challenge showcased the internet's better instincts, and a genuinely strong crop of films and television gave the year real creative vitality even as trust in police, media, Congress, and the NFL measurably eroded.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary end-of-year retrospectives emphasized Ebola, the disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 370, ISIS's beheading videos, Ferguson, the Sochi Olympics, the Ice Bucket Challenge, the CIA torture report, the Sony hack, the US-Cuba opening, and the Republican midterm sweep -- a list dominated by discrete shocks and mysteries rather than structural change.

    What actually mattered

    Of that contemporary list, only Ferguson, ISIS, and Ukraine (as the leading edge of a much larger conflict) still read as first-order historical events. What coverage undersold or missed entirely were the structural stories: the reconstitution of Russian revisionism, the platformization of American politics, the coalescence of a networked online right through Gamergate, the tech mega-acquisitions that would define antitrust politics for a decade, and the Cosby moment as the leading indicator of a broader reckoning over power and consent that would arrive three years later as #MeToo.

    Most forgotten story

    The disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 generated more sustained cable coverage than any other story of the year -- CNN's ratings roughly doubled on the strength of it -- yet within a few years it had vanished almost entirely from public consciousness, punctuated only by occasional debris-fragment updates from the Indian Ocean. It endures mainly as a media-criticism artifact: enormous attention, essentially zero downstream historical consequence.

    Most underrated story

    The Russian seizure of Crimea and the war in the Donbas was treated in real time as a manageable regional aggression that sanctions and diplomacy would freeze. The consensus underestimated that Putin believed the post-1991 European settlement was reversible, that the Western sanctions response would be strong enough to insult Russia but too weak to deter it, and that years of low-boil war in eastern Ukraine would harden a generation of Ukrainian soldiers and civil society into the force that would resist the full-scale 2022 invasion.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The Islamic State's June capture of Mosul, in which a force estimated in the low thousands routed several US-trained Iraqi divisions and seized the country's second-largest city within days, exposed an intelligence failure that startled even hawks barely two and a half years after US combat forces had withdrawn from Iraq. It reopened the American war in Iraq, drew the US into Syria, and helped produce the refugee wave that would later feed Brexit and Europe's populist upsurge -- a single event whose eventual magnitude was wildly disproportionate to its initial framing as an Iraqi problem.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The disappearance of Malaysia Airlines Flight 370Months of continuous cable-news speculation about the missing jet produced new maritime search protocols but no lasting geopolitical, cultural, or economic consequence once the plane was never definitively located.
    The domestic Ebola panic (Dallas, the infected nurses, the Kaci Hickox quarantine dispute)Two Americans ultimately died on US soil; the panic reordered news coverage and briefly the midterm campaign but was almost entirely forgotten within a year, and its deeper lesson about pandemic readiness went unlearned until 2020.
    The Ice Bucket ChallengeThe viral fundraiser raised over $100 million and helped fund a genuine scientific discovery, but as a cultural template it produced few durable imitators and by 2016 already felt like a period piece of pre-polarization internet optimism.
    The Bowe Bergdahl prisoner swapWeeks of coverage, a Rose Garden ceremony the White House later regretted, and a House investigation now read as a small footnote within the much larger Afghanistan withdrawal story that would not resolve until 2021.
    The Ray Rice and Adrian Peterson NFL scandalsWall-to-wall sports-media coverage did meaningfully accelerate NFL domestic-violence policy, but the sheer volume of attention now looks disproportionate to the durable institutional shift that followed.
    The Sochi Winter OlympicsCoverage fixated on Putin's $50 billion vanity project, unfinished hotels, and "gay propaganda" laws, but underplayed that Putin used the Games as diplomatic cover for the Crimea operation launched before the closing ceremony; as sport or spectacle Sochi left almost no lasting trace.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    GamergateCovered at the time as a niche gaming-and-misogyny story, its coordinated harassment tactics, meme warfare, and fusion of grievance politics with anonymous online organizing are now understood as the operational proving ground for the online alt-right that migrated into the 2016 Trump campaign.
    Russia's annexation of Crimea and the war in the DonbasTreated at the time as a contained European crisis comparable to Georgia 2008, it is now understood as the opening phase of the largest land war in Europe since 1945, with nearly every element of the 2022 full-scale invasion rehearsed in 2014.
    ISIS's fall of Mosul and declaration of a caliphateFramed largely as an Iraqi problem, it triggered a second American war in Iraq, the Syrian refugee wave that reshaped European politics, and the wave of terror attacks across the West that defined the late 2010s security environment.
    Facebook's acquisitions of WhatsApp ($19B) and Oculus ($2B)Reported as expensive but idiosyncratic bets, WhatsApp became the messaging backbone of the Global South and a monetization target, while Oculus seeded the decade-later pivot to "the metaverse" and the Meta rebrand.
    Hannibal Buress's viral stand-up bit about Bill CosbyA five-minute club routine that resurfaced decade-old rape allegations, went viral through social media, and led dozens of women to come forward was arguably the first sign that social platforms could break the celebrity-protection cartel around powerful men, directly prefiguring the Weinstein reckoning three years later.
    The Republican Senate takeover in the November midtermsRead at the time mainly as a rebuke of Obama's approval ratings, the new Republican majority two years later would refuse a hearing to Supreme Court nominee Merrick Garland, a decision without which the post-2016 conservative Court, the Dobbs decision, and much of the 2020s legal landscape would look very different.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Malaysia Airlines Jet Vanishes With 239 Aboard; Search Sweeps Two Oceans
    2. Russian Forces Move Into Crimea as Ukraine Reels From Revolution
    3. Ebola Outbreak Reaches U.S.: Dallas Patient Confirmed as Global Toll Mounts
    4. Militants Sweep Into Mosul; Iraqi Army Melts Away as ISIS Declares Caliphate
    5. Unarmed Teen Killed by Police in Ferguson; Missouri Suburb Erupts in Protest
    6. Malaysia Airlines Jet Downed Over Eastern Ukraine; 298 Dead, Russia Blamed
    7. Ice Bucket Challenge Raises Tens of Millions for ALS as Videos Sweep Social Media
    8. Republicans Ride Wave Election to Senate Majority, Largest House Gains Since 1928
    9. Grand Jury Declines to Indict Officer in Eric Garner Chokehold Death; 'I Can't Breathe' Fills the Streets
    10. Obama and Castro Announce Historic Thaw; Cuba Embargo Era Nears Its End

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryThe Affordable Care Act's individual mandate and insurance exchanges took full effect on January 1, and a punishing "polar vortex" gripped much of the country, helping drag first-quarter GDP into contraction. Unemployment stood at 6.7 percent. The Grammys on January 26 gave Album of the Year to Daft Punk, and at Davos and in his State of the Union, Obama dismissed the Islamic State as a "JV team," a characterization that would soon prove badly mistaken.
    FebruaryJanet Yellen was sworn in as the first woman to chair the Federal Reserve on February 3, and Obama raised the federal contractor minimum wage to $10.10 by executive order on February 12. The Sochi Winter Olympics ran February 7 to 23, with the US winning 28 medals amid controversy over Russia's anti-LGBTQ laws. Facebook announced its $19 billion acquisition of WhatsApp, the 2014 Farm Bill was signed, and in Ukraine, President Viktor Yanukovych fled Kyiv amid the Maidan uprising as Russian forces began seizing Crimea.
    MarchRussia formally annexed Crimea on March 18 following a Kremlin-backed referendum, triggering the first rounds of US and EU sanctions. Malaysia Airlines Flight 370 disappeared en route to Beijing on March 8, launching a monthslong multinational search that would never definitively locate the wreckage. Facebook acquired Oculus VR for $2 billion, and first-quarter GDP data confirmed the winter's contraction.
    AprilReports emerged from the Phoenix VA medical center of secret waiting lists concealing months-long delays in veterans' care, beginning a scandal that would claim Secretary Eric Shinseki's job. The Supreme Court decided McCutcheon v. FEC, striking down aggregate campaign-contribution limits. The Heartbleed OpenSSL bug exposed a critical web security flaw, and Kathleen Sebelius resigned as HHS Secretary.
    MayEric Shinseki resigned as VA Secretary on May 30 amid the wait-time scandal, and the next day the administration announced the release of Sgt. Bowe Bergdahl in exchange for five Guantanamo detainees, a swap that quickly became politically contentious. Narendra Modi's BJP swept India's elections. Elliot Rodger killed six people in the Isla Vista massacre on May 23, and unaccompanied Central American minors began crossing the southwest border in unprecedented numbers.
    JuneIslamic State forces captured Mosul on June 10 and declared a caliphate, routing Iraqi divisions and threatening Baghdad and Erbil; the same day, House Majority Leader Eric Cantor lost his primary to David Brat, the first sitting majority leader ever defeated in a primary. The Supreme Court issued a run of major rulings, including Burwell v. Hobby Lobby, Riley v. California, and NLRB v. Noel Canning. GDP rebounded sharply to 4.6 percent annualized growth, and Obama previewed the EPA's Clean Power Plan.
    JulyEric Garner died after a police chokehold in Staten Island on July 17, the same day Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 was shot down over eastern Ukraine, killing 298. War erupted in Gaza (Operation Protective Edge) on July 8. The Ice Bucket Challenge began spreading virally for ALS awareness, oil prices still traded near $107 a barrel, and Amazon's Fire Phone flopped on release.
    AugustMichael Brown was fatally shot by a police officer in Ferguson, Missouri on August 9, igniting weeks of protest and a national reckoning over policing. Obama authorized the first US airstrikes against ISIS in Iraq on August 8, and journalist James Foley was beheaded by ISIS later that month. Robin Williams died by suicide on August 11, Gamergate's harassment campaign began, and Ebola cases in West Africa accelerated sharply.
    SeptemberObama addressed the nation on September 10 to announce a broader campaign against ISIS, with airstrikes extending into Syria on September 22; journalist Steven Sotloff was beheaded in early September. Alibaba's NYSE debut on September 19 raised roughly $25 billion, the largest IPO in history, the same day Apple launched the iPhone 6 and 6 Plus. The Ray Rice elevator video surfaced on September 8, NATO held its Wales Summit, and the People's Climate March drew some 400,000 to New York.
    OctoberThe Federal Reserve formally ended QE3 on October 29 after tapering purchases throughout the year. Thomas Eric Duncan died of Ebola in Dallas on October 8, triggering a domestic panic and quarantine disputes, while two nurses who treated him were also infected. A Treasury market "flash crash" hit October 15, oil prices continued falling sharply, and Hannibal Buress's viral stand-up bit revived long-dormant rape allegations against Bill Cosby.
    NovemberRepublicans won a decisive midterm victory on November 4, retaking the Senate with a nine-seat gain and expanding their House majority to its largest since 1928. A St. Louis County grand jury declined to indict Officer Darren Wilson in Michael Brown's death on November 24, triggering renewed nationwide protests. OPEC declined to cut oil production on November 27, accelerating the price collapse, and Obama announced sweeping executive action on immigration (DAPA) on November 20.
    DecemberObama and Raul Castro jointly announced the restoration of US-Cuba diplomatic relations on December 17, ending more than five decades of estrangement. A Staten Island grand jury declined to indict Officer Daniel Pantaleo in Eric Garner's death on December 3, and on December 20 two NYPD officers were assassinated in Brooklyn in claimed retaliation. The Senate released its CIA torture report on December 9, Detroit exited the largest municipal bankruptcy in US history, Sony Pictures was hacked over "The Interview," and the Dow crossed 18,000 for the first time as the year closed with oil near $53 a barrel.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+2.4% real GDP growth for full-year 2014 (Q1 approx. -0.9%, Q2 +4.6%, Q3 +5.0%)
    InflationCPI +0.8% year-over-year in December 2014 (core CPI +1.6%)
    Unemployment5.6% in December 2014, down from 6.7% in January; U-6 underemployment 11.2%
    S&P 500 return+13.7% total return (price +11.4%), closing at 2,058.90
    Dow return+10.0% total return (price +7.5%), closing at 17,823.07
    Fed funds rateHeld at 0-0.25% all year; QE3 tapered to zero by the October 29 FOMC meeting
    Oil priceWTI collapsed from about $98 (year-end 2013) to roughly $53.27 by year-end 2014
    Gold priceApproximately $1,184/oz, essentially flat (-1.7%) for the year
    PopulationApproximately 318.9 million (July 2014 Census estimate)
    Median home priceApproximately $208,900 median existing-home price (NAR), up about 5.7% year-over-year
    National debtGross federal debt approximately $18.15 trillion (about 103% of GDP) at year-end

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2014 was to feel two contradictory things at once and never quite reconcile them. On paper, the year delivered the recovery Americans had been promised since 2009: three million new jobs, unemployment down more than a point to 5.6 percent, gas prices sliding toward $2.50 a gallon by Christmas, the stock market notching its sixth straight winning year, and a federal deficit shrinking back toward pre-crisis norms. Consumer confidence hit a seven-year high. And yet almost nobody experienced 2014 as a triumphant year. It felt instead like a year of accumulating unease, as though the country's attention were being pulled, week after week, toward some new and unfamiliar form of dread: a plane that simply vanished and was never found; a virus from West Africa that suddenly had an American face in a Dallas hospital room; a black-clad group beheading American journalists on camera; a small Missouri suburb burning after a police shooting, its images circulating instantly and inescapably on phones.

    What made the year distinctive was the sense that the machinery mediating all of this -- cable news, and increasingly social media -- had itself become part of the story. Ferguson was not just a local tragedy but a referendum on how Americans saw each other across racial lines, amplified and hardened by a Twitter feed that never stopped scrolling. Gamergate showed that the same platforms could curdle into organized cruelty. The Ice Bucket Challenge, by contrast, showed those platforms at their most generous. Americans that year were being asked, more insistently than ever before, to have an opinion about everything, instantly, and the toll of that showed up in falling trust: in police, in Congress, in the NFL, in institutions generally. Obama's approval sank into the low 40s, and November's midterms delivered Republicans their most commanding congressional position in nearly a century, a verdict less about any single policy than about a diffuse, structural dissatisfaction.

    Still, it would be wrong to remember 2014 as simply bleak. There was real joy in it: Guardians of the Galaxy's ragtag ensemble, the sprawling ambition of Boyhood, Taylor Swift's total pop reinvention, a US men's soccer team that made an entire country care about a Round of 16 loss. Marriage equality's rapid spread across state after state felt, to millions of Americans, like durable moral progress arriving faster than anyone had expected. And beneath the noise, most people's material lives were genuinely improving, even if it did not yet feel that way at the kitchen table. What Americans could not see in 2014, but what a decade of hindsight makes obvious, is that this was the year the ground shifted under them: the year great-power war returned to Europe, jihadism reasserted itself, a new civil-rights movement was born, and the internet's capacity for both solidarity and cruelty became fully, permanently visible. It felt, in the moment, like a strange and unsettled year. In retrospect, it was the hinge on which the following decade turned.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2015
    Econ +1Mark +0Poli +1Cult +2Over +1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The United States in 2015 occupied an unusual hinge point: an economy that was unambiguously healing, a government that despite total partisan division in Washington produced more substantive bipartisan legislation than anyone expected, and a Supreme Court that closed out a decade-long legal campaign by declaring a constitutional right to same-sex marriage.
