Unemployment fell from 5.7 percent in January to 5.0 percent by December, gasoline sank below two dollars a gallon, and the Federal Reserve felt confident enough in the recovery to raise interest rates in December for the first time since 2006, formally ending seven years of near-zero rates. Republicans held unified control of Congress for the first time in Barack Obama's presidency, yet still passed a rewrite of No Child Left Behind, a permanent Medicare physician-payment fix, a five-year highway bill, and fast-track trade authority, even as Speaker John Boehner was pushed into retirement by his own party's right flank.

    That institutional progress sat uneasily beside a year of racial reckoning and violence that made 2015 feel anything but settled. The murder of nine Black worshippers in Charleston forced the Confederate flag from statehouses across the South even as it exposed the durability of American racism; Freddie Gray's death in Baltimore police custody produced riots and a National Guard deployment; and the deaths of Walter Scott and Sandra Bland kept Black Lives Matter at the center of national life. Terrorism intruded twice in devastating fashion, in the Chattanooga recruiting-center shooting in July and the San Bernardino massacre in December, while the Paris attacks in November, though French, reshaped the American conversation about refugees and Islam. Amid all this, American popular culture had one of its most fertile years in memory, from Kendrick Lamar's "To Pimp a Butterfly" and Adele's record-shattering "25" to "Mad Max: Fury Road," "Inside Out," and the December release of "Star Wars: The Force Awakens," while a real-estate developer's escalator ride in June began a transformation of American politics that almost no one, at the time, took seriously.

    Viewed from the vantage of what came later, 2015's true center of gravity was not the headline achievements that dominated year-end retrospectives, such as the Iran deal, the Cuba opening, and the Paris climate accord, several of which would prove reversible within a few years, but a set of quiet developments almost nobody flagged as important: the founding of OpenAI, the first landing of a reusable SpaceX rocket, the launch of the Ethereum blockchain, Tesla's first Autopilot software, and the utter misjudgment by essentially the entire American political and media establishment of Donald Trump's candidacy. The year's economy was healthy but unspectacular, its markets flat and narrow, its politics functional but visibly fraying beneath the surface, and its culture unusually vibrant, a genuine mixture of authentic progress and the seeds of a decade of disruption that few contemporaries could see coming.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1A second consecutive year of solid job creation pushed unemployment to 5.0 percent and cheap gasoline acted as a household tax cut, but soft wage growth, an oil-driven energy-sector recession, and fading late-year momentum kept the year from being exceptional.
    Markets+0The S&P 500 was essentially flat and the Dow posted its first down year since 2008 amid an August correction and historically narrow FANG-driven breadth, making 2015 a neutral rather than negative year for equities.
    Politics+1Landmark Supreme Court rulings and unexpectedly productive bipartisan legislation were offset by the Freedom Caucus's ouster of a sitting Speaker, intensifying racial unrest, and the destabilizing arrival of Trumpism in the Republican primary.
    Culture+2An extraordinarily productive creative year across music, film, and television paired with the landmark civil-rights milestone of marriage equality, offset but not overwhelmed by rising mass violence and polarization.
    Overall Mood+1Genuine institutional and economic progress, including marriage equality, falling unemployment, and diplomatic openings, was shadowed by escalating mass violence, terrorism, and the disorienting rise of Trumpism, netting out to modest optimism edged with unease.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Supreme Court legalizes same-sex marriage nationwide in Obergefell v. Hodges
    2. White supremacist murders nine at Charleston's Emanuel AME Church; Confederate flag removed from SC statehouse
    3. Donald Trump launches presidential campaign and seizes lead in GOP primary polls
    4. Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA) reached in Vienna
    5. United States and Cuba reopen embassies after 54 years
    6. Freddie Gray dies in Baltimore police custody; city erupts in unrest
    7. Federal Reserve raises interest rates for first time since 2006
    8. Coordinated terrorist attacks in Paris kill 130
    9. San Bernardino attack kills 14 in ISIS-inspired shooting
    10. Star Wars: The Force Awakens shatters box-office records

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The US economy logged its sixth consecutive year of expansion following the financial crisis, growing at a moderate pace estimated at 2.4 percent on first release for 2015 and later revised up into the 2.7-to-2.9-percent range. Growth was uneven across quarters, with a weak winter dragged down by harsh weather, West Coast port disruptions, and a strong dollar giving way to firmer spring and summer readings before momentum faded again late in the year, though the underlying trend remained solidly positive. The collapse in oil prices, which fell from roughly $53 a barrel in January to about $37 by year's end, produced the year's defining macro paradox: a labor market strong enough to push unemployment to 5.0 percent alongside headline inflation of well under one percent, trends that together gave the Federal Reserve room to finally begin normalizing interest rates.

    Important Statistics

    Nonfarm payrolls grew by roughly 2.7 million jobs and the unemployment rate fell from 5.7 percent in January to 5.0 percent in December, reaching the Fed's estimated full-employment threshold, even as labor-force participation slipped to about 62.6 percent and wage growth remained a still-modest 2.5 percent year-over-year. Headline CPI inflation was just 0.7 percent year-over-year in December, averaging near 0.1 percent for the full year, while core CPI held near 2.1 percent. Housing continued a steady non-frothy recovery, with the Case-Shiller national home price index up about 5.2 percent, the median existing-home price near $222,400, 30-year mortgage rates averaging 3.85 percent, and existing-home sales reaching roughly 5.25 million, the strongest since 2006. Auto sales set an all-time record of 17.5 million units. The federal deficit narrowed to about $438 billion, or 2.4 percent of GDP, the smallest of the Obama era, even as total gross federal debt rose toward $18.9 trillion by year's end.

    Major Events

    The single defining economic event of the year came on December 16, when the Federal Reserve under Chair Janet Yellen raised the federal funds target range by a quarter point to 0.25-0.50 percent, ending more than seven years at the zero lower bound and formally closing the crisis-era monetary chapter, with its "dot plot" projecting, overoptimistically as it turned out, four more hikes in 2016. Congress and the White House reached the Bipartisan Budget Act in late October and November, suspending the debt ceiling through March 2017 and lifting sequester-era spending caps, followed by a $1.15 trillion December omnibus that lifted the decades-old ban on crude oil exports and made permanent several business and family tax credits. Puerto Rico defaulted on part of its bond obligations in August, the first such default by a US commonwealth, while sustained oil-price weakness pushed a wave of energy and mining companies into bankruptcy by year's end. China's surprise August currency devaluation and a Shanghai stock crash briefly rattled confidence in the global growth outlook and contributed to volatility in US markets, though the domestic expansion itself continued largely uninterrupted.

    Why this score

    A second consecutive year of solid job creation that pushed unemployment down to the Fed's estimated full-employment rate, falling gas prices that acted as a tax cut for households, and a housing market recovering at a healthy, sustainable pace all argued for a clearly above-trend year, but soft wage growth, an energy-sector recession driven by collapsing oil prices, weakening late-year momentum, and stubbornly low labor-force participation kept 2015 from qualifying as an exceptional year, landing it at a modest +1.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    US equities delivered one of the most disappointing years of the post-crisis expansion without ever approaching a bear market. The S&P 500 finished essentially flat, down about 0.7 percent on price but up roughly 1.4 percent including dividends, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell about 2.2 percent on price for its first losing year since 2008. The Nasdaq Composite was the exception, gaining roughly 5.7 percent on price, about 7 percent total return, powered almost entirely by the "FANG" cohort of Facebook, Amazon, Netflix, and Google/Alphabet. Breadth was historically poor: the median S&P constituent finished the year down, the small-cap Russell 2000 fell about 4.4 percent, and value stocks badly lagged growth. The year's defining volatility episode came on August 24's "Black Monday," when a Chinese currency devaluation and Shanghai stock crash sent the Dow down more than 1,000 points intraday, the S&P 500 down about 11 percent peak-to-trough within a week, and the VIX above 40 for the first time since 2011; markets recovered much of the loss by October before fading again into year-end amid renewed oil weakness and high-yield credit stress.

    Character

    Mixed

    Major Companies

    Google restructured under a new holding company, Alphabet, announced in August. Dell agreed in October to acquire EMC for roughly $67 billion, then the largest technology acquisition ever. Volkswagen's market value was hammered after its diesel-emissions scandal broke in September. Notable IPOs included Shake Shack, Etsy, Fitbit, Ferrari (spun off from Fiat Chrysler), First Data (the year's largest US IPO at about $2.6 billion), Match Group, and Square, whose November listing priced below its range in a symbolic markdown of Silicon Valley "unicorn" valuations. Third Avenue Management's Focused Credit Fund halted redemptions on December 10 in a rare mutual-fund gating event that briefly evoked memories of 2008. Anheuser-Busch InBev's roughly $107 billion agreement to acquire SABMiller, the completed Kraft-Heinz merger under Berkshire Hathaway and 3G Capital, Charter's $78 billion deal for Time Warner Cable, and Pfizer's roughly $160 billion announced, and later abandoned, merger with Allergan defined a record year for global M&A, which topped $4.7 trillion.

    Investment Themes

    The year crystallized a narrow-leadership market in which a handful of mega-cap technology "FANG" stocks masked broad underlying weakness, foreshadowing a theme that would recur for the rest of the decade. A strengthening dollar, up roughly 9 percent for a second straight year, squeezed multinational earnings and emerging-market borrowers, while collapsing commodity prices, with oil, gold, copper, and iron ore all posting double-digit declines, hammered energy and materials companies and triggered a wave of high-yield energy-sector distress. Record global M&A activity reflected corporations' preference for financial engineering and consolidation over organic growth in a low-rate, low-growth environment, while the autumn markdown of high-flying private "unicorns" like Square signaled the first cracks in venture-backed technology valuations.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The Supreme Court delivered two landmark rulings within a day of each other in June: Obergefell v. Hodges (June 26) legalized same-sex marriage nationwide in a 5-4 decision, and King v. Burwell (June 25) preserved Affordable Care Act subsidies on federal exchanges. A white supremacist murdered nine Black parishioners at Charleston's Emanuel AME Church on June 17, prompting South Carolina to remove the Confederate battle flag from its statehouse grounds. Donald Trump announced his presidential candidacy on June 16 and led national Republican primary polling for the rest of the year, upending the field and sidelining establishment favorites. Speaker John Boehner, worn down by his own party's Freedom Caucus, resigned in late September; after Majority Leader Kevin McCarthy's surprise withdrawal, Paul Ryan was elected Speaker on October 29. Freddie Gray's death in Baltimore police custody in April triggered riots and a National Guard deployment, extending the Black Lives Matter movement that had begun with Ferguson the previous year. On December 2, a married couple inspired by ISIS killed fourteen people in San Bernardino, the year's deadliest domestic terrorist attack, which directly preceded Trump's call for a ban on Muslim entry into the United States.

    Domestic Policy

    Despite united Republican control of Congress for the first time in Obama's presidency, divided government proved unexpectedly productive, in part because a departing Boehner was willing to pass major bills with Democratic votes. Congress enacted the Every Student Succeeds Act replacing No Child Left Behind, the MACRA "doc fix" permanently repealing Medicare's physician sustainable growth rate formula, the five-year FAST Act highway bill, Trade Promotion Authority for the TPP, and the USA Freedom Act ending bulk NSA telephone-metadata collection. Obama relied heavily on executive action elsewhere, finalizing the Clean Power Plan's power-sector emissions targets, reclassifying broadband under Title II in the FCC's net neutrality order, and defending the DAPA immigration program in federal court, where it remained enjoined pending a 2016 Supreme Court showdown. Racial-justice pressure intensified following Ferguson, Baltimore, and the killings of Walter Scott and Sandra Bland, prompting Justice Department pattern-or-practice investigations into the Chicago and Baltimore police departments, even as Congress again declined to act on gun legislation after Charleston. There were no government shutdowns in 2015, a marked improvement over 2013, despite conservative brinkmanship over Planned Parenthood funding.

    Foreign Policy

    The Obama administration's most ambitious foreign-policy year centered on the July 14 conclusion of the Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA) in Vienna, which survived a September Senate vote after Democrats sustained a filibuster against a disapproval resolution. Full diplomatic relations with Cuba advanced with the July 20 reopening of embassies in Washington and Havana. The war against ISIS continued via Operation Inherent Resolve, complicated by Russia's late-September military intervention in Syria on behalf of the Assad regime, a Taliban seizure of the Afghan provincial capital of Kunduz, and an American airstrike that mistakenly struck a Medecins Sans Frontieres hospital there in October. The November 13 Paris attacks, which killed 130, intensified the American debate over Syrian refugee resettlement, with more than thirty Republican governors declaring they would refuse resettlement. Xi Jinping's September state visit produced a bilateral pledge against commercial cyber espionage, even as tensions rose over Chinese island-building in the South China Sea, where the US conducted its first freedom-of-navigation operation in October. The year closed with the Paris Climate Agreement, adopted December 12 under US leadership, and the conclusion, though not ratification, of the Trans-Pacific Partnership trade negotiations in October.

    Important Elections

    2015 was an off-year with no federal elections, but the 2016 field took shape decisively: the Republican primary swelled to seventeen candidates with Trump leading from midsummer onward, while Jeb Bush's expensively funded campaign faltered against expectations. Hillary Clinton entered as the Democratic frontrunner in April, but Bernie Sanders's insurgent late-April campaign drew unexpectedly large crowds, and Vice President Biden ultimately declined to run in October following his son Beau's death. In gubernatorial races, Republican Matt Bevin flipped Kentucky in November while Democrat John Bel Edwards won a runoff in deep-red Louisiana over a scandal-tainted David Vitter. Houston voters repealed the city's equal-rights ordinance in a bruising campaign over transgender bathroom access that foreshadowed a larger national fight, while Ohio voters rejected a ballot measure to legalize marijuana.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    The domestic box office set an all-time record of $11.1 billion, driven by an unusually strong slate of blockbusters. "Star Wars: The Force Awakens," released December 18, became the highest-grossing domestic film ever at the time; "Jurassic World," "Avengers: Age of Ultron," and "Furious 7," Paul Walker's emotional final film, rounded out a dominant summer. Pixar's "Inside Out" was the year's critical-commercial favorite, while George Miller's "Mad Max: Fury Road" emerged as the surprise auteur triumph of the year, eventually winning six Oscars. Ridley Scott's "The Martian," the Boston Globe drama "Spotlight," Alejandro Inarritu's "The Revenant," and the crash drama "The Big Short" anchored a strong prestige slate, while "Straight Outta Compton" was the year's biggest August surprise hit. The February ceremony crowned "Birdman" Best Picture for 2014's films, but that same ceremony's all-white acting nominee slate prompted activist April Reign to coin #OscarsSoWhite, a controversy that would intensify the following year.

    Music

    Adele's "25" sold 3.38 million copies in its first US week, a record that still stands, while "Hello" became the year's inescapable single. Taylor Swift's "1989" continued its dominance from late 2014 through a sold-out stadium tour. Kendrick Lamar's "To Pimp a Butterfly" was the year's critical landmark, its jazz-inflected meditation on Black identity yielding "Alright," which became an anthem of the Black Lives Matter movement. Mark Ronson and Bruno Mars's "Uptown Funk" was the year's top Hot 100 hit, while Drake's surprise-released "If You're Reading This It's Too Late" and his feud with Meek Mill defined the summer's rap conversation, and Justin Bieber completed a career rehabilitation with "Purpose." Streaming reached a new threshold as Apple Music and Jay-Z's Tidal both launched and Spotify's paid subscriber base passed 20 million, while Chris Stapleton's surprise November CMA sweep signaled a coming shift back toward country traditionalism.

    Television

    FX executive John Landgraf declared American television had reached "Peak TV" in August, counting 409 scripted series airing that year. "Game of Thrones" reached its acclaimed "Hardhome" episode and closed its fifth season with Jon Snow's cliffhanger death; Fox's "Empire" became a rare broadcast phenomenon that grew its audience week over week. Streaming asserted its cultural weight with Netflix's true-crime sensation "Making a Murderer," alongside acclaimed originals like "Master of None," "Jessica Jones," and "Mr. Robot." Late night transitioned generationally: David Letterman retired in May, Stephen Colbert took over "The Late Show" in September, and Jon Stewart departed "The Daily Show" in August after sixteen years, succeeded by Trevor Noah. Trump's November 7 hosting stint on "Saturday Night Live" previewed a media normalization debate that would recur throughout his campaign.

    Technology

    The Apple Watch shipped in April as Apple's first new product category since the iPad, while the iPhone 6S introduced 3D Touch in September and Windows 10 launched as a free upgrade in July. Amazon's Echo speaker went on general sale in June, planting the flag for voice assistants and making "Alexa" a household name, while Google reorganized under new parent company Alphabet. Facebook surpassed 1.55 billion monthly users and Instagram crossed 400 million, while Snapchat's ephemeral "stories" format captured younger users and Periscope and Meerkat briefly made live-streaming a novelty. Uber and Airbnb were both privately valued above $50 billion at the height of "sharing economy" enthusiasm, even as the Ashley Madison hack exposed 32 million users and became a cautionary tale about data security. Three quieter developments would matter far more in hindsight than their coverage suggested: the December 11 founding of the AI research lab OpenAI, the July 30 launch of the Ethereum blockchain, and Tesla's October rollout of its first Autopilot software, alongside SpaceX's first successful landing of a reusable Falcon 9 booster on December 22.

    Social Trends

    Black Lives Matter operated at full intensity following Ferguson, driven by the deaths of Walter Scott, Freddie Gray, and Sandra Bland and by a scathing March Justice Department report on Ferguson's police department; the movement's momentum spilled onto college campuses that fall, where protests at the University of Missouri forced the resignation of the university's president and Yale's Halloween-costume dispute intensified a national debate over "safe spaces" and free speech. Caitlyn Jenner's April interview and July Vanity Fair cover pushed transgender identity into mainstream conversation with no real precedent. The Pew Research Center's Religious Landscape Study documented Christians falling to 70.6 percent of Americans while the religiously unaffiliated "nones" rose to 22.8 percent, and Anne Case and Angus Deaton's landmark November paper on rising "deaths of despair" among middle-aged white Americans quietly supplied the empirical foundation for narratives that would dominate the 2016 election. The Fight for $15 minimum-wage movement notched victories in Seattle and Los Angeles, while the Syrian refugee crisis became a political flashpoint after the Paris and San Bernardino attacks, with more than thirty governors announcing they would refuse resettlement.

    Crime

    A white supremacist's murder of nine Black parishioners at Charleston's Emanuel AME Church in June was the year's most consequential act of violence. Terrorism struck domestically twice more: a gunman killed four Marines and a sailor at Chattanooga military facilities in July, and a married couple inspired by ISIS killed fourteen at a San Bernardino holiday event in December. Mass shootings also struck Umpqua Community College in Oregon, nine dead in October, and a Colorado Springs Planned Parenthood clinic, three dead in November. Boston Marathon bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev was sentenced to death in May; Mexican drug lord El Chapo escaped through a mile-long tunnel in July; and Bill Cosby was criminally charged in Pennsylvania in December after dozens of women had come forward. Obama commuted a record number of federal sentences and became the first sitting president to visit a federal prison, part of a rare moment of bipartisan momentum on criminal-justice reform.

    Sports

    The New England Patriots beat the Seattle Seahawks in Super Bowl XLIX on Malcolm Butler's goal-line interception, while Katy Perry's halftime show produced the "Left Shark" meme and the offseason was consumed by "Deflategate." The Golden State Warriors beat Cleveland to win the NBA title, launching the Stephen Curry-led dynasty, and the Kansas City Royals won their first World Series since 1985. Ohio State won the inaugural College Football Playoff championship in January. American Pharoah won horse racing's first Triple Crown in 37 years, and the US women's national team won the World Cup behind Carli Lloyd's first-half hat-trick. Serena Williams completed the "Serena Slam" before a stunning US Open semifinal upset, and Jordan Spieth won both the Masters and US Open at age 21. Floyd Mayweather's long-anticipated bout with Manny Pacquiao grossed record pay-per-view revenue despite a disappointing fight, and mounting attention to football's concussion crisis was reflected in the Christmas Day release of "Concussion."

    Public Mood

    The year felt turbulent and charged rather than settled, a sense that something was cracking open even as the culture itself produced an extraordinary run of acclaimed work. Marriage equality and Pope Francis's visit suggested moral progress, while Charleston, Baltimore, and a string of mass shootings suggested a country coming apart at the seams. Commentators across the political spectrum converged on a diagnosis of American fracturing along racial, class, and religious lines, and Trump's rise, which pundits kept confidently predicting would collapse, was increasingly read as a symptom of that fracturing rather than an aberration from it.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporaries were confident the year's defining stories were the Supreme Court's marriage-equality ruling, the Iran nuclear deal, the Cuba opening, the Paris climate accord, the Charleston massacre and the resulting removal of Confederate symbols, and the ongoing Black Lives Matter movement. These were the stories that topped year-end lists, filled magazine covers, and anchored the president's own retrospective accounting of his final years in office.

    What actually mattered

    Several of the year's celebrated diplomatic achievements proved fragile: the Iran deal would be abandoned in 2018, the Paris commitments would be withdrawn from and later rejoined, the Cuba opening would be substantially reversed, and the just-concluded TPP would never be ratified. What mattered more durably was largely invisible at the time: the collision of a fragmenting media ecosystem with a candidate who intuitively understood it; the quiet founding of the technologies, including generative AI, reusable rockets, and smart-contract blockchains, that would define the following decade; the accelerating opioid and "deaths of despair" curve; the ISIS-driven refugee flow that would recompose European politics and fuel Brexit; and Volkswagen's diesel fraud, which hastened the auto industry's turn toward electrification.

    Most forgotten story

    The July 16 shooting at Chattanooga's military recruiting facilities, in which a gunman inspired by jihadist ideology killed four Marines and a Navy sailor, was a leading story for about a week before being overshadowed by Paris and San Bernardino and displaced entirely by the 2016 campaign, despite killing more service members on US soil than any comparable attack of the decade.

    Most underrated story

    The founding of OpenAI on December 11, 2015, as a billion-dollar nonprofit AI lab backed by Elon Musk, Sam Altman, and others, drew only polite technology-page coverage at year's end. Within seven years its products would trigger the largest inflection in the technology industry since the smartphone and become a locus of geopolitical and cultural conflict, making it the single 2015 event that aged into the greatest importance with the least contemporary notice.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The near-universal misjudgment of Donald Trump's candidacy stands as the year's largest surprise in hindsight. In June 2015, prediction markets, editorial boards, rival campaigns, and The Huffington Post, which filed his campaign under its Entertainment section, all treated Trump as a ratings-driven novelty with a low ceiling. That the man who rode down the escalator that day would win the Republican nomination within a year and the presidency within eighteen months represents one of the largest misreadings in modern American political journalism.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    "Deflategate"The six-month national litigation over NFL football air pressure, including Tom Brady's suspension and federal court filings, consumed enormous sports-media bandwidth but changed nothing about the league or the Patriots, and is remembered mainly as a case study in disproportionate media obsession.
    "The Dress" and viral micro-events like Left Shark and the killing of Cecil the lionEach dominated a news cycle and briefly broke the internet, but each is now recalled as a symptom of peak-viral, algorithmically amplified culture rather than a substantive event.
    Rachel Dolezal's outing as a white woman presenting as BlackThe story produced a week of cable coverage and endless op-eds about race and identity but disappeared almost as fast as it arrived, revealed in retrospect as an oddity rather than a cultural inflection point.
    The three-week manhunt for escaped New York prisoners David Sweat and Richard MattInescapable summer cable programming ended with one escapee dead and one recaptured, producing no lasting policy consequences.
    Kim Davis's refusal to issue same-sex marriage licensesA brief Fox News fixture and a photo-op for presidential candidates Ted Cruz and Mike Huckabee, her role in the culture war evaporated within months.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Donald Trump's June 16 escalator descent and campaign announcementDismissed by serious outlets as a summer novelty, it began the political realignment that produced the Trump presidency, two impeachments, January 6, and a decade of American political convulsion.
    The founding of OpenAI on December 11Covered as a modest philanthropic experiment, it became the organization that shipped GPT-3 and ChatGPT, launching the generative-AI era.
    Ethereum's mainnet launch on July 30Covered with almost no mainstream attention, it became the foundation for DeFi, NFTs, stablecoins, and the crypto boom-bust cycles of the following decade.
    SpaceX's first successful landing of a reusable Falcon 9 booster on December 22Treated as a nice technology story, it was the moment reusable orbital rockets became real, eventually making SpaceX dominant in global launch and enabling Starlink.
    The Aylan Kurdi photograph and the Syrian refugee crisisAmerican coverage was episodic, but the image and the underlying refugee wave reshaped European politics, becoming a proximate cause of Brexit and the rise of the European populist right.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Supreme Court Rules 5-4 That the Constitution Guarantees a Right to Marry
    2. Gunman Kills Nine at Historic Black Church in Charleston
    3. South Carolina Takes Down the Confederate Flag After Decades of Debate
    4. Trump Rides Down an Escalator and Upends the Republican Race
    5. World Powers and Iran Strike Nuclear Deal in Vienna
    6. US Flag Rises Over Havana Embassy for First Time in Over Half a Century
    7. Baltimore Erupts in Unrest After Freddie Gray's Death in Police Custody
    8. Gunmen and Suicide Bombers Kill 130 Across Paris
    9. Married Couple Kills 14 Co-Workers in San Bernardino Terror Attack
    10. Fed Lifts Rates Off Zero for First Time in Nearly a Decade

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryConsumer confidence opens the year near multi-year highs and cheap gasoline lifts household sentiment. Ohio State defeats Oregon on January 12 to win the inaugural College Football Playoff championship. Activist April Reign coins #OscarsSoWhite after the Academy Awards nominate an all-white slate of acting nominees. Shake Shack's IPO kicks off a year that will end up as the domestic stock market's most disappointing since the crisis, and the Republican presidential field, eventually to number seventeen candidates, continues to take shape.
    FebruaryThe New England Patriots defeat the Seattle Seahawks 28-24 in Super Bowl XLIX on Malcolm Butler's goal-line interception, while Katy Perry's halftime show spawns the "Left Shark" meme. The FCC votes to reclassify broadband under Title II in its Open Internet Order, enshrining net neutrality. "Birdman" wins Best Picture at the Oscars for 2014's films. Late in the month, a poorly lit photograph of a dress divides the country over whether it is blue-and-black or white-and-gold, briefly consuming the entire internet.
    MarchThe Justice Department releases its scathing report on the Ferguson Police Department, documenting systemic racial bias. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu addresses a joint session of Congress at Speaker Boehner's invitation to oppose the emerging Iran deal, straining relations with the White House. Kendrick Lamar releases "To Pimp a Butterfly," instantly recognized as a landmark hip-hop album. Obama announces that 9,800 troops will remain in Afghanistan through the year rather than the planned drawdown, and live-streaming apps Periscope and Meerkat debut at SXSW.
    AprilFreddie Gray dies from a spinal injury sustained in Baltimore police custody on April 19; his death triggers riots and a curfew by April 27, with Governor Hogan activating the National Guard, days after video surfaces of a North Charleston officer shooting Walter Scott in the back as he fled. Hillary Clinton formally launches her presidential campaign on April 12. Congress passes the bipartisan MACRA "doc fix." Jordan Spieth wins the Masters at 21, Bruce Jenner's Diane Sawyer interview begins the Caitlyn Jenner story, the Apple Watch ships, and Corinthian Colleges collapses.
    MayAn Amtrak train derails in Philadelphia on May 12, killing eight. Floyd Mayweather defeats Manny Pacquiao in boxing's long-awaited but anticlimactic superfight. American Pharoah wins the Kentucky Derby. Vice President Biden's son Beau dies of brain cancer, a personal tragedy that will shadow Biden's decision on whether to run for president. Boston Marathon bomber Dzhokhar Tsarnaev is sentenced to death, and "Mad Max: Fury Road" opens to rapturous reviews.
    JuneAn extraordinarily consequential month: the Supreme Court legalizes same-sex marriage nationwide in Obergefell v. Hodges (June 26) and preserves Affordable Care Act subsidies in King v. Burwell (June 25). A white supremacist murders nine Black parishioners at Charleston's Emanuel AME Church (June 17). Donald Trump descends the Trump Tower escalator to announce his candidacy (June 16), instantly dominating coverage. American Pharoah completes the first Triple Crown in 37 years, the USA Freedom Act ends bulk NSA metadata collection, two convicted killers escape from a New York prison triggering a three-week manhunt, and the Golden State Warriors beat Cleveland to win the NBA title.
    JulyThe Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA) is announced in Vienna on July 14, and the US and Cuba formally reopen embassies on July 20, ending a 54-year diplomatic freeze. South Carolina removes the Confederate flag from its statehouse grounds. Sandra Bland is found dead in a Texas jail cell days after a pretextual traffic stop, and a gunman kills four Marines and a sailor at Chattanooga military facilities. The Ashley Madison hack becomes public, and the US women's soccer team wins the World Cup behind Carli Lloyd's hat-trick.
    AugustChina's surprise yuan devaluation and a Shanghai stock crash trigger "Black Monday" on Wall Street (August 24), with the Dow plunging over 1,000 points intraday and the VIX spiking above 40. Google announces its reorganization under new holding company Alphabet, the EPA's Clean Power Plan is finalized, and Secretary of State Kerry raises the American flag over the reopened embassy in Havana. "Straight Outta Compton" becomes a surprise box-office hit, Jon Stewart hosts his final "Daily Show" after sixteen years, and FX executive John Landgraf declares American television has reached "Peak TV."
    SeptemberThe Volkswagen diesel-emissions scandal breaks on September 18 after the EPA issues a notice of violation. Pope Francis visits the United States, addressing a joint session of Congress for the first time by any pope, and Speaker John Boehner abruptly announces his resignation days later. Xi Jinping makes a state visit producing a US-China pledge against commercial cyber espionage, the Taliban briefly seize the provincial capital of Kunduz, Afghanistan, Kim Davis is jailed for refusing same-sex marriage licenses in Kentucky, and Stephen Colbert debuts as host of "The Late Show."
    OctoberPaul Ryan is elected Speaker of the House on October 29 after Kevin McCarthy's surprise withdrawal. Dell announces its roughly $67 billion acquisition of EMC, the largest technology deal in history. A US airstrike mistakenly hits a Medecins Sans Frontieres hospital in Kunduz, killing 42, while the Trans-Pacific Partnership negotiations conclude in Atlanta. Tesla rolls out its Autopilot software update, the US Navy conducts its first freedom-of-navigation operation in the contested South China Sea, and "The Martian" draws mainstream audiences.
    NovemberCoordinated terrorist attacks across Paris kill 130 people on November 13, intensifying American debate over refugee resettlement and the fight against ISIS. Republican Matt Bevin wins Kentucky's governorship while Democrat John Bel Edwards wins Louisiana's; Houston voters repeal the city's equal-rights ordinance. Vice President Biden announces he will not run for president, Congress passes the Bipartisan Budget Act suspending the debt ceiling, Anne Case and Angus Deaton publish their landmark paper on rising "deaths of despair," a gunman attacks a Colorado Springs Planned Parenthood clinic killing three, and University of Missouri protests force the resignation of the university president.
    DecemberA married couple inspired by ISIS kills fourteen people at a San Bernardino holiday event on December 2, prompting Trump's call five days later for a "total and complete shutdown" on Muslim entry to the US. The Federal Reserve raises interest rates on December 16 for the first time since 2006, ending seven years of near-zero rates. World leaders adopt the Paris Climate Agreement on December 12, Obama signs the Every Student Succeeds Act and the FAST Act highway bill, "Star Wars: The Force Awakens" opens December 18 to record-shattering box office, SpaceX successfully lands a reusable Falcon 9 booster on December 22, and days earlier, on December 11, a little-noticed nonprofit called OpenAI is founded.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+2.4% (first estimate); later revised to roughly +2.7% to +2.9%
    InflationHeadline CPI +0.7% y/y (Dec 2015), averaging about +0.1% for the year; core CPI +2.1% y/y
    Unemployment5.0% in December, down from 5.7% in January
    S&P 500 return+1.4% total return (price roughly -0.7%)
    Dow returnAbout -2.2% on price (roughly flat total return); first down year since 2008
    Fed funds rateRaised Dec 16 to 0.25-0.50%, the first hike since June 2006, from near-zero since Dec 2008
    Oil priceWTI fell from about $53/barrel in January to $37.04 at Dec 31
    Gold priceAbout $1,060/oz at year-end, down roughly 10-11% for a third straight losing year
    PopulationAbout 321 million
    Median home priceAbout $222,400 (NAR median existing-home sales price, full-year average)
    National debtAbout $18.15 trillion at FY end (Sept 30, 2015); about $18.9 trillion by calendar year-end

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    What did it feel like to be an American in 2015? It felt, more than anything, like living through two different years stacked on top of each other. In one of those years, things were manifestly getting better: the unemployment rate fell to five percent, gas dropped under two dollars a gallon, the housing market kept healing without another bubble, and the Supreme Court delivered a decision, nationwide marriage equality, that millions of Americans had spent a generation fighting for and millions more had, with surprising speed, come to accept. The country reopened an embassy in Havana after fifty-four years, struck a landmark nuclear deal with Iran, and joined nearly two hundred nations in a climate accord in Paris. A pope addressed Congress. For a certain kind of contented, optimistic American, 2015 could plausibly be read as the high summer of the Obama era, the moment the arc of history seemed, once again, to be bending the way people on the political left and center believed it always eventually bent.

    In the other year happening simultaneously, something felt badly broken. A young white supremacist murdered nine people in a Bible study in Charleston, and in the same season Baltimore burned after Freddie Gray's death, adding to a drumbeat of police killings that had begun the year before in Ferguson. A married couple radicalized online walked into a holiday party in San Bernardino and killed fourteen people, months after another gunman killed servicemen in Chattanooga, and after Paris, not American soil, but close enough to feel like a preview, showed what coordinated terrorism could do to a Western capital. And in June, almost as a footnote, a reality-television star and real-estate developer announced a presidential campaign that every serious person in American public life was certain would collapse within weeks. It did not collapse. By autumn, he led every poll, and the country's political and media class was beginning, uneasily, to reckon with the possibility that they had badly misjudged something important about their own country.

    Ordinary daily life, for most Americans, was neither of these things. It was cheaper gasoline, a slowly recovering paycheck, tickets to a new musical called "Hamilton" that were suddenly impossible to get, "Empire" and "Game of Thrones" as appointment television, Adele's comeback album breaking sales records, and a fall spent debating whether a dress was blue or gold. But underneath the ordinary hummed both genuine optimism and genuine anxiety, and by year's end, most thoughtful observers sensed that the anxiety was winning. The year's real headline, a country whose formal institutions were quietly succeeding while its informal fabric was visibly fraying, would not become fully legible until the following November. In 2015 itself, it merely felt like living at a hinge, sensing that something was about to give, without yet knowing which direction it would fall.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2016
    Econ +1Mark +2Poli -1Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The American economy in 2016 delivered its slowest full-year growth of the postcrisis expansion, real GDP up roughly 1.6 percent, even as the labor market kept tightening toward full employment and wages finally began to accelerate. A brutal January-February scare, driven by collapsing oil prices and fears of a Chinese hard landing, gave way to a steadier second half; the United Kingdom's shock vote to leave the European Union in June rattled global markets and briefly froze Federal Reserve policy, while OPEC's late-November decision to cut production ended the two-year shale bust. Equity investors were rewarded handsomely regardless: the S&P 500 returned almost 12 percent and the Dow nearly 17 percent, capped by a ferocious post-election reflation rally that rotated leadership into banks, industrials, and small-caps. The Federal Reserve, under Janet Yellen, managed only one quarter-point hike all year, in December, far short of what it had projected twelve months earlier.

    Politically, 2016 was the most consequential and corrosive year in a generation. Justice Antonin Scalia's sudden death in February touched off an unprecedented Senate refusal to consider any Obama nominee, leaving the Supreme Court short-handed for the balance of the year. A sprawling, insurgent presidential campaign produced two of the most disliked major-party nominees in modern polling, and ended on November 8 with Donald Trump's stunning defeat of Hillary Clinton despite losing the popular vote by nearly three million ballots, an outcome almost no forecaster had treated as likely. FBI Director James Comey's two public interventions in the Clinton email inquiry, confirmed Russian government hacking of Democratic targets, and a campaign that broke long-standing rhetorical and normative boundaries left the country's democratic institutions visibly strained even as Congress passed a genuinely productive slate of bipartisan legislation and the transfer of power, however unsettled the mood surrounding it, proceeded peacefully and on schedule.

    Beneath the political turbulence, American culture produced one of its richest years in memory: Beyonce's Lemonade, Barry Jenkins's Moonlight, Hamilton at the height of its cultural saturation, and Chicago's long-awaited World Series title, alongside a string of shocking celebrity deaths from David Bowie and Prince to Muhammad Ali and Carrie Fisher. Pulse, Dallas, and Baton Rouge made 2016 one of the deadliest years for mass and targeted violence in decades, and technologies from Pokemon Go to Facebook's news feed revealed both the exhilaration and the fragility of a newly algorithmic public square. Taken as a whole, the year now reads less as an ordinary election cycle than as the hinge on which the following decade's politics, technology, and geopolitics turned, even though almost nobody living through it recognized that in real time.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Real GDP growth of about 1.6 percent was the weakest of the expansion, but unemployment fell to 4.7 percent, wage growth accelerated to 2.9 percent, and inflation normalized back toward target, a durable if unspectacular year by the era's post-crisis standard.
    Markets+2The S&P 500 returned nearly 12 percent and the Russell 2000 over 21 percent with broad late-year participation, though a ten-percent January-February drawdown and a sharp Brexit shock kept it just short of an exceptional year.
    Politics-1Genuine bipartisan legislative output and a peaceful transfer of power were offset by an unprecedented ten-month Supreme Court blockade, confirmed foreign election interference, an FBI director's public interventions in the race, and campaign norms that visibly thinned democratic guardrails.
    Culture+1A landmark year for Black American cultural production and cathartic sports triumphs was counterbalanced by an unusually brutal toll of celebrity deaths, mass and targeted violence, and a country that ended the year feeling more divided than it began.
    Overall Mood+0Real prosperity and cultural brilliance sat uneasily beside a bruising, norm-breaking election, an intelligence-community warning about foreign interference, and a widespread sense of civic vertigo, leaving 2016 as a genuinely split verdict rather than a clear positive or negative year.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Donald Trump's stunning election victory over Hillary Clinton despite losing the popular vote
    2. Justice Antonin Scalia's sudden death and the Senate's unprecedented blockade of Merrick Garland
    3. Britain's Brexit vote to leave the European Union and its shock through US markets
    4. The Pulse nightclub massacre in Orlando, the deadliest mass shooting in US history at that time
    5. FBI Director James Comey's twin public interventions in the Clinton email investigation
    6. US intelligence formally attributing DNC and campaign hacking to the Russian government
    7. The Dallas police ambush and Baton Rouge shootings following the deaths of Alton Sterling and Philando Castile
    8. The Chicago Cubs winning the World Series, ending a 108-year championship drought
    9. A relentless year of celebrity deaths, from David Bowie and Prince to Muhammad Ali and Carrie Fisher
    10. Colin Kaepernick's national anthem protests and the Standing Rock Dakota Access Pipeline demonstrations

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The US economy grew about 1.6 percent in real terms in 2016, the slowest full year of the post-2009 expansion, as a weak first half dragged down by an energy-sector investment collapse, an inventory correction, and a strong dollar gave way to firmer momentum once oil prices and industrial activity recovered. Consumer spending, up roughly 2.7 percent, remained the clear engine of growth while business investment barely rose and trade was a modest drag. The labor market nonetheless kept tightening, with unemployment falling from 5.0 percent in January to 4.7 percent at year-end and wage growth firming to its best pace of the expansion, while inflation climbed back over the course of the year to a December headline print of 2.1 percent as energy prices rebounded from their February trough. Housing continued its steady recovery, with existing-home sales hitting a decade high and national home prices finally surpassing their 2006 nominal peak, though mortgage rates jumped sharply after the election.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP growth of about 1.6 percent for the year; unemployment falling from 5.0 percent to a year-end 4.7 percent, with a November low of 4.6 percent; roughly 2.24 million nonfarm payroll jobs added; average hourly earnings up about 2.9 percent year-over-year by December; headline CPI at 2.1 percent and core CPI at 2.2 percent in December; the Federal Reserve's single rate hike of the year, on December 14, to a 0.50-0.75 percent target range; the 10-year Treasury yield swinging from 2.27 percent at the start of the year to an all-time closing low near 1.36 percent in July and back up to 2.44 percent by December 31.

    Major Events

    The year opened with a ferocious January-February growth scare as WTI crude briefly touched about $26 a barrel, high-yield credit spreads blew out past 800 basis points, and fears of a Chinese hard landing pushed the Federal Reserve toward a dovish pause. The June 23 Brexit referendum then set off a two-day, roughly two-trillion-dollar global equity selloff and drove the 10-year Treasury yield to an all-time low, deferring Fed tightening for months. OPEC's November 30 agreement to cut production for the first time in eight years, joined by Russia and other producers in December, put a floor under oil prices and ended the two-year shale-patch downturn that had bankrupted dozens of energy companies, including Peabody Energy and SunEdison in April. In September, Wells Fargo's cross-selling scandal came to light, with the bank paying a $185 million settlement over roughly two million fake accounts and CEO John Stumpf resigning the following month, a landmark moment for post-crisis bank governance. Donald Trump's November 8 election win triggered an overnight futures crash that fully reversed by the next close and inaugurated a sharp reflation rally, and the Fed closed the year with its long-delayed second rate hike of the cycle on December 14.

    Why this score

    The +1 score reflects a durable, if unspectacular, expansion in which the labor market clearly tightened and inflation normalized back toward target even though headline real growth of 1.6 percent was the weakest print of the entire post-crisis recovery, appropriately mid-single-positive for a still-accommodative but maturing expansion.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    US equities delivered strong full-year gains despite one of the most volatile openings in modern memory. The S&P 500 returned about 11.96 percent on a total-return basis, closing at 2,238.83; the Dow Jones Industrial Average returned roughly 16.5 percent, closing at 19,762.60 and repeatedly flirting with 20,000 in the year's final days; the NASDAQ Composite returned about 8.87 percent; and the small-cap Russell 2000 led all major indices with a roughly 21.3 percent total return on the domestic-focused reflation trade. The path there was anything but smooth: the S&P 500 fell about 10.5 percent in the worst New Year's start on record before recovering, then dropped another 5.3 percent in two days after the Brexit vote before fully retracing within a week, and the VIX spiked above 28 in February and above 26 the day after the referendum before averaging a calmer 15.8 for the year as a whole.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    It was an active year for large-cap dealmaking, headlined by AT&T's $85.4 billion agreement to acquire Time Warner, Bayer's $66 billion deal for Monsanto, and Microsoft's $26.2 billion purchase of LinkedIn. Verizon agreed in July to buy Yahoo's core business for $4.83 billion, only for Yahoo's disclosure of two of the largest data breaches in history to cut $350 million from the final price. Wells Fargo's fake-account scandal cost CEO John Stumpf his job, Deutsche Bank shares hit multi-decade lows on fears of a $14 billion Justice Department penalty before settling for $7.2 billion in December, and a wave of bankruptcies swept energy, retail, and shipping, including Peabody Energy, SunEdison, Aeropostale, Sports Authority, and South Korea's Hanjin Shipping, the largest container-shipping collapse in history.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was the post-election 'reflation trade,' which rotated leadership from defensive and mega-cap technology names into cyclicals, banks, and small-caps on hopes for tax cuts, deregulation, and infrastructure spending; financials rose 22.8 percent and energy 27.4 percent for the year while health care and consumer staples lagged. Energy and materials rebounded sharply as OPEC's production cut and a recovering oil price (WTI up about 45 percent to $53.72) ended the shale-patch depression, gold had a two-part year rallying to $1,366 on Brexit fears before giving back gains to close near $1,151, and a weak IPO calendar (roughly $19 billion raised, the least since 2009) reflected caution even as the broader market climbed. Housing-linked and rate-sensitive assets swung hard on the Fed's on-again, off-again tightening path, with mortgage rates falling to an all-time low after Brexit before spiking in the post-election reflation move.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Justice Antonin Scalia was found dead on February 13, and within hours Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell announced the vacancy would not be filled in an election year; Obama's March nominee, Merrick Garland, never received a hearing in a 293-day blockade that held through year's end. The presidential race was upended repeatedly: FBI Director James Comey's unprecedented July 5 press conference declined to charge Hillary Clinton over her private email server while calling her conduct 'extremely careless,' and his October 28 letter reopening the inquiry, followed by a November 6 letter closing it again, injected the Bureau directly into the campaign's final days. Confirmed Russian government hacking of the DNC and John Podesta's emails, released through WikiLeaks across the summer and fall, became a central campaign issue that US intelligence agencies formally attributed to Moscow on October 7, the same day the Access Hollywood tape of Trump's remarks about women surfaced. Omar Mateen's June 12 massacre at Orlando's Pulse nightclub killed 49 people, the deadliest mass shooting in modern US history to that point, and a July stretch in which police killed Alton Sterling and Philando Castile was followed by the killing of five Dallas officers in an ambush and three more in Baton Rouge, the bloodiest fortnight for both civilians and police in recent memory. Donald Trump's November 8 defeat of Clinton, despite losing the popular vote by nearly three million ballots and against nearly every forecaster's expectation, capped a campaign that had already seen a 16-candidate Republican field collapse before Trump's insurgency and Bernie Sanders mount an unexpectedly durable primary challenge from the left.

    Domestic Policy

    Despite a bitterly divided election year, the 114th Congress and President Obama produced a surprising volume of bipartisan legislation: the 21st Century Cures Act in December funded biomedical research and the Cancer Moonshot and streamlined FDA approvals; the Comprehensive Addiction and Recovery Act became the first major federal response to an opioid crisis that killed more than 42,000 Americans that year; PROMESA created a federal oversight board for Puerto Rico's debt crisis; and Congress overrode Obama's veto of JASTA, allowing 9/11 families to sue Saudi Arabia, in the only successful override of his presidency. An eight-member Supreme Court, hobbled by the Garland blockade, struck down Texas's restrictive abortion law in Whole Woman's Health, upheld race-conscious admissions in Fisher, and deadlocked 4-4 in United States v. Texas, killing Obama's expanded immigration executive actions. North Carolina's HB2 'bathroom bill' triggered national boycotts, Colin Kaepernick's anthem protests and the Black Lives Matter movement intensified debates over policing, and the Standing Rock protests against the Dakota Access Pipeline drew thousands before the Army Corps denied its easement in December. There was no government shutdown and no impeachment proceeding, and the peaceful transfer of power went forward on schedule despite an unusually charged atmosphere.

    Foreign Policy

    Obama's final year mixed legacy consolidation with a darkening strategic backdrop. January's Iran nuclear-deal 'Implementation Day' lifted sanctions and freed frozen funds alongside a prisoner swap, while the US-led campaign against ISIS advanced with October's launch of the Mosul offensive even as Aleppo fell to Assad and Russian forces in December after failed ceasefire efforts. Obama became the first sitting president to visit Hiroshima and made a historic trip to Havana, the Trans-Pacific Partnership was signed but never ratified by the Senate as both major candidates opposed it, and the US formally joined the Paris Agreement on climate change in September. North Korea conducted two nuclear tests, Turkey survived a July coup attempt followed by mass purges, and confirmed Russian election interference led Obama to expel 35 Russian diplomats and impose sanctions on December 29, even as the administration broke with precedent by abstaining on a UN resolution condemning Israeli settlements.

    Important Elections

    Donald Trump and Mike Pence won 304 electoral votes to Hillary Clinton and Tim Kaine's 227, flipping Michigan, Wisconsin, Pennsylvania, and other industrial-Midwest states by combined margins under eighty thousand votes, even as Clinton won the national popular vote by roughly 2.87 million ballots. Republicans retained the Senate 52-48 despite losing seats in Illinois and New Hampshire, and kept House control 241-194 despite a smaller-than-expected Democratic gain of six seats; Chuck Schumer succeeded the retiring Harry Reid as Senate Democratic leader and Paul Ryan remained Speaker. In primaries, Trump's insurgent takeover of the Republican Party over a sixteen-candidate field and Bernie Sanders's unexpectedly strong challenge to Clinton, winning roughly 43 percent of pledged delegates, marked the most significant partisan realignments of the decade, while Republicans reached historic dominance at the state level with 33 governorships and full control of 32 legislatures heading into 2017.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Barry Jenkins's Moonlight and Damien Chazelle's La La Land dominated the year's critical conversation alongside Kenneth Lonergan's Manchester by the Sea, Denis Villeneuve's Arrival, and awards contenders Hidden Figures, Fences, Loving, and Lion. At the box office, Disney's grip tightened with Rogue One: A Star Wars Story, Finding Dory, Captain America: Civil War, Zootopia, and a photoreal Jungle Book remake, while Deadpool's R-rated success and Batman v Superman's critical failure defined superhero cinema's extremes; the year's earlier February Oscars had crowned Spotlight Best Picture and finally given Leonardo DiCaprio his Best Actor award amid a second consecutive #OscarsSoWhite controversy.

    Music

    Beyonce's Lemonade, released as an HBO visual album in April, became the year's defining cultural statement, joined by Frank Ocean's long-awaited Blonde, Kanye West's The Life of Pablo, Chance the Rapper's Coloring Book (the first streaming-only album to win a Grammy), Solange's A Seat at the Table, and Drake's Views, which spent thirteen weeks atop the Billboard 200. David Bowie's posthumous Blackstar arrived two days before his death, and streaming decisively overtook downloads as the industry's growth engine in a year widely marked as hip-hop's tipping point over rock as America's dominant genre.

    Television

    Game of Thrones' sixth season and its 'Battle of the Bastards' episode remained the biggest event on television until Netflix's Stranger Things became the summer's runaway phenomenon in July. FX's The People v. O.J. Simpson and ESPN's O.J.: Made in America both revisited the 1990s to critical acclaim, HBO's Westworld and Atlanta premiered to strong reception, and Saturday Night Live posted its highest ratings in two decades on the strength of Alec Baldwin's Trump and Kate McKinnon's Clinton as late-night television reoriented itself almost entirely around the election.

    Technology

    Apple's iPhone 7 eliminated the headphone jack and introduced AirPods, Amazon's Echo and the newly launched Google Home made smart speakers a mainstream category, and Niantic's Pokemon GO became a genuine summer phenomenon that briefly made augmented reality feel imminent. Instagram's Stories launch cloned Snapchat's core feature within days, Tesla unveiled the Model 3 to hundreds of thousands of reservations, Google DeepMind's AlphaGo defeated world champion Lee Sedol in a match later seen as the moment the deep-learning revolution became unignorable, and Samsung's Galaxy Note 7 battery-fire recall became the year's most infamous tech failure, while growing awareness of Facebook's 'filter bubble' and fake-news problem cast a shadow over the industry's self-image.

    Social Trends

    Black Lives Matter remained the year's defining protest movement after the police killings of Alton Sterling and Philando Castile and the Dallas and Baton Rouge police killings that followed, while Colin Kaepernick's anthem kneeling spread from the NFL into wider public debate. The Standing Rock protests against the Dakota Access Pipeline drew thousands of Indigenous activists and allies, North Carolina's HB2 'bathroom bill' triggered a wave of corporate and sports boycotts, and the millennial generation surpassed baby boomers as the largest living American generation even as religiously unaffiliated 'nones' continued to grow fastest among them. The election's aftermath brought the alt-right into mainstream vocabulary and a documented spike in hate incidents alongside the first organizing for the following January's Women's March.

    Crime

    Omar Mateen's massacre at Orlando's Pulse nightclub killed 49 people in the deadliest mass shooting in modern US history to that point, and the Brock Turner Stanford sexual-assault sentencing produced a viral victim-impact statement that became a defining pre-#MeToo document. Ahmad Khan Rahimi's September bombings in Chelsea and Seaside Park, an Ohio State knife-and-car attack, and the conviction of Charleston church shooter Dylann Roof rounded out a violent year, alongside the continuing Apple-FBI encryption standoff over the San Bernardino shooter's iPhone.

    Sports

    The Chicago Cubs won the World Series in a seven-game classic over Cleveland, ending a 108-year championship drought, while the Cleveland Cavaliers overcame a 3-1 deficit to beat the 73-win Golden State Warriors and deliver LeBron James Cleveland's first title in 52 years. At the Rio Olympics the United States topped the medal table behind Michael Phelps's five closing golds, Simone Biles's four golds, and Katie Ledecky's distance-freestyle dominance, while Peyton Manning closed his career atop the Denver Broncos' Super Bowl 50 win, Kobe Bryant retired with a 60-point farewell, and Muhammad Ali's death in June closed out an era.

    Public Mood

    Contemporary journalism treated 2016 as a genuine hinge year, its dominant register a mix of exhaustion and dread punctuated by real cultural brilliance; 'post-truth' was crowned the year's word and 'dumpster fire' entered common usage after a relentless string of celebrity deaths, a norm-breaking campaign, and a stretch of mass and targeted violence that left many Americans feeling the country was coming apart even as Lemonade, Moonlight, Hamilton, the Cubs, and the Cavaliers offered some of the decade's most cathartic and creatively rich moments.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary observers treated 2016 primarily as a story about individuals: Trump's personality, Clinton's emails, Comey's letters, Sanders's insurgency, Boris Johnson's Brexit, Assad's and Putin's aggression, and Duterte's brutality, with cable news and campaign coverage framing the year as an unusually chaotic but ultimately containable episode within a fundamentally stable order.

    What actually mattered

    Historians now point to slower currents that were visible but rarely front-paged in 2016: the collapse of local newspapers and the rise of algorithmic news feeds, the completion of a decade-long educational and racial realignment of the two parties, the shift of global economic gravity toward a China consolidating under Xi Jinping, the arrival of usable deep-learning AI previewed by AlphaGo, and the acceleration of an opioid-driven mortality crisis in working-class communities that would soon be named 'deaths of despair.'

    Most forgotten story

    The Colombian government's September peace agreement with FARC, its narrow October referendum defeat, and its subsequent passage through congress ended 52 years of civil war and won Juan Manuel Santos the Nobel Peace Prize, yet received only modest US coverage sandwiched between the campaign and Aleppo and is barely remembered today outside Colombia specialists.

    Most underrated story

    Russian military intelligence's penetration of the DNC and probing of election infrastructure in at least 21 states, disclosed piecemeal over the summer, was covered at the time mainly as colorful opposition-research sourcing rather than as a sustained state cyber operation whose consequences would include the Mueller investigation, Trump's first impeachment, and a decade of hardened US-Russia relations.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The near-total failure of polling, forecasting, and prediction markets to anticipate a plausible Trump victory, with major models giving Clinton a 91 to 99 percent chance in the final week, revealed an epistemic failure across the political, journalistic, and financial establishment that historians now treat as more consequential than any single voter shift.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Rio Olympics and swimmer Ryan Lochte's fabricated robbery storyIt drove weeks of cable coverage as tabloid gold but by the following decade was a trivia answer that changed nothing about global sport or US-Brazil relations.
    The Cincinnati Zoo's killing of the gorilla HarambeA viral image dominated cable news for a week and spawned a durable meme, but it produced no lasting policy change and vanished from serious retrospective coverage almost immediately.
    The conclusion of Tom Brady's Deflategate suspensionIt received sustained front-page sports coverage for years but shaped no institution beyond NFL discipline procedure and is remembered mainly as a curiosity.
    The spring and summer Zika virus panicWarnings of a domestic epidemic and Olympic-linked spread dominated public-health coverage but the feared mass US outbreak never materialized.
    The Republican primary's personality sideshows, from hand-size jokes to 'Ted Cruz is the Zodiac Killer'Each dominated a news cycle but none explained the underlying story of a party base disconnected from its donor class.
    The Apple-FBI San Bernardino iPhone encryption standoffIt was covered as an epochal privacy-versus-security battle but ended anticlimactically when the FBI paid a vendor to crack the phone, leaving the legal question unresolved and soon forgotten.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The DNC and Podesta email hacks and the later-confirmed identity of the Russian military intelligence units behind themIt was the opening move of a decade of great-power information conflict that launched the Mueller investigation and became the template Russia reused against Ukraine, France, and Germany.
    Cambridge Analytica's psychographic microtargeting work for the Cruz and Trump campaignsThe underlying Facebook data harvesting received only glancing coverage in 2016 but became the field test for a scandal that would not break fully until 2018 and reshaped global data-privacy regulation.
    Google DeepMind's AlphaGo defeating Lee Sedol in MarchConfined largely to tech press at the time, it is now marked by AI historians as the moment the deep-learning revolution became unignorable, a direct line to the 2020s AI boom.
    Xi Jinping's formal designation as 'core leader' of the Communist Party in OctoberCovered as a routine Beijing-desk story, it marked the end of China's collective-leadership era and set the stage for the trade war and great-power competition of the following decade.
    Rodrigo Duterte's election as president of the Philippines in MayTreated mostly as a curiosity, it was an early data point in the global authoritarian-populist wave that political scientists now treat as the decade's defining democratic story.
    The fall of Aleppo to Assad and Russian forces in DecemberCovered primarily as a humanitarian tragedy, it was Russia's first successful post-Cold-War projection of decisive military power abroad, emboldening actions that led toward the 2022 invasion of Ukraine.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Trump Triumphs: Businessman Stuns Clinton in Electoral College Upset
    2. Britain Votes to Leave the European Union; Cameron to Resign
    3. Justice Antonin Scalia Dead at 79; Senate Republicans Vow No Hearing for Obama Nominee
    4. Gunman Kills 49 at Orlando Gay Nightclub in Deadliest Mass Shooting in US History
    5. FBI Director Comey Reopens Clinton Email Inquiry 11 Days Before Election
    6. US Intelligence Concludes Russia Directed Hacks to Aid Trump
    7. Five Dallas Officers Killed in Ambush Following Sterling and Castile Police Shootings
    8. "Access Hollywood" Tape: Trump Recorded Boasting About Groping Women
    9. Chicago Cubs Win World Series, Ending 108-Year Championship Drought
    10. Prince, Music's Purple Icon, Dies at 57 at Paisley Park

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryDavid Bowie died on January 10, two days after releasing Blackstar, opening what would become a year of shocking celebrity losses. Oil prices collapsed toward $26 a barrel and a Chinese growth scare hit global markets, giving the S&P 500 its worst New Year's start on record, while Iran's January 16 'Implementation Day' lifted nuclear sanctions and freed detained Americans including Jason Rezaian. Armed occupiers led by Ammon Bundy took over Oregon's Malheur National Wildlife Refuge, and North Korea conducted its fourth nuclear test.
    FebruaryJustice Antonin Scalia was found dead at a Texas ranch on February 13, and Senate Majority Leader Mitch McConnell announced within hours that no Obama nominee would be considered, setting up a vacancy that would last the rest of the year. The S&P 500 bottomed near its February 11 low before beginning a steady recovery, Denver beat Carolina in Super Bowl 50, and the Apple-FBI standoff over the San Bernardino shooter's iPhone dominated technology-policy coverage.
    MarchPresident Obama nominated Merrick Garland to the Supreme Court on March 16, a nomination Senate Republicans refused to act on. Google DeepMind's AlphaGo defeated Go champion Lee Sedol in Seoul, Obama made a historic visit to Havana, and Donald Trump and Ted Cruz split the month's primary contests as Trump's delegate lead continued to grow toward the Republican nomination.
    AprilPrince died at Paisley Park on April 21, an accidental fentanyl overdose that compounded the year's run of musical losses. Beyonce released Lemonade as an HBO visual album, energy producers Peabody Energy and SunEdison filed for bankruptcy amid the shale-patch downturn, and Kobe Bryant closed his career with a 60-point farewell game.
    MayTrump effectively clinched the Republican nomination in early May after Ted Cruz and John Kasich withdrew following the Indiana primary. Obama became the first sitting president to visit Hiroshima during a valedictory Asia trip that also lifted the US arms embargo on Vietnam, and Rodrigo Duterte was elected president of the Philippines on a platform of extrajudicial killing.
    JuneOmar Mateen killed 49 people at Orlando's Pulse nightclub on June 12 in the deadliest mass shooting in modern US history to that point. Britain's June 23 Brexit referendum shocked global markets and prompted a two-day equity selloff and an all-time-low Treasury yield, an eight-member Supreme Court struck down Texas's restrictive abortion law in Whole Woman's Health and deadlocked on Obama's immigration actions, and Bernie Sanders's primary challenge to Hillary Clinton effectively concluded.
    JulyPolice killings of Alton Sterling in Baton Rouge and Philando Castile in Minnesota were followed on July 7 by Micah Xavier Johnson's ambush killing of five Dallas police officers, the deadliest single incident for US law enforcement since 9/11, and further officer killings in Baton Rouge ten days later. FBI Director James Comey declined to recommend charges against Hillary Clinton over her email server while calling her conduct 'extremely careless,' Turkey survived a coup attempt, WikiLeaks began releasing hacked DNC emails that forced Debbie Wasserman Schultz's resignation, and Niantic's Pokemon GO became a nationwide phenomenon.
    AugustThe Rio Olympics ran August 5-21, with Michael Phelps, Simone Biles, and Katie Ledecky headlining a dominant US medal haul overshadowed briefly by swimmer Ryan Lochte's fabricated robbery story. San Francisco 49ers quarterback Colin Kaepernick began kneeling during the national anthem in preseason games, and Frank Ocean finally released Blonde after a long-awaited delay.
    SeptemberWells Fargo's cross-selling scandal came to light with a $185 million settlement over millions of fake accounts, prompting CEO John Stumpf's resignation the following month. The United States formally joined the Paris climate agreement, Colombia signed a peace deal with FARC after 52 years of civil war, Ahmad Khan Rahimi's bombings struck Chelsea and Seaside Park, and the first Trump-Clinton presidential debate drew a record television audience.
    OctoberThe Access Hollywood tape of Donald Trump's remarks about women surfaced on October 7, the same day US intelligence agencies formally attributed DNC hacking to the Russian government. Colombian voters narrowly rejected the FARC peace deal in a referendum before it was pushed through congress, the US-backed offensive to retake Mosul from ISIS began, and FBI Director Comey's October 28 letter disclosing newly found Clinton emails roiled the campaign's final stretch.
    NovemberThe Chicago Cubs won the World Series on November 2, ending a 108-year championship drought, and James Comey's second letter on November 6 reclosed the email inquiry days before Donald Trump's stunning November 8 election victory over Hillary Clinton. Fidel Castro died on November 25, Leonard Cohen died on November 7, and the cast of Hamilton addressed Vice President-elect Mike Pence from the stage.
    DecemberThe Federal Reserve delivered its only rate hike of the year on December 14, and equity markets closed out a powerful post-election reflation rally with the Dow just short of 20,000. Aleppo fell to Assad and Russian forces after years of siege, the Army Corps of Engineers denied the Dakota Access Pipeline easement at Standing Rock, Obama expelled 35 Russian diplomats over election interference, and George Michael and Carrie Fisher's deaths closed out an unusually devastating year for celebrity losses.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+1.6% real GDP growth (annual average, BEA)
    Inflation2.1% headline CPI (December y/y); 2.2% core CPI; 1.6% PCE
    Unemployment4.7% year-end (U-3); low of 4.6% in November
    S&P 500 return+11.96% total return (closed at 2,238.83)
    Dow return+16.5% total return (closed at 19,762.60)
    Fed funds rate0.50%-0.75% (single 25bp hike, December 14)
    Oil priceWTI $53.72/barrel year-end (+~45% for the year); Brent $56.82
    Gold price$1,151/oz year-end (+~8-9% for the year)
    Population~323 million
    Median home price~$233,900 median existing-home sale price (NAR)
    National debt~$19.98 trillion total public debt by calendar year-end; ~$14.4 trillion held by the public

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2016 was to feel two contradictory things at once and never quite reconcile them. On one hand, the daily texture of life was, for most people, better than it had been in years: gas was cheap, unemployment kept falling, paychecks were finally growing again, home values had climbed back above their 2006 peak, and the stock market notched double-digit gains. On the other hand, the year's public narrative felt like a slow-motion nervous breakdown. It began with a market panic and a Supreme Court death, moved through a presidential primary season that made a mockery of both parties' establishments, and built through a summer of mass shootings, police killings, and retaliatory violence that made the news feel physically dangerous to watch. By autumn, an FBI director was intervening in an election twice in eleven days, a foreign government was confirmed to be hacking a major party, and a recorded conversation about assaulting women was competing for airtime with all of it. Then, on November 8, the country elected the candidate almost every serious forecaster had called a long shot, and millions of Americans went to bed uncertain whether they had just witnessed a democratic renewal or a democratic emergency.

    What made 2016 strange, in hindsight, was how little the anxiety displaced the ordinary pleasures of American life. People still queued for Hamilton tickets, obsessively played Pokemon GO in parking lots, and wept, genuinely, when the Cubs finally won it after 108 years. Beyonce's Lemonade and Moonlight felt like statements of purpose from a culture working through its own reckonings even as the political class seemed to be losing its grip on consensus reality. The deaths of Bowie, Prince, Ali, Cohen, George Michael, and Carrie Fisher gave the year an almost gothic soundtrack, reinforcing a feeling, only half in jest, that 2016 itself was a malevolent entity. That the same twelve months could produce Lemonade and Pulse, the Cubs and Dallas, AirPods and a $14 billion Deutsche Bank scare, was disorienting in a way that no single score can capture.

    For partisans on either side, the emotional register of the year's final weeks diverged completely: elation and vindication for Trump's coalition, disbelief and, for many, genuine fear for Clinton's. But almost everyone, regardless of which side of November 8 they woke up on, sensed that something structural had shifted rather than merely that an election had been won or lost. The institutions that were supposed to referee American public life — pollsters, newsrooms, the FBI, the Supreme Court, even Facebook's news feed — had each, in their own way, been shown to be more fallible or more compromised than most people had believed possible in January.
