    JuneNationwide protests over policing and racial justice continued, prompting the removal of Confederate monuments, corporate diversity pledges, and debate over 'defunding the police.' The Supreme Court ruled in Bostock v. Clayton County that federal law protects LGBTQ workers from discrimination, China imposed its Hong Kong National Security Law on June 30, and OpenAI quietly released GPT-3, an event almost unnoticed outside the tech press at the time.
    JulyThe administration ordered the closure of China's Houston consulate amid escalating tensions, deployed federal officers to Portland amid ongoing protests, and announced withdrawal from the World Health Organization. Ghislaine Maxwell was arrested on Epstein-related charges, the federal government resumed executions after a 17-year pause, and Major League Baseball opened a shortened 60-game season.
    AugustThe Beirut port exploded on August 4 in one of history's largest non-nuclear blasts, and California's wildfires produced the state's first-ever 'gigafire.' The U.S. brokered the Abraham Accords normalizing relations between Israel and the UAE, Kamala Harris was named Biden's running mate, and the shooting of Jacob Blake in Kenosha on August 23 was followed two days later by Kyle Rittenhouse's fatal shootings of protesters.
    SeptemberJustice Ruth Bader Ginsburg died on September 18, and President Trump nominated Amy Coney Barrett just eight days later. San Francisco's Bay Area woke to an apocalyptic orange sky from wildfire smoke on September 9, Israel and Bahrain signed a normalization agreement, and the first presidential debate, marked by chaotic interruptions, was widely panned.
    OctoberPresident Trump tested positive for COVID-19 on October 2 and was briefly hospitalized at Walter Reed. The Senate confirmed Amy Coney Barrett on October 26, cementing a 6-3 conservative Supreme Court majority, the Los Angeles Lakers won the NBA title in the Orlando bubble, and the Justice Department filed its antitrust suit against Google.
    NovemberJoe Biden defeated Donald Trump in the presidential election, a result called by major networks on November 7, but Trump refused to concede and launched dozens of lawsuits contesting the outcome. Pfizer's November 9 announcement of a highly effective COVID-19 vaccine triggered a sharp market rotation, while Defense Secretary Mark Esper was fired and CISA director Christopher Krebs, who had defended the election's integrity, was dismissed as well.
    DecemberThe FDA authorized the Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna vaccines for emergency use, and nurse Sandra Lindsay became the first American vaccinated outside a clinical trial on December 14, the same day the Electoral College formally confirmed Biden's win. Congress passed a $900 billion year-end relief package on December 27, the SolarWinds hack was disclosed as one of the worst cyber-espionage breaches in federal history, and President Trump issued a wave of controversial pardons including Michael Flynn, Roger Stone, and Paul Manafort.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth-2.8% real GDP for the year (BEA revised; originally reported as -3.5%), with Q2 at -31.2% annualized and Q3 at +33.8% annualized
    Inflation1.4% CPI year-over-year in December (dipped to 0.1% in May); core CPI 1.6%
    Unemployment3.5% (February) to 14.7% (April) to 6.7% (December)
    S&P 500 return+18.4% total return (+16.3% price)
    Dow return+9.7% total return (+7.2% price)
    Fed funds rateCut to 0-0.25% via two emergency moves in March (from 1.50-1.75%), held there through year-end
    Oil priceWTI closed near $48.52/barrel year-end (briefly traded at -$37.63 intraday on April 20); Brent closed near $51
    Gold priceApproximately $1,895/oz year-end, up about 25% for the year (record intraday high of $2,067 in August)
    PopulationApproximately 331.4 million (2020 Census, April 1 reference date); year-over-year growth of just 0.35%, the lowest since at least 1900
    Median home priceApproximately $309,800 (NAR median existing-home price, December), up about 13% year-over-year
    National debtApproximately $27.75 trillion in total public debt outstanding at year-end (about 128% of GDP)

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2020 was to live inside a year that refused to let up. It began, for most people, almost normally — a presidential impeachment trial droned on, primary season unfolded, the stock market drifted toward new highs — and then, within about two weeks in mid-March, everything familiar simply stopped. Schools closed. Offices emptied. Restaurants shuttered. Sports vanished from television. For a population that had never experienced anything like a synchronized national shutdown, the disorientation was total, and it did not lift for months. Grocery runs became fraught expeditions; hospital workers became the year's most visible heroes and, later, its most exhausted casualties; nearly 350,000 Americans died by year's end, a toll that would have seemed unthinkable in February.

    Yet 2020 was not simply a story of loss. It was also, unmistakably, a year of adaptation and improvisation. Living rooms became offices, classrooms, and gyms. Zoom calls, banana bread, and Animal Crossing filled hours that used to belong to commutes and social lives. Families found new, sometimes moving ways to mark birthdays, graduations, and even funerals from a distance. And running alongside the pandemic was an equally consuming reckoning over race and policing, ignited by George Floyd's killing in May, that put more Americans in the streets, by any credible estimate, than any protest movement in the nation's history — a summer that reshaped corporate America, toppled monuments, and hardened political divisions that had already been calcifying for years.

    Politically, the year offered no rest: an impeachment acquittal in February, a Supreme Court vacancy and rushed confirmation in the fall, and a presidential election in November that a sitting president refused to accept, previewing a constitutional crisis that would fully arrive weeks into the new year. For those watching markets and portfolios, the experience was almost surreal — a 34% crash in a matter of weeks followed by a face-ripping rally to record highs, evidence that the country containing the crisis and the country registering it in asset prices were, in some sense, living in different realities. That divergence, between a resilient, even booming, financial system and a battered, grieving, unemployed, and anxious populace, was the defining paradox of the year.

    By December, as the first vaccine doses went into American arms, there was, for the first time since March, a genuine and widely shared sense that an end was visible, even if still distant. Historians would later argue that 2020's real significance lay less in its headlines than in the quieter decisions — about vaccines, about monetary policy, about remote work, about a curious new AI model — that would shape the decade to come. But for the people living through it, none of that mattered yet. What mattered was survival, grief, isolation, and the exhausted hope that 2021 might, somehow, be better.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2021
    Econ +2Mark +2Poli -1Cult +0Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    In 2021, the United States lived through the year in which the trauma of 2020 both ended and mutated. It opened with the first attack on the peaceful transfer of presidential power in American history, when a mob of Donald Trump's supporters stormed the Capitol on January 6 to disrupt certification of Joe Biden's election; it closed with a country economically overheated, politically exhausted, and staring uneasily at a Russian army massing on Ukraine's border and a fast-mutating Omicron variant filling emergency rooms. In between, vaccines arrived fast enough to produce a genuine "hot vax summer," only for the Delta wave to reintroduce fear, mandates, and division by the fall. Biden was inaugurated under National Guard occupation of the capital and governed with a 50-50 Senate that a single pair of Georgia runoffs and a vice-presidential tiebreaker had barely delivered to his party.

    Economically, 2021 was, on paper, one of the best years in modern American history: real GDP grew at its fastest pace since 1984, unemployment fell from 6.4% to 3.9%, and a $1.9 trillion stimulus bill plus a genuinely bipartisan $1.2 trillion infrastructure law both passed into law within the same twelve months. But the same forces that powered the boom -- enormous fiscal support, a snapped-back consumer, and a supply chain broken by the pandemic and amplified by a container ship wedged sideways in the Suez Canal -- also produced the fastest inflation in four decades, which the Federal Reserve and the administration spent the year insisting, wrongly, was "transitory." Markets soared regardless: the S&P 500 returned nearly 29%, punctuated by a retail-trader assault on Wall Street short-sellers, a blow-off top in cryptocurrency and meme stocks, and a record-breaking wave of IPOs and SPACs.

    Culturally and politically, the year felt like whiplash. Derek Chauvin's conviction, the Kyle Rittenhouse acquittal, Squid Game, Olivia Rodrigo, and the fall of Kabul all competed for a shrinking national attention span already worn down by eighteen months of pandemic. Glenn Youngkin's upset win in Virginia in November signaled the midterm wave to come. Yet the stories Americans argued about loudest that year -- January 6's culpability, critical race theory in schools, vaccine mandates -- were mostly relitigating the recent past; the developments quietly reshaping the future, from a Russian invasion being staged in plain sight to a large-language-model coding tool almost no one outside Silicon Valley noticed, arrived largely unremarked. It was, more than anything, the hinge year between the pandemic-and-Trump era and the era of inflation, war, and artificial intelligence that followed.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+2Real GDP grew at its fastest pace since 1984 and unemployment nearly halved over the year, a historic recovery that outweighs an inflation problem few besides hindsight fully priced in at the time.
    Markets+2The S&P 500 returned almost 29% with 70 record closes and broad-based gains across cyclicals and tech, even as meme-stock mania, crypto's blow-off top, and a record SPAC boom signaled speculative excess.
    Politics-1A violent attempt to disrupt a certified election and a chaotic end to the Afghanistan war outweighed real bipartisan wins on infrastructure and pandemic relief, even judged against an already polarized era.
    Culture+0Genuine creative highs like Squid Game and Olivia Rodrigo's SOUR offset pandemic exhaustion, mass-casualty tragedies, and a public mood that landed closer to "languishing" than to celebration.
    Overall Mood+0A booming economy and returning cultural vitality were counterbalanced by insurrection, a chaotic war's end, resurgent COVID waves, and rising prices, leaving Americans simultaneously relieved and drained by year's end.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. A mob of Trump supporters stormed the U.S. Capitol on January 6 to try to stop certification of Biden's election victory.
    2. Joe Biden and Kamala Harris were inaugurated on January 20 under the protection of 25,000 National Guard troops.
    3. Biden signed the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan, sending most Americans a $1,400 stimulus check.
    4. Derek Chauvin was convicted on all counts for murdering George Floyd.
    5. Vaccines rolled out nationwide, fueling a giddy reopening before the Delta variant reversed the mood by summer's end.
    6. The U.S. withdrawal from Afghanistan collapsed into chaos as Kabul fell and 13 U.S. service members died in an airport bombing.
    7. Congress passed a genuinely bipartisan $1.2 trillion infrastructure law, even as its larger Build Back Better companion collapsed in the Senate.
    8. Inflation climbed all year to a four-decade high of 7%, forcing the Fed to abandon its "transitory" framing.
    9. Glenn Youngkin's upset win in Virginia's governor's race was read nationwide as a warning of Democratic trouble ahead.
    10. Facebook renamed itself Meta and pivoted toward the "metaverse" just as a Russian troop buildup on Ukraine's border began drawing warnings from Washington.

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy staged one of the fastest recoveries in its history in 2021, as vaccine-driven reopening, unleashed pent-up consumer demand, and roughly $2.5 trillion in accumulated household savings combined with the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan to produce the strongest annual growth since 1984. Unemployment fell from 6.4% in January to 3.9% in December as employers added roughly 6.7 million jobs, even as a historic wave of quits -- the "Great Resignation" -- reshaped bargaining power between workers and employers. The same forces that drove the boom, however, collided with a battered global supply chain to produce the fastest inflation in four decades, a problem the Federal Reserve spent most of the year dismissing as "transitory" before reversing course in November and December.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew roughly 5.7% on a Q4/Q4 basis (later revised toward 5.8-5.9%), lifting nominal GDP to about $23.3 trillion. Consumer prices rose from 1.4% year-over-year in January to 7.0% by December, the highest reading since June 1982, with core CPI ending the year at 5.5% and full-year average inflation near 4.7%. The unemployment rate fell from 6.7% at the end of 2020 to 3.9% in December 2021, though labor-force participation remained stuck around 61.9%, well below its pre-pandemic level, and average hourly earnings rose roughly 4.7%. Housing prices rose faster than at any point in the history of the Case-Shiller index, up about 18.8% for the year, while the 30-year mortgage rate ended near 3.11% after touching a record low of 2.65% in January. Federal debt held by the public reached roughly $23.0 trillion, with total gross debt near $29.6 trillion, and Congress raised the debt ceiling twice, including a $2.5 trillion increase in December after a partisan standoff.

    Major Events

    Biden signed the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan on March 11, delivering $1,400 stimulus checks, extended unemployment supplements, and an expanded Child Tax Credit that began monthly payments in July. Supply chains buckled under the weight of the reopening: container ships backed up for miles off Los Angeles and Long Beach, semiconductor shortages idled auto plants and sent used-car prices up roughly 37%, and the six-day Suez Canal blockage by the container ship Ever Given in March became the visual symbol of a global logistics crisis with much deeper roots. In November, Biden signed the $1.2 trillion Bipartisan Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, one of the only genuinely bipartisan legislative achievements of his presidency, even as Senator Joe Manchin's late-December refusal to support the companion Build Back Better social-spending bill killed that larger package. The Federal Reserve, having insisted for most of the year that inflation would prove transitory, announced tapering of its asset purchases in November, doubled the pace in December, and signaled three rate hikes for 2022 as Chair Jerome Powell, renominated by Biden, formally retired the word "transitory" in Senate testimony.

    Why this score

    The fastest annual growth in nearly four decades and a labor market that recovered far faster than after any prior recession outweigh the inflation problem that was still widely -- if wrongly -- considered temporary by the people responsible for managing it, earning the year a strong positive score even though, in hindsight, 2021 planted the seeds of 2022's pain.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities had an unambiguously strong year: the S&P 500 returned about 28.7% including dividends and closed at a record high 70 separate times, the second-most in any calendar year on record. The Dow Jones Industrial Average returned roughly 20.9%, the Nasdaq Composite about 22.2%, and the small-cap Russell 2000 a comparatively modest 14.8%. Realized volatility stayed moderate for most of the year, with a maximum drawdown of only around 5%, even as leadership rotated from reopening cyclicals and value stocks early in the year to mega-cap technology in the second half.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    GameStop became the year's most improbable story when retail traders organized on Reddit's r/wallstreetbets squeezed hedge funds short the stock, sending shares from about $17 to an intraday peak of $483 in late January and forcing Robinhood's controversial trading restrictions and a congressional hearing. Archegos Capital Management, Bill Hwang's family office, collapsed in late March after concentrated swap positions unwound, costing Credit Suisse $5.5 billion and Nomura roughly $2.9 billion. Coinbase went public via direct listing in April at an $86 billion valuation, Rivian's November IPO briefly made it worth more than Ford and General Motors combined, and roughly 1,035 U.S. IPOs -- including about 613 SPACs -- raised a record $317 billion over the year. AT&T announced it would spin off WarnerMedia to merge with Discovery as part of a record ~$5.9 trillion year for global M&A.

    Investment Themes

    Bitcoin roughly doubled from about $29,000 to an all-time high near $69,000 in November before easing to end the year around $46,300, while Ethereum multiplied more than fourfold and NFTs became a genuine speculative asset class, epitomized by Beeple's $69 million Christie's sale. Growth and speculative technology stocks, epitomized by the ARK Innovation ETF, peaked in February and fell roughly 24% for the year after a blistering 2020, even as the broader Nasdaq notched solid gains. The 10-year Treasury yield swung from 0.93% to 1.74% and back to 1.19% before ending near 1.52%, reflecting an unresolved market debate over whether inflation was truly transitory. China's regulatory crackdown on its own technology, education, and property sectors -- culminating in Evergrande's default on roughly $300 billion in liabilities -- wiped about $1.5 trillion off Chinese equities and became a cautionary tale for global investors.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    On January 6, a mob of Trump supporters stormed the Capitol in an attempt to disrupt certification of Biden's Electoral College victory, an attack that left five people dead, injured roughly 140 police officers, and led to Trump's historic second impeachment; he was acquitted by the Senate on February 13 with seven Republicans voting to convict. Biden and Kamala Harris -- the first woman, first Black American, and first person of South Asian descent to serve as vice president -- were inaugurated on January 20 under the protection of some 25,000 National Guard troops. The American Rescue Plan passed in March on a party-line vote, the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law passed in November with meaningful Republican support, and the larger Build Back Better package collapsed in December when Joe Manchin withdrew his support. Afghanistan's government collapsed with stunning speed in August as the Taliban retook the country; Kabul fell on August 15, an ISIS-K suicide bombing at the airport's Abbey Gate killed 13 U.S. service members on August 26, and the last American troops departed on August 30, ending the twenty-year war. The Supreme Court allowed Texas's six-week abortion ban, SB 8, to take effect in September and heard oral argument in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization in December, signaling its willingness to overturn Roe v. Wade. Republican Glenn Youngkin's November victory in Virginia, alongside a near-loss for New Jersey's Democratic governor, was widely read as a warning of a difficult 2022 midterm environment for Democrats.

    Domestic Policy

    Democrats governed with the barest possible majority: a 50-50 Senate delivered by the January 5 Georgia runoff wins of Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff, broken only by Vice President Harris's tiebreaking vote, alongside Nancy Pelosi's slim House majority. The administration's first year unfolded in three phases -- pandemic relief and an accelerated vaccine rollout, a split infrastructure and social-spending push in which the former succeeded and the latter collapsed, and a wave of executive orders on climate, racial equity, and LGBTQ+ protections. Inflation, initially dismissed as transitory by both the administration and the Fed, hardened into the year's dominant economic story as consumer prices rose 7% year-over-year by December. Immigration became one of the toughest political fronts of the year, with migrant encounters at the southwest border exceeding 1.7 million in fiscal 2021 and roughly 15,000 mostly Haitian migrants massing at Del Rio, Texas, in September. Derek Chauvin's conviction for George Floyd's murder was treated as a rare accountability moment even as the broader George Floyd Justice in Policing Act stalled in the Senate; Biden did sign legislation making Juneteenth a federal holiday in June. A near-catastrophic debt-ceiling standoff was twice resolved without default, and Biden renominated Jerome Powell as Fed chair in November while elevating Lael Brainard to vice chair.

    Foreign Policy

    Biden's foreign policy debut emphasized "America is back" multilateralism: rejoining the Paris Agreement and WHO on day one, extending the New START treaty with Russia in February, and touring the G7, NATO, and a Geneva summit with Vladimir Putin in June. Afghanistan overshadowed everything else in foreign affairs -- the speed of the Taliban's advance, the chaotic Kabul airlift that ultimately evacuated more than 120,000 people, the Abbey Gate bombing, and a U.S. drone strike that killed ten civilians (later acknowledged as a tragic mistake) badly damaged the administration's credibility with allies. Strategic competition with China intensified, marked by a combative March meeting in Anchorage, the September announcement of the AUKUS submarine pact with Australia and the United Kingdom (which enraged France badly enough to briefly recall its ambassador), and a virtual Biden-Xi summit in November. Russia moved from a policy irritant to the year's gravest strategic threat: after the SolarWinds, Colonial Pipeline, and JBS ransomware attacks, U.S. intelligence detected an unprecedented Russian military buildup near Ukraine beginning in the fall, prompting Biden to warn Putin of "severe consequences" by December -- two months before the actual invasion. An 11-day war between Israel and Hamas in May killed roughly 260 Palestinians and 13 Israelis before a U.S.-brokered ceasefire, indirect talks to revive the Iran nuclear deal made only modest headway in Vienna, and the U.S. rejoined global climate diplomacy at the Glasgow COP26 summit in November.

    Important Elections

    The single most consequential election of the year came before Biden even took office: the January 5 Georgia Senate runoffs, in which Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff defeated Kelly Loeffler and David Perdue, handing Democrats effective Senate control. On November 2, Republican Glenn Youngkin defeated Terry McAuliffe in Virginia's governor's race while Democrat Phil Murphy barely survived reelection in New Jersey, both results read as a warning of a strong Republican 2022. Governor Gavin Newsom easily defeated a September recall attempt in California, New York City elected former police captain Eric Adams as mayor on a public-safety message, and Boston elected Michelle Wu as its first woman and first person of color as mayor.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Theatrical exhibition slowly convalesced through simultaneous streaming-and-theater releases before ending the year with a clear answer: "Spider-Man: No Way Home," opening in December to $260 million domestically, proved audiences would still turn out for spectacle, while Denis Villeneuve's "Dune" reset the visual bar for blockbuster science fiction. "Nomadland," Chloé Zhao's recessionary, pandemic-adjacent drama, won Best Picture at the April Oscars, with Zhao becoming only the second woman to win Best Director, while Jane Campion's "The Power of the Dog" and Bo Burnham's lockdown-filmed Netflix special "Inside" anchored the year's more introspective awards conversation.

    Music

    Olivia Rodrigo's January single "drivers license" and May debut album "SOUR" made her the year's defining new pop star, while Adele's November comeback album "30" posted the year's biggest debut week on the strength of "Easy on Me." Taylor Swift released two re-recorded albums, "Fearless (Taylor's Version)" and "Red (Taylor's Version)," the latter featuring the ten-minute "All Too Well." Dua Lipa's "Levitating" topped the year-end Hot 100, Kanye West's "Donda" and Drake's "Certified Lover Boy" staged a much-hyped rollout rivalry, and TikTok cemented itself as the dominant force in music discovery, breaking songs from Måneskin to Doja Cat.

    Television

    "Squid Game" became Netflix's biggest debut ever in September, drawing over 100 million viewers and turning a Korean-language drama into a genuine watercooler phenomenon for American audiences. "Ted Lasso" won the Emmy for Best Comedy and cemented Apple TV+ as a serious competitor, HBO's "Mare of Easttown" and "The White Lotus" anchored a strong prestige-drama year, and Disney+ used "WandaVision," "Loki," and "The Falcon and the Winter Soldier" to extend the Marvel brand into episodic television.

    Technology

    The iPhone 13 launched alongside a broader chip shortage that made PlayStation 5 and Xbox Series X consoles nearly impossible to find at retail all year. Crypto had a maximalist year, with Bitcoin nearing $69,000, Coinbase going public, and El Salvador adopting Bitcoin as legal tender, while NFT marketplaces briefly rivaled traditional art auction houses. TikTok surpassed one billion monthly users and reshaped music, fashion, and news consumption; the May Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack put cybersecurity into everyday conversation after triggering real East Coast gasoline shortages; and Facebook's October rebrand as Meta -- a bet on the "metaverse" amid Frances Haugen's whistleblower revelations -- became the year's dominant tech narrative, even as GitHub's little-noticed June preview of Copilot, the first mass-market product built on a large language model, quietly foreshadowed where the industry was actually headed.

    Social Trends

    The racial reckoning that defined 2020 continued but fractured in 2021: Derek Chauvin's conviction was treated as a rare accountability moment, but broader police-reform legislation stalled, and "critical race theory" became a manufactured flashpoint in school-board elections nationwide. The March 16 Atlanta-area spa shootings, which killed eight people including six Asian women, made anti-Asian violence a defining social story and spurred nationwide #StopAsianHate rallies. Labor reasserted itself through the "Great Resignation" and a wave of walkouts nicknamed "Striketober" at John Deere, Kellogg's, and Kaiser Permanente, while Starbucks workers in Buffalo voted in December to become the company's first unionized U.S. store. Texas's SB 8 effectively banned abortion after six weeks in September, foreshadowing the following year's Dobbs decision.

    Crime

    Homicide rates, which had spiked in 2020, stayed elevated in many cities through 2021. The disappearance and death of Gabby Petito became a national obsession that prompted broader conversation about media bias in missing-persons coverage; the Ghislaine Maxwell trial ended in a conviction in December; R. Kelly was convicted on federal racketeering and sex-trafficking charges in September; and the November acquittal of Kyle Rittenhouse in the 2020 Kenosha shootings reignited debate over self-defense law. A car plowed through the Waukesha Christmas parade in late November, killing six, and a crowd crush at Travis Scott's Astroworld festival killed ten in one of the year's genuine tragedies.

    Sports

    Tom Brady won his seventh Super Bowl, his first outside New England, as Tampa Bay beat Kansas City in February, and the Atlanta Braves and Milwaukee Bucks won the World Series and NBA Finals respectively. The pandemic-delayed, spectator-less Tokyo Olympics produced the year's defining sports story when gymnast Simone Biles withdrew from several events to protect her mental health, a decision that reset public conversation about elite-athlete pressure and was echoed by tennis star Naomi Osaka's earlier withdrawal from the French Open; 18-year-old qualifier Emma Raducanu's stunning U.S. Open title provided a rare feel-good counterpoint.

    Public Mood

    The year felt like emotional whiplash: a Capitol riot and winter COVID wave gave way to vaccine-fueled "hot vax summer" euphoria by June, only for the Delta variant to reintroduce fear and mandate fights by August, followed by an inflation-and-Afghanistan autumn that curdled into a widely diagnosed national mood of "languishing" -- Adam Grant's April essay on the subject became one of the year's most-shared pieces of writing. Trust in institutions from the CDC to the media to elections fell sharply, even as genuine creative highs -- Squid Game, Olivia Rodrigo, Bo Burnham's Inside -- suggested American culture had fragmented and gone online rather than died.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary Americans believed the defining stories of 2021 were the survival of democracy after January 6, the up-and-down battle against COVID through vaccines, variants, and mandates, and the racial reckoning playing out through the Chauvin and Rittenhouse trials and corporate DEI commitments -- these were the subjects that filled cable news, drove subscriptions, and dominated dinner-table arguments all year.

    What actually mattered

    The developments that would actually reshape the following five years were quieter: the Federal Reserve's mistaken insistence that inflation was transitory, Russia's autumn military buildup around Ukraine, the FDA's approval of semaglutide for weight management, GitHub's technical preview of an AI coding assistant, Xi Jinping's consolidation of power through a crackdown on China's private tech sector, a global minimum corporate tax agreement among 136 countries, and a bipartisan infrastructure law that began quietly reindustrializing parts of the country -- none of which drew anywhere near the sustained attention of the year's culture-war trials.

    Most forgotten story

    The May 2021 Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack, which caused real gasoline shortages and panic-buying across the Southeast and prompted an emergency presidential cybersecurity order, dominated front pages for a week and then vanished from memory almost entirely as ransomware became so routine in subsequent years that no single incident, however disruptive, still seemed novel.

    Most underrated story

    The Federal Reserve's insistence throughout 2021 that inflation was "transitory" was treated at the time as a technical dispute among economists, but in hindsight it is the single policy misjudgment that most shaped the following half-decade of American economic and political life, from the aggressive 2022-23 rate-hike cycle to the 2023 regional-bank stresses to the inflation-driven political mood of 2024.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    Meta's October rebrand and metaverse pivot looked bold at the time -- a $900 billion company reorganizing around a big technological bet -- but in hindsight reads as one of the largest strategic misreads by a major tech CEO in a generation, since Mark Zuckerberg poured tens of billions into VR headsets during precisely the window when generative AI was actually crystallizing at OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google; a close runner-up is that the Afghanistan withdrawal, treated in August as possibly presidency-defining, turned out not to be what most damaged Biden -- inflation, immigration, and age were.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Ever Given container ship blocking the Suez Canal for six days in MarchThe ship was refloated within a week and the episode changed nothing structurally; the actual multi-year supply-chain crisis had causes -- port shutdowns, container imbalances, chip shortages -- that had nothing to do with one grounded vessel, leaving the Suez blockage as a meme and a visual metaphor rather than a turning point.
    The Britney Spears conservatorship saga and its #FreeBritney resolution in NovemberIt mattered enormously to Spears and to a small legal-reform community focused on conservatorship abuse, but its broader cultural footprint faded within about eighteen months.
    The Harry and Meghan Oprah interview in MarchIt broke international viewership records and dominated a week of coverage, but the British monarchy neither reformed nor collapsed, and the interview now reads as a family dispute with unusually high production values.
    The Chauvin and Rittenhouse verdicts as symbolic referendums on American justiceBoth trials generated wall-to-wall coverage, but the broader post-George-Floyd push for police reform, "defund" politics, and corporate DEI escalation had stalled or reversed within the following two years, making the trials feel like the high-water mark of a moment already receding.
    The Elizabeth Holmes fraud trial's startTheranos was already defunct by the time the trial dominated Silicon Valley accountability coverage, and the eventual verdict landed against a backdrop of far larger frauds -- FTX, Terra/Luna -- that soon eclipsed it.
    The Astroworld crowd-crush tragedy in NovemberIt briefly seemed likely to force a broad reckoning over concert safety and artist liability, but no lasting regulatory change followed.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    Russia's autumn military buildup around Ukraine's bordersMost Western coverage treated the buildup as coercive theater, but the February 2022 invasion it preceded reordered European energy markets, expanded NATO to Finland and Sweden, and reshaped the global economy for years.
    GitHub's June technical preview of Copilot, the first mass-market product built on a large language modelAlmost no one outside developer circles treated it as historic, but it marked the moment AI capability crossed a genuine commercial threshold, more than a year before ChatGPT made the AI boom feel sudden.
    The FDA's June approval of semaglutide (Wegovy) for chronic weight managementCovered as a niche pharmaceutical story, it launched the GLP-1 revolution that went on to reshape obesity medicine and food consumption patterns.
    The October OECD/G20 agreement among 136 countries on a 15% global minimum corporate taxIt was the largest coordinated international tax reform in a century, but it received barely a day of sustained coverage before vanishing from the news cycle even as it began reshaping how multinational corporations plan.
    Xi Jinping's "common prosperity" crackdown on Alibaba, Ant Group, DiDi, and private tutoringIt was reported largely as China business news, but it marked the irreversible consolidation of Xi's third-term control and a fundamental pivot in China's economic model with consequences that only became fully legible years later.
    The Christmas Day launch of the James Webb Space TelescopeCovered mostly as a space-nerd curiosity after decades of delays, its subsequent scientific returns went on to rewrite early-universe cosmology.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Trump Mob Storms the Capitol
    2. Biden Sworn In as 46th President Behind Fences and 25,000 Guardsmen
    3. Chauvin Guilty on All Counts in Death of George Floyd
    4. Giant Container Ship Wedges Shut the Suez Canal
    5. Ransomware Attack Shuts Colonial Pipeline, Triggers East Coast Gas Panic
    6. Kabul Falls to the Taliban as U.S. Evacuation Turns Chaotic
    7. 13 U.S. Troops Killed in Kabul Airport Bombing
    8. Powell Drops 'Transitory' as Inflation Hits Highest Rate Since 1982
    9. Facebook Rebrands Itself Meta, Bets the Company on the Metaverse
    10. Youngkin's Virginia Win Rattles Democrats Ahead of 2022

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryDemocrats Raphael Warnock and Jon Ossoff won Georgia's Senate runoffs on January 5, handing their party a 50-50 Senate that Vice President Harris's tiebreaker would control. The following day, a mob of Trump supporters stormed the Capitol in a failed attempt to block certification of Biden's Electoral College victory, leaving five dead and prompting Trump's second impeachment on January 13. Biden and Harris were inaugurated on January 20 in a Washington locked down by 25,000 National Guard troops, and late in the month retail traders on Reddit's r/wallstreetbets sent GameStop's stock into a historic short squeeze against hedge funds.
    FebruaryThe Senate acquitted Trump on February 13 of inciting the Capitol attack, with seven Republicans voting to convict -- short of the two-thirds needed. Tom Brady won his seventh Super Bowl, his first with Tampa Bay, on February 7. A catastrophic mid-February winter storm knocked out Texas's power grid for days, killing hundreds and exposing the fragility of the state's isolated electricity system, while Biden extended the New START nuclear arms treaty with Russia for five years.
    MarchBiden signed the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan on March 11, sending most Americans $1,400 stimulus checks and expanding the Child Tax Credit. The container ship Ever Given wedged itself across the Suez Canal for six days, halting roughly a tenth of global trade and becoming the year's most-memed image. Eight people, including six Asian women, were killed in the March 16 Atlanta-area spa shootings, galvanizing a wave of #StopAsianHate activism, while Bill Hwang's Archegos Capital Management collapsed in a swap-driven implosion that cost major banks billions.
    AprilDerek Chauvin was convicted on all counts for the murder of George Floyd on April 20, a rare moment of accountability watched live nationwide. The Census Bureau released 2020 apportionment data showing the slowest decade of U.S. population growth since the 1930s, Coinbase went public via direct listing at an $86 billion valuation, and Chloé Zhao's "Nomadland" won Best Picture at the pandemic-delayed Oscars, with Zhao becoming only the second woman to win Best Director.
    MayA ransomware attack forced the shutdown of the Colonial Pipeline, triggering real gasoline shortages and panic-buying across the Southeast and prompting an emergency White House cybersecurity order. An 11-day war between Israel and Hamas killed roughly 260 Palestinians and 13 Israelis before a U.S.-brokered ceasefire, Olivia Rodrigo released her debut album "SOUR," and Naomi Osaka withdrew from the French Open citing her mental health.
    JuneBiden signed legislation making Juneteenth a federal holiday. He held his first foreign trip as president, attending the G7 summit in Cornwall, a NATO summit in Brussels, and a tense but stable summit with Vladimir Putin in Geneva. GitHub released a technical preview of Copilot, the first mass-market coding tool built on a large language model, and the FDA approved semaglutide (Wegovy) for chronic weight management -- both stories that drew only modest attention at the time.
    JulyHaitian President Jovenel Moïse was assassinated on July 7. The expanded Child Tax Credit began delivering monthly payments to most American parents, Cuba experienced its largest anti-government protests in decades, and a giddy "hot vax summer" gave way to rising anxiety as the Delta variant began driving case counts back up; gymnast Simone Biles withdrew from several Tokyo Olympics events on July 27 to protect her mental health.
    AugustAfghanistan's government and military collapsed within days as the Taliban swept the country; Kabul fell on August 15 in a chaotic scene that saw Afghans clinging to departing U.S. aircraft at the airport. An ISIS-K suicide bombing at the airport's Abbey Gate killed 13 U.S. service members and roughly 170 Afghans on August 26, and the last American troops departed on August 30, ending the nearly twenty-year war. Hurricane Ida slammed into Louisiana on the anniversary of Katrina's landfall, and the Delta wave pushed COVID hospitalizations to some of the pandemic's highest levels.
    SeptemberGovernor Gavin Newsom easily defeated a recall attempt in California on September 14. Texas's SB 8 took effect on September 1, banning most abortions after roughly six weeks via a novel private-enforcement mechanism the Supreme Court declined to block. The U.S., U.K., and Australia announced the AUKUS submarine pact, infuriating France badly enough that it briefly recalled its ambassador; roughly 15,000 mostly Haitian migrants massed at the border in Del Rio, Texas; and Netflix's "Squid Game" premiered to become the platform's biggest debut ever.
    OctoberFacebook rebranded itself Meta on October 28, betting its future on the "metaverse" even as Frances Haugen's whistleblower revelations about the company's internal research dominated Washington hearings. The OECD and G20 finalized a 136-country agreement on a 15% global minimum corporate tax, Denis Villeneuve's "Dune" opened in theaters and on HBO Max, and Pope Francis met with Biden, only the second Catholic president, at the Vatican.
    NovemberGlenn Youngkin's upset win in Virginia's governor's race, alongside a near-loss for New Jersey's Democratic incumbent, was read nationwide as a warning of a difficult 2022 midterm environment for Democrats. Biden signed the $1.2 trillion Bipartisan Infrastructure Law on November 15, Kyle Rittenhouse was acquitted of all charges on November 19, ten people died in a crowd crush at Travis Scott's Astroworld festival, six were killed when a car plowed through the Waukesha Christmas parade, and the newly identified Omicron variant began raising alarm just as Adele's comeback album "30" arrived to the year's biggest debut week.
    DecemberSenator Joe Manchin announced on December 19 that he could not support the Build Back Better social-spending bill, killing Biden's signature domestic policy ambition for the year. Omicron drove case counts to record highs, the Federal Reserve accelerated its taper and signaled multiple 2022 rate hikes as Chair Powell formally retired the word "transitory," the James Webb Space Telescope launched successfully on Christmas Day after decades of delay, and Biden warned Vladimir Putin of "severe consequences" as Russia's troop buildup on Ukraine's border continued to grow.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+5.7% real GDP growth Q4/Q4 (initial BEA estimate; later revised toward +5.8% to +5.9%) -- the fastest since 1984
    InflationCPI +7.0% year-over-year in December 2021, the highest since June 1982; core CPI +5.5%; full-year average CPI ~4.7%
    Unemployment3.9% in December 2021, down from 6.7% in December 2020
    S&P 500 return+28.7% total return (dividends reinvested), including 70 record closing highs
    Dow return+20.9% total return
    Fed funds rate0%-0.25% target range held all year; tapering of asset purchases announced in November and accelerated in December
    Oil priceWTI crude ~$75.21/barrel at year-end, +55% for the year (Brent ~$77.78)
    Gold price~$1,829/oz at year-end, -3.6% for the year
    Population~331.9 million per the 2020 Census (results released April 2021); slowest decade of population growth (7.4%) since the 1930s
    Median home price~$346,900 full-year median existing-home sale price (NAR); ~$358,000 in December, +15.8% year-over-year
    National debt~$29.6 trillion gross federal debt at year-end (~123% of GDP); ~$23.0 trillion held by the public

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2021 was to live through a year that promised relief and delivered whiplash instead. The year began in the worst way imaginable for a functioning democracy -- a mob storming the Capitol to overturn a certified election -- and for a few raw days in January it was genuinely unclear whether the constitutional order would hold. It did, and Biden's inauguration two weeks later, however heavily guarded, felt to many like an exhale. Then came spring's real one: vaccines arriving faster than almost anyone had dared hope, a $1,400 check in the mail, restaurants and stadiums and offices tentatively reopening. For a few months in the late spring and early summer of 2021, ordinary American life resembled something like its old self, and headlines spoke, only half-ironically, of a "hot vax summer."

    That reprieve did not last. The Delta variant arrived by midsummer and, with it, the return of masks, arguments over vaccine mandates that split workplaces and families, and a creeping sense that the pandemic's end was not a door but a treadmill. By autumn, a new anxiety had layered itself onto COVID fatigue: prices were rising everywhere -- at the gas pump, the used-car lot, the grocery store -- and the officials responsible for managing the economy kept insisting it was temporary in a way that felt, to anyone paying their own bills, increasingly disconnected from lived experience. Meanwhile the images from Kabul in August, of a two-decade war ending not in vindication but in chaos and thirteen American deaths, complicated whatever relief the summer's reopening had offered; it was the kind of story that made people angry without anyone being entirely sure at whom.

    And yet 2021 was not simply grim. It was, by most conventional measures, an excellent year to have a job or a stock portfolio: wages rose, workers quit in record numbers for better offers, and the S&P 500 notched a near-30% return with 70 record highs. Culture kept producing genuine surprises and delights -- Squid Game became a global sensation seemingly overnight, Olivia Rodrigo announced herself as pop's newest star, and audiences proved, at the very end of the year with Spider-Man, that they still wanted to go to the movies together. What made 2021 feel so exhausting was less any single event than the accumulation -- insurrection, pandemic waves, a lost war, workplace upheaval, and rising prices, arriving one after another without the clean resolution any of them seemed to promise. Psychologists borrowed the word "languishing" to describe the year's dominant mood: not depression, not crisis, but a flat, drained sense that things should have felt better than they did. Viewed from the vantage of 2026, the year now looks like a hinge -- the last chapter of the pandemic-and-Trump era closing just as inflation, war, and artificial intelligence were quietly beginning the next one -- but lived day to day, it mostly just felt like a very long year.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2022
    Econ -1Mark -3Poli -1Cult +0Over -1
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2022 was the year the post-pandemic world revealed its bill. Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine on February 24 ended a three-decade assumption that great-power land war in Europe was a closed chapter, and it detonated a global energy and food shock that, layered onto an already-overheating post-COVID economy, pushed US inflation to 9.1% in June, the worst reading since November 1981. The Federal Reserve answered with the fastest tightening cycle since Paul Volcker, seven hikes totaling 425 basis points, which crushed a decade of cheap-money assumptions, more than doubled mortgage rates, and produced the worst combined stock-and-bond year since 1937. Real GDP technically contracted for two straight quarters even as unemployment fell to a fifty-year low of 3.5%, an almost unprecedented macroeconomic split-screen that left ordinary Americans reporting record-low consumer sentiment even while spending freely and holding jobs. Domestically, the Supreme Court's June 24 Dobbs decision eliminated the federal right to abortion first recognized in Roe v. Wade, instantly nationalizing abortion politics and reordering the electoral map for the rest of the decade.

    Washington itself proved more functional than the mood suggested. A Congress controlled by the barest Democratic majorities passed, in a single frantic summer, the largest climate investment in American history (the Inflation Reduction Act), a bipartisan semiconductor and science bill (the CHIPS and Science Act), and the first meaningful federal gun-safety law in nearly thirty years, passed after the Uvalde school massacre killed nineteen children and two teachers. The November midterms, forecast almost universally as a Republican "red wave," instead delivered Democrats a Senate gain and Republicans only a threadbare House majority, a result widely read as a Dobbs-driven and candidate-quality-driven rebuke of the party's most Trump-aligned nominees. The year's institutional stress tests, an FBI search of a former president's residence for classified documents, and ten televised January 6th Committee hearings ending in a criminal referral of Donald Trump, played out without producing a constitutional crisis, testament to a system straining but holding.

    Culturally, 2022 was simultaneously abundant and exhausting. Beyoncé's Renaissance, Taylor Swift's Midnights, Top Gun: Maverick's box-office resurrection, and Argentina's World Cup coronation of Lionel Messi coexisted with mass-shooting trauma at Uvalde and Buffalo, a crypto industry that imploded in the FTX bankruptcy, and a pervasive "vibecession" in which strong jobs data failed to dislodge a national mood of grinding fatigue. Almost unnoticed beneath all of it, OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public on November 30, a two-paragraph tech-section story at the time that would come to be recognized as the year's most consequential event. Judged by the standards of its own turbulent moment, 2022 was a year of real economic pain, real market carnage, real institutional strain, and real cultural vitality, one that most Americans experienced as exhausting rather than collapsing, and whose quietest thirty minutes of news, a chatbot demo in late November, mattered more than nearly everything that came before it.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy-1A 50-year-low 3.5% unemployment rate and no formal recession were offset by 9.1% peak inflation, falling real wages, a two-quarter GDP contraction, and a housing market frozen by doubling mortgage rates.
    Markets-3A -18.1% S&P 500, a -32.5% Nasdaq, a historic -13% bond-market rout, the worst 60/40 portfolio year since 1937, a frozen IPO market, and the FTX/crypto collapse combined to make 2022 the worst market year since 2008.
    Politics-1Dobbs, an FBI search of a former president's home, and a January 6th criminal referral produced acute institutional strain, but a bipartisan gun law passed and a fair, closely contested midterm was conducted and its results accepted, evidence of underlying resilience.
    Culture+0Enormous creative output from Beyoncé to Top Gun to the World Cup was counterbalanced almost exactly by mass-shooting trauma, Dobbs-driven anguish, and a pervasive inflation-fatigue "vibecession," yielding a vivid but fraying cultural year.
    Overall Mood-1A resilient labor market and vivid cultural output were outweighed in most Americans' lived experience by inflation, war anxiety, mass violence, and a market collapse, producing a mood of grinding exhaustion rather than crisis or confidence.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Russia launches a full-scale invasion of Ukraine on February 24, the largest war in Europe since 1945
    2. The Supreme Court overturns Roe v. Wade in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, ending the federal right to abortion
    3. US inflation peaks at 9.1% in June, the highest reading in more than 40 years, as gas tops $5 a gallon nationally
    4. The Federal Reserve raises interest rates seven times, the fastest tightening campaign since the early 1980s
    5. A gunman kills 19 children and two teachers at Robb Elementary School in Uvalde, Texas
    6. Elon Musk closes his $44 billion purchase of Twitter and immediately begins mass layoffs
    7. Democrats defy predictions of a Republican "red wave," gaining a Senate seat while Republicans win only a narrow House majority
    8. President Biden signs the Inflation Reduction Act and the CHIPS and Science Act into law
    9. The FBI searches former President Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate and recovers classified documents
    10. The FTX cryptocurrency exchange collapses into bankruptcy and founder Sam Bankman-Fried is arrested

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The US economy in 2022 collided post-pandemic overheating with the fastest monetary tightening in four decades, a war-driven commodity shock, and a synchronized bear market in stocks and bonds. Real GDP grew roughly 2.1% for the year on an annual-average basis, but the path was jarring: real GDP contracted at annualized rates of -1.6% in the first quarter and -0.6% in the second, meeting the informal two-quarter definition of recession even though the NBER pointedly declined to certify one because payrolls, industrial production, and real income all kept rising, before rebounding to +3.2% in the third quarter and +2.6% in the fourth. Nominal GDP grew about 9.2%, a reminder that much of the year's headline growth was price rather than volume. Inflation was the defining story: CPI opened the year already elevated at 7.5% year-over-year, accelerated sharply after Russia's February 24 invasion of Ukraine disrupted energy and food markets, and peaked at 9.1% in June, the hottest reading since November 1981, before cooling to 6.5% by December as gasoline retreated. Real wages fell for a second straight year as pay gains of roughly 4.6% trailed price increases of 6.5%. Despite the inflation and the mid-year contraction, the labor market ran red-hot, adding roughly 4.5 million jobs and pushing unemployment down to 3.5% by year's end, a fifty-year low, even as job openings, quits, and the so-called Great Resignation signaled a historically tight market that coexisted uneasily with the fastest rate-hike campaign since Paul Volcker.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew about 2.1% on an annual-average basis for 2022, with quarterly patterns of -1.6%, -0.6%, +3.2%, and +2.6%. CPI inflation peaked at 9.1% year-over-year in June and closed the year at 6.5%, with core CPI (ex food and energy) still at 5.7% in December. Unemployment fell from 4.0% in January to a cycle low of 3.5% by mid-year and closed December at 3.5%, matching a fifty-year low; nonfarm payrolls added roughly 4.5 million jobs. The Federal Reserve raised its target rate seven times for a cumulative 425 basis points, from a 0-0.25% pandemic floor to 4.25-4.50% by year-end, while beginning quantitative tightening in June. The 30-year fixed mortgage rate rose from about 3.2% in January to a peak of 7.08% in early November, the sharpest one-year rise on record, closing near 6.4%. NAR's median existing-home price peaked at $413,800 in June before easing to about $366,900 in December. WTI crude spiked above $130 intraday in March before closing the year near $80; gasoline hit an all-time nominal high of about $5.02 a gallon in June. The federal deficit fell to about $1.38 trillion in FY2022 as pandemic programs wound down, while total federal debt closed the year near $31.4 trillion.

    Major Events

    Jerome Powell, renominated by President Biden and confirmed by the Senate in May for a second term as Fed Chair, pivoted decisively from 2021's "transitory" framing to explicit hawkishness, delivering hikes of 25, 50, 75, 75, 75, 75, and 50 basis points across the year's eight meetings, including the first back-to-back 75-basis-point moves since 1994. Housing whipsawed as mortgage rates doubled, with existing-home sales collapsing from an annualized 6.5 million pace in January to about 4.02 million in December, the weakest since 2010. Corporate and deal activity was consequential: Elon Musk's $44 billion Twitter acquisition closed October 27 after months of legal drama and immediate mass layoffs followed; Microsoft announced its $68.7 billion Activision Blizzard deal in January; and Big Tech began aggressive layoffs in the second half, with Meta cutting roughly 11,000 jobs in November and Amazon announcing cuts totaling about 18,000 through year-end. Cryptocurrency imploded across the year, from the May Terra/Luna stablecoin collapse (roughly $60 billion erased in days) and the subsequent failures of Three Arrows Capital, Voyager, and Celsius, to the November 11 bankruptcy of FTX and the December 12 arrest of Sam Bankman-Fried in the Bahamas. Fiscally, Congress enacted the CHIPS and Science Act (August 9, roughly $52 billion for domestic semiconductor manufacturing) and the Inflation Reduction Act (August 16, roughly $370 billion in climate, energy, and health-care provisions), the largest US industrial-policy and climate legislation in decades, while the Supreme Court's Dobbs decision and the November midterms, which produced a divided Congress, reshaped the political backdrop for economic policy heading into 2023.

    Why this score

    The US avoided an officially certified recession and closed the year with unemployment at a fifty-year low, but a two-quarter GDP contraction, 9.1% peak inflation, falling real wages, and a housing market frozen by doubling mortgage rates made 2022 a materially below-average year by the era's own baseline, clearly negative but nowhere near the severity of 2008 or 2020.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    Financial markets had their worst year since 2008, and a uniquely painful one because bonds sold off in lockstep with stocks rather than cushioning the decline. Full-year total returns were brutal across the board: the S&P 500 returned -18.1% (price -19.4%), the Dow Jones Industrial Average -6.9% (price -8.8%), and the Nasdaq Composite -32.5% (price -33.1%). The S&P 500 officially entered a bear market on June 13, more than 20% below its January 3 high, rallied about 17% into mid-August on hopes of a Fed "pivot," made fresh lows on October 12, and then staged a modest fourth-quarter recovery. Growth stocks were annihilated, with Meta down 64%, Tesla down 65%, Amazon down 50%, and Netflix down 51% for the year, while energy was the sole positive S&P sector, up 59%. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index returned about -13%, its worst year on record, driving a traditional 60/40 stock/bond portfolio to roughly -17%, its worst showing since 1937. The 2-year Treasury yield rose from about 0.73% to 4.43% and the 10-year from about 1.51% to 3.88%, with the 2s/10s curve inverting in July and remaining deeply inverted, a classic recession signal that did not, in the end, presage a 2023 downturn.

    Character

    Bear

    Major Companies

    Elon Musk announced a $44 billion bid for Twitter in April, tried to walk away in July, and closed the deal on October 27 after Delaware Chancery Court proceedings, immediately taking the company private and launching mass layoffs. Microsoft announced its $68.7 billion acquisition of Activision Blizzard in January, which would not close until 2023 after protracted antitrust review; Broadcom announced a $61 billion deal for VMware in May; and Kroger announced a $24.6 billion agreement to acquire Albertsons in October. Big Tech pivoted sharply from growth to retrenchment in the second half, with Meta cutting roughly 11,000 jobs in November, Amazon announcing cuts totaling about 18,000 through year-end, and Twitter halving its own workforce under Musk. Cryptocurrency's marquee names collapsed in sequence: the Terra/Luna algorithmic stablecoin in May, Three Arrows Capital, Voyager, and Celsius through the summer, and finally Sam Bankman-Fried's FTX and Alameda Research on November 11, with Bankman-Fried arrested in the Bahamas on December 12. Bitcoin fell from about $47,700 to about $16,500, a 64% decline.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was that there was nowhere to hide, as the Fed's most aggressive tightening cycle since Volcker punished growth stocks, long-duration bonds, and speculative assets simultaneously. The IPO market effectively shut, with US IPO proceeds falling roughly 95% from 2021's record to about $8 billion, one of the weakest years in two decades, and the SPAC boom that had defined 2021 ended entirely. Energy was the year's standout theme, buoyed by the war-driven commodity shock, while richly valued unprofitable tech and crypto-adjacent equities were repriced hardest. The most acute non-US spillover event was the UK "mini-budget" crisis under Prime Minister Liz Truss in late September, which briefly broke the gilt market and forced Bank of England intervention, rattling global rates for about a week. There was no US bank failure in 2022, but the year's historic bond rout planted the seeds of 2023's regional-bank stress, as institutions accumulated large unrealized losses on held-to-maturity Treasury and mortgage-backed portfolios. Gold was strikingly unremarkable given the inflation backdrop, essentially flat for the year as real yields surged and the dollar rallied roughly 8%.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    The dominant domestic event was the Supreme Court's June 24 ruling in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization, which overturned Roe v. Wade and Planned Parenthood v. Casey and eliminated the federal constitutional right to abortion, a decision effectively previewed by an unprecedented leaked draft opinion published by Politico on May 2. The same Court term produced NYSRPA v. Bruen (June 23), expanding Second Amendment protections, and West Virginia v. EPA (June 30), curbing the EPA's regulatory authority; in April, Ketanji Brown Jackson was confirmed 53-47 as the first Black woman to sit on the Supreme Court, replacing the retiring Justice Stephen Breyer. The FBI's August 8 search of former President Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate, which recovered classified documents he had failed to return, became a defining and polarizing story of the year's second half and led to a special-counsel investigation. The House Select Committee on January 6th held ten televised hearings from June through the fall, most memorably featuring Cassidy Hutchinson's June 28 testimony, and closed the year with a December 19 criminal referral of Trump to the Justice Department. Congress passed the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act (signed June 25) after the May 24 Uvalde school shooting, the first major federal gun-safety law in nearly thirty years, and closed the year with a lame-duck sprint that produced the Respect for Marriage Act (signed December 13) and the Electoral Count Reform Act (enacted December 29).

    Domestic Policy

    Democrats used their final year of unified, if razor-thin, control of Congress to pass a substantial legislative record. The Inflation Reduction Act (signed August 16), a roughly $370 billion climate, energy, and health-care package and the largest US climate investment in history, passed the Senate 51-50 via reconciliation after a surprise July 27 deal between Majority Leader Chuck Schumer and holdout Senator Joe Manchin revived what had been a stalled and much larger "Build Back Better" agenda; it also let Medicare negotiate some drug prices and imposed a 15% corporate minimum tax. The CHIPS and Science Act (signed August 9) committed roughly $52 billion to domestic semiconductor manufacturing plus a broad science-funding reauthorization, passing with meaningful bipartisan Senate support. President Biden announced federal student-loan forgiveness of up to $10,000 (or $20,000 for Pell Grant recipients) on August 24, immediately challenged in court, and signed the PACT Act (August 10) expanding veterans' health benefits for burn-pit exposure. Manchin and Senator Kyrsten Sinema remained decisive centrist votes throughout the year, repeatedly narrowing the scope of Biden's original economic agenda.

    Foreign Policy

    Russia's February 24 invasion of Ukraine reoriented Biden's foreign policy for the year, prompting a coordinated Western sanctions campaign, a US ban on Russian oil imports (March 8), and a June NATO summit in Madrid that formally invited Finland and Sweden to join the alliance, ending decades of Finnish neutrality. Congress approved roughly $113 billion in cumulative Ukraine aid across several packages, and US-supplied weapons including HIMARS rocket systems helped enable Ukrainian counteroffensives in the fall; Volodymyr Zelensky's December 21 address to Congress was a high point of bipartisan wartime unity. Biden traveled to Saudi Arabia in July, drawing criticism for a fist-bump greeting with Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman undertaken chiefly to seek oil-production relief amid the energy shock. A US drone strike in Kabul killed al-Qaeda leader Ayman al-Zawahiri on July 31, the most significant counterterrorism strike since the killing of Osama bin Laden. House Speaker Nancy Pelosi's August 2-3 visit to Taiwan drew an aggressive Chinese military response, including the PLA's largest live-fire exercises around the island in decades, and the Commerce Department's October 7 export controls restricting advanced chip sales to China marked a significant escalation of US-China technological competition.

    Important Elections

    The November 8 midterms defied near-universal predictions of a Republican "red wave." Republicans won only a narrow House majority, finishing with 222 seats to Democrats' 213, one of the smallest midterm gains for an out-party in decades, while Democrats gained a Senate seat, with John Fetterman flipping Pennsylvania and Raphael Warnock defeating Herschel Walker in a December 6 Georgia runoff to secure an outright 51-49 majority. Dobbs-driven turnout among women and independents and the weakness of several Trump-endorsed candidates in swing states were widely cited explanations; Florida Governor Ron DeSantis was reelected in a nearly 20-point landslide and emerged as the midterms' biggest individual winner. Nancy Pelosi announced on November 17 that she would step down from House Democratic leadership after two decades, clearing the way for Hakeem Jeffries to become the first Black American to lead a party in Congress, while Kevin McCarthy emerged as the presumptive Republican Speaker-designate without yet having secured the votes to be elected. Trump announced his 2024 presidential campaign on November 15, a launch met with unusually cool coverage given his endorsed candidates' disappointing midterm showing.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    Tom Cruise's Top Gun: Maverick (May 27) grossed nearly $1.5 billion globally and became the defining story of theatrical exhibition's post-pandemic rebound, followed by James Cameron's Avatar: The Way of Water (December 16), which ultimately grossed over $2 billion. Marvel had a mixed year, with Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness and the Chadwick Boseman tribute film Black Panther: Wakanda Forever performing well even as franchise fatigue began to show, while Jordan Peele's Nope and Baz Luhrmann's Elvis were the year's arthouse-crossover successes. Critical darlings included A24's Everything Everywhere All at Once, which would sweep the 2023 Oscars, alongside Steven Spielberg's The Fabelmans, Todd Field's TAR, Martin McDonagh's The Banshees of Inisherin, and Sarah Polley's Women Talking, while Rian Johnson's Glass Onion debuted at festivals before hitting Netflix in December. Apple TV+'s CODA won the Best Picture Oscar at the March ceremony (honoring 2021 films), the first streaming Best Picture winner in Academy history.

    Music

    Beyonce's Renaissance (July 29), her first solo album in six years and an ecstatic celebration of Black queer club culture, was the year's most acclaimed release, with "Break My Soul" providing a Great Resignation-era anthem. Bad Bunny's Un Verano Sin Ti (May) was the biggest album on the planet and the most-streamed album globally on Spotify, while Harry Styles's Harry's House delivered "As It Was," the longest-running No. 1 of the year. Taylor Swift's Midnights (October 21) became a cultural earthquake, breaking Spotify's single-day streaming record and occupying all top ten slots of the Billboard Hot 100 for the first time in history. Kendrick Lamar's Mr. Morale & the Big Steppers, SZA's SOS (closing the year with "Kill Bill"), and Lizzo's TikTok-powered "About Damn Time" were other major moments, alongside Sam Smith and Kim Petras's "Unholy," which made Petras the first openly transgender solo artist with a No. 1 hit, and the mainstream breakthrough of Nigerian Afrobeats via Rema's "Calm Down." TikTok remained the dominant discovery engine, resurrecting older hits like Kate Bush's "Running Up That Hill."

    Television

    Stranger Things Season 4 (May/July) was the streaming event of the year, Netflix's biggest English-language debut ever, and its use of Kate Bush's "Running Up That Hill" became one of the great pop-culture crossovers. HBO's House of the Dragon (August 21) successfully revived the Game of Thrones universe, while Amazon's The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power, the most expensive TV series ever made, drew polarized reactions. HBO's The White Lotus Season 2 was the year's most-discussed prestige show, Hulu's The Bear became an instant obsession after its June debut, and Apple's Severance was the year's high-concept critical darling. Netflix's Dahmer-Monster drew huge audiences alongside criticism for retraumatizing victims' families, and the January 6th Committee hearings, aired in prime time through the summer, were themselves major television events.

    Technology

    The single most consequential release of the year was ChatGPT, which OpenAI launched November 30 and which made generative AI a mass consumer experience almost overnight, reaching one million users within five days. Earlier in the year, DALL-E 2, Midjourney's public beta, and the open-source release of Stable Diffusion had already made 2022 the "AI image year," moving image generation from novelty to mass phenomenon. Apple launched the iPhone 14 line in September with Emergency SOS via satellite and the Dynamic Island, while Meta's Horizon Worlds metaverse push floundered publicly as Zuckerberg's avatar screenshot became a meme and Meta's stock cratered. Cryptocurrency and NFTs peaked early in the year and collapsed spectacularly across Terra/Luna, Celsius, and FTX, ending the "crypto is inevitable" narrative, while TikTok cemented its dominance among young users and BeReal had a brief moment as a curated-social-media antidote.

    Social Trends

    Post-Dobbs abortion-rights protests dominated the summer, while anti-drag and anti-trans legislation proliferated in state legislatures and the Club Q shooting in Colorado Springs killed five at an LGBTQ nightclub on November 19. A genuine labor resurgence accompanied Great Resignation and quiet-quitting discourse, with historic union wins at Starbucks and Amazon's Staten Island JFK8 warehouse and a near-national rail strike averted by federal intervention in December. Florida's "Don't Say Gay" law (signed March 28) launched the year's most heated K-12 culture-war fight and a public feud between Disney and Governor Ron DeSantis, while the NAEP "Nation's Report Card" revealed the largest reading-score decline in thirty years, framed as a generational pandemic-learning-loss catastrophe. The Southern Baptist Convention's May sexual-abuse report was a watershed moment for evangelical Christianity, and Pew's continuing data on religious "nones" showed roughly 30% of Americans now identifying with no religion.

    Crime

    A gunman killed 19 children and two teachers at Robb Elementary School in Uvalde, Texas on May 24, with police-response failures becoming a national scandal, while a white supremacist killed 10 Black shoppers in a racially motivated massacre at a Buffalo Tops supermarket on May 14. Other notable violence included the Highland Park July 4th parade shooting, the November 13 University of Idaho murders (whose arrest of suspect Bryan Kohberger became a media obsession in December), and the April 12 Brooklyn subway shooting. Ahmaud Arbery's killers received federal hate-crime sentences in August, Ghislaine Maxwell was sentenced to 20 years in June, Steve Bannon was convicted of contempt of Congress in July, and Sam Bankman-Fried was arrested in the Bahamas in December following FTX's collapse.

    Sports

    The Los Angeles Rams beat the Cincinnati Bengals 23-20 in Super Bowl LVI, with a Dr. Dre-Snoop Dogg-Eminem-Mary J. Blige-Kendrick Lamar halftime show serving as a landmark celebration of West Coast hip-hop. The Golden State Warriors beat the Boston Celtics for the NBA title, the Colorado Avalanche won the Stanley Cup, and the Houston Astros beat the Philadelphia Phillies in the World Series. At the Beijing Winter Olympics, Nathan Chen and Chloe Kim won golds and Erin Jackson became the first Black woman to win an individual Winter Olympic gold, while Argentina beat France in one of the greatest World Cup finals ever, completing Lionel Messi's global coronation as the US reached the Round of 16. Serena Williams retired at the US Open in an emotional cultural moment, Aaron Judge hit 62 home runs to break Roger Maris's American League record, and Brittney Griner, detained in Russia since February, was released in a December prisoner swap for Viktor Bout.

    Public Mood

    The year felt exhausting, angry, and uncertain: the pandemic was fading but had been replaced by 8-9% inflation, $5-a-gallon gas, an aggressive Fed hiking cycle that killed the tech boom, and pervasive recession fear that never quite materialized, a divergence contemporary journalists dubbed the "vibecession." Existential dread came from multiple directions at once, Russia's invasion reviving nuclear fears, Dobbs making half the country feel the nation was regressing, Uvalde and Buffalo making mass violence feel ambient, the January 6th hearings replaying democratic fragility all summer, and crypto's collapse punishing the last cohort of retail bulls. Yet there were genuine bright spots, Beyonce, Top Gun, Everything Everywhere All at Once, The Bear, and Messi's World Cup among them, and the November midterms' failure to produce the predicted "red wave" was a rare piece of political news that surprised elites in the direction of relief, leaving the dominant mood one of a country grinding through compounding shocks while waiting to feel normal again.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Americans in 2022 believed the year would be remembered for the return of great-power war in Europe, the end of Roe v. Wade, the return of high inflation, and the political fate of Donald Trump. These stories filled front pages and cable hours for the entire year, alongside the Depp v. Heard trial's tabloid saturation and the horse-race coverage of a predicted Republican "red wave" that never arrived.

    What actually mattered

    Those front-page stories do still matter: the Ukraine war reshaped European security and defense budgets, Dobbs restructured American electoral politics for at least the following two cycles, and the Fed's tightening ended the fifteen-year zero-interest-rate era that had defined post-2008 finance. But the year's most durable consequences flowed from stories treated as secondary at the time, clustered in a five-month window from August to November: the passage of the Inflation Reduction Act and CHIPS and Science Act, the October chip export controls on China, and the November 30 release of ChatGPT. Together, those events mark 2022 as the year the United States simultaneously revived its industrial policy, formally escalated a technology cold war with China, and inadvertently launched the largest technology-platform shift since mobile computing.

    Most forgotten story

    The 2022 monkeypox (mpox) outbreak dominated public-health coverage from May through October, was declared a WHO Public Health Emergency in July, and provoked serious debate about the Biden administration's pandemic-response competence, including a national vaccine-rationing controversy. By 2024 it had almost entirely disappeared from public consciousness, contained and now cited mainly as a narrow public-health case study.

    Most underrated story

    ChatGPT's November 30 release is the most radically re-weighted story of the year. Contemporary coverage framed it as an interesting chatbot demo from a company most Americans had never heard of; it has since become the defining consumer-technology launch of the decade, the trigger for a trillion-dollar AI capital-expenditure cycle, and the origin point of the ongoing debate over artificial intelligence's economic, educational, and existential implications.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    The biggest hindsight surprise is how quickly the crypto industry's 2022 collapse, the Terra/Luna implosion, the Celsius and Voyager bankruptcies, and Sam Bankman-Fried's FTX fraud, became a comparative footnote. Covered as an existential, decade-defining scandal at the time, the industry instead rebounded, mainstreamed via spot Bitcoin ETFs by 2024, and receded as a political controversy, leaving FTX reading more like a garden-variety financial fraud than a foundational moment.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Depp v. Heard defamation trial (April-June)It drew tabloid and TikTok saturation unmatched since the O.J. Simpson trial, but produced no jurisprudential shift and its cultural residue faded within a year.
    Will Smith slapping Chris Rock at the Oscars (March 27)Weeks of discourse about masculinity and the Academy's response gave way to Smith headlining films again within two years; it is a trivia answer now, not a cultural pivot.
    The 2022 midterms as an anticipated "red wave"The narrative absorbed enormous political-media bandwidth from spring through November, but the more durable story was Dobbs-driven Democratic overperformance, a theme that received far more retrospective attention than the horse-race coverage itself.
    The January 6th Select Committee hearings (June-December)Ten prestige-paced televised sessions produced a criminal referral and a bestselling report, but their political impact proved small once Trump won the 2024 nomination and general election.
    Queen Elizabeth II's death (September 8)Genuinely historic as the end of an era, with a state funeral watched by an estimated four billion people, but its policy and geopolitical consequences have been modest under Charles III's ceremonially competent reign.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    ChatGPT's public release (November 30)Announced as a modest research preview, it reached 100 million users faster than any consumer product in history and by 2024 had triggered a full reorganization of the software industry and a still-unresolved crisis in higher education.
    The October 7 chip export controls on ChinaReported as a technical trade-policy story, the rules restricting advanced chip sales are now treated as the opening move of a durable technology cold war between the US and China.
