    The Inflation Reduction Act (signed August 16)Covered mainly through the lens of Joe Manchin's negotiations, its roughly $370 billion in climate provisions became the largest climate investment in US history and triggered a Sun Belt manufacturing boom in solar, batteries, and EV supply chains.
    Sweden and Finland's NATO applications (May 18)Treated as a predictable, procedural second-order effect of the invasion, it became one of the largest expansions of the Western alliance since the 1990s, reversing seventy-five years of Finnish neutrality.
    Stable Diffusion's open-source release and Midjourney's public opening (summer 2022)These niche-covered generative image models arrived months before ChatGPT and set off the copyright, labor, and authenticity debates that would help produce the 2023 SAG-AFTRA and WGA strikes.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Russia Launches Full-Scale Invasion of Ukraine; Largest War in Europe Since 1945
    2. Supreme Court Overturns Roe v. Wade in Dobbs Ruling, Ending Federal Abortion Right
    3. Inflation Hits 9.1%, Highest in Four Decades, as Fed Launches Aggressive Rate Hikes
    4. Queen Elizabeth II Dies at 96, Ending a Seven-Decade Reign
    5. FBI Searches Mar-a-Lago, Recovers Classified Documents from Former President
    6. 19 Children, Two Teachers Killed in Uvalde Elementary School Shooting
    7. Democrats Defy 'Red Wave' Predictions in Midterms; Senate Held, House Narrowly Lost
    8. Biden Signs $370 Billion Climate Law and $52 Billion CHIPS Act in Bipartisan Industrial Turn
    9. FTX Collapses; Sam Bankman-Fried Arrested as Crypto Empire Unravels
    10. OpenAI Quietly Releases ChatGPT to the Public

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryRussia continued massing more than 100,000 troops on Ukraine's border as US and NATO diplomats held tense, ultimately fruitless talks in Geneva and Brussels warning of an imminent invasion. Microsoft announced a $68.7 billion agreement to acquire Activision Blizzard, and Jerome Powell's Senate confirmation hearing for a second term as Fed Chair signaled the coming shift to aggressive tightening as January CPI came in at 7.5% year-over-year. Unemployment stood at 4.0% as the labor market continued its rapid post-pandemic recovery.
    FebruaryThe Beijing Winter Olympics ran February 4-20, with Nathan Chen and Chloe Kim winning golds for the US amid the Kamila Valieva doping scandal. On February 24, Russia launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine, the largest war in Europe since 1945, sending oil prices surging and triggering an immediate, sweeping Western sanctions campaign; Brittney Griner was detained at a Moscow airport the same month. CPI for the month came in at 7.9% year-over-year, and WTI crude climbed toward $100 a barrel.
    MarchThe Federal Reserve delivered its first rate hike since 2018, a 25-basis-point move on March 16, opening what would become the most aggressive tightening cycle since the early 1980s. The US banned Russian oil imports on March 8 and Biden traveled to Brussels for an emergency NATO summit; WTI crude spiked to an intraday high above $130. Ketanji Brown Jackson's Supreme Court confirmation hearings ran March 21-24, Florida's "Don't Say Gay" law was signed March 28, and Will Smith slapped Chris Rock at the Oscars on March 27 before winning Best Actor for King Richard.
    AprilKetanji Brown Jackson was confirmed 53-47 on April 7 as the first Black woman on the Supreme Court. Elon Musk disclosed a large Twitter stake and then launched his $44 billion takeover bid; Amazon's Staten Island JFK8 warehouse voted to unionize on April 1. The Depp v. Heard defamation trial began April 11 and became a televised, TikTok-saturated spectacle, and a gunman opened fire on a Brooklyn subway train on April 12, wounding ten. DALL-E 2's expanding access helped launch 2022's AI-image moment.
    MayA leaked draft opinion published by Politico on May 2 revealed the Supreme Court intended to overturn Roe v. Wade. A white supremacist killed 10 Black shoppers in a racially motivated massacre at a Buffalo Tops supermarket on May 14, and a gunman killed 19 children and two teachers at Robb Elementary School in Uvalde, Texas on May 24, prompting Senate negotiations on gun legislation. Finland and Sweden applied to join NATO on May 18, the Fed hiked rates 50 basis points on May 4, Jerome Powell was confirmed by the Senate for a second term, and Bad Bunny's Un Verano Sin Ti and Harry Styles's Harry's House were both released.
    JuneThe Supreme Court's June 24 ruling in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization overturned Roe v. Wade, triggering trigger-law abortion bans across more than a dozen states and mass protests nationwide; the same term produced the gun-rights ruling NYSRPA v. Bruen (June 23) and the EPA-limiting West Virginia v. EPA (June 30). Congress passed the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, signed June 25 after Uvalde, the first major federal gun law in nearly thirty years. The Federal Reserve delivered a 75-basis-point hike on June 15, the first since 1994, as CPI for the month hit a 9.1% year-over-year peak; the January 6th Committee began its televised hearings on June 9, and Ketanji Brown Jackson was sworn in June 30. NATO's Madrid summit formally invited Finland and Sweden to join the alliance.
    JulyThe Fed hiked rates another 75 basis points on July 27, the same day Senator Joe Manchin and Majority Leader Chuck Schumer announced a surprise deal reviving what would become the Inflation Reduction Act. A US drone strike killed al-Qaeda leader Ayman al-Zawahiri in Kabul on July 31, and Biden traveled to Saudi Arabia earlier in the month, drawing criticism for a fist-bump greeting with Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. Beyonce released Renaissance on July 29, gas prices began retreating from their June peak, and Serena Williams's retirement announcement loomed as the US Open approached.
    AugustPresident Biden signed the CHIPS and Science Act on August 9 and the Inflation Reduction Act on August 16, the year's two landmark pieces of industrial and climate legislation. The FBI searched former President Trump's Mar-a-Lago estate on August 8, recovering classified documents, while House Speaker Nancy Pelosi's August 2-3 visit to Taiwan drew an aggressive Chinese military response. Biden announced federal student-loan forgiveness on August 24, signed the PACT Act expanding veterans' benefits on August 10, and Powell's hawkish Jackson Hole speech on August 26 reinforced the Fed's tightening resolve; HBO's House of the Dragon premiered August 21.
    SeptemberThe Federal Reserve hiked rates 75 basis points for a third consecutive time on September 21 as mortgage rates and bond yields kept climbing. Queen Elizabeth II died September 8 at 96, a global cultural moment consumed heavily by American audiences; Serena Williams retired at the US Open in an emotional farewell. Hurricane Ian struck Florida on September 28 as a catastrophic Category 4 storm, and the UK's late-September "mini-budget" crisis under Prime Minister Liz Truss briefly broke the gilt market and rattled global rates.
    OctoberOPEC+ announced a significant production cut on October 5, angering the White House amid the ongoing energy shock, and the Commerce Department issued sweeping export controls on October 7 restricting advanced chip sales to China. Elon Musk closed his $44 billion Twitter acquisition on October 27 after months of legal wrangling, immediately firing executives and beginning mass layoffs; the S&P 500 made a fresh bear-market low on October 12 before a partial rebound. Paul Pelosi, husband of the House Speaker, was violently attacked in a home invasion on October 28, and Aaron Judge hit his 62nd home run to break Roger Maris's American League record.
    NovemberThe November 8 midterms defied predictions of a Republican "red wave": Republicans won only a narrow House majority while Democrats gained a Senate seat behind John Fetterman's Pennsylvania win. FTX collapsed into bankruptcy on November 11 amid a bank run that exposed massive fraud, and Meta announced roughly 11,000 layoffs on November 9 as Amazon began its own rounds of cuts. Trump announced his 2024 presidential campaign on November 15, Nancy Pelosi announced she would step down from House Democratic leadership on November 17, and the FIFA World Cup opened in Qatar on November 20. OpenAI released ChatGPT to the public on November 30, and the Club Q shooting in Colorado Springs killed five on November 19.
    DecemberRaphael Warnock defeated Herschel Walker in a December 6 Georgia Senate runoff, giving Democrats an outright 51-49 majority; Sam Bankman-Fried was arrested in the Bahamas on December 12. The Federal Reserve delivered a final, smaller 50-basis-point hike on December 14, closing the year's target range at 4.25-4.50%. President Biden signed the Respect for Marriage Act on December 13, and Congress enacted the Electoral Count Reform Act as part of a $1.7 trillion year-end omnibus spending bill on December 29. Argentina beat France in a legendary World Cup final on December 18, completing Lionel Messi's coronation; Volodymyr Zelensky addressed Congress on December 21, Brittney Griner was released from Russian detention in a prisoner swap on December 8, and the January 6th Committee issued its final report and a criminal referral of Trump on December 19.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growthAbout 2.1% (annual average); real GDP contracted -1.6% (Q1) and -0.6% (Q2) before rebounding to +3.2% (Q3) and +2.6% (Q4)
    InflationCPI peaked at 9.1% YoY in June, the highest since November 1981; ended December at 6.5% YoY (core CPI 5.7%)
    UnemploymentFell from 4.0% in January to a cycle low of 3.5% by mid-year, closing December at 3.5%, a 50+ year low
    S&P 500 return-18.1% total return (price -19.4%), the worst year since 2008
    Dow return-6.9% total return (price -8.8%)
    Fed funds rateRaised seven times for a cumulative 425 bps, from 0-0.25% to 4.25-4.50% by year-end
    Oil priceWTI opened near $76, spiked above $130 intraday in March, closed near $80/barrel; Brent closed near $86
    Gold priceRoughly flat: opened near $1,829/oz, closed near $1,824/oz
    PopulationApproximately 333 million
    Median home priceNAR median existing-home price peaked at $413,800 in June, easing to about $366,900 by December
    National debtGross federal debt closed near $31.4 trillion, up from about $29.6 trillion at year-start

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2022 was to live through a year that never let up. It opened with troop-movement maps of the Ukrainian border and closed with a war still raging in Europe, a war that, within days of starting, sent gas prices toward $5 a gallon and helped push inflation to a 40-year high of 9.1% by June. For most of the year, the paycheck and the news told the same grim story: a Federal Reserve raising rates faster than at any point since Paul Volcker, a mortgage rate that doubled in twelve months, a stock and bond market that both fell together for the first time since 1937, and a persistent, gnawing sense, however statistically unfounded, that a recession was just around the corner. Yet the jobs report kept surprising to the upside, unemployment kept falling to lows unseen in half a century, and the two facts sat uneasily side by side all year, a genuine split-screen economy that pollsters started calling a "vibecession," bad vibes attached to numbers that, in isolation, looked fine.

    Politically, the year delivered one of the most consequential Supreme Court rulings in half a century. Dobbs did not just end a constitutional right; it rewired how tens of millions of Americans understood their own citizenship, their own bodies, their own states. For some it felt like liberation restored after fifty years of federal overreach; for others it felt like the floor giving way beneath a right they had assumed was permanent. That whiplash extended to Uvalde and Buffalo, to a January 6th hearing schedule that replayed the fragility of the last transfer of power all summer, and to an FBI search of a former president's beachfront home that would have been unthinkable a decade earlier. And yet, by November, the system had not broken. A bipartisan gun law passed. A closely fought midterm election was held, and its results, unlike two years earlier, were broadly accepted, even by candidates who had once questioned the last one. Democracy, dented and shouted-at all year, still worked.

    Culturally, there was real joy on offer for anyone willing to look for it: Beyonce's Renaissance, Top Gun's improbable box-office resurrection, Messi's World Cup coronation in December, Serena's tearful farewell. But the dominant feeling, for most people most of the time, was exhaustion, a sense of grinding through one compounding shock after another, waiting for normal to return and half-suspecting it might not. Almost nobody noticed, amid FTX's collapse and the World Cup final, that a company called OpenAI had quietly released something called ChatGPT the night before December began, a detail that would come to define the years that followed far more than anything else that happened in 2022.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2023
    Econ +2Mark +2Poli -2Cult +2Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    2023 was the year the American economy defied its own funeral. A year that opened with a near-consensus recession forecast instead delivered a genuine soft landing: inflation was cut roughly in half, unemployment stayed under 4 percent, real GDP grew faster than in 2022, and the stock market fully erased the prior year's bear market on the back of a narrow but ferocious rally in seven mega-cap technology stocks riding the sudden mainstreaming of generative artificial intelligence. That macroeconomic triumph, however, coexisted with a March banking panic that felled three of the largest bank failures in American history, a bond market that whipsawed from a 3.88% to a 5%-plus 10-year Treasury yield and back again, and a public mood that never caught up to the good news — the "vibecession" became the year's defining economic paradox.

    Washington, by contrast, delivered one of the rockiest years of institutional performance in modern memory. Kevin McCarthy needed fifteen ballots to become Speaker in January and was then ousted by his own conference in October, the first mid-term speaker removal in American history, paralyzing the House for three weeks. Donald Trump, the dominant frontrunner for the Republican presidential nomination, was indicted four separate times in four different jurisdictions, becoming the first former president criminally charged, while a sitting senator was indicted for bribery and a sitting House member was expelled. A debt-ceiling standoff was resolved just short of default, only for Fitch to strip the United States of its AAA credit rating months later. And in October, Hamas's attack on Israel and the ensuing Gaza war reopened American foreign policy and campus politics to a level of division not seen since the Iraq War, fracturing the Democratic coalition even as the Ukraine war ground into a stalemated counteroffensive.

    Culturally, 2023 pulsed with an energy that the pandemic years had drained away. "Barbenheimer" brought communal moviegoing roaring back, Taylor Swift's Eras Tour became a measurable macroeconomic event in the cities it visited, and Succession delivered what many called the era's defining television finale. Beneath the surface, though, two changes that would reorder the coming decade were taking shape with comparatively little fanfare: the release of GPT-4 and Nvidia's emergence as the indispensable supplier of the AI buildout, and the mainstreaming of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs like Ozempic. Both were treated in 2023 as interesting sidebars to the year's louder stories — the indictments, the balloon, the bank runs — but in hindsight they may prove to be the two developments future historians remember 2023 for above all others.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+2Growth accelerated above trend, unemployment stayed below 4%, and inflation was cut roughly in half without the widely forecast recession materializing.
    Markets+2The S&P 500 returned roughly 26% and the Nasdaq surged over 40%, but the rally's extreme narrowness around the Magnificent Seven and a mid-year bond/banking shock kept it short of an exceptional year.
    Politics-2A mid-term speaker ouster, three weeks of House paralysis, four criminal indictments of a former president, a debt-ceiling near-miss, and an impeachment inquiry reflected institutional strain well beyond normal dysfunction.
    Culture+2Barbenheimer, the Eras Tour, and a strong Oscars and TV season marked a genuinely vital creative year, tempered by strike-driven production shutdowns and post-October 7 campus polarization.
    Overall Mood+0Superb macroeconomic data and a vibrant cultural year were offset almost exactly by dysfunctional politics, a banking scare, and a fourth-quarter war shock, leaving the country's self-assessed mood stubbornly neutral-to-sour despite the numbers.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Silicon Valley Bank collapses in the second-largest U.S. bank failure in history, followed within days by Signature Bank and, in May, First Republic
    2. Donald Trump is indicted four times in 2023 — Manhattan, federal classified documents, federal January 6, and Georgia RICO — becoming the first former president criminally charged
    3. Kevin McCarthy is elected Speaker after fifteen ballots in January, then removed by his own conference in October, the first mid-term speaker ouster in U.S. history
    4. The Federal Reserve raises rates to 5.25%-5.50%, their highest since 2001, then signals a pivot to cuts as inflation cools sharply
    5. Hamas's October 7 attack on Israel kills roughly 1,200 people and ignites the Gaza war, fracturing U.S. politics and campus life
    6. "Barbenheimer" — the joint release of Barbie and Oppenheimer — becomes the cultural event of the summer and a symbol of moviegoing's post-pandemic revival
    7. Taylor Swift's Eras Tour becomes the highest-grossing concert tour in history and a measurable driver of local economic activity
    8. The debt ceiling standoff is resolved via the Fiscal Responsibility Act just before a possible default, and Fitch later downgrades U.S. sovereign credit
    9. Hollywood's writers and actors strike simultaneously for the first time since 1960, shutting down production for months over AI and streaming pay
    10. GPT-4 and the generative AI boom drive Nvidia's stock and the broader technology sector, powering a narrow but historic stock market rally

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The U.S. economy delivered a soft landing that almost no forecaster expected a year earlier: real GDP grew 2.5% for the year, accelerating from 2022, while headline inflation was cut roughly in half from January to December and unemployment never rose above the high-3% range. Consumer spending proved remarkably resilient, sustained by a still-tight labor market, real wage gains as inflation cooled faster than pay, and the drawdown of pandemic-era savings, while fiscal support from the CHIPS Act, the Inflation Reduction Act, and the infrastructure law fueled a boom in manufacturing construction. The labor market cooled from red-hot to merely tight without breaking, as job openings and the quits rate normalized toward pre-pandemic levels and the "great resignation" quietly ended. The year's gravest scare came in March, when Silicon Valley Bank's rapid, social-media-accelerated deposit run led to its seizure by regulators, followed within days by Signature Bank and, in May, First Republic — episodes that recalled 2008 but were contained through emergency backstops without becoming systemic. Housing sat in an uneasy stasis, with existing-home sales falling to their lowest level since 1995 as owners locked into low pandemic-era mortgages refused to sell, even as home prices stayed roughly flat to modestly higher. Corporate America saw a wave of high-profile labor action, including the UAW's novel "stand-up" strike against all three Detroit automakers and the concurrent Hollywood writers' and actors' strikes, alongside notable bankruptcies (Bed Bath & Beyond, Yellow Corporation, WeWork) that closed out the zero-rate-era excesses of the prior cycle.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew 2.5% for 2023 (versus 1.9% in 2022), with a standout 4.9% annualized Q3 print. Headline CPI fell from 6.4% year-over-year in January to 3.4% in December, while core CPI fell from 5.6% to 3.9% over the same span. Unemployment began the year at a 54-year-low 3.4% in January and ended at 3.7% in December, with nonfarm payrolls adding roughly 2.7 million jobs for the year. The Federal Reserve raised its target range four times to 5.25%-5.50% by July before holding steady through year-end. The 30-year mortgage rate spiked to a 23-year high near 7.79% in October before easing to about 6.6% by December, and existing-home sales fell to roughly 4.09 million units, the lowest since 1995. Gross national debt crossed $34 trillion in the final week of December.

    Major Events

    Silicon Valley Bank was seized by the FDIC on March 10 after a $42 billion deposit run, followed by Signature Bank on March 12 and First Republic's acquisition by JPMorgan Chase on May 1; regulators invoked the systemic risk exception to guarantee all uninsured deposits and the Fed launched the Bank Term Funding Program. The debt ceiling standoff was resolved through the Fiscal Responsibility Act, signed June 3, narrowly avoiding a default, and Fitch Ratings downgraded U.S. sovereign debt from AAA to AA+ on August 1 citing fiscal deterioration. Federal student loan payments restarted in October after a 43-month pandemic pause, affecting roughly 28 million borrowers. The UAW's "stand up" strike against Ford, GM, and Stellantis (September-October) won roughly 25% cumulative wage gains, while the concurrent WGA and SAG-AFTRA strikes shut down Hollywood for months. Major corporate consolidation included ExxonMobil's $59.5 billion acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources and Chevron's $53 billion bid for Hess, while Nvidia briefly touched a $1 trillion market capitalization in May on the strength of the generative AI boom.

    Why this score

    Growth above trend, sub-4% unemployment, inflation cut in half, and a banking scare contained without contagion together produced the closest thing to an unambiguous soft landing since the mid-1990s, though sticky shelter inflation and a widening fiscal deficit kept the year just short of an exceptional score.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    U.S. equities staged one of the more improbable rebounds of the era. The S&P 500 delivered a total return of roughly 26.3%, fully recovering 2022's bear-market losses and closing the year at 4,769.83, just shy of its January 2022 record. The Nasdaq Composite surged about 43-44%, its best year since 2020, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average returned roughly 16.2% and set a fresh all-time high in December. Bonds whipsawed dramatically: the 10-year Treasury yield began the year at 3.88%, spiked to a 16-year high above 5.0% in October on fiscal and supply worries, then rallied hard on the Fed's dovish pivot to close the year back at 3.88%, an almost surreal round trip. Commodities diverged sharply — gold closed near $2,062 an ounce, up about 13% and setting nominal records, while WTI crude fell about 11% to roughly $71.65 despite a mid-year spike above $80. Bitcoin nearly tripled over the course of the year on anticipation of spot ETF approval.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    The "Magnificent Seven" — Nvidia, Meta, Tesla, Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Apple — accounted for roughly two-thirds of the S&P 500's gain, with Nvidia alone up about 239% and Meta up roughly 194% as the generative AI trade became the dominant investment thesis of the year; Nvidia briefly reached a $1 trillion market capitalization in May. Regional bank equities lagged badly after the March failures of Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and First Republic, while WeWork's November bankruptcy, collapsing from a peak paper valuation of $47 billion to roughly $45 million in equity value, closed out the era of zero-rate-fueled unicorns. The IPO window reopened in the second half of the year with Arm Holdings, Instacart, Klaviyo, and Birkenstock, though aftermarket performance was mixed. Major M&A activity included ExxonMobil's $59.5 billion deal for Pioneer Natural Resources, Chevron's $53 billion bid for Hess, Cisco's $28 billion acquisition of Splunk, and Microsoft's long-delayed $69 billion closing of its Activision Blizzard deal.

    Investment Themes

    Generative AI dominated investment thinking after ChatGPT's late-2022 debut and the March 2023 release of GPT-4, driving enormous capital expenditure commitments from hyperscalers and an unprecedented run in AI-infrastructure suppliers led by Nvidia. Market breadth was historically narrow, with the equal-weighted S&P 500 returning only about 13.9% versus the cap-weighted index's 26%, underscoring how concentrated the year's gains were. The banking-sector stress in March briefly revived fears of a 2008-style crisis and produced the biggest three-day move in two-year Treasury yields since 1987, while the Fed's ultimate pivot to a dovish stance in December fueled a powerful "everything rally" that added roughly 16% to the S&P 500 in the final two months of the year. Gold's record highs and bitcoin's rebound reflected a broader hedge against fiscal and monetary uncertainty even as risk assets thrived.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Kevin McCarthy needed fifteen ballots over four days in January to win the speakership, the longest such contest since 1859, and was then removed by a 216-210 vote on October 3 after Rep. Matt Gaetz invoked the motion to vacate McCarthy had conceded to secure his election — the first mid-term speaker ouster in American history, which left the House without a functioning leader for three weeks until Mike Johnson was elected on October 25. Donald Trump was indicted four times over the year — in Manhattan over hush-money payments, in Florida over retained classified documents, in Washington over efforts to overturn the 2020 election, and in Georgia on RICO charges — becoming the first former U.S. president criminally charged, and surrendered for a mugshot in Fulton County in August while still dominating the Republican primary. The Supreme Court's June term struck down race-conscious college admissions, blocked Biden's student loan forgiveness plan, ruled for a Christian web designer, and rejected the "independent state legislature" theory. Senator Bob Menendez was indicted on bribery charges involving gold bars, and Rep. George Santos became only the sixth member ever expelled from the House. Hamas's October 7 attack on Israel and the ensuing Gaza war became the most contentious foreign-policy issue of the Biden presidency, fracturing the Democratic coalition.

    Domestic Policy

    Divided government arrived for the first time in the Biden presidency, with a narrow House Republican majority and a razor-thin Senate Democratic majority producing scarce major legislation and recurring fiscal brinkmanship. The White House promoted its economic record as "Bidenomics," pointing to groundbreakings for semiconductor and clean-energy plants funded by the infrastructure law, the CHIPS Act, and the Inflation Reduction Act, but polling showed persistent public skepticism and Biden's approval rating stuck in the high-30s to low-40s. The administration pursued an aggressive antitrust agenda, with the FTC suing to block the Microsoft-Activision merger (ultimately losing) and the DOJ filing its landmark monopolization case against Google, while Biden signed a sweeping executive order on AI safety in October. The southern border remained an intractable crisis after Title 42 ended in May, with record monthly encounters and continued friction between Republican governors busing migrants to Democratic-led cities and the mayors receiving them. Biden's September 26 visit to a UAW picket line in Michigan was the first by a sitting president to an active strike.

    Foreign Policy

    Foreign policy was shaped by three interlocking theaters. In Ukraine, a much-anticipated summer counteroffensive failed to achieve breakthrough despite Western tanks and eventual F-16 training commitments, and the year ended with a $106 billion Ukraine-Israel-border supplemental stalled in Congress amid eroding Republican support. In the Middle East, a quietly advancing Saudi-Israel normalization effort was shattered by Hamas's October 7 attack; the ensuing Gaza war saw the U.S. veto multiple UN Security Council ceasefire resolutions while pressing Israel on humanitarian access, and Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping beginning in November prompted a multinational U.S.-led maritime security operation. With China, February's Chinese spy balloon incident briefly derailed diplomacy before Secretary Blinken and other cabinet officials completed rescheduled visits to Beijing, culminating in a Biden-Xi meeting in November that resumed military-to-military communication even as the administration expanded semiconductor export controls. The Camp David trilateral summit between the U.S., Japan, and South Korea marked a historic security thaw between Tokyo and Seoul, while coups in Niger and Gabon further eroded U.S. influence in the Sahel.

    Important Elections

    In an off-year that previewed 2024, liberal candidate Janet Protasiewicz won the most expensive judicial race in U.S. history to flip the Wisconsin Supreme Court, Ohio voters enshrined abortion rights in their state constitution after rejecting a measure designed to raise the threshold for doing so, and Virginia Democrats denied Governor Glenn Youngkin a governing trifecta by holding the state Senate and flipping the House of Delegates. Democratic incumbent Andy Beshear won reelection as Kentucky governor in a state Trump had carried by 26 points, while Republican Tate Reeves won a closer-than-expected Mississippi governor's race. On the presidential side, Donald Trump maintained commanding 30-plus-point primary leads over a crowded Republican field despite his four indictments, Ron DeSantis's campaign never recovered from a glitch-plagued launch, and Nikki Haley emerged as his chief rival by year's end, while Biden faced only token Democratic primary opposition.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    The simultaneous July 21 release of Greta Gerwig's Barbie and Christopher Nolan's Oppenheimer — "Barbenheimer" — became the defining cultural event of the year, generating a combined opening weekend north of $244 million and driving Barbie to $1.44 billion worldwide, the highest-grossing film of the year and the top-grossing film ever directed by a woman. The Super Mario Bros. Movie, Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse, Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3, and Mission: Impossible - Dead Reckoning Part One rounded out the year's biggest hits, even as superhero fatigue set in with disappointing results for The Marvels, The Flash, and Ant-Man and the Wasp: Quantumania. Everything Everywhere All at Once swept the March Academy Awards with seven Oscars including Best Picture and historic wins for Michelle Yeoh and Ke Huy Quan, while critically acclaimed year-end releases included Killers of the Flower Moon, Poor Things, The Holdovers, and Past Lives.

    Music

    Taylor Swift dominated the year almost entirely on her own terms: her Eras Tour, launched in March, became the highest-grossing concert tour in history and a measurable driver of local economic activity, her re-recorded 1989 (Taylor's Version) topped the charts, and she was named TIME's Person of the Year in December. Morgan Wallen's One Thing at a Time was Billboard's top album of the year amid country music's broader crossover surge, led also by breakout artist Zach Bryan and viral populist hit "Rich Men North of Richmond" by Oliver Anthony. Olivia Rodrigo's GUTS, SZA's continuing dominance with SOS, and Beyoncé's Renaissance World Tour rounded out a year of major pop and R&B releases, while Miley Cyrus's "Flowers" was the year's biggest single globally.

    Television

    Succession concluded its fourth season on May 28 with what many critics called the definitive prestige-television finale of the era, while The Last of Us and The Bear's celebrated second season confirmed HBO's and FX's continued creative strength. Beef swept the Emmys, Netflix's May crackdown on password sharing became a defining and controversial industry moment even as the platform continued to grow, and streaming surpassed cable in total audience share for the first time in July. The Hollywood writers' and actors' strikes, the first joint work stoppage since 1960, shut down production for months over streaming residuals and AI protections, delaying the 2024 slate and reshaping the industry's approach to generative AI.

    Technology

    2023 was the year generative artificial intelligence became inescapable: OpenAI released GPT-4 in March, competitors including Google's Bard, Anthropic's Claude, and Meta's LLaMA arrived through the year, and Sam Altman's bizarre five-day firing and reinstatement at OpenAI in November became a landmark moment in AI governance. Apple announced its Vision Pro mixed-reality headset in June and brought USB-C to the iPhone 15 in September, ending the Lightning era, while Elon Musk's rebranding of Twitter to X continued a chaotic platform overhaul that many experienced as a genuine cultural loss. Meta's Threads app signed up 100 million users within five days of its July launch before rapidly losing momentum, and Sam Bankman-Fried's November conviction closed the book on the FTX collapse.

    Social Trends

    Trans rights, particularly for minors, became the year's dominant social flashpoint as more than twenty states restricted gender-affirming care, while the Bud Light boycott over a Dylan Mulvaney partnership and backlash to Target's Pride Month merchandise cost major retailers billions in market value amid an accelerating "anti-woke" and anti-DEI political current. Union activity reached multi-decade highs in public approval, embodied by the UAW's historic strike against the Big Three automakers and a last-minute deal averting a UPS-Teamsters strike. Semaglutide and tirzepatide drugs like Ozempic and Wegovy crashed into the cultural mainstream, reshaping conversations about weight loss and body image, and the mainstreaming of AI-generated images raised new anxieties about misinformation. Following October 7, pro-Palestinian campus protests became the era's defining student movement, culminating in a contentious December congressional hearing with the presidents of Harvard, Penn, and MIT.

    Crime

    The Idaho student murders case, in which Bryan Kohberger awaited trial for the killing of four University of Idaho students, and the Alex Murdaugh trial, which ended in a March conviction for the murder of his wife and son, dominated true-crime coverage. Robert Card's killing of eighteen people in Lewiston, Maine in October and the March shooting at Nashville's Covenant School, which killed six including three nine-year-olds, extended the grim baseline of American mass shootings, while FTX founder Sam Bankman-Fried was convicted of fraud in November and E. Jean Carroll won her civil case against Donald Trump for sexual abuse and defamation in May.

    Sports

    The Kansas City Chiefs defeated the Philadelphia Eagles in Super Bowl LVII in February, with Rihanna's pregnancy-announcing halftime show a cultural moment in its own right, and Lionel Messi's July move to Inter Miami generated a surge of interest in Major League Soccer. The Denver Nuggets won their first NBA title, the Vegas Golden Knights won their first Stanley Cup, and the Texas Rangers won their first World Series. LSU's win over Iowa in the NCAA women's basketball final, driven by the Angel Reese-Caitlin Clark rivalry, marked women's college basketball's decisive cultural arrival, while the U.S. women's national soccer team suffered a shocking round-of-16 exit at the FIFA Women's World Cup. The PGA Tour's stunning June merger announcement with Saudi-backed LIV Golf reshaped professional golf, and Taylor Swift's fall romance with Travis Kelce fused pop culture with the NFL.

    Public Mood

    The year had a genuinely strange texture: an economy that was statistically excellent by nearly every measure — unemployment near fifty-year lows, sharp disinflation, wage growth outpacing inflation for lower-income workers — that Americans nonetheless persistently rated as poor, a disconnect that gave rise to the widely discussed "vibecession" concept. Yet culturally the year felt alive in a way the pandemic years had not: Barbenheimer, the Eras Tour, and Beyoncé's Renaissance tour produced genuine communal experiences, and the return of the movie theater and record-breaking live music offered real relief from years of isolation. That vitality coexisted with real dread over AI's implications and, by the fourth quarter, the war in Gaza cast a dark shadow over the year's close and previewed the deeper polarization of 2024.

    🔭 Historical Perspective

    What people thought mattered

    Contemporary Americans, reading their newspapers through 2023, would have said the year was about inflation and interest rates, Donald Trump's mounting legal jeopardy, a grinding proxy war in Ukraine, and a strange new chatbot everyone was suddenly talking about. The domestic political drama that consumed daily coverage — the speaker fight, the indictments, the Hunter Biden inquiry — felt like the story of the era.

    What actually mattered

    In hindsight, 2023 was the year a phase change in artificial intelligence began in earnest, a metabolic-drug revolution quietly took hold, the post-1979 Middle Eastern order started to unravel, and the first visible cracks appeared in the Putin regime's hold on power. The economic story people assumed was central — Fed hikes and a recession that never came — turned out to be a soft-landing footnote, while the AI capex buildout and GLP-1 drugs, treated as curiosities, proved structural; the war people thought was defining, Ukraine, ground into stalemate, while the war people did not yet know was coming, Gaza, arrived in October and reshaped shipping lanes, campus politics, and the 2024 election alike.

    Most forgotten story

    The failure of Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and First Republic in March through May 2023 was, for roughly six weeks, the dominant American economic story and among the largest bank failures in U.S. history, complete with an emergency Federal Reserve backstop and real fears of systemic contagion — yet it has almost entirely vanished from popular memory, overshadowed within its own year by the AI boom and largely absent from later retrospectives.

    Most underrated story

    Nvidia's May 24 earnings call, in which the company guided to far higher revenue than Wall Street expected on the strength of AI datacenter demand, was covered at the time as an unusually strong tech print but in retrospect marks the starting gun of a capital cycle that has since exceeded half a trillion dollars in announced hyperscaler AI infrastructure spending and reordered American industrial policy around the semiconductor supply chain.

    Biggest surprise in hindsight

    That the chaotic five-day firing and reinstatement of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman in November — treated at the time as bizarre boardroom gossip below the fold — would come to be seen as the single most-cited event in serious analyses of why AI safety concerns failed to constrain the commercial development of frontier models.

    🔊 Signal vs Noise

    📢 Dominated the news — little lasting impact

    StoryWhy it faded
    The Chinese spy balloon drifting across the continental United States before being shot down off South CarolinaIt generated two weeks of wall-to-wall cable coverage and congressional hearings but proved a minor intelligence embarrassment that did not measurably alter the pre-existing trajectory of U.S.-China strategic competition.
    The OceanGate Titan submersible implosion during its dive to the Titanic wreckA ninety-six-hour viral spectacle about billionaire hubris, it is remembered today mainly as a data point about luxury-adventure risk rather than a story with lasting historical weight.
    "Barbenheimer" as proof that theatrical moviegoing had fully reboundedThe summer box office surge proved a one-off spike rather than a trend, especially once the Hollywood strikes shut down production for months immediately afterward.
    The debt-ceiling standoff and Fiscal Responsibility Act negotiationsThe final deal's fiscal footprint was negligible next to the interest-rate environment and entitlement trajectory, and its most consequential political effect — feeding Kevin McCarthy's ouster — is now read as a story about House Republican fragmentation rather than fiscal policy.
    The launch of Meta's Threads app as the presumed end of Twitter/XThreads settled into a middling, unremarkable platform, while the more consequential social-media story — the structural erosion of X's advertiser base and the fragmentation of political discourse across smaller platforms — was not captured by any single 2023 headline.

    🌱 Little attention — later changed history

    StoryWhy it mattered
    The public release of GPT-4 in March 2023It marked the moment generative AI crossed into professional utility, triggering the AI capital expenditure supercycle and the Nvidia-led reordering of the semiconductor and datacenter industries that dominates the mid-2020s economy.
    The clinical and cultural mainstreaming of semaglutide and tirzepatide (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro)Trial data published that year on cardiovascular and other benefits transformed GLP-1 drugs from a diabetes niche into what looks like the most consequential pharmaceutical category since statins, with ripple effects across the food, beverage, and healthcare industries.
    The Wagner Group's June mutiny and march on MoscowTreated as a 36-hour thriller and quickly closed, it is now understood as the first public crack in the Putin regime's monopoly on force and a preview of the succession anxieties shaping Kremlin politics today.
    The East Palestine, Ohio train derailment in February 2023Covered intensely for about two weeks and then dropped, it seeded a durable populist grievance later exploited in the 2024 rust-belt political map and drove meaningful federal rail-safety rule changes.

    Representative Headlines

    1. Chinese Surveillance Balloon Drifts Across U.S.; Shot Down Off Carolina Coast
    2. Silicon Valley Bank Seized in Second-Largest U.S. Bank Failure in History; Fed and Treasury Backstop All Deposits
    3. OpenAI Releases GPT-4, Passing Bar and Medical Licensing Exams
    4. Trump Indicted in Manhattan, First Ex-President to Face Criminal Charges
    5. Wagner Mercenaries March on Moscow, Then Turn Back
    6. Supreme Court Strikes Down Race-Conscious College Admissions
    7. Lahaina Destroyed in Deadliest U.S. Wildfire in a Century
    8. Hamas Attack Kills More Than 1,200 in Israel; Gaza War Begins
    9. Speaker McCarthy Ousted in Historic House Vote, Chamber Paralyzed for Weeks
    10. OpenAI Board Fires Sam Altman, Reinstates Him Five Days Later After Employee Revolt

    🗓️ Timeline

    JanuaryKevin McCarthy won the House speakership only after fifteen rounds of voting over four days, the longest such contest since 1859, conceding a rule allowing any single member to force a vote to vacate the chair. Classified documents from Joe Biden's vice presidency were discovered at his Wilmington home, prompting appointment of a special counsel, while unemployment fell to a 54-year-low 3.4%.
    FebruaryA suspected Chinese surveillance balloon drifted across the continental United States for roughly a week before being shot down off the South Carolina coast, dominating news coverage and briefly derailing U.S.-China diplomacy. A Norfolk Southern train derailed in East Palestine, Ohio, releasing hazardous chemicals, and a mass shooting struck Michigan State University.
    MarchSilicon Valley Bank was seized by regulators on March 10 in the second-largest U.S. bank failure ever, followed within days by Signature Bank and, weeks later, the collapse of Credit Suisse; OpenAI released GPT-4; the Nashville Covenant School shooting killed six; the Alex Murdaugh murder trial ended in conviction; and Taylor Swift's Eras Tour launched in Glendale, Arizona.
    AprilFirst Republic Bank continued hemorrhaging deposits ahead of its eventual seizure, Donald Trump was indicted in Manhattan over hush-money payments, Fox News settled with Dominion Voting Systems for $787.5 million, and Bed Bath & Beyond filed for bankruptcy.
    MayJPMorgan Chase acquired First Republic Bank out of receivership, the largest U.S. bank failure since Washington Mutual in 2008; Title 42 border restrictions ended; the Hollywood writers' strike began; and the Supreme Court's term neared its blockbuster conclusions on affirmative action and student debt relief.
    JuneThe debt ceiling standoff was resolved through the Fiscal Responsibility Act, narrowly avoiding default; the Supreme Court struck down race-conscious college admissions and Biden's student loan forgiveness plan; the OceanGate Titan submersible imploded during a dive to the Titanic wreck; and the Wagner Group staged a brief armed mutiny and march toward Moscow.
    JulySAG-AFTRA joined the writers' strike, producing Hollywood's first joint work stoppage since 1960; Twitter was rebranded as X under Elon Musk; Meta's Threads app briefly surged to 100 million users; and "Barbenheimer" — the joint release of Barbie and Oppenheimer — became the summer's defining cultural phenomenon.
    AugustTrump was indicted twice more, in Washington over efforts to overturn the 2020 election and in Georgia on RICO charges, surrendering in Fulton County for his mugshot; Fitch downgraded U.S. sovereign credit from AAA to AA+; and wildfires devastated Lahaina, Maui, killing more than 100 people in the deadliest U.S. wildfire in over a century.
    SeptemberThe UAW launched its unprecedented simultaneous "stand up" strike against Ford, GM, and Stellantis; House Republicans opened a formal impeachment inquiry into President Biden; Senator Bob Menendez was indicted on bribery charges; and student loan payments resumed after a 43-month pandemic pause.
    OctoberHamas's attack on Israel killed roughly 1,200 people and ignited the Gaza war, reshaping U.S. foreign policy for the remainder of the year; Kevin McCarthy was ousted as Speaker on October 3, leaving the House without a leader for three weeks until Mike Johnson's election on October 25; and Robert Card killed eighteen people in Lewiston, Maine.
    NovemberOpenAI's board fired CEO Sam Altman and reinstated him five days later after an employee revolt, a defining moment for AI governance; George Santos was expelled from the House; a near-shutdown was averted through a novel "laddered" continuing resolution; and the Federal Reserve held rates steady while markets rallied on hopes of a dovish pivot.
    DecemberThe Federal Reserve's dot plot signaled three anticipated 2024 rate cuts, fueling a powerful year-end stock rally; gross national debt crossed $34 trillion; Time named Taylor Swift its Person of the Year; and the House formalized its Biden impeachment inquiry by a near party-line vote as the year closed with the Israel-Hamas war still raging and Ukraine aid stalled in Congress.

    📊 Data Appendix

    GDP growth+2.5% real GDP growth (2023 annual, BEA)
    InflationHeadline CPI 3.4% year-over-year in December 2023 (down from 6.4% in January); core CPI 3.9% in December
    Unemployment3.7% in December 2023 (3.4% January low; 3.6% annual average)
    S&P 500 return+26.3% total return (approx. +24.2% price return), closing at 4,769.83
    Dow return+16.2% total return (approx. +13.7% price return)
    Fed funds rate5.25%-5.50% target range at year-end (up from 4.25%-4.50%)
    Oil priceWTI crude approximately $71.65/barrel at year-end (down about 11% year-over-year; mid-year peak above $80)
    Gold priceApproximately $2,062/oz (December 29 spot), up roughly 13% on the year
    PopulationApproximately 335 million
    Median home priceApproximately $387,600 (NAR median existing-home price, December 2023)
    National debtGross national debt crossed $34 trillion in late December 2023; federal debt held by the public roughly $26.9 trillion at fiscal year-end

    ⚖️ Final Verdict — What did it feel like?

    To be an American in 2023 was to live inside a contradiction that never fully resolved. By almost every conventional economic measure, it was a remarkably good year: inflation was cut nearly in half, unemployment stayed under 4 percent for the entire year, wages finally grew faster than prices, and the stock market roared back from the prior year's losses. Yet surveys showed persistent gloom, a phenomenon commentators labeled the "vibecession" — Americans told pollsters the economy was bad even as the data said otherwise, still smarting from two years of the highest inflation in four decades and unnerved by mortgage rates that made homeownership feel newly unreachable even as home prices stayed stubbornly high.

    Politically, the year offered little reassurance that the country's institutions were functioning well. The spectacle of a fifteen-ballot speakership fight in January gave way to an even stranger spectacle in October, when the House ousted its own speaker for the first time in history and sat leaderless for three weeks while a little-known Louisiana congressman emerged as compromise choice. A former president was indicted four times and somehow grew stronger within his own party for it. A sitting senator was accused of taking bribes in the form of gold bars. It was possible to feel, reading the news each morning, that the machinery of American governance was being held together with tape — and yet, somehow, default was avoided, a government shutdown was averted, and by year's end even a bipartisan defense bill passed. Dysfunction, not collapse, was the texture of the year.

    Then, in October, a war seven thousand miles away reached into American life in a way few had anticipated. Hamas's attack on Israel and the devastating war that followed split families, campuses, and the Democratic coalition itself, adding a new and bitter fault line to a country already exhausted by its domestic ones. College campuses, still recovering their footing after the pandemic, became the site of a new and painful argument about free speech, antisemitism, and the limits of protest that would carry, unresolved, into the following year.

    And yet, for all that, 2023 did not feel like a bleak year to live through — it felt, more than anything, alive again. The pandemic's flattening of communal experience finally lifted: strangers filled movie theaters in pink and black on the same July weekend, stadiums of fans screamed every word of a three-hour Taylor Swift set, and a prestige drama's finale became something like a national event people discussed the next morning at work. A new technology, a chatbot that could write essays and pass the bar exam, provoked equal parts wonder and dread at dinner tables across the country, without anyone quite grasping yet how much it would reorder in the years ahead. Beneath the loud, familiar arguments — about Trump, about the Fed, about a balloon drifting over Montana — quieter revolutions in artificial intelligence and medicine were taking root that would prove, in time, far more consequential than anything on the front page. Americans in 2023 could not have told you that; they could only tell you that the year felt strange, exhausting, occasionally thrilling, and never quite as bad, or as good, as the headlines insisted it was.

    ↑ back to heatmap
    2024
    Econ +1Mark +2Poli -1Cult +1Over +0
    ▸

    Executive Summary

    The American economy in 2024 pulled off the rare trick of aging expansion without a recession that nearly everyone, at the start of the year, still expected. Real GDP grew 2.8 percent, unemployment drifted up to 4.3 percent in July before settling back to 4.1 percent by December, and headline inflation fell into the twos even as core inflation proved stickier than the Federal Reserve hoped. The Fed, holding at a two-decade-high 5.25-5.50 percent through the summer, opened its easing cycle in September with a surprise half-point cut and delivered two more quarter-point cuts by year-end, even as a "hawkish" December projection and a rising 10-year Treasury yield complicated the narrative of victory over inflation. Equity investors, meanwhile, enjoyed a second consecutive roughly 25 percent total-return year for the S&P 500, propelled by an unprecedented concentration of gains in Nvidia and the rest of the "Magnificent Seven" as an AI-infrastructure capital-spending boom reordered corporate America; gold, Bitcoin, and the dollar all posted strong gains simultaneously, an unusual combination that reflected both genuine optimism and deep hedging against fiscal and political uncertainty.

    Politically, 2024 delivered the most dramatic presidential campaign in generations and ended in a decisive, history-making result. President Biden's halting June 27 debate performance triggered a four-week party crisis that ended with his July 21 withdrawal, the first exit by a sitting president seeking renomination since 1968, and Vice President Kamala Harris's rapid consolidation of the Democratic nomination. Two assassination attempts against Donald Trump, a Manhattan jury's conviction of him on 34 felony counts, and a Supreme Court ruling granting former presidents broad immunity for official acts gave the year an atmosphere of genuine constitutional strain even as the underlying institutions kept functioning. On November 5, Trump swept all seven swing states and won the national popular vote, becoming only the second president to win non-consecutive terms, while Republicans retook the Senate and narrowly held the House. Abroad, the collapse of the Assad regime in Syria, the near-total dismantling of Hezbollah's and Hamas's leadership, and Ukraine's incursion into Russia's Kursk region reshaped two regional wars whose endings Americans would only later come to understand as a hinge in the post-Cold-War order.

    Culturally, 2024 offered genuine, shared joys — the Paris Olympics and Simone Biles's redemption, the total solar eclipse crossing the country in April, Taylor Swift's Eras Tour finale and her romance with Travis Kelce, the Kendrick Lamar-Drake feud and "Brat summer" — set against a backdrop of campus unrest over Gaza, a jittery "vibecession" in which decent economic data coexisted with sour public sentiment, and a decisive election that many cultural commentators experienced as the end of a decade-long ideological consensus. Beneath the daily headlines, quieter developments — the Supreme Court's overturning of Chevron deference, the first confirmed spillover of H5N1 bird flu into dairy cattle, and Nvidia's ascent as the linchpin of an AI capital-spending supercycle — would prove, in hindsight, to be the developments that most reshaped the following two years, even though almost none of them led the evening news at the time.

    Scorecard

    CategoryScoreJustification
    Economy+1Growth beat every forecast at 2.8 percent, inflation kept falling without a recession, and unemployment ended near 4 percent, but sticky core inflation, a frozen housing market, and a 6.4-percent-of-GDP deficit kept it short of an exceptional year.
    Markets+2Back-to-back 25-percent S&P 500 total-return years alongside record highs in gold and Bitcoin were genuinely exceptional, though extreme concentration in a handful of AI-linked mega-caps and a rising 10-year yield during a Fed cutting cycle kept it a notch below a perfect score.
    Politics-1A voluntary presidential withdrawal, two failed assassination attempts, and a peaceful transition set in motion amid a functioning Congress and courts reflect real institutional resilience, but a felony-convicted president-elect, an impeached cabinet secretary, a convicted senator, sweeping presidential pardons, and a Supreme Court immunity ruling represent extraordinary strain by any pre-2016 standard.
    Culture+1Genuine creative and communal high points, from the Olympics to Brat summer to Caitlin Clark's rise, were real but coexisted with campus turmoil, a politically exhausted public, and a year that ended more culturally divided than it began.
    Overall Mood+0Objectively strong growth, markets, and several unifying cultural moments were counterbalanced by a bruising election, two assassination attempts, foreign-war anxiety, and a persistent gap between healthy macro data and sour public sentiment, leaving 2024 as a genuinely split verdict rather than a clear positive or negative year.

    Top 10 Headlines

    1. Donald Trump defeats Kamala Harris to win a second, non-consecutive presidential term, sweeping all seven swing states
    2. Joe Biden withdraws from the race on July 21 after a disastrous June debate, and endorses Harris
    3. A gunman's bullet grazes Trump's ear at a rally in Butler, Pennsylvania, killing a bystander who shielded his family
    4. A Manhattan jury convicts Trump on all 34 felony counts in the hush-money trial, the first such conviction of a former president
    5. The Supreme Court rules that former presidents have broad immunity for official acts, reshaping the January 6 prosecution
    6. The Federal Reserve begins cutting interest rates in September with a surprise half-point cut, ending its two-year hiking cycle
    7. The S&P 500 notches a second straight roughly 25 percent year as Nvidia becomes the world's most valuable company
    8. The Paris Olympics captivate the country, led by Simone Biles's comeback and Team USA topping the medal table
    9. Bashar al-Assad's government collapses in Syria after a lightning rebel offensive, ending 54 years of family rule
    10. Hurricanes Helene and Milton devastate the Southeast weeks before the election, straining FEMA and federal disaster response

    💵 Economy

    Summary

    The US economy in 2024 delivered the "soft landing" that had been forecast, doubted, and forecast again for nearly two years: real GDP grew 2.8 percent for the year, decelerating only slightly from 2.9 percent in 2023 and comfortably above the roughly 1.8 percent long-run potential estimated by the Congressional Budget Office. Consumer spending, backed by real wage gains that had turned positive in mid-2023, remained the clear engine of growth, while business investment was buoyed by an AI-driven capital-spending boom in data centers, semiconductors, and power infrastructure. The labor market cooled without cracking: unemployment rose from 3.7 percent in January to a worrying 4.3 percent in July, briefly triggering the Sahm Rule recession indicator and a summer growth scare, before settling back to 4.1 percent by December on the strength of roughly 2.2 million net payroll additions for the year. Housing remained the expansion's clear laggard, locked in an affordability recession as mortgage rates oscillated between roughly 6.1 and 7.5 percent, existing-home sales fell to their lowest annual pace since 1995, and the median existing-home price nonetheless climbed to a record.

    Important Statistics

    Real GDP grew 2.8 percent for the year (2.5 percent on a Q4/Q4 basis); headline CPI ended December at 2.9 percent year-over-year (down from 3.4 percent at the end of 2023 but up from a September low of 2.4 percent), with core CPI stuck at 3.2 percent and core PCE, the Fed's preferred gauge, at roughly 2.8 percent. Unemployment ended the year at 4.1 percent after a July peak of 4.3 percent; the Federal Reserve held its funds rate at 5.25-5.50 percent for the first eight months before cutting 50 basis points on September 18, 25 basis points on November 7, and 25 basis points on December 18, ending the year at 4.25-4.50 percent. The 10-year Treasury yield nonetheless rose over the year, from about 3.88 percent to about 4.57 percent, a "bear steepener" read as markets pricing in fiscal risk and stickier inflation. The 30-year mortgage rate ended near 6.85 percent, the median existing-home price reached roughly $404,400 in December on an annual average near $407,500, and existing-home sales fell to about 4.06 million units, the lowest since 1995. The FY2024 federal deficit came in near $1.83 trillion (about 6.4 percent of GDP), gross federal debt closed near $36.2 trillion, and net interest outlays exceeded $880 billion, surpassing defense spending for the first time since the 1990s.

    Major Events

    The year's central economic drama was the Federal Reserve's pivot: after holding rates at a two-decade high through the summer, the FOMC opened its easing cycle on September 18 with an unusually large 50-basis-point cut that signaled real concern about softening jobs data, following the July and August labor reports that had briefly convinced markets a recession was starting. The August 5 "yen carry trade unwind," triggered by a Bank of Japan rate hike layered onto a soft US jobs report, sent the Nikkei down 12.4 percent in a single session and the VIX briefly above 65 before markets recovered within weeks. Boeing endured a near-catastrophic year after the January 5 Alaska Airlines door-plug blowout, culminating in a two-month machinists' strike, a new CEO in Kelly Ortberg, and a $24 billion capital raise; its shares fell about 32 percent. The SEC's January 10 approval of spot Bitcoin ETFs, followed by July's spot Ethereum ETF launch, mainstreamed cryptocurrency investing, and Bitcoin broke $100,000 in December. Consolidation continued across finance and energy, with Capital One's $35 billion agreed acquisition of Discover Financial and ExxonMobil's completed $60 billion purchase of Pioneer Natural Resources as signature deals, while Red Lobster, Spirit Airlines, Tupperware, Party City, and Big Lots each filed for bankruptcy. Trump's decisive November 5 election victory reshaped market expectations for tariffs, taxes, immigration, and regulation heading into 2025, and December's "hawkish cut," with the Fed penciling in only two rate reductions for 2025, jolted equities on the day it was announced.

    Why this score

    The +1 score reflects a genuinely good outcome by post-pandemic-era standards: growth beat every forecast, inflation kept falling without a recession, and unemployment ended near 4 percent even after a mid-year scare, but sticky core inflation that never reached target, a housing market frozen at its least active pace in nearly three decades, and a structural deficit near 6.4 percent of GDP outside any recession or war kept the year from earning a higher grade.

    📈 Markets

    Performance

    US equities delivered a second consecutive blockbuster year, the first back-to-back 20-plus percent gains for the S&P 500 since 1997-98. The S&P 500 returned about 25.0 percent on a total-return basis, the Nasdaq Composite about 29.6 percent, the Dow Jones Industrial Average about 15.0 percent, and the Russell 2000 a more modest 11.5 percent. Volatility stayed low for most of the year outside the August 5 yen-carry-trade shock, when an intraday VIX spike above 65 briefly unsettled global markets before a rapid recovery. A late-year "Trump trade" rally following the November 5 election lifted small-caps, financials, and crypto, though much of the move faded in December after the Fed's more hawkish rate projections; the S&P 500 fell nearly 3 percent on the day of the December 18 decision alone. Breadth was historically narrow: the equal-weighted S&P 500 returned only about 13 percent, roughly half the cap-weighted index's return, underscoring how concentrated the year's gains were in a small number of mega-cap technology names.

    Character

    Bull

    Major Companies

    Nvidia's ascent defined the year, its shares rising roughly 171 percent and briefly overtaking Apple and Microsoft as the world's most valuable company at more than $3.5 trillion in market capitalization on the strength of AI-chip demand; Broadcom joined the trillion-dollar club in December, and TSMC and Meta each gained roughly 65 and 70 percent. Notable IPOs included Reddit in March (pricing at $34 and closing the year near $160), Astera Labs, Lineage's roughly $4.4 billion July offering (the year's largest), ServiceTitan in December, and Trump Media & Technology Group's March debut via SPAC merger. Boeing was the year's most conspicuous corporate crisis, falling about 32 percent after the Alaska Airlines door-plug blowout, a machinists' strike, and a $24 billion capital raise under new CEO Kelly Ortberg. Financial-system stress was mercifully contained relative to 2023's regional-bank turmoil, with the April failure of the small Republic First Bank an orderly exception, though commercial real estate, particularly office space, continued to simmer without metastasizing.

    Investment Themes

    The dominant theme was AI-driven capital expenditure: hyperscaler spending on data centers, semiconductors, and power infrastructure drove the "Magnificent Seven" (Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Tesla) to account for more than half of the S&P 500's total return. Gold was the standout traditional asset, ending the year around $2,624 an ounce after peaking above $2,800 in October, a roughly 27 percent gain and its best year since 2010, driven by central-bank buying, especially from China and other emerging markets, alongside geopolitical hedging. Bitcoin and crypto more broadly had a defining year, with spot Bitcoin ETFs approved in January, Bitcoin breaking $100,000 in December, and a post-election pro-crypto political tailwind under the incoming Trump administration. WTI crude was remarkably calm at around $71.72 a barrel given ongoing Middle East conflict and record US output near 13.5 million barrels a day, while the dollar rose about 7 percent on the year, hitting two-year highs after the election, an unusual combination of simultaneous strength in equities, gold, Bitcoin, and the dollar that reflected both genuine optimism and deep-seated hedging against fiscal and political uncertainty.

    🏛️ Politics

    Major Events

    Donald Trump defeated Vice President Kamala Harris 312 to 226 in the Electoral College on November 5, sweeping all seven battleground states and becoming the first Republican to win the national popular vote since 2004, while Republicans flipped the Senate 53-47 and held the House by a razor-thin margin. The race had been upended on June 27 when President Biden's halting, hoarse debate performance triggered nearly four weeks of escalating pressure from Democratic elites that ended with his July 21 withdrawal, the first mid-cycle exit by a sitting president since Lyndon Johnson in 1968, and Harris's rapid consolidation of the nomination with Minnesota Governor Tim Walz as her running mate. Trump survived two assassination attempts, a graze to the ear from a rooftop shooter at a Butler, Pennsylvania rally on July 13 that killed rally-goer Corey Comperatore, and a second foiled attempt near his West Palm Beach golf club on September 15. On May 30 a Manhattan jury convicted Trump on all 34 felony counts of falsifying business records in the Stormy Daniels hush-money case, making him the first former president convicted of a crime, even as his federal January 6 and classified-documents cases collapsed after the election and the Georgia case stalled amid the prosecutor's disqualification fight. The Supreme Court's July 1 ruling in Trump v. United States granted former presidents broad immunity for official acts, while its June 28 Loper Bright decision overturned the 40-year Chevron doctrine of judicial deference to federal agencies. Congress impeached DHS Secretary Alejandro Mayorkas in February over border management, the first cabinet impeachment in nearly 150 years, though the Senate dismissed the charges without trial; Senator Bob Menendez was convicted on federal corruption charges in July and resigned in August; and pro-Palestinian campus protests, beginning at Columbia in April, produced thousands of arrests and helped force out several university presidents.

    Domestic Policy

    Governance unfolded under divided control, with Democrats holding the White House and a narrow Senate majority and Republicans holding the House under Speaker Mike Johnson, whose position was saved in May when Democrats crossed the aisle to defeat Marjorie Taylor Greene's motion to vacate. Legislative output was thin but not empty: Congress cleared FY2024 appropriations in March after months of continuing resolutions, passed a $95 billion foreign-aid package for Ukraine, Israel, and the Indo-Pacific in April alongside a law forcing TikTok's divestment or ban, reauthorized FISA Section 702, and passed a five-year FAA reauthorization in May. Immigration dominated domestic argument: a bipartisan Senate border deal collapsed in February after Trump publicly opposed it, but Biden's June 4 executive order sharply restricting asylum, combined with tightened Mexican enforcement, drove border encounters down substantially from their December 2023 peak. The Supreme Court punted on major post-Dobbs abortion questions, dismissing challenges to mifepristone access and to Idaho's emergency-abortion law on procedural grounds, while ballot measures protecting abortion access passed in seven of ten states that voted on the question in November. Other consequential rulings narrowed the January 6 obstruction statute, upheld a federal gun ban for domestic-violence offenders, allowed cities to punish public sleeping, and curbed in-house administrative enforcement proceedings. Violent crime continued a sharp post-2020 decline, and Biden closed the year with a controversial blanket pardon of his son Hunter on December 1 and clemency for roughly 1,500 people on home confinement and dozens of federal death-row inmates.

    Foreign Policy

    The Biden administration's foreign policy was consumed by two grinding wars and a widening Middle East that transformed by year's end. In Ukraine, the long-delayed April aid package restored artillery and air-defense supplies, Biden progressively lifted restrictions on strikes into Russian territory, and Ukrainian forces launched a surprise incursion into Russia's Kursk Oblast in August that held territory into year's end even as Russia advanced in the Donbas; Sweden formally joined NATO in March. In the Middle East, the Israel-Hamas war passed its first anniversary with the death toll continuing to climb, Iran launched unprecedented direct missile and drone attacks on Israel in April and October, and Israel killed Hamas leader Ismail Haniyeh in Tehran, Hezbollah leader Hassan Nasrallah in Beirut following the September pager and walkie-talkie attacks, and Hamas military chief Yahya Sinwar in Rafah, effectively dismantling the leadership of Iran's regional "Axis of Resistance" over a matter of months. That collapse culminated on December 8 when Islamist rebels captured Damascus in a lightning offensive, ending 54 years of Assad family rule as Bashar al-Assad fled to Moscow. On China, the administration raised tariffs on electric vehicles, batteries, and semiconductors, signed the TikTok divest-or-ban law, and Biden met Xi Jinping at the November APEC summit in Lima; North Korea deepened its alignment with Russia, sending roughly 10,000 troops to fight in Kursk by year's end. An August prisoner exchange, the largest East-West swap since the Cold War, freed journalist Evan Gershkovich, former Marine Paul Whelan, and dissident Vladimir Kara-Murza.

    Important Elections

    Trump and running mate JD Vance defeated Harris and Walz 312-226 in the Electoral College, with Trump winning the popular vote roughly 49.8 to 48.3 percent and improving among nearly every demographic group, including Latino men, Black men, and young voters, a shift Democratic analysts attributed to persistent inflation memory, immigration concerns, and a broader working-class realignment. Turnout was about 64 percent of eligible voters, slightly below 2020's record. Republicans netted four Senate seats to reach a 53-seat majority, unseating incumbent Democrats in Ohio, Montana, and Pennsylvania and taking the open West Virginia seat, while Democrats held competitive races in Michigan, Wisconsin, Arizona, Nevada, and Maryland. In the House, Republicans held on with a two-seat, 220-215 margin that left Speaker Johnson vulnerable to any internal faction, while Sarah McBride of Delaware became the first openly transgender person elected to Congress. At the state level, Democrat Josh Stein routed Republican Mark Robinson in North Carolina's governor's race after damaging reporting on Robinson's past online conduct, and ballot measures approved abortion protections in seven states while falling short in Florida, South Dakota, and Nebraska.

    🎬 Culture

    Movies

    The March 10 Academy Awards gave Best Picture to Oppenheimer, capping the 2023 "Barbenheimer" summer with Christopher Nolan's first directing Oscar. Among films released in 2024, Inside Out 2 became both the year's highest-grossing film worldwide at nearly $1.7 billion and the highest-grossing animated film in history, Deadpool & Wolverine revived Marvel's fortunes at $1.3 billion, Dune: Part Two delivered the year's most critically praised blockbuster, and Wicked exceeded expectations with more than $700 million in worldwide gross and a season-long press tour that made Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo inescapable. On the awards circuit, Anora, The Brutalist, A Complete Unknown, Conclave, The Substance, Emilia Perez, and Nickel Boys defined the fall conversation, with Anora going on to win Best Picture at the following March's ceremony; theatrical attendance nonetheless remained roughly 25 percent below 2019 levels as the release calendar continued to feel the effects of 2023's industry strikes.

    Music

    Charli XCX's Brat, released June 7 with its lime-green cover, became the year's defining cultural artifact, spawning a viral "Brat summer" aesthetic that briefly fused pop music, internet culture, and presidential politics after Harris's campaign embraced the "kamala IS brat" meme in July. Beyonce's Cowboy Carter debuted at number one in March and later won Album of the Year at the following February's Grammys, her first win in that category, while Taylor Swift's The Tortured Poets Department shattered first-day streaming records in April. Kendrick Lamar's spring feud with Drake culminated in "Not Like Us" on May 4, the song of the summer and a cultural verdict that reshaped hip-hop's hierarchy, later winning Record and Song of the Year at the Grammys. Sabrina Carpenter broke through with Short n' Sweet and a trilogy of hit singles, Chappell Roan's 2023 album became a 2024 phenomenon on the strength of "Good Luck, Babe!" and a standout Coachella set, and country music enjoyed a genuine crossover boom led by Post Malone, Shaboozey, and Zach Bryan.

    Television

    FX's Shogun swept the September Emmys with a record 18 wins and became the year's consensus prestige-drama pick, while Netflix's Baby Reindeer and HBO's The Penguin were the other dominant conversation-pieces of the year, the former spawning a real-world legal controversy over its "true story" claims. Ripley, Fallout, and 3 Body Problem drew large audiences earlier in the year, and The Bear's third season proved polarizing after two beloved predecessors. Streaming's push into live sports accelerated, with Netflix's Jake Paul-Mike Tyson fight in November drawing 108 million global viewers despite technical failures, and its Christmas Day NFL doubleheader with a Beyonce halftime show cementing the platform's live-sports ambitions, even as linear television continued its long decline, propped up mainly by the NFL and breaking news.

    Technology

    Generative AI moved from novelty to ambient utility over the course of the year: OpenAI released GPT-4o with real-time voice in May and the "reasoning" model o1 in September, ChatGPT reached roughly 300 million weekly users, Anthropic's Claude 3.5 Sonnet became a developer favorite, and Google's Gemini and Meta's open-weights Llama 3 pushed a genuinely competitive AI market. Apple's June announcement of "Apple Intelligence" was widely judged underwhelming on rollout, while Nvidia's stock surge briefly made it the world's most valuable company on the back of the AI-infrastructure buildout. Consumer hardware had a mixed year, with the February Vision Pro launch met with muted reception, humbler Meta Ray-Ban smart glasses becoming a genuine hit, and AI-hardware startups Humane and Rabbit both flopping publicly; Bluesky surged past 20 million users as X continued a perceived cultural decline, and Bitcoin's climb past $100,000 in December capped a defining year for cryptocurrency, catalyzed by January's spot ETF approvals and a pro-crypto president-elect.

    Social Trends

    The pro-Palestinian campus movement was the year's dominant protest story, beginning with Columbia's April 17 encampment and spreading to more than 130 campuses before mass arrests and stricter university policies quieted it by fall. Abortion access remained a mobilizing issue two years after Dobbs, with ballot measures passing in seven of ten states that considered them in November. Religious affiliation continued its long decline, with Pew's landmark study finding 62 percent of Americans identifying as Christian, down from 78 percent in 2007, while Jonathan Haidt's book The Anxious Generation, published in March, sharpened public anxiety over phone addiction and adolescent mental health and helped drive a bipartisan wave of state-level classroom phone bans. Demographically, the US population reached roughly 335 million, with immigration remaining the primary driver of growth as fertility held at a record low of roughly 1.6 births per woman.

    Crime

    Violent crime continued a sharp decline from its 2020-21 peak, with preliminary FBI data showing murder down roughly 15 percent year-over-year even as public perception of crime lagged the actual trend. The year's dominant celebrity and legal stories included Sean "Diddy" Combs's September federal indictment on racketeering and sex-trafficking charges, the Karen Read retrial and Delphi murders trial, which drew sustained true-crime attention, and the December 4 killing of a UnitedHealthcare executive in Manhattan and the subsequent arrest of suspect Luigi Mangione, which provoked a startling, cross-ideological wave of public sympathy that many commentators called the year's most revealing moment about American attitudes toward the health-care system. Trump's own May 30 conviction on 34 felony counts made him the first former president found guilty of a crime, and the two assassination attempts against him in July and September were the year's most shocking acts of political violence.

    Sports
